document_name: S v Luzhi (7 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 7 (6 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: BLESSING LUZHI versus THE STATE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Luzhi (7 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 7 (6 January 2022)
subject_area: Criminal procedure; Bail pending trial; Armed robbery; Cash-in-transit robbery
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – bail pending trial – armed cash-in-transit robbery – Third Schedule offence – exceptional circumstances – defective alibi affidavit – strength of State case – risk of abscondment – bail refused
headnote: The applicant sought bail pending trial on a charge of armed robbery arising from an alleged cash-in-transit robbery of a Fawcett Security vehicle. The applicant denied involvement and relied, among other things, on affidavits intended to support an alibi. The court noted an attempt to replace an affidavit because the first affidavit related to the wrong date and took a dim view of the attempted correction. The State opposed bail on the basis that the charge was a serious Third Schedule robbery involving firearms, that accomplices remained at large, and that there was evidence linking the applicant to the offence, including recovery of money. The court held that the applicant had failed to show that release was in the interests of justice. Bail was dismissed.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant had shown that the interests of justice permitted his release.
Whether exceptional circumstances were established for a Third Schedule armed-robbery charge.
Whether the applicant’s alibi evidence could be accepted for bail purposes.
Whether the strength of the State case and risk of abscondment justified detention.
legal_principles: For serious robbery involving firearms, the statutory bail framework requires the applicant to satisfy the court that release is in the interests of justice. The court evaluates all relevant factors as a whole, including the strength of the State case, the gravity of the offence, likely penalty, risk of abscondment, interference with investigations and the effect of release on the administration of justice. Bail proceedings do not decide the trial merits but may consider prima facie links to the offence.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant failed to show that it was in the interests of justice that he be released on bail pending trial.
Held, further, that the affidavits filed to support an alibi raised concern and did not assist the applicant.
Held, further, that the State had placed prima facie evidence linking the applicant to the offence, including alleged recovery of cash.
Held, further, that the seriousness of the firearm robbery and risk of abscondment justified refusal of bail.
ratio_decidendi: The decisive reasoning was that the applicant faced a serious armed cash-in-transit robbery charge, the State case was prima facie strong, the alibi material was unsatisfactory, and the risk of abscondment and prejudice to the administration of justice outweighed the applicant’s liberty interest.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court commented adversely on the attempted replacement of an affidavit and reminded counsel that a legal practitioner is an officer of court, not a mere agent of the client.
cases_referred_to:
S v Viljoen 2002 (2) SACR 550 (SCA) para 25
S v Acheson 1991 (2) SA 805 Nm
S v Jongwe SC 62/2002
legislation_considered:
Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 126
Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], ss 115C and 117; Part 1 of the Third Schedule

S v Acheson 1991 (2) SA 805 Nm

document_name: S v Ncube (8 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 8 (6 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: NKOSANA NCUBE Versus THE STATE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Ncube (8 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 8 (6 January 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
    Bail pending trial
    Changed circumstances
    Flight risk
    Murder
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – bail pending trial on changed circumstances – applicant previously refused bail as flight risk – alleged new docket detail, defence outline and deferred trial date relied on – facts already considered or not sufficiently changed – evasion and flight on arrest remained relevant – bail refused.
headnote: The applicant sought bail pending trial on alleged changed circumstances after a previous bail application had failed. He relied on a fuller docket, his defence outline and the postponement of his trial because State witnesses had not been subpoenaed. The Court held that the matters relied on had either been before the previous judge or did not constitute changed circumstances sufficient to warrant release. The applicant had been arrested only after five months of police searching and had fled when police attempted to arrest him. The fear of abscondment remained and the application was dismissed.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant had established facts not previously placed before the court and discovered or arising after the previous refusal of bail.
    Whether the alleged changed circumstances materially reduced the flight risk.
    Whether postponement of the trial because of witness subpoena issues was a changed circumstance warranting bail.
legal_principles: A subsequent bail application after refusal must be based on facts not previously placed before the court and which arose or were discovered after the earlier determination. The Court must ask whether the changed circumstances have changed sufficiently to warrant release without compromising the reasons for the initial refusal.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the matters relied upon by the applicant had substantially been before the previous judge or did not materially alter the bail position.
    Held, further, that the deferment of the trial date did not qualify as a changed circumstance warranting bail.
    Held, further, that the applicant's previous evasion of police and attempted flight on arrest showed that fear of abscondment remained.
    Held, further, that the application had to be dismissed.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that there were no material changed circumstances reducing the previously established risk of abscondment; the applicant remained a flight risk.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to:
S v Ncube HB-163-21
    Daniel Range v The State HB-127-04

Daniel Range v The State HB-127-04

document_name: Mpofu v Chirwa and 2 Others (9 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 9 (6 January 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: SHEILA MPOFU v EMMANUEL CHIRWA and 2 OTHERS
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: Mpofu v Chirwa and 2 Others (9 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 9 (6 January 2022)
subject_area:
Mining law
    Urgent chamber applications
    Interdicts
    Dirty hands
    Domestic remedies
catchwords_flynote: Urgent chamber application – interim interdict in mining partnership dispute – applicant seeking moratorium on mining pending action proceedings – points in limine – dirty hands for non-compliance with extant order to restore gold ore – urgency – delay and prior similar proceedings – internal remedies under Mines and Minerals Act – court's inherent jurisdiction over mining disputes.
headnote: The applicant sought urgent interim relief restraining the 1st respondent from mining or removing ore from Herbenia Mine pending action proceedings concerning the alleged retirement of the 1st respondent from a mining partnership. The 1st respondent raised points in limine, including dirty hands, lack of urgency and failure to exhaust internal remedies. The Court held that the applicant had failed to comply with an extant High Court order requiring restoration of seven tonnes of gold ore and had approached the Court with dirty hands. It also held that the matter was not urgent because the dispute and need to act arose earlier, and the applicant had previously filed and withdrawn similar proceedings. The internal-remedies objection failed because the High Court retained inherent jurisdiction over mining disputes unless the parties agreed otherwise. The application was struck off the roll with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant was barred by the dirty-hands principle because of non-compliance with an extant court order.
    Whether the application was urgent.
    Whether the applicant was required to pursue internal remedies under the Mines and Minerals Act.
    Whether the urgent chamber application should proceed to the merits.
legal_principles: A litigant in contempt of an extant court order may be denied audience until the contempt is purged. Urgency depends on when the need to act arose and whether the applicant acted promptly. A party may not create urgency through delay or repeated proceedings. Under the Mines and Minerals Act, the Mining Commissioner has judicial powers, but the High Court's inherent jurisdiction over mining disputes remains unless the parties agree otherwise.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant had not complied with an extant order requiring restoration of seven tonnes of gold ore and therefore approached the Court with dirty hands.
    Held, further, that the application was not urgent because the need to act arose before the filing of the present application, including from earlier disputes and proceedings.
    Held, further, that the internal-remedies point failed because section 345 of the Mines and Minerals Act preserved the High Court's inherent jurisdiction over mining disputes.
    Held, further, that the dirty-hands and urgency points should be upheld and the urgent chamber application struck off the roll with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The application was disposed of on threshold grounds: the applicant was in non-compliance with an extant order and failed the urgency test. Those findings prevented the Court from granting urgent interim relief, despite rejecting the internal-remedies objection.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to:
Nhapata v Maswi & Another SC 38-16
    Econet Wireless (Private) Limited v The Minister of Public Service Labour and Social Welfare and Others SC 31-2016
legislation_considered: Mines and Minerals Act [Chapter 21:05], sections 345 and 346

document_name: S v Goremusandu (10 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 10 (13 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: TAFADZWA GOREMUSANDU v THE STATE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Goremusandu (10 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 10 (13 January 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
    Bail pending appeal
    Reinstatement of bail
    Stock theft
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – bail pending appeal – reinstatement of bail after appeal previously deemed abandoned – appeal later reinstated – whether earlier bail status should be restored – no voluntary breach of bail conditions by applicant – previous bail order reinstated.
headnote: The applicant had been convicted in the Magistrates Court at Gwanda of stock theft under s 114 of the Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act and sentenced to eighteen years' imprisonment. He had appealed and been granted bail pending appeal, but his appeal was later deemed abandoned after his legal practitioners failed to prosecute it, causing his bail to lapse. After the appeal was reinstated, he applied for reinstatement of bail. The Court noted reservations about prospects of success but declined to revisit an issue already determined when bail was first granted. Since the lapse of bail arose from non-prosecution by erstwhile legal practitioners rather than breach of bail conditions, the Court restored the status quo that existed before dismissal of the appeal and reinstated bail on conditions.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant's bail pending appeal should be reinstated after the underlying appeal was reinstated.
    Whether the Court should revisit the earlier bail court's findings on prospects of success and risk of abscondment.
    What conditions should attach to reinstated bail.
legal_principles: A court considering bail pending appeal has regard to prospects of success, risk of abscondment, the applicant's liberty interest and the likely delay before appeal. Where bail pending appeal lapses because an appeal was deemed abandoned but the appeal is later reinstated, the Court may restore the status quo ante where the lapse was not due to voluntary breach of bail conditions. A prior bail order may be reinstated where no material change justifies a different result.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the earlier grant of bail pending appeal necessarily entailed a finding that there were prospects of success and no sufficient risk of abscondment.
    Held, further, that although the Court had serious reservations about prospects of success, it was neater and appropriate not to revisit the inquiry already decided in the first bail application.
    Held, further, that the lapse of bail had not resulted from the applicant's voluntary breach of conditions but from failure by his former legal practitioners to prosecute the appeal.
    Held, further, that the applicant's bail pending appeal should be reinstated on conditions.
ratio_decidendi: The decisive reason was that the appeal had been reinstated and the bail lapse arose from procedural non-prosecution rather than breach of bail; therefore the appropriate course was to restore the prior bail status pending appeal.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court stated that it had serious reservations about the applicant's prospects of success after reading the record, but treated that inquiry as already determined by the prior bail court.
cases_referred_to: S v Khan HH 66/2006
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], section 114
cases_referred_to_terms: S v Khan HH 66/2006
legislation_considered_terms: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], section 114

document_name: S v Hlabangana and 2 Others (101 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 101 (31 March 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: NKANYISO HLABANGANA and DALUBUHLE MNKANDLA and EMMANUEL SIBANDA versus THE STATE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Hlabangana and 2 Others (101 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 101 (31 March 2022)
subject_area: Criminal procedure; Bail pending appeal; Rape; Alibi; Prospects of success
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – bail pending appeal – rape conviction – prospects of success on appeal – alibi defence – complainant credibility – prejudice to administration of justice – effective 12-year sentence – bail refused
headnote: The applicants were convicted by a regional magistrate on three counts of rape and each sentenced to an effective 12 years’ imprisonment. They applied for bail pending appeal, relying on numerous grounds challenging the complainant’s credibility, the rejection of alibi defences, the treatment of the investigating officer’s evidence and the sentence. The High Court reviewed the principles governing bail after conviction and the role of prospects of success. It held that the grounds of appeal did not disclose any real prospects of success. The complainant’s credibility had been accepted by the trial court; the alibi evidence had inconsistencies and had been properly rejected; and the appeal had no fighting chance. Bail pending appeal was refused.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicants had prospects of success on appeal.
Whether the complainant’s credibility and alleged contradictions created an arguable appeal.
Whether the alibi defences had been improperly rejected.
Whether the administration of justice would be prejudiced by release pending appeal.
Whether bail should be granted after conviction and sentence.
legal_principles: In bail pending appeal, the presumption of innocence has fallen away and prospects of success on appeal are central. The applicant must show that release is in the interests of justice. The seriousness of the offence, seriousness of the sentence, risk of abscondment and prejudice to the administration of justice are relevant. An alibi must be sufficiently disclosed to allow investigation, though the State bears the burden of disproving it beyond reasonable doubt.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicants had not shown prospects of success on appeal.
Held, further, that the trial court’s findings on the complainant’s credibility and on the alibi evidence did not disclose appealable misdirection.
Held, further, that the grounds of appeal did not amount to an arguable case.
Held, further, that the application for bail pending appeal had no merit.
ratio_decidendi: The decisive reasoning was that the applicants stood convicted of a serious sexual offence and sentenced to substantial imprisonment, while their grounds of appeal were either unsupported, based on immaterial matters, or answered by the trial court’s credibility and alibi findings. Without prospects of success, bail pending appeal could not be justified.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: Exmin Syndicate v Dube and 4 Other (102 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 102 (31 March 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: EXMIN SYNDICATE v LUKE DUBE and 4 Others
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
case_number:
HC 468/22
XREF HC 887/21
XREF HC 127/22
XREF HC 131/22
XREF HC 190/22
XREF HC 373/22
XREF HC 325/22
citation: Exmin Syndicate v Dube and 4 Other (102 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 102 (31 March 2022)
subject_area:
Mining law
Spoliation
Urgent chamber applications
Practice and procedure
Mandament van spolie
catchwords_flynote: Mining law – urgent chamber application for spoliation – adjacent mining claims – Tigress and Lion West 25 – multiple related proceedings – disputed boundaries and competing mining rights – requirement of clear proof for final spoliation order – effect of extant order restoring respondent to Lion West 25 – paragraph requiring Provincial Mining Director to identify location – application dismissed.
headnote: The applicant sought a spoliation order removing the first respondent and persons claiming through him from the Tigress mining claims and barring mining activity. The parties had a long history of related mining litigation involving adjacent claims, Tigress and Lion West 25. The first respondent denied spoliation, asserted that he operated from his own mining location, and relied on an extant order restoring him to Lion West 25. The Court dismissed most preliminary objections but upheld the objection to the competency of a draft-order paragraph requiring the Provincial Mining Director to identify the correct mining location, which suggested uncertainty as to what property was to be restored. On the merits, the Court held that the applicant had not proved by clear evidence that it was in peaceful and undisturbed possession of the property allegedly despoiled and forcibly deprived of it. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the urgent chamber application was procedurally competent despite related contempt proceedings and multiple pending matters.
Whether material disputes of fact defeated the application.
Whether the application was urgent.
Whether the draft order was competent where it required the Provincial Mining Director to identify the mining location for execution.
Whether the applicant proved the requirements for a mandament van spolie by clear proof.
legal_principles: Spoliation proceedings restore possession and do not determine substantive rights. However, because a spoliation order is final in effect on possession, the applicant must prove the facts necessary for the order by clear proof, not merely a prima facie case. The applicant must prove peaceful and undisturbed possession and forcible or wrongful deprivation without consent. The Court may take note of its own records and related proceedings. Urgent spoliation applications may still fail where the property to be restored is uncertain or the evidence does not clearly establish dispossession.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the existence of related contempt proceedings did not render the spoliation application a nullity, although multiplicity of proceedings was discouraged.
Held, further, that the matter was urgent and the applicant had not simply sat on its rights.
Held, further, that the proposed order requiring the Provincial Mining Director to identify the mining location indicated uncertainty and that aspect of the draft order was incompetent.
Held, further, that the applicant failed to establish by clear proof that it was forcibly deprived of possession of the alleged mining claim.
Held, further, that given the first respondent’s extant order restoring Lion West 25 and the unresolved competing mining interests, the applicant had not established a clear right to spoliatory relief.
ratio_decidendi: The application was dismissed because the applicant failed to prove with the required clarity that it had been in peaceful possession of a clearly identified mining location and had been wrongfully dispossessed by the first respondent; the competing claims, uncertainty of boundaries and extant order in favour of the first respondent defeated the final spoliation relief sought.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: S v Shoshera and 4 Others (103 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 103 (31 March 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: EDMORE SHOSHERA And ALBERT MAKETO TEMBO And PERCY MUKWATURI And TALENT IMBAYAGO And PANASHE MAKUVAZA Versus THE STATE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Shoshera and 4 Others (103 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 103 (31 March 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
    Bail pending trial
    Public violence
    Murder
    Compelling reasons
    Identification evidence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – bail pending trial – public violence and murder allegations arising from political rally violence – State making broad assertions of abscondment, danger to public and witness interference – no identification parade and inadequate link between applicants and alleged offences – compelling reasons not established – bail granted.
headnote: The applicants sought bail pending trial after violence broke out at a Citizens Coalition for Change rally at Mbizo 4 Shopping Centre, Kwekwe. The State alleged that the applicants acted in concert with others in public violence and murder, but initially sixteen suspects had been picked up and only the five applicants were detained. The applicants denied being at the scene or participating in the violence. The Court held that it was not enough for the State to make bold assertions that the matter was serious, that the applicants might abscond, endanger the public or interfere with witnesses. Grounds for refusal of bail had to be substantiated by facts, including facts linking each applicant to the offence and the role allegedly played. Since the State had not established compelling reasons for continued detention, bail was granted in terms of the draft order.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the State established compelling reasons for refusing bail.
    Whether the State sufficiently linked each applicant to the alleged public violence and murder.
    Whether seriousness of the offences alone justified denial of bail.
    Whether alleged pending cases could be relied upon without sufficient detail of their status.
    Whether appropriate bail conditions could protect the administration of justice.
legal_principles: In bail pending trial, courts lean in favour of liberty unless the interests of justice are compromised. The presumption of innocence and section 50 of the Constitution require release unless compelling reasons justify detention. The State must substantiate allegations of abscondment, witness interference, danger to the public or undermining the justice system. Seriousness of the offence alone is not sufficient; the facts must show a link between the accused and the alleged offence and the role each played.
held_decision_points:
Held, that it was insufficient for the State to make broad assertions that grounds for refusing bail existed.
    Held, further, that where several suspects were initially arrested, the State had to provide adequate information linking the applicants ultimately charged to the alleged offences and their roles.
    Held, further, that alleged pending cases could not be relied upon without sufficient details and status.
    Held, further, that the State had not shown compelling reasons for continued detention.
    Held, further, that bail should be granted in terms of the draft order.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the State failed to substantiate its opposition with facts linking each applicant to the offences or proving a risk to the interests of justice, so the constitutional preference for pre-trial liberty prevailed.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: S v Sibanda (104 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 104 (25 March 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE Versus CLIFFORD SIBANDA
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Sibanda (104 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 104 (25 March 2022)
judges: MOYO J with Assessors Mr J. Sobantu & Mr E. Mashingaidze
subject_area:
Criminal law
    Murder
    Constructive intent
    Common purpose
    Sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – accused and associates assaulting deceased after earlier confrontation – deceased stabbed and later struck with stone and log after fleeing and lying injured – constructive intent – common purpose – murder conviction – sentence.
headnote: The accused was charged with murder arising from an incident at Garikai intersection, Cowdray Park. State witnesses described the accused and his associates harassing and attacking the deceased after he intervened in the harassment of another person. The accused admitted that, after the deceased fled and was no longer a threat, he followed him, used a knife and later struck him with a log while he lay injured, and acknowledged acting in common purpose with another person who struck the deceased with a stone. The Court held that the accused acted recklessly because striking a man on the head with a log of the size described created a real possibility of death. He was convicted of murder with constructive intent and sentenced to 14 years' imprisonment after allowance for time spent in remand prison.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the accused caused the deceased's death.
    Whether the accused acted with the mens rea required for murder, including constructive intent.
    Whether the accused acted in common purpose with the other assailants.
    What sentence was appropriate.
legal_principles: Murder may be established by constructive intent where an accused realises that death is a real possibility and proceeds regardless. Common purpose may be inferred from participation in a joint assault. In sentencing for murder, the Court must weigh personal mitigation, time spent in remand custody and the need to mark society's displeasure at fatal violence.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the accused and his colleagues hit the deceased with a stone, a log and at some stage stabbed him with a knife.
    Held, further, that the final assault occurred after the deceased had already fled and was no longer a threat.
    Held, further, that by striking the deceased on the head with a log of the size described, the accused was reckless because there was a real possibility of death.
    Held, further, that the accused was guilty of murder with constructive intent.
    Held, further, that a sentence of 14 years' imprisonment was appropriate after discounting one year for time spent in remand custody.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the accused persisted in a violent assault after the deceased had fled and was no longer a threat, and his use of a log to strike the deceased on the head showed recklessness as to the real possibility of death, establishing constructive intent for murder.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to: None identified
legislation_considered:
Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], section 47
    Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], section 314
cases_referred_to_terms: None identified

Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], section 314

document_name: S v Mthimkhulu (105 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 105 (25 March 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE versus HERBERT MTHIMKHULU
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Mthimkhulu (105 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 105 (25 March 2022)
judges: MOYO J with Assessors Mr J. Sobantu & Mr E. Mashingaidze
subject_area:
Criminal law
    Murder
    Culpable homicide
    Domestic violence
    Provocation
    Sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – accused assaulting wife with hosepipe after allegation of infidelity – prolonged assault but weapon and circumstances creating doubt on legal intention – benefit of doubt – conviction for culpable homicide – domestic violence aggravating – five years' imprisonment imposed after discount for remand custody.
headnote: The accused was charged with murder for the death of his wife after he assaulted her with a hosepipe following an allegation that she had been unfaithful. He denied murder and tendered a limited plea to culpable homicide. The Court heard two viva voce State witnesses, admitted other evidence and considered the accused's confirmed warned and cautioned statement. Although the assault was serious and prolonged, the Court held that the State had not proved legal intention to kill beyond reasonable doubt, having regard to the accused's anger, the nature of the weapon and the circumstances of the assault. The accused was convicted of culpable homicide. In sentence, the Court treated domestic violence as a serious social harm but gave credit for the plea, remorse, first-offender status and about one year seven months spent on remand, imposing five years' imprisonment.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the accused had the legal intention required for murder.
    Whether the limited plea to culpable homicide should be accepted.
    Whether anger, the weapon used and circumstances of the assault created reasonable doubt on murder intent.
    What sentence was appropriate for culpable homicide in a domestic violence setting.
legal_principles: Murder requires proof of the intention to kill, whether actual or legal intention. Legal intention may be inferred only where the only reasonable inference is that the accused foresaw death as a real possibility and continued regardless. Where factual circumstances such as anger, provocation, intoxication, intelligence, weapon type and assault circumstances create reasonable doubt on intention, the accused receives the benefit of doubt. Domestic violence causing death remains a serious aggravating factor in sentence.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the State proved that the accused unlawfully assaulted the deceased and caused her death.
    Held, further, that the State did not prove beyond reasonable doubt that the accused had legal intention to kill.
    Held, further, that the accused's anger, the nature of the hosepipe and the circumstances of the assault created reasonable doubt on murder.
    Held, further, that the accused was guilty of culpable homicide.
    Held, further, that a custodial sentence of five years' imprisonment was appropriate after discount for remand custody.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the accused negligently caused the deceased's death through a domestic assault, but the State failed to prove murder intent beyond reasonable doubt in light of the weapon, emotional context and surrounding circumstances.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to:
R v Jolly 1923 AD 176
    R v Mkwetshana 1965 (2) SA 493
    S v Mugwanda 2002 (1) ZLR 574 (S)

document_name: S v Sibanda (106 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 118 (30 March 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE Versus LUCKY SIBANDA
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Sibanda (106 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 118 (30 March 2022)
judges: DUBE-BANDA J with Assessors Mr. Mashingaidze and Mr. Ndlovu
subject_area:
Criminal law
    Murder
    Circumstantial evidence
    Robbery-related killing
    Evidence
    Sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – circumstantial evidence – deceased disappeared while selling tomatoes – body later discovered decomposed – deceased's cellphone traced to purchaser who identified accused as seller – accused's photograph found on cellphone – accused's explanation rejected – circumstantial evidence excluding reasonable innocent inference – murder in aggravating circumstances – life imprisonment.
headnote: The accused was charged with murder after the deceased disappeared while selling tomatoes and was later found dead in a decomposed state. The cause of death could not be ascertained because of decomposition. The State relied on circumstantial evidence linking the accused to the deceased's missing cellphone. The cellphone was traced through call records, recovered from a purchaser, and the purchaser identified the accused as the person who sold it. A photograph of the accused was found on the phone. The accused claimed that he had acquired the phone from another person, but that explanation was rejected. The Court held that the proven facts excluded any reasonable inference other than guilt. The accused was convicted of murder and sentenced to life imprisonment because the murder was committed in aggravating circumstances.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the deceased's death was unlawfully caused.
    Whether the circumstantial evidence linked the accused to the deceased's death beyond reasonable doubt.
    Whether the accused's explanation for possession and sale of the deceased's cellphone was reasonably possibly true.
    Whether the murder was committed in aggravating circumstances.
    What sentence was appropriate.
legal_principles: In a criminal case based on circumstantial evidence, the inference of guilt must be consistent with all proved facts and the proved facts must exclude all reasonable inferences other than guilt. The State bears the burden of proving guilt beyond reasonable doubt. Murder committed during or in connection with robbery, or accompanied by serious aggravating circumstances, attracts severe punishment including life imprisonment.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the State's case rested on circumstantial evidence and had to satisfy the rules governing such evidence.
    Held, further, that the cellphone evidence, identification by the purchaser, and the accused's photograph on the phone linked the accused to the deceased and the murder.
    Held, further, that the accused's explanation was not reasonably possibly true.
    Held, further, that the accused killed the deceased and the murder was committed in aggravating circumstances.
    Held, further, that the appropriate sentence was life imprisonment.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the circumstantial evidence concerning the deceased's cellphone, together with the rejection of the accused's explanation, excluded any reasonable inference consistent with innocence and established murder beyond reasonable doubt.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: S v Moyo (107 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 107 (7 April 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: MAHLULI MOYO versus THE STATE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Moyo (107 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 107 (7 April 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
    Bail pending trial
    Attempted murder
    Malicious damage to property
    Unlawful entry
    Assault
    Identification
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – bail pending trial – applicant charged with attempted murder and related offences – complainant's daughter knowing applicant as former classmate and allegedly identifying him at scene – applicant allegedly attempted to flee on arrest – strong prima facie case – flight risk – bail refused.
headnote: The applicant applied for bail pending trial on charges including attempted murder, malicious damage to property, unlawful entry and assault. He denied the allegations and said this was a case of mistaken identity. The State alleged that the applicant and accomplices attacked the complainant's homestead at night, assaulted the complainant and his wife, and that the complainant's daughter positively identified the applicant as a former classmate. The Court held that the State had a strong prima facie case, that the applicant faced serious charges and likely imprisonment if convicted, and that his alleged attempted flight on arrest pointed to a risk of abscondment. Bail was refused.
issues_for_determination:
Whether there were compelling reasons to deny bail pending trial.
    Whether the State case was strong despite the applicant's denial and claim of mistaken identity.
    Whether the applicant was a flight risk.
    Whether stringent bail conditions would protect the interests of justice.
legal_principles: In bail pending trial, a court balances liberty against the administration of justice. Bail may be refused where the seriousness of the offence, strength of the State case and likely penalty create a likelihood of abscondment. Positive identification by a witness who knew the accused before the offence may strengthen the State case for bail purposes. Attempted flight on arrest is relevant to flight risk.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the State had a strong prima facie case because the applicant was allegedly positively identified by Providence Dube, who knew him as a former classmate.
    Held, further, that the attempted-murder charge and related violent allegations were serious and likely to attract a lengthy custodial sentence if proved.
    Held, further, that the applicant's alleged attempt to flee during arrest was relevant to risk of abscondment.
    Held, further, that the applicant was not a suitable candidate for bail pending trial.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the strong identification evidence, seriousness of the charges, likely sentence and alleged attempted flight established a real risk that the applicant would abscond if released.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

S v Kuruneri 2004 (1) ZLR 2004
    S v Jongwe SC 62-2002
    Aitken & Anor v Attorney General 1992 (1) ZLR 249 (S)

document_name: S v Mumpande (108 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 108 (6 April 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE Versus NICHOLAS MUMPANDE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Mumpande (108 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 108 (6 April 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
    Criminal review
    Road traffic offences
    Negligent driving
    Reckless driving
    Sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – review – accused charged with negligent driving after driving against one-way traffic and hitting pedestrian – admitted facts disclosing reckless driving – prosecutor as dominus litis but magistrate should query inadequate lesser charge – sentence too lenient – certificate withheld.
headnote: This was a criminal review at the instance of the scrutinising Regional Magistrate. The accused had pleaded guilty to negligent driving after driving against a one-way direction and hitting a pedestrian. The Regional Magistrate queried why the accused had not been charged with reckless driving. The High Court agreed that the facts disclosed reckless driving and that the trial magistrate should at least have queried the lesser charge. The Court also found the sentence of a fine and driving prohibition disturbingly inappropriate given the deliberate driving on the wrong side of the road. The Court withheld its certificate because the proceedings were not in accordance with real and substantial justice.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the admitted facts disclosed negligent driving or the more serious offence of reckless driving.
    Whether a trial magistrate should query a lesser charge where the facts disclose a more serious offence.
    Whether the sentence was in accordance with real and substantial justice.
    Whether the High Court should certify the proceedings on review.
legal_principles: Although the prosecutor is dominus litis and has the prerogative to prefer charges, that rule is not absolute. Where admitted facts disclose a more serious offence, the trial court should query why the lesser charge has been preferred. Driving on the wrong side of the road is serious and may connote recklessness. A reviewing court may withhold its certificate where proceedings or sentence are not in accordance with real and substantial justice.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the admitted facts disclosed reckless driving rather than mere negligent driving.
    Held, further, that the trial magistrate should at least have queried why the lesser charge was preferred.
    Held, further, that the sentence imposed was a mockery of justice and disturbingly inappropriate.
    Held, further, that the proceedings were not in accordance with real and substantial justice and the certificate had to be withheld.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the accused's admitted conduct of deliberately driving against one-way traffic warranted a more serious charge or, at minimum, judicial inquiry into the charge, and the lenient sentence failed to reflect the gravity of the conduct.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: S v Ndlovu (106 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 109 (7 April 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: SIKHANYISO NDLOVU Versus THE STATE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Ndlovu (106 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 109 (7 April 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
    Bail pending trial
    Appeal against refusal of bail
    Previous convictions
    Propensity to commit offences
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – appeal against refusal of bail – theft charge – magistrate refusing bail because applicant had previous conviction and alleged pending matter – no evidence of pending case or offending while on bail – finding speculative and contrary to presumption of innocence – bail granted with conditions.
headnote: The applicant appealed against a magistrate's refusal of bail pending trial on a theft charge. The magistrate had refused bail essentially because the applicant had a previous conviction and was thought to have a pending matter, from which the magistrate inferred a propensity to commit similar offences. The High Court held that the 2021 warrant had been cancelled, the applicant had been placed off remand, and there were no pending cases. A prior conviction alone did not show a propensity to commit offences or justify denial of bail. The State had not shown that the applicant committed an offence while on bail or breached bail conditions. The appeal succeeded and the applicant was admitted to bail on conditions.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the magistrate misdirected herself by refusing bail based on a previous conviction and a supposed pending matter.
    Whether the State proved compelling reasons for continued detention.
    Whether a prior conviction alone established propensity to commit offences while on bail.
    Whether the applicant had proffered a plausible defence.
legal_principles: An accused is entitled to bail pending trial unless the State establishes compelling reasons justifying continued detention. A previous conviction does not by itself show a propensity to commit further offences. Under section 117, evidence that an accused previously committed an offence while released on bail or failed to comply with bail conditions may be relevant, but it must be proved. In bail applications, an accused need only proffer a plausible defence, not prove innocence.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the magistrate erred in concluding that the 2021 matter remained pending after the warrant had been cancelled.
    Held, further, that the magistrate's conclusion that the applicant would return to court on similar allegations was speculative and unsupported by evidence.
    Held, further, that the previous conviction did not establish a propensity to commit offences while on bail.
    Held, further, that the applicant had proffered a plausible defence and was a suitable candidate for bail.
    Held, further, that bail should be granted on conditions.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that bail could not be refused on speculation drawn from a previous conviction where the State had not proved pending charges, offending while on bail, breach of bail conditions or another compelling reason justifying detention.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: Maguma v City of Kwekwe and 2 Others (11 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 11 (13 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: CLEOPAS MAGUMA v CITY OF KWEKWE and 2 Others
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: Maguma v City of Kwekwe and 2 Others (11 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 11 (13 January 2022)
subject_area:
Administrative law
Local government
Housing and urban property
Declaratory relief
Prescription
catchwords_flynote: Administrative law – local authority housing – ministerial circular on title deeds for home ownership schemes – sitting tenant of council accommodation – section 313 of Urban Councils Act [Chapter 29:15] – whether circular binding and enforceable by tenant – prescription – review disguised as declaratur – privity – discretion of council – court not compelling offer to contract.
headnote: The applicant sought an order compelling the City of Kwekwe to comply with a ministerial circular on title deeds for home ownership schemes and transfer to him a council property he had occupied for more than twenty years, or alternatively to value and offer the property to him for purchase. The Council opposed, arguing prescription and that the application was a review disguised as a declaratur. The Court dismissed both preliminary objections. On the merits, the Court held that a ministerial circular issued under section 313 of the Urban Councils Act is binding only if the statutory process, including giving council an opportunity to make counter-proposals, is shown to have been followed. The applicant had not adduced evidence of that process. The circular was not binding and had no force of law on the facts before the Court. It was also directed to local authorities, not the applicant, and did not create a legal relationship enforceable by him. In any event, the circular left local authorities with discretion. The alternative relief was incompetent because the Court could not compel a party to make an offer and enter a contract. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant’s claim had prescribed.
Whether the application was in substance a review disguised as a declaratur.
Whether Local Authorities Circular No. 1 of 2015 was binding and had force of law.
Whether the applicant could found a cause of action on the circular.
Whether the Council had discretion under the circular.
Whether the Court could compel Council to make an offer to sell the property to the applicant.
legal_principles: A party raising prescription bears the evidential onus to prove that the claim is prescribed; allegations in heads of argument are not evidence. A ministerial circular under section 313 of the Urban Councils Act becomes binding only after the statutory process, including the opportunity for council to submit its views and counter-proposals, has been followed. A third party cannot enforce a circular directed to local authorities where it creates no legal relationship with that party. The doctrine of privity prevents a non-party from suing on an agreement or instrument directed to others. Courts should defer to a local authority’s discretion absent a properly founded case of illegality, irrationality or procedural impropriety. A court generally cannot compel a party to make an offer and enter a contract.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the Council failed to prove prescription because it adduced no evidence contradicting the applicant’s statement that he became aware of the circular in September 2018.
Held, further, that the application was not a review disguised as a declaratur.
Held, further, that the applicant failed to show that Circular No. 1 of 2015 was binding under section 313 of the Urban Councils Act.
Held, further, that the circular did not create a legal relationship between the applicant and Council and the applicant could not found a cause of action upon it.
Held, further, that the circular left Council with discretion and the Court could not simply usurp that discretion.
Held, further, that the alternative relief compelling valuation and an offer to purchase was incompetent because a court cannot compel a party to make an offer to contract.
ratio_decidendi: The application failed because the applicant could not enforce a ministerial circular that had not been shown to be binding under section 313 of the Urban Councils Act and that, in any event, was directed to local authorities and left Council with discretion rather than conferring an enforceable right on the applicant.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court stated for completeness that even if the circular were binding, Council retained discretion to determine whether a sitting tenant was genuine and deserving, and the Court could not substitute its own view absent a proper case for interference.

Van Brooker v Mudhanda & Another AND Pierce v Mudhanda & Another SC 5 / 2018
Community Water Alliance Trust & Anor v City of Harare & Anor HH 194 of 2020
TBIC (Private) Limited & Another v Mangenje & 5 Others SC 13/18

Administrative Justice Act [Chapter 10:28], sections 3 and 5
Prescription Act [Chapter 8:11]

document_name: Dare v Dare and 3 Others (110 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 110 (14 April 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: STEVEN ARTHUR DARE v ARTHUR DARE and 3 Others
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: Dare v Dare and 3 Others (110 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 110 (14 April 2022)
subject_area:
Urgent chamber applications
Practice and procedure
Interdicts
Immovable property
Company property
catchwords_flynote: Urgent chamber application – interim interdict to prevent sale and transfer of immovable property – family and shareholder dispute – delay from date need to act arose – applicant aware of threat to sell in January but filed urgent application in March – negotiations not sufficient to preserve urgency – matter struck off roll.
headnote: The applicant sought urgent interim relief preventing the sale or transfer of 45 Southway Road, Burnside, Bulawayo, pending the finalisation of related proceedings. The property was registered in the name of a company in which the applicant and respondents were shareholders. The applicant contended that he had personally funded its purchase. The Court held that the need to act arose on 5 January 2022 when the applicant had been told of the intended sale and had already instructed a solicitor to object and seek a court order. He did not file the urgent chamber application until March 2022. Family discussions and negotiations did not preserve urgency in the absence of an undertaking that the sale would not proceed. The matter was struck off the roll with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the urgent chamber application satisfied the requirements of urgency.
When the need to act arose.
Whether family discussions or negotiations justified the delay.
Whether the matter should be heard urgently or struck off the roll.
legal_principles: Urgency is assessed by identifying when the need to act arose and whether the applicant acted promptly. A litigant who delays after recognising the need for legal action cannot create urgency by later approaching the court. Negotiations or family discussions do not preserve urgency where there is no undertaking that the threatened act will not occur.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the need to act arose on 5 January 2022 when the applicant was informed of the intended sale and stated that he had instructed a solicitor to seek a court order.
Held, further, that the applicant delayed until March before bringing the urgent application and did not act when the need to do so arose.
Held, further, that the matter was no longer urgent.
Held, further, that the application should be struck off the roll with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The decisive basis for striking the matter off was that the applicant knew of the threatened sale and the need for court intervention in January 2022 but waited until March to bring the urgent chamber application, without any adequate explanation or undertaking preserving the status quo.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: Mhlanga v Magistrate Dzira N.O and Another (111 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 111 (14 April 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: SIBUSISIWE MHLANGA v MAGISTRATE DZIRA N.O and THE STATE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: Mhlanga v Magistrate Dzira N.O and Another (111 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 111 (14 April 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
    Review
    Stay of proceedings
    Application for discharge at close of State case
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – urgent chamber application for stay of unterminated magistrates court proceedings – review pending against refusal of discharge at close of State case – exceptional circumstances required – section 198(3) of Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act – theft between spouses – section 120 of Criminal Law Code – whether review had prospects of success.
headnote: The applicant sought a stay of criminal proceedings in the magistrates court pending review of the magistrate's refusal to discharge her at the close of the State case. She faced theft and money-laundering charges and argued that, because the theft count concerned property allegedly stolen from a spouse, the prosecution was a nullity without Prosecutor-General authorisation under section 120 of the Criminal Law Code. The Court held that superior courts should interfere with unterminated criminal proceedings only in exceptional circumstances. The magistrate had considered whether there was a prima facie case and had given reasons. The Court held that no grave injustice or exceptional circumstance had been shown, and that the review appeared designed to delay the criminal proceedings. The stay application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the pending review had sufficient prospects of success to justify staying the criminal proceedings.
    Whether exceptional circumstances existed for High Court interference in unterminated criminal proceedings.
    Whether the alleged absence of Prosecutor-General authorisation under section 120 of the Criminal Law Code justified a stay.
    Whether refusing the stay would occasion grave injustice.
legal_principles: A superior court should be slow to interfere in unterminated proceedings in a lower court. Interference is reserved for exceptional circumstances of proven gross irregularity causing a miscarriage of justice that cannot otherwise be redressed, or where the interlocutory decision is clearly wrong and seriously prejudicial. A stay pending review of a refusal of discharge depends on prospects of success. An application for discharge is not designed to shield an accused person from being placed on their defence where a prima facie case has been found.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the Court must not prejudge the pending review but may assess whether it is sufficiently meritorious to justify a stay.
    Held, further, that the magistrate had made a finding that the State established a prima facie case and had given detailed reasons for that finding.
    Held, further, that no exceptional circumstances or grave injustice had been shown to justify interference in unterminated proceedings.
    Held, further, that the application for stay appeared to have been filed to frustrate and delay the criminal trial.
    Held, further, that the application should be dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that a stay of unterminated criminal proceedings is justified only in exceptional circumstances, and the applicant failed to show that the magistrate's refusal of discharge was irrational, untenable or productive of grave injustice that could not be remedied later.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: Magwaro v Ndiweni (112 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 112 (21 April 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: LINDA MAGWARO v MTHOKOZISI NDIWENI
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: Magwaro v Ndiweni (112 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 112 (21 April 2022)
subject_area:
Family law
Divorce
Maintenance
Children
Variation of consent order
Currency and monetary law
catchwords_flynote: Family law – variation of divorce consent paper and order – maintenance for minor children – section 9 of Matrimonial Causes Act [Chapter 5:13] – best interests of the child – section 81 of Constitution – effect of S.I. 33/2019 on United States dollar maintenance order – good cause – changed financial circumstances – respondent’s candour – payment in United States dollars or RTGS equivalent at bank rate.
headnote: The applicant sought variation of a divorce consent paper and order requiring the respondent to pay US$500 per child per month. After S.I. 33/2019, the respondent treated the obligation as RTGS$500 per child, later paying US$50 per child and then arguing he could not afford US$500 per child because his circumstances had changed. The Court dismissed the respondent’s points in limine concerning documents attached to the answering affidavit. On the merits, the Court held that S.I. 33/2019 had altered the monetary effect of the original order to an amount that was plainly inadequate for the children’s needs, and that this constituted good cause for variation. The Court found that the respondent was not candid about his alleged lost income sources because those losses predated the original maintenance order, and that his later remarriage was a relevant changed circumstance requiring some reduction. In the best interests of the children, the Court varied the order to require payment of US$400 per child per month in cash or into a Nostro account or the RTGS equivalent at the prevailing bank rate, effective from March 2021.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant showed good cause for variation of the maintenance order.
Whether S.I. 33/2019 created a change in circumstances requiring variation.
Whether the respondent was candid about his financial circumstances and ability to pay.
Whether the applicant sought to live lavishly off the children’s maintenance.
Whether it was in the best interests of the children to allow payment of RTGS$7 500 per child.
What varied amount should be ordered.
legal_principles: Under section 9 of the Matrimonial Causes Act, a court may vary a maintenance order on good cause shown. In determining maintenance, the Court considers income-earning capacity, assets, financial needs and responsibilities, and the standard of living and educational needs of the children. The best interests of the child are paramount under section 81 of the Constitution, and the High Court acts as upper guardian. S.I. 33/2019 altered pre-existing United States dollar obligations to RTGS at one-to-one, but where that produces an amount plainly inadequate for children, it may constitute good cause for variation. A party seeking or opposing variation must make full and candid disclosure of financial circumstances.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the points in limine concerning annexures and new matter in the answering affidavit lacked merit because the annexures met issues raised in the founding and opposing affidavits.
Held, further, that the applicant showed good cause for variation because S.I. 33/2019 reduced the real value of the original maintenance order to a manifestly inadequate amount.
Held, further, that the respondent was bound by his election to pay amounts in United States dollars and could not rely on alleged overpayment to excuse non-payment of maintenance.
Held, further, that the respondent had not been candid about his financial circumstances, because the alleged loss of income sources predated the original order and he failed to provide full financial disclosure.
Held, further, that the respondent’s remarriage was a relevant changed circumstance warranting reduction from the original US$500 per child.
Held, further, that it was not in the children’s best interests to allow payment of RTGS$7 500 per child and the order should be varied to US$400 per child per month or the RTGS equivalent at the bank rate.
ratio_decidendi: The variation was justified because the statutory currency conversion had rendered the original maintenance order practically inadequate for the children’s needs, and the respondent failed to prove a genuine inability to pay beyond the relevant factor of his remarriage; the best interests of the children required a meaningful upward variation.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: Kapfumo v Mutimutema and Another (115 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 115 (28 April 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: MICHAEL TOZIVEPI KAPFUMO v KILLIAN MUTIMUTEMA and CONSTANCE MUSAFARE MUTIMUTEMA (Executrix Dative in the Estate of the late Augustine Mutizwa Mutimutema)
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: Kapfumo v Mutimutema and Another (115 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 115 (28 April 2022)
subject_area:
Property law
Immovable property
Deeds and transfer
Rei vindicatio
Civil trial
Evidence
catchwords_flynote: Property law – immovable property – registered title in name of nominee – beneficial ownership – transfer into true owner’s name – title deed as prima facie but not conclusive proof of ownership – rei vindicatio – eviction before transfer – holding-over damages – family arrangement not lease – onus and credibility in civil trial.
headnote: The plaintiff claimed transfer of a Glen View property registered in the names of the first defendant and her late husband, eviction of the first defendant and persons claiming occupation through her, holding-over damages and costs. He alleged that he had purchased the stand while resident in Botswana, had caused it to be registered in the first defendant’s name as his nominee, and had built the house. The defendants contended that they had purchased the property and that any assistance from the plaintiff and his nephew was a donation or family support. After hearing evidence, the Court accepted the plaintiff’s evidence and found the first defendant’s evidence unreliable and inconsistent. It held that the plaintiff had paid for the stand and the development of the house, and that the first defendant held the property as nominee. A title deed was prima facie but not conclusive proof of ownership. The plaintiff was entitled to transfer and eviction. The claim for holding-over damages failed because the occupation arose from a family arrangement and not a lease agreement, and there was no evidence proving the value of occupation.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the plaintiff was entitled to transfer of Stand number 9795 Glenview Township into his name.
Whether the first defendant held the property as nominee for the plaintiff.
Whether the plaintiff was entitled to eviction of the first defendant and all those claiming occupation through her.
Whether the plaintiff was entitled to holding-over damages of US$500 per month from summons to eviction.
Costs.
legal_principles: A nominee is a person in whose name property is registered but who holds it for the benefit of the true or beneficial owner. A title deed is prima facie evidence of ownership but is not conclusive and may be challenged where evidence establishes that it does not reflect the true state of affairs. The rei vindicatio entitles an owner to recover property from another who has no lawful right to retain it. Holding-over damages are not available merely because a person occupies property under a family arrangement; absent a lease or evidence proving value of occupation, such damages are not sustainable.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the plaintiff proved on a balance of probabilities that he paid the purchase price for the stand and single-handedly paid for the development of the house.
Held, further, that the first defendant held the property as the plaintiff’s nominee and that inclusion of the late Mutimutema’s name in the title deed was not authorised by the plaintiff.
Held, further, that registration in the defendants’ names was not conclusive of ownership and the plaintiff had rebutted the presumption arising from the deed of transfer.
Held, further, that the plaintiff was entitled to transfer of the property into his name and to eviction of the first defendant and those claiming occupation through her.
Held, further, that the claim for holding-over damages failed because the relationship was not a lease and the quantum was not proved.
ratio_decidendi: The plaintiff’s claim to transfer and eviction succeeded because the evidence established that he was the beneficial owner, having paid for the stand and its development, while the first defendant held title only as nominee; however, holding-over damages were not recoverable because occupation was not under a lease and no rental value was proved.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: Dhliwayo v Warman Zimbabwe (Pvt) Ltd and 2 Others (12 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 12 (13 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: STEWART DHLIWAYO v WARMAN ZIMBABWE (PVT) LTD and 2 Others
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: Dhliwayo v Warman Zimbabwe (Pvt) Ltd and 2 Others (12 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 12 (13 January 2022)
subject_area:
Civil procedure
Rescission of judgment
Peremption
Company law
Default judgment
catchwords_flynote: Rescission of judgment – rule 63(1) of High Court Rules, 1971 – application to rescind default judgment – preliminary point of peremption – applicant’s conduct after judgment – acquiescence inconsistent with intention to challenge judgment – authority to depose to opposing affidavit – application dismissed without considering merits.
headnote: The applicant sought rescission under rule 63(1) of the High Court Rules, 1971 of a default judgment granted in HC 2693/15 concerning the disputed indigenisation and control of Warman Zimbabwe (Pvt) Ltd. The respondents raised preliminary objections, including that the applicant had acquiesced in the judgment and was therefore perempted from seeking rescission. The applicant also challenged the authority of the deponent to the respondents’ opposing papers. The Court rejected the authority objection, distinguishing between a party’s authority to oppose proceedings and a witness’s competence to depose to facts. On peremption, the Court held that the applicant’s conduct was unequivocal and inconsistent with an intention to challenge the judgment. The preliminary point was upheld and the rescission application dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the opposition by the second and third respondents was properly before the Court.
Whether the applicant had acquiesced in the default judgment and thereby perempted his right to seek rescission.
Whether the application should be dismissed on the preliminary point without reaching the merits.
legal_principles: A person who can swear positively to facts may depose to an affidavit; the deponent is not the party’s legal representative. Peremption arises where a litigant, knowing of a judgment, acts unequivocally and inconsistently with an intention to challenge it. Acquiescence in a judgment precludes a later attempt to appeal or seek rescission. The onus lies on the party alleging peremption, and doubtful cases are resolved against peremption.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant’s challenge to the authority of the respondents’ deponent was misplaced and failed.
Held, further, that the respondents discharged the onus of showing that the applicant had voluntarily acquiesced in the default judgment.
Held, further, that the applicant’s conduct was unequivocal and inconsistent with any intention to challenge the judgment in HC 2693/15.
Held, further, that the preliminary point on peremption succeeded and the rescission application had to be dismissed without reaching the remaining points or merits.
ratio_decidendi: The application was dismissed because the applicant’s post-judgment conduct showed unequivocal acquiescence in the default judgment, thereby perempting his right to seek rescission under rule 63(1).
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: S v Moyo (13 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 13 (13 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE versus LEVIOUS MOYO
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Moyo (13 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 13 (13 January 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
    Criminal review
    Assault
    Plea proceedings
    Trial de novo
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – review – magistrate entering plea of not guilty after equivocal guilty plea – matter later improperly treated as plea recording again – accused acquitted after further questioning without trial – State not afforded opportunity to lead evidence – gross irregularity – proceedings quashed – trial de novo ordered.
headnote: This review arose after the magistrate herself referred the record to the High Court, having realised that the proceedings were irregular. The accused had initially pleaded guilty to assault but disputed use of an axe, leading the magistrate correctly to enter a plea of not guilty and remand the matter for trial. On the trial date, the prosecutor wrongly insisted that the matter was again for plea recording. The magistrate repeated the plea process, asked further questions, found that self-defence had been raised and acquitted the accused without a trial. The High Court held that the trial court committed a fatal irregularity by failing to conduct a trial and by denying the State the opportunity to lead evidence. The proceedings were set aside and a trial de novo before another magistrate was ordered.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the magistrates court proceedings were in accordance with real and substantial justice.
    Whether the magistrate should have proceeded to trial after entering a plea of not guilty.
    Whether acquitting the accused without hearing evidence from the State was a gross irregularity.
    Whether a trial de novo should be ordered.
legal_principles: Where a plea of guilty is not an unequivocal admission of guilt, the court must enter a plea of not guilty and proceed to trial. Once such plea has been entered, the State must be given an opportunity to lead evidence. Failure to conduct the trial after a not-guilty plea is a gross irregularity capable of causing a substantial miscarriage of justice. On review, the High Court may set aside such proceedings and order a trial de novo before a different magistrate.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the magistrate correctly entered a plea of not guilty on the first appearance when the accused did not unequivocally admit guilt.
    Held, further, that it was irregular to repeat plea proceedings on the trial date and then acquit without a trial.
    Held, further, that the State had been improperly denied the opportunity to lead evidence.
    Held, further, that the irregularity was fundamental and required the proceedings to be quashed and a trial de novo ordered.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that once a plea of not guilty had been entered, the failure to conduct a trial and the acquittal of the accused without evidence constituted a gross irregularity causing a substantial miscarriage of justice.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to: None identified

    High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], section 29(2)(b)(i)
cases_referred_to_terms: None identified

document_name: S v Ndlovu (136 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 136 (23 May 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: STATE Versus PRINCE NDLOVU
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Ndlovu (136 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 136 (23 May 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
    Criminal review
    Environmental law
    Hazardous substances
    Forfeiture
    Sentencing
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – review – storage or sale of hazardous substance without licence – miscitation of penal provision – no prejudice where essential elements canvassed – forfeiture of petrol – magistrate prescribing disposal by public auction – court's role ending with forfeiture order – sentence amended by deleting public-auction direction.
headnote: The accused was convicted on his own plea of guilty of storing or selling hazardous substances without a licence after police found petrol in his vehicle. He was fined and the petrol was forfeited, with the trial court additionally ordering that the exhibit be disposed of by public auction. On review, the Regional Magistrate queried whether the cited provision created an offence and whether the trial court could prescribe the manner of disposal of forfeited items. The High Court held that the miscitation was harmless because the correct essential elements had been canvassed, but that the trial court had no power to prescribe the mode of disposal. The sentence was amended by deleting the direction that the exhibit be disposed of by public auction.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the cited section created an offence or was merely a penalty provision.
    Whether the miscitation prejudiced the accused.
    Whether a convicting court may prescribe how forfeited hazardous substances are to be disposed of.
    Whether the proceedings and sentence should be certified or amended on review.
legal_principles: A mere miscitation of a statutory section is not necessarily fatal where the correct essential elements are canvassed and the accused is not prejudiced. A convicting court may order forfeiture or return of seized hazardous substances, but absent statutory authority it should not prescribe the mode of disposal. The Agency has statutory responsibility for maintaining records and eventual disposal of seized items.
held_decision_points:
Held, that section 10(3) did not create the offence and was only a penal provision, but the miscitation did not prejudice the accused because the correct elements were canvassed.
    Held, further, that the trial court erred by prescribing disposal of the forfeited petrol by public auction.
    Held, further, that the Court's role ended after ordering forfeiture and there was no statutory basis for prescribing the mode of disposal.
    Held, further, that the sentence had to be amended by deleting the direction concerning public auction.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that while the conviction survived because the miscitation caused no prejudice, the magistrate exceeded her powers by directing how the forfeited hazardous substances should be disposed of.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: S v Ncube (14 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 14 (13 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: MTHOKOZISI NCUBE Versus THE STATE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Ncube (14 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 14 (13 January 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
    Bail pending appeal
    Rape
    Identification evidence
    Flight risk
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – bail pending appeal – rape conviction and ten-year sentence – mistaken identity argued from alleged tattoo and dreadlocks – complainant had spent time with applicant and pointed him out at his workplace – weak prospects of success – risk of abscondment – bail refused.
headnote: The applicant applied for bail pending appeal after being convicted of rape and sentenced to ten years' imprisonment. He argued that the appeal had prospects because the complainant had described the rapist as having a tattoo and dreadlocks, while he had neither. The Court held that, for bail purposes, the risk of mistaken identification was reduced by evidence that the complainant and her mother had spent time with the applicant and his friend when collecting firewood, that the complainant led police to the applicant's workplace, identified him, and that he fled before anything was said to him. The Court held that the alleged tattoo and dreadlocks had been taken out of context, that there were no prospects of success and that the applicant was a flight risk. Bail pending appeal was refused.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the appeal had prospects of success on the issue of identification.
    Whether the alleged tattoo and dreadlocks rendered the conviction vulnerable on appeal.
    Whether the applicant was likely to abscond if released pending appeal.
    Whether the interests of justice favoured bail pending appeal.
legal_principles: In bail pending appeal, the Court considers prospects of success, likelihood of abscondment, gravity of the offence, sentence imposed, likely delay and liberty. The application is not a dress rehearsal for the appeal, but the Court may assess prospects at the bail stage. Prospects of success and abscondment are interconnected: weaker prospects increase the incentive to abscond.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant had no prospects of success on appeal against conviction for bail purposes.
    Held, further, that the complainant's identification was supported by her prior encounter with the applicant, her pointing him out to police, and his immediate flight.
    Held, further, that the tattoo and dreadlocks issue was taken out of context and did not undermine the conviction for bail purposes.
    Held, further, that the applicant was a flight risk because of the ten-year sentence and weak prospects.
    Held, further, that bail pending appeal had to be refused.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the identification evidence was sufficiently strong for bail purposes and the applicant's poor prospects of appeal, together with his custodial sentence, created a high risk of abscondment.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
