document_name: S v Nhliziyo (15 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 15 (13 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: ARTHUR NHLIZIYO Versus THE STATE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
case_number:
HCB 359/21
   XREF CRB BYO P 2112/21
   XREF CR ZRP JOTSHOLO 06-07/10/21
citation: S v Nhliziyo (15 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 15 (13 January 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
    Bail pending trial
    Armed robbery
    Flight risk
    Strength of State case
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – bail pending trial – armed robbery – hijacking of trucks at gunpoint – trailer and stolen property allegedly found at applicant's homestead – fingerprints and witness allegations – prima facie strong State case – risk of abscondment – bail refused.
headnote: The applicant sought bail pending trial on an armed robbery charge. He denied involvement and said he was at his homestead in Filabusi at the relevant time, and that he was merely a mechanic who attended to trucks brought to his homestead. The State alleged that two trucks had been hijacked at gunpoint near Fatima Mission, one trailer and stolen property including complainants' belongings were recovered at the applicant's homestead, witnesses had been hired by him to offload the hijacked trucks, and his fingerprints had been uplifted at the scene. The Court held that the State case was prima facie strong, the offence serious, and a lengthy sentence likely on conviction. The risk of abscondment was real and bail was refused.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant was a suitable candidate for bail pending trial.
    Whether the State case was prima facie strong.
    Whether the seriousness of armed robbery and likely sentence created a risk of abscondment.
    Whether the interests of justice would be jeopardised by release.
legal_principles: In assessing bail pending trial, the Court weighs the presumption of innocence and liberty against the interests of justice. In judging risk of abscondment, the Court considers the nature of the charge, probable penalty and strength of the State case. A strong prima facie case on a serious charge carrying a lengthy sentence may create a real risk of abscondment.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the State case was prima facie strong.
    Held, further, that armed robbery is a serious offence carrying a lengthy custodial sentence on conviction.
    Held, further, that the strength of the State case and seriousness of the charge created a natural risk of abscondment.
    Held, further, that it was not in the interests of justice to release the applicant on bail.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the prima facie strength of the State case and the seriousness and likely penalty of armed robbery created a sufficient risk of abscondment to justify refusal of bail.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

    Aitken v The State

document_name: S v West (152 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 152 (20 June 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE Versus LEON WEST
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
judges: KABASA J with Assessors Mr P. Damba and Mr E. Mashingaidze
subject_area:
Criminal law
    Murder
    Culpable homicide
    Self-defence
    Youthful offenders
    Sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – limited plea to culpable homicide accepted – youthful accused striking deceased on head with 1005g stone after earlier fight – self-defence only partial because means used unreasonable – conviction for culpable homicide – sentence of three years with two years suspended.
headnote: The accused, aged 17 at the time, was charged with murder after striking the deceased on the head with a stone during a dispute related to the accused having impregnated the deceased's sister. The State accepted a limited plea to culpable homicide. The Court held that although self-defence can be a complete defence where its requirements are met, it operated only partially in this case because the deceased was unarmed and the accused used a 1005g stone to strike the head with enough force to fracture the skull. The accused was convicted of culpable homicide and sentenced to three years' imprisonment, two years suspended, for an effective one year.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the deceased's death was caused by the accused.
    Whether self-defence applied as a complete or partial defence.
    Whether the State properly accepted the limited plea to culpable homicide.
    What sentence was appropriate for a youthful offender.
legal_principles: Under section 253 of the Criminal Code, self-defence may be a complete defence where all requirements are met. Where all requirements are met except that the means used to avert the attack were unreasonable, the defence operates as a partial defence reducing murder to culpable homicide. Sentencing youthful first offenders requires mercy, but violence causing loss of life normally calls for imprisonment.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the accused caused the deceased's death by striking him on the head with a stone.
    Held, further, that the deceased was unarmed and the means used were unreasonable in all the circumstances.
    Held, further, that self-defence was only a partial defence and the proper conviction was culpable homicide.
    Held, further, that imprisonment was required despite the accused's youth.
    Held, further, that an effective sentence of one year was appropriate.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the accused acted in response to confrontation but used unreasonable means by striking an unarmed person on the head with a heavy stone, reducing murder to culpable homicide rather than providing a complete defence.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: S v Moyo (153 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 153 (9 June 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE` versus CENT MOYO
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Moyo (153 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 153 (9 June 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal law
    Murder
    Culpable homicide
    Self-defence
    Firearms
    Proof beyond reasonable doubt
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – ranger shooting sand-poacher during pursuit – accused alleging warning shot and shot aimed at truck wheel – State failing to prove murder or culpable homicide beyond reasonable doubt – negligence not established on facts – accused acquitted.
headnote: The accused, a ranger employed by the Bulawayo City Council, was charged with murder after a sand poacher was shot while fleeing in a truck. The State alleged that the accused shot directly into the loading box. The accused said he first fired a warning shot and then aimed at the truck's wheel when the truck was being driven towards the ranger vehicle. The Court found shortcomings in the State evidence and accepted the defence evidence in material respects. It held that the State had not proved murder and that convicting on culpable homicide would stretch negligence too widely because the accused could not have foreseen that a bullet aimed at a wheel would miss, hit the body of the truck, ricochet and fatally injure a person in the loading box. The accused was acquitted.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the accused unlawfully and intentionally killed the deceased.
    Whether the accused acted in self-defence or in lawful performance of ranger duties.
    Whether the State proved culpable homicide if murder was not proved.
    Whether the accused should be acquitted because the State failed to prove negligence beyond reasonable doubt.
legal_principles: In a criminal trial the State bears the burden of proving guilt beyond reasonable doubt. No onus rests on the accused to prove innocence. The Court may accept portions of a witness's evidence and reject others depending on credibility and probabilities. Culpable homicide requires proof that the accused negligently caused death; negligence must be assessed on the particular facts and reasonable foreseeability of death must be established.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the State did not prove beyond reasonable doubt that the accused aimed at or intentionally shot the deceased.
    Held, further, that the accused's version that he fired a warning shot and then aimed at the truck wheel was reasonably possibly true.
    Held, further, that the State did not prove culpable homicide because it was not reasonably foreseeable that the shot aimed at the wheel would miss, strike the truck and ricochet into the deceased.
    Held, further, that the accused was entitled to an acquittal.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the State failed to establish intention or negligence beyond reasonable doubt, since the accepted or reasonably possible version did not show foresight of death or negligent causing of death in the particular circumstances.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to: R v Difford 1937 AD 370

document_name: S v Mazendame (156 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 156 (17 May 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE versus MASIMBA MAZENDAME
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Mazendame (156 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 156 (17 May 2022)
judges:
MOYO J
Assessors: Mr A.B Mpofu and Mr W.T Matemba
subject_area: Criminal law; Murder; Defence of person and property; Security guard; Acquittal
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – security guard firing rifle at intruders – defence of person and property – ss 252, 253, 257, 258 and 259 of Criminal Law Code – warning shot – no proof of intent or negligence – acquittal
headnote: The accused, a security guard, was charged with murder after firing a rifle shot which killed the deceased during an alleged intrusion at premises he guarded. The State witnesses did not see the shooting and testified after the fact. The accused’s version was that several intruders scaled the durawall at about 3 a.m., stones were thrown, the dogs reacted, and he fired what he intended as a warning shot to scare and disperse them. The court considered the statutory defences of defence of person and defence of property under the Criminal Law Code. It held that the State had not proved that the accused deliberately fired at the intruders or that his conduct was wrongful or negligent in the circumstances. The accused was acquitted.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the State proved that the accused intentionally or negligently killed the deceased.
Whether the accused was entitled to rely on defence of person or defence of property.
Whether the means used were reasonable in the circumstances.
Whether s 259, concerning excessive defence of property, applied.
legal_principles: An accused may rely on defence of person or defence of property where an unlawful attack on life, bodily integrity or property has commenced or is imminent and the response is necessary and reasonable. The court must assess the circumstances confronting the accused, not with hindsight. Section 259 applies where defence of property is otherwise made out except that the means used were unreasonable. The State bears the burden of proving unlawfulness, intention or negligence beyond reasonable doubt.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the State had not disproved the accused’s version that there were intruders and an imminent unlawful attack.
Held, further, that the accused could not reasonably be expected simply to flee and abandon the premises and other occupants.
Held, further, that the evidence did not establish that the accused deliberately fired at the deceased or that the shot was not a warning shot.
Held, further, that neither intent nor negligence had been proved beyond reasonable doubt.
ratio_decidendi: The decisive reasoning was that the accused’s version of an imminent unlawful intrusion and warning shot was not rebutted by the State, whose witnesses did not observe the shooting. In the absence of proof that his response was unlawful, intentional or negligent, the State failed to prove murder or any lesser offence.

document_name: S v Walters (157 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 157 (14 June 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE Versus CRAIG WALTERS
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Walters (157 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 157 (14 June 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal law
    Murder
    Culpable homicide
    Provocation
    Infidelity
    Evidence
    Sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – accused assaulting wife after finding her in compromising position and being provoked – State failing to disprove accused's version on how head injuries were sustained – provocation as partial defence – murder not proved beyond reasonable doubt – culpable homicide – effective sentence of 3½ years' imprisonment.
headnote: The accused was charged with murdering his wife after assaulting her following allegations and evidence of infidelity. He tendered a limited plea to culpable homicide. The State accepted that he had been provoked, but argued for murder. The Court held that the State had not rebutted the accused's version that the deceased may have sustained head injuries when she fell, and it could not infer facts not proved. The accused's conduct fell within provocation as a partial defence under section 239 of the Criminal Code. He was acquitted of murder and convicted of culpable homicide. He was sentenced to five and a half years' imprisonment, two years suspended, leaving an effective three and a half years.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the State proved murder beyond reasonable doubt.
    Whether the accused's version on provocation and the manner of injury was reasonably possibly true.
    Whether provocation reduced liability to culpable homicide.
    What sentence was appropriate.
legal_principles: The State bears the burden to prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt and an accused's version cannot be rejected merely because he is an accused. Inferences must be drawn from proved facts only. Provocation may operate as a partial defence to murder under section 239 of the Criminal Code where it negates the intention or realisation required for murder, reducing the offence to culpable homicide.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the State had not proved beyond reasonable doubt that the deceased's head injuries were inflicted by the accused rather than sustained in a fall.
    Held, further, that the accused was provoked by the deceased's conduct and his actions fell within section 239(1)(a) of the Code.
    Held, further, that murder was not proved.
    Held, further, that the accused was guilty of culpable homicide.
    Held, further, that the appropriate effective sentence was 3½ years' imprisonment.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that because the State did not disprove the accused's account or prove the mechanism of the fatal head injuries, and because provocation negated the intent required for murder, the proper conviction was culpable homicide.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to: None identified
legislation_considered:
Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], section 47, section 239(1)(a)
    Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], section 314
cases_referred_to_terms: None identified

Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], section 314

document_name: S v Sibanda (159 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 159 (23 June 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE Versus JAGGER ALBERT SIBANDA
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Sibanda (159 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 159 (23 June 2022)
judges:
KABASA & DUBE-BANDA JJ
   Judgment by: DUBE-BANDA J
   KABASA J agreed
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
    Criminal review
    Stock theft
    Mandatory minimum sentence
    Sentence per count not per bovine
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – automatic review – stock theft involving two heifers charged as one count – magistrate imposing nine years per beast – sentence of eighteen years – mandatory minimum sentence applying per count rather than per bovine – conviction confirmed – sentence substituted with nine years' imprisonment.
headnote: The accused was convicted on his plea of guilty to one count of stock theft involving two heifers. The magistrate found no special circumstances and imposed eighteen years' imprisonment, reasoning that the mandatory minimum of nine years applied per beast. On automatic review, the High Court held that the penalty is applied per count and not per bovine where the accused is charged and convicted on one count. The Court confirmed the conviction, quashed the eighteen-year sentence and substituted nine years' imprisonment.
issues_for_determination:
Whether a sentence for stock theft involving more than one bovine is to be imposed per count or per beast.
    Whether the sentence of eighteen years' imprisonment on one count was competent.
    What sentence should be substituted on review.
legal_principles: Section 114(2)(e) of the Criminal Law Code prescribes a minimum sentence for stock theft involving a bovine where there are no special circumstances. The mandatory minimum is applied per count and not mechanically per beast where the charge is framed as one count. Where a sentence is irregular and incompetent, the High Court may set it aside on review and substitute a lawful sentence.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the conviction was proper.
    Held, further, that the sentence of eighteen years' imprisonment was irregular because the accused had been convicted on one count of stock theft.
    Held, further, that the mandatory minimum sentence is per count and not per bovine.
    Held, further, that the sentence had to be quashed and replaced with nine years' imprisonment.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that because the accused was charged and convicted on a single count of stock theft involving two bovines, the mandatory minimum sentence applied per count, making the eighteen-year sentence imposed per beast incompetent.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: Gazi v Mbalabala Properties (16 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 16 (20 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: JIMMY GAZI v MBALABALA PROPERTIES
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: Gazi v Mbalabala Properties (16 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 16 (20 January 2022)
subject_area:
Contract
Sale of land
Immovable property
Regional planning
Companies
Prescription
catchwords_flynote: Contract – oral agreement for sale and transfer of subdivided immovable property – corporate defendant acting through director – prescription and cause of action – discharge of purchase-price obligations – section 39(1)(b)(i) of Regional Town and Country Planning Act [Chapter 29:12] – agreement concluded before subdivision permit – illegality and unenforceability – claim for transfer dismissed, each party to bear own costs.
headnote: The plaintiff sued for an order compelling the defendant to transfer Lot 3 of Lot 1 of Swaite to him. He alleged that the defendant, through its director, had entered into an oral memorandum of understanding under which the plaintiff would provide money, livestock, a motor vehicle and other benefits and the defendant would subdivide and transfer the land. The defendant denied authority, raised prescription, illegality under the Regional Town and Country Planning Act, exchange-control issues and the alleged inability of a company to contract orally. The Court found that there was an agreement, that the plaintiff had discharged his obligations, that prescription did not defeat the claim, that a company could enter into a verbal agreement, and that foreign-currency issues did not render the agreement unenforceable. The plaintiff failed, however, because the agreement was concluded before the subdivision permit was granted, contrary to section 39(1)(b)(i) of the Regional Town and Country Planning Act. The claim was dismissed, with each party bearing its own costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the parties entered into an agreement of sale of Lot 3 of Lot 1 of Swaite.
Whether the claim had prescribed.
What the agreement’s terms were and whether the plaintiff performed.
Whether the agreement was illegal under section 39(1)(b)(i) of the Regional Town and Country Planning Act.
Whether foreign-currency payments rendered the agreement void or unenforceable.
Whether a corporate entity could enter into a verbal agreement.
Whether the plaintiff was entitled to transfer.
legal_principles: A cause of action is the set of facts necessary to support the plaintiff’s right to judgment, and prescription turns on when the final fact giving rise to the right to sue occurred. Civil proof is on a balance of probabilities. A company may enter into a verbal contract unless the law or the parties require writing. The Turquand rule permits an outsider to presume regularity in internal corporate authority. Section 39(1)(b)(i) of the Regional Town and Country Planning Act prohibits agreements for change of ownership of a portion of property except in accordance with a subdivision permit; an agreement concluded before the permit is illegal and unenforceable.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the plaintiff and the defendant, acting through Nkala, entered into an agreement for the sale of Lot 3 of Lot 1 of Swaite.
Held, further, that the plaintiff’s claim was not prescribed because the final act giving rise to the right to sue occurred after efforts to secure performance following Nkala’s death failed.
Held, further, that the plaintiff discharged his obligations under the agreement and the foreign-currency component did not render the agreement unenforceable on the evidence.
Held, further, that a corporate entity can enter into a verbal agreement and the absence of a written agreement did not make the contract invalid.
Held, further, that the agreement was concluded before the subdivision permit was granted and therefore fell foul of section 39(1)(b)(i) of the Regional Town and Country Planning Act.
Held, further, that the plaintiff was not entitled to transfer because the agreement was illegal and unenforceable.
ratio_decidendi: Although the Court accepted the existence of the sale agreement and the plaintiff’s performance, the claim failed because the agreement for transfer of a subdivided portion of land was concluded before the subdivision permit was granted, making it illegal and unenforceable under section 39(1)(b)(i) of the Regional Town and Country Planning Act.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that the case illustrated the unfortunate consequences of innocently entering into agreements without full knowledge of statutory requirements and that, but for the failure to obtain a subdivision permit before contracting, the plaintiff would have succeeded.

document_name: Muchena v Police Service Commission and Another (160 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 160 (23 June 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: LIBERTY MUCHENA v POLICE SERVICE COMMISSION and THE COMMISSIONER GENERAL OF POLICE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: Muchena v Police Service Commission and Another (160 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 160 (23 June 2022)
subject_area:
Civil procedure
    Service of process
    Review
    Police disciplinary proceedings
    Points in limine
catchwords_flynote: Civil procedure – service of court application – whether service at Attorney-General's office constituted good service on Commissioner General of Police – computation of time for notice of opposition – whether respondent barred – effect on first respondent's associated opposition – point in limine dismissed.
headnote: The matter returned for a hearing de novo after an appeal from an earlier judgment. The applicant raised a point in limine that the 2nd respondent had filed its notice of opposition out of time and was barred, and that the 1st respondent's opposition, being tied to the 2nd respondent's, also fell away. The Court held that service of the application at the Attorney-General's office was not good service on the 2nd respondent because that office had no mandate at the time to represent the 2nd respondent. The 2nd respondent only became aware of the application later and its notice of opposition was filed within time when reckoned from that date. The point in limine was dismissed with no order as to costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether service of the application at the Attorney-General's office was good service on the 2nd respondent.
    Whether the 2nd respondent's notice of opposition was filed out of time.
    Whether the 2nd respondent was barred.
    Whether the 1st respondent's opposition also failed if the 2nd respondent was barred.
    Costs on the point in limine.
legal_principles: Service on a legal practitioner or public office is not good service merely because that office previously represented or may later represent a party; proper service must be effected on the party in accordance with the rules unless the legal practitioner has instructions and provides an address for service. Where process is served at an office without mandate to act, service may be only for information. Time limits for opposition must be reckoned from proper service or from the date the party became aware where proper service is lacking.
held_decision_points:
Held, that service at the Attorney-General's office was not good service on the 2nd respondent because that office had no mandate at the time to represent him.
    Held, further, that the 2nd respondent became aware of the application on 12 June 2017 and the notice of opposition filed on 23 June 2017 was not out of time.
    Held, further, that the 2nd respondent was not barred, and the related argument against the 1st respondent's opposition also failed.
    Held, further, that no condonation was necessary because there was nothing to condone.
    Held, further, that the point in limine should be dismissed with no order as to costs.
ratio_decidendi: The point in limine failed because the alleged delay was calculated from invalid service at the Attorney-General's office. Once time was reckoned from when the 2nd respondent actually became aware of the application, the notice of opposition was within time.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to:
PG Industries (Zimbabwe) Limited v Mark Bvekerwa & 34 Others SC 79-14
    Hungwe and Commissioner-General Chihuri v Mawereza HB 49-10

document_name: S v Ncube and Another (164 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 164 (14 June 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE Versus SAVIOURES NCUBE And SIPHETHANGANI MOYO
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Ncube and Another (164 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 164 (14 June 2022)
judges: MAKONESE J with Assessors Mr J. Ndubiwa and Mr J.L.M Zulu
subject_area:
Criminal law
    Murder
    Culpable homicide
    Child abuse
    Sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – death of one-and-a-half-year-old child after stepfather assaulted him with stick for refusing food – body concealed and buried in ant-hill – limited plea to culpable homicide accepted – children's rights and protection from abuse – custodial sentence.
headnote: The accused was charged with murder after assaulting a one-and-a-half-year-old child with a stick when the child refused to eat porridge. The child died the following morning. The accused and the child's mother concealed the death, kept the body in a bedroom hut and later buried it in an ant-hill before fleeing. The accused admitted causing the death and tendered a plea to culpable homicide, which the State accepted. The Court held that the accused wrongfully caused the death of the child but that there was no evidence proving the mens rea for murder. He was convicted of culpable homicide. In sentence the Court emphasised children's constitutional right to protection from maltreatment and imposed six years' imprisonment with two years suspended, for an effective four years.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the accused caused the deceased child's death.
    Whether the proved facts established murder or culpable homicide.
    What sentence was appropriate given the child's age, abuse, concealment of the death and the accused's mitigation.
legal_principles: Children are protected under section 81 of the Constitution and are entitled to protection from abuse and maltreatment. A parent or caregiver has no right to abuse a child. Where death results from unlawful assault but intention to kill is not proved, culpable homicide is the appropriate verdict. Sentencing must reflect seriousness, vulnerability of the child, concealment, guilty plea, age of the offender and time spent in remand custody.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the accused assaulted the deceased child and wrongfully caused his death.
    Held, further, that the State did not prove the mens rea required for murder.
    Held, further, that the accused was guilty of culpable homicide.
    Held, further, that the abuse and concealment of the child's death were aggravating, but the guilty plea, youthfulness and remand custody were mitigating.
    Held, further, that a partly suspended custodial sentence was appropriate.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the fatal assault of a vulnerable child established culpable homicide because death was unlawfully caused but the intention required for murder was not proved.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to: None identified

constitutional_provisions_considered: Constitution of Zimbabwe Amendment No. 20, 2013, section 81

document_name: S v Mhlanga (165 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 165 (8 June 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE versus KWANELE PROMISE MHLANGA
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Mhlanga (165 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 165 (8 June 2022)
judges:
MAKONESE J
Assessors: Mr J. Ndubiwa and Mr J.L.M Zulu
subject_area: Criminal law; Culpable homicide; Knife violence; Sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – limited plea to culpable homicide – fight after beer drink – stabbing with Okapi knife during assault – no mens rea for murder proved – youthful offender – deterrence – effective three-year sentence
headnote: The accused was charged with murder after stabbing the deceased during a fight. He denied murder but tendered a limited plea of guilty to culpable homicide, which the State accepted. The agreed facts showed that the accused and deceased exchanged insults, engaged in a fist fight, and the deceased strangled the accused while another person pelted the accused with stones. The accused produced an Okapi knife and stabbed the deceased on the thigh and arm. The deceased died from hypovolemic shock due to laceration of the femoral artery. The court found that the State had not proved the mens rea for murder but that the accused negligently caused death. It convicted him of culpable homicide and imposed an effective three-year sentence.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the facts proved murder or culpable homicide.
Whether the State proved dolus directus or dolus eventualis.
What sentence was appropriate for a youthful first offender who used a knife in a fatal fight.
legal_principles: Where the State does not prove the intention required for murder, but the accused unlawfully and negligently caused death, culpable homicide is the proper verdict. Knife-related violence is a serious sentencing concern. Sentencing must balance youth, remorse, first-offender status and pre-trial custody against the sanctity of human life and deterrence.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the accused caused the deceased’s death by stabbing him.
Held, further, that no evidence proved the requisite mens rea for murder.
Held, further, that the accused was guilty of culpable homicide.
Held, further, that the offence warranted imprisonment, but mitigation justified a partly suspended sentence.
ratio_decidendi: The decisive reasoning was that the accepted facts proved a fatal negligent stabbing during a fight, but not intention to kill. The proper conviction was therefore culpable homicide, with a custodial sentence reflecting the seriousness of knife violence and the accused’s mitigation.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: S v Moyo (166 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 166 (6 June 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE versus MHLAWUMBE MOYO
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Moyo (166 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 166 (6 June 2022)
judges: MAKONESE J with Assessors Mr J. Ndubiwa and Mr J.L.M. Zulu
subject_area:
Criminal law
    Culpable homicide
    Murder
    Intoxication
    Sentence
    Delay
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – limited plea to culpable homicide accepted – fight at night club after drinking – deceased aggressor but accused using excessive force with stone – lengthy delay partly caused by accused's default – effective sentence of five years' imprisonment.
headnote: The accused was charged with murder for killing the deceased during a fight after drinking at Lambada Nite Club. He tendered a limited plea to culpable homicide, which the State accepted. The agreed facts showed that after insults, laughter and a fight, the deceased chased the accused and armed himself with stones, but the accused later struck the deceased several times on the head with a stone. The Court accepted the limited plea, finding no intention to kill. In sentence, it noted the seriousness of fatal violence at drinking places, the accused's age and family circumstances, his plea and remorse, but also that he had defaulted and caused delay. He was sentenced to eight years' imprisonment, three years suspended, leaving an effective five-year term.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the facts supported murder or culpable homicide.
    Whether the accused had intention to kill or negligently caused death.
    What sentence was appropriate considering the provocation, excessive force, delay and seriousness of fatal violence.
legal_principles: Where the evidence does not establish intention to kill, a murder charge may result in conviction for culpable homicide where death was negligently caused. In sentencing for culpable homicide arising from violence, the Court considers the sanctity of life, the degree of force, provocation, intoxication, personal circumstances, plea of guilty and any delay attributable to the accused.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the accepted facts established that the accused caused the death of the deceased.
    Held, further, that the accused and deceased had been fighting and the deceased had been the aggressor at stages, so intention to kill was not proved.
    Held, further, that the accused used excessive force by striking the deceased several times on the head with a stone.
    Held, further, that an effective sentence of five years' imprisonment was appropriate.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the accused negligently caused the deceased's death in a fight without the intention required for murder, but his excessive force and the fatal head injuries justified a substantial custodial sentence.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: S v Ncube and Another (167 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 167 (7 June 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE Versus MDUDUZI NCUBE And NOTHANI NCUBE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Ncube and Another (167 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 167 (7 June 2022)
judges: MAKONESE J with Assessors Mr J. Ndubiwa & Mr J. l. M. Zulu
subject_area:
Criminal law
    Murder
    Culpable homicide
    Sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – accused persons and others assaulting deceased after allegedly finding him committing bestiality with a dog – limited plea to culpable homicide accepted – vigilante justice – absence of proved mens rea for murder – guilty of culpable homicide – sentence.
headnote: The accused persons were charged with murder after they and others assaulted the deceased with switches, a whip, sticks and an axe. The background was that the deceased was allegedly discovered having sexual intercourse with a dog belonging to the first accused. The accused persons admitted causing the deceased's death but tendered limited pleas to culpable homicide, which the State accepted. The Court accepted that the deceased was assaulted and died from the injuries inflicted, but held that the State had not established the requisite mens rea for murder. The accused persons were convicted of culpable homicide. In sentence, the Court condemned vigilante justice and emphasised that the deceased should have been taken to the police. Each accused was sentenced to five years' imprisonment, two years suspended, for an effective three years.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the accused persons caused the deceased's death.
    Whether the proved facts established murder or only culpable homicide.
    What sentence was appropriate for vigilante violence committed after alleged bestiality.
legal_principles: A person who commits a sexual act with an animal commits the offence of bestiality. However, members of the public may not administer vigilante justice; suspected criminal conduct must be reported to the police. Where the State does not prove intention to kill or realisation of the risk of death, but proves negligent unlawful killing, culpable homicide is the proper verdict.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the accused persons and their associates assaulted the deceased and inflicted injuries that caused his death.
    Held, further, that the State had not established the mens rea required for murder.
    Held, further, that the accused persons were guilty of culpable homicide.
    Held, further, that the accused persons' conduct amounted to vigilante justice and warranted a custodial sentence despite their guilty pleas and personal mitigation.
ratio_decidendi: The decisive reason was that the accused persons negligently caused death through a retaliatory and unlawful group assault, but the State did not prove the intention required for murder.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: S v Hadebe (168 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 168 (13 June 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE versus MTHOKOZISI HADEBE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Hadebe (168 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 168 (13 June 2022)
judges:
MAKONESE J
Assessors: Mr J Ndubiwa & Mr J. L. M. Zulu
subject_area: Criminal law; Culpable homicide; Child protection; Sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – limited plea to culpable homicide – fatal assault of child by parent – intoxication – lack of mens rea for murder – constitutional protection of children – deterrent sentence
headnote: The accused was charged with murder after the death of his eight-year-old daughter. He pleaded not guilty to murder and guilty to culpable homicide. The agreed facts showed that, while drunk and after a misunderstanding with his wife, he assaulted his children and threw the deceased head-long onto a mud floor, causing fatal cervical-spine injuries. The court accepted that the accused caused the death but that the State had not proved the mens rea required for murder. It convicted him of culpable homicide. In sentence, the court emphasised the constitutional protection of children against maltreatment and abuse and the brutality of the assault. An effective sentence of six years’ imprisonment was imposed.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the facts supported murder or culpable homicide.
Whether intoxication and the circumstances of chastisement negatived the mens rea for murder.
What sentence was appropriate for a parent’s fatal assault on a child.
legal_principles: A conviction for murder requires proof of the necessary intention. Where the evidence establishes unlawful negligent killing but not the requisite mens rea for murder, culpable homicide is the proper verdict. Children are constitutionally protected from maltreatment, neglect and abuse, and the High Court as upper guardian must protect children. Sentencing must balance mitigation with the need for deterrence and the seriousness of loss of life.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the accused caused the injuries which led to the death of the deceased.
Held, further, that the accused lacked the requisite mens rea for murder.
Held, further, that the appropriate verdict was culpable homicide.
Held, further, that a deterrent custodial sentence was required because the deceased was a defenceless child subjected to a brutal assault by her parent.
ratio_decidendi: The decisive reasoning was that the accused’s conduct caused the child’s death, but the accepted facts did not prove the intention required for murder. The unlawful and negligent killing therefore amounted to culpable homicide, with the gravity of child abuse requiring an effective custodial sentence.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: S v Ndlovu (169 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 169 (7 June 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE Versus LAMECK NDLOVU
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Ndlovu (169 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 169 (7 June 2022)
judges: MAKONESE J with Assessors Mr J. Ndubiwa & Mr J.L.M. Zulu
subject_area:
Criminal law
    Murder
    Culpable homicide
    Sentence
    Delayed trial
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – limited plea to culpable homicide – single blow with log during dispute over debt – death caused after complications from brain damage – no mens rea for murder – culpable homicide – sentence.
headnote: The accused was charged with murder for striking the deceased once on the head with a log after a misunderstanding over an outstanding debt while they were walking home. The deceased was hospitalised and died months later from complications of brain damage. The accused tendered a limited plea to culpable homicide, which the State accepted. The Court held that the single blow caused the death but that the evidence did not establish the mens rea required for murder. The accused was convicted of culpable homicide and sentenced to ten years' imprisonment, two years suspended, for an effective eight years.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the accused caused the deceased's death.
    Whether the facts established murder or culpable homicide.
    What sentence was appropriate given the single blow, seriousness of the offence and mitigating circumstances.
legal_principles: Where an accused unlawfully causes death but the State does not prove intention to kill or constructive intent, culpable homicide is the proper verdict. Sentencing for culpable homicide arising from violence must reflect the seriousness of the loss of life, deterrence, mitigation, guilty plea and personal circumstances.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the single blow to the deceased's head caused the brain damage that led to death.
    Held, further, that there was insufficient evidence to establish mens rea for murder.
    Held, further, that the accused negligently caused the deceased's death and was guilty of culpable homicide.
    Held, further, that an effective sentence of eight years' imprisonment was appropriate.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that death resulted from the accused's unlawful negligent blow to the head, but the circumstances did not prove the intention required for murder.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: SIBAMB~1.DOC
case_name: SIBAMBENE TRADERS ASSOCIATION Versus FINDLEY INVESTMENTS (PVT) LTD And MPUMELELO INVESTMENTS (PVT) LTD And THE SHERIFF OF ZIMBABWE And NHLANHLA DUBE And OLIVER MAPENZAUSWA And DROSILLA SIMELA And MORIA SIBANDA And THEMBI KHUMALO And HAPPINESS SIBANDA
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
subject_area:
Civil procedure
    Urgent chamber applications
    Stay of execution
    Interdicts
    Rescission of judgment
    Execution by Sheriff
    Costs
catchwords_flynote: Civil procedure – urgent chamber application – stay of execution pending rescission application – Sheriff served with urgent application but eviction executed before hearing – return of service prima facie evidence of ejectment – matter rendered moot after eviction and handover of keys – balance of convenience not favouring restoration of occupation – application dismissed – punitive costs against first respondent for conduct pre-empting court process.
headnote: The applicant sought an urgent stay of execution of an amended court order in HC 774/17 pending its application for rescission. The applicant had not been a party to the original matter and complained that the amended order had made it a trade name of the second respondent. Although the urgent stay application had been served, the Sheriff proceeded with the eviction and recorded that ejectment had been completed and the keys handed over to the first respondent's representative. The Court held that the Sheriff's return of service was prima facie evidence of execution and that the dispute had become moot because the eviction had already occurred. The balance of convenience did not favour restoration of occupation. The application was dismissed, but the first respondent was ordered to pay costs on the attorney-client scale because its conduct had pre-empted the court process.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the urgent stay application had been overtaken by events.
    Whether the Sheriff's return of service should be accepted as prima facie evidence of ejectment.
    Whether a court should still grant a stay or restore occupation after eviction had been completed.
    Whether the balance of convenience favoured the applicant.
    What costs order was appropriate in view of the conduct of the first and third respondents.
legal_principles: A return of service by the Sheriff or Deputy Sheriff is prima facie evidence of service or execution. Once an urgent application for stay of execution has been served and set down, execution should ordinarily be suspended until the court hears the matter. A stay of execution is in substance an interdict and the requirements for interim and final interdictory relief, including balance of convenience, are relevant. Courts decide live controversies and will not determine moot matters where changed circumstances have made the dispute academic.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the Court could not ignore the Sheriff's return of service, which recorded that ejectment had been completed and keys handed over.
    Held, further, that the matter no longer presented a live controversy because execution had already occurred.
    Held, further, that the balance of convenience did not favour restoring occupation to the applicant's members.
    Held, further, that the conduct of the first and third respondents in proceeding with execution after service of the urgent application was to be frowned upon.
    Held, further, that the application had to be dismissed but the first respondent had to pay costs on the attorney-client scale.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the stay application was rendered moot by completed execution and the applicant had not shown that the balance of convenience favoured restoration of occupation, although the first respondent's conduct justified punitive costs.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court commented that once an urgent stay application is served and awaiting set down, the Sheriff should not proceed with execution because doing so renders the hearing academic.

    Ndlovu v The Officer Commanding Zimbabwe Republic Police – Bulawayo Province and Others (HC 618/10) (2010) ZWBHC 100 (8 September 2010)

    Livetouch Investments v Philcool Investments and Sheriff of The High Court of Zimbabwe HB 173-20

document_name: S v Dube (170 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 170 (16 June 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE v SUCCESS DUBE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
judges: MAKONESE J with Assessors Mr J. Ndubiwa and Mr J.L.M. Zulu
subject_area:
Criminal law
    Murder
    Common purpose
    Constructive intent
    Sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – accused and associates removing deceased to dam and assaulting him with axe and bricks – accused pleading limited culpable homicide – State rejecting plea – common purpose – whether accused realised real risk of death – conviction for murder under s 47(1)(b) – sentence of 15 years with 2 years suspended.
headnote: The accused was charged with murder after the deceased was taken to a dam and assaulted with an axe and bricks. He tendered a limited plea to culpable homicide, which the State rejected. The evidence showed that after an earlier misunderstanding between the deceased and another person had been resolved, the accused escalated the matter, joined others in removing the deceased while he was asleep, and struck him with bricks after he had already been struck with an axe. The Court rejected the accused's attempt to minimise his role and rejected the suggestion that he was coerced. It held that the attack was planned and executed, lethal weapons were used, excessive force was applied, and common purpose applied. The accused was convicted of murder as defined in s 47(1)(b) and sentenced to 15 years' imprisonment, with two years suspended for five years.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the State proved murder rather than culpable homicide.
    Whether the accused participated willingly in the fatal assault.
    Whether common purpose applied.
    Whether the accused foresaw death as a real risk or possibility.
    The appropriate sentence.
legal_principles: Under s 47 of the Criminal Law Code, murder is committed where a person causes death intending to kill or realising that there is a real risk or possibility that conduct may cause death and persists despite that risk. It is not necessary under the Code to specify actual or constructive intent in the common-law sense. Common purpose may render an accused liable where he is a willing participant in a joint attack and the fatal result occurs during execution of the shared criminal purpose.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the accused was not provoked by the deceased and inserted himself into a dispute that had already been resolved.
    Held, further, that the accused and others isolated the deceased, used lethal weapons and applied excessive force.
    Held, further, that the doctrine of common purpose applied, and it was immaterial which participant delivered the fatal blow.
    Held, further, that the accused must have realised the real risk that his conduct could cause death and continued regardless.
    Held, further, that the accused was guilty of murder as defined in s 47(1)(b).
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the accused knowingly and willingly joined a revenge assault with lethal weapons against a helpless victim, and by participating in that common purpose he realised the real risk of death yet persisted; liability for murder was therefore established.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to: Mapfoche v The State SC 84/21

Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], section 47(1)(a) and section 47(1)(b)
    Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], section 314

document_name: S v Tshuma (171 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 171 (8 June 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE Versus MEHLULI TSHUMA
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Tshuma (171 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 171 (8 June 2022)
judges: MAKONESE J with Assessors Mr J. Ndubiwa and Mr J.L.M. Zulu
subject_area:
Criminal law
    Murder
    Witchcraft belief
    Constructive or actual intent
    Aggravating circumstances
    Sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – accused believing elderly deceased was bewitching him – consulting witch-hunters and prophet – accused attacking deceased with log several times on head and body – belief in witchcraft not reducing liability on facts – murder committed in aggravating circumstances – 20 years' imprisonment.
headnote: The accused believed that the 73-year-old deceased was bewitching him and sucking his blood after consultations with a witch-hunter and a prophet. He armed himself with a log and assaulted the deceased repeatedly on the head and upper body while she was on her way to fetch water. The State rejected the accused's limited plea to culpable homicide. The Court held that the accused made a deliberate decision to end the life of a defenceless elderly woman, and that the attack was brutal and callous. The murder was found to have been committed in aggravating circumstances under section 47(4)(a) of the Criminal Code. The accused was sentenced to 20 years' imprisonment.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the accused caused the deceased's death.
    Whether his belief in witchcraft, rage or related circumstances reduced the offence from murder to culpable homicide.
    Whether murder was committed in aggravating circumstances.
    What sentence was appropriate.
legal_principles: Belief in witchcraft does not of itself justify unlawful violence or killing. Murder is committed where an accused intentionally kills or realises the real risk of death and proceeds. Under section 47(4)(a) of the Criminal Code, murder in aggravating circumstances attracts a minimum sentence of not less than 20 years' imprisonment unless special circumstances justify otherwise.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the accused caused the death of the deceased by repeatedly assaulting her with a log.
    Held, further, that the deceased was a defenceless 73-year-old woman and the accused showed no mercy.
    Held, further, that the accused's belief in witchcraft did not reduce the offence to culpable homicide on the facts.
    Held, further, that the murder was committed in aggravating circumstances.
    Held, further, that the appropriate sentence was 20 years' imprisonment.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the accused deliberately and repeatedly struck a vulnerable elderly woman on the head and upper body with a heavy log, making a decision to kill her, and his witchcraft belief did not negate the mens rea for murder.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court commented on the negative impact of deep-rooted belief in witchcraft and the harmful role of Tsikamutandas manipulating communities without offering real solutions.
cases_referred_to: None identified
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], section 47(1), section 47(4)(a)
constitutional_provisions_considered: Constitution of Zimbabwe Amendment No. 20, 2013: the Court referred generally to every citizen's constitutional right to hold whatever belief they desire, without citing a section number.

document_name: S v DN (A Juvenile) (172 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 172 (16 May 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE v DUMISANI NYONI
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v DN (A Juvenile) (172 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 172 (16 May 2022)
judges: MOYO J with Assessors Mr A.B Mpofu and Mr W.T Matemba
subject_area:
Criminal law
    Culpable homicide
    Juvenile offenders
    Sentence
    Child victims
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – juvenile accused charged with murder – limited plea to culpable homicide accepted – accused aged 14 when placed in charge of five-year-old niece – fatal assault with whip and switches after child soiled herself – immaturity and inappropriate caregiving responsibility – non-custodial wholly suspended sentence imposed.
headnote: The accused, a juvenile aged 14 at the time of the offence, was charged with murder after assaulting his five-year-old niece with a whip and switches, causing fatal head injuries. He tendered a limited plea to culpable homicide, which the State accepted, and the matter proceeded on agreed facts with the post-mortem report, probation report and weapons admitted. The Court found him not guilty of murder but guilty of culpable homicide. In sentence, it emphasised that the accused was a minor himself and had been burdened with caregiving responsibilities beyond his capacity. It imposed a non-custodial sentence of three years' imprisonment wholly suspended for five years on condition of good behaviour in relation to violence.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the accepted facts supported murder or culpable homicide.
    How the Court should sentence a juvenile offender who negligently caused death while placed in an inappropriate caregiving role.
    Whether a custodial sentence was appropriate.
legal_principles: Culpable homicide is appropriate where death is unlawfully caused negligently rather than with the intention required for murder. In sentencing a juvenile, the Court must consider immaturity, prospects of rehabilitation, the circumstances in which responsibility was imposed on the child offender, remorse and the seriousness of loss of life. A non-custodial sentence may be appropriate where custodial punishment would be unfair in light of the juvenile's age and circumstances.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the accepted facts did not establish murder but did establish culpable homicide.
    Held, further, that the accused was a first offender, had pleaded guilty to the appropriate charge and showed remorse.
    Held, further, that the accused's immaturity and the fact that he had been given caregiving responsibilities beyond his capacity were material mitigating factors.
    Held, further, that a custodial sentence would be unfair and a wholly suspended sentence was appropriate.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the juvenile negligently caused the death of the deceased without the requisite intent for murder, and his age and inappropriate placement in a caregiving role justified a non-custodial wholly suspended sentence.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to: None identified
legislation_considered:
Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], section 47
    Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], section 314 referred to by context of admitted evidence
cases_referred_to_terms: None identified

Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], section 314 referred to by context of admitted evidence

document_name: S v Ndlovu (173 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 173 (6 June 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE Versus NOTHANDO NDLOVU
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Ndlovu (173 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 173 (6 June 2022)
judges: MAKONESE J with Assessors Mr J. Ndubiwa & Mr J. L. M. Zulu
subject_area:
Criminal law
    Murder
    Culpable homicide
    Youthful offender
    Community service
    Sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – limited plea to culpable homicide – teenage rivalry over lover – accused stabbed deceased once after deceased introduced knife and stabbed accused – no mens rea for murder – youthful female first offender – custodial portion suspended on community service.
headnote: The accused, a 19-year-old female, was charged with murder after a fight with a 15-year-old deceased arising from rivalry over a man. The State accepted a limited plea to culpable homicide. The agreed facts showed that the deceased had introduced a kitchen knife and stabbed the accused before the accused grabbed the knife and stabbed the deceased once near the left breast. The Court held that the accused caused death but that the evidence did not establish the mens rea for murder. In sentencing, the Court emphasised youth, first-offender status, difficult upbringing and rehabilitation. The accused was sentenced to three years' imprisonment, with one year suspended and the remaining two years suspended on condition of 840 hours of community service.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the accused caused the deceased's death.
    Whether the evidence proved murder or culpable homicide.
    What sentence was appropriate for a youthful female first offender convicted of culpable homicide.
legal_principles: Where death is caused without proof of the mens rea for murder, culpable homicide is the appropriate verdict. Sentencing must balance society's interest, the victim's interest and the accused's circumstances. Youthful and female first offenders may be treated with leniency where rehabilitation is a realistic sentencing aim, although the sanctity of life remains paramount.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the accused caused the death of the deceased.
    Held, further, that there was insufficient evidence to show that the accused had the mens rea for murder.
    Held, further, that the accused was guilty of culpable homicide.
    Held, further, that the accused's youth, gender, first-offender status and background justified a rehabilitative sentence with community service rather than direct imprisonment.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the accused unlawfully and negligently caused death during a fight in which the deceased introduced the knife, but the circumstances did not prove murder and justified a community-service based sentence.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to: S v Dezzy Ncube SC-14-87
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], section 47(1)

document_name: S v Mabuzane (174 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 174 (17 May 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE versus HLUPEKO MABUZANE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Mabuzane (174 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 174 (17 May 2022)
judges:
MOYO J
Assessors: Mr A. B. Mpofu & Mr W. T. Matemba
subject_area: Criminal law; Culpable homicide; Sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – limited plea to culpable homicide – assault with stick after deceased initiated confrontation – negligence – delay in trial – sentence – part-suspended custodial sentence
headnote: The accused was charged with murder after assaulting the deceased with a stick. He pleaded not guilty to murder but tendered a plea of guilty to culpable homicide, which the State accepted. The statement of agreed facts showed that the accused approached the deceased about a donkey transaction, the deceased accused him of greed and struck him once with a walking stick, after which the accused dispossessed him of the stick and assaulted him repeatedly. The deceased later died. The court accepted that the accused negligently caused the death and convicted him of culpable homicide. In sentence, the court considered that the accused was an elderly first offender, that the deceased was the initial aggressor, and that there had been a delay of almost five years. The accused was sentenced to five years’ imprisonment with three years suspended.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the accepted facts proved murder or culpable homicide.
Whether the accused acted with the requisite intention for murder.
What sentence was appropriate given the deceased’s initial aggression and the long delay before trial.
legal_principles: Where the State accepts a limited plea and the agreed facts establish negligent causing of death without proof of dolus directus or dolus eventualis, the proper verdict is culpable homicide. In sentencing culpable homicide involving violence, the court considers the sanctity of life, the offender’s personal circumstances, provocation or aggression by the deceased, delay, remorse and the need for deterrence.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the accused caused the death of the deceased but the accepted facts did not prove intention to kill.
Held, further, that the proper verdict was culpable homicide.
Held, further, that a custodial sentence was required despite mitigation because a life had been lost through violence.
ratio_decidendi: The decisive reasoning was that the accused’s repeated assault with the stick negligently caused death, but the State accepted that the requisite intention for murder had not been proved. The deceased’s initial aggression and the long delay mitigated sentence but did not remove the need for imprisonment.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: S v Dhiga (175 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 175 (16 May 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE v WARDSON DHIGA
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
judges: MOYO J with Assessors Mr A. B. Mpofu & Mr T. Matemba
subject_area:
Criminal law
    Murder
    Mental disorder
    Insanity
    Special verdict
    Mental Health
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – plea of not guilty by reason of insanity – psychiatrist's report showing schizophrenia and substance-induced psychosis at time of offence – accused unable to appreciate wrongfulness or be held responsible – special verdict entered – accused committed to Mlondolozi Mental Institution.
headnote: The accused was charged with murder after assaulting his aunt with a steel wheelbarrow handle, causing her death. He pleaded not guilty by reason of insanity. The State accepted that plea, and the parties prepared a statement of agreed facts. The post-mortem report established death from subarachnoid haemorrhage and head trauma. Psychiatric evidence from Dr N. Mawere concluded that at the time of the offence the accused suffered from schizophrenia and substance-induced psychosis to the extent that he could not be held responsible for his actions or appreciate their wrongfulness. The Court entered a special verdict of not guilty by reason of insanity, acquitted him of murder, and committed him to Mlondolozi Mental Institution because he was a danger to society.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the accused was criminally responsible at the time of the killing.
    Whether a special verdict of not guilty by reason of insanity was appropriate.
    What disposal should follow given the accused's mental condition and danger to society.
legal_principles: Where psychiatric evidence establishes that an accused suffered from a mental disorder at the time of the act to such an extent that he could not appreciate the wrongfulness of his conduct or be held legally responsible, the court may enter a special verdict of not guilty by reason of insanity under the Mental Health Act. The Court may then order commitment to an appropriate mental institution where the accused is a danger to society.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the post-mortem evidence established that the deceased died from subarachnoid haemorrhage and head trauma.
    Held, further, that the psychiatric evidence showed that the accused could not be held responsible for his actions at the time of the offence.
    Held, further, that the accused was not guilty by reason of insanity and a special verdict was appropriate.
    Held, further, that because the accused was a danger to society he should be committed to Mlondolozi Mental Institution.
ratio_decidendi: The decisive reasoning was that the unchallenged psychiatric evidence established the accused's mental disorder and lack of criminal responsibility at the time of the fatal assault; therefore a conviction for murder could not follow and a special verdict was required.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to: None identified

    Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], section 47

document_name: S v Chivende (178 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 178 (18 May 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE v TAPIWANASHE CHIVENDE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Chivende (178 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 178 (18 May 2022)
judges: MOYO J with Assessors Mr A.B Mpofu and Mr W.T Matemba
subject_area:
Criminal law
    Murder
    Constructive intent
    Provocation
    Intoxication
    Sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – accused admitting assault and strangulation in confirmed warned and cautioned statement – attempted reliance on provocation and drunkenness – whether accused foresaw death as a possibility – strangulation as conduct from which foresight of death inferred – conviction for murder with constructive intent – sentence discounted for pre-trial incarceration.
headnote: The accused was charged with murder arising from the death of Anywhere Tshuma. The Court admitted the State summary, defence outline, confirmed warned and cautioned statement and post-mortem report. The accused's own account was that he assaulted and strangled the deceased after she asked him to leave. He sought to rely on anger, provocation and drunkenness. The Court accepted the confirmed statement as the reliable account, rejected the suggested provocation and found that the accused's vivid account showed that he was in control of his senses. Because strangulation deprives a person of oxygen and death is a foreseeable result, the Court inferred constructive intent. The accused was convicted of murder with constructive intent and sentenced to thirteen years' imprisonment after discounting about two years spent in pre-trial custody.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the accused's confirmed warned and cautioned statement was reliable and admissible.
    Whether drunkenness deprived the accused of capacity.
    Whether provocation reduced the offence from murder.
    Whether the accused foresaw death as a possibility from strangulation.
    The appropriate sentence.
legal_principles: A confirmed warned and cautioned statement is admissible unless successfully challenged. Murder with legal or constructive intent may be inferred where the only reasonable inference from the accused's conduct is that he foresaw death as a possibility and persisted. Strangulation is conduct from which foresight of death may be inferred because it deprives the victim of oxygen. Intoxication and provocation must be supported by credible facts sufficient to negate or reduce criminal responsibility.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the confirmed warned and cautioned statement was admissible and represented the correct version of events.
    Held, further, that the accused's detailed and vivid account stripped him of any defence of incapacity through drunkenness.
    Held, further, that the alleged provocation was not established because the accused and deceased were no longer in an ordinary relationship and the deceased had made plain the nature of her conduct.
    Held, further, that by strangling the deceased the accused must have foreseen death as a possible result.
    Held, further, that the accused was guilty of murder with constructive intent.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the accused intentionally strangled the deceased, and on the facts there was no credible intoxication or provocation sufficient to negate liability; death is a foreseeable consequence of strangulation, so constructive intent was established beyond reasonable doubt.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to: None identified
legislation_considered:
Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], section 47
    Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], provisions concerning confirmed warned and cautioned statements referred to generally

document_name: S v Dube (179 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 179 (30 June 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: PRECIOUS DUBE v THE STATE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
    Bail pending trial
    Pending lower-court proceedings
    Jurisdiction
    Unterminated proceedings
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – bail pending trial – applicant filing bail application in High Court while bail proceedings pending before magistrates court – magistrate still to rule on jurisdiction under s 116 – superior court slow to interfere in unterminated lower-court proceedings – High Court declining to entertain matter as court of first instance – application struck off roll.
headnote: The applicant was charged with attempted robbery after an alleged police ambush and exchange of fire in Harare, with firearms and ammunition recovered. He first filed a bail application at the High Court, Harare, but withdrew it on the basis that the Magistrates Court had jurisdiction. His legal practitioners then appeared before the Magistrates Court, where the magistrate raised jurisdiction under s 116 of the Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act. Before that court had ruled, the applicant filed another bail application in the High Court at Bulawayo. The State objected that the bail application was still pending before the Magistrates Court. The Court held that the preliminary jurisdiction point arose within a bail application and that the Magistrates Court was seized with the bail matter. There was no sound basis for the High Court to entertain the same relief as a court of first instance while the lower court was still seized. The preliminary objection was upheld and the application struck off the roll.
issues_for_determination:
Whether there was a pending bail application before the Magistrates Court.
    Whether the High Court should entertain a bail application as a court of first instance while the Magistrates Court was seized with the same relief.
    Whether alleged delay in the Magistrates Court justified High Court intervention.
    Whether the preliminary objection should be upheld.
legal_principles: Superior courts are slow to interfere with unterminated proceedings of lower courts, except where there is a gross irregularity or clearly wrong decision causing serious prejudice that cannot be corrected otherwise. A bail application remains pending in the lower court even where the lower court must first determine a jurisdictional point arising in that bail application. The High Court should not unnecessarily interfere where the same relief is already before a court established by law.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the substance of the matter before the Magistrates Court was a bail application, not merely an isolated jurisdictional point.
    Held, further, that the Magistrates Court was seized with the bail application and had to rule first on its jurisdiction under s 116.
    Held, further, that the High Court could not entertain the same bail application as a court of first instance without a sound legal basis.
    Held, further, that the preliminary objection had merit and the application should be struck off the roll.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the Magistrates Court was already seized with the applicant's bail application, including the preliminary issue of jurisdiction, and the High Court should not interfere with those unterminated proceedings or duplicate the same relief.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to: Mamombe and Another v Mushure N.O and Another CCZ 4/2022

    Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], section 116

document_name: Jin Yang Africa v Estate Late George Makurira and 4 Others (18 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 18 (20 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: JIN YANG AFRICA v ESTATE LATE GEORGE MAKURIRA (Represented by ANGELA CHANDAENGERWA) and 4 Others
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: Jin Yang Africa v Estate Late George Makurira and 4 Others (18 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 18 (20 January 2022)
subject_area:
Mining law
Interdicts
Urgent chamber applications
Property and mineral rights
catchwords_flynote: Mining law – adjacent mining claims – confirmation of provisional order – Bonsor South and Olympia 7 – over-pegging – prior pegger – sections 50, 58 and 177(3) of Mines and Minerals Act [Chapter 21:05] – Provincial Mining Director’s survey report – final interdict – cancellation of registration certificate.
headnote: The applicant sought confirmation of a provisional order interdicting the first and second respondents from conducting mining operations at Bonsor South and Bonsor South West mines. The dispute formed part of a long history of mining litigation involving adjacent claims, including Olympia 7. The Court relied on a consolidated report by the Provincial Mining Director, prepared after a court-directed ground verification exercise. The report showed overlapping claims and a historical encroachment dating back to the earlier pegging and registration of Bonsor South. The Court held that the applicant, as prior pegger, had superior rights protected by section 177(3) of the Mines and Minerals Act, and that section 58 could not protect a claim pegged in an area not open for pegging. The applicant established the requirements for a final interdict. The provisional order was confirmed, the respondents were interdicted from interfering with the applicant’s mining operations, Olympia 7 was cancelled for encroaching into Bonsor South, and costs were awarded against the first and second respondents.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the provisional order restraining mining activity on Bonsor South and Bonsor South West should be confirmed.
Whether Bonsor South had prior and superior rights over Olympia 7.
Whether section 58 of the Mines and Minerals Act protected Olympia 7 despite the encroachment.
Whether the applicant established the requirements for a final interdict.
Whether Olympia 7 should be cancelled as having been pegged on ground not open for prospecting.
legal_principles: Under section 177(3) of the Mines and Minerals Act, priority of acquisition of title determines rights between competing peggers, and a subsequent pegger’s rights are subordinated to those of a prior pegger to the extent of any conflict. Section 58 cannot be used to protect a claim pegged in ground not open for pegging where prior rights have been established. A final interdict requires a clear right established on a balance of probabilities, irreparable injury actually committed or reasonably apprehended, and the absence of similar protection by another remedy.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the Provincial Mining Director’s consolidated report established the historical and factual basis of the dispute and showed that Olympia 7 encroached into Bonsor South.
Held, further, that Bonsor South, as the prior pegged and registered claim, enjoyed superior rights under section 177(3) of the Mines and Minerals Act.
Held, further, that section 58 of the Act could not protect Olympia 7 because it had been pegged in an area not open for pegging and its rights were subordinate to those of the applicant.
Held, further, that the applicant established a clear right, irreparable harm from the extraction of finite mineral resources, and the absence of an adequate alternative remedy.
Held, further, that the provisional order should be confirmed and Olympia 7 cancelled.
ratio_decidendi: The applicant was entitled to final interdictory relief because the survey evidence and historical mining records established that Bonsor South was the prior pegged claim and that Olympia 7 encroached into it; consequently the first and second respondents had no legally protected right to continue mining there.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that the Ministry of Mines had not shown a willingness over the years to resolve the illegal pegging and that finality was needed in litigation repeatedly brought over the same mining dispute.
cases_referred_to:
K & G Mining Syndicate v Mugangavari & Ors HB 131/17
ZESA Staff Pension Fund v Mushambadzi SC-57-02

ZESA Staff Pension Fund v Mushambadzi SC-57-02
