document_name: S v Tshuma (181 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 181 (14 June 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE Versus CALVIN TSHUMA
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Tshuma (181 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 181 (14 June 2022)
judges: MAKONESE J with Assessors Mr J. Ndubiwa & Mr J. L. M. Zulu
subject_area:
Criminal law
    Rape
    Murder
    Child victims
    Juvenile offenders
    Aggravating circumstances
    Sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – juvenile accused – rape and murder of nine-year-old girl – accused luring child from school route with snacks, raping and strangling her – diminished responsibility rejected – murder in aggravating circumstances – recommendation for training institute rejected – sentences of 8 years for rape and 20 years for murder to run consecutively.
headnote: The 16-year-old accused followed a nine-year-old child on her way home from school, lured her into a bushy area using corn snacks, raped her without consent and strangled her to death. He pleaded guilty to rape and denied murder, tendering a plea to culpable homicide. The State rejected the limited plea. The Court found from the accused's own evidence that he strangled the child because he did not want her to report the rape. The Court held that the murder was committed in aggravating circumstances. The probation officer's recommendation of placement in a training institution was rejected. The accused was sentenced to eight years' imprisonment for rape and twenty years' imprisonment for murder, the sentences to run consecutively.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the accused was guilty of rape.
    Whether the accused intentionally killed the deceased or was guilty only of culpable homicide.
    Whether diminished responsibility or youthfulness affected liability.
    Whether the murder was committed in aggravating circumstances.
    What sentence was appropriate for a juvenile offender convicted of rape and murder.
legal_principles: A juvenile offender's youth is a substantial sentencing consideration, but it does not prevent conviction or an appropriately severe sentence where the facts show courage, wickedness or aggravating circumstances. Murder committed in the course of or in pursuance of rape may fall under aggravating circumstances. The Court must protect children and impose sentences that reflect the gravity of rape and murder of a child.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the accused raped the deceased without her consent.
    Held, further, that the accused strangled the deceased to prevent her from reporting the rape.
    Held, further, that the facts established murder and not culpable homicide.
    Held, further, that the murder was committed in aggravating circumstances.
    Held, further, that placement in a training institute was inappropriate.
    Held, further, that the sentences of 8 years and 20 years had to run consecutively.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the accused's own evidence showed that he deliberately strangled the deceased to prevent report of the rape, establishing murder in aggravating circumstances notwithstanding his youth.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to:
S v Zaranyika & Others 1995 (1) ZLR 270 (H)
    S v Dezzy Ncube SC-14-87

document_name: S v Mapope (182 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 182 (29 June 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE versus NOBERT MAPOPE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Mapope (182 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 182 (29 June 2022)
subject_area: Criminal law; Murder; Self-defence; Evidence; Acquittal
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – shooting by police officer during anti-illegal-mining operation – self-defence – gold panners advancing with weapons – State bearing onus – accused’s lies not necessarily proof of guilt – benefit of doubt – acquittal
headnote: The accused, a police officer, was charged with murder after shooting a gold panner during an operation against illegal mining. His defence was that he acted in self-defence when a group of about twenty gold panners advanced towards him armed with axes, picks, machetes and shovels. The State relied substantially on admitted evidence and two oral witnesses. The court found that although the accused had lied in some respects, the State had not called evidence directly contradicting the core of his version that he was under attack. The court applied the law of self-defence under s 253 of the Criminal Law Code and held that, on the evidence, the attack was unlawful, directed at the accused, had commenced or was imminent, and the means used were reasonable in the circumstances. The accused was acquitted.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the accused unlawfully and intentionally killed the deceased.
Whether the accused’s self-defence was reasonably possibly true.
Whether the accused’s lies on some aspects justified conviction.
Whether the State proved guilt beyond reasonable doubt.
legal_principles: The State bears the burden of proving guilt beyond reasonable doubt. A false explanation by an accused does not itself prove guilt; an innocent person may lie because he believes the truth will not be accepted. Self-defence under s 253 of the Criminal Law Code requires an unlawful attack that has commenced or is imminent, conduct necessary to avert the attack, reasonable means, and harm directed at the attacker without gross disproportionality.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the accused lied in some respects, including aspects of how the deceased was shot, but those lies did not establish guilt.
Held, further, that the accused’s core version that he was under attack by a group of armed gold panners was reasonably possibly true.
Held, further, that the State failed to disprove self-defence beyond reasonable doubt.
Held, further, that the accused was entitled to the benefit of the doubt.
ratio_decidendi: The decisive reasoning was that the State did not lead evidence sufficient to disprove the accused’s claim that he fired while under imminent unlawful attack from a large group of armed gold panners. Because the evidence left a reasonable doubt on self-defence, a murder conviction could not stand.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: S v Sibanda (183 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 183 (29 June 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE Versus JOSPHAT SIBANDA
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Sibanda (183 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 183 (29 June 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal law
    Murder
    Intoxication
    Drowning
    Violence against women
    Sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – accused drowning deceased in sewer stream – eyewitnesses seeing repeated immersion while deceased struggled for air – intoxication raised – conduct deliberate and sustained – murder proved – mitigating factors including intoxication and remorse – sentence of 18 years' imprisonment.
headnote: The accused was charged with murder after he drowned the deceased in a sewer stream. Joggers heard the deceased screaming for help and saw the accused holding her, pushing her into the water and immersing her several times while she struggled for air. Police later attended the scene and the accused pointed out where the body was. The accused relied on intoxication and claimed a blackout. The Court rejected intoxication as excluding liability because the accused's conduct was deliberate and directed. He was convicted of murder. In sentencing, the Court considered remorse and intoxication but held that the offence involved extreme violence against a vulnerable woman. The accused was sentenced to 18 years' imprisonment.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the accused caused the deceased's death by drowning.
    Whether intoxication deprived the accused of the required mens rea.
    Whether the State proved murder beyond reasonable doubt.
    What sentence was appropriate.
legal_principles: Voluntary intoxication may be relevant to criminal responsibility but does not automatically exclude intent; the Court assesses whether the accused retained appreciation and control sufficient for the required mental element. Murder is proved where an accused intentionally causes death or realises the real possibility of death and proceeds. Sentencing for murder requires balancing mitigation against the seriousness of the unlawful taking of life.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the eyewitness evidence proved that the accused deliberately immersed the deceased in the sewer water until she died.
    Held, further, that the accused's intoxication did not absolve him of responsibility.
    Held, further, that the State proved murder.
    Held, further, that the offence was grave and involved serious violence against a defenceless woman.
    Held, further, that a sentence of 18 years' imprisonment was appropriate.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the accused's repeated and deliberate immersion of the deceased in the sewer stream while she struggled for air showed the necessary intention or realisation of the risk of death, and intoxication did not render him incapable of murder liability.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to: None identified
legislation_considered:
Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], section 47(1), section 221
    Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], section 314

document_name: Kufandada v Assistant Commissioner Wilson and 4 Others Marecha (184 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 184 (7 July 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: SAMUEL KUFANDADA v ASSISTANT COMMISSIONER WILSON MARECHA and 4 Others
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: Kufandada v Assistant Commissioner Wilson and 4 Others Marecha (184 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 184 (7 July 2022)
subject_area:
Practice and procedure
Recusal
Administrative law
Police disciplinary proceedings
Judicial impartiality
catchwords_flynote: Practice and procedure – application for recusal – test for apparent bias – judge previously heard rescission application involving same parties – adverse observations made about applicant’s conduct – duty to sit balanced against perception of bias – section 69 of Constitution – borderline case – recusal granted.
headnote: The applicant sought the recusal of DUBE-BANDA J from hearing his review application challenging his discharge from the Zimbabwe Republic Police. The same judge had previously granted a rescission application brought by the police in respect of a default judgment the applicant had obtained, and had made adverse observations about the applicant’s litigation conduct. The applicant alleged that respondents’ counsel had boasted of controlling the courts; those allegations were rejected as false. The Court held that the fact that a judge had previously dealt with a rescission application between the same parties was not, without more, a bar to hearing the main review. However, the adverse observations made in the rescission judgment about the applicant’s truthfulness and alleged underhand tactics could cause a reasonable, objective and informed person to apprehend that the judge might not bring an impartial mind to the review. The judge recused himself.
issues_for_determination:
Whether a reasonable, objective and informed person, on the correct facts, would reasonably apprehend that the judge would not bring an impartial mind to the review application.
Whether the judge’s prior involvement in the rescission application between the same parties required recusal.
Whether adverse comments made in the rescission judgment created a reasonable perception of bias.
Whether the applicant’s allegations against counsel had any factual foundation.
legal_principles: The test for recusal is objective: whether a reasonable, objective and informed person would, on the correct facts, reasonably apprehend that the judge will not bring an impartial mind to the adjudication. The law against bias protects both actual bias and the appearance of bias. Judges have a duty to sit in cases allocated to them unless recusal is required in the interests of justice. Prior involvement in a matter does not automatically disqualify a judge, particularly where the earlier matter was disposed of on preliminary grounds, but prior adverse findings or observations may contribute to a reasonable apprehension of bias.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant’s allegations that counsel had boasted of controlling the courts were false and unsupported.
Held, further, that the judge’s previous determination of the rescission application, standing alone, did not bar him from hearing the review.
Held, further, that the observations made in the rescission judgment about the applicant’s truthfulness and underhand tactics could unsettle a reasonable, objective and informed person and create a reasonable apprehension of lack of impartiality.
Held, further, that although the case was borderline, recusal should be granted to protect the appearance of justice.
ratio_decidendi: The judge recused himself not because the applicant’s allegations against counsel were accepted or because prior involvement alone required recusal, but because the judge’s earlier adverse observations about the applicant’s conduct in the same review matter could reasonably create a perception that he might not approach the review with an impartial mind.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court cautioned that recognising apparent bias does not permit litigants to engage in judge-shopping; the right to a fair hearing must be protected without allowing manipulation of judicial allocation.

document_name: S v Donga (185 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 185 (7 July 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: KHOLWANI DONGA v THE STATE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Donga (185 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 185 (7 July 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
    Leave to appeal
    Murder
    Attempted murder
    Identification evidence
    Alibi
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – application for leave to appeal against conviction – applicant convicted in S v Donga HB 97/22 – test of reasonable prospects of success – challenge to identification evidence and rejection of alibi – identification by witness who knew applicant and made immediate reports – no reasonable prospect that Supreme Court would reach different conclusion – leave dismissed.
headnote: After being convicted in S v Donga HB 97/22 of one count of murder and six counts of attempted murder, the applicant sought leave to appeal against conviction. His proposed grounds attacked the credibility of the identifying witness, alleged inconsistency in State evidence, asserted a motive for false testimony and challenged rejection of his alibi. The Court applied the test whether there were reasonable prospects that the Supreme Court would reach a different conclusion. It held that Ayanda Donga knew the applicant well, saw him at close range with light from solar lights and the fire, immediately named him to several persons, and described clothing later found at the applicant's home. The Court rejected the asserted contradictions and found that the alibi evidence had been correctly rejected. It held that no realistic prospects of success had been shown and dismissed the application.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant showed reasonable prospects of success on appeal.
    Whether the identification evidence was reliable and properly treated with caution.
    Whether alleged contradictions in State evidence created appeal prospects.
    Whether the alibi evidence had been wrongly rejected.
    Whether leave to appeal should be granted.
legal_principles: Leave to appeal is granted only where there are reasonable prospects that an appellate court could reach a different conclusion. More is required than a mere possibility of success or an arguable case. Identification evidence must be approached with caution, but where a witness knows the accused well, observes him at close range and immediately reports his identity, the evidence may be reliable. A trial court's credibility findings will not be disturbed absent sound reason.
held_decision_points:
Held, that Ayanda Donga's identification of the applicant was reliable and supported by surrounding evidence.
    Held, further, that the alleged contradictions based on police statements had no prospects because the statements were not handed in as exhibits and the complaint reduced to semantics.
    Held, further, that the alleged contradiction between Ayanda Donga and Angela Mpala did not undermine the conviction.
    Held, further, that the alibi evidence had correctly been rejected as false.
    Held, further, that there was no basis to conclude that the Supreme Court might take a different view, and leave to appeal should be refused.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the applicant failed to show any sound, rational basis for concluding that the Supreme Court could reasonably overturn the conviction; the identification and rejection of alibi remained sustainable on the record.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

    A-G v Muchadehama & Anor SC 23/14
    S v Mthethwa 1972 (3) SA 766 (A)
    Pretorius JP, Cross-Examination in South African Law (LexisNexis Butterworths 1977), referred to

document_name: LARRIC~1.DOC
case_name: LARRIC SERVICES (PRIVATE) LIMITED v BLACKIYNX (PRIVATE) LIMITED and 2 Others
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
subject_area:
Civil procedure
Declaratory relief
Material disputes of fact
Property law
Deeds and transfer
Company law
catchwords_flynote: Civil procedure – opposed application for declaratory order – cancellation of transfer of immovable property – alleged fraudulent sale and transfer – validity of notice of opposition and opposing affidavit – affidavit by corporate representative – material disputes of fact in motion proceedings – mutually destructive versions – alleged lack of authority by former director – non-joinder of alleged fraudster – referral to trial.
headnote: The applicant sought declaratory relief setting aside as fraudulent the sale and transfer of its immovable property to the first respondent and reversing the transfer. The first respondent opposed and raised preliminary objections, including material disputes of fact. The applicant also contended that the first respondent’s notice of opposition was invalid because the opposing affidavit did not use conventional oath language and did not expressly state personal knowledge. The Court held that the affidavit was sworn, that its defects were formal, and that the deponent’s authority and connection to the first respondent appeared from interlocutory proceedings in HC 422/21. The notice of opposition was valid. On material disputes, the Court found mutually destructive versions about the authority of Michael Horwitz to sign the sale agreement and transfer documentation, the authenticity of documents, and the alleged fraud. Because these disputes could not be resolved on the papers without injustice, and because dismissal was not appropriate, the matter was referred to trial with costs in the cause.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the first respondent’s notice of opposition and opposing affidavit were valid.
Whether the matter involved material disputes of fact incapable of resolution on the papers.
Whether the competing versions on authority, fraud and transfer documentation should be resolved by oral evidence or trial.
Whether the application should be dismissed or referred to trial.
Costs.
legal_principles: Courts should avoid elevating form over substance where an affidavit is plainly sworn before a commissioner of oaths. A corporate litigant’s deponent is a witness and may swear to facts where the source of authority and knowledge is established, including from interlocutory proceedings in the same matter. In motion proceedings, the court may adopt a robust approach but cannot resolve genuine and material disputes of fact on the papers where doing so would cause injustice. Where material disputes exist, the court may dismiss the application, hear oral evidence, or refer the matter to trial; dismissal is generally appropriate where the applicant should have realised that serious disputes were bound to arise.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the opposing affidavit was a sworn affidavit notwithstanding the absence of conventional opening words such as “make oath”.
Held, further, that the first respondent’s notice of opposition was valid because it comprised Form 29A and an opposing affidavit, and the deponent’s connection to the first respondent appeared from the court’s records in HC 422/21.
Held, further, that the application was affected by genuine and material disputes of fact concerning authority, the authenticity of transfer documents and alleged fraud.
Held, further, that resolving those disputes on the papers would cause injustice to either party, particularly because Michael Horwitz had not been joined.
Held, further, that the matter should be referred to trial rather than dismissed, with costs in the cause.
ratio_decidendi: The application could not be decided on affidavit because the applicant’s fraud case and the first respondent’s reliance on the transfer documents created material, mutually destructive factual disputes about authority and authenticity; justice required those issues to be tried on oral evidence.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

Mhungu v Mtindi 1986 (2) ZLR 171 (SC) at 173A-B
Zimbabwe Open University v Magaramombe & Anor HH 45/12
Chiadzwa v Paulkner 1991(2) ZLR 33 SC @ 36G-H
Dobbie & Ors ZB Bank Ltd & Anor HH 126/17

document_name: Mpofu N.O v Dzumbunu and 2 Others (188 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 188 (14 July 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: BATANDI MICHAEL MPOFU N.O v FELIX DZUMBUNU and 2 OTHERS
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: Mpofu N.O v Dzumbunu and 2 Others (188 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 188 (14 July 2022)
subject_area:
Contract
    Sale of land
    Special case
    Specific performance
    Occupational damages
    Civil procedure
catchwords_flynote: Contract – sale of immovable property – special case on agreed facts – purchaser in breach of instalment sale – notice of cancellation – Contractual Penalties Act – whether notice effectively served by leaving it with worker at chosen address – subsequent sale to second purchaser while default judgment was extant – who entitled to transfer – specific performance not available to purchaser in breach – eviction moot – holding-over damages where occupation followed sale agreement, not lease.
headnote: The matter proceeded as a special case on agreed facts. The plaintiff had sold an immovable property to the 1st defendant, who paid a deposit but failed to pay the balance. A notice of termination was served at the address provided in the agreement and the initial agreement was cancelled. A default judgment confirming cancellation was later granted, the 1st defendant was evicted, and the plaintiff sold the property to the 2nd defendant, who paid the full purchase price. The default judgment was later rescinded and the 1st defendant attempted to pay the balance. The Court held that the initial agreement had been lawfully cancelled because the notice was effectively served in terms of the Contractual Penalties Act and Interpretation Act. The 1st defendant, being in breach, was not entitled to specific performance. The 2nd defendant was entitled to transfer. Eviction was moot because no party was in occupation, and occupational damages were refused because the 1st defendant had occupied as purchaser, not tenant.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the initial agreement of sale between plaintiff and 1st defendant was lawfully terminated.
    Whether there was any legal impediment to the second agreement of sale between plaintiff and 2nd defendant.
    To whom the property should be transferred.
    Whether eviction and occupational damages were competent.
    Costs.
legal_principles: A special case is decided on agreed facts and parties are generally bound by those facts. Under the Contractual Penalties Act, a seller must give effective notice of breach and afford the purchaser an opportunity to remedy before cancellation. Section 40(2) of the Interpretation Act informs valid service by leaving a document at a person's usual or last-known place of abode. Specific performance is discretionary and is not available to a party who has breached the contract. Occupation as a purchaser under a sale agreement does not without more create a lease or entitlement to holding-over damages.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the notice of cancellation was effectively served on the 1st defendant at his chosen address by leaving it with his worker.
    Held, further, that the 1st defendant breached the initial agreement by failing to pay the balance of the purchase price and the agreement was lawfully cancelled.
    Held, further, that there was no legal impediment to the second agreement of sale, which was concluded while the default judgment confirming cancellation was extant.
    Held, further, that the 1st defendant, as the party in breach, was not entitled to specific performance.
    Held, further, that the property should be transferred to the 2nd defendant.
    Held, further, that eviction was moot and the claim for occupational damages was not sustainable.
ratio_decidendi: The decisive basis was that the 1st defendant was in breach, was given effective statutory notice, failed to remedy the breach, and the initial sale was lawfully cancelled before the second sale. As a result, the 1st defendant could not demand transfer or specific performance, while the 2nd defendant, who had paid the full price under the second agreement, was entitled to transfer.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

    Washaya and Another v Makebreak Trading (Private) Limited and 2 Others SC 163/2021
    Savanhu v Marere NO & Ors 2009 (1) ZLR 320
    Hativagone & Another v CAG Farms (Pvt) Ltd & Others SC 42-2015
    Silonda v Nkomo HB 60-19
    Silonda v Nkomo SC 6/2022

document_name: Kufazvinei and Another v Mangoza and 3 Others (189 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 189 (14 September 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: MAVIS KUFAZVINEI and MUTEMO MANGWIRO v SUSAN MANGOZA and 3 Others
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: Kufazvinei and Another v Mangoza and 3 Others (189 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 189 (14 September 2022)
subject_area:
Delict
Medical negligence
Damages
Unopposed matters
Common law development
catchwords_flynote: Delict – medical negligence – death of adult child during medical procedure – unopposed claim after defence struck out – claim by parents for shock, pain and suffering – requirement of actionable harm – nervous shock and physical injury or illness – grief at death of another not actionable without proof of personal injury – development of common law not pleaded – claim dismissed.
headnote: The plaintiffs, parents of a deceased adult son, pursued an unopposed claim for US$100 000 damages for shock, pain and suffering arising from alleged medical negligence at Mpilo Hospital. The defendants had previously defended the matter but were in default at trial, their defence was struck out, and the plaintiffs were granted leave to finalise the matter on the unopposed roll. The Court accepted on the uncontroverted evidence that the death was caused by negligence. The decisive issue was whether the plaintiffs suffered actionable harm. The Court distinguished Namibian authority where specialist evidence established emotional shock affecting health and expenses incurred. In the present matter, there was no medical or other evidence showing that the plaintiffs’ shock impaired their bodily health or physical organism, and no evidence from the first plaintiff. The Court held that grief and distress arising from the death of another are not, without personal injury, actionable under a claim for shock, pain and suffering. The claim was dismissed with no order as to costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the deceased’s death was caused by the negligence of the defendants.
Whether the plaintiffs suffered actionable harm as a result of the negligence.
Whether damages for shock, pain and suffering may be awarded absent proof that the shock caused personal injury or impaired health.
Whether the common law should be developed on the basis advanced in supplementary heads of argument.
Costs.
legal_principles: A plaintiff claiming damages for nervous shock in an action founded on negligence must establish actionable harm, including that the shock directly impaired bodily health or affected the physical organism. Pain and suffering is a form of non-patrimonial loss associated with the plaintiff’s own bodily injuries. Grief and distress at the suffering or death of another, without proof of the plaintiff’s personal injury, is not actionable. A request to develop the common law should be pleaded and supported by evidence and legal foundation; it cannot be raised for the first time in heads of argument.
held_decision_points:
Held, that on the uncontroverted facts, the death of the plaintiffs’ son was caused by the negligence of the defendants.
Held, further, that the plaintiffs failed to prove actionable harm because there was no evidence that shock impaired their bodily health or physical organism.
Held, further, that grief and distress arising from the death of another, without personal injury, is not actionable as pain and suffering.
Held, further, that the common-law development argument was not pleaded and, in any event, the difficulty in the case was absence of evidence rather than a deviation from the Bill of Rights.
Held, further, that the claim should be dismissed with no order as to costs.
ratio_decidendi: Although negligence was accepted, the plaintiffs’ claim failed because they did not prove actionable harm to themselves: damages for shock, pain and suffering require evidence that the plaintiffs personally suffered injury or health impairment, and grief alone at the death of another is not compensable under that head.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that research should precede the issuance of summons and not be undertaken only after a matter is referred to the unopposed roll and legal authority is requested.

document_name: S v Ndlovu (19 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 19 (20 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: WISDOM MPILWENHLE NDLOVU Versus THE STATE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Ndlovu (19 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 19 (20 January 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
    Bail pending trial
    Murder
    Flight risk
    Third Schedule offences
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – bail pending trial – murder charge – applicant allegedly killed sex worker at lodge and concealed body – confirmed warned and cautioned statement, video footage and indications relied upon – previous evasion of arrest – exceptional circumstances not shown – bail refused.
headnote: The applicant sought bail pending trial on a murder charge. The State alleged that he hired the deceased, a sex worker, went with her to Silver Sands Lodge, killed her by strangulation, concealed the body under a bed and fled. The applicant denied murder and said a stranger arrived claiming to be the deceased's husband. The Court considered the evidence relied upon by the State, including video footage, positive indications, the body found under the bed, and a confirmed warned and cautioned statement. The applicant had allegedly booked the lodge under a false name and remained on the run for about a year. The Court held that the State had a strong prima facie case and that the applicant was a flight risk. The application was dismissed.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant had shown exceptional circumstances justifying bail on a Part II Third Schedule murder charge.
    Whether the State case was strong.
    Whether the applicant was likely to abscond if released on bail.
    Whether release would undermine the proper functioning of the criminal justice system.
legal_principles: For a Part II Third Schedule murder charge, the applicant bears the burden of showing, on a balance of probabilities, that exceptional circumstances exist which in the interests of justice permit release on bail. A strong State case, seriousness of the offence, likelihood of a long sentence, previous evasion of arrest and use of false details are relevant to flight risk and the interests of justice.
held_decision_points:
Held, that for bail purposes the Court could not ignore the confirmed warned and cautioned statement, video footage and indications relied upon by the State.
    Held, further, that the State had a strong prima facie case against the applicant.
    Held, further, that the applicant's alleged use of a false name and evasion of arrest for about a year showed a real danger of abscondment.
    Held, further, that the applicant had not shown exceptional circumstances permitting his release on bail.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the combination of a strong prima facie murder case, alleged prior evasion of arrest, use of a false name and likelihood of a lengthy sentence created a real risk of abscondment and outweighed the applicant's personal circumstances.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to: None identified
legislation_considered:
Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], section 47(1)(a)
    Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], section 115C(2)(a)(ii)(B) and Schedule 3

document_name: S v Smith (191 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 191 (14 July 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: LYLE SMITH Versus THE STATE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Smith (191 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 191 (14 July 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
    Bail pending trial
    Fraud
    Extradition
    Withdrawal by complainant
    Changed circumstances
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – bail pending trial – fraud charge – applicant extradited from Tanzania – complainant, sole key witness, testifying that he wished to withdraw complaint – withdrawal amounting to changed circumstance – no compelling reason for continued detention – bail granted with conditions.
headnote: The applicant was charged with fraud involving an alleged misrepresentation that he could secure mining equipment and receipt of US$335 000. The State opposed bail on the basis that he was a flight risk, having left Zimbabwe and been extradited from Tanzania. Before the bail hearing, the complainant filed and confirmed under oath a statement that he wished to withdraw the complaint. The investigating officer confirmed that the complainant was the sole key witness. The Court held that the complainant's attitude amounted to a changed circumstance and tilted the scales in favour of release. Bail pending trial was granted on conditions.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the High Court could entertain the bail application while a Magistrates Court bail application had also been filed.
    Whether the complainant's withdrawal of interest in the prosecution amounted to a changed circumstance.
    Whether there remained compelling reasons to deny bail.
    What bail conditions should be imposed.
legal_principles: An accused person should not simultaneously pursue bail applications in different courts. Under section 50 of the Constitution, bail pending trial should be granted unless there are compelling reasons for detention. A complainant's withdrawal of interest does not prevent prosecution, but where the complainant is the sole key witness it may be a material changed circumstance in assessing bail.
held_decision_points:
Held, that filing two bail applications in different courts at the same time was improper.
    Held, further, that the complainant's sworn wish to withdraw the complaint amounted to a changed circumstance.
    Held, further, that there was no compelling reason to deny bail at that stage.
    Held, further, that bail should be granted subject to reporting, residence and non-interference conditions.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that because the complainant was the sole key witness and had credibly stated under oath that he no longer wished to press the complaint, the basis for continued detention was materially weakened and no compelling reason remained to refuse bail.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court stated that it is improper for an applicant to file two bail applications in two different courts at the same time.

document_name: Main Protective Clothes (Pvt) Ltd v Ncube (192 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 192 (14 July 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: MAIN PROTECTIVE CLOTHES (PVT) LTD v NIMROD NCUBE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
case_number:
HC 1398/20
XREF HC 184/16
XREF HC 1271/19
XREF HC 996/20
citation: Main Protective Clothes (Pvt) Ltd v Ncube (192 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 192 (14 July 2022)
subject_area:
Arbitration
Labour law
Practice and procedure
Condonation
catchwords_flynote: Arbitration – application for condonation of late filing of application to set aside arbitral award – Article 34 of Arbitration Act [Chapter 7:15] – mistaken appeal to Labour Court – whether delay reasonably explained – prospects of success – currency of arbitral award – Finance (No. 2) Act, 2019 – interbank rate – public policy.
headnote: The applicant sought condonation for the late filing of an application to set aside an arbitral award made under the Arbitration Act. The award had quantified damages in lieu of reinstatement in United States dollars, payable in local currency at the interbank rate utilised by CBZ at the date of payment. The applicant had first appealed to the Labour Court, although the arbitrator had been appointed under the Arbitration Act and not under the Labour Act. The Court held that the explanation for delay was not reasonable because the applicant, which was legally represented, failed to follow readily available law. On prospects of success, the Court held that the Finance (No. 2) Act, 2019 did not invalidate the award because the damages had not been quantified or expressed in United States dollars immediately before the effective date. The parties had also agreed that any damages sounding in United States dollars would be paid in local currency at the CBZ interbank rate. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant gave a reasonable explanation for the delay in filing the Article 34 application.
Whether the applicant had prospects of success in setting aside the arbitral award on public-policy grounds.
Whether the arbitrator erred by expressing damages in United States dollars payable in local currency at the interbank rate.
legal_principles: A party seeking condonation must give a reasonable explanation for the delay and show prospects of success. An error of law by a legally represented party may be self-created where the law was readily available. Under Article 34 of the Arbitration Act, an award may be set aside if it conflicts with public policy. The conversion provisions of the Finance (No. 2) Act, 2019 apply to assets and liabilities quantified and expressed in United States dollars immediately before the effective date; they do not apply where the debt was not yet quantified at that date. Where parties agree that damages expressed in United States dollars are payable in local currency at an authorised dealer’s interbank rate, that rate may be used.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant’s delay was not reasonably explained because its decision to appeal to the Labour Court was a self-created error and the Labour Court had no jurisdiction over an arbitrator appointed under the Arbitration Act.
Held, further, that the applicant had no good prospects of success because the damages had not been quantified, stated or expressed in United States dollars as at 22 February 2019 and therefore did not fall within the conversion provisions relied upon by the applicant.
Held, further, that the arbitrator properly awarded payment in local currency at the CBZ interbank rate in accordance with the parties’ agreement and the Finance Act framework.
Held, further, that condonation should be refused and the application dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The decisive reason for dismissing the application was that the applicant failed to provide a reasonable explanation for the delay and, in any event, had no prospects of success because the arbitral award did not offend public policy: the damages were not an existing United States dollar liability before the effective date and were payable in local currency at the agreed interbank rate.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to:
Nimrod Ncube v Main Protective Clothing (Pvt) Ltd HB 212-16
Temprac supra
legislation_considered:
Arbitration Act [Chapter 7:15], Article 34 and Article 34(2)(b)(ii)
Labour Act [Chapter 28:01], section 98(10)
Finance (No. 2) Act No. 7 of 2019, section 22

document_name: Mhlanga v Mhlanga and 2 Others (193 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 193 (14 July 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: SIBUSISIWE MHLANGA v BONGANI MHLANGA and 2 OTHERS
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: Mhlanga v Mhlanga and 2 Others (193 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 193 (14 July 2022)
subject_area:
Family law
    Divorce and matrimonial property
    Civil procedure
    Execution and enforcement of court orders
    Transfer of immovable property
catchwords_flynote: Divorce – consent paper incorporated in court order – property awarded to former spouse – application to compel transfer – respondent alleging fraud and nondisclosure in consent paper – effect of extant court order – duty to obey court orders unless varied or set aside – Sheriff authorised to sign transfer documents on default.
headnote: The applicant sought an order compelling the 1st respondent, her former husband, to sign all documents required to transfer immovable properties into her name pursuant to their divorce order. The 1st respondent resisted on the basis that the consent paper and divorce order had allegedly been induced by nondisclosure or fraud and that he had applied for rescission. The Court held that the order awarding the properties to the applicant was extant, had not been varied or set aside, and had to be obeyed. The respondent's allegations about the consent paper could not defeat enforcement of an existing court order. The application succeeded, and the Sheriff was authorised to sign documents if the respondent failed to do so.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the 1st respondent had a valid defence to compliance with the divorce order.
    Whether allegations of fraud or nondisclosure in relation to the consent paper could defeat enforcement of the extant court order.
    Whether the Sheriff should be authorised to sign transfer documents if the 1st respondent failed to do so.
    Costs.
legal_principles: An extant order of court must be obeyed and given effect to unless and until varied or set aside by a court of competent jurisdiction. Parties may not disregard a court order on the basis of their own view that it was irregular or fraudulently obtained. Execution and enforcement proceed from the court order, not from the parties' private characterisation of the facts or reasons behind it.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the divorce order awarding the properties to the applicant was extant and binding.
    Held, further, that the 1st respondent's allegation that the consent paper was induced by fraud did not permit him to disregard the court order.
    Held, further, that the pending or attempted rescission application was of no moment because the order had not been varied or set aside and the rescission application had been struck off the roll.
    Held, further, that the applicant was entitled to an order compelling transfer and authorising the Sheriff to sign if the 1st respondent failed to do so.
ratio_decidendi: The decisive reason was that the properties had been awarded to the applicant under an extant divorce order. Since that order had not been varied or set aside, the respondent had no legal defence to an application compelling him to comply with it.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: S v Regiment (194 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 194 (14 July 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: JIMSTONE REGIMENT Versus THE STATE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Regiment (194 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 194 (14 July 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
    Bail pending trial
    Parks and wildlife
    Unlawful possession of raw ivory
    Strong prima facie case
    Flight risk
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – bail pending trial – unlawful possession of raw ivory – ivory found in bag and vehicle where applicant was seated – applicant alleging delivery for absent third party – no affidavit from alleged third party – defence not reasonably probable – strong prima facie case and mandatory sentence – bail refused.
headnote: The applicant applied for bail pending trial on a charge of unlawful possession of raw ivory. The State alleged that police acting on information found the applicant seated in a Toyota Gaia with a bag containing ivory and recovered more ivory from the vehicle. The applicant denied knowledge and said he was delivering a parcel for a person called Matters, whose affidavit was not produced. The Court held that a bail applicant must place a reasonably probable defence before the Court and that the alleged explanation was not reasonably possibly true. Because the applicant was found in physical possession of raw ivory, the State had a strong prima facie case, the charge was serious and a lengthy mandatory sentence was likely on conviction. The application was dismissed.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant was a suitable candidate for bail pending trial.
    Whether the applicant placed a recognised and reasonably probable defence before the Court.
    Whether the State case was strong.
    Whether the likelihood of a mandatory prison sentence created a risk of abscondment.
legal_principles: A bail applicant must tender a defence recognised at law and reasonably probable on the facts. Bare denials or far-fetched explanations may be rejected at the bail stage when assessing strength of the State case and risk to the interests of justice. Where conviction is almost certain and a lengthy mandatory sentence is likely, the Court is less inclined to grant bail unless the interests of justice permit release.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant was found in physical possession of raw ivory.
    Held, further, that the State had established a strong prima facie case.
    Held, further, that the applicant's explanation about delivering a parcel for 'Matters' was not reasonably possibly true and amounted to a bare denial.
    Held, further, that the seriousness of the charge and likelihood of a lengthy prison term created a risk of abscondment.
    Held, further, that bail had to be refused.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that physical possession of ivory, absence of a plausible supporting explanation, and the likely mandatory sentence created a strong prima facie case and risk of abscondment incompatible with bail.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: S v Ncube (195 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 195 (14 July 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: DUMOLWENKOSI NCUBE Versus THE STATE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: S v Ncube (195 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 195 (14 July 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
    Bail pending trial
    Murder
    Self-defence
    Flight risk
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – bail pending trial – murder charge – applicant admitting stabbing deceased while claiming self-defence – strong State case and likely lengthy custodial sentence – partial defence not sufficient to justify release – bail refused.
headnote: The applicant applied for bail pending trial on a murder charge. He admitted that he stabbed the deceased but said he acted in self-defence after the deceased and another person assaulted him during a dispute about a cellphone. The State opposed bail, arguing that the force used was disproportionate, the State case was strong and the likely sentence created an incentive to abscond. The Court held that the applicant had admitted the stabbing, that the State had overwhelming evidence, that the charge was serious and that it was not in the interests of justice to grant bail. The application was dismissed.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant had shown that it was in the interests of justice to release him on bail pending trial.
    Whether the admitted stabbing and State evidence established a strong prima facie case.
    Whether the seriousness of the charge and likely sentence created a risk of abscondment.
    Whether the claim of self-defence justified release.
legal_principles: In bail pending trial, the Court balances liberty and the administration of justice. Under section 117(2) of the Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act, bail may be refused where there is a likelihood that the accused will not stand trial, endanger the public, interfere with witnesses, conceal evidence or undermine the criminal justice system. The nature of the charge, severity of likely sentence and strength of the State case are relevant.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the State had a strong case because the applicant admitted stabbing the deceased.
    Held, further, that the applicant faced a serious charge and, upon conviction, was likely to receive a lengthy custodial sentence.
    Held, further, that the applicant's asserted self-defence was only a partial answer for bail purposes and did not displace the strong State case.
    Held, further, that it was not in the interests of justice to grant bail.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the admitted stabbing, seriousness of the murder charge and likely lengthy sentence made the applicant unsuitable for bail pending trial notwithstanding his asserted self-defence.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to: Aitken & Anor v Attorney General 1992 (1) ZRL 249 (S)
legislation_considered:
Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], section 47(1)(a)
    Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], section 117(2)
cases_referred_to_terms: Aitken & Anor v Attorney General 1992 (1) ZRL 249 (S)
legislation_considered_terms: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], section 47(1)(a)
Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], section 117(2)

document_name: Mudawini and Another v Darangwa N.O and 10 Others (196 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 196 (14 July 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: SIPHATHISIWE MUCHIYANI MUDAWINI and HENRY MUCHIYANI v PATRICIA DARANGWA N.O and 10 OTHERS
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: Mudawini and Another v Darangwa N.O and 10 Others (196 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 196 (14 July 2022)
subject_area:
Administration of estates
    Customary law succession
    Property law
    Sale and transfer of immovable property
    Rei vindicatio
    Civil procedure
catchwords_flynote: Administration of estates – deceased estate – whether surviving spouse could inherit immovable property where deceased died before statutory amendments – section 68F of Administration of Estates Act – sale of estate property by executrix dative – challenge to First and Final Liquidation and Distribution Account – allegations of fraud in sale price – whether sale and title to purchasers should be set aside – counterclaim by registered owners for eviction – occupational damages.
headnote: The plaintiffs challenged the administration of the estate of the late Wilson Muchiyani Mudawini and sought to set aside the sale and transfer of the Montgomery property to the 5th and 6th defendants. They contended that the late Ema Mudawini, Wilson's second wife, was entitled to the property or to compensation for improvements, and that the executrix dative had administered the estate irregularly. The Court held that the earlier judgment, HH 492-17, did not itself disqualify the late Ema from inheriting, but the applicable law was the law in force at Wilson's death in 1994, before the later statutory succession reforms. Ema, being a second wife married under customary law, could not inherit the immovable property. The Court held that fraud in the sale had not been proved, that objections to the distribution account should have been raised through the Master's processes, and that the purchasers' title was not tainted. The plaintiffs' claim was dismissed. The purchasers' counterclaim succeeded in part: eviction was granted, but holding-over damages were refused.
issues_for_determination:
Whether HH 492-17 disqualified the late Ema Mudawini from inheriting the Montgomery property.
    Whether the late Ema could inherit the property under section 68F of the Administration of Estates Act.
    Whether the executrix dative administered the late Wilson's estate lawfully.
    Whether the late Ema effected improvements to the Montgomery property and whether compensation was due.
    Whether the sale and transfer of the Montgomery property to the 5th and 6th defendants were fraudulent or invalid.
    Whether the purchasers were entitled to eviction and occupational damages.
legal_principles: The applicable law of succession is generally determined as at the date of death. Before the relevant statutory reforms, a widow married under customary law did not have a right to inherit immovable property from her husband's estate in the circumstances addressed by the Court. He who alleges must prove, including where fraud is alleged. Complaints about an executor's administration should ordinarily be directed first to the Master where the statute provides remedies. A registered owner may vindicate property from a possessor who has no enforceable right against the owner. Holding-over damages require a proper legal basis and are not automatically recoverable merely because a purchaser or owner seeks eviction.
held_decision_points:
Held, that HH 492-17 did not itself finally decide that the Montgomery property belonged to or did not belong to the late Ema Mudawini, but the question of inheritance had to be determined according to the law applicable at the time of Wilson's death.
    Held, further, that section 68F of the Administration of Estates Act did not assist the plaintiffs because Wilson died before the relevant statutory regime came into force.
    Held, further, that the late Ema, as second wife under the applicable pre-amendment customary-law succession position, could not inherit the immovable property.
    Held, further, that fraud in the sale price and transfer to the 5th and 6th defendants was not proved.
    Held, further, that the purchasers' title was not tainted and they were entitled to eviction of the plaintiffs and those claiming through them.
    Held, further, that the purchasers had not established a basis for holding-over or occupational damages.
ratio_decidendi: The decisive reason for refusing the plaintiffs' claim was that the applicable succession law was the law in force at Wilson's death in 1994 and that law did not entitle Ema Mudawini to inherit the immovable property; no fraud or invalidity in the sale to the 5th and 6th defendants was proved. Because the purchasers held registered title and the plaintiffs had no enforceable right to remain, eviction followed, but damages were refused for want of an adequate legal basis.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

    Nyathi and Another v Ncube & Others 2011 (2) ZLR 156 (H)

document_name: The Prosecutor General of Zimbabwe v Smith (198 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 198 (21 July 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE PROSECUTOR GENERAL OF ZIMBABWE Versus LYLE SMITH
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: The Prosecutor General of Zimbabwe v Smith (198 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 198 (21 July 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
    Bail
    Suspension of bail pending appeal
    Section 121(3) Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act
    Urgent chamber applications
    Certificate of urgency
    Extradition
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – bail granted pending trial – Prosecutor General noting appeal and applying under section 121(3) to suspend release – certificate of urgency by legal practitioner employed by National Prosecuting Authority valid – respondent previously extradited from Tanzania – reasonable possibility that interests of justice may be defeated if released before appeal – bail order suspended.
headnote: After the High Court granted Lyle Smith bail pending trial, the Prosecutor General sought suspension of that order under section 121(3) of the Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act pending appeal. The respondent challenged the certificate of urgency because it had been signed by a legal practitioner employed by the National Prosecuting Authority. The Court rejected the point, holding that the practitioner was a registered legal practitioner and was not barred from certifying urgency. On the merits, the Court accepted that the respondent had been extradited to Zimbabwe and that if he absconded after release the State would have no adequate remedy. The balance of convenience favoured suspension. The order granting bail was suspended pending appeal.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the certificate of urgency was valid when signed by a National Prosecuting Authority legal practitioner.
    Whether section 121(3) requirements for suspension of a bail order were met.
    Whether there was a reasonable possibility that the interests of justice would be defeated by release before the appeal.
    Whether the respondent's extradition history justified continued detention pending appeal.
legal_principles: Rule 60(4) of the High Court Rules, 2021 does not bar a legal practitioner from the same office or institution as the applicant from certifying urgency, although independent certification may be desirable. Under section 121(3) of the Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act, after the Prosecutor General or Public Prosecutor notes an appeal against bail, the court may suspend the decision to admit the accused to bail if satisfied that there is a reasonable possibility that the interests of justice may be defeated by release before the appeal is determined.
held_decision_points:
Held, that a registered legal practitioner employed by the National Prosecuting Authority could validly sign the certificate of urgency.
    Held, further, that the fact that the respondent had been extradited to Zimbabwe was material.
    Held, further, that if the respondent absconded after release the State would not have an adequate remedy and extradition would be lengthy and costly.
    Held, further, that the basis for suspension under section 121(3) was established.
    Held, further, that the order granting bail should be suspended pending appeal.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that where an accused who had previously been extradited is granted bail and the Prosecutor General appeals, there is a reasonable possibility that release before appeal may defeat the interests of justice because abscondment would leave the State without an effective remedy.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court stated that the law on who may sign a certificate of urgency is now settled: the rules do not forbid a legal practitioner from the same firm or institution from signing, though independent certification is desirable where possible.
cases_referred_to:
Chafanza v Edgars Stores & Anor HB 27-2005
    Mudekunye and Ors v Mudekunye & Ors HB 190-2002
legislation_considered:
Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], section 121(3)
    High Court Rules, 2021, rule 60(4), rule 60(6)
subject_area_terms: Section 121(3) Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act

document_name: Bonda and Another v Jr Goddard Contracting (Pvt) Ltd (199 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 199 (21 July 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: ABISHAI BONDA and Another v JR GODDARD CONTRACTING (PVT) LTD
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: Bonda and Another v Jr Goddard Contracting (Pvt) Ltd (199 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 199 (21 July 2022)
subject_area:
Labour law
Practice and procedure
Declaratory relief
Employment contracts
catchwords_flynote: Labour law – mutual separation agreements – application for declaratur – alleged illegality and unenforceability – Labour Act – duress, undue influence and quantification already disposed of as inappropriate for application proceedings – material disputes of fact – wrong forum and wrong procedure – application dismissed.
headnote: The applicants sought a declarator that mutual separation agreements signed with the respondent were null, void and unenforceable, and sought reinstatement. Earlier preliminary rulings had disposed of alternative relief relating to quantification and had held that allegations of duress and undue influence could not be determined in the application. The remaining issue was whether the agreements were unlawful for want of compliance with the Labour Act. The Court held that the applicants’ argument on illegality relied on the same factual issues already found unsuitable for determination in a declaratory application, including notice, explanation of rights, duress, undue influence and calculation of remuneration. The applicants had approached the wrong court and used the wrong procedure. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the mutual separation agreements could be declared unlawful and null for non-compliance with the Labour Act.
Whether the remaining issue could be determined by declaratory application after the preliminary rulings.
Whether the factual disputes and quantification questions belonged before the Labour Court or National Employment Council rather than the High Court in declaratory proceedings.
legal_principles: Declaratory relief is not appropriate where the decisive issues are factual disputes that cannot be resolved on affidavits. The High Court will not use a declaratur to quantify remuneration or determine fact-heavy labour disputes already held to be unsuitable for application proceedings. A litigant must use the correct forum and procedure for relief connected to labour quantification and factual allegations such as duress or undue influence.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the remaining illegality argument was not divorced from the factual matters already found unsuitable for determination in the application.
Held, further, that the alleged non-compliance with the Labour Act depended on disputed facts and quantification issues that the Court could not resolve by declaratur.
Held, further, that the applicants had approached the wrong court on the wrong platform for the relief sought.
Held, further, that the application should be dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The application failed because the applicants did not identify an independent legal basis on which the mutual separation agreements contravened the Labour Act; their case rested on disputed factual allegations and quantification issues that were inappropriate for resolution by declaratory application in the High Court.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to: None identified
legislation_considered:
Labour Act
Collective Bargaining Agreement referred to as CBA

document_name: Sibanda v G & G Preserves (Pvt) Ltd and Another (20 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 20 (20 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THEMBA SIBANDA Versus G & G PRESERVES (PVT) LTD And SHERIFF OF THE HIGH COURT N.O
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: Sibanda v G & G Preserves (Pvt) Ltd and Another (20 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 20 (20 January 2022)
subject_area:
Civil procedure
    Urgent chamber applications
    Interim interdicts
    Stay of execution
    Mining law
    Execution of judgments
    Withdrawal of chamber applications
catchwords_flynote: Civil procedure – urgent chamber application – stay of eviction pending return date – earlier chamber application withdrawn after judge raised query but before determination – no dismissal or final order in earlier matter – fresh application competent – applicant later obtained certificate of registration authorising mining on land – prima facie right and irreparable harm established – interim stay of eviction granted.
headnote: The applicant sought urgent interim relief staying execution of a writ of eviction arising from HC 1430/17 and restraining the Sheriff from ejecting him from the remaining extent of Lot B Lower Rangemore, Umguza. Although he had previously lacked lawful authority to occupy the land, after the earlier eviction judgment and appeal he obtained an order compelling the Provincial Mining Director to issue a certificate of registration. The Court held that an earlier urgent application had not been heard or dismissed because only a query had been raised and the matter had been withdrawn before determination. The present application was therefore properly before the Court. The applicant had acted promptly, had shown a prima facie right based on the certificate of registration, and faced irreparable harm if evicted. Interim relief was granted pending the return date.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the present application was barred by an earlier similar urgent application.
    Whether the earlier application had been heard or dismissed, or merely queried and withdrawn.
    Whether the matter was urgent.
    Whether the applicant established the requirements for interim interdictory relief.
    Whether the newly issued certificate of registration established a prima facie right pending the return date.
legal_principles: A judge may raise queries in determining the fate of a chamber application, but a query is not a final determination or order. The rules do not preclude withdrawal of a matter after a judicial query, nor do they bar a fresh application addressing the query. A matter is urgent where, when the need to act arises, it cannot wait. Interim interdictory relief requires a prima facie right, apprehension of irreparable harm, absence of another satisfactory remedy, and balance of convenience favouring relief.
held_decision_points:
Held, that HC 1925/21 had not been heard or dismissed; there was only a query and the matter had been withdrawn.
    Held, further, that there was no legal impediment to filing a fresh application addressing the query.
    Held, further, that the applicant acted promptly after learning of the intended eviction.
    Held, further, that the certificate of registration issued after HC 1430/17 and the Supreme Court decision established a prima facie right.
    Held, further, that eviction would cause irreparable harm and the balance of convenience favoured interim relief.
    Held, further, that the writ of eviction should be stayed pending the return date.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that because the applicant had acquired a new and extant certificate of registration after the eviction judgment, and because the earlier urgent application had not been determined, the applicant was entitled to interim protection pending ventilation of the validity and effect of the certificate on the return date.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that it is permissible for a litigant to withdraw a matter after a judicial query and to file a fresh application addressing the query.

    Documents Support Centre P/L v Mapuvire 2006 (2) ZLR 240
    Gold Reef Mining (Pty) Ltd v Mnjiva Consulting Engineers (Pty) Ltd & Anor HH-631-15

    Mines and Minerals Act [Chapter 21:05]

document_name: S v Dlodlo (200 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 200 (21 July 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: CHRISTPHER DLODLO v THE STATE
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
case_number:
HCB 239/22
   XREF CRB P ACC 09/22
   HCA 70/22
citation: S v Dlodlo (200 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 200 (21 July 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
    Bail pending appeal
    Bribery
    Sentence appeal
    Community service
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – bail pending appeal – conviction on plea of guilty to bribery – appeal against sentence only – effective custodial sentence within community-service range – prospects of success on sentence appeal – risk of abscondment not argued by State – bail pending appeal granted.
headnote: The applicant was convicted on his own plea of guilty to bribery after offering R50 to a police officer at a roadblock. He was sentenced to 30 months' imprisonment, with three months suspended. He appealed against sentence and sought bail pending appeal. The Court accepted that corruption offences are serious but held that each case must be considered on its own merits. It noted that the State did not argue risk of abscondment, that appeals take time, and that there was a real possibility that the applicant could serve a substantial part or all of his sentence before the appeal was heard. Because there were reasonable prospects of success on sentence, the application was granted in terms of the draft order.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant had reasonable prospects of success in his sentence appeal.
    Whether there was a risk of abscondment.
    Whether the likely delay before the appeal justified release on bail pending appeal.
    Whether the sentencing court may have misdirected itself by failing to consider community service.
legal_principles: In bail pending appeal, the Court considers the likelihood of abscondment, prospects of success on appeal, the right to liberty and likely delay before the appeal is heard. Corruption offences are serious, but seriousness alone does not end the inquiry; each case must be assessed on its own facts. Where an effective sentence may be served before the appeal is heard and prospects of success are reasonable, bail may be granted.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the State had not argued that there was a risk of abscondment.
    Held, further, that appeals take time and the applicant might serve a greater portion or the entirety of his sentence before the appeal was heard.
    Held, further, that there were reasonable prospects of success in the sentence appeal.
    Held, further, that the applicant was a suitable candidate for bail pending appeal.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the combination of reasonable prospects of success on sentence, absence of a demonstrated abscondment risk and possible completion of sentence before appeal justified bail pending appeal.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to: S v Williams 1980 ZLR 466 (SC)

document_name: ZONDOS~1.DOC
case_name: ZONDO SYNDICATE Versus PROVINCIAL MINING DIRECTOR MATABELELAND NORTH PROVINCE And SECRETARY FOR MINES & MINING DEVELOPMENT And FUNDISI PARK (PVT) LTD
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
subject_area:
Administrative law
    Mining law
    Review proceedings
    Legal practitioners' renunciation and assumption of agency
    Heads of argument
    Natural justice
    Constitutional administrative justice
catchwords_flynote: Administrative law – review of decision suspending mining operations – respondent's legal practitioners renouncing agency then purporting to file heads without assuming agency – heads a nullity and expunged – mining suspension decision set aside for want of compliance with Mines and Minerals Act, Administrative Justice Act and constitutional rights to administrative justice and fair hearing.
headnote: The applicant sought review of the Provincial Mining Director's decision suspending mining operations at Fundisi H mining claim. The third respondent had filed opposition, but its former legal practitioners later renounced agency and nonetheless purported to file heads of argument without first assuming agency. The Court held that the heads of argument were a nullity and expunged them from the record. The review application was granted. The decision suspending the applicant's mining operations was set aside for want of compliance with section 354(2) of the Mines and Minerals Act, section 3(2) of the Administrative Justice Act, and sections 68(1) and 69 of the Constitution.
issues_for_determination:
Whether heads of argument filed by former legal practitioners after renunciation of agency were valid.
    Whether the third respondent was properly before the Court on heads of argument.
    Whether the impugned mining suspension decision should be reviewed and set aside.
    Whether the decision complied with the Mines and Minerals Act, the Administrative Justice Act and constitutional fair administrative justice requirements.
legal_principles: Heads of argument must be filed by a party's legal practitioners of record. Where legal practitioners renounce agency, steps taken by them thereafter are of no force and effect unless they properly resume or assume agency. A decision affecting mining operations must comply with statutory and constitutional requirements for administrative justice and fair hearing, including the relevant provisions of the Mines and Minerals Act and Administrative Justice Act.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the third respondent's former legal practitioners had renounced agency and had no mandate to file heads of argument.
    Held, further, that the purported heads of argument were a nullity and had to be expunged.
    Held, further, that the application for review was properly before the Court.
    Held, further, that the decision suspending the applicant's mining operations was made without compliance with statutory and constitutional requirements.
    Held, further, that the decision had to be set aside and discharged.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the third respondent had no valid heads of argument because they were filed by legal practitioners who had renounced agency, and in the absence of a proper procedural answer the review succeeded because the impugned suspension failed to comply with mandatory administrative-justice requirements.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to: Haishid v Masomela and Ors HH-255-22

    Mines and Minerals Act [Chapter 21:05], section 354(2), section 354(7)

document_name: Acumec PBC v Zimbabwe Revenue Auth (203 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 203 (28 July 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: ACUMEC PBC v ZIMBABWE REVENUE AUTHORITY
court: IN THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: Acumec PBC v Zimbabwe Revenue Auth (203 of 2022) [2022] ZWBHC 203 (28 July 2022)
subject_area:
Tax and revenue
Customs and excise
Practice and procedure
Prescription
Declaratory relief
catchwords_flynote: Customs and excise – seizure and forfeiture of vehicle – application framed as declaratory relief – recovery of seized goods – section 193(12) of Customs and Excise Act – three-month prescriptive period – communications with Commissioner not suspending prescription – claim for release of seized vehicle barred.
headnote: The applicant sought a declaratory order that the respondent’s decision to forfeit its Mercedes Benz truck was premature, unlawful and void, and an order for the immediate release of the vehicle. The respondent raised a preliminary objection that the proceedings were prescribed because the relief, although framed as a declarator, was in substance a claim for recovery of seized goods. The vehicle had been seized after being used to ferry uncustomed goods through an undesignated entry point. The Court held that any claim for release of goods seized by the Commissioner had to be instituted within the three-month period prescribed by section 193(12) of the Customs and Excise Act. The application was filed about fifteen months after the seizure. The point in limine was upheld and the application dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the application, although framed as a declaratory order, was in substance a claim for release of seized goods.
Whether the application was barred by section 193(12) of the Customs and Excise Act because it was brought outside the prescribed three-month period.
Whether correspondence with the Commissioner suspended or avoided prescription.
legal_principles: Where goods have been seized under the Customs and Excise Act, proceedings for recovery of the goods or compensation must be instituted within the period prescribed by section 193(12). A party cannot avoid the statutory prescriptive period by framing the relief as a declarator when the substance of the relief is recovery or release of seized goods. Communication with the Commissioner does not suspend the operation of the statutory prescriptive period unless the statute provides otherwise.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the application was in substance an attempt to obtain release of a seized vehicle even though it was framed as declaratory relief.
Held, further, that section 193(12) of the Customs and Excise Act required proceedings for release of seized goods to be instituted within three months of notice of seizure.
Held, further, that the applicant had brought the proceedings about fifteen months after seizure and was barred from doing so.
Held, further, that the Court did not need to consider the merits of the forfeiture.
ratio_decidendi: The application failed because the relief sought was the release of a seized vehicle and, under section 193(12) of the Customs and Excise Act, such proceedings had to be instituted within three months; the applicant filed the application well outside that statutory period.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
