document_name: S v Mada (B 228_22; HCC 30-22; Ref Case No. CRB_ CNHR 01_22) [2022] ZWCHHC 3 (6 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: JOHN MADA versus THE STATE
case_number:
B 228/22
Ref Case No. CRB: CNHR 01/22
citation: S v Mada (B 228_22; HCC 30-22; Ref Case No. CRB_ CNHR 01_22) [2022] ZWCHHC 3 (6 October 2022)
judges:
BACHI –MZAWAZI
Judgment by: BACHI MZAWAZI J
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
Bail pending appeal
Armed robbery
Circumstantial evidence
Prospects of success on appeal
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – bail pending appeal – armed robbery conviction – applicant convicted on circumstantial evidence after being found with stolen goods – innocent taxi-driver explanation – prospects of success on appeal – circumstantial evidence must exclude every reasonable inference other than guilt – bail pending appeal granted in terms of draft order
headnote:
The applicant sought bail pending appeal after noting an appeal against conviction and sentence for armed robbery under s 126(1)(a) of the Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act. The trial court had convicted him principally on circumstantial evidence, namely that he was found with part of the stolen property soon after the robbery. The applicant contended that the proved facts did not exclude the reasonable inference that he was an innocent taxi driver hired to ferry goods. The State opposed bail, relying on the seriousness of the offence, the sentence imposed and the alleged absence of prospects of success.

The Court reviewed the principles governing circumstantial evidence and bail pending appeal. It noted that a conviction on circumstantial evidence is competent only where the proved facts are consistent with guilt and exclude every reasonable inference except guilt. The record showed that the trial court had explicitly distanced the applicant from the scene of the robbery and that his taxi-driver explanation had not been effectively rebutted. The Court found that the applicant had prospects of success on appeal and an arguable case. It also held that abscondment risks could be mitigated by stringent bail conditions. Bail pending appeal was therefore granted in terms of the draft order.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant had prospects of success on appeal against conviction based on circumstantial evidence.
Whether the proved facts excluded the reasonable inference that the applicant was an innocent taxi driver hired to ferry goods.
Whether the applicant discharged the onus for bail pending appeal.
Whether risk of abscondment outweighed the applicant’s liberty interests and prospects of success.
legal_principles:
A conviction may be based on circumstantial evidence, but the proved facts must be consistent with guilt and must exclude every reasonable inference other than guilt.
Circumstantial evidence must form a complete chain leading inescapably to guilt and be incapable of explanation on any hypothesis other than guilt.
In bail pending appeal, the applicant no longer enjoys the presumption of innocence and bears the onus to show that he is a proper candidate for bail.
Prospects of success on appeal are a highly material factor, but the court must balance those prospects with the interests of the administration of justice and the applicant’s liberty.
Abscondment alone is not necessarily decisive where stringent bail conditions can mitigate the risk.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant had been convicted on circumstantial evidence and that the trial court had accepted that there was no direct evidence placing him at the scene.

Held, further, that the applicant’s explanation that he was a taxi driver hired to ferry goods had not been effectively rebutted by the State evidence.

Held, further, that the inference of innocence had not been excluded and the applicant therefore had prospects of success on appeal.

Held, further, that although the applicant bore the onus in bail pending appeal, he had shown an arguable case and was a proper candidate for bail.

Held, further, that the application for bail pending appeal succeeded in terms of the draft order.
ratio_decidendi: Bail pending appeal was granted because the circumstantial evidence supporting the conviction did not appear to exclude the reasonable inference that the applicant was an innocent taxi driver hired to transport goods, giving him prospects of success on appeal and an arguable case despite the seriousness of the offence.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: DUNHUR~1.DOC
case_name: DUNHURAMAMBO (PVT) LTD T/A ZAMBEZI CRUISE & SAFARI Versus AVALON STEEL PROJECTS & CONSTRUCTION And CHARLES NIKOBASA
subject_area:
Civil procedure
Urgent chamber applications
Attachment ad fundandam jurisdictionem
Peregrini
Ex parte applications
Non-disclosure
catchwords_flynote: Civil procedure – ex parte urgent chamber application – attachment ad fundandam jurisdictionem – attachment of houseboat to found jurisdiction against peregrinus – final order originally sought ex parte – amended provisional order – urgency – property alleged to belong to intended foreign defendant – applicant required to show prima facie ownership by respondent – material non-disclosure and misrepresentation – application dismissed with attorney-client costs
headnote:
The applicant brought an ex parte urgent chamber application for attachment of a houseboat known as The Royal Nikobasa to found jurisdiction for an anticipated pecuniary claim against the first respondent, a peregrinus domiciled in South Africa. It also sought leave to serve process by email. The Court queried why a final order was sought ex parte and the applicant amended the draft order to a provisional order. The second respondent was joined and opposed the application, contending that The Royal Nikobasa belonged to him, not the first respondent.

The Court accepted that urgency had been established because there was a prima facie apprehension that the houseboat might change hands and the applicant had acted promptly. On the merits, however, the Court held that attachment ad fundandam jurisdictionem requires proof, at least prima facie, that the property to be attached belongs to the peregrinus respondent and is within the jurisdiction. The applicant’s founding affidavit relied on facts concerning another boat, the Transcruiser, and did not prove that the first respondent owned, possessed or controlled The Royal Nikobasa. The second respondent’s material showed that the first respondent was a manufacturer and that the applicant knew of the second respondent’s interest. The applicant had failed to disclose material facts in an ex parte application and had attempted to obtain relief prejudicial to the second respondent in his absence. The application was dismissed with costs on the attorney and client scale.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the matter was urgent.
Whether the applicant was entitled to proceed ex parte and to seek attachment ad fundandam jurisdictionem.
Whether the applicant established on a prima facie basis that The Royal Nikobasa belonged to the first respondent.
Whether the applicant failed to disclose material facts or misrepresented the ownership and manufacturing history of the houseboat.
Whether punitive costs were justified.
legal_principles:
Urgency is established where imminent harm threatens a right, irreparable harm will be suffered if the court does not intervene immediately, and the applicant treated the matter as urgent when the need to act arose.
A court lacks personal jurisdiction over a peregrinus domiciled and resident outside the country unless jurisdiction is founded or confirmed by attachment of property within the court’s jurisdiction.
For attachment ad fundandam jurisdictionem, the applicant must show prima facie that the intended defendant is a peregrinus, that the applicant has a pecuniary claim and intends to sue, and that the property to be attached belongs to that respondent and is within the jurisdiction.
An ex parte application requires utmost good faith and full disclosure of all material facts. Courts discourage urgent applications characterised by material non-disclosure, mala fides or dishonesty.
An application stands or falls on the founding affidavit; a new case cannot be made in an answering affidavit.
held_decision_points:
Held, that urgency was established because the applicant had acted after learning of a possible disposal and the second respondent’s steps to register the houseboat gave credence to the apprehension that the property might change hands.

Held, further, that the applicant had to show on a prima facie basis that the houseboat sought to be attached belonged to the first respondent.

Held, further, that the applicant failed to establish ownership by the first respondent. Its reliance on HC6954/20 was misplaced because that matter concerned the Transcruiser, not The Royal Nikobasa.

Held, further, that the applicant deliberately failed to disclose material facts concerning the second respondent’s interest and the manufacturing arrangement, and its case was based on speculation, misrepresentation and an attempt to snatch at a judgment.

Held, further, that the application had to be dismissed with costs on an attorney and client scale.
ratio_decidendi: The decisive reason for dismissal was that the applicant did not establish the essential requirement that the property to be attached belonged to the peregrinus first respondent, and in an ex parte context it had materially failed to disclose known facts showing the second respondent’s interest in the property.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court stated that litigants who misrepresent facts or attempt to snatch judgments in urgent or ex parte proceedings should be discouraged and may be visited with punitive costs.

document_name: S v Kasiyabvuma (CON08_22; HCC03_22) [2022] ZWCHHC 5 (30 March 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: TRAVOLTA TAKUDZWA KASIYABVUMA versus THE STATE
citation: S v Kasiyabvuma (CON08_22; HCC03_22) [2022] ZWCHHC 5 (30 March 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
Condonation
Reinstatement of appeal
Bail pending appeal
Contempt of court
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – chamber application – condonation for late filing of appeal and reinstatement of appeal – previous appeal dismissed for want of prosecution – bail pending appeal lapsing on dismissal of appeal – applicant required to submit to custody before seeking reinstatement – dirty hands principle in criminal proceedings – application struck off until contempt purged
headnote:
The applicant sought condonation for late filing of an appeal and reinstatement of an appeal. He had been convicted on his plea of guilty on two Road Traffic Act counts, namely driving without a driver’s licence and negligent driving. He noted an appeal against sentence and was granted bail pending appeal. The appeal was set down but dismissed on 7 March 2022 for want of prosecution. In the present application, he explained that his legal practitioner had suffered a breakdown on the hearing date and addressed prospects of success.

The State opposed the application on the basis that the applicant’s bail pending appeal had lapsed when the appeal was dismissed. The Court held that bail pending appeal subsists only pending determination of the appeal; once an appeal is dismissed, the appellant’s status reverts to the position after conviction and sentence, and he must submit to custody before seeking to resuscitate the appeal. The Court applied the same principle as the dirty hands doctrine and held that it would be irresponsible to hear an applicant who had not complied with the law. The merits were not considered and the application was struck off until the applicant purged his contempt by submitting to custody.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the Court could hear an application for condonation and reinstatement of an appeal where the appeal had previously been dismissed and the applicant had not submitted to custody.
Whether bail pending appeal lapsed automatically on dismissal of the appeal.
Whether the applicant had to purge contempt before the merits of the application could be considered.
legal_principles:
Bail pending appeal granted under s 123 of the Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07] operates only while the appeal is pending.
When an appeal is dismissed, bail pending appeal automatically terminates and the appellant’s status reverts to the status quo ante.
An appellant whose appeal has been dismissed must submit to custody before applying to resuscitate the appeal or seek further bail.
A court may decline audience to a litigant who has not complied with the law; the principle is analogous to the dirty hands doctrine in civil matters.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant’s bail pending appeal automatically terminated when his appeal was dismissed for want of prosecution on 7 March 2022.

Held, further, that there was no legal basis for the applicant to bring the application from home without first submitting to custody.

Held, further, that the Court could not responsibly give audience to an applicant who had not complied with the law.

Held, further, that because the merits had not been considered, the matter would not be dismissed but struck off until the applicant purged his contempt.
ratio_decidendi: The Court struck the matter off because the dismissal of the prior appeal terminated bail pending appeal, leaving the applicant obliged to submit to custody before seeking condonation or reinstatement; without purging that contempt, the application was improperly before the Court.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], s 123

document_name: S v Mararahanda (B74_22; HCC04_22) [2022] ZWCHHC 4 (28 April 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: KELVIN MARARAHANDA versus THE STATE
citation: S v Mararahanda (B74_22; HCC04_22) [2022] ZWCHHC 4 (28 April 2022)
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
Bail pending appeal
Stock theft
Identification evidence
Appeal and review
Police misconduct
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – bail pending appeal – applicant convicted of stock theft and sentenced to mandatory nine years’ imprisonment – leave to appeal granted – prospects of success – appeal grounds raising review-type procedural complaints and identification – distinction between appeal and review – no prospects of success – bail refused – registrar directed to serve judgment on Officer in Charge CID Chinhoyi regarding police assault causing applicant’s paraplegia
headnote:
The applicant applied for bail pending appeal after conviction for stock theft under s 114 2(a)(i) of the Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act and imposition of the mandatory nine-year sentence. He had been granted leave to appeal in 2022. Before dealing with the merits, the Court recorded that the applicant appeared bedridden and paraplegic, and that correspondence in the record indicated he had sustained a spinal cord injury after being taken from prison by police for interviews and assaulted. The Court expressed concern that nothing appeared to have been done about the complaint and directed that the judgment be served on the Officer in Charge CID Chinhoyi.

On the bail application, the Court held that after conviction the presumption of innocence falls away, and the applicant bears the onus under s 115(c)(2)(b) of the Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act to show on a balance of probabilities that he is a proper candidate for bail. The grounds of appeal raised procedural complaints more appropriate to review, and the remaining identification grounds had no prospects because the trial court had properly considered the evidence of identification. The application was dismissed.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant discharged the onus for bail pending appeal.
Whether the grounds of appeal disclosed prospects of success.
Whether procedural complaints about advice on legal representation and assistance during cross-examination were properly appeal grounds or review grounds.
Whether the trial court misdirected itself on identification evidence.
Whether the Court should direct attention to the applicant’s alleged police assault and resulting paraplegia.
legal_principles:
After conviction and sentence, the presumption of innocence under s 70 of the Constitution is no longer applicable, and admission to bail is no longer a constitutional right under s 50 but arises from the limited instances in s 123(i)(b)(ii) of the Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act.
The applicant for bail pending appeal bears the onus under s 115(c)(2)(b) of the Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act to show on a balance of probabilities that he is a proper candidate for bail.
Prospects of success are a highly determinant factor; where there are no prospects of success, bail pending appeal should not be granted.
Appeal and review are different procedures: an appeal impugns the merits, while review addresses procedural irregularities and is generally remedied by remittal or redress rather than ordinary appellate relief.
Evidence not challenged in cross-examination may be accepted as admitted.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant bore the onus to show that he was a proper candidate for bail pending appeal because the presumption of innocence no longer applied after conviction and sentence.

Held, further, that the procedural grounds relating to legal representation and cross-examination were review issues and were improperly before the Court as appeal grounds.

Held, further, that the trial court properly addressed the identification evidence and warned itself of the dangers of visual identification.

Held, further, that the remaining grounds of appeal had no prospects of success.

Held, further, that bail pending appeal had to be dismissed and that the judgment should be served on the Officer in Charge CID Chinhoyi to address the police assault issues raised.
ratio_decidendi: The bail application failed because the applicant’s grounds of appeal did not establish prospects of success: the procedural complaints were review-type grounds and the trial court had properly dealt with the identification evidence.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court strongly criticised the apparent police assault that resulted in the applicant’s paraplegia and expressed the hope that the Officer in Charge CID Chinhoyi would seriously consider the issue.

document_name: S v Zhakata (CRB67_22; HCC32-22) [2022] ZWCHHC 1 (26 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE versus GABRIEL ZHAKATA
citation: S v Zhakata (CRB67_22; HCC32-22) [2022] ZWCHHC 1 (26 October 2022)
judges:
MUZOFA J,
Assessors: Mrs Mawoneke; Mr Manyangadze
subject_area:
Criminal law
Murder
Constructive intent
Self-defence
Sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – accused assaulting deceased with log after previous assault by deceased and companions – defence of self-defence – requirements under s 253 of Criminal Law Code – partial defence under s 254 – accused entitled to defend himself initially but means became disproportionate after threat ceased – vengeance rather than self-defence – constructive intent – sentence of ten years’ imprisonment
headnote:
The accused was charged with murder after assaulting the deceased with a log, causing fatal head injuries. The accused raised self-defence. The evidence showed that the previous night the deceased and his companions had assaulted the accused after an altercation involving a girl at a drinking place. The next morning, while searching for his belongings, the accused encountered the deceased and another member of the group. The Court accepted that the accused had been bullied and assaulted the previous night and that he reasonably believed an attack was imminent when Robert and later the deceased approached him.

The Court held that self-defence requires that an unlawful attack be underway or imminent, that the defensive conduct be necessary, and that the means used be reasonable. The accused was entitled to take some pre-emptive action, and had no absolute duty to run away. However, after the first blow the deceased fell down and the threat ceased. The accused continued to assault him with a log while he lay helpless, and admitted that the further blows were inspired by anger and vengeance. The means used were disproportionate and self-defence failed. Because the accused was intoxicated and the killing was not premeditated, the Court found constructive rather than actual intention. The accused was convicted of murder with constructive intent and sentenced to ten years’ imprisonment.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the accused acted in self-defence.
Whether an attack was underway or imminent when the accused assaulted the deceased.
Whether the accused’s conduct was necessary to avert the attack.
Whether the means used were reasonable and proportionate in the circumstances.
Whether the accused should be convicted of murder with actual intent, murder with constructive intent, or a lesser offence.
What sentence was appropriate.
legal_principles:
Self-defence is a complete defence under s 253(1)(a)-(d) of the Criminal Law Code where the accused is under attack or an attack is imminent, the conduct is necessary to avert the attack, the means used are reasonable, and the harm is directed at the attacker and is not grossly disproportionate.
The test has a subjective component as to the accused’s perception and an objective component as to the reasonableness of the means used.
A court must not take an armchair approach and must put itself in the accused’s position.
There is no absolute duty to retreat where a person is under attack.
Under s 254 of the Criminal Law Code, self-defence may operate as a partial defence where the requirements are satisfied except that the means used were unreasonable.
Where an accused foresees a real risk or possibility that conduct may cause death and continues despite that risk, constructive intent is established.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the accused assaulted the deceased with the log produced in court and caused the injuries resulting in death.

Held, further, that the Court accepted the accused’s evidence that the deceased and his companions had assaulted him the previous night and that Robert and the deceased approached him menacingly the following morning.

Held, further, that the accused was initially entitled to take defensive action and had no absolute duty to run away.

Held, further, that once the deceased fell after the first blow the threat had ceased, and the continued assault was inspired by anger and vengeance rather than self-defence.

Held, further, that the means used were disproportionate and self-defence was not available as a complete defence.

Held, further, that the accused did not form actual intention to kill but realised the real risk that his conduct might cause death and continued regardless; he was guilty of murder with constructive intention.

Held, further, that the appropriate sentence was ten years’ imprisonment.
ratio_decidendi: The accused was convicted because, although he initially faced an imminent attack, he continued to strike the deceased after the threat had ceased, using disproportionate force out of vengeance and foreseeing the real possibility of death.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court expressed disquiet that a 15-year-old girl was carousing in bottle stores with much older men and observed that at her age she was supposed to be at school.

R v Grigor (2012) ZASCA 95
S v Chitate HH267/21

Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], s 314

document_name: Nhowe v Gondo and Others (HC 2840_22; HH 711-22) [2022] ZWCHHC 2 (14 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: NTOMBIZODWA NHOWE versus MONICA GONDO and SHINGIRIRAI FIBION NHOWE and CITY OF HARARE and REGISTRAR OF DEEDS
citation: Nhowe v Gondo and Others (HC 2840_22; HH 711-22) [2022] ZWCHHC 2 (14 October 2022)
subject_area:
Civil procedure
Res judicata
Property law
Deeds and transfer
Divorce and matrimonial property
catchwords_flynote: Civil procedure – opposed application – special plea of res judicata – cancellation of deed of transfer under Deeds Registries Act – prior High Court declarator and Supreme Court appeal concerning same property – same parties, cause of action and relief – point in limine upheld – each party to bear own costs
headnote:
The applicant sought cancellation of Deed of Transfer No. 7167/18 in favour of the first respondent over Stand 40715 Harare Township and revival of a prior deed relating to Stand 13687 Salisbury Township. The dispute arose from a divorce order in which the applicant had been awarded an immovable property and subsequent proceedings concerning the sale and transfer of a subdivided property. The first respondent raised a special plea of res judicata, contending that the applicant had already litigated the same issue in HC 3715/19, where judgment in the applicant’s favour had been set aside on appeal by the Supreme Court.

The Court held that res judicata prevents relitigation of the same or similar issues between the same parties where there has been a final judgment on the merits. The requirements are the same parties, same cause of action and same relief. The Court found that the earlier case and the present application involved substantially the same parties, the same cause of action arising from the sale and transfer of the property, and the same effective relief despite differences in wording. The matter was therefore res judicata. The point in limine was upheld and each party was ordered to bear its own costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the present application was barred by res judicata.
Whether the parties, cause of action and relief were the same as, or substantially the same as, those in HC 3715/19.
Whether the applicant had shown that the relief now sought differed from the relief determined in the previous proceedings.
legal_principles:
Res judicata bars a matter already adjudged by a competent court from being pursued further between the same parties.
The special plea is declinatory and is intended to put an end to proceedings.
The requirements are the same parties, the same cause of action and the same relief sought.
A previous judgment is res judicata only as regards matters between the parties which that judgment actually affects.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the earlier matter and the present application involved the same parties for res judicata purposes, notwithstanding the earlier removal of a party without legal rights over the property.

Held, further, that the cause of action in both matters arose from the sale and transfer of the same property by the second respondent to the first respondent.

Held, further, that the relief sought was effectively the same, even though the wording of the draft orders differed.

Held, further, that the application was res judicata and the point in limine had to be upheld.
ratio_decidendi: The application was barred because it sought, between substantially the same parties, relief arising from the same property transfer dispute that had already been determined in earlier proceedings and affected by the Supreme Court appeal.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court stated that litigation must come to finality and that courts should not be clogged with stale issues already dealt with.

document_name: MAFUND~1.DOC
case_name: Mafunda v The Zimbabwe East Annual Conference of the United Methodist Church of Zimbabwe and Another
subject_area: Administrative law; church governance; voluntary associations; procedural fairness; vote of no confidence.
catchwords_flynote: Administrative law – Administrative Justice Act – ecclesiastical body – removal of conference lay leader – vote of no confidence – whether internal church process was lawful, reasonable and procedurally fair – waiver – locus standi – voting by districts rather than individual delegates.
headnote: The applicant, the elected conference lay leader of the Zimbabwe East Annual Conference of the United Methodist Church of Zimbabwe, sought to set aside a vote of no confidence passed during a virtual annual meeting. He alleged that the process was unlawful and procedurally irregular under the Administrative Justice Act and the church’s Book of Discipline. The respondents raised waiver and locus standi objections, contending that the applicant had accepted the process and had left the church by joining the Wesleyan Covenant Association. The court found that he had not waived his rights, that the Wesleyan Covenant Association was not a church, and that the process used to remove him was not rooted in the Book of Discipline. It held that no proper charge or hearing had preceded the vote, the matter had not been properly placed on the agenda, and voting by administrative districts was irregular. The vote was set aside and the applicant was reinstated.
issues_for_determination: Whether the applicant waived his right to challenge the vote; whether he lacked locus standi because of alleged automatic resignation; whether the first respondent’s process complied with the Administrative Justice Act and the church’s Book of Discipline; whether the vote of no confidence was validly conducted.
legal_principles: An administrative authority or body exercising power affecting rights, interests or legitimate expectations must act lawfully, reasonably and fairly. Fair procedure requires notice, an opportunity to make representations, and compliance with the governing instrument. A removal process must be grounded in the applicable rules; a vote may not be substituted for a disciplinary process where the governing rules require or imply due process.
held_decision_points: The applicant had not waived his rights and retained locus standi. The WCA was not another church and his association with it did not amount to automatic resignation. The vote of no confidence was procedurally irregular and unsupported by the Book of Discipline. Removal by district vote was improper. The applicant proved his claim.
ratio_decidendi: Because the applicant’s removal affected his elected office and legitimate expectations, the first respondent had to comply with the Administrative Justice Act and its own Book of Discipline. The vote of no confidence had no proper procedural or legal basis, was not preceded by notice or a hearing, and was conducted through an irregular voting method; it therefore could not stand.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court observed that, absent a special majority requirement, voting should have been by simple majority of delegates present and voting, using recognized methods such as secret ballot or show of hands, not by administrative districts.
cases_referred_to: None identified in the extracted judgment text.
legislation_considered: Administrative Justice Act [Chapter 10:23]; Book of Discipline of the United Methodist Church.
reviewer_comments: 27. Approval status: Draft
subject_area_terms: Administrative law; church governance; voluntary associations; procedural fairness; vote of no confidence.
cases_referred_to_terms: None identified in the extracted judgment text.

document_name: KUBAKA~1(1).DOC
case_name: Kubakaidze Mining Syndicate v Minister of Mines and Mining Development and Others
subject_area: Civil appeal; mining law; defective notice of appeal; appellate jurisdiction; Mines and Minerals Act.
catchwords_flynote: Appeal – mining dispute – decision by Minister and Mining Commissioner – cancellation of mining certificate – need to identify statutory right of appeal – defective notice of appeal – appeal raising review issues – decision-makers cited as parties – punitive costs.
headnote: The appellant sought to challenge decisions arising from a mining dispute concerning Chigwell claims, including cancellation of its certificates of registration and suspension of mining activities. The third and fourth respondents raised numerous preliminary objections to the appeal, including failure to cite the statutory provision conferring a right of appeal, incompetence of appealing against both a first instance and appellate decision, citation of decision-makers as parties, raising review grounds in appeal proceedings, lateness, unclear grounds and incompetent relief. The court held that the appeal was fatally defective. It emphasized that appellate jurisdiction is statutory and that a party invoking it must identify the source of the right of appeal. The appeal was struck off the roll with costs on a legal practitioner and client scale because the defects had been drawn to counsel’s attention well before hearing.
issues_for_determination: Whether the appeal was properly before the High Court; whether the appellant had identified a statutory right of appeal; whether it was competent to appeal against the decisions cited; whether the relief sought and grounds of appeal were clear, focused and within appellate jurisdiction; whether punitive costs were warranted.
legal_principles: A right of appeal is statutory and must be grounded in an enactment. A notice of appeal should identify the statutory basis where the right of appeal is not inherent. Appeals and reviews are distinct procedures; review grounds cannot be improperly dressed as appeal grounds. Defective appeals may be struck off, and punitive costs may be awarded where defects are persisted in after being pointed out.
held_decision_points: The appeal was fatally defective. It did not properly identify the statutory provision relied upon, raised issues suitable for review, cited decision-makers as parties, sought incompetent relief, and left the dispute unresolved. It was struck off the roll with costs on a legal practitioner and client scale.
ratio_decidendi: The High Court could not exercise appellate jurisdiction in the absence of a properly invoked statutory right of appeal and a competent notice of appeal. The appellant’s amended appeal did not cure the foundational defects and the court could not reconstitute the matter for the appellant.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court criticized the manner in which counsel persisted with the appeal despite defects having been pointed out, justifying admonitory costs.
cases_referred_to: Bushu v GMB HH 326/17; Minister of Mines and Mining Development & Anor v Fidelity Printers & Refiners (Pvt) Ltd & Anor CCZ 9/22; Omarshah v Karasa 1996 (1) ZLR 584 (H); Tamanikwa & Ors v Zimbabwe Manpower Development Fund & Anor SC 73/17; other cases as cited in the full text.

document_name: Kuseri v Kuseri and Another (893 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 893 (21 December 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: Kuseri v Kuseri and Another
subject_area: Property law; rei vindicatio; eviction; family property dispute; alleged donation.
catchwords_flynote: Rei vindicatio – father and adult sons – title deed in plaintiff’s name – alleged verbal donation and employment-based entitlement – onus on possessor to prove right of retention – company separate legal personality.
headnote: The plaintiff, father of the two defendants, sought eviction of his adult sons from a property registered in his name. He had allowed them to live there out of love and affection, but withdrew consent after they sought a peace order barring him from the property. The defendants contended that the property had been promised or donated to them in lieu of salaries or work done for the plaintiff or his company. The court held that the plaintiff proved ownership and the defendants failed to prove donation, employment or any right of retention. It also emphasized the separate legal personality of the plaintiff’s company. The defendants and those claiming through them were ordered to vacate, with costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether the plaintiff owned the property; whether the defendants had any right of retention; whether an alleged promise or donation displaced the plaintiff’s vindicatory right; whether claims against a company could be asserted against the plaintiff’s personal property.
legal_principles: Under the actio rei vindicatio, the owner need only prove ownership and that the defendant is in possession. The onus then shifts to the possessor to prove a right of retention. A company has separate legal personality from its shareholders and directors.
held_decision_points: The plaintiff was the owner and the defendants were in possession without consent. They failed to establish donation, employment-based entitlement or any other right of retention. Eviction was granted.
ratio_decidendi: The plaintiff’s title deed established ownership, and the defendants’ unproved allegations of a verbal donation or unpaid employment benefits did not defeat the owner’s vindicatory right. Any alleged claim against a company could not be enforced by retaining the plaintiff’s personal property.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court observed that the family background was sad but could not displace settled property-law principles.
cases_referred_to: Lafarge Cement (Zimbabwe) Ltd v Chatizembwa HH 413/18; Mashave v Standard Bank of South Africa Ltd 1998 (1) ZLR 436 (S); Chetty v Naidoo 1974 (3) SA 13 (A); Oakland F Nominees (Pty) Ltd v Gelria Mining and Investment Co Ltd 1976 (1) SA 441 (A); Jolly v Shannon and Anor 1998 (1) ZLR 78 (H); Stanbic Finance Zimbabwe Ltd v Chivhungwa 1999 (1) ZLR 262 (H); Zavazava & Anor v Tendere 2015 (2) ZLR 394 (H).
legislation_considered: Common-law actio rei vindicatio; property law principles.

document_name: M. M. W (PVT) LTD v ZIMRA (31 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 31 (19 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: M. M. W (Pvt) Ltd v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority
court: Special Court for Income Tax Appeals, High Court of Zimbabwe, Harare
subject_area: Tax law; income tax deductions; charitable trusts; private voluntary organisations; penalties.
catchwords_flynote: Income tax – s 15(2)(r)(iii) – donation to private voluntary organisation – whether PVO capable of being a charitable trust – administration by Minister responsible for Social Welfare – contra fiscum rule – penalty.
headnote: The appellant made a US$400,000 payment to a registered private voluntary organisation, the I. Cultural Institute, and claimed it as a deductible donation under s 15(2)(r)(iii) of the Income Tax Act. ZIMRA disallowed the deduction, contending that the Institute was not a charitable trust administered by the Minister responsible for Social Welfare, and imposed a 60% penalty. The court held that a private voluntary organisation may be a trust for the purposes of the Act where the substance of the arrangement shows separation of control and benefit and charitable objectives. The Institute was a charitable trust administered by the relevant Minister. Any ambiguity in the fiscal provision had to be resolved contra fiscum. The appeal succeeded, the Commissioner’s decision was set aside, the taxable income was reduced by the donation amount, and the penalty was removed.
issues_for_determination: Whether the Institute was a charitable trust administered by the Minister responsible for Social Welfare within s 15(2)(r)(iii); whether registration as a PVO excluded treatment as a trust; whether the 60% penalty should be reduced or removed.
legal_principles: Fiscal provisions are interpreted strictly, and ambiguity is resolved in favour of the taxpayer under the contra fiscum rule. Substance may prevail over form in determining whether a trust relationship exists. A private voluntary organisation is not automatically excluded from being a charitable trust where its structure and objects satisfy the substantive characteristics of a trust.
held_decision_points: The Institute was capable of being, and was, a charitable trust administered by the Minister responsible for Social Welfare. The donation was deductible. The penalty had to be removed.
ratio_decidendi: The Act did not define “trust” restrictively or require a formal trust deed for all trusts. The Institute’s constitution separated control of funds from beneficial enjoyment and required funds to be applied for charitable community objectives. The Minister’s statutory powers under the PVO legislation constituted administration for purposes of the tax provision. Ambiguity favoured the taxpayer.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted that tax law must be applied to the reality of the taxpayer’s position and not merely the label attached by the parties or the Commissioner.
cases_referred_to: Endeavour Foundation and UDC Ltd v Commissioner of Taxes (1995) 57 SATC 297; Zambezi Gas Zimbabwe (Pvt) Ltd v NR Barber (Pvt) Ltd SC 3-20; ZIMRA v Murowa Diamonds (Pvt) Ltd 2009 (2) ZLR 213 (S); Badenhorst v CIR 1995 (2) SA 207 (N); AB CC v CSARS [2014] ZATC 4; other authorities cited in the full text.
legislation_considered: Income Tax Act [Chapter 23:06], especially s 15(2)(r)(iii); Private Voluntary Organizations Act [Chapter 17:05]; Welfare Organizations Act; Revenue Authority Act [Chapter 23:11].
reviewer_comments: 27. Approval status: Draft

document_name: Mabika v Danga N.O and 3 Others (819 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 819 (16 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Mabika v Danga N.O and Others
subject_area: Urgent chamber application; interim interdict; deceased estate; disputed ownership; transfer of immovable property.
catchwords_flynote: Interdict – deceased estate – sale of property by executor – applicant claiming beneficial ownership despite registration in deceased’s name – urgency – prima facie right – preservation pending action proceedings.
headnote: The applicant sought an urgent interim interdict stopping transfer of a property registered in the name of his late brother but alleged by the applicant to be his own. The first respondent, the deceased’s widow and executor, had sold the property to the second respondent and commenced transfer. The applicant alleged that he bought several properties and registered some in relatives’ names due to then-existing restrictions, retained the original title deeds, and had collected rentals. The respondents argued that the matter was not urgent and that the property formed part of the estate by virtue of registration. The court held that, given the imminent transfer and the applicant’s pending ownership action, real issues required preservation. A provisional order was granted.
issues_for_determination: Whether the matter was urgent; whether the applicant had established a prima facie right; whether an interim interdict should issue to preserve the property pending determination of the ownership action.
legal_principles: For an interim interdict, the applicant must establish a prima facie right, apprehension of irreparable harm, balance of convenience and absence of adequate alternative remedy. Urgency arises when the need to act cannot wait, particularly where transfer of immovable property is imminent.
held_decision_points: The court was satisfied that the applicant had made out a case for interim relief. A provisional order was granted pending the return date.
ratio_decidendi: The disputed property was about to be transferred to a third party, while the applicant had a plausible ownership claim supported by surrounding circumstances that required ventilation in pending action proceedings. Preservation was necessary to prevent irreversible prejudice.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court emphasized that the unusual facts of property registered in a deceased relative’s name but allegedly held for the applicant warranted interim preservation without finally resolving ownership.
cases_referred_to: Diamond Bird Service (Private) Limited and Trustees of Zimnat Value Preservation Trust Fund v Masbreed Investments (Private) Limited and City of Harare HH 413/21; Setlogelo v Setlogelo 1914 AD 221; Kuvarega v Registrar General and Another 1998 (1) ZLR 188 (H); other authorities cited in the full text.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021; laws governing deceased estates and registration/transfer of immovable property.
reviewer_comments: 27. Approval status: Draft
subject_area_terms: Urgent chamber application; interim interdict; deceased estate; disputed ownership; transfer of immovable property.

document_name: Machekera v Milmath Security (Private) Limited and 3 Others (824 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 824 (16 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Machekera v Milmath Security (Private) Limited and Others
subject_area: Mining law; declaratory order; mining claims; title and registration; competing certificates.
catchwords_flynote: Declaratur – mining rights – Radnor 58 converted into Radnor 58, 60 and 61 – competing Koo Doo 61 certificate – alleged forfeiture – material dispute of fact – rights confirmed by Ministry – certificate declared null and void.
headnote: The applicant sought a declaratory order that he was the sole and legitimate holder of rights in mining claims formerly constituting Radnor 58 and that the first respondent’s Koo Doo 61 certificate was null and void. The first respondent contended that Radnor 58 had been forfeited in 2013 and that it lawfully acquired its certificate in 2014. The second to fifth respondents did not oppose the application and explained that the original 25-hectare block had been converted into three current gold blocks: Radnor 58, Radnor 60 and Radnor 61. The court dismissed the first respondent’s preliminary objections and held that the applicant had established his mining rights. It declared the first respondent’s certificate null and restrained occupation and mining under it.
issues_for_determination: Whether there were material disputes of fact barring motion proceedings; whether the applicant’s rights remained extant; whether the first respondent’s Koo Doo 61 certificate was valid; whether declaratory relief should be granted.
legal_principles: A declaratur may be granted where the applicant establishes an existing, future or contingent right and the matter is proper for exercise of the court’s discretion. Mining rights depend on valid registration and the lawful status of certificates. Bare allegations of factual dispute do not defeat motion proceedings where the material facts are sufficiently ascertainable.
held_decision_points: The applicant established rights to Radnor 58 (10Ha), Radnor 60 (10Ha) and Radnor 61 (5Ha), formerly constituting Radnor 58. The first respondent’s Koo Doo 61 certificate was null and void and could not justify occupation or mining.
ratio_decidendi: The official position of the mining authorities confirmed that the converted Radnor blocks were current and had not been forfeited. The first respondent’s contrary claim rested on an untenable forfeiture argument and did not create a material dispute barring the relief.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court treated the matter as one capable of determination on the papers despite earlier comments in related proceedings about factual disputes.
cases_referred_to: HC 6647/17 referred to; other authorities as cited in the full text.
legislation_considered: Mines and Minerals Act [Chapter 21:05]; High Court Act [Chapter 7:06].
reviewer_comments: 27. Approval status: Draft
subject_area_terms: Mining law; declaratory order; mining claims; title and registration; competing certificates.
cases_referred_to_terms: HC 6647/17 referred to; other authorities as cited in the full text.

document_name: Machiwenyika v Chingwaru and 2 Others (818 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 818 (16 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Machiwenyika v Chingwaru and Others
subject_area: Civil procedure; condonation; suspension of sale in execution of dwelling; execution; judgment debt.
catchwords_flynote: Execution – sale of dwelling – condonation for late filing – r 71(14) – combined applications – half share in matrimonial/family home – alternative executable asset – bitumen emulsion manufacturing plant – real and substantial justice.
headnote: The applicant filed a combined application for condonation for late filing and suspension of sale in execution of his undivided half share in a dwelling. The debt arose from a deed of settlement after a failed immovable-property transaction. A prior order had made a bitumen emulsion manufacturing plant executable, but the respondents contended that the machine had been forfeited or was unavailable. The applicant disputed this and asserted that the machine still existed, had potential buyers and could satisfy the debt. The court granted condonation and suspended the sale of the dwelling on condition that the bitumen plant be sold in execution to extinguish the judgment debt.
issues_for_determination: Whether condonation for late filing should be granted; whether the combined procedure should be entertained; whether sale of the dwelling should be suspended; whether real and substantial justice required use of the bitumen plant before the dwelling.
legal_principles: Rules of court may be approached flexibly where justice and expedition require and no prejudice arises. Sale in execution of a dwelling may be suspended where real and substantial justice so requires, especially where another asset is available to satisfy the judgment debt.
held_decision_points: Condonation was granted. The sale in execution of the dwelling was suspended on condition that the bitumen emulsion manufacturing plant be sold in execution under the prior order to extinguish the debt.
ratio_decidendi: The respondents did not sufficiently disprove the applicant’s contention that the bitumen plant remained available and could satisfy the debt. Selling the dwelling for a debt that could be satisfied by the plant would be disproportionate and prejudicial, so real and substantial justice favoured conditional suspension.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted that the format of the combined application was unusual but not prohibited and could be entertained under the court’s discretion and r 58(13).
cases_referred_to: Astra Industries v Peter Chamburuka SC 21/2012; other authorities cited in the full text.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, including r 58(13) and r 71(14); rules on execution and suspension of sale of dwellings.
reviewer_comments: 27. Approval status: Draft
subject_area_terms: Civil procedure; condonation; suspension of sale in execution of dwelling; execution; judgment debt.

document_name: Madake v Makonza and Another (890 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 890 (21 December 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Madake v Makonza and Another
subject_area: Civil procedure; exception; defamation pleadings; vague and embarrassing pleadings; cause of action.
catchwords_flynote: Exception – defamation – Whatsapp messages and spoken words – pleadings must disclose actual words complained of – High Court Rules 2021 – Form 11 – time for exception – vague and embarrassing – amendment ordered.
headnote: The plaintiff sued for US$65,000 in defamation damages, alleging that the second defendant defamed her on a WhatsApp group and in the presence of church leaders. The defendants excepted, contending that the summons and declaration did not disclose a valid cause of action and were vague and embarrassing. The plaintiff raised several preliminary objections to the exception, including lateness, wrong form, failure to allow twelve days, incompetent prayer and attachments to the exception. The court dismissed those objections. On the merits, it held that the pleadings did not specify the actual words allegedly uttered or published, which was necessary especially where retraction of defamatory words was sought. The exception was upheld and the plaintiff was directed to amend her summons and declaration.
issues_for_determination: Whether the exception was filed in time and in proper form; whether non-compliance with form requirements was fatal; whether the summons and declaration disclosed a valid defamation cause of action; whether the actual defamatory words had to be pleaded.
legal_principles: Pleadings must disclose the material facts constituting the cause of action. In defamation, the actual words complained of should be pleaded with sufficient clarity to allow the defendant to answer and, where sought, to retract the words. Technical objections to form should not defeat substantial justice absent prejudice.
held_decision_points: All preliminary objections to the exception were dismissed. The exception was upheld because the summons and declaration failed to specify the words complained of and were insufficiently clear.
ratio_decidendi: The failure to state the actual defamatory words deprived the defendants of the ability to know what statements they had to answer or retract. That omission meant the pleadings lacked averments necessary to sustain the action and would prejudice or delay a fair trial.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted that punitive costs were not justified because the excipients could have requested further particulars and ordinary costs sufficed.
cases_referred_to: Telecel Zimbabwe (Pvt) Ltd v Postal and Telecommunications Regulatory Authority of Zimbabwe and Others HH 446/15; Abrahams & Sons v SA Railways and Harbours 1933 CPD 626; other authorities cited in the full text.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, including rr 12, 36, 37, 42 and 7; common-law defamation principles.
reviewer_comments: 27. Approval status: Draft
subject_area_terms: Civil procedure; exception; defamation pleadings; vague and embarrassing pleadings; cause of action.

document_name: Madamombe v Madamombe and 3 Others (742 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 742 (19 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Madamombe v Madamombe and Others
subject_area: Civil procedure; pre-trial conference; striking out defence; deceased estate; eviction to facilitate sale of estate property.
catchwords_flynote: Pre-trial conference – no triable issue – r 49(12) – executor dative – existing order authorising sale of estate property – beneficiaries resisting eviction – defence struck out – attorney-client costs.
headnote: The plaintiff, executor dative and beneficiary of an estate, sought eviction of the defendants, also beneficiaries, from Stand 9208 Paradise Park, Highfield, to enable sale and distribution of the proceeds in terms of an existing court order. At the pre-trial conference, the defendants asserted that they had offered to buy out the plaintiff’s one-fifth share. The court held that this did not raise a triable issue because the extant order required sale of the property to enable all beneficiaries to receive their shares. The defendants had barred evaluators and buyers and frustrated the executor’s implementation of the order. Their defence was struck out, eviction was granted and costs were awarded on an attorney-client scale.
issues_for_determination: Whether there were issues to refer to trial; whether an alleged offer to buy out the plaintiff’s share could defeat or delay the extant sale order; whether the court could enter judgment at pre-trial stage.
legal_principles: At a pre-trial conference, where no genuine triable issue exists and a defence is meritless, the court may strike out the defence and enter judgment. An extant court order must be complied with unless set aside.
held_decision_points: No triable issues existed. The defendants’ defence was struck out. Eviction was granted to facilitate the sale of the property.
ratio_decidendi: The prior order requiring sale of the estate property was clear and extant. The defendants’ desire to buy out the plaintiff was not part of that order and did not create a defence to eviction where their occupation was obstructing implementation.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court endorsed the principle that a pre-trial judge should not refer a matter to trial where there are no disputed issues for determination.
cases_referred_to: KM Insurance v Reuben Marumahoko HH 678/14; other authorities cited in the full text.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, including r 49(12); law relating to administration of deceased estates.
reviewer_comments: 27. Approval status: Draft

document_name: Madhlayo and Another v Megalink Investments (Pvt) Ltd (724 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 724 (19 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Madhlayo and Another v Megalink Investments (Pvt) Ltd
subject_area: Civil procedure; rescission of judgment; rule 29; default judgment; erroneous grant; prescription and illegality as defences.
catchwords_flynote: Rescission – High Court Rules 2021 r 29 – judgment allegedly erroneously granted – distinction from common-law rescission and r 27 rescission – default judgment on acknowledgement of debt – alleged non-service, fraud, illegality, prescription – no error shown.
headnote: The applicants sought rescission under r 29 of a default judgment granted against them in 2020 for US$170,168.75. The debt arose from an acknowledgement of indebtedness after a mandate to secure letters of credit allegedly failed. The applicants argued that the judgment had been erroneously granted because summons were not served, the underlying contract was tainted by fraud and illegality, the award was in United States dollars, the order against the first applicant was incompetent, and the claim had prescribed. The court held that the applicants had largely advanced a full defence rather than a specific error that existed at the time judgment was granted. Prescription could not be taken by the court mero motu, and alleged illegality was not a proper r 29 error on the facts pleaded. The application was dismissed.
issues_for_determination: Whether the default judgment was erroneously sought or granted within r 29; whether non-service, fraud/illegality, currency issues, incompetence against the first applicant or prescription constituted an error for r 29 purposes; whether the application was pleaded under the correct rescission regime.
legal_principles: Rule 29 rescission requires proof that judgment was erroneously sought or granted in the absence of an affected party. It is distinct from r 27 rescission, which requires absence of wilful default and a bona fide defence, and from common-law rescission. A defence not placed before the court does not automatically make a default judgment erroneous. Prescription must be pleaded and the court does not raise it mero motu.
held_decision_points: The applicants failed to satisfy r 29 requirements. Their grounds amounted largely to defences on the merits and not errors that precluded the grant of default judgment. The application was dismissed with ordinary costs.
ratio_decidendi: A judgment is not erroneous merely because the defendant later asserts defences that were not before the court. The alleged defects did not demonstrate that the court, at the time of granting default judgment, was unaware of a fact that would have precluded the order. The applicants also blurred the requirements of different rescission routes.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court warned against using r 29 to recast a defaulted defendant’s entire defence, and referred to the maxim that no one should benefit from their own wrongdoing.
cases_referred_to: Standard Chartered Bank Zimbabwe Limited v Matsika 1997 (2) ZLR 389; Grantully (Pvt) Ltd & Anor v UDC Ltd; Munyimi v Tauro SC 41-13; Jonas Mushosho v Lloyd Mudimu & Anor HH 443-13; Godknows Jonas v Rhona Shawlyn Mabwe HH 72-16; Chengeta N.O. & 3 Ors v Tabana HH 23-18; other authorities cited in the full text.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, r 29; Prescription Act [Chapter 8:11], ss 15(d) and 20; Exchange Control Regulations, 1982; Finance Act.
reviewer_comments: 27. Approval status: Draft

document_name: SANCTU~1.DOC
case_name: Sanctuary Insurance Company (Private) Limited v Micromart Zimbabwe (Private) Limited and 2 Others
subject_area: Civil procedure; condonation; review of taxation; bill of costs; currency conversion; SI 33 of 2019
catchwords_flynote: Condonation - late application for review of taxed bill - Order 38 r 314(1) of the 1971 High Court Rules - explanation for delay - prospects of success - taxed costs alleged to be wrongly denominated in United States dollars and on wrong scale - condonation granted.
headnote: The applicant sought condonation for failing to bring a review of a taxed bill within four weeks. The bill had been taxed in the applicant’s absence and execution had been attempted. The applicant argued that the costs were wrongly treated as hard-currency obligations and that the bill was taxed on the wrong scale. The court accepted that there was a reasonable explanation for the delay and that the applicant had an arguable case on review.
issues_for_determination: Whether the delay in filing a review of taxation should be condoned; whether the explanation for delay was reasonable; whether there were prospects of success; and what prejudice and interests of justice required.
legal_principles: In condonation applications the court considers length of delay, explanation, prospects of success, and prejudice. The same general principles apply to condonation for late review and late appeals. The factors are weighed together and are not individually determinative.
held_decision_points: Condonation was granted. The applicant was given leave to file an application for review of the taxing officer’s decision within seven days. Each party was ordered to bear its own costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that where a litigant provides a reasonable explanation for delay and raises an arguable issue on the taxation, especially involving currency and scale of costs, condonation may be granted in the interests of justice.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court criticised the applicant’s attempt to exclude proof of service after the court had asked for it, noting that this reflected desperation to exclude material showing the applicant was not candid.
cases_referred_to: Director of Civil Aviation v Hall 1990 (2) ZLR 354 (S); Kodzwa v Secretary for Health & Anor 1999 (1) ZLR 313 (S); Melane v Santam Insurance Co Ltd 1962 (4) SA 531 (A); South African Poultry Association and Others v Minister of Trade and Industry and Others 2018 (1) NR 1 (SC); Paul Gary Friendship v Cargo Carriers Limited & Anor SC 1/13; Zizhou v The Taxing Officer & Anor SC 7/20.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 1971, Order 38 r 314(1); Statutory Instrument 33 of 2019.

document_name: Zimunya and Another v Shumba and 2 Others (1 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 100 (4 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Zimunya and Another v Shumba and 2 Others
subject_area: Declaratur; specific performance; cancellation of agreements of sale; development levies; double sale; bona fide purchaser; special circumstances; immovable property rights.
catchwords_flynote: Sale of stands – purchasers seeking declaration that cancellation of agreements was void – default in development levies – property resold to third respondent – double-sale principles – second purchaser proving special circumstances – application dismissed.
headnote: The applicants bought two plots at Halfway Farm, Kadoma from the first respondent. The first respondent later cancelled the agreements, alleging breach through non-payment of development levies, and sold the property to the third respondent. The applicants sought declarations that the cancellations were void and that they remained lawful holders of rights in the plots. The court found that the applicants were in default on development levies and that the third respondent, as second purchaser, had established special circumstances including payment of a much higher price, possession and improvements. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether the applicants had paid development levies; whether the cancellation notices were valid; whether the applicants established a right or obligation supporting declaratory relief; whether the matter was governed by double-sale principles; whether the third respondent was a bona fide second purchaser; and whether special circumstances justified preferring the second purchaser.
legal_principles: An applicant for declaratory relief must show an interest and a right or obligation suitable for inquiry, and the court must be satisfied that the declaration is appropriate. A party seeking specific performance must show performance of its own obligations. In double-sale cases, the first purchaser ordinarily has the stronger right where transfer has not passed, unless the second purchaser proves special circumstances. Courts consider price paid, possession, improvements and knowledge of the prior sale.
held_decision_points: The court held that the applicants had not proved compliance with the development-levy obligations and that cancellation was therefore justified. The third respondent established special circumstances as a bona fide second purchaser who paid more, took possession and made improvements. The application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The applicants’ right depended on valid, subsisting agreements, but they failed to prove payment of development levies and were in breach. Even if double-sale principles were engaged, equity and special circumstances favoured the third respondent, whose rights could not be displaced on the record.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified.
cases_referred_to: MDC v President of Zimbabwe & Ors 2007 (1) ZLR 257 (H); Guga v Moyo 2000 (2) ZLR 458 (S); Charuma Blasting & Earthmoving Services (Pvt) Ltd v Njanji & Ors 2000 (1) ZLR 85 (S).
legislation_considered: High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], s 14 considered generally through declaratory relief principles.

document_name: CARRUT~1(1).DOC
case_name: MITZI CARRUTHERS, in her capacity as Executrix Dative and sole beneficiary of the Estate Late Martha Elizabeth Van Der Linde, versus THE COMBINED SERVICE ORGANISATIONS' TRUST and THE MASTER OF THE HIGH COURT N.O.
subject_area: Declaratory relief; contract; deceased estate; retirement-home occupancy agreement; currency conversion; SI 33 of 2019; executrix dative.
catchwords_flynote: Declaratur – executrix and beneficiary seeking declaration of residual value payable under life-right or occupancy agreement – whether refund payable in United States dollars or Zimbabwe dollars equivalent – interpretation of contract and SI 33 of 2019 – respondent obliged to preserve and market cottage – application granted in amended terms.
headnote: The applicant, as executrix dative and sole beneficiary, sought a declaration of the residual value payable under an agreement by which the deceased occupants had advanced US$75 000 for occupation of cottage 28 at Athol Evans Complex. The Court held that the applicant had the requisite interest for a declaratur. It found that no direct United States dollar payment was due, but that the respondent would be obliged to pay the equivalent in Zimbabwe dollars of US$48 750 at the interbank rate when payment became due. The Court also accepted the applicant's complaint that the respondent had not made meaningful efforts to secure a new occupant and had used the cottage as a store room. The application was granted as prayed in the amended draft order.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant had standing and a direct and substantial interest to seek declaratory relief.
    Whether the residual value was payable in United States dollars or in Zimbabwe dollars equivalent.
    Whether the respondent was obliged to keep the cottage in good repair and avoid using it pending occupation by a new tenant.
    Whether the Court should grant declaratory relief in the amended terms.
legal_principles: Section 14 of the High Court Act permits the Court to determine existing, future or contingent rights or obligations at the instance of an interested person. A declaratur requires an interested person, a right or obligation to be determined, a non-academic controversy, interested parties bound by the declaration and public-policy considerations favouring relief. Currency obligations affected by SI 33 of 2019 may require payment in Zimbabwe dollars at the relevant interbank rate rather than direct payment in United States dollars.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant satisfied the requirements for declaratory relief.
    Held, that the residual value due under the contract was US$48 750 as a reference value, payable in ZWL/RTGS at the applicable Reserve Bank/interbank rate on the date of payment.
    Held, that the respondent had not made meaningful efforts to find a new tenant and could not prejudice the applicant by using the cottage as a store room.
    Held, that the application was granted as prayed in the amended draft order.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that an executrix and beneficiary has a sufficient direct interest to seek a declaratur on the estate's contractual entitlement, and that the residual contractual value must be declared in a manner consistent with SI 33 of 2019 and the respondent's contractual obligation to preserve and re-let the cottage.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that the respondent could not plausibly claim that it failed to secure a new occupant for almost three consecutive years after the deceased vacated the cottage.
cases_referred_to:
Munn Publishing (Pvt) Ltd v ZBC 1994 (1) ZLR 337
    RK Footwear Manufacturing (Pvt) Ltd v Boka Book Sales (Pvt) Ltd 1986 (2) ZLR 209
    Benefit Friendly Society v Commissioner for Inland Revenue and Another 1955 (4) SA 120 (T)
    Okeke v Duro & Co (Pvt) Ltd

document_name: Fernoughty v Chataika and 2 Others (4 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 4 (12 January 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: DONALD CHARLES FERNOUGHTY versus NATHAN CHATAIKA and 2 OTHERS
citation: Fernoughty v Chataika and 2 Others (4 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 4 (12 January 2022)
subject_area: Civil procedure; special plea; prescription; eviction; immovable property; cause of action; factual dispute.
catchwords_flynote: Special plea of prescription – eviction claim and damages – defendant relying on occupation since 2010 – plaintiff alleging fresh occupation/construction in January 2021 and interruption by earlier proceedings – factual dispute as to cause of action and occupation – prescription plea not decidable without evidence – special plea dismissed.
headnote: The plaintiff sued for eviction and damages concerning Stand 905 Forbes Road, Waterfalls. The first defendant raised a special plea that the claim had prescribed because the plaintiff allegedly knew of his occupation in 2010. The plaintiff responded that the cause arose in January 2021 when the defendant again occupied and built structures, and also relied on earlier litigation. The Court held that a special plea of prescription required taking the plaintiff’s declaration as pleaded; the facts on when the cause of action arose were disputed and could not be resolved without evidence. The special plea was dismissed.
issues_for_determination:
When the cause of action arose.
    Whether the special plea of prescription could be resolved on the pleadings.
    Whether previous proceedings interrupted prescription or constituted a 30-year judgment basis.
    Whether viva voce evidence was required.
legal_principles: In a special plea, the defendant must take the plaintiff’s declaration as pleaded. A plea of prescription often raises a factual question as to when the cause of action arose and when the creditor knew or ought to have known the facts necessary to sue. Where these matters are genuinely disputed, the issue should not be decided without evidence.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the plaintiff’s declaration pleaded a fresh cause of action based on 2021 occupation and construction.
    Held, that the defendant’s version that occupation persisted from 2010 created a factual dispute.
    Held, that it would be inappropriate to determine prescription without viva voce evidence.
    Held, that the special plea was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that prescription could not be determined on special plea where the pleadings disclosed a contested factual issue on when the cause of action arose and whether there was fresh interference in 2021.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to:
Sabina Altaf Ahmed v Joina Development Company (Private) Limited HH 242/20
    Brooker v Mudhanda & Another SC 5/18
legislation_considered: Prescription Act [Chapter 8:11], section 15; rules and principles governing special pleas.

document_name: Sakutombo v Master of the High Court and 3 Others (5 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 5 (12 January 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: Tracy Sakutombo v Master of the High Court and 3 Others
subject_area: Administration of estates; customary law marriage; surviving spouse; review; Master of the High Court
catchwords_flynote: Deceased estate - disputed surviving spouses - Master’s determination that applicant and third respondent were not surviving spouses - magistrate’s confirmation a nullity - evidence of customary unions - tsvakirai kuno and mbonano - separation not dissolution - determination set aside.
headnote: The deceased died intestate. At the edict meeting, relatives disputed whether the applicant and third respondent were surviving spouses. A Chinhoyi magistrate purported to confirm the customary marriages, but the Master later determined that there was no surviving spouse. The applicant sought review. The court held that the magistrate lacked jurisdiction in the circumstances and that the confirmation was a nullity. However, on the record, evidence including customary payments and family acknowledgements established that the applicant and third respondent were surviving spouses.
issues_for_determination: Whether the Chinhoyi magistrate validly determined marital status; whether the Master’s determination could stand; and whether the applicant and third respondent were surviving spouses under customary law.
legal_principles: A customary-law union is not dissolved by mere separation; dissolution requires appropriate customary-law steps such as gupuro or proceedings before a customary-law court. Payment of tsvakirai kuno can evidence a customary union. Informal retractions cannot lightly override affidavit evidence.
held_decision_points: The review succeeded. The Master’s determination was set aside and the applicant and third respondent were declared surviving spouses. Each party was to bear own costs.
ratio_decidendi: The court could, despite the invalid magistrate confirmation, determine surviving-spouse status on the evidence and set aside a Master’s contrary determination where it was unsupported by the record.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court viewed some relatives’ later retractions with suspicion and noted that separation is not divorce in customary-law unions.
cases_referred_to: Folly Cornishe (Pvt) Ltd & Anor v Shingirayi Tapomwa N.O. & Others SC 26/14; Attorney-General v Mudisi & Others SC 48/15; Nyandoro v Mukowamombe & Others HH 209/10; In re Estate Late Amos John Chirunda HH 119/2006; Antonio v Antonio 1991 (2) ZLR 42 (SC); Pasipanodya v Muchoriwa 1997 (2) ZLR 182 (S); Machafa v Mukumirwa 2001 (2) ZLR 540 (H).
legislation_considered: Administration of Estates Act [Chapter 6:01], ss 68(1), 68G and 68I; Administration of Estates (Designated Magistrates) Notice, 1997, SI 224 of 1997.
reviewer_comments: Full text extracted from the mounted DOCX. This is a duplicate upload of a judgment processed in an earlier batch, but it has been preserved as part of the current supplied batch.

document_name: Bennet v Bennet and 4 Others (6 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 6 (12 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: DEBORAH SUSAN BENNETT versus RODNEY ALLEN BENNETT and 4 OTHERS
citation: Bennet v Bennet and 4 Others (6 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 6 (12 January 2022)
subject_area: Civil procedure; mandament van spolie; urgent application; matrimonial home; ex parte magistrates' order; appeal and suspension of execution.
catchwords_flynote: Spoliation – matrimonial home – applicant appealing ex parte magistrates' spoliation order – appeal suspending execution absent leave to execute – first respondent entering occupation after appeal had been served – requirements for mandament van spolie satisfied – spoliation order granted – request for urgent set down of appeal refused – ordinary costs.
headnote: The applicant urgently sought restoration of exclusive occupation of 12 Beach Road, Borrowdale, after the first respondent took occupation pursuant to an ex parte magistrates' court spoliation order. The applicant had already noted and served an appeal against that order. The Court held that the appeal suspended execution and the first respondent should have sought leave to execute pending appeal. The applicant was in occupation when the first respondent entered. The requirements for spoliation were satisfied and a spoliation order was granted. The request for urgent set-down of the appeal was refused and costs were awarded on an ordinary scale.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the matter was urgent.
    Whether the applicant had been despoiled.
    Whether the magistrates' ex parte order could be executed after an appeal had been noted.
    Whether the applicant satisfied the requirements for mandament van spolie.
    Whether the High Court should order urgent set-down of the magistrates' court appeal.
legal_principles: Mandament van spolie protects peaceful and undisturbed possession against unlawful dispossession, irrespective of the merits of the parties' underlying rights. An appeal ordinarily suspends execution of the order appealed against unless leave to execute pending appeal is obtained. A party who acts under a suspended order risks unlawful dispossession.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant was in occupation of the premises when the first respondent entered.
    Held, that the first respondent's occupation occurred after service of the notice of appeal and without leave to execute pending appeal.
    Held, that the applicant satisfied the requirements for spoliation.
    Held, that the request for urgent set down of the appeal was refused because adequate administrative procedures existed.
    Held, that costs were awarded on an ordinary scale.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that where a party takes occupation under an order after an appeal against that order has been noted and served, without leave to execute pending appeal, the occupation is unlawful and spoliatory if it disturbs the prior possessor's peaceful possession.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court distinguished urgent electoral litigation on set-down from the present case, holding that there was no impending event that would leave the applicant without remedy.
cases_referred_to:
Chidawu & Ors v Shah & Ors SC 12/13
    Zimbabwe Development Party v Minister of Justice & Others
    Mawarire v Mugabe N.O. and Others CCZ 1/13
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, rule 95(20); common-law mandament van spolie principles.
constitutional_provisions_considered: Constitution of Zimbabwe Amendment No. 20, 2013, election-related authorities considered by distinction.
