document_name: Kawanzaruwa v Esquire Matova N.O and Another (7 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 7 (12 January 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: MICHAEL KAWANZARUWA versus ESQUIRE MATOVA N.O. AND ANOTHER
subject_area: Criminal procedure; review of unterminated proceedings; discharge at close of State case; fraud; prima facie case; appeal versus review.
catchwords_flynote: Review – magistrate refused discharge at close of State case – fraud allegations – prima facie case found – High Court slow to intervene in unterminated criminal proceedings – challenge went to correctness not procedural validity – review dismissed and trial remitted for defence case.
headnote: The applicant sought review of a magistrate’s refusal to discharge him at the close of the State case in fraud proceedings. The High Court held that there was evidence linking the applicant to the alleged fraudulent transaction and that the trial court’s conclusion that a prima facie case existed could not be faulted. The complaint was essentially an appeal on facts and law, not a reviewable procedural irregularity. The application was dismissed and the matter remitted for continuation before the same magistrate.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the evidence at close of State case established a prima facie case.
    Whether the refusal to discharge the applicant was reviewable before the criminal trial concluded.
    Whether the applicant’s challenge went to procedural irregularity or substantive correctness.
    Whether exceptional circumstances justified interference in unterminated proceedings.
legal_principles: A discharge at the close of the State case is appropriate where there is no evidence on an essential element, no evidence upon which a reasonable court might convict, or evidence is so unreliable that no reasonable court could act on it. Superior courts interfere in unterminated criminal proceedings only in exceptional cases to prevent grave injustice. Review addresses procedural validity, not mere correctness of a decision on facts or law.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the trial court’s finding of a prima facie case could not be faulted.
    Held, that no exceptional circumstances justified interference in unterminated proceedings.
    Held, that the challenge was not properly one for review.
    Held, that the application for review was dismissed and the matter remitted to the Magistrates Court for continuation before the same magistrate.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the magistrate’s ruling was based on an assessment of evidence and disclosed no gross procedural irregularity; the applicant’s disagreement with the finding of a prima facie case was not a ground for review.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that only the applicant and other participants could explain the fate of the money and why he dealt with complainants under a different name.
cases_referred_to: S v Tsvangirai and Others 2003 (1) ZLR 88; S v Petronella Nyarugwe HH 42/16; Ndlovu v Regional Magistrate, Eastern Division and Another 1989 (1) ZLR 264 (H); S v Sibanda 1994 (2) ZLR 19; Rose v S HH 71-12; Masedza and Others v Magistrate Rusape and Another 1998 (1) ZLR 36; Archinulo v Moyo and Another 2016 (2) ZLR 417.
legislation_considered: Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], section 198(3); Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], section 136; High Court review jurisdiction.
subject_area_terms: Criminal procedure; review of unterminated proceedings; discharge at close of State case; fraud; prima facie case; appeal versus review.

document_name: Ruzvidzo v Mandizvidza and 7 Others (8 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 8 (6 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Ruzvidzo v Mandizvidza and 7 Others
subject_area: Succession; declaratur; beneficiary status; DNA testing; intestate estate
catchwords_flynote: Deceased estate — legitimacy/beneficiary status — per stirpes claim through predeceased father — declaratur or DNA testing — proof of family relationship.
headnote: The applicant sought a declaration that he was a legitimate beneficiary in his grandfather’s estate through his late father, alternatively an order compelling relatives to submit to DNA testing. The dispute concerned whether he should inherit per stirpes despite bearing his mother’s surname on his birth certificate.
issues_for_determination: Whether the applicant was entitled to be declared a beneficiary per stirpes; whether DNA testing should be ordered; whether the facts established his relationship to the deceased family line.
legal_principles: A declaratur may be granted where an interested person establishes a live right or obligation requiring determination; disputed paternity/beneficiary claims may require evidentiary proof including DNA testing where appropriate.
held_decision_points: The court considered evidence of recognition, family conduct and whether a declaratur or DNA order was appropriate.
ratio_decidendi: Beneficiary status in an estate may be proved by direct and circumstantial evidence of family recognition; DNA testing is an available but discretionary tool where biological relationship is materially disputed.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified
cases_referred_to: See Kapfudza v Mushiri HH

document_name: E (Private) Limited v ZIMRA (10 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 10 (6 January 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: E (PRIVATE) LIMITED versus ZIMBABWE REVENUE AUTHORITY
court: SPECIAL COURT FOR INCOME TAX APPEALS, HARARE
citation: E (Private) Limited v ZIMRA (10 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 10 (6 January 2022)
subject_area: Tax law; income tax appeal; non-residents tax on fees; telecommunications roaming and interconnection fees; double taxation agreement; penalties.
catchwords_flynote: Non-residents tax on fees – roaming and interconnection fees paid to foreign telecommunications companies – technical, administrative or managerial services – source within Zimbabwe – double taxation agreement with Mauritius – business profits/taxation classification – penalty – appeal dismissed.
headnote: The appellant, a licensed telecommunications operator, appealed against ZIMRA assessments for non-residents tax on fees arising from payments to foreign operators for interconnection and roaming services. The Special Court held that the services were technical in nature within the Seventeenth Schedule to the Income Tax Act. It also held that payments to the Mauritian entity were taxable in Zimbabwe under the applicable double taxation agreement and that the reduced 5% penalty had a statutory basis. The appeal was dismissed.
issues_for_determination:
Whether roaming and interconnection fees were subject to non-residents tax on fees.
    Whether payments to non-resident companies in several jurisdictions were taxable in Zimbabwe.
    Whether the Mauritius double taxation agreement precluded Zimbabwe taxation.
    Whether there was a legal basis for the 5% penalty.
legal_principles: Under section 30 of the Income Tax Act read with the Seventeenth Schedule, non-residents tax on fees applies to fees from a Zimbabwean source payable for technical, managerial, administrative or consultative services. In tax appeals, section 63 places the burden on the taxpayer to show that the Commissioner’s decision is wrong. Penal provisions in the Seventeenth Schedule allow penalties for failure to withhold or remit tax.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the telecommunications interconnection and roaming services involved technical services.
    Held, that the appellant failed to discharge the burden of proving ZIMRA wrong.
    Held, that the Mauritius double taxation agreement did not preclude the assessment in the circumstances.
    Held, that the 5% penalty had a statutory basis.
    Held, that the appeal was dismissed in its entirety.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that roaming and interconnection arrangements requiring telecommunications infrastructure, software, systems, signalling, engineering and maintenance fall within the statutory concept of technical services, and payments for them from a Zimbabwean source are liable to non-residents tax on fees unless the taxpayer proves a valid exemption.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted that the appellant did not produce all invoices or call data records, weakening its attempt to challenge the assessments.

document_name: G (Private) Limited v ZIMRA (11 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 11 (6 January 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: G (PRIVATE) LIMITED versus ZIMBABWE REVENUE AUTHORITY
court: SPECIAL COURT FOR INCOME TAX APPEALS, HARARE
subject_area: Value Added Tax; zero-rating; services to foreign donor organisations; resident/non-resident benefit; VAT penalties.
catchwords_flynote: VAT appeal – monitoring and project services supplied to donor organisations – whether zero-rated under section 10(2)(l) of VAT Act – donor entities deemed resident to extent of activity and permanent place in Zimbabwe – standard-rate VAT applicable – penalty set aside due bona fide reliance on ZIMRA advice.
headnote: The appellant appealed VAT assessments for services rendered to foreign donor organisations. It argued the services were supplied contractually to and for the benefit of non-residents, and thus zero-rated. The Court considered the contracts and the statutory definition of resident. It dismissed the substantive appeal, holding that the services did not qualify for zero-rating as claimed. However, because the appellant had earlier approached ZIMRA and was told it was not liable to VAT, the Court found no intention to evade tax and set aside the penalty.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the services were supplied for the benefit of and contractually to non-residents outside Zimbabwe.
    Whether the foreign donor organisations were deemed residents of Zimbabwe for VAT purposes.
    Whether the 100% penalty was justifiable in the circumstances.
legal_principles: Section 10(2)(l) of the VAT Act zero-rates services supplied contractually to and for the benefit of a non-resident outside Zimbabwe, subject to statutory conditions. A person or company may be deemed resident to the extent it carries on trade or activity in Zimbabwe and has a fixed or permanent place in Zimbabwe relating to that activity. Penalties may be remitted where the taxpayer acted bona fide and without intent to evade tax.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the appellant’s services did not qualify for zero-rating.
    Held, that the substantive VAT appeal was dismissed.
    Held, that the appellant acted bona fide on previous ZIMRA advice and had no intention to evade tax.
    Held, that the 100% penalty was set aside.
    Held, that there would be no order as to costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that donor-related services performed through Zimbabwe-based offices and for projects implemented in Zimbabwe did not meet the zero-rating requirements merely because the contracting entities were foreign; however, penalty liability required consideration of the taxpayer’s bona fides and reliance on official advice.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court accepted that the appellant had approached ZIMRA years earlier and was advised that it was not liable to register for VAT, which was material to the penalty issue.
cases_referred_to: None identified
legislation_considered: Value Added Tax Act [Chapter 23:12], sections 10(2)(l), 23(1) and 33; Revenue Authority Act [Chapter 23:11].
subject_area_terms: Value Added Tax; zero-rating; services to foreign donor organisations; resident/non-resident benefit
cases_referred_to_terms: None identified

document_name: S v Kambudzi (111 of 2023) [2022] ZWHHC 111 (25 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Kambudzi v The State
subject_area: Criminal law; criminal appeal; robbery; identification evidence; recent possession; sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal appeal – three counts of robbery – recent possession of stolen property – identification evidence – rejection of innocent-purchaser defence – aggravated robbery – long custodial sentence – appeal dismissed.
headnote: The appellant appealed convictions and sentences for three robberies. The High Court held that his defence that he innocently bought stolen goods was vague and false, that recent possession and identification evidence linked him to the robberies, and that the effective 27-year sentence was not shocking because the robberies were violent, repeated, and committed in aggravating circumstances.
issues_for_determination: Whether the convictions were unreasonable; whether the trial court properly accepted identification evidence; whether the individual or cumulative sentences were excessive.
legal_principles: Recent possession of stolen property can support an inference of participation in robbery where the explanation is false. Identification evidence must be assessed in context, including opportunity to observe. Robbery in aggravating circumstances attracts serious custodial punishment.
held_decision_points: Appeal against convictions and sentences dismissed.
ratio_decidendi: The appellant’s possession of goods stolen in three separate robberies, together with identification evidence and false explanations, proved guilt beyond reasonable doubt; the sentence reflected the seriousness and repeated nature of the offences.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The judgment emphasised the constitutional rights violated by violent robberies, including rights to life, liberty, dignity, security, privacy and property.
cases_referred_to: S v Mutters and Anor S 66/89; S v Makoni & Ors S 67/89; S v Madondo 1989 (1) ZLR 300 (H).
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 126.
reviewer_comments: The filename citation is [2022] ZWHHC 111, while the judgment heading gives HH 111-23; both have been preserved.

document_name: Discovery Foods (Pvt) Limited and Another v Makamure and Another (114 of 2023) [2022] ZWHHC 2023 (15 August 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: DISCOVERY FOODS (PVT) LIMITED and STANLEY FORWARD MADLAZI versus HELLEN MAKAMURE and SHERIFF HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
citation: Discovery Foods (Pvt) Limited and Another v Makamure and Another (114 of 2023) [2022] ZWHHC 2023 (15 August 2022)
subject_area: Urgent chamber application; stay of execution; urgency; rescission of default judgment; acknowledgment of debt; defective founding affidavit; foreign affidavit before notary public.
catchwords_flynote: Stay of execution – default judgment and sale in execution – execution commenced months earlier – applicants delayed until later sale – urgency self-created/waiting for day of reckoning – matter removed from urgent roll.
headnote: The applicants sought urgent stay of execution after default judgment had been granted against them for US$197 000. Execution had commenced in April 2022, payments had been made, and a later sale in execution followed in August. The Court rejected the challenge to the respondent’s notarised affidavit and held that the matter was not urgent. The second sale was a continuation of an existing execution process, not a fresh event creating urgency. The founding affidavit did not explain the earlier inaction. The matter was removed from the urgent roll and costs awarded against the applicants.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the notice of opposition was defective because the opposing affidavit was notarised abroad.
    Whether the matter was urgent.
    Whether the second execution sale created a new basis for urgency.
    Whether the applicants had explained their delay.
    Whether the court should consider further preliminary points or remove the matter from the urgent roll.
legal_principles: A sworn statement commissioned by a notary public abroad may constitute an affidavit if it bears the essential characteristics of an affidavit. A matter is urgent if, when the need to act arises, it cannot wait; urgency created by deliberate or careless abstention until the day of reckoning does not qualify. The founding affidavit and certificate must explain any delay.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the first respondent’s notarised sworn statement had the essential features of an affidavit.
    Held, that execution had commenced in April 2022 and the later August execution was a continuation of that process.
    Held, that the applicants did not explain their failure to act earlier.
    Held, that the founding affidavit was terse and insufficient on urgency.
    Held, that the matter was not urgent and should be removed from the roll of urgent matters.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that where execution has been underway for months and the applicant fails to explain why no urgent relief was sought earlier, a later continuation of execution does not create urgency; the proper course is removal from the urgent roll.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court criticised the founding affidavit as terse, cryptic and difficult to follow, observing that a case must be clear from the founding papers without needing the opposing papers to understand it.
cases_referred_to:
Kuvarega v Registrar-General & Anor 1998 (1) ZLR 188 (H)
    Document Support Centre Ltd v Mapuvire 2006 (2) ZLR 240 (H)
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, rules concerning urgent applications and affidavits; law on stay of execution and rescission.
subject_area_terms: Urgent chamber application; stay of execution; urgency; rescission of default judgment; acknowledgment of debt; defective founding affidavit; foreign affidavit before notary public.
cases_referred_to_terms: Kuvarega v Registrar-General & Anor 1998 (1) ZLR 188 (H)
Document Support Centre Ltd v Mapuvire 2006 (2) ZLR 240 (H)
legislation_considered_terms: High Court Rules, 2021, rules concerning urgent applications and affidavits; law on stay of execution and rescission.

document_name: ZIED69~1.DOC
case_name: Zimbabwe Rural District Councils Workers’ Union v Nyanga Rural District Council and Another; and Eleven Other Cases
subject_area: Labour law; designated agents; employment councils; registration of awards; inherent jurisdiction; inherent power of High Court; statutory lacuna; enforcement of awards.
catchwords_flynote: Designated agents – employment council awards – whether High Court may register determinations for enforcement where Labour Act contains no express registration mechanism – scope of inherent jurisdiction and inherent power – applications dismissed.
headnote: The applicant filed a series of chamber applications seeking registration, for enforcement, of determinations issued by designated agents in rural district council labour disputes. There was no express statutory provision permitting such registration. The applicant argued that the High Court should exercise inherent jurisdiction to register the awards, relying on the finality of designated agents’ decisions after Isoquant/ZIMOCO. The court held that inherent jurisdiction is not unlimited and does not permit the court to invent a statutory registration mechanism. The applications were dismissed, with the court noting that Parliament should address the anomaly.
issues_for_determination: Whether the High Court could register designated agents’ determinations for enforcement purposes absent express statutory authority; whether inherent jurisdiction or inherent power could fill the legislative gap; whether s 14 of the High Court Act and s 63(3a) of the Labour Act supported registration; and whether the awards could instead found other proceedings.
legal_principles: Inherent jurisdiction is a residual source of power used exceptionally to protect due process, prevent oppression, secure fair trial and regulate procedure. It is not unlimited and does not allow courts to legislate or create substantive law. Where the legislature intended registration of awards from tribunals, it has expressly provided for it. Designated agents may redress disputes, and their determinations are final, but finality does not itself create an enforcement-registration route.
held_decision_points: The applications were dismissed. The court held that it was inappropriate to extend inherent jurisdiction to register designated agents’ awards absent legislative authority. Such awards may form the basis of proceedings for judgment, similar to liquid documents, but cannot simply be registered as High Court orders by inherent power.
ratio_decidendi: Because registration of external awards is a statutory procedure requiring defined safeguards and criteria, the High Court could not create such a mechanism for designated agents. The omission in the Labour Act had to be addressed by Parliament, not by the court through inherent jurisdiction.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted that there is an apparent legislative anomaly requiring Parliament to provide for enforcement of designated agents’ awards.
cases_referred_to: Isoquant Investments (Pvt) Ltd t/a ZIMOCO v Memory Darikwa CCZ 6/20; Anthony Piller KG v Manufacturing Process Ltd [1976] Ch 55 (CA); Al Rawi v The Security Service [2011] UKSC 34; Derdale Investments (Pvt) Ltd v Econet Wireless (Pvt) Ltd & Ors 2014 (2) ZLR 662 (H); Seaford Court Estates Ltd v Asher [1949] 2 All ER 155 (CA); Chingombe & Another v City of Harare & Ors SC 177/20; Management Corporation Strata Title Plan No 301 v Lee Tat Development Pte Ltd [2010] SGCA 39; Machote v Zimbabwe Manpower Development Fund HH 813-15; Mubaiwa v Chiwenga SC 86/20; Martin Sibanda and Anor v Benson Chinemhute and Anor HH 131/04.
legislation_considered: High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], s 14; Labour Act [Chapter 28:01], ss 2A, 3, 63(3a), 92B, 93(5a), 98; Arbitration Act [Chapter 7:15], Article 35; Maintenance Act [Chapter 5:09], ss 18 and 20; Mines and Minerals Act [Chapter 21:05], s 345; Consumer Protection Act [Chapter 14:14], Part V; Defence Act [Chapter 11:02]; Civil Matters Mutual Assistance Act [Chapter 8:02]; High Court Rules, 2021, r 77.

document_name: AMALEM~1(1).DOC
case_name: AMALEM INVESTMENTS (PVT) LTD T/A FULL TASK PROJECT MANAGERS versus TAPIWA MABHIZA and LOADSHED CHIDA and CHARLES KANDIRA and JUSTICE MUREHWA and OBERT MUREHWA and PATRICIA MADAVANA and ZIMBABWE REPUBLIC POLICE
subject_area: Civil procedure; urgent chamber application; interim interdict; land occupation; land development; locus standi; material disputes of fact.
catchwords_flynote: Urgent chamber application – land developer with Ministry offer letter and approved plans – respondents occupying, pegging and trenching residential stands – urgency established by renewed unlawful pegging and trenching – locus standi established by real and substantial interest – bald fraud allegations insufficient – material disputes of fact not established – interim interdict granted but final relief such as eviction and police dispersal refused at interim stage.
headnote: The applicant sought urgent relief against respondents occupying and demarcating a 14-hectare Saturday Retreat property in Waterfalls, Harare. The respondents claimed to have been in occupation since 2020 and raised urgency, locus standi, invalid offer letter and disputes of fact as points in limine. The Court held that urgency arose from the renewed pegging and trenching in June 2022, which threatened to disrupt the applicant's approved land development and surveying process. The applicant established at least a prima facie right through the Ministry offer letter and related documents. The Court rejected the preliminary points and granted interim prohibitory relief against disruption, harassment and interference, but refused to include eviction and police dispersal in interim relief because those were final in effect.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the matter was urgent.
    Whether the applicant had locus standi.
    Whether the offer letter should be treated as invalid at the interim stage.
    Whether material disputes of fact precluded determination on affidavit.
    Whether the requirements for an interim interdict had been met.
    Whether eviction and police dispersal could be granted as interim relief.
legal_principles: Urgency exists where the need to act arises and the matter cannot wait. The timing of the need to act, the absence of alternative remedy, prompt action and the interim nature of relief are relevant. A party has locus standi where it has a real and substantial interest in the subject matter. Interim interdict requirements include a prima facie right, apprehension of irreparable harm, absence of another satisfactory remedy and balance of convenience. Final relief should not be granted under the guise of interim relief on a provisional order.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the matter was urgent because the renewed pegging and trenching threatened the applicant's approved land-development plans.
    Held, that the applicant had locus standi by virtue of its offer letter and approved development documents.
    Held, that the respondents' fraud allegations were bald and unsupported.
    Held, that no material dispute of fact prevented interim relief.
    Held, that the applicant had established a prima facie right, apprehension of irreparable harm, no alternative remedy and balance of convenience.
    Held, that interim eviction and police dispersal relief were final in effect and could not be granted at the interim stage.
    Held, that interim interdictory relief should be granted in part.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that a developer with official allocation and approved plans has a prima facie right to urgent interim protection against unauthorised trenching and pegging that threatens to frustrate the development before substantive rights are finally determined.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court cautioned that eviction and police dispersal relief sought as interim relief was final in effect and could not be incorporated into the provisional order.

Kuvarega v Registrar-General & Anor 1998 (1) ZLR 188 (H)
    Document Support Centre Ltd v Mapuvire 2006 (2) ZLR 240 (H)

    SA Optometrie Association v Frames Distributors 1985 (3) SA 100

document_name: Marisa and 4 Others Marisa and 4 Others (121 of 2023) [2022] ZWHHC 121 (12 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Marisa and 4 Others v Marisa and 4 Others
subject_area: Civil procedure; rescission of consent judgment; property/ejectment from farm; succession
catchwords_flynote: Rescission of judgment — consent order — legal practitioners’ authority to consent — delay of 14 years — good and sufficient cause — farm ejectment.
headnote: The applicants sought rescission of a 2007 consent order ejecting them from Farm No. 48 Zinyaningwe, Mashava. They alleged that the legal practitioners who appeared for them had no instructions to consent and that they personally never signed the order. The court held that the consent order had been entered after hearing counsel of record and that bare allegations of lack of instructions, raised 14 years later, did not satisfy the requirement of good and sufficient cause.
issues_for_determination: Whether a consent judgment entered through legal practitioners of record could be rescinded on the basis of alleged lack of instructions; whether the applicants had shown good and sufficient cause for rescission after a 14-year delay.
legal_principles: A consent judgment may be set aside only on good and sufficient cause and on terms the court considers just. A legal practitioner who has entered appearance may sign or consent to judgment on behalf of a party. Bald allegations of fraud or lack of authority are insufficient where the court record shows counsel appeared and participated.
held_decision_points: The applicants failed to establish good and sufficient cause for rescission. The application for rescission of the consent judgment was dismissed, with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The court treated the record of the consent order and appearances by counsel as decisive unless cogent evidence displaced it. The applicants’ delayed, unsupported allegations could not invalidate a consent order entered by legal practitioners of record.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court observed that the applicants’ allegations effectively accused senior legal practitioners and officers of the court of fraud, a serious allegation which required more than bald assertion.
cases_referred_to: Not specifically identified in the extracted text.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, r 21; former High Court Rules, 1971, Order 8 rr 54–55.

document_name: Sivan v Dera N.O and 3 Others (13 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 13 (12 January 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: Sivan v Dera N.O. and 3 Others
subject_area: Civil procedure; execution pending appeal; corporate rescue; company law; directors’ circular resolution; interim interdict
catchwords_flynote: Execution pending appeal — provisional order suspending corporate rescue resolution — prospects of appeal — Companies and Other Business Entities Act s 196 — circular resolution without all directors’ written consent — corporate rescue founded on allegedly defective resolution.
headnote: The applicant sought leave to execute a provisional order granted in HH 668/21 pending appeals to the Supreme Court. The underlying order suspended a circular resolution placing Adlecraft Investments (Pvt) Ltd under voluntary corporate rescue. The court considered the prospects of the appeals and the balance of convenience. It held that the appeals had no prospects of success because the impugned circular resolution was prima facie non-compliant with s 196 of the Companies and Other Business Entities Act, since all directors entitled to vote had not signed or consented. Leave to execute pending appeal was granted.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the appeals had prospects of success.
    Whether the applicant had locus standi as a director to challenge the circular resolution.
    Whether s 196(1) of the Companies and Other Business Entities Act had been complied with.
    Whether balance of convenience favoured execution pending appeal.
    Whether the corporate rescue appointment could stand if the founding resolution was suspended.
legal_principles:
Noting an appeal generally suspends the operation of the judgment appealed against, but the court may grant leave to execute where real and substantial justice requires it.
    Relevant factors include prospects of success, irreparable harm, prejudice, and balance of convenience.
    A circular resolution under s 196(1) requires written consent signed by all directors entitled to vote.
    Where the foundation of a corporate rescue process is prima facie invalid, steps built on it are also vulnerable.
    A prima facie illegality should not be allowed to continue merely because an appeal has been noted.
held_decision_points:
The applicant had locus standi as a director to challenge the director’s resolution.
    The appeals by the respondents had no prospects of success.
    The circular resolution was prima facie invalid because it lacked the written consent of all directors entitled to vote.
    The corporate rescue process was founded on the impugned resolution and should not proceed pending determination.
    Leave to execute HH 668/21 pending appeal was granted.
ratio_decidendi: The applicants for appeal could not show prospects of overturning the finding that the circular resolution was prima facie non-compliant with s 196. Allowing the corporate rescue process to continue would perpetuate an apparent statutory violation, while suspending it preserved the status quo pending the return date and appeal process.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court restated that an act done contrary to a direct statutory prohibition is void and of no effect.
cases_referred_to:
Dabengwa & Anor v Minister of Home Affairs 1982 (1) ZLR 223
    Jeremy Prince (Pvt) Ltd v Owen & Anor HH 14/86
    Van T. Hoff v Van T. Hoff & Ors 1988 (1) ZLR 335 (H)
    Net One Cellular (Pvt) Ltd v Net One Employees & Anor 2005 (1) ZLR 275 (S)

document_name: S v Chimwalila (138 of 2024) [2022] ZWHHC 138 (26 September 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Chimwalila
subject_area: Criminal procedure – bail pending trial – murder – circumstantial evidence – public safety
catchwords_flynote: Bail pending trial; murder during robbery; circumstantial evidence; strength of State case; risk to public safety; abscondment.
headnote: The accused faced a charge of murder allegedly committed during a robbery at Seke Flyover. The State’s investigation traced the deceased’s cellphone through several persons to the accused, who allegedly gave it to another person at about the time the deceased’s body was discovered.
issues_for_determination: Whether the strength of the circumstantial evidence, seriousness of the charge, risk to public safety and risk of abscondment justified refusal of bail.
legal_principles: In bail pending trial, the court considers the interests of justice, including the strength of the State case, gravity of the offence, risk to public safety and likelihood of abscondment.
held_decision_points: The court found overwhelming circumstantial evidence and held that the seriousness of murder committed during robbery, coupled with public safety considerations, justified refusal of bail.
ratio_decidendi: Where the evidence strongly links an accused to recently stolen property from a murder scene and the explanation is unsupported, the interests of justice may require denial of bail.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court emphasised the gravity of murder committed during a robbery and the public safety concerns arising from such allegations.
cases_referred_to: None identified in the extracted text.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 47; Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], including s 117.

document_name: SD Mining Syndicate v Nyevera and 3 Others (16 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 16 (12 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: SD Mining Syndicate v Nyevera and 3 Others
subject_area: Urgent chamber application; execution pending appeal; mining rights; prospecting documentation; abuse of process
catchwords_flynote: Leave to execute pending appeal - mining interdict - appeal noted - applicant seeking order operational pending appeal - urgency - prospects of appeal - irregular prospecting documents - balance of convenience - leave granted.
headnote: The applicant held mining rights under a tribute agreement over Trafalgar Mine and had obtained an interdict against the first respondent. The first respondent appealed, suspending the order. The applicant sought leave to execute pending appeal, alleging continuing interference with its mining operations. The court dismissed urgency objections, found the first respondent’s prospecting documents suspicious and unsupported, held that the appeal lacked prospects and was intended to delay or harass, and granted leave to execute pending appeal.
issues_for_determination: Whether the application was urgent; whether the applicant should be allowed to execute the earlier order pending appeal; whether the first respondent’s appeal was bona fide and had prospects; and where the balance of hardship lay.
legal_principles: An appeal usually suspends execution, but the court has a discretion to grant leave to execute pending appeal where the appeal is not bona fide, has poor prospects, or is intended to buy time or harass. Relevant factors include irreparable harm, prospects of success, and balance of convenience. Urgency is assessed by whether the matter could wait when the need to act arose.
held_decision_points: The application was granted. The order under HC 6560/21 was made operational pending determination of the appeal in SC 460/21. The first respondent was ordered to bear costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that execution pending appeal may be permitted where the appellant’s prospects are poor and refusal would irreparably prejudice the successful party’s mining operations, especially where the appellant has not produced valid competing mining documentation.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court stated that applications for leave to execute pending appeal do not require a return day because the appeal itself functions as the practical sunset event.
cases_referred_to: Nzara v Tsanyau and Others 2014 (1) ZLR 674 (H); Kuvarega v Registrar General and Another 1998 (1) ZLR 188 (H); Old Mutual Life Assurance Company (Pvt) Ltd v Makgatho HH 39-07; South Cape Corporation (Pty) Ltd v Engineering Management Services (Pty) Ltd 1977 (3) SA 534 (A); Jonga v Chabata HH 276-17.
legislation_considered: Mines and Minerals Act [Chapter 21:05], s 345(1).

document_name: Marimo v Mtombeni N.O and Another (162 of 2023) [2022] ZWHHC 2033 (11 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Marimo v Mtombeni N.O and Another
subject_area: Criminal procedure; review of unterminated proceedings; attempted rape and rape charges; magistrates’ court procedure
catchwords_flynote: Review — unterminated criminal proceedings — magistrate stopping attempted rape trial and directing rape charge — s 54 Magistrates Court Act — prosecutorial discretion — gross irregularity — proceedings quashed.
headnote: The applicant was on trial for attempted rape when the trial magistrate, after hearing evidence from the complainant, stopped the trial and directed that the applicant be charged with rape. The applicant sought review. The court held that the magistrate failed to follow the proper statutory procedure, usurped the Prosecutor-General’s function, and created a gross irregularity by exposing the accused to multiple charges on the same facts.
issues_for_determination: Whether the magistrate acted lawfully in abandoning the attempted rape trial and directing the State to prefer a rape charge; whether the proceedings should be reviewed despite being unterminated; whether bias was established.
legal_principles: Superior courts are slow to interfere in unterminated criminal proceedings but will do so in exceptional cases to prevent grave injustice. A magistrate must follow s 54 of the Magistrates Court Act where a matter appears more appropriate for a court of greater jurisdiction. The decision to charge lies with the prosecuting authority.
held_decision_points: The review succeeded. The rape proceedings were quashed. The magistrate was directed to continue with the attempted rape trial and follow s 54(1) if circumstances require. No order as to costs.
ratio_decidendi: The magistrate’s direction to change the charge and commencement of rape proceedings were illegal because they bypassed the statutory route and usurped prosecutorial discretion, resulting in a gross irregularity and nullity.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court cautioned judicial officers against treating litigants’ procedural applications with disdain and against responding to reviews in a manner suggesting personal interest.
cases_referred_to: S & Anor v Sparkles Services (Pvt) Ltd & Anor HH 135/17; other authorities on review of unterminated proceedings considered in the full judgment.
legislation_considered: Magistrates Court Act [Chapter 7:10], s 54(1); Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], including s 225; Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], ss 65 and 189; High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], s 27; High Court Rules, 2021, r 62.
constitutional_provisions_considered: None identified as a decided constitutional issue.

document_name: APOSTO~3(1).DOC
case_name: APOSTOLIC FAITH MISSION IN ZIMBABWE versus APOSTOLIC FAITH MISSION OF ZIMBABWE and SOLOMON TOGARA and PAUL HWERU and FARAI MANYANYE
subject_area: Civil procedure; final interdict; church property; lease rights; spoliation order; peace order; voluntary association/universitas; religious property disputes.
catchwords_flynote: Interdict – religious organisation leasing church property – breakaway faction conducting parallel church activities – respondents relying on prior spoliation and peace orders – lease agreement confers superior right to possess/use premises – spoliation order protects immediate possession only and does not extinguish lease rights – requirements for final interdict established – respondents interdicted from entering, occupying or using property except with applicant's consent – ordinary costs awarded.
headnote: The applicant sought an interdict against respondents entering, occupying or using Stand 19812 Lezard Road, Milton Park, Harare. The applicant relied on a lease with the Municipality of Harare and argued that respondents were members of a breakaway faction conducting parallel church activities. Respondents relied on a Magistrates' Court spoliation order and a peace order. The Court held that the applicant's lease agreement established a clear right superior to the respondents' possessory right under the spoliation order. The spoliation and peace orders did not prevent future proceedings based on superior rights. The respondents' conduct disturbed the applicant's church activities. A final interdict was granted, with the qualification that access or use by the second to fourth respondents required applicant's consent. Costs were awarded on the ordinary scale.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant was entitled to a final interdict.
    Whether the lease agreement established a clear right.
    Whether prior spoliation and peace orders gave respondents rights superior to the applicant's lease.
    Whether the respondents' parallel activities caused irreparable harm.
    Whether there was another adequate remedy.
legal_principles: A final interdict requires a clear right, irreparable injury actually committed or reasonably apprehended, and absence of a similar adequate remedy. A spoliation order finally determines immediate possession but does not resolve ultimate rights and does not preclude later proceedings based on superior rights. A member who breaks away from a voluntary association or universitas does not take the association's property to the new body.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant established a clear right through its lease agreement.
    Held, that the spoliation and peace orders did not extinguish or override the applicant's lease rights.
    Held, that the second to fourth respondents had effectively separated themselves from the applicant and were conducting parallel activities.
    Held, that their occupation and use of the church building caused injury to the applicant.
    Held, that the applicant had no adequate alternative remedy.
    Held, that the application for interdict should be granted subject to amendments concerning consent for future access and ordinary costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that a prior spoliation order does not defeat a later interdict founded on superior lease rights, and breakaway congregants cannot continue using a voluntary association's leased property for parallel activities without the association's consent.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that the order should allow the applicant, as an ecumenical body, discretion to permit access if respondents reform or rejoin.

    Apostolic Faith Mission in Zimbabwe v Apostolic Faith Mission of Zimbabwe and Others HH 269/22
    Mankowitz v Lownthal

document_name: Bowes (nee Scionning) v Bowes (17 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 19 (19 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: WENDY KATHRYN BOWES (nee SCHIONNING) versus CLYDE MATTHEW BOWES
citation: Bowes (nee Scionning) v Bowes (17 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 19 (19 January 2022)
subject_area: Family law; minor children; parental rights; schooling; boarding school; best interests of the child; access arrangements; urgent chamber application.
catchwords_flynote: Family law – parents separated pending divorce – dispute over children's schooling – mother seeking enrolment at Springvale Junior School with bursary – father preferring home schooling or Chisipite Junior School – best interests of minor children – court hearing viva voce evidence in urgent application – children ordered to attend Springvale Junior School – access arrangements granted – each party to bear own costs.
headnote: The parties were separated pending divorce and disagreed over schooling for their two minor children. The applicant wanted the children to attend Springvale Junior School as weekly boarders on bursary, while the respondent preferred home schooling or Chisipite Junior School. After hearing evidence, the Court held that the respondent had not shown that enrolment at Springvale was irrational or contrary to the children's best interests. The Court ordered that the children be educated at Springvale, authorised the applicant to sign necessary documents, and granted the respondent structured access.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the children should be home schooled, attend Springvale Junior School, or attend Chisipite Junior School.
    Whether the applicant's decision to enrol the children at Springvale was in their best interests.
    Whether respondent had shown that boarding school would prejudice the children.
    What access arrangements should apply.
legal_principles: In matters concerning children, the best interests of the child are paramount. The court may hear viva voce evidence in urgent proceedings where necessary. A parent challenging the other parent's schooling decision must show that the decision is irrational or contrary to the child's best interests. Parental means, access, continuity, education quality and emotional welfare are relevant.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the respondent did not show that enrolment at Springvale was irrational or contrary to the children's best interests.
    Held, that the children's interests would be served by formal schooling at Springvale.
    Held, that the applicant was authorised to sign documents necessary for schooling.
    Held, that respondent was granted alternate weekend, birthday, public holiday and holiday access.
    Held, that each party should bear own costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that where the proposed schooling arrangement is shown to be beneficial and the objecting parent fails to prove that it is irrational or contrary to the children's best interests, the court may authorise enrolment and regulate access accordingly.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court recognised that the children's own view favoured Springvale and that weekly boarding would still allow regular weekend contact with both parents.
cases_referred_to:
Leonard Sibanda HH 207/18
    Ralph v Vuuren HH 5/2009
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, rule 60(8); family-law principles on custody/access.
constitutional_provisions_considered: Constitution of Zimbabwe Amendment No. 20, 2013, sections 60(3) and 81 considered in relation to parental rights and children's interests.

document_name: Maswela N.O v The Master of the High Court and 2 Others (171 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 171 (3 March 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Maswela N.O. v The Master of the High Court and Others
subject_area: Company liquidation; Master’s decision; liquidation and distribution account; accounting for foreign currency sale proceeds.
catchwords_flynote: Liquidation – objection to liquidation and distribution account – Master dismissing objection – US$70,000 received in cash by liquidator – whether properly accounted for – Companies Act – Insolvency Act.
headnote: The applicant challenged the Master’s dismissal of an objection to the second interim liquidation and distribution account in the liquidation of Kunganda Farm (Private) Limited. The dispute concerned US$70,000 received in cash by the liquidator as part of the purchase price of the company’s farm. The applicant contended that the money was irregularly dealt with and should have been preserved in a Nostro account. The court held that the Master had not sufficiently investigated how the funds had been dealt with. It set aside the Master’s decision and the account, and directed the Master to investigate and issue appropriate directions.
issues_for_determination: Whether the company should have been joined; whether leave was required; whether the application was frivolous; whether the Master properly dismissed the objection; how the US$70,000 should be investigated and accounted for.
legal_principles: Interested parties in a winding up may object to a liquidation account. A person aggrieved by the Master’s refusal to sustain an objection may approach the court. The Master must properly investigate material objections to a liquidation account.
held_decision_points: The Master’s decision and the second interim account were set aside. The Master was ordered to investigate how the US$70,000 was dealt with and direct amendment or other appropriate steps.
ratio_decidendi: The Master treated the objection too narrowly and did not adequately investigate the movement and treatment of the cash proceeds. Proper administration of the liquidation required investigation before confirmation of the account.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court rejected preliminary objections that did not go to the real liquidation-account issue.
cases_referred_to: Authorities cited in the full text.
legislation_considered: Companies Act [Chapter 24:03], s 282; Insolvency Act [Chapter 6:04], s 127; SI 142 of 2019.
reviewer_comments: Draft. Preserved heading judgment number HH 171-20 while filename citation is [2022] ZWHHC 171.

document_name: Faith Ministries Church v Nhau and 2 Others (178 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 178 (18 March 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: FAITH MINISTRIES CHURCH versus MR. F NHAU and 2 OTHERS
citation: Faith Ministries Church v Nhau and 2 Others (178 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 178 (18 March 2022)
subject_area: Property law; eviction; locus standi of lessee; material disputes of fact; church stand; self-help.
catchwords_flynote: Eviction – applicant holding lease over church stand – respondents occupying through housing cooperative without allocation or right of occupation – preliminary points dismissed – leaseholder had substantial interest and locus standi – occupation unlawful – eviction ordered.
headnote: The applicant sought eviction of the first and second respondents from Stand 16549 Hatcliffe, Harare, which it held under a lease from the Ministry. The respondents claimed occupation through a housing cooperative but produced only correspondence showing an application or regularisation process, not an allocation or right of occupation. The Court dismissed preliminary points based on lis pendens, disputes of fact and locus standi, held that the respondents had no demonstrated right, and granted eviction with ordinary costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether another pending application barred the present proceedings.
    Whether there were material disputes of fact requiring action proceedings.
    Whether a lessee had locus standi to seek eviction.
    Whether the respondents established any right of occupation.
    Whether eviction and demolition should be ordered.
legal_principles: A party with a direct and substantial interest, including a lessee, may institute proceedings to protect possession and occupation. A material dispute of fact exists only where factual disputes cannot be resolved on the papers; bare allegations do not suffice. A mere application for allocation of land does not confer occupation rights. Self-help is not tolerated.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the earlier matter had been withdrawn and did not bar the application.
    Held, that there were no genuine material disputes of fact.
    Held, that the applicant had locus standi as leaseholder.
    Held, that the respondents failed to prove any right of occupation.
    Held, that the respondents’ conduct amounted to self-help.
    Held, that eviction and demolition relief was granted.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that a leaseholder with a demonstrated right to occupy state land may evict persons who have no allocation or legal right of occupation and who interfere through self-help.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court stated that courts are courts of record and not forums for mere bold allegations unsupported by documentary proof.
cases_referred_to:
Zimbabwe Teachers Association and Others v Minister of Education and Culture 1990 (2) ZLR 48
    Supa Plant Investment (Pvt) Ltd v Edgar Chidavaenzi HH 92-09
    Room Hire Co v Jeppe Street Mansions 1949 (3) SA
legislation_considered: Principles of property law and eviction; High Court procedure on disputes of fact.

document_name: Gombe Holdings (Private) Limited and Another v Chitombo (18 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 18 (12 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: GOMBE HOLDINGS (PRIVATE) LIMITED and BITCOIN t/a KUCHI CONSTRUCTION (PRIVATE) LIMITED versus JOSEPHY CHITOMBO
subject_area: Property law; rei vindicatio; company motor vehicle; employment dispute; locus standi; recovery order.
catchwords_flynote: Rei vindicatio – employer-related company vehicle – respondent retained vehicle after termination – locus standi objection raised late and dismissed – ownership by second applicant established – labour dispute no defence to vindication – return and disclosure orders granted.
headnote: The applicants sought return of a Toyota Avensis motor vehicle used by the respondent while employed by the first applicant. The respondent raised a late point that the first applicant lacked locus standi and argued that he could retain the vehicle pending a labour challenge. The Court dismissed the locus standi objection and held that the second applicant, as owner, had satisfied the rei vindicatio requirements. The respondent was ordered to deliver the vehicle, failing which the Sheriff could recover it and the respondent could be compelled to disclose its location.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the first applicant had locus standi.
    Whether the second applicant established ownership of the motor vehicle.
    Whether the respondent’s employment dispute gave him a right to retain the vehicle.
    Whether return, Sheriff recovery and committal/disclosure relief were appropriate.
legal_principles: The actio rei vindicatio protects ownership and allows an owner to recover property wherever found by proving ownership, possession by the defendant, and existence/identity of the property. A labour dispute or asserted employment benefit does not defeat vindication where the property was not contractually granted as an employment benefit. Points of law may be raised late only with proper notice and fairness.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the late locus standi point was improperly raised and lacked merit.
    Held, that the applicants made out a case for rei vindicatio.
    Held, that the respondent had no right to retain the motor vehicle.
    Held, that the respondent must deliver the Toyota Avensis registration AFF 7889 to the second applicant within 24 hours of service.
    Held, that the Sheriff could recover the vehicle and police assistance could be enlisted.
    Held, that the respondent must disclose the vehicle’s location if he failed to deliver it.
    Held, that ordinary costs were awarded.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that ownership of the vehicle, possession by the respondent and continued existence of the vehicle were sufficient for vindication; the respondent’s employment challenge did not constitute a lawful basis for withholding property owned by the second applicant.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted that it was inconsistent for the respondent to base his defence on his employment relationship with the first applicant while simultaneously denying the first applicant’s interest in the proceedings.
cases_referred_to:
Allied Bank Limited v Caleb Dengu & Another SC 52/16
    Ndlovu v Marufu HH 480-15
    Stevenson v Minister of Local Government & National Housing SC 38/02
    Trustees, Leonard Cheshire Homes Zimbabwe Central Trust v Chite & Others 2010 (1) ZLR 631 (H)
    Alspite Investments (Private) Limited v Westerhoff & Others 2009 (2) ZLR 226
legislation_considered: Common-law actio rei vindicatio; employment contract principles.

document_name: MARSHA~1.DOC
case_name: Marshall and Safetop (Proprietary) Limited v Atupele Investments (Proprietary) Limited t/a Workboss Hardware
subject_area: Intellectual property; trade marks; industrial designs; passing off; account of profits.
catchwords_flynote: Trade marks – infringement – industrial design – passing off – goodwill and reputation – final interdict – account of profits – Safe Top roof seals and nails.
headnote: The applicants brought proceedings for infringement of an industrial design, trade mark infringement and passing off in relation to Safe Top roof-seal products. The respondent conceded infringement of the industrial design and consented to interdicts restraining further use of the infringing products and marks. The remaining issues included past trade mark infringement, passing off and accounting for profits. The court held that trade mark infringement had been established, but passing off failed because goodwill and reputation were not proved by adequate evidence. The respondent was directed to render accounts of income, profits and purchases relating to infringing products and to pay costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether the respondent had infringed registered trade marks; whether the applicants established passing off through goodwill, misrepresentation and damage; whether an account of profits should be ordered.
legal_principles: Trade mark infringement is determined by comparing the marks and assessing likelihood of deception or confusion. Passing off requires proof of goodwill, misrepresentation and damage. A registered proprietor may seek statutory accounting for profits where trade mark infringement is proved.
held_decision_points: Trade mark infringement was established. Passing off was not proved because the applicants failed to establish goodwill or reputation on the evidence. The respondent was ordered to render accounts and pay costs.
ratio_decidendi: The respondent’s products and marks were sufficiently similar to the registered marks to constitute infringement, and the concession to remove infringing marks reinforced that finding. Passing off required separate proof of goodwill, which was absent.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court emphasized that goodwill is a factual matter and ordinarily requires evidence such as sales figures, advertising, market reach or surveys.
cases_referred_to: Setlogelo v Setlogelo 1914 AD 221; Flamelily Investments Company (Pvt) Ltd v Zimbabwe Salvage (Pvt) Ltd 1980 ZLR 378; Smith and Nephew Ltd v Mediplast Pharmaceutical Sales CC 1999 (4) SA 646 (D); Bon Marche (Pvt) Ltd v Brazier & Anor 1984 (2) ZLR 50 (SC); Adidas AG & Anor v Pepkor Retail 2013 BIP 203 (SCA); Montres Rolex SA v Kleynhans 1985 (1) SA 55 (C).
legislation_considered: Industrial Designs Act [Chapter 26:02]; Trade Marks Act [Chapter 26:04], including s 8 and s 9A(2).

document_name: AFM v Chiangwa (21 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 21 (13 January 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: APOSTOLIC FAITH MISSION IN ZIMBABWE versus COSSAM CHIANGWA
case_number:
HC 6465/21
    Ref Case No. SC 433/21
    HC 5549/21
citation: AFM v Chiangwa (21 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 21 (13 January 2022)
subject_area: Civil procedure; execution pending appeal; constitutional referral; spoliation; church leadership dispute; abuse of appeal process.
catchwords_flynote: Execution pending appeal – respondent sought referral to Constitutional Court arguing that High Court common law power to order execution pending appeal was unconstitutional – referral refused as frivolous and lacking merit in context – appeal appeared designed to buy time after spoliation order – High Court may grant execution pending appeal where justice and balance of hardship require it – leave to execute spoliation judgment granted pending Supreme Court appeal.
headnote: The applicant had obtained a spoliation order restoring church premises under HC 5549/21. The respondent appealed to the Supreme Court, and the applicant sought leave to execute pending appeal. The respondent sought referral to the Constitutional Court, contending that a lower court determining prospects of appeal pending before the Supreme Court offended the hierarchy of courts, impartiality, fair hearing and access to courts. The Court rejected the referral, holding that there is nothing inherently unconstitutional in ordering execution pending appeal where an appeal is used to buy time or harass the successful party. On the merits, the balance of hardship and the weak prospects of appeal favoured execution. The applicant was granted leave to execute HH 626-2021 notwithstanding the appeal.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the respondent's constitutional referral request should be granted.
    Whether the High Court's common-law jurisdiction to order execution pending appeal is inconsistent with court hierarchy, judicial impartiality, fair hearing or access to courts.
    Whether the applicant established grounds for execution pending appeal.
    Whether the appeal had bona fide prospects or was lodged to buy time and harass the applicant.
legal_principles: An appeal ordinarily suspends execution, but the court that gave judgment may permit execution pending appeal where factual circumstances make suspension impractical or undesirable. The court must consider potential prejudice to both sides, prospects of success on appeal, whether the appeal is frivolous or vexatious or brought for an indirect purpose, and the balance of hardship. A constitutional referral is not warranted where the issue is frivolous or vexatious in the factual context.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the proposed constitutional questions were generalised, non-contextual and lacked merit.
    Held, that allowing the High Court to determine execution pending appeal does not undermine the Supreme Court's power to determine the appeal.
    Held, that the respondent had poor prospects of success on appeal and the appeal appeared to be intended to buy time.
    Held, that the applicant would suffer irreparable prejudice if execution was refused because it could not conduct services, while respondent could use alternative premises.
    Held, that the application for leave to execute pending appeal was granted.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the High Court's power to grant execution pending appeal remains constitutionally permissible and may be exercised where the appeal appears to be an abuse of process and the balance of hardship favours enforcing the judgment pending appeal.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that a single High Court judge's assessment of prospects for purposes of execution pending appeal does not bind or influence the Supreme Court, which remains free to determine the appeal on its merits.
cases_referred_to:
Old Mutual Life Assurance Company (Private) Limited v L. Makgatho HH 39-07
    NetOne Cellular (Pvt) Ltd v NetOne Employees & Anor 2005 (1) ZLR 275 (SC)
legislation_considered: Common law principles on execution pending appeal
constitutional_provisions_considered: Constitution of Zimbabwe Amendment No. 20, 2013, sections 3(1)(b), 56, 69(2), 69(3), 162, 164, 168, 169, 175(4), 176

document_name: Chiduku and 2 Others v Chiduku and 4 Others (214 of 2023) [2022] ZWHHC 2031 (22 December 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: ISAAC CHIDUKU and 2 OTHERS versus AMOS CHIDUKU and 4 OTHERS
citation: Chiduku and 2 Others v Chiduku and 4 Others (214 of 2023) [2022] ZWHHC 2031 (22 December 2022)
subject_area: Urgent chamber application; interim interdict; family property dispute; arbitration clause; preservation of res litigiosa; misjoinder; disputed shares in estate property.
catchwords_flynote: Urgent application – siblings' agreement to share family farm – first respondent resiling from memorandum of agreement and asserting 50% undivided share – arbitration clause – interim preservation pending arbitration – prima facie right – misjoinder of local authority – provisional order granted, costs reserved.
headnote: The applicants sought interim relief restraining the first respondent from dealing with Swallowfield Farm as a 50% owner pending arbitration. The parties were siblings who had entered into a memorandum of agreement recognising 20% interests each in the farm left by their late father, although title was registered in the names of the first applicant and first respondent. The Court held that the matter was urgent because the first respondent had recently resiled from the agreement. It rejected the jurisdictional objection because the relief was interim and preservatory pending arbitration, upheld Norton Town Council's misjoinder objection, and granted the provisional order to preserve the asset pending arbitration with costs reserved.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the matter was urgent.
    Whether the arbitration clause deprived the High Court of jurisdiction to grant interim relief.
    Whether the interim relief impermissibly duplicated the final relief.
    Whether there were material disputes of fact.
    Whether Norton Town Council was misjoined.
    Whether the applicants established the requirements for an interim interdict pending arbitration.
legal_principles: The existence of an arbitration clause does not prevent the High Court from granting interim relief to preserve property pending arbitration. A provisional order may issue where the applicant establishes a prima facie right, apprehension of irreparable harm, balance of convenience and absence of an adequate alternative remedy. A party with no direct and substantial interest may be expunged for misjoinder. Property that is the subject of a dispute should be preserved as res litigiosa pending final determination.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the matter was urgent.
    Held, that the Court retained jurisdiction to grant interim preservatory relief despite the arbitration clause.
    Held, that there were no material disputes of fact preventing determination of the urgent application.
    Held, that Norton Town Council was misjoined and its name was expunged from the record with costs.
    Held, that the applicants established a prima facie right and risk of irreparable harm.
    Held, that a provisional order was granted and costs reserved.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that where parties to a family property agreement have referred the dispute to arbitration, the High Court may grant interim interdictory relief to preserve the disputed asset and prevent one party from unilaterally defeating the claimed shares before arbitration is finalised.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that it would be harsh and oppressive to expose parties who prima facie claimed 80% of the property to potential loss at the instance of one party asserting a disputed 50% share.
cases_referred_to: None identified in the extracted text.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021; principles of arbitration and interim interdicts; memorandum of agreement between the parties.

document_name: ZIMBAB~3.DOC
case_name: Zimbabwe Nickel Exploration Company (Private) Limited v Raiz-El Nickel Processing Company (Private) Limited and 2 Others
subject_area: Mining law; urgent chamber application; interim interdict; tribute agreement; sale of slag and slag dump; breach of contract; prima facie right; balance of convenience.
catchwords_flynote: Urgent interdict – mining operations and slag removal at Welcomeback 18 Mine – applicant alleging breach of sale and tribute agreements – first respondent raising counter-allegations and disputing breach – whether prima facie case established – interim relief refused.
headnote: The applicant sought an urgent provisional order stopping the first respondent from mining or removing slag from Welcomeback 18 Mine pending finalisation of HC 7367/21. The applicant alleged that the first respondent had breached an agreement of sale and tribute arrangements by failing to pay the balance of the purchase price, rent and royalties. The first respondent disputed breach and raised counter-allegations, including that the applicant had failed to ratify or regularise the tribute in time and had allowed other illegal miners on site. The court found that the disputes were too serious to sustain a prima facie case for interim relief. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether the matter was urgent; whether the applicant had established a prima facie case, though open to doubt; whether irreparable harm was reasonably apprehended; whether there was no adequate alternative remedy; whether the balance of convenience favoured the applicant; and whether the court could determine breach of contract at the interim stage.
legal_principles: For an interim interdict, an applicant must establish a prima facie case, irreparable harm actually committed or reasonably apprehended, absence of another adequate remedy, and that the balance of convenience favours the grant of relief. Where the alleged breach of contract is seriously disputed and forms the subject of pending proceedings, the court may decline urgent interim relief if the applicant cannot show a sufficient prima facie right.
held_decision_points: The urgency objection was dismissed. On the merits, the applicant failed to establish a prima facie case because serious disputes remained on whether the first respondent had breached the sale or tribute agreements. The application was dismissed with costs on the ordinary scale.
ratio_decidendi: The applicant’s entitlement to interdict depended on proving breach or cancellation of the agreement, but those issues were seriously disputed and already pending in HC 7367/21. Without a sufficiently clear prima facie right, the balance of convenience and irreparable harm requirements could not assist the applicant.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified.
cases_referred_to: Kuvarega v Registrar General & Anor 1998 (1) ZLR 188 (H); Nzara v Tsanyau and Others 2014 (1) ZLR 674 (H); Setlogelo v Setlogelo 1914 AD 221; Flame Lily Investments Company (Pvt) Ltd v Zimbabwe Salvage (Pvt) Ltd & Anor 1980 ZLR 378; Crief Investments (Pvt) Ltd & Anor v Grand Home Centre (Pvt) Ltd & Ors HH 12/18.
legislation_considered: Mines and Minerals Act [Chapter 21:05] considered generally in relation to mining rights and approved tribute arrangements.

document_name: S v Chitsungo (228 of 2023) [2022] ZWHHC 2030 (30 May 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Chitsungo
judges: MUREMBA J; assessors Mr Barwa and Mr Chimonyo
subject_area: Criminal law – murder – competent verdict – culpable homicide – defence of another – excessive force – sentence
catchwords_flynote: Murder; brick assault; defence of another; contradictory State evidence; culpable homicide; sentence.
headnote: The accused was charged with murder after striking the deceased on the head with a brick following a confrontation over allegedly stolen sneakers. The deceased died from brain injury, depressed skull fracture and subdural haematoma caused by blunt trauma.
issues_for_determination: Whether murder was proved; whether the accused acted in defence of another; whether excessive force reduced liability to culpable homicide; appropriate sentence.
legal_principles: Intention to kill must be proved beyond reasonable doubt. Where defensive force is excessive but death results from negligent conduct, culpable homicide may be the competent verdict.
held_decision_points: The accused was acquitted of murder and convicted of culpable homicide. He was sentenced to 10 years’ imprisonment, with 3 years suspended for 5 years on condition of good behaviour, leaving an effective 7 years.
ratio_decidendi: The accused did not have the intention required for murder, but his use of a heavy brick to the head was an unreasonable and negligent overreaction causing death.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court observed that life was unnecessarily lost over stolen sneakers and that violence is not a lawful method of resolving disputes.
cases_referred_to: State v Mugwanda; State v Sigwahla; State v Rowayi Chimene HH; State v Mpofu; State v Kizito Mutsure HH.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], ss 47, 49; Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], s 314.

document_name: Shava v President, Republic of Zimbabwe (23 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 23 (12 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Obey Shava v President, Republic of Zimbabwe
subject_area: Constitutional law; locus standi; public interest litigation; Statutory Instrument 127 of 2021; legality
catchwords_flynote: Constitutional application - applicant challenging constitutionality of SI 127 of 2021 under public interest standing - locus standi - affected classes were bankers, foreign exchange dealers and traders - applicant not acting for marginalised persons and no direct/substantial interest - application dismissed.
headnote: The applicant, a legal practitioner, sought to declare Statutory Instrument 127 of 2021 unconstitutional. The Minister of Finance opposed, arguing that the applicant lacked standing because the instrument affected defined classes such as bankers, foreign exchange dealers and traders. The court held that the applicant had not shown a direct and substantial interest or a proper basis for public-interest standing, especially where the directly affected classes were capable of suing for themselves. The application was dismissed.
issues_for_determination: Whether the applicant had locus standi under s 85(1)(d) of the Constitution to challenge SI 127 of 2021 in the public interest; whether he had a direct and substantial interest; and whether the alleged affected persons were unable to act for themselves.
legal_principles: Public-interest standing under s 85 is broad but must still show a constitutional issue and a genuine public-interest basis. Where a litigant asserts the rights of identifiable, capable and economically empowered classes, he cannot merely stand in their shoes without explaining why they cannot vindicate their own rights. Locus is shown on the papers, not by argument.
held_decision_points: The application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that public-interest standing cannot be used by an applicant with no direct and substantial interest to litigate on behalf of classes capable of litigating for themselves.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court described the applicant as “a voice crying in the wilderness” and declined to engage in an academic exercise.
cases_referred_to: Trustees of the Makomo E Chimanimani v The Minister of Lands & Another 2016 (2) ZLR 324 (H); Mudzuri & Another v Minister of Justice, Legal & Parliamentary Affairs & Others CCZ 12/15.
legislation_considered: Statutory Instrument 127 of 2021.
reviewer_comments: Full text extracted from the mounted DOCX. The heading contains the typo “REPUBIC”; preserved in full judgment text.

document_name: S v Chikanga and Another (233 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 233 (5 April 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Chikanga and Another
subject_area: Criminal review – stock theft – evidence – single witness – implication – mandatory sentence
catchwords_flynote: Stock theft; criminal review; minimum mandatory sentence; circumstantial evidence; single-witness caution; conviction without cogent evidence.
headnote: Two self-acting accused persons were convicted after trial on two counts of stock theft and sentenced to nine years per count, with the sentences running concurrently. The alleged theft concerned two beasts, which had been sold to a purchaser who implicated the accused persons.
issues_for_determination: Whether the State proved the actus reus of stock theft beyond reasonable doubt; whether the convictions and sentences were in accordance with real and substantial justice.
legal_principles: The court must apply caution to single-witness and implication evidence, especially in serious offences carrying mandatory minimum penalties. Conviction must rest on cogent evidence establishing each accused’s participation.
held_decision_points: The court held that the State had not proved its case beyond reasonable doubt. The proceedings were not in accordance with real and substantial justice.
ratio_decidendi: A stock theft conviction cannot rest on untested or inadequately supported implication without cogent proof of participation in the theft.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court observed that caution is required when dealing with single-witness evidence and with offences carrying severe statutory penalties.
cases_referred_to: Kombayi v State HH; Kapende v S; S v Mupfumira HH; R v Mokoena; Banana v The State SC; S v Mupfumburi HH; Fox v HM Advocate, as referenced in the full text.
legislation_considered: Magistrates Court Act [Chapter 7:10], s 57; High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], s 26; Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 114.
