document_name: S v Muteve (234 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 234 (29 March 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: The State v Felix Muteve
subject_area: Criminal law; murder; common purpose; circumstantial evidence; single witness; mob assault
catchwords_flynote: Murder - alleged thief assaulted at car park - accused struck deceased on head with guava switch and resisted his release - subsequent assaults by others - common purpose - post-mortem evidence - credibility of single witness - constructive intent - conviction for murder under s 47(1)(b) - 20 years imprisonment.
headnote: The accused was charged with murder together with others after the deceased, suspected of theft at a car park, was assaulted and later died. The other accused were discharged at the close of the State case, leaving Felix Muteve to answer. A key witness testified that the accused struck the deceased on the head with a guava switch, resisted suggestions to let him go, and helped create circumstances in which others further assaulted and disabled him. The court rejected the accused’s denial, accepted the witness’s evidence, and found that the accused’s conduct and common purpose with later assailants materially contributed to death.
issues_for_determination: Whether the accused assaulted the deceased; whether the State witness was an accomplice or credible single witness; whether the accused’s actions formed common purpose with subsequent assailants; whether the accused had the requisite mens rea for murder; and what sentence was appropriate.
legal_principles: A court may convict on the evidence of a competent and credible single witness. A witness is not an accomplice merely because he was arrested or present unless he participated in the crime. Common purpose may arise where an accused’s conduct facilitates or associates with further assaults. Striking a vulnerable part of the body with a weapon and persisting in unlawful detention can establish constructive intent.
held_decision_points: The accused was convicted of murder as defined in s 47(1)(b). The court sentenced him to 20 years’ imprisonment, taking into account the seriousness of the offence and three years of pre-trial incarceration.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that an accused who initiates or contributes to unlawful assault and detention of a suspected thief, and thereby exposes the victim to further violence by others, may be liable for murder where the facts show common purpose and foresight of fatal consequences.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court emphasized that suspected thieves must be handed to police and that taking the law into one’s own hands, especially where human life is lost, will not be condoned.
cases_referred_to: State v Donga and Another 1993 (2) ZLR 291; State v Mazhambe 1997 (2) ZLR 587; Jonathan Burchell, Principles of Criminal Law referred to.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 47(1)(b); Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], ss 314, 115, 115B; Constitution of Zimbabwe, s 48(2)(c)(i); Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], s 358.
constitutional_provisions_considered: Constitution of Zimbabwe, s 48(2)(c)(i) considered in relation to death penalty and age.

document_name: Dahaw (Private) Limited and Another v Willdale Limited and 5 Others (235 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 235 (6 April 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: DAHAW (PRIVATE) LIMITED and NYASHA NOREEN NYOROVAI DEL CAMPO (NEE MUJURU) versus WILLDALE LIMITED and 5 OTHERS
case_number: HC 1723/22; Ref Case No. HC 254/22; Ref Case SC 76/22
citation: Dahaw (Private) Limited and Another v Willdale Limited and 5 Others (235 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 235 (6 April 2022)
subject_area: Company law; urgent chamber application; shareholder dispute; derivative action; corporate authority; company resolutions; AGM voting rights.
catchwords_flynote: Company proceedings – authority to litigate – no resolution by applicant company – director/shareholder purporting to sue on behalf of company and by derivative action – derivative action not available where company itself sues and wrongdoer is an outsider – application struck off roll.
headnote: The applicants sought urgent relief to protect Dahaw’s voting rights in Willdale’s AGM and to bar the third to sixth respondents from acting for Dahaw pending related litigation. The Court held that the first applicant company was not properly before the court because no resolution authorised litigation or the second applicant to represent it. The second applicant could not institute proceedings on behalf of the company merely as director, and the requirements of a derivative action under s 61 of the Companies and Other Business Entities Act were not satisfied. With no proper applicants before it, the Court struck the matter off the roll with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the first applicant company authorised litigation and the second applicant to represent it.
    Whether a director can sue in her own name on behalf of a company.
    Whether the second applicant could bring a derivative action under s 61 of the Companies and Other Business Entities Act.
    Whether the respondent’s deponent was authorised.
    Whether the matter could proceed if both applicants were not properly before the court.
legal_principles: A company is a separate legal persona and ordinarily litigates through a properly authorised resolution or representative. A director cannot simply sue in her personal name on behalf of the company. A derivative action is a statutory exception allowing a member or shareholder to sue on behalf of the company where those controlling the company prevent it from suing wrongdoers, usually insiders, and statutory requirements are met. A company cannot itself sue by derivative action to protect its own rights.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the respondent’s resolution authorising its deponent was acceptable and the applicants’ objection failed.
    Held, that the first applicant was not properly before the court because no resolution authorised the litigation or the second applicant’s representation.
    Held, that the second applicant could not sue as director on behalf of the company.
    Held, that the facts did not fit a derivative action under s 61.
    Held, that no applicants were properly before the court and the matter was struck off the roll with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that company litigation must be properly authorised and a derivative action cannot be used where the company itself is named as applicant and the statutory circumstances for a shareholder to sue on its behalf are absent.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court explained the derivative action as an exception to the rule that a wronged company sues in its own name, arising when wrongdoers controlling the company refuse to allow it to sue.
cases_referred_to: Minister of Mines & Ors v Grandwell Holdings (Pvt) Ltd & Ors 2018 (1) ZLR 660 (S)
legislation_considered: Companies and Other Business Entities Act [Chapter 24:31], section 61; High Court Rules, 2021.
subject_area_terms: Company law; urgent chamber application; shareholder dispute; derivative action; corporate authority; company resolutions
cases_referred_to_terms: Minister of Mines & Ors v Grandwell Holdings (Pvt) Ltd & Ors 2018 (1) ZLR 660 (S)
legislation_considered_terms: Companies and Other Business Entities Act [Chapter 24:31], section 61

document_name: Offer Sivani and Another v Shibtai and 2 Others (236 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 236 (7 April 2022) Copy(2).docx
case_name: Sivani and Another v Shibtai and Others
subject_area: Company law; urgent interdict; shareholder dispute; derivative protection; bank accounts; corporate control.
catchwords_flynote: Urgent application – corporate control dispute – dispute over shareholding/directorship of Adlecraft Investments – pending appeal and related proceedings – interim protection of company bank accounts – test for provisional interdict – prima facie right shown.
headnote: The applicants sought interim relief in a dispute concerning the ownership, shareholding and management of Adlecraft Investments (Pvt) Ltd. The first applicant claimed to be the sole shareholder and co-director of the second applicant and alleged that the first and second respondents were operating company accounts and conducting business without proper authority. The respondents disputed his shareholding and raised objections including the existence of appeal proceedings and alleged material disputes of fact. The court held that the first applicant had demonstrated at least a prima facie right, and that interim preservation was necessary to protect the bank accounts pending determination of the related proceedings. A provisional order was granted in varied terms.
issues_for_determination: Whether the urgent application was properly before the court despite related appeal proceedings; whether the first applicant had locus standi or a prima facie right; whether the court should grant interim protection over specified company bank accounts; whether the relief sought was too close to final relief.
legal_principles: For interim relief the applicant must show a prima facie right, a well-grounded apprehension of irreparable harm, a balance of convenience favouring relief and absence of another satisfactory remedy. Material factual disputes that cannot be finally resolved on papers may be left for determination on the return date where the relief is interim and preservatory.
held_decision_points: The provisional order was granted in varied terms. The first and second respondents were interdicted from operating the specified Getbucks accounts in the name of Adlecraft Investments pending HC 4541/21 or a contrary order, and from soliciting business on behalf of Adlecraft outside the knowledge and consent of the first applicant as co-director.
ratio_decidendi: The first applicant’s share certificate and company documents established a prima facie right. Uncontrolled use of company bank accounts could cause irreparable prejudice before the main dispute was determined, while the respondents could seek variation if corporate rescue or other circumstances required access to the funds.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted that the order did not negate the corporate rescue status of the company and that a corporate rescue practitioner could seek variation or discharge of the order if necessary.
cases_referred_to: Sivani and Another v Shibtai and Others HH 550-21; Mushoriwa v City of Harare HH 195/14; Broadcasting Authority of Zimbabwe & Anor v Dr Dish (Pvt) Ltd SC 62/18; Amalgamated Rural Teachers Union of Zimbabwe & Anor v ZANU PF & Anor HMA 36/18; Attorney General v Punch Ltd & Anor [2002] UKHL 50; American Cyanamid Co v Ethicon Ltd [1975] AC 396.
legislation_considered: Companies and Other Business Entities Act [Chapter 24:31], including derivative action provisions; High Court Rules, 2021.

document_name: Du Toit v Mushambi and Another (237 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 237 (13 April 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: DANIEL FRANCOIS DU TOIT versus GEORGE MUSHAMBI and THE SHERIFF OF THE HIGH COURT N.O.
case_number: HC 2106/21; Ref Case HC 4742/21; HC 2086/22 referred to in the judgment
citation: Du Toit v Mushambi and Another (237 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 237 (13 April 2022)
subject_area: Civil procedure; urgent chamber application; stay of execution; rescission of default judgment; agricultural land; illegality; joint venture agreements.
catchwords_flynote: Stay of execution pending rescission – applicant relying on oral joint venture in agricultural land – statutory prohibition on partnership without ministerial consent – illegality – no cause of action or prospects of success – application dismissed.
headnote: The applicant sought an urgent stay of ejectment from Odzi Drift Estate pending rescission of a default judgment. His asserted defence was that he had an oral joint venture agreement with the late permit holder. The Court held that, under the Agricultural Land Settlement (Permit Terms and Conditions) Regulations, a permit holder may not enter a partnership for the working of allocated land without ministerial consent. The applicant did not deny that no such consent existed. The alleged agreement was therefore illegal and could not found a cause of action or a bona fide defence. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the urgent application disclosed a cause of action.
    Whether an oral joint venture over agricultural land without ministerial consent could support a bona fide defence.
    Whether the applicant had prospects of success in the rescission application.
    Whether execution should be stayed pending rescission.
legal_principles: A thing done contrary to a direct statutory prohibition is void and of no force or effect. Section 7(1)(b) of the Agricultural Land Settlement (Permit Terms and Conditions) Regulations, SI 53 of 2014, prohibits a permit holder from ceding, assigning, alienating, subletting or entering into a partnership for the working of allocated land without the consent of the Minister. A stay of execution pending rescission requires prospects of success and a legally sustainable basis for relief.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the oral joint venture agreement relied upon by the applicant was illegal because no ministerial consent was alleged or proved.
    Held, that no cause of action can be founded on an illegality.
    Held, that the applicant had no prospects of success in the main matter or rescission application.
    Held, that the first respondent’s point in limine based on illegality was upheld.
    Held, that the application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that an alleged oral joint venture for agricultural land, concluded without the consent required by statute, is void and cannot constitute a bona fide defence capable of justifying stay of execution pending rescission.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted an additional inconsistency: the applicant alleged that the oral joint venture was entered into sometime in 2021, while the letters of administration showed that Christopher Francis Mushambi died on 15 November 2020.
cases_referred_to:
Schierhout v Minister of Justice 1926 AD 99
    ZMDC and Kamative Mine v Jambata HH 681/21
legislation_considered: Agricultural Land Settlement (Permit Terms and Conditions) Regulations, SI 53 of 2014, section 7(1)(b); High Court Rules, 2021; law governing rescission and stay of execution.

document_name: Kanyekanye v Kanyekanye (Nee Sithole) (238 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 238 (14 April 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: CELESTINO CHENJERAI KANYEKANYE versus MEMORY KANYEKANYE (NEE SITHOLE)
subject_area: Family law; divorce; custody; child maintenance; major child support; postnuptial agreement; matrimonial property distribution.
catchwords_flynote: Divorce – best interests of minor child – custody awarded to father where mother works away from home – maintenance – major child abroad – postnuptial agreement/acquiescence – each party retains separately acquired immovable property – division of movables – decree of divorce granted.
headnote: The parties agreed that their marriage had irretrievably broken down but disputed custody, maintenance, the effect of a postnuptial agreement, and division of immovable property. The Court held that the best interests of the minor child favoured custody to the father because he was available in Harare while the mother worked in Shamva and relied on a maid. The mother was ordered to pay US$50 per month maintenance. The father retained the Borrowdale property and the mother retained No. 23 Kimmich Heights. A decree of divorce and ancillary relief were granted.
issues_for_determination:
Whether custody of the minor child should be awarded to the plaintiff.
    How much maintenance the non-custodial parent should pay.
    Whether the plaintiff should contribute to the major child’s education abroad.
    Whether the postnuptial agreement was binding.
    How the immovable and movable property should be distributed.
legal_principles: In custody matters, the governing test is the best interests of the child, not biological motherhood alone. Both parents have a duty to maintain minor children according to means. Property distribution on divorce is governed by section 7 of the Matrimonial Causes Act and the “his, hers and theirs” approach in Takafuma v Takafuma. Parties may bind themselves by agreement or acquiescence.
held_decision_points:
Held, that a decree of divorce was granted.
    Held, that custody of the minor child was awarded to the plaintiff/father.
    Held, that the defendant must pay US$50 per month maintenance for the minor child and continue supporting the major child as she had been doing.
    Held, that the plaintiff was awarded 100% of the Borrowdale property and the defendant was awarded No. 23 Kimmich Heights.
    Held, that movable property was divided as agreed and each party bears own costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the child’s best interests favoured the more available parent, and equitable property distribution required each party to retain the immovable property separately acquired and developed by that party, given the parties’ conduct and evidence.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court emphasised that parental roles are social constructs and not determined simply by the biological fact of motherhood.
cases_referred_to: Denhere v Denhere SC 51/17; Hackim v Hackim 1988 (2) ZLR 61; Saqidi v Muteswa HH-249-20; Tangirai v Tangirai HC 30/2011; Mubaiwa v Chigwanda HC 1249/2012; Fleiner v Fleiner & Anor HH 261/18; Revesai v Windmill HH-790-16; Hepner v Roodepoort-Maraisburg Town Council 1962 (4) SA 771 (A); Laws v Rutherford 1924 AD 261; Takafuma v Takafuma 1994 (2) ZLR 103 (S); Hatendi v Hatendi 2001 (2) ZLR 530; Ncube v Ncube 1993 (1) ZLR 39 (S); Shenje v Shenje 2001 (2) ZLR 160; Dzova v Dzova HC 753/08.
legislation_considered: Marriage Act [Chapter 5:11]; Matrimonial Causes Act [Chapter 5:13], sections 7 and 8.

document_name: S v Jambo (239 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 239 (11 April 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Jambo
subject_area: Criminal law; criminal review; guilty plea procedure; sentence legality
catchwords_flynote: Criminal review – deliberately supplying false information to public authority – guilty plea – s 271 procedure not followed – sentence exceeding statutory maximum – proceedings set aside.
headnote: The accused pleaded guilty to two counts of deliberately supplying false information to a public authority. The High Court held that the magistrate failed to explain the charge before plea and record the explanation as required by s 271, and also imposed a sentence beyond the statutory maximum. The proceedings were set aside and the accused was ordered released if still serving.
issues_for_determination: Whether the guilty plea procedure complied with s 271; whether the sentence was competent under s 180 of the Criminal Law Code.
legal_principles: The charge must be explained and the explanation recorded before an unrepresented accused pleads guilty. Failure to follow legislated fair-trial procedure is fatal. A court must acquaint itself with the statutory penalty provision before sentencing.
held_decision_points: Proceedings set aside for procedural irregularity; accused to be released forthwith if still serving sentence.
ratio_decidendi: The failure to comply with mandatory plea procedure and imposition of a sentence exceeding the statutory maximum rendered the proceedings grossly irregular.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court observed that most magistrates now generally follow the correct guilty-plea procedure, but this case was an exception.
cases_referred_to: S v Mangwende HH 695/20; S v Mutokodzi HH 299/21.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 180; Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], s 271(2)(b), s 271(3).
constitutional_provisions_considered: Constitution of Zimbabwe, s 86(3)(e), referred to in the context of fair trial as an absolute right.

document_name: NJANIK~1(2).DOC
case_name: Njanike Construction (Private) Limited v Lafarge Cement Zimbabwe Limited
subject_area: Contract; specific performance; motion proceedings; existence of contract; founding affidavit.
catchwords_flynote: Specific performance – alleged purchase of cement – applicant seeking delivery of 25 200 bags – respondent disputing contract – applicant’s founding affidavit skeletal – applicant cannot make case in answering affidavit – application dismissed.
headnote: The applicant sought specific performance compelling the respondent to deliver 25 200 bags of cement. The applicant alleged that it had purchased 27 000 bags in 2018 and later a further 1 800 bags, and that the respondent had wrongfully reduced its entitlement after currency changes. The respondent disputed the existence of a sale contract and gave a different explanation for the deposit. The court held that the applicant bore the onus to prove a valid contract and that its founding affidavit did not plead the necessary facts of offer, acceptance and contract formation. It was impermissible to build the case in the answering affidavit. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether a valid contract of sale had been proved; whether specific performance could be granted; whether the founding affidavit contained sufficient averments.
legal_principles: Specific performance presupposes a valid contract and performance or readiness to perform by the applicant. Motion proceedings stand or fall on the founding affidavit. A litigant may not make out or materially fortify its case for the first time in the answering affidavit.
held_decision_points: The application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The founding affidavit did not establish the necessary facts of contract formation. In the face of a direct denial of contract by the respondent, the applicant’s attempt to cure deficiencies in the answering affidavit was impermissible.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The preliminary objection based on spacing and punctuation in the respondent’s corporate name lacked merit.
cases_referred_to: ZUPCO Ltd v Parkhorse Services (Private) Ltd SC 13/17; Chiarelli v Bouna Investments (Pvt) Ltd HH 678-15; Victoria Falls Municipality v Ndlovu; Fuyana v Moyo SC 54/06; Muchini v Adams & Ors SC 47/13; Austerlands (Pvt) Ltd v Trade & Investment Bank Ltd; Turner & Sons (Private) Limited v Master of the High Court & Ors HH 49-18; Kaskay Properties (Pvt) Ltd v Minister of Lands HH 762-18.

document_name: Craft v Craft nee Moss (241 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 241 (8 April 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: MICHAEL CRAFT versus SARAH CRAFT (nee MOSS)
citation: Craft v Craft nee Moss (241 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 241 (8 April 2022)
subject_area: Family law; urgent chamber application; access to minor children; international travel; best interests of the child; procedure; costs.
catchwords_flynote: Minor children – father seeking leave to travel with children to United Kingdom and Kenya – best interests of children – mother’s objections not bona fide – urgent chamber procedure despite final relief – attorney-client costs for vexatious opposition.
headnote: The applicant sought permission to travel with his two minor children to the United Kingdom for a family holiday and to Kenya for a wedding. The respondent opposed, raising concerns about brainwashing and abduction, but the abduction concern was allayed by the draft order requiring return of the children. The Court held that the trips were in the children’s best interests and that the respondent’s opposition was not bona fide, especially given her delay and prior cooperative access arrangements. The application was granted and the respondent was ordered to pay attorney-client costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether urgent chamber procedure could be used despite final relief being sought.
    Whether international travel with the children was in their best interests.
    Whether the respondent’s objections of brainwashing, abduction or insufficient detail were valid.
    Whether attorney-client costs were justified.
legal_principles: The court may overlook use of chamber application instead of court application where no prejudice results, in terms of rule 57(13) of the High Court Rules, 2021. The paramount consideration in matters involving children is their best interests. A parent’s access and travel with children may be authorised where the concerns raised are not legitimate or are addressed by safeguards. Punitive costs may be awarded for vexatious, mala fide opposition or abuse of court process.
held_decision_points:
Held, that no prejudice arose from the use of urgent chamber procedure and the procedural objection failed.
    Held, that the applicant had disclosed sufficient detail about the proposed travel.
    Held, that the abduction concern had been largely addressed by the return obligation and was unsupported by evidence.
    Held, that the respondent’s opposition was vexatious and lacked bona fides.
    Held, that it was in the best interests of the children to travel with the applicant.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that, where proposed travel with a non-custodial parent is shown to be beneficial and the opposing parent’s objections are unsupported, delayed and not bona fide, the court may authorise travel in the children’s best interests and impose punitive costs.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted that the draft order is only a draft and that the court is responsible for the precise wording of the final order as long as the substance is supported by the papers.
cases_referred_to: None identified in the extracted text.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, rule 57(13); child law principles on best interests and parental access.
subject_area_terms: Family law; urgent chamber application; access to minor children; international travel; best interests of the child; procedure; costs.
cases_referred_to_terms: None identified in the extracted text.
legislation_considered_terms: High Court Rules, 2021, rule 57(13); child law principles on best interests and parental access.

document_name: Jivan v Salzman Et Cie SA and Another (242 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 242 (13 April 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: MANOJKUMAR JIVAN versus SALZMAN ET CIE SA AND ANOTHER
subject_area: Civil procedure; execution; superannuation of judgment; revival of judgment; currency conversion; SI 33/2019; outstanding obligations.
catchwords_flynote: Judgment debt – superannuated judgment – writ issued without revival – debt expressed in United States dollars – whether SI 33/2019 converted debt to RTGS at one-to-one – foreign loan exception not established – RTGS payment held to settle debt – costs.
headnote: The dispute arose from execution of a judgment in HC 7916/14. The parties agreed that the judgment had superannuated and the writ and attachment were a nullity. The remaining issue was whether the judgment debt, expressed in United States dollars, was converted by SI 33/2019 at one-to-one, and whether RTGS 540 000 paid through the Sheriff settled the debt. The Court held that the judgment debt was an outstanding obligation subject to SI 33/2019 and that the RTGS payment extinguished it.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the judgment had superannuated and the writ was a nullity.
    Whether the judgment debt was an outstanding obligation subject to SI 33/2019.
    Whether the foreign-loan/foreign-obligation exception applied.
    Whether RTGS 540 000 paid to the Sheriff settled the debt.
    Whether the judgment should be revived.
legal_principles: A superannuated judgment cannot be executed without revival. SI 33/2019 converted specified assets and liabilities expressed in United States dollars into RTGS dollars at one-to-one unless they fell within an exempt category such as a foreign loan or foreign obligation. The party asserting an exemption must show that the debt falls within the exemption.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the judgment debt constituted an outstanding obligation subject to section 4(1)(d) of SI 33/2019.
    Held, that the debt was payable in local currency at one-to-one.
    Held, that RTGS 540 000 paid to the second respondent on 5 April 2021 fully settled and extinguished the judgment debt.
    Held, that the first respondent shall pay costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the court order created an outstanding obligation valued in United States dollars which, absent proof that it was an exempt foreign loan or obligation, was converted by SI 33/2019 at one-to-one; payment of the converted amount discharged the judgment debt.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court stressed that the exemption depends on the character of the debt or loan, not merely on the creditor’s foreign incorporation or domicile.
cases_referred_to: Zambezi Gas Zimbabwe (Pvt) Ltd v N.R. Barber (Pvt) Ltd & Anor SC 3/20; FBC Bank Ltd v Dunleth Enterprises (Pvt) Ltd SC 4/20; Zimbabwe Leaf Tobacco Co (Pvt) Ltd authorities as cited.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, rule 58(8); Statutory Instrument 33 of 2019; Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe Act [Chapter 22:15], section 44C(2).
subject_area_terms: Civil procedure; execution; superannuation of judgment; revival of judgment; currency conversion
cases_referred_to_terms: Zambezi Gas Zimbabwe (Pvt) Ltd v N.R. Barber (Pvt) Ltd & Anor SC 3/20
FBC Bank Ltd v Dunleth Enterprises (Pvt) Ltd SC 4/20
Zimbabwe Leaf Tobacco Co (Pvt) Ltd authorities as cited.

document_name: S v Jose (243 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 243 (28 March 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Aluwisi Joe
subject_area: Criminal law; murder; provocation; self-defence; circumstantial evidence; sentencing
catchwords_flynote: Murder – accused stabbing deceased with okapi knife during fight over woman – provocation and self-defence raised – confirmed warned and cautioned statement – recent fabrication rejected – murder with constructive intent – sentence.
headnote: The accused was charged with murdering Solomon Munyezi by stabbing him on the chest. He pleaded provocation and, alternatively, self-defence. The court rejected the accused’s trial version that the deceased had armed himself with an axe and that the accused acted defensively, holding that this was a recent fabrication inconsistent with the confirmed warned and cautioned statement. The accused was convicted of murder under s 47(1)(b) and sentenced to 15 years’ imprisonment.
issues_for_determination: Whether the accused acted in self-defence or under provocation; whether the State proved murder; what sentence was appropriate considering youth and pre-trial incarceration.
legal_principles: Self-defence requires a lawful defensive response to an unlawful attack. A confirmed warned and cautioned statement may be admissible and can undermine a later inconsistent defence. Constructive intent for murder may be inferred where a lethal weapon is intentionally used on a vulnerable part of the body and death is a real risk appreciated by the accused.
held_decision_points: The accused was convicted of murder under s 47(1)(b) and sentenced to 15 years’ imprisonment.
ratio_decidendi: The accused’s use of an okapi knife to stab the deceased in the chest after deciding to continue the fight showed legal intent sufficient for murder; his self-defence version was not reasonably possibly true.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted the uncertainty around the accused’s age, accepted the defence submission that he was below 21 at the time, and therefore did not consider the death penalty.
cases_referred_to: None specifically identified beyond the statutory framework.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 47; Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], ss 115B, 358; Constitution of Zimbabwe, s 48(2)(c)(i).
constitutional_provisions_considered: Constitution of Zimbabwe, s 48(2)(c)(i), on prohibition of death penalty for persons below 21 at the time of offence.
reviewer_comments: The uploaded filename says S v Jose, while the judgment heading uses the accused’s name as Aluwisi Joe. The case name field follows the judgment heading.
export_history: Created as part of Harare High Court 2022 batch 21 consolidated TXT export.
subject_area_terms: Criminal law; murder; provocation; self-defence; circumstantial evidence; sentencing

Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], ss 115B, 358

document_name: Beauro Tech Investments Pvt Ltd t_a Trip Trans v Sanyanga and 2 Others (244 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 244 (30 March 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: BEAURO TECH INVESTMENTS PVT LTD t/a TRIP TRANS versus DADIRAI AGNES SANYANGA and SHERIFF ZIMBABWE N.O and MARSHALL RUKWEZA
citation: Beauro Tech Investments Pvt Ltd t/a Trip Trans v Sanyanga and 2 Others (244 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 244 (30 March 2022)
subject_area: Civil procedure; urgent chamber application; stay of execution; rescission of default judgment; delictual damages; legal practitioner negligence; irreparable harm.
catchwords_flynote: Stay of execution – default judgment for damages following fatal road accident – applicant seeking stay pending rescission – failure to explain default and apparent wilful default – prospects of rescission dim – alternative remedies available to avoid sale in execution – irreparable harm not established – application dismissed with costs.
headnote: The applicant sought a stay of execution of a default judgment granted against it in damages proceedings arising from the death of the first respondent's husband in a bus accident. The Court found that the applicant had failed to adequately explain the default that led to the judgment, and its prospects of success in rescission were dim. The Court held that alleged harm from a possible sale in execution could be avoided through negotiation, payment into trust, financing, or potential claims against negligent legal practitioners. The applicant had not established irreparable harm, an essential requirement for interim relief. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether execution of the default judgment should be stayed pending rescission.
    Whether the applicant adequately explained the default leading to judgment.
    Whether the pending rescission application had prospects of success.
    Whether the applicant established irreparable harm.
    Whether alternative remedies were available.
legal_principles: A party seeking interim stay of execution pending rescission must establish a prima facie case, prospects of success, irreparable harm, balance of convenience and absence of an adequate alternative remedy. A litigant may be bound by its legal practitioner's conduct, although the rule is not immutable. Failure to explain apparent wilful default undermines prospects of rescission and interim relief.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant failed to satisfactorily explain the default leading to automatic bar and default judgment.
    Held, that prospects of success in rescission were dim.
    Held, that alleged irreparable harm was not proved because alternative remedies were available to avoid sale or recover losses.
    Held, that the requirements for interim stay of execution were not met.
    Held, that the application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that a stay of execution pending rescission will not be granted where the applicant's default appears wilful or unexplained, prospects of rescission are dim, and the feared harm is compensable or avoidable through alternative remedies.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that the applicant might have stronger prospects in a claim against its erstwhile legal practitioners if their negligence caused the loss.
cases_referred_to: Not available
legislation_considered: High Court Rules governing default judgment, rescission and urgent chamber applications.
subject_area_terms: Civil procedure; urgent chamber application; stay of execution; rescission of default judgment; delictual damages; legal practitioner negligence; irreparable harm.
cases_referred_to_terms: Not available
legislation_considered_terms: High Court Rules governing default judgment, rescission and urgent chamber applications.

document_name: Tafeni v Gumbo; Gumbo v Tafeni (245 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 245 (13 April 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: Tafeni v Gumbo; Gumbo v Tafeni
subject_area: Family law; urgent chamber application; child travel; passport; custody; counter-application; curator ad litem; urgency
catchwords_flynote: Urgency — mother seeking return of minor child’s passport and travel authority — urgency not established in founding affidavit — interim relief effectively final — counter-application for custody without curator ad litem and Master’s report — irregular proceedings.
headnote: The main application sought urgent relief requiring the respondent to return the minor child’s passport and sign documents authorising travel to the United Kingdom. The court held that urgency was not established in the founding affidavit and that the application appeared to conflate interim and final relief. The counter-application sought custody but did not comply with the mandatory procedure for applications involving minors, including appointment of a curator ad litem and a Master’s report. The main application was removed from the urgent roll and the counter-application was struck off.
issues_for_determination:
Whether urgency was properly established.
    Whether the founding affidavit identified when the need to act arose and showed irreparable harm.
    Whether the interim relief sought was effectively final.
    Whether the counter-application for custody was properly before the court without the required curator ad litem process.
legal_principles:
Urgency must be established in the founding affidavit, including when the need to act arose and why the matter cannot wait.
    A certificate of urgency cannot substitute for facts missing from the founding affidavit.
    Interim relief should not simply grant the final relief.
    Applications concerning minors require compliance with the High Court Rules and the Guardianship of Minors Act, including appointment of a curator ad litem and appropriate reports to protect the child’s best interests.
held_decision_points:
The main application did not establish urgency and was removed from the urgent roll.
    The counter-application for custody was irregular because no curator ad litem was appointed and no Master’s certificate or curator report was filed.
    Each party was to bear its own costs.
ratio_decidendi: The applicant did not place adequate facts before the court showing when urgency arose, and the counter-application attempted to determine custody without the procedural safeguards required in matters involving minor children.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court emphasised that a child cannot be treated as common property in a tussle between adults and that custody/guardianship issues require compliance with detailed statutory and procedural safeguards.
cases_referred_to:
Jim Kunaka v Minister of Health and Child Care and 2 Others HH 46/21
    Memory Minezhi v Boyland Boora HH 201/20
    Constantine Guvheya Dominic Chiwenga v Marry Mubaiwa SC 86/20
legislation_considered:
Guardianship of Minors Act [Chapter 5:08], ss 4 and 5
    High Court Rules, 2021, r 61(2)(b)
reviewer_comments: Mounted file was extractable. Main and counter-application were dealt with in one judgment.

document_name: Katsimberis v Rwodzi N.O and Another (246 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 246 (13 April 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: GEORGE KATSIMBERIS versus LETWIN RWODZI N.O. AND ANOTHER
subject_area: Criminal procedure; review; referral to Constitutional Court; compliance with review notice requirements; rule 257; procedural defects.
catchwords_flynote: Review application – magistrate refused referral to Constitutional Court – application brought under old High Court Rules – notice failed to state relief sought ex facie – rule 257 peremptory – no condonation – court may raise procedural non-compliance – application struck off roll with no order as to costs.
headnote: The applicant sought review of a regional magistrate’s refusal to refer criminal proceedings to the Constitutional Court. The Court raised whether the review application complied with rule 257 of the 1971 High Court Rules. The application stated grounds of review but did not state the exact relief sought on the face of the court application. The Court held that this was non-compliance with a peremptory rule, declined to condone the defect, and struck the application off the roll with no order as to costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the review application complied with rule 257.
    Whether stating the relief only in the founding affidavit or draft order cured the defect.
    Whether the court could raise procedural non-compliance mero motu.
    Whether non-compliance should be condoned under rule 4C.
legal_principles: Rule 257 required a review application to state shortly and clearly both the grounds for review and the exact relief prayed for on the face of the application. Non-compliance may bar relief. A court may raise prima facie non-compliance with its own rules, provided parties are afforded an opportunity to be heard. Condonation must be justified as required in the interests of justice.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the application did not comply with rule 257 because the relief was not stated ex facie the court application.
    Held, that reference to a draft order or founding affidavit did not cure the defect.
    Held, that the court could raise the procedural defect mero motu after hearing the parties.
    Held, that the application was struck off the roll with no order as to costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that rule 257 imposed two equal mandatory requirements—grounds and exact relief on the face of the application—and the omission of either rendered the review application improperly before the court.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that the 2021 rules expressly reinforced the same requirement by stating that both grounds and relief shall appear on the face of the court application.
cases_referred_to: Minister of Labour & Ors v Pen Transport (Pvt) Ltd 1989 (1) ZLR 293 (S); Utterton v Utterton 1969 (2) RLR 404 (GD); Rimayi v Minister of National Supplies & Anor S-86-90; Chataira v Zimbabwe Electricity Supply Authority 2001 (1) ZLR 30 (H); Mambo v National Railways of Zimbabwe & Anor 2003 (1) ZLR 347 (H); Gonye v Mtombeni N.O. and Others HH 356/17; Oasis Medical Centre Private Limited v Rosemary A.M. Beck & Others HH 84/2016; Proton v Takaendesa 2005 (1) ZLR 60.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 1971, rules 257 and 4C; High Court Rules, 2021, rule 62(2); Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07]; Constitution of Zimbabwe, 2013, section 175(4).
subject_area_terms: Criminal procedure; review; referral to Constitutional Court; compliance with review notice requirements; rule 257; procedural defects.

document_name: Dixson v Registrar of Deeds N.O and 2 Others (247 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 247 (14 April 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: ALAN JOHN WILLIAM DIXSON versus REGISTRAR OF DEEDS N.O. and 2 OTHERS
citation: Dixson v Registrar of Deeds N.O and 2 Others (247 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 247 (14 April 2022)
subject_area: Chamber application; preliminary points; cancellation of title deeds; Titles Registration and Derelict Lands Act; effect of rescission order; disputes of fact; form of chamber application.
catchwords_flynote: Cancellation of title deeds – applicant seeking to cancel titles obtained after default order – preliminary objection based on form and alleged factual disputes – no prejudice from form irregularity – issue was legal effect of earlier rescission order, not disputed sale facts – preliminary points dismissed.
headnote: The applicant sought cancellation of deeds of transfer issued to the second and third respondents after an earlier default order had directed transfer of the property under the Titles Registration and Derelict Lands Act. That order was later set aside by consent. The second respondent raised preliminary points that the chamber application was defective and that material disputes of fact existed. The Court dismissed both. The form/dies issue caused no prejudice and could be condoned. The dispute did not turn on whether a sale existed but on the legal effect of the order setting aside the earlier transfer order.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the application failed for non-compliance with rule 60(1) and use of a shortened dies period.
    Whether the defect caused prejudice and should be condoned.
    Whether material disputes of fact required oral evidence.
    Whether the issue before the court was a factual sale dispute or the legal consequence of the consent order setting aside the transfer order.
legal_principles: Under rule 60(1) of the High Court Rules, a chamber application must use the prescribed form and, where served on interested parties, Form 23 with appropriate modifications. Non-compliance may be condoned where no prejudice is caused. A material dispute of fact does not arise merely because background sale facts are disputed if the application can be resolved on the legal effect of an extant court order.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the use of a five-day dies period rather than ten days did not prejudice the second respondent and could be condoned.
    Held, that there were no material disputes of fact requiring oral evidence because the application turned on interpretation and effect of the 23 July 2015 order.
    Held, that whether there had been a sale was a separate issue in HC 3625/12 and not decisive for the preliminary points.
    Held, that both preliminary points failed and were dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that procedural defects in a served chamber application can be condoned absent prejudice, and disputes about the historic sale did not prevent determination of the legal question whether the rescission of the transfer order affected subsequent title.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified beyond the court’s comments on the proper framing of the real issue.
cases_referred_to: Not available
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, rule 60(1); Titles Registration and Derelict Lands Act [Chapter 20:20].
subject_area_terms: Chamber application; preliminary points; cancellation of title deeds
Titles Registration and Derelict Lands Act; effect of rescission order; disputes of fact; form of chamber application.

document_name: Sithole v ZIMRA (248 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 248 (13 April 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: Sithole v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority
subject_area: Administrative law; customs and excise; immigrant’s rebate; prescription; declaratur; constitutional challenge
catchwords_flynote: Customs and excise — immigrant’s rebate — proceedings against ZIMRA — s 196 notice and eight-month time limit — declaratory relief and prescription — constitutional challenge to notice requirement.
headnote: The applicant, who had returned from the United Kingdom, challenged ZIMRA’s refusal to grant an immigrant’s rebate for a motor vehicle and sought declaratory relief. ZIMRA raised prescription under s 196 of the Customs and Excise Act. The court held that the applicant had instituted proceedings more than eight months after the cause arose and had not proved service of the mandatory 60-day notice. The applicant’s reliance on the declaratory nature of the claim did not avoid prescription. Her constitutional challenge to the notice requirement also failed. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant’s proceedings were barred by s 196 of the Customs and Excise Act.
    Whether declaratory relief is immune from prescription.
    Whether the 60-day notice requirement in s 196(1) is unconstitutional.
    Whether the applicant had complied with mandatory statutory preconditions for proceedings against ZIMRA.
legal_principles:
Section 196 of the Customs and Excise Act creates mandatory requirements: 60 days’ notice and institution within eight months after the cause arises.
    A declaratory claim does not permit a party to disregard peremptory statutory provisions.
    The High Court does not determine abstract or academic questions where the underlying right has prescribed.
    A constitutional challenge must be properly grounded and must show that the limitation is not justified.
held_decision_points:
The proceedings were instituted outside the eight-month period.
    The applicant failed to prove that she gave the required notice of intention to sue.
    The declaratory form of relief did not prevent prescription.
    The constitutional attack on s 196(1) was not established.
    The application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The applicant’s underlying right to challenge the customs decision was subject to the special statutory prescription and notice regime in s 196. Once those requirements were not met, no existing right remained for declaratory determination.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified.
cases_referred_to:
Dube v ZIMRA HB 2/14
    Ndlovu v Ndhlovu & Anor 2013 (1) ZLR 110 (H)
    Mangwiro v Minister of Justice, Legal and Parliamentary Affairs HH 172/17
    Munn Publishing (Private) Limited v Zimbabwe Broadcasting Corporation 1995 (4) SA 675
legislation_considered:
Customs and Excise Act [Chapter 23:02], s 196
    High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], s 14
    State Liabilities Act [Chapter 8:15]
reviewer_comments: Mounted file was extractable. The judgment turns on prescription and statutory notice requirements.

document_name: Kangara v Kangara and 2 Others (25 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 25 (13 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: AGREANAH KANGARA versus DARLINGTON KANGARA AND 2 OTHERS
subject_area: Spoliation; urgent chamber application; deceased estate; peaceful possession; costs; family property dispute.
catchwords_flynote: Urgent chamber application – alleged spoliation from house forming part of deceased estate – parties agree to provisional order by consent – costs disputed – ordinary costs appropriate – respondent ordered to restore possession pending finalisation of estate.
headnote: The applicant sought restoration of peaceful possession and occupation of 193 Fleetwood, Bindura, pending finalisation of the estate of the late Kudzanai Kangara. She alleged that the first respondent had ejected her, blocked access and threatened renovations. At the hearing the case against the second and third respondents was withdrawn and the remaining parties agreed to the substantive provisional relief. The Court dealt with costs and ordered ordinary costs, not attorney-client costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether possession should be restored pending finalisation of the estate.
    Whether the first respondent’s conduct justified a punitive costs order.
    Whether ordinary costs were sufficient.
legal_principles: Spoliation protects peaceful and undisturbed possession regardless of underlying rights. Costs generally follow the cause, but punitive costs require conduct deserving censure beyond ordinary litigation. The court may consider whether a respondent mitigated or saved costs by agreeing to relief.
held_decision_points:
Held, by consent, that the applicant’s peaceful occupation and possession should be restored pending finalisation of the estate.
    Held, that costs on the higher scale were not justified.
    Held, that the first respondent shall bear costs on the ordinary scale.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that, although restoration of possession was warranted, the first respondent’s conduct did not justify punitive costs because he removed the rubble and agreed to the order, saving litigation resources.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that the costs order should be sufficient to discourage the first respondent from taking the law into his own hands.
cases_referred_to: Hearle and McEwan v Mitchell’s Executor 1922 TPD 192; authorities on costs including the Biowatch principle and related texts as cited.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021; law of mandament van spolie; law of costs.
subject_area_terms: Spoliation; urgent chamber application; deceased estate; peaceful possession; costs; family property dispute.

document_name: Shaw and Another v Registrar General of Citizenship and 3 Others (250 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 250 (25 March 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Brian Shaw and Ashleigh Kerryn Shaw v Registrar General of Citizenship and 3 Others
subject_area: Constitutional law; citizenship by birth; dual citizenship; ordinary residence; national identity documents and passports
catchwords_flynote: Citizenship by birth - persons born outside Zimbabwe to Zimbabwean citizen parent ordinarily resident in Zimbabwe - persons born in Zimbabwe to Zimbabwean parent - dual citizenship - refusal to issue identity documents and passports unless South African citizenship renounced - constitutional entitlement declared.
headnote: The first applicant was born in South Africa to a mother who was a Zimbabwean citizen and, on the evidence, ordinarily resident in Zimbabwe. The second applicant was born in Zimbabwe to a Zimbabwean mother. The Registrar General and immigration authorities treated them as foreigners or required them to renounce South African citizenship before enjoying Zimbabwean citizenship rights. The court held that both were Zimbabwean citizens by birth under s 36 of the Constitution, and that the recognition of their citizenship was not conditional upon renouncing South African citizenship.
issues_for_determination: Whether the first applicant qualified as a Zimbabwean citizen by birth under s 36(2) despite being born outside Zimbabwe; whether the second applicant qualified under s 36(1); whether either applicant was required to renounce South African citizenship before enjoying Zimbabwean citizenship rights; and what ordinary residence meant for citizenship by birth.
legal_principles: Citizenship by birth under s 36 of the Constitution is not subject to revocation or further qualification except as provided by the Constitution. A person born outside Zimbabwe is a citizen by birth if a parent was a Zimbabwean citizen and ordinarily resident in Zimbabwe at the time of birth. Ordinary residence is assessed from the totality of evidence, including whether Zimbabwe was the person’s real or usual home notwithstanding temporary absence.
held_decision_points: The application succeeded. Both applicants were declared Zimbabwean citizens by birth, entitled to all rights, privileges and benefits and subject to the duties and obligations of citizenship. Their entitlement was not conditional upon renouncing South African citizenship. No order as to costs was made.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that s 36 of the Constitution confers citizenship by birth where its requirements are met, and administrative authorities cannot impose an additional renunciation requirement inconsistent with that constitutional entitlement.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court treated the concept of ordinary residence as a factual inquiry into a person’s true home and intention, drawing on comparative residence jurisprudence.
cases_referred_to: Mutumwa Dziva Mawere v Registrar General and Others CCZ 4/15; Cohen v CIR 13 SATC 362; CIR v Kuttel 1992 (3) SA 242 (A); Soldier v COT 1943 SR 130; Thompson v Minister of National Revenue 2 DTC 812 (SCC); Shah v Barnet London Borough Council and Other Appeals 1983 1 All ER 226 (HL).

document_name: S v Chikanya (251 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 251 (1 April 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Chikanya
subject_area: Criminal law – murder – domestic violence – circumstantial evidence – constructive intent – sentence
catchwords_flynote: Murder; constructive intent; domestic violence; circumstantial evidence; credibility; sentencing; suspended sentence brought into effect.
headnote: The accused was charged with murdering Sarah Munhu by assaulting her with a chisel, aluminium pot and wooden stool. He pleaded not guilty, claiming that he fought with the deceased, that she later apologised and left, and that he only discovered the next morning that she had died.
issues_for_determination: Whether the accused caused the deceased’s death; whether he acted with actual or constructive intent to kill; the appropriate sentence.
legal_principles: Intention in murder may be established by constructive intent where the accused realised a real risk or possibility that death might result and persisted. The court may rely on circumstantial evidence and conduct after the event.
held_decision_points: The accused was found guilty of murder with constructive intent. The court imposed a sentence of 20 years’ imprisonment and brought into effect a suspended sentence of three and a half years.
ratio_decidendi: The brutality of the assault, concealment-related conduct and objective circumstances established constructive intent to kill beyond reasonable doubt.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court criticised passion-related violence and emphasised the need to protect the right to life and deter domestic violence.
cases_referred_to: None identified in the extracted text.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 47(1)(b).

document_name: ZIMRA v Arreta Chidodo (252 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 252 (20 April 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Zimbabwe Revenue Authority v Chidodo
case_number: HC 2154/22; Ref Case No. HC 2123/22; X-Ref Case No. HC 181/22
subject_area: Urgent chamber application; stay of execution; rescission of default judgment; immigrant’s rebate; customs and excise; defective form/certificate objections; prospects of success.
catchwords_flynote: Stay of execution – default judgment ordering relief in immigration rebate dispute – ZIMRA applying urgently pending rescission – mistake over withdrawn matter – whether urgency self-created – defective form and certificate objections – stay granted.
headnote: ZIMRA applied urgently to stay execution of an order granted by default in favour of the respondent under HC 181/22, pending finalisation of its rescission application under HC 2123/22. The default arose after ZIMRA’s legal practitioner mistook a notice of withdrawal in a related case for withdrawal of the case in which opposing papers were due. The respondent objected on urgency and alleged defects in the form and certificate of urgency. The court condoned the bundling defects, found the matter urgent and accepted that ZIMRA had prospects of success on rescission. A provisional order staying execution was granted.
issues_for_determination: Whether the matter was urgent or self-created; whether the urgent chamber application form was fatally defective; whether the certificate of urgency defect was fatal; whether ZIMRA had prospects of success in rescission; whether failure to oppose resulted from an excusable mistake; and whether execution should be stayed pending HC 2123/22.
legal_principles: Urgency depends on when the need to act arises and whether the applicant acted promptly thereafter. Mistakes by legal practitioners may, depending on circumstances, be excusable. Courts may condone bundling or pagination defects where the correct document exists and the other party is not prejudiced. In applications for stay pending rescission, prospects of success and the balance of equities are material. Immigrant rebate eligibility depends on statutory conditions, including proof of residence outside Zimbabwe for the required period.
held_decision_points: The preliminary objections were dismissed. The court held that the matter was urgent, the defects were due to bundling errors and could be condoned, and ZIMRA had prospects of success because the respondent’s altered passport affected verification of the rebate requirements. A provisional order staying execution of HC 181/22 pending determination of HC 2123/22 was granted.
ratio_decidendi: ZIMRA acted promptly once it discovered the default judgment, and the default was explained by an excusable confusion between related matters. Since there was a real dispute about whether the respondent qualified for an immigrant’s rebate and the passport was altered, ZIMRA had sufficient prospects to justify preserving the position pending rescission.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified.
cases_referred_to: Kuvarega v Registrar General and Another 1998 (1) ZLR 188 (H).
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, r 60; Customs and Excise (General) Regulations, SI 154 of 2001, s 105; Customs and Excise Act [Chapter 23:02], s 120.

document_name: APOSTO~2(1).DOC
case_name: APOSTOLIC FAITH MISSION IN ZIMBABWE versus APOSTOLIC FAITH MISSION OF ZIMBABWE and AMON NYIKA CHINYEMBA and ALEX MWANZA and CAESAR MANGWENTSHU and DENNIS MUTUNGI
subject_area: Civil procedure; urgent chamber application; interim interdict; urgency; church conference; fresh matter in answering affidavit; pagination.
catchwords_flynote: Urgent chamber application – interdict to stop church conference – fresh supporting affidavit filed with answering affidavit expunged – challenge to founding affidavit and pagination dismissed – urgency not established because applicant knew of planned conference from correspondence and delayed until eve of event – police involvement did not justify inaction – application struck off roll of urgent matters with costs.
headnote: The applicant sought an urgent interdict prohibiting respondents from holding a conference at 164D Northway, Prospect, Waterfalls, Harare. The Court expunged an affidavit attached to the answering affidavit because it raised fresh allegations requiring a response and should have been filed with the founding papers. The respondents' objections that the founding affidavit was fraudulent and that papers were not paginated were dismissed. On urgency, however, the Court held that the applicant had known since at least 25 February 2022 of the planned conference, including its dates and venue. The applicant's reliance on police refusal to act on 7 April was self-created urgency. The matter was struck off the urgent roll with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the affidavit attached to the answering affidavit should be expunged.
    Whether the founding affidavit was fraudulent.
    Whether lack of pagination/indexing invalidated the application.
    Whether the matter was urgent.
    When the need to act arose.
    Whether reliance on police refusal to intervene justified urgent treatment.
legal_principles: A supporting affidavit is ordinarily filed with the founding affidavit. Fresh allegations in an answering affidavit or affidavit filed with it may be expunged where they require a response and no explanation is given for not placing them in founding papers. Urgency requires that the applicant act when the need to act arises; urgency created by deliberate abstention until the event is imminent is not the urgency contemplated by the rules.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the affidavit attached to the answering affidavit introduced fresh allegations and was expunged.
    Held, that the objection to the founding affidavit failed because there was no evidence proving fraud.
    Held, that pagination/indexing defects did not invalidate the application.
    Held, that the applicant knew of the conference and venue from February/March 2022.
    Held, that the need to act arose soon after 25 February 2022, not on 7 April 2022.
    Held, that reliance on police refusal to stop the conference was self-created urgency.
    Held, that the application should be struck off the urgent roll with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that a party aware of an impending event must seek urgent relief when the need to act arises, and cannot create urgency by waiting until the event is imminent or until police decline to intervene.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that police are there to maintain law and order and do not determine proprietary disputes unless a criminal dimension arises.
cases_referred_to:
Pickering v Zimbabwe Newspapers (1980) Ltd 1991 (1) ZLR 71 (H)
    Dilwin Investments (Pvt) Ltd t/a Formscaff v Jopa Engineering Company (Pvt) Ltd HH 116-98
    Kuvarega v Registrar General and Anor 1998 (1) ZLR 188 (H)
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021 provisions on urgent chamber applications and affidavits in application proceedings
subject_area_terms: Civil procedure; urgent chamber application; interim interdict; urgency; church conference; fresh matter in answering affidavit; pagination.

document_name: APOSTO~1(2).DOC
case_name: APOSTOLIC FAITH MISSION IN ZIMBABWE versus APOSTOLIC FAITH MISSION OF ZIMBABWE and AMON NYIKA CHINYEMBA and CAESAR MAGWENTSHU and DENNIS MUTUNGI
subject_area: Civil procedure; urgent chamber application; mandament van spolie; urgency; certificate of urgency; final relief in spoliation.
catchwords_flynote: Urgent chamber application – mandament van spolie – amended certificate of urgency and amended draft order allowed – spoliation usually urgent but applicant still required to act when need to act arose – conference had ended and occupation had ceased by time application filed – no evidence contradicting respondents' assertion that property was no longer occupied – matter struck off urgent roll with costs.
headnote: The applicant filed an urgent spoliation application concerning the same Waterfalls property and substantially the same facts as a prior urgent interdict application that had been struck off the urgent roll the previous day. The Court allowed the applicant to regularise errors in the certificate of urgency and to file an amended draft order. On urgency, however, the Court held that the conference giving rise to the complaint had ended at 13:00 on 10 April 2022 and that the applicant had not contradicted the respondents' evidence that they had vacated by the time the application was filed. The applicant also failed to explain why it did not institute proceedings before 10 April despite becoming aware on 8 April. The matter was struck off the roll of urgent matters with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the amended certificate of urgency and amended draft order should be admitted.
    Whether the application for mandament van spolie was urgent.
    Whether spoliation proceedings are inherently urgent regardless of delay.
    Whether the application remained urgent after the conference had ended and occupation ceased.
    Whether the applicant had explained its delay from 8 April to 10 April 2022.
legal_principles: Spoliation proceedings are generally urgent, but urgency depends on the circumstances of each case and the applicant must act expeditiously when the need to act arises. Urgency is not created merely by the nature of the cause of action. What is not denied on affidavit must be taken to be admitted. A mandament van spolie is final in effect and an amended draft seeking final spoliatory relief may be appropriate where the cause of action is spoliation.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the amended certificate of urgency and amended draft order were admitted.
    Held, that spoliation matters may be urgent but the applicant must still satisfy the urgency test.
    Held, that the respondents' evidence that the conference had ended and the property had been vacated was not contradicted.
    Held, that the applicant had not adequately explained why it did not act before 10 April 2022.
    Held, that the urgency had been lost and the matter did not qualify for an urgent hearing.
    Held, that the matter should be struck off the roll of urgent matters with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that even in spoliation proceedings, urgency is fact-specific, and where the alleged dispossession has ceased and the applicant fails to explain delay, the matter will not be heard urgently.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted that a delay of even one or two days may deprive a matter of urgency depending on all the circumstances; urgency is not determined by a mathematical count of days.

document_name: Haisaid v Masomera and Others (HC 5914 of 2020) [2022] ZWHHC 255 (20 April 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: FRANCIS ROLAND HAISAID versus OLIVER MASOMERA AND OTHERS
subject_area: Civil procedure; rescission; default judgment; renunciation and assumption of agency; service on legal practitioners; executor dative; estate administration.
catchwords_flynote: Rescission – rule 449(1)(a) – judgment erroneously sought or granted – service on erstwhile legal practitioners after renunciation of agency – failure to serve notices of renunciation/assumption – applicant absent and adversely affected – default judgment set aside – bar uplifted.
headnote: The applicant sought rescission of a judgment granted in favour of the first respondent and upliftment of a bar. The dispute arose after the Master removed the applicant as executor dative of an estate and replaced him. The applicant had challenged that removal, but his application was dismissed for want of prosecution after papers were served on legal practitioners who had renounced agency. The Court held that the mishap arose from failures by retiring and incoming legal practitioners to serve the relevant notices and that the judgment adversely affected the applicant. The application was granted with costs, with an unnecessary reinstatement paragraph expunged.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the default judgment was erroneously sought or granted.
    Whether service on the applicant’s former legal practitioners was effective.
    Whether the applicant was absent and adversely affected within rule 449(1)(a).
    Whether the bar should be uplifted and the applicant allowed to oppose.
legal_principles: Rule 449(1)(a) permits rescission of a judgment or order erroneously sought or granted in the absence of an affected party. Renunciation and assumption of agency must be properly communicated. The court retains a discretion to correct an injustice caused by procedural irregularity, especially where a party was deprived of an opportunity to be heard.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the judgment was erroneously sought and granted in circumstances where service was affected on erstwhile legal practitioners.
    Held, that the applicant was absent and adversely affected.
    Held, that paragraph 3 of the draft order was unnecessary and expunged.
    Held, that the application was granted with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that a default judgment founded on service to legal practitioners who were no longer properly acting, where agency changes were mishandled and the affected party was thereby denied a hearing, constitutes an error justifying rescission under rule 449(1)(a).
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court emphasised that finality in litigation cannot justify maintaining a judgment where the affected litigant was denied an opportunity to ventilate important issues.
cases_referred_to: Tiriboyi v Jani & Anor 2004 (1) ZLR 470 (H)
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 1971, rules 236(3)(b) and 449(1)(a).
subject_area_terms: Civil procedure; rescission; default judgment; renunciation and assumption of agency; service on legal practitioners; executor dative; estate administration.

document_name: S v Nhemwa (257 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 257 (11 March 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Jealous Nhemwa v The State
subject_area: Criminal appeal; theft; trust property; credibility; farm inputs
catchwords_flynote: Theft - farm manager intercepted fertiliser deliveries and sold bags before delivery to complainant - appeal against conviction on four counts - defence that fertiliser belonged to transporter rejected - factual findings and credibility - appeal dismissed.
headnote: The appellant, a farm manager, was convicted of four counts of theft involving 360 bags of fertiliser meant for the complainant’s farm. It was common cause that he took bags from consignments before delivery, sold most of them, and caused some bags and proceeds to be delivered to his girlfriend. His defence was that the fertiliser belonged to the transporter and that he sold it on the transporter’s instructions. The transporter denied this and his evidence was supported by delivery notes. The High Court held that the appeal grounds were irrelevant or without merit and upheld the convictions.
issues_for_determination: Whether the court a quo misdirected itself in accepting the State witnesses; whether an internal audit of the farm was necessary; whether the girlfriend should have been called; and whether the appellant’s explanation that the fertiliser belonged to the transporter created reasonable doubt.
legal_principles: An appellate court will not interfere with trial-court credibility findings unless they defy logic or common sense. Where theft occurs before goods reach the complainant’s store, an internal audit of later stock may be irrelevant. Common cause facts and documentary delivery notes may support rejection of an accused’s explanation.
held_decision_points: The appeal against conviction was dismissed.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that where an accused’s own conduct shows dominion over stolen goods and the alleged lawful source is rejected on credible and documentary evidence, conviction for theft will stand despite arguments about absent internal audits or uncalled witnesses.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not available.
cases_referred_to: S v Lawrence & Anor 1989 (1) ZLR 29(S) not in text; general appellate credibility principles applied without specific case citation in extracted judgment.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 113(2)(d).
reviewer_comments: Full text extracted from the mounted DOCX.

document_name: Muganhiri v City of Harare (258 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 258 (20 April 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Muganhiri v City of Harare
subject_area: Contract; sale of land; breach; damages; restitution; municipal stands.
catchwords_flynote: Agreement of sale – residential stand sold by City of Harare but already unavailable – breach admitted – damages claimed in US dollars based on later market valuations – actual loss not proved – refund of purchase price ordered.
headnote: The applicant paid the full purchase price for a residential stand sold by the City of Harare. The City admitted breach because the stand was unavailable, having earlier been sold to someone else. The applicant sought replacement land or US$58,666.66 damages. The court held that damages must be proved and are ordinarily assessed by financial loss attributable to breach, not later market quotations alone. The applicant had shown only payment of ZW$44,620.00. The City was ordered to pay that amount and costs.
issues_for_determination: What measure of damages applied; whether the applicant proved US dollar replacement damages; whether refund of purchase price was the proper remedy.
legal_principles: Contractual damages seek to place the innocent party in the position he would have occupied had the contract been performed, but the claimant bears the onus to prove actual financial loss. Inflation or later market valuations do not by themselves prove damages.
held_decision_points: The respondent was ordered to pay the applicant ZWL44,620 and costs.
ratio_decidendi: The applicant did not prove the claimed US dollar loss; he proved payment of the purchase price only. The appropriate award was reimbursement of the amount paid.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted the long-standing principle in Victoria Falls & Transvaal Power Co Ltd v Consolidated Langlaagte Mines Ltd on contractual damages.
cases_referred_to: Rowland Electro Engineering (Pvt) Ltd v Zimbank 2007 (1) ZLR 1 (H); Victoria Falls & Transvaal Power Co Ltd v Consolidated Langlaagte Mines Ltd 1915 AD 122; Marume and Another v Murangnwa HH 27/07.
legislation_considered: None identified.

document_name: Gatsi v D Med Healthcare (Pvt) Ltd (259 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 259 (20 April 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: GODFREE GATSI versus D MED HEALTHCARE (PVT) LTD
subject_area: Company law; labour law; declaratur; directorship; disciplinary proceedings; lis pendens; abuse of process.
catchwords_flynote: Declaratur – removal as director and suspension challenged – disciplinary process earlier referred to Labour Officer then withdrawn – effect of withdrawal – pending disciplinary proceedings – lis pendens and prematurity – application dismissed.
headnote: The applicant sought declarations that his removal as director and secretary, suspension, CR14 filing and non-payment of remuneration were null and void. The respondent argued that the matter was lis pendens and premature because disciplinary proceedings had commenced, were referred to the Labour Officer by the applicant, and after withdrawal reverted to the disciplinary forum. The Court held that the pending disciplinary proceedings had to run their course and that the applicant was prematurely moving from forum to forum. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
What was the effect of withdrawal of the Labour Officer proceedings.
    Whether disciplinary proceedings remained pending.
    Whether the declaratur application was barred by lis pendens or was premature.
    Whether the Court could reinstate or declare rights before disciplinary proceedings were finalised.
legal_principles: Lis pendens applies where another pending matter exists between the same parties, on the same cause of action and involving the same subject matter. Withdrawal of a referral to a Labour Officer may return the matter to the pending disciplinary process. Courts should avoid intervening prematurely where internal or disciplinary processes remain pending and may resolve the dispute.
held_decision_points:
Held, that withdrawal of the Labour Officer matter reverted the dispute to the disciplinary hearing.
    Held, that the pending disciplinary proceedings involved the same parties, cause of action and subject matter.
    Held, that the matter was premature and amounted to abuse of process.
    Held, that the application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that a party who withdraws labour proceedings after initiating them cannot bypass the revived disciplinary process by seeking a declaratur on the same substance before the internal process determines liability or termination status.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court commented that the applicant was moving from one forum to another without allowing finality in any process.
cases_referred_to:
Nestle (SA) (Pty) Ltd v Mars Incorporated (2001) 4 All SA 315 (SCA)
    Herbstein and Van Winsen, The Civil Practice of the High Courts and the Supreme Court of Appeal of South Africa, referenced
