document_name: Mungate and Another v ZIMRA (538 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 538 (10 August 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Mungate and Another v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority
subject_area: Customs and excise; seizure and forfeiture of vehicles; special plea; notice of intention to sue; prescription; issue estoppel.
catchwords_flynote: Customs and excise – vehicles seized and forfeited – forfeiture order successfully appealed – ZIMRA refusing release – special plea alleging defective statutory notice and prescription – prior ruling on notice – issue estoppel – special plea referred to trial.
headnote: The plaintiffs sued ZIMRA for release of two vehicles, or their monetary values, after the High Court had set aside a magistrates court forfeiture order and ordered release of the vehicles. ZIMRA raised a special plea contending that the plaintiffs had not served a proper notice under s 196 of the Customs and Excise Act and that the claim had prescribed under s 193(12). The court held that the s 196 notice issue had already been determined in earlier proceedings and amounted to issue estoppel. It further held that prescription could not be decided as pleaded because the forfeiture had been by court order, not by ZIMRA under s 193(6), and because factual issues on seizure notices remained. The special plea was removed from the roll and referred to trial.
issues_for_determination: Whether the plaintiffs were non-suited for failure to serve a proper s 196 notice; whether the issue had already been decided; whether the claim had prescribed under s 193(12) of the Customs and Excise Act; whether the special plea could be determined on the papers.
legal_principles: Issue estoppel prevents re-litigation of an issue already necessarily determined between the parties. Statutory notice provisions exist to alert public authorities to claims and permit investigation; failure may be condoned where there is substantial compliance or no undue prejudice. Prescription under s 193(12) must be fitted to the correct statutory context and factual matrix.
held_decision_points: The special plea was removed from the roll and referred to trial. Costs were ordered to be in the cause.
ratio_decidendi: The prior judgment had already decided the adequacy of the notice. ZIMRA was aware of the plaintiffs’ claim and could not rely on the notice point to avoid pleading to the merits. The prescription point was not amenable to disposal on the papers because the forfeiture was court-ordered and the propriety of the notices of seizure required evidence.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court commented that ZIMRA could not, without explaining its non-compliance with an extant High Court order directing release of the vehicles, simply claim that the plaintiffs were non-suited.
cases_referred_to: Machacha v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority HB 186/11; Care International in Zimbabwe v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority & Ors SC 76-17; earlier related judgments HH 265-19 and HH 347-21.
legislation_considered: Customs and Excise Act [Chapter 23:02], ss 193 and 196; State Liabilities Act [Chapter 8:15], s 6; Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], s 62; Revenue Authority Act [Chapter 23:12].
reviewer_comments: Draft. The mounted DOCX package was structurally invalid/theme-only; parsed source excerpts were used for the judgment text.

document_name: Shanfari v G.N. Mlotshwa & Co. Legal Practitioners and 7 Others (539 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 539 (10 August 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Thamer Said Ahmed Al Shanfari v G.N. Mlotshwa & Co. Legal Practitioners and 7 Others
subject_area: Civil procedure; joinder; motion proceedings; legal practitioners; caveat; property documents; conflict of interest
catchwords_flynote: Joinder - motion proceedings - r 87(2)(b) of High Court Rules 1971 - court’s inherent and rule-based power to regulate procedure - law firm holding property documents and writing demand letter - direct and substantial interest - joinder granted with punitive costs.
headnote: The applicant sought to join his former legal practitioners and related parties to a pending application for a caveat over immovable properties. He alleged they had held key property documents and later acted for parties claiming the properties. The respondents objected that r 87 applied only to actions and that no cause of action existed. The court held that r 87 could apply to motion proceedings, alternatively that the court could rely on r 4C and constitutional inherent powers. The respondents had a direct and substantial interest in the main application and were joined.
issues_for_determination: Whether r 87(2)(b) applied to motion proceedings; whether the application followed proper procedure; whether the proposed parties had a direct and substantial interest in the main proceedings; and whether joinder was necessary for effective determination of all issues.
legal_principles: A person may be joined where he has a direct and substantial interest in the issues or where his presence is necessary for all matters in dispute to be effectually and completely determined. The High Court may regulate procedure under r 4C and s 176 of the Constitution where the rules are silent. Equal protection and the interests of justice support allowing joinder in motion proceedings where appropriate.
held_decision_points: The application for joinder was granted. The first, second and third respondents were joined as seventh, eighth and ninth respondents in HC 8780/19, given ten days to file opposition, and ordered to pay costs on a legal practitioner and client scale jointly and severally.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that parties whose conduct forms a material part of the dispute and whose interests may be affected should be joined, even in motion proceedings, so that the court can determine the real issues effectively and completely.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court found the first to third respondents should not have opposed joinder because the main respondents had rightly argued that many averments could only be answered by them.
cases_referred_to: Confederation of Zimbabwe Industries v Mbatha HH 125/15; Chiwawa v Mutzuris & 4 Others HH 7/09; Marais & Another v Pangola Sugar Milling Co. & Others 1961 (2) SA 698 (N); Gordon v Department of Health KwaZulu-Natal 2008 (6) SA 522 (SCA); Shumbairerwa v Chiraramiro & 3 Others HH 731/15; Macey’s Supermarket & Bottle Store (Greencroft) Ltd v Edwards 1964 RLR 13 (SR); Henri Viljoen (Pty) Ltd v Waterbuck Brothers 1953 (2) SA 151 (O); United Watch Diamond Co & Others v Disa Hotels & Another 1972 (4) SA 409 (C); Mugano v Fintrack & Others HH 394-13; Nyamweda v Georgias SC 200-88; Zimbabwe Teachers Association & Others v Minister of Education & Culture 1990 (2) ZLR 48 (HC).
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 1971, rr 87(2)(b), 4C; High Court Rules, 1971, Order 13.

document_name: S v Pilate; S v Pilate (54 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 54 (24 January 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: The State v Mike Pilate; The State v Sibanda Pilate
subject_area: Criminal procedure; criminal review; guilty plea procedure; sentence calculation
catchwords_flynote: Criminal review - guilty pleas - theft - s 271(2)(b) and s 271(3) of the Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act - requirement to explain and record explanation of charge - failure to comply with peremptory guilty plea procedure - sentence calculations - convictions and sentences set aside.
headnote: Two guilty-plea theft matters from the Harare Magistrates Court were placed on review. In one matter the accused admitted stealing a Samsung handset. In the other, the accused admitted stealing three mountain bicycles. The magistrate imposed partially suspended sentences, but also made sentence-calculation errors. The central review concern was whether the magistrate had complied with the statutory guilty plea procedure by explaining the charge and recording the explanation. The magistrate accepted that, although the charge had been explained, the explanation was not recorded. The High Court held that strict compliance with s 271(2)(b) and s 271(3) is imperative and that non-compliance vitiated the proceedings.
issues_for_determination: Whether failure to record the explanation of a charge in guilty-plea proceedings under s 271(2)(b) and s 271(3) of the Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act vitiated the convictions and sentences; and what should happen to proceedings affected by that procedural defect.
legal_principles: In a guilty plea, the court must explain the charge to the accused before calling on the accused to plead. The explanation and the accused’s acknowledgement of understanding must appear on the record. Failure to comply with the peremptory requirements of s 271(2)(b) and s 271(3) vitiates proceedings based on a guilty plea. Sentence calculations must also be recorded accurately because sentence affects liberty.
held_decision_points: The proceedings were quashed; the convictions and sentences were set aside; the Prosecutor General may prosecute the accused persons afresh; and, if retried and convicted, portions of sentence already served must be taken into account.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that a guilty plea conviction is unsafe where the record does not show compliance with the mandatory requirement that the charge be explained and that the explanation be recorded. The court cannot assume compliance from an unrecorded explanation.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court observed that sentencing is an integral part of the trial process and that mistakes in calculating suspended portions of sentence must be avoided because sentence directly affects personal freedom.
cases_referred_to: S v Mangwende HH 695/20; subsequent decisions following Mangwende referred to generally.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 113; Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], s 271(2)(b), s 271(3).
constitutional_provisions_considered: Right to a fair trial implicated generally through the requirement of proper plea recording, but no constitutional provision expressly considered in the extracted judgment.
reviewer_comments: Full text extracted from the mounted DOCX. The filename citation [2022] ZWHHC 54 and the heading judgment number HH 54-22 have both been preserved.
subject_area_terms: Criminal procedure; criminal review; guilty plea procedure; sentence calculation

document_name: Mboma v Kambarami and Another (540 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 540 (10 August 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Mboma and Another v Kambarami and Another
subject_area: Civil procedure; rescission of judgment; rule 449; default judgment; spoliation; service; mining dispute.
catchwords_flynote: Rescission – spoliation order granted in default – rule 449 and rule 63 – allegation of no service – whether judgment erroneously granted – wrong procedure – application struck off roll.
headnote: The applicants sought rescission under r 449(1)(a) of a default spoliation order granted against them in a mining dispute. They alleged they had not been served with the urgent chamber application or notice of set down. The respondent argued that the applicants had used the wrong procedure and should have proceeded under r 63, and that they were avoiding the need to seek condonation. The court held that the circumstances did not justify r 449 relief and that the application was not properly before the court. It was struck off the roll with costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether r 449 was the correct procedure; whether the order had been erroneously sought or granted; whether the application should have been brought under r 63; whether service was sufficient.
legal_principles: Rule 449 is an exception to the functus officio principle and applies only in restricted circumstances where a judgment was erroneously sought or granted in the absence of an affected party. A party seeking rescission of default judgment ordinarily proceeds under the appropriate rescission rule and must account for delay if out of time.
held_decision_points: The application was not properly before the court and was struck off the roll. Applicants were ordered to pay costs.
ratio_decidendi: The applicants’ challenge was in substance an ordinary rescission challenge, not a clear case of a judgment erroneously granted within r 449. The chosen procedure was inappropriate.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court did not find justification for punitive costs.
cases_referred_to: Unitrack (Private) Limited v TelOne (Private) Limited SC 10/18; Tiriboyi v Jani & Anor 2004 (1) ZLR 470 (H); authorities on rescission under r 449.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 1971, rr 449 and 63; High Court Rules, 2021.
reviewer_comments: Draft.

document_name: FALCON~1(3).DOC
case_name: FALCON GOLD ZIMBABWE LIMITED and INYAMAZANE GOLD (PRIVATE) LIMITED versus MINISTER OF MINES AND MINING DEVELOPMENT N.O.
subject_area: Mining law; administrative review; cancellation of mining titles; output declarations; show-cause notice; tribute agreements; right to be heard.
catchwords_flynote: Mining titles – Minister issued show-cause notice and cancelled Antelope claims for failure to declare output – applicants argued section 400(1)(a) did not apply and tributary was not heard – Court held cancellation lawful and notice to title holder sufficient – review dismissed.
headnote: Falcon Gold and its tributary sought review of the Minister’s cancellation of mining titles over Antelope claims. They argued that section 400(1)(a) of the Mines and Minerals Act applied only after commencement of mining operations, that the Minister acted irregularly and with ulterior motive, and that the tributary was denied the right to be heard. The Court held that the Minister acted under the applicable statutory provisions; the title holder was properly notified, and representations included the tributary’s position. The application was dismissed.
issues_for_determination:
Whether section 400(1)(a) of the Mines and Minerals Act authorised cancellation.
    Whether the show-cause notice and cancellation were grossly irregular.
    Whether there was evidence of ulterior motive.
    Whether the tributary, Inyamazane, had to receive a separate notice and hearing.
    Whether the Minister complied with administrative-law requirements.
legal_principles: The Minister may cancel mining rights under the Mines and Minerals Act where statutory grounds exist and after calling upon the holder to show cause. A tribute holder’s interests may be affected, but section 400(1)(a) does not necessarily require separate notice to the tributary where the holder has been notified and made representations covering the tributary’s position. Review grounds must be proved and not merely alleged.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the respondent adhered to the relevant statutory provisions.
    Held, that the show-cause notice to the first applicant as title holder was sufficient.
    Held, that the second applicant was not denied the right to be heard because the first applicant’s representations covered its concerns.
    Held, that ulterior motive and gross irregularity were not proved.
    Held, that the application was dismissed.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that cancellation of mining titles for failure to declare output was not reviewable where the title holder received notice, responded, and the Minister acted within the relevant Mines and Minerals Act provisions; a tributary did not require a separate hearing on the facts.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted that many arguments were framed around the Administrative Justice Act, but the factual record showed statutory compliance rather than gross irregularity.
cases_referred_to: Authorities on administrative review and Mines and Minerals Act cancellation principles referred to in the judgment; specific names not all available in extracted text.
legislation_considered: Mines and Minerals Act [Chapter 21:05], sections 251, 285 and 400(1)(a); Administrative Justice Act [Chapter 10:28], section 3(2).
subject_area_terms: Mining law; administrative review; cancellation of mining titles; output declarations; show-cause notice; tribute agreements; right to be heard.

document_name: METROP~1.DOC
case_name: Metropolitan Development (Private) Limited v Minister of Transport and Infrastructural Development N.O. and Another
subject_area: Urgent chamber application; interim interdict; road construction; compensation for land affected by Mbudzi interchange project; public works.
catchwords_flynote: Interim interdict – road construction works – Mbudzi roundabout interchange – affected private land – compensation offer made after filing – whether further interdict justified – publication and notice of affected properties – application dismissed.
headnote: The applicant, owner of Subdivision A of Marshlands of Delft of Hopley near Mbudzi roundabout, sought urgent relief compelling the Minister to issue a compensation offer and restraining road construction works on its land without consent. After the application was filed, the Minister issued an offer letter proposing US$562,913 compensation for 8062 square metres. The court held that the compensation-offer relief had been overtaken by events. It also held that the applicant had not established the requirements for a continuing interim interdict to stop the works. The application was dismissed, each party bearing its own costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether the matter remained live after the compensation offer letter; whether the applicant established a prima facie right, irreparable harm, lack of alternative remedy and balance of convenience; whether notice had been given through the Government Gazette and newspapers.
legal_principles: Interim interdicts require a prima facie right, apprehension of irreparable harm, absence of adequate alternative remedy and favourable balance of convenience. Relief overtaken by events should not be granted.
held_decision_points: The application was dismissed. Each party was ordered to bear its own costs.
ratio_decidendi: The applicant’s principal complaint, the lack of a compensation offer, had been addressed. The remaining interdict was not justified because the statutory/public notice process had been undertaken and the applicant had not established the full requirements for interim relief.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court treated the compensation issue and the interdict issue in light of the changed factual position after filing.
cases_referred_to: Setlogelo v Setlogelo 1914 AD 221; authorities on interim interdicts.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021; Government Gazette General Notice 3363 of 2021; statutory framework governing road construction and acquisition/compensation for affected land.

document_name: Proestos v Proestos and 5 Others (544 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 544 (11 August 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Proestos v Proestos and Others
subject_area: Urgent chamber application; interim interdict; deceased estates; wills; preservation of immovable property.
catchwords_flynote: Estate property; disputed will; sale of inherited immovable property; urgent interdict pending main action; preservation of status quo; trees and physical alterations.
headnote: The applicant sought an urgent interim interdict to preserve 94 Churchill Avenue, Gunhill, pending determination of a main action challenging wills, letters of administration and sale of estate property. The fourth respondent, who had bought the property, had started cutting trees and making physical changes despite the pending litigation. The court held that urgency arose when the applicant discovered the physical alterations, that a prima facie right existed through the disputed estate entitlement, and that irreparable harm was likely if development continued. Interim relief was granted in amended form.
issues_for_determination: Whether the matter was urgent; whether the applicant had a prima facie right; whether the fourth respondent’s ownership/title prevented interim relief; whether the property should be preserved pending the main action.
legal_principles: An interim interdict requires a prima facie right, a well-grounded apprehension of irreparable harm, a balance of convenience favouring relief, and no other satisfactory remedy. Urgency is assessed from when the need to act arises in relation to the harm complained of.
held_decision_points: The preliminary objections were dismissed. Interim relief was granted to preserve the property and stop physical changes pending finalization of the matter.
ratio_decidendi: Where title to inherited property and the validity of estate administration are under challenge, a purchaser who is aware of the pending litigation may be interdicted from altering the property to avoid rendering the main proceedings nugatory.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court treated the cutting of long-standing trees and physical alteration of the property as harm not easily reversible by repainting or rebuilding.
cases_referred_to: Kuvarega v Registrar General and Anor 1998 (1) ZLR 188; Setlogelo v Setlogelo 1914 AD 221.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, rule 57; Administration of Estates Act provisions discussed.

document_name: S v Karomo (546 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 546 (8 August 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Karomo
subject_area: Criminal review; mental health; guilty plea; aggravated indecent assault; fair trial
catchwords_flynote: Criminal review – accused indicating psychiatric treatment and missed medication during mitigation – guilty plea procedure – duty to alter plea where defence appears – Mental Health Act procedure – conviction and sentence set aside.
headnote: The accused pleaded guilty to aggravated indecent assault of a child and was sentenced to ten years’ imprisonment with two years suspended. During mitigation he disclosed that he had been a psychiatric patient for 25 years and had missed injections before the offence. The High Court held that the magistrate should have altered the plea to not guilty and ordered medical examination. The conviction and sentence were set aside and the matter remitted for trial de novo.
issues_for_determination: Whether the plea remained valid once mental illness was raised; whether the magistrate was required to invoke the Mental Health Act and enter a plea of not guilty.
legal_principles: Under s 272 of the Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act, where doubt arises as to guilt, admission of elements, or availability of a defence, the court must record a plea of not guilty. Where mental disorder is alleged or appears, s 192 requires the accused to be dealt with under the Mental Health Act, including medical examination where necessary.
held_decision_points: Conviction and sentence set aside; matter remitted for trial de novo before a different magistrate.
ratio_decidendi: The accused’s disclosure of long-term psychiatric illness and missed medication raised a possible defence of mental disorder, making the guilty plea unsafe and the failure to order examination a gross irregularity.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The judgment gives guidance to magistrates on mental-health cases and warns against forcing guilty pleas for convenience.
cases_referred_to: S v Taanorwa 1987 (1) ZLR 63 (SC); S v Chiwambutsa 1987 (2) ZLR 59 (SC); S v Dube 1988 (2) ZLR 385 (SC); S v Bizwick 1987 (2) ZLR 83 (SC); S v Tachiona 1994 (2) ZLR 402 (H); S v Matimbe & Ors 1984 (1) ZLR 283 (H); S v Milanzi & Anor 1998 (2) ZLR 212 (H).
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 66(1)(a)(i), s 227; Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], ss 192, 271, 272; Mental Health Act [Chapter 15:12], ss 28, 29.
constitutional_provisions_considered: Constitution of Zimbabwe, s 86(3)(e), on the non-derogable right to a fair trial.

document_name: Kanyasa v Peterhouse Boys High School and Another (547 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 547 (17 August 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: WILLARD KANYASA versus PETERHOUSE BOYS HIGH SCHOOL AND ANOTHER
subject_area: Education law; private school disciplinary proceedings; urgent chamber application; interim interdict; administrative fairness.
catchwords_flynote: School discipline – lower sixth pupil – disciplinary hearing chaired by independent chairperson not provided for in code – threatened expulsion – urgency – points in limine dismissed – prima facie right to challenge process – interim relief granted.
headnote: The applicant, father of a minor pupil at Peterhouse Boys High School, sought urgent interim relief after disciplinary proceedings were conducted in circumstances he alleged were inconsistent with the school disciplinary code. The Court dismissed the preliminary objections and found a prima facie basis to impugn the disciplinary process, particularly because the code did not provide for an independent chairperson. Interim relief restraining expulsion pending final determination was granted, with costs on the ordinary scale.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the matter was urgent.
    Whether the applicant had alternative remedies or had prematurely approached the court.
    Whether the disciplinary process disclosed prima facie non-compliance with the school code.
    Whether interim interdictory relief should be granted.
legal_principles: Urgency arises when the need to act arises and the matter cannot wait. Interim interdict requirements include a prima facie right, reasonable apprehension of harm, balance of convenience, and absence of adequate alternative remedy. In deciding prima facie right, the court considers applicant’s facts together with undisputed respondent facts.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the points in limine had no merit and were dismissed.
    Held, that there was prima facie non-compliance with the disciplinary code.
    Held, that the applicant had established grounds for interim relief.
    Held, that the application was allowed and the interim relief granted with costs on the ordinary scale.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio decidendi was that a disciplinary hearing conducted outside the governing code, where expulsion was reasonably apprehended, gave the applicant a prima facie right to interim protection pending final determination of the procedural challenge.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted that only two weeks of the school term remained, which affected the balance of convenience and prejudice analysis.
cases_referred_to: Webster v Mitchell 1948 (1) SA 1186; Setlogelo v Setlogelo 1914 AD 221 and related interdict authorities as cited.
legislation_considered: Education Act [Chapter 25:04]; High Court Rules, 2021; Peterhouse disciplinary code.
subject_area_terms: Education law; private school disciplinary proceedings; urgent chamber application; interim interdict; administrative fairness.

Setlogelo v Setlogelo 1914 AD 221 and related interdict authorities as cited.

document_name: Matoka v Dube and Another (548 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 548 (4 August 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Matoka v Dube and Another
subject_area: Civil procedure; exception; prescription; res judicata; unjust enrichment; sale of immovable property by deceased estate.
catchwords_flynote: Exception – unjust enrichment claim – prescription – res judicata – sale of land by mentally unsound seller – purchase price and improvements – claim partly barred by res judicata.
headnote: The plaintiff sought declaratory and consequential relief based on unjust enrichment after a sale of immovable property by the late Daniel Dube had been successfully challenged by the executrix dative of the estate on the basis that the seller was mentally unsound. The plaintiff claimed repayment of the purchase price and improvements. The defendant excepted and raised prescription and res judicata. The court dismissed the exception, dismissed the special plea of prescription, and upheld res judicata only in respect of the US$50,000 purchase price because that matter had already been determined. The claim for US$30,000 improvements had not previously been determined and could proceed.
issues_for_determination: Whether the plaintiff’s pleadings disclosed a cause of action in unjust enrichment; whether the claim had prescribed; whether the claim was res judicata because of earlier proceedings.
legal_principles: An exception should not be upheld where amendment may cure defects or where the pleading sufficiently discloses the nature of the cause. Prescription runs from when a debt is due and may be interrupted by pending proceedings. Res judicata requires the same parties, same subject-matter and same cause of action.
held_decision_points: The exception was dismissed. Prescription was dismissed. Res judicata succeeded only against the US$50,000 purchase price claim and failed against the US$30,000 improvements claim.
ratio_decidendi: The purchase price issue had already been determined in earlier litigation, but the claim for improvements was a separate, new claim not previously before the court. The pleadings were capable of amendment and the defendant had not established prescription.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted the need to avoid overtechnical objections where pleadings can be cured by amendment.
cases_referred_to: Callander Enterprises (Private) Limited v Makomborero Muza HH 232/18; Nyamunokera v Makkosi & Anor HH 28/21; McKelvey v Cowan NO 1980 (4) SA 525 (Z); other authorities cited in the full text.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, r 42; Prescription Act [Chapter 8:11].

document_name: S v Mutyambizi (550 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 550 (16 August 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: The State v Batsirai Mutyambizi
subject_area: Criminal procedure; guilty plea; dangerous drugs; possession; review
catchwords_flynote: Criminal review - guilty plea - unlawful possession of dangerous drugs - accused’s explanation inconsistent with possession - plea not unequivocal - court should have altered plea to not guilty - charge not supported by admitted facts - conviction quashed and sentence set aside.
headnote: The accused pleaded guilty to unlawful possession of dangerous drugs after methamphetamine was found. During plea canvassing she explained that the drugs belonged to her sister-in-law, that she had brought a buyer, and that she expected payment for facilitating the sale. The High Court held that once she denied possession and described conduct outside the charged section, the plea was equivocal and should have been altered to not guilty. The admitted facts did not establish physical or mental possession of the drugs.
issues_for_determination: Whether the guilty plea was unequivocal; whether the admitted facts established possession under s 157(1)(a); and whether the magistrate should have converted the matter to a trial after the accused’s explanation showed a possible defence.
legal_principles: A guilty plea must be genuine, unequivocal and freely made. If an accused gives an explanation that affects guilt or denies an essential element, the court must alter the plea to not guilty and proceed to trial. Possession of dangerous drugs requires physical or mental control; mere facilitation of a sale by another person does not, without more, establish possession under the charged provision.
held_decision_points: The conviction was quashed and the sentence set aside. The accused was entitled to immediate release.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that a guilty plea cannot stand where the accused’s answers negate an essential element of the offence charged; courts and prosecutors must intervene when a plea becomes equivocal during canvassing.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court commented that prosecutors must assist courts in identifying anomalies during plea recording so that cases are converted to trial when fairness requires it.
cases_referred_to: Not available.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 157(1)(a); Dangerous Drugs Act [Chapter 15:02], s 14(2).
reviewer_comments: Full text extracted from the mounted DOCX.
subject_area_terms: Criminal procedure; guilty plea; dangerous drugs; possession; review

document_name: SVDZOT~1.DOC
case_name: S v Dzotizei and Related Matters
subject_area: Criminal law; criminal review; guilty pleas; fair trial; magistrates’ courts procedure
catchwords_flynote: Criminal review – several guilty-plea records – s 271(2)(b) and s 271(3) procedure – failure to explain charge and record accused’s responses – proceedings vitiated – convictions and sentences set aside.
headnote: Six Murehwa matters were reviewed together because the same magistrate had disposed of guilty pleas without recording compliance with the mandatory procedure under s 271(3). The High Court held that the defect vitiated the convictions and sentences and quashed the proceedings, leaving any fresh prosecutions to the Prosecutor-General’s discretion.
issues_for_determination: Whether failure to comply with the mandatory guilty-plea procedure under s 271(3) rendered the proceedings reviewably irregular.
legal_principles: Where an unrepresented accused pleads guilty, the charge and essential elements must be explained and the explanation and responses recorded. Failure to comply with a legislated fair-trial procedure is fatal.
held_decision_points: All convictions and sentences in the listed records were set aside for gross procedural irregularity.
ratio_decidendi: A plea of guilty is not validly taken unless the record shows that the accused understood the charge and admitted all essential elements after proper explanation.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The judgment acknowledges the trial magistrate’s concession and underscores the value of the review process for correcting procedural practice.

document_name: EX-CON~1.DOC
case_name: EX-CONSTABLE GANI 987343Y versus THE COMMISSIONER GENERAL OF POLICE and THE POLICE SERVICE COMMISSION
subject_area: Administrative law; police service; condonation; late review; discharge from employment; reasons for decision; prospects of success.
catchwords_flynote: Condonation – late filing of review of discharge from Police Service – long delay – prior declaratur struck off because review was correct procedure – Commissioner-General failed to provide valid reasons; reasons given by subordinate invalid – prospects of success high – condonation granted.
headnote: The applicant sought condonation for late filing of review proceedings challenging her discharge from the Zimbabwe Republic Police. Although the delay was substantial, the Court considered the explanation, importance of the matter and prospects of success. It held that the Commissioner-General had still not furnished his own reasons for dismissing her appeal and discharging her; reasons given by an assistant commissioner were not valid. Because failure to give reasons was a serious irregularity and the matter affected the applicant’s livelihood, condonation was granted.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the late filing of the review application should be condoned.
    Whether the applicant gave a satisfactory explanation for the delay.
    Whether the intended review had prospects of success.
    Whether reasons supplied by a subordinate could stand as reasons of the Commissioner-General.
legal_principles: Condonation requires the court to weigh the degree of non-compliance, explanation, importance of the case, prospects of success, finality, convenience of the court and avoidance of delay. Condonation is not granted merely for the asking, but where fairness and justice require. A failure by a decision-maker to furnish reasons may vitiate proceedings and enhances prospects of success on review.
held_decision_points:
Held, that although the delay was inordinate, the matter was important to the applicant’s livelihood.
    Held, that the applicant gave an acceptable explanation when the procedural history was considered.
    Held, that reasons given by Assistant Commissioner R.M. Basera were not reasons of the Commissioner-General.
    Held, that the intended review had high prospects of success.
    Held, that the application for condonation was granted.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that where the decision-maker has failed to provide valid reasons for a dismissal decision and the applicant has otherwise explained her procedural route, the prospects of success and importance of the matter may justify condonation despite a long delay.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court described the matter as relating to the applicant’s “bread and butter” and livelihood, requiring fairness rather than procedural short-cuts.
cases_referred_to:
Jongwe v National Foods Ltd HB 147/18
    National Social Security Authority v Chipunza SC 116/14
    Kodwa v Secretary for Health & Another 1991 (2) ZLR 313 (SC)
    Botes & Another v Nedbank Ltd 1983 (3) SA 27

document_name: THESHE~1.DOC
case_name: The Sheriff for Zimbabwe and Another v Best Southern Drilling Supplies (Pvt) Ltd
subject_area: Interpleader; judicial attachment; ownership of motor vehicle; claimant’s onus; presumption arising from attachment at judgment debtor’s premises; personal right versus real right; collusion.
catchwords_flynote: Interpleader – Toyota Hilux attached at judgment debtor’s premises – claimant relying on termination-benefits agreement with former employer – vehicle not registered in claimant’s name – no proof of payment or transfer – whether claimant proved ownership – whether alleged collusion undermined claim.
headnote: The claimant asserted ownership of a Toyota Hilux attached by the Sheriff in execution of a judgment debt. He relied on an agreement allegedly awarding the vehicle to him as part of termination benefits from a former employer. The judgment creditor challenged the claim, arguing that the vehicle was attached from the judgment debtor’s premises and that the claimant had not proved ownership. The court held that the claimant had not established a real right in the vehicle and had failed to discharge the onus in interpleader proceedings.
issues_for_determination: Whether the claimant proved ownership of the attached vehicle; whether the agreement relied on created only a personal right; whether the presumption arising from attachment at the judgment debtor’s premises had been rebutted; and whether there was collusion between claimant and judgment debtor.
legal_principles: In interpleader proceedings the claimant bears the onus of proving ownership of property attached in execution. Where property is attached at the judgment debtor’s premises, it is presumed to belong to the judgment debtor unless rebutted. A contractual entitlement or personal right does not equate to a real right of ownership. Failure to register title, prove payment, or produce supporting evidence from the alleged transferor may defeat the claim.
held_decision_points: The claimant failed to prove ownership. The agreement relied on was inconclusive, did not establish that title had passed, and had not been supported by proof of payment or registration. The vehicle remained registered in another entity’s name and was attached from the judgment debtor’s premises. The court also accepted that indications of collusion weakened the claim.
ratio_decidendi: A claimant who relies on an alleged contractual entitlement to attached property must prove ownership, not merely a personal claim. The claimant’s failure to transfer the vehicle, explain the delay, prove payment or secure support from the alleged transferor meant that the presumption in favour of the judgment debtor was not rebutted.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified beyond the caution against collusive interpleader claims designed to defeat execution.
cases_referred_to: Muzanenhamo v Fishtown Investments (Pvt) Ltd & Anor SC 8/17; The Sheriff of the High Court v Tiritose Consulting (Pvt) Ltd HH 347/15; Phillips & Anor v Ameen & Anor HH 108/99.

document_name: S v Mugarisanwa (62 of 2023) [2022] ZWHHC 62 (5 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: The State v Tinashe Mugarisanwa
subject_area: Criminal law; murder; self-defence; defence of another; mine security; illegal gold panners
catchwords_flynote: Murder - security guard - fatal shooting of illegal gold panner - accused armed with shotgun - deceased and others advancing with machetes despite warning shots - defence of person under s 253 of the Criminal Law Code - cumulative requirements for self-defence and defence of another - complete defence established - acquittal.
headnote: The accused, a security guard at Shamva Mine, was charged with murder after shooting an illegal gold panner who died from a gunshot wound to the chest. The evidence of the State witnesses and the accused was largely similar. Illegal gold panners had earlier blasted their way out of a mine shaft and were being pursued by security teams. When some emerged from hiding near a river bank, three carried machetes and continued advancing despite repeated warning shots. The accused, the only armed member in his team, fired the fatal shot when the deceased was only a few metres away and ready to strike. The court accepted that the accused acted to defend himself and fellow security guards.
issues_for_determination: Whether the requirements for self-defence and defence of another under s 253 of the Criminal Law Code were satisfied; whether the accused believed on reasonable grounds that an unlawful attack was imminent; whether shooting was necessary and reasonable; and whether the harm caused was proportionate.
legal_principles: Self-defence and defence of another are complete defences where an unlawful attack has commenced or is imminent; the accused reasonably believes the conduct is necessary to avert the attack; the means used are reasonable; and the harm is caused to the attacker and is not grossly disproportionate. The requirements are cumulative. In assessing them, the court must consider the circumstances confronting the accused, including stress, fear, knowledge and capability.
held_decision_points: The court held that the deceased and his group posed an imminent unlawful attack; that the accused could not reasonably flee; that the use of the shotgun was reasonable in the circumstances; and that the harm was proportionate. The accused was found not guilty and acquitted.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that where armed illegal gold panners persist in advancing at close range despite warning shots, and the accused is the only armed member of the threatened security team, a fatal defensive shot may satisfy all statutory requirements of defence of person.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court cautioned against an “armchair approach” when assessing a person confronted by an immediate armed threat in difficult terrain and under pressure.
cases_referred_to: Mahingaidze v The State S-79-84.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], ss 47, 252, 253, 254; Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], s 314.
reviewer_comments: Full text extracted from the mounted DOCX. The file name gives [2022] ZWHHC 62 while the heading shows HH 62-23; both have been preserved.

document_name: Kagonye v S (HC B 112 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 572 (26 September 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: PETRONELLA KAGONYE versus THE STATE
subject_area: Criminal procedure; bail pending appeal; theft of trust property; prospects of success; recognisance and guarantees; public policy.
catchwords_flynote: Bail pending appeal – conviction for theft of trust property – former Minister and Member of Parliament – laptops donated for e-learning – weak prospects of success – failure to give undertakings and guarantees under section 123(10) – appeal ready for hearing – application dismissed.
headnote: The applicant sought bail pending appeal after conviction and sentence for theft of trust property relating to laptops requested for distribution to schools and underprivileged persons in her constituency. The Court held that the prospects of success on conviction and sentence were poor, that the applicant had not given the necessary undertaking and guarantees contemplated by section 123(10) of the Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act, and that the appeal was ready for prompt hearing. Bail pending appeal was dismissed.
issues_for_determination:
Whether bail pending appeal should be granted.
    Whether the applicant had prospects of success on conviction and sentence.
    Whether she had complied with or offered guarantees required by section 123(10).
    Whether there was a risk of abscondment.
    Whether the appeal should instead be heard promptly on the merits.
legal_principles: Bail pending appeal is not granted merely for the asking. A convicted person bears the onus under section 115C to show that release is in the interests of justice. Prospects of success and risk of abscondment are central. Section 123(10) requires recognisance and sufficient guarantees where bail pending appeal is granted. The court may consider public policy and prompt disposal of appeals.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant was unlikely to succeed on appeal against conviction or sentence.
    Held, that the applicant failed to place adequate undertakings and guarantees before the Court.
    Held, that the appeal was ready to be heard and should be disposed of promptly.
    Held, that the application was dismissed.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that weak prospects of appeal, inadequate guarantees of compliance with section 123(10), and the immediate availability of the appeal for hearing outweighed the applicant’s claim to liberty pending appeal.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court took judicial notice of statistics showing a pattern of convicts released on bail pending appeal abandoning appeals and absconding where no adequate guarantees were given.
cases_referred_to: Gomana v State SC 166/20; Aitken v The State; S v Williams 1980 ZLR 466 (A); S v Mutasa 1988 (2) ZLR 4 (S); S v McGowan 1995 (2) ZLR 81 (S); Mungwira v S HH 216/20; Kereke v Maramwidze HH 632-16; S v Dzvairo 2006 (1) ZLR 45 (H).
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], section 113(2); Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], sections 115C and 123(10); High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], sections 29(3) and 38.

document_name: S v Chirwa (66 of 2023) [2022] ZWHHC 66 (14 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Chirwa
judges: MUREMBA J; assessors Mr Mutombwa and Mr Kunaka
subject_area: Criminal law – murder – self-defence – spousal violence – complete defence
catchwords_flynote: Murder; defence of person; self-defence; domestic violence; single stab wound; requirements under s 253.
headnote: The accused was charged with murdering her husband after stabbing him once in the stomach with an okapi knife at their home. She admitted the stabbing but stated that she acted in self-defence after the deceased attacked her, threatened to kill her, and bit her shoulder.
issues_for_determination: Whether the State disproved self-defence; whether the accused satisfied the requirements of defence of person under s 253 of the Criminal Law Code.
legal_principles: Defence of person is a complete defence where the accused unlawfully attacked or threatened is acting to avert an imminent attack, the means used are reasonable, and the harm is caused to the attacker.
held_decision_points: The court held that the accused satisfied all requirements of self-defence and was entitled to a complete defence.
ratio_decidendi: Where the State cannot rebut an accused’s credible account that she used force reasonably to avert an imminent attack, the defence of person defeats a murder charge.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The judgment recognises the factual context of a domestic attack and the absence of contrary eyewitness evidence.
cases_referred_to: None identified in the extracted text.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], ss 47(1), 253.

document_name: Tsiko v Shamu (662 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 662 (29 September 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Tsiko v Shamu
subject_area: Civil appeal; lease and sublease; ejectment; locus standi of lessee to evict subtenant; compensation for improvements; lien or right of retention.
catchwords_flynote: Landlord and tenant – respondent leased shop from City of Harare and sublet to appellant – notice to vacate – whether lessee/sublessor had locus standi after main lease expiry – whether compensation for improvements could be raised as a defence to eviction.
headnote: The appellant appealed against a magistrates court judgment ordering his eviction from Shop 7 Old Tafara Shopping Centre. The respondent leased the property from the City of Harare and sublet it to the appellant. The appellant argued that the respondent lacked locus standi because the main lease had expired, and that he could resist eviction pending compensation for improvements. The High Court dismissed the appeal, holding that a lessee may evict a subtenant and that compensation for improvements did not justify holding over.
issues_for_determination: Whether the respondent had locus standi to seek ejectment; whether expiry of the main lease affected the respondent’s ability to evict the subtenant; whether compensation for improvements could be raised as a defence rather than by counterclaim; and whether the appeal should succeed.
legal_principles: A lessee or sublessor may evict a subtenant even without proving ownership of the premises, provided the subtenant had enjoyed possession under the lease. A lessee is generally estopped from challenging the lessor’s title after receiving undisturbed possession. A lessee or subtenant cannot resist eviction on the basis that compensation for improvements has not yet been paid; such claim must be pursued separately and does not create a right to hold over.
held_decision_points: The first ground of appeal failed because precedent established that a lessee may lawfully evict a subtenant. The second ground failed because compensation for improvements was not part of the trial issues and, in any event, did not afford a right to remain in occupation. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The respondent’s lack of ownership or expiry of the main lease did not entitle the appellant, as subtenant, to resist ejectment; nor could alleged improvements operate as a lien against eviction.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified.
cases_referred_to: Chatprill Enterprises (Pvt) Ltd v Mahere HH 994/15; Robson v Grimm 1996 (2) ZLR 73 (S); Clark v Nourse Mines Ltd 1910 TS 512; Zuva Petroleum Limited v S Chirenje HH 166/16; Hillock and Another v Hilsage Investments (Pvt) Ltd 1975 (1) SA 508; Ormashah v Karasa 1996 (1) ZLR 584 (H).

document_name: S v Haruzivishe (663 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 663 (22 September 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Haruzivishe
subject_area: Criminal law; criminal appeal; incitement to public violence; resisting peace officer; credibility; defective record
catchwords_flynote: Criminal appeal – inciting public violence – resisting peace officer – contradictory police evidence – defective record – appellate interference with credibility findings – convictions quashed.
headnote: The appellant had been convicted of inciting public violence and resisting a peace officer. On appeal, the High Court held that the trial court had relied on a defective record and had missed fundamental contradictions in the evidence of the police witnesses. The appeal succeeded, the convictions were quashed, and the appellant was acquitted on both counts.
issues_for_determination: Whether the evidence supported the convictions; whether the trial court’s credibility findings could stand; whether the defective record affected the judgment.
legal_principles: An appellate court may interfere with factual findings based on credibility where the record shows gross irregularity or contradictions going to the root of the matter. A conviction cannot rest on mutually destructive versions from a key witness.
held_decision_points: Appeal allowed; convictions quashed and sentences set aside; appellant acquitted on both counts.
ratio_decidendi: The State evidence was so contradictory and unreliable that guilt was not proved beyond reasonable doubt.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The judgment emphasised that the rectified record disclosed vital testimony omitted from the initial record and revealed contradictions missed by the trial court.
cases_referred_to: State v Lawrence & Anor 1989 (1) ZLR 29 (S); State v Soko SC 118/92; Chimbwanda v Chimbwanda S 28-02.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], ss 36, 176, 187.
reviewer_comments: Drafted from the mounted source judgment and retained for legal editorial review.

document_name: S v Biri (722 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 722 (17 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Biri
subject_area: Criminal procedure; bail pending trial; High Court original jurisdiction; public violence
catchwords_flynote: Bail pending trial — public violence — High Court original jurisdiction over non-Third Schedule offences — exceptional circumstances for approaching High Court directly — bail factors under s 117.
headnote: The applicant, charged with public violence, approached the High Court directly for bail. The State argued that the Magistrates Court had first-instance jurisdiction. The court held that the High Court has original jurisdiction over all criminal matters but generally discourages direct bail applications for non-Third Schedule offences unless justified by exceptional circumstances.
issues_for_determination: Whether the High Court should hear the bail application as a court of first instance; whether bail should be granted under s 117; whether the fact that co-accused were denied bail was a valid ground.
legal_principles: The High Court has original criminal jurisdiction. Bail refusal grounds are those in s 117(2) of the Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act, including abscondment, interference, further offences, undermining justice, or disturbance of public order in exceptional circumstances.
held_decision_points: The court accepted jurisdiction given the explanation for approaching the High Court and considered the merits of bail under the statutory grounds.
ratio_decidendi: A co-accused’s bail refusal is not itself a statutory ground for refusing bail; each applicant’s personal circumstances and statutory risk factors must be assessed individually.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified

document_name: S v Manyenye (666 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 1 (29 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Manyenye
subject_area: Criminal law; criminal review; sentencing; physical abuse; malicious injury to property
catchwords_flynote: Criminal review – sentence for physical abuse – suspended term omitted period of suspension – correction of sentence – duty to notify accused of corrected sentence.
headnote: The accused was convicted of physical abuse and malicious injury to property. On review, the High Court queried the sentence on count one because it suspended two months’ imprisonment without specifying the suspension period. The trial magistrate confirmed the omission and stated that the intended period was five years. The High Court corrected the sentence accordingly.
issues_for_determination: Whether the sentence should be corrected to include the omitted period of suspension.
legal_principles: A suspended sentence must clearly state both the condition and the period for which the condition operates. Where an omission is clear and conceded, the review court may correct the wording of the sentence.
held_decision_points: The sentence was corrected to suspend the two months’ imprisonment for five years on the stated good-behaviour condition.
ratio_decidendi: The omission of the suspension period was a correctable sentencing defect rather than a basis for setting aside the conviction.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified.
cases_referred_to: None identified.

document_name: The Sherriff for Zimbabwe and Another v Njokoya and Another (667 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 667 (6 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: The Sheriff for Zimbabwe and Another v Njokoya and Another
subject_area: Interpleader; trust property; execution against judgment debtor; non-disclosure; dishonesty; burden of proof; movable property not transferred to trust.
catchwords_flynote: Interpleader – second attachment after earlier failed claim by judgment debtor’s wife – trust formed by judgment debtor and wife claimed attached goods – whether movable goods had been transferred to trust – non-disclosure of relationship and prior proceedings – whether trust proved ownership.
headnote: The Sheriff attached movable goods in execution of a default judgment. A trust, represented by the judgment debtor’s wife, claimed that the attached goods belonged to the trust rather than to the judgment debtor. The court examined prior interpleader proceedings in which the same deponent had claimed attached goods as her personal property. It found that the trust had not proved ownership of the attached movable goods and that material facts had been withheld from the court.
issues_for_determination: Whether the trust proved ownership of the attached movable goods; whether the prior proceedings and non-disclosure undermined the claim; whether the trust deed established ownership of movable goods; and whether the claim was an attempt to frustrate execution.
legal_principles: The claimant in interpleader proceedings must prove ownership of attached goods on a balance of probabilities. A claimant must disclose material facts, including related prior proceedings. A trust deed or transfer of immovable property does not prove that movable goods were transferred to a trust. Courts will not reward dishonesty or material non-disclosure by a claimant seeking relief.
held_decision_points: The claimant failed to prove ownership. The documents relied on did not show that the movable goods were transferred to the trust. The deponent had withheld her relationship to the judgment debtor and the earlier related interpleader proceedings. The court found the trust claim to be an attempt to frustrate execution and dismissed it.
ratio_decidendi: The claim failed because the trust produced no proof that the movable goods belonged to it, and the deponent’s non-disclosure of material prior proceedings and family relationships fatally undermined the credibility of the claim.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The judgment contains strong remarks on candour, honesty and the court’s refusal to assist litigants who mislead it or withhold vital information.
cases_referred_to: Mhungu v Mtindi 1986 (2) ZLR 171 (SC); Mauladi v Batchelor HH 256/15; Silberberg and Schoeman, The Law of Property; Stanbic Finance Zimbabwe Ltd v Chivhunga 1999 (1) ZLR 262 (H); Smit Investment Holdings & Anor v Pungwe Mining (Private) Limited SC 33/18; Centra (Pvt) Ltd v Pralene Moyas & Anor HC 981/21; Deputy Sheriff, Harare v Mahleza 1997 (2) ZLR 425; Underbay v Underbay 1977 (4) SA 23; Leather Trade Zimbabwe (Pvt) Ltd v Smith HH 131/03.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021 provisions on interpleader proceedings.

document_name: Katambare v Tukuta (671 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 671 (5 October 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: FARO KATAMBARARE versus PATRICIA TUKUTA
subject_area: Family law; divorce; matrimonial property; business shares; post-divorce maintenance; consent paper; custody and access.
catchwords_flynote: Divorce – ancillary relief – unsigned consent paper not enforceable – division of matrimonial home – company shares awarded to spouse – post-divorce maintenance granted – minor child custody to mother – decree of divorce granted.
headnote: The parties agreed their marriage had irretrievably broken down but disputed property distribution and post-divorce maintenance. The Court refused to enforce an unsigned consent paper. It awarded custody of the minor child to the defendant, allocated movables and company shares, awarded the defendant a 30% share in the matrimonial home while the plaintiff retained 70%, and ordered the plaintiff to pay spousal maintenance of US$600 per month for five years.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the unsigned consent paper could be enforced.
    Which assets constituted matrimonial property and how they should be divided.
    Whether the defendant was entitled to post-divorce maintenance.
    How custody, access and minor-child maintenance should be regulated.
legal_principles: Section 7 of the Matrimonial Causes Act governs equitable distribution of spouses’ assets on divorce. A litigant is bound by pleadings. An unsigned consent paper is not enforceable as a deed of settlement. Spousal maintenance may be awarded where circumstances justify continued support after divorce, including where a spouse was not allowed or enabled to work during the marriage.
held_decision_points:
Held, that a decree of divorce was granted.
    Held, that custody of the minor child was awarded to the defendant with access to the plaintiff.
    Held, that the defendant received specified household goods, 22.5% shares in Powerdrive Engineering, 42.5% shares in Dynamic Seals and Bearings, and a 30% share in the Mainway Meadows house.
    Held, that the plaintiff received the Isuzu vehicle and 70% of the house.
    Held, that plaintiff must pay defendant US$600 per month maintenance for five years.
    Held, that each party bears own costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the court must equitably distribute matrimonial property under section 7 while considering contributions and circumstances, and an unsigned draft settlement cannot displace the issues properly before the court.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court held that the defendant was not to be treated as a young woman who could simply work post-divorce because evidence suggested the plaintiff had accepted or encouraged her not working while funding her education.
cases_referred_to: Farisai Nando v Godwills Masimirembwa HH 154/17; Takafuma v Takafuma 1994 (2) ZLR 103 (S); Cressy Magumise v Alpheus Magumise SC 14/14; Chiomba v Chiomba 1992 (2) ZLR 197; Robinson v Randfontein Estates GM Co Ltd 1925 AD 173; other matrimonial-property authorities cited.
legislation_considered: Matrimonial Causes Act [Chapter 5:13], sections 7 and 8; Marriage Act [Chapter 5:11].

document_name: Inamo Investments (Private)Limited (672 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 714 (5 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: INAMO INVESTMENTS (PRIVATE) LIMITED versus ZIMBABWE REVENUE AUTHORITY
subject_area: Value Added Tax; declaratur; input tax and output tax; foreign currency VAT; set-off; tax statutory rights.
catchwords_flynote: VAT – declaratur sought that ZWL input tax be offset against USD output tax at interbank rate – sections 15, 28 and 38(4) VAT Act – no statutory framework for claimed cross-currency set-off – legal rights cannot arise from absence of prohibition – application dismissed with costs.
headnote: The applicant sought a declaration that it could offset Zimbabwe-dollar input tax against United States dollar output tax at the prevailing interbank rate when computing VAT. It argued that the VAT Act did not prohibit such set-off. ZIMRA opposed. The Court held that the applicant had no statutory right to the claimed set-off and that a legal right cannot be founded merely on the absence of a prohibition. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the VAT Act permits offsetting ZWL input tax against USD output tax at the interbank rate.
    Whether sections 15, 28 and 38(4) create the right claimed by the applicant.
    Whether absence of an express prohibition can found a legal right enforceable by declaratur.
legal_principles: A declaratory order requires an existing, future or contingent right or obligation. Tax rights and remedies must be founded in statute. A taxpayer cannot claim a set-off mechanism not provided by the VAT Act merely because no provision expressly prohibits it. Foreign currency VAT obligations under section 38(4) must be dealt with according to the statutory framework.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant had no legal right to the claimed cross-currency VAT set-off.
    Held, that absence of a statutory prohibition did not create a right.
    Held, that the application was devoid of merit.
    Held, that the application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that enforceable tax rights must be grounded in legislation; because the VAT Act did not create a right to set off ZWL input tax against USD output tax at the interbank rate, the court could not grant a declaratur compelling ZIMRA to allow such set-off.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that causes of action and legal rights cannot be founded on the absence of law; the right must be given by law, not by the absence of law.
cases_referred_to: Prosperous Days Investments v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority
legislation_considered: Value Added Tax Act [Chapter 23:12], sections 15, 28, 38(4) and 38(9); Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe Act [Chapter 22:15]; Exchange Control Act [Chapter 22:05].

document_name: GIGATR~1(1).DOC
case_name: S J W and H C M versus LOUISE MORRISBY and 4 OTHERS
subject_area: Administrative law; education law; review; school discipline; rights of learners; mootness; natural justice.
catchwords_flynote: Review – school disciplinary process regarding minor pupils – alleged conversation of violent and sexual nature – decision not to offer Lower Six places – whether disciplinary process capable of being set aside – joinder and mootness objections dismissed – relief incompetent – application dismissed with costs.
headnote: The guardians of two minor pupils sought review of the school authorities’ handling of allegations that the pupils had engaged in a disturbing conversation. They alleged breaches of natural justice and sought to set aside the disciplinary process and remove conviction/sentence records. The Court dismissed technical objections on concision and joinder and considered mootness. On the merits, it held that no formal disciplinary process capable of being set aside had taken place and that, although the decision not to admit the pupils to Lower Six could be construed as a sanction, the remedy sought was incompetent. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the application was defective for lack of concise review grounds.
    Whether trustees and governors were misjoined.
    Whether the matter was moot.
    Whether there was a disciplinary process capable of being set aside.
    Whether natural justice required the requested relief.
    Whether the decision not to offer Lower Six places should be set aside.
legal_principles: Review grounds must be shortly and clearly stated, but technical objections should not be used as ritualistic barriers where substance is clear. Mootness is a question of law and does not automatically bar determination if interests of justice require a decision. Natural justice in school discipline depends on context. A reviewing court must grant competent relief tied to an identifiable decision or process.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the objection that the grounds of review were not short, clear and concise was dismissed.
    Held, that the trustees and governors were properly cited.
    Held, that the remedy sought was incompetent because there was no disciplinary process capable of being set aside.
    Held, that the decision not to allow R and C back for Lower Six would not be set aside.
    Held, that the application was dismissed with costs jointly and severally.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that review relief must correspond to an identifiable administrative or disciplinary process; where the pleaded remedy seeks to set aside a process that did not formally occur, and the Court is not persuaded to set aside the admissions decision, the application cannot succeed notwithstanding concerns about procedure.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that the alleged conversation was serious and disturbing, and that the school authorities had a duty to investigate, but formal disciplinary proceedings did not ultimately take place in the manner alleged.
cases_referred_to:
Telecel Zimbabwe (Pvt) Ltd v Postal & Telecommunications Regulatory Authority of Zimbabwe & Others 2015 (1) ZLR 651 (H)
    Rufasha v Bindura University & Others 2016 (2) ZLR 668 (H)
    Muskwe v Nyajena 2001 (2) ZLR 514 (S)
    Zambezi Gas Zimbabwe (Pvt) Ltd v N.R. Barber (Pvt) Ltd and Another SC 3/20
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, rule 62(2); Education Act [Chapter 25:04], section 68A.

document_name: Mukarakati v The Trustees of the Don Moyo Family Trust and 2 Others (677 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 677 (5 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Mukarakati v The Trustees of the Don Moyo Family Trust and Others
subject_area: Sale in execution; setting aside confirmed sale; cause of action; pleading; common-law review; bad faith or fraud; punitive costs.
catchwords_flynote: Sale in execution – confirmed sale and transfer to purchaser – application to set aside sale and cancel title – failure to plead common-law grounds of fraud, bad faith or knowledge of irregularities – no cause of action – application dismissed with attorney-client costs.
headnote: The applicant sought to set aside a sale in execution and cancel title after a property had been sold, confirmed and transferred to the second respondent. The court held that once a sale in execution has been confirmed and transfer passed, the applicant must plead common-law grounds such as fraud, bad faith or knowledge of prior irregularities. The founding affidavit did not set out the required cause of action and the applicant sought to make his case in heads of argument. The point in limine of no cause of action was upheld and the application dismissed with attorney-client costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether the founding affidavit disclosed a cause of action to set aside a confirmed and transferred sale in execution; whether bad faith, fraud or knowledge of irregularities had been pleaded; whether service irregularities sufficed.
legal_principles: An application stands or falls on its founding affidavit. After confirmation and transfer following a sale in execution, common-law review grounds such as fraud, bad faith or knowledge of irregularities must be pleaded and proved. A Sheriff’s return of service is prima facie evidence of service.
held_decision_points: The point in limine on absence of a cause of action was upheld. The application was dismissed with attorney-client costs.
ratio_decidendi: The applicant failed to plead the essential common-law grounds required to impeach a sale after confirmation and transfer. Broad allegations in heads of argument could not cure the founding affidavit.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court criticised attempts to make out a case in heads of argument and highlighted the applicant’s failure to use rule-based remedies within time.
cases_referred_to: Durbach v Fairway Hotel Ltd 1949 (3) SA 1081 (SR); Farrell v Secretary of State for Defence [1980] 1 All ER 166; Patel v Controller of Customs and Excise 1982 (2) ZLR 82 (H); Peebles v Dairiboard Zimbabwe (Pvt) Ltd 1999 (1) ZLR 41 (H); Muchini v Adams & Ors SC 47/13; Mapedzamombe v Commercial Bank of Zimbabwe; Chiwanza v Matanda and Ors 2004 (2) ZLR 203 (H); Mutasa v Telecel Zimbabwe HH 331-14; Gundani v Kanyemba 1988 (1) ZLR 226 (S); TM Supermarkets (Pvt) v Avondale Holdings (Pvt) Ltd SC 37-17; Erasmus v Michael James (Pty) Ltd 1994 (2) SA 258 (C).

document_name: ZI1B84~1.DOC
case_name: Zimbabwe School Examinations Council v A R Mandizvidza Architect t/a Amandiz Architect
subject_area: Civil procedure; declaratur; arbitration clause; prescription; domestic remedies; contract for architectural services; premature application.
catchwords_flynote: Declaratory order – applicant seeking declaration that respondent’s architectural-fee claims had prescribed – contract contained arbitration clause – whether High Court should entertain declaratur before arbitration – application struck off as premature.
headnote: ZIMSEC sought a declaratur that the respondent’s claims for payment under a 2006 architectural-services contract had prescribed and were no longer enforceable. The respondent raised a preliminary objection that the matter was premature because the contract required disputes to be referred to arbitration. The court held that the dispute was fundamentally about non-payment of fees for services under the contract, and that the arbitration clause had to be respected. The application was struck off the roll with costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether the application was premature because of the arbitration clause; whether the High Court should determine prescription by declaratur despite the agreed arbitration process; whether the dispute was essentially one for non-payment of contractual fees; and whether domestic remedies had to be exhausted first.
legal_principles: Courts are slow to disregard contractual dispute-resolution clauses freely agreed by parties with legal capacity. Where a contract requires arbitration of disputes, that domestic remedy should ordinarily be pursued before court intervention, unless shown to be inadequate or ineffective. Declaratory proceedings should not be used to circumvent contractual arbitration merely because a party wishes to pre-empt a claim or invoice.
held_decision_points: The respondent’s point in limine succeeded. The court held that the matter was prematurely before it because the dispute had to be referred to arbitration under the contract. The application for declaratur was struck off the roll and the applicant ordered to pay costs.
ratio_decidendi: The real dispute was contractual non-payment, not an abstract question suitable for immediate declaratur. Since the contract provided a binding arbitration mechanism, the applicant had to follow that route before invoking the court’s declaratory jurisdiction.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified beyond warning against using declaratur applications to forestall ordinary contractual recovery claims or agreed arbitration procedures.
cases_referred_to: Books v Davidson 1988 (1) ZLR 365 (S); ZIMRA v Mudzimuwaona SC 4/18; Old Mutual Shared Services (Pvt) Ltd v Shadaya HH 15/13; Mwayipaida Family Trust v Madoroba & Ors 2004 (1) ZLR 439 (S); Moyo v Forestry Commission 1996 (1) ZLR 173 (H); Cargo Carriers (Pvt) Ltd v Zambezi & Others 1996 (1) ZLR 613 (S).
legislation_considered: High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], s 14.

document_name: Shangiwa and 5 Others v Ndimbarimba N.O and 2 Others (679 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 679 (5 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Benedict Shangiwa and 5 Others v Liona Ndimbarimba N.O. and 2 Others
subject_area: Labour law; jurisdiction; declaratur; mandamus; Labour Court exclusive jurisdiction; labour officer/designated agent ruling
catchwords_flynote: Labour dispute - applicants seeking declaratur and mandamus compelling designated agent to refer ruling to Labour Court under s 93(5a) of Labour Act - High Court jurisdiction - subject matter purely labour - application dismissed for want of jurisdiction.
headnote: The applicants were former employees whose contracts had been terminated under a retrenchment process. A designated agent issued a ruling which the applicants treated as a draft ruling requiring confirmation by the Labour Court under s 93(5a), while the respondents treated it as a final ruling. The applicants sought a declaratur and mandamus in the High Court. The court held that the matter was purely labour-related and fell within the exclusive jurisdiction of the Labour Court. The High Court declined jurisdiction.
issues_for_determination: Whether the High Court had jurisdiction to grant declaratory and mandamus relief in a labour dispute; whether the relief sought was in substance an employment/labour remedy; and whether the Labour Court was the appropriate forum.
legal_principles: Although the High Court has broad jurisdiction, labour matters fall within the specialised Labour Court’s exclusive purview where the real subject matter is labour or employment. A litigant cannot avoid the Labour Court’s jurisdictional framework by framing labour relief as a declaratur or mandamus.
held_decision_points: The application was dismissed, each party bearing its own costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that the High Court must decline jurisdiction where the dispute, rights asserted and relief sought arise from the Labour Act and employment proceedings, even if framed as declaratory or mandamus relief.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted that anomalies in the designated agent’s ruling contributed to the litigation and, for that reason, made no adverse costs order.
cases_referred_to: Johnsen v AFC 1994 (4) 95; Constable Sibanda v The Trial Officer (Chief Superintendent Masuku C) & 2 Others HB 128-18; Tribac Tobacco Limited v Tobacco Marketing Board 1996 (2) ZLR 52 (S); Nhari v Mugabe & Others SC 161/20; Chingombe & Another v City of Harare & Others SC 177/20; Muchenje v Mutangadura & 4 Others HH 21-21; TN Harlequin Luxaire Limited v Mberikunashe Matsvimbo & 14 Others SCB 84-22; Drum City (Pvt) Ltd v Brenda Garudzo SC 57-18; Isoquant Investments (Pvt) Ltd v Memory Darikwa CCZ 6-20.
