document_name: Busangabanye and Another v Matsika and 5 Others (680 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 680 (28 September 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: PHIBEON BUSANGABANYE and TRANSFRONTIER INVESTMENTS (PVT) LTD versus FARAI MATSIKA and 5 OTHERS
citation: Busangabanye and Another v Matsika and 5 Others (680 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 680 (28 September 2022)
subject_area: Civil procedure; company law; derivative action; answering affidavits; new evidence; interlocutory applications; corporate governance dispute.
catchwords_flynote: Civil procedure – answering affidavit – application to expunge portions of answering affidavit – whether answering affidavit impermissibly introduced new facts or evidence – derivative action under Companies and Other Business Entities Act – disputed corporate asset transfer – court's discretion to admit additional material – prejudice curable by further affidavits and costs – application to expunge dismissed.
headnote: The respondents sought to expunge parts of the applicants' answering affidavit in proceedings concerning an ex parte provisional order obtained by way of derivative action. The impugned material concerned the sale and transfer of the Graniteside property, allegedly an asset of Doves Life Assurance. The Court held that the additional material related to the existing dispute and that a robust approach was justified given the corporate governance and policyholder interests implicated. Any prejudice could be addressed by allowing respondents to file further affidavits and heads. The application to expunge was dismissed, with the applicants to pay costs of the interlocutory application.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the answering affidavit introduced new facts or evidence that materially altered the cause of action.
    Whether the impugned annexures and affidavit should be expunged.
    Whether any prejudice to respondents could be cured by further affidavits, heads of argument and costs.
    Whether the wider corporate-governance context justified admission of the material.
legal_principles: An answering affidavit may not ordinarily be used to make out a new case or introduce a new cause of action. The court retains a discretion, in appropriate circumstances, to permit additional facts or material where justice requires and prejudice can be cured. The discretion is exercised with regard to materiality, explanation for omission, absence of mala fides, prejudice, and the interests of final determination of the real dispute.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the impugned material did not warrant excision at this stage.
    Held, that the corporate context and the potential interests of shareholders and policyholders justified a robust approach.
    Held, that respondents could respond through additional affidavits and supplementary heads of argument.
    Held, that the application to expunge was dismissed.
    Held, that the applicants were to pay the costs of the interlocutory application.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that allegedly new material in an answering affidavit should not automatically be expunged where it is connected to the existing cause of action and where any prejudice can be cured by directions allowing responsive affidavits and heads; the court may adopt a robust approach in complex corporate disputes affecting wider stakeholder interests.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted the risk of further legal clutter but considered that the rules afforded a mechanism for dealing with it through further affidavits, heads and costs.
cases_referred_to:
United Refineries Limited v The Mining Industry Pension Fund
    Kombayi v Berkout 1988 (1) ZLR 53 (S)

document_name: Nyakudya v Vibranium Resources (Pvt) Ltd and 2 Others (681 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 681 (5 October 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: Nyakudya v Vibranium Resources (Pvt) Ltd and Others
subject_area: Mining law; review; cancellation of mining certificate; procedural irregularity; statutory notice; registered post.
catchwords_flynote: Mining certificate cancellation – s 50 Mines and Minerals Act – peremptory notice requirements – proposed cancellation date omitted – service by registered post not proved – cancellation set aside – counterclaim for eviction dismissed.
headnote: The applicant sought review of the Provincial Mining Director’s cancellation of mining certificate number 47622 for Shamva “X” Mine. A boundary dispute had led the second respondent to conclude that the applicant had over-pegged onto claims held by the first respondent. The court held that cancellation of a mining registration certificate must comply strictly with s 50 of the Mines and Minerals Act. The notice of intention to cancel failed to specify a proposed date of cancellation and the respondents failed to prove service by registered post. The cancellation was procedurally invalid. The cancellation was set aside and the first respondent’s counterclaim for eviction dismissed.
issues_for_determination: Whether the cancellation complied with s 50 of the Mines and Minerals Act; whether the notice specified the proposed date of cancellation and was properly served; whether the first respondent could obtain eviction on the basis of the cancellation.
legal_principles: Where a statute prescribes a procedure for cancellation of a mining certificate, the procedure is peremptory. The notice must state the grounds, proposed date of cancellation, and inform the holder of the right to appeal before that date. Service must be by registered post to the recorded postal address, or by Gazette publication where no address exists. Proof of posting alone does not prove delivery where service is denied.
held_decision_points: The decision to cancel mining licence certificate “X” Mine registration number 47622 was set aside and the status quo ante the purported cancellation was restored. The first respondent’s counterclaim was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The notice was defective and service was not proved. Because the cancellation procedure was statutory and peremptory, non-compliance rendered the cancellation invalid and removed the foundation for the eviction counterclaim.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted that whether the cancellation decision was reasonable was not the issue; the decisive issue was procedural compliance.
cases_referred_to: Affretair (Pvt) Ltd and Another v MK Airlines (Pvt) Ltd 1996 (2) ZLR 15 (S); authorities on peremptory statutory provisions and review as cited in the judgment.
legislation_considered: Mines and Minerals Act [Chapter 21:05], especially s 50; High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], s 27; High Court Rules, 2021.
reviewer_comments: Draft.

document_name: The Sheriff of Zimbabwe v Chikango and Another (683 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 683 (6 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: The Sheriff of Zimbabwe v Chikango and Another
subject_area: Interpleader; execution; movable property at judgment debtor’s residence/business premises; customary law assertions; burden of proof; legal practitioner-client costs.
catchwords_flynote: Interpleader – movable property attached at judgment debtor’s premises – claimant alleged property belonged to him after moving into deceased uncle’s house under customary law – no estate registration or supporting affidavits – whether claimant discharged onus of ownership – whether attached property executable.
headnote: The Sheriff filed an interpleader notice relating to movable property attached at Stand 125 Monavale, Harare. The claimant alleged that the property belonged exclusively to him because he had moved into his late uncle’s house with his own property under customary law after the judgment debtor’s death. The judgment creditor opposed, contending that the property belonged to the judgment debtor and that the premises doubled as the debtor’s business premises. The court found the claimant’s story implausible and held that he had failed to prove ownership.
issues_for_determination: Who was in possession of the attached property at the time of attachment; whether the claimant proved ownership; whether customary-law assertions and occupation of the premises rebutted the presumption arising from the judgment debtor’s possession; and whether the property should be declared executable.
legal_principles: In interpleader proceedings, possession at the time of attachment determines the operative presumption. If the judgment debtor is in possession, the property is presumed to belong to the debtor and the claimant bears the onus to prove ownership on a balance of probabilities. Bald assertions are insufficient; documentary or other credible evidence may be required depending on the facts.
held_decision_points: The claimant failed to prove ownership. The court found it implausible that he had replaced all the judgment debtor’s property with his own after moving into the premises. There was no supporting affidavit from the deceased’s widow and no proper evidence of lawful distribution of the debtor’s property. The property was declared executable.
ratio_decidendi: Because the goods were attached at the judgment debtor’s residence and business premises, and the claimant did not produce satisfactory proof of ownership, the claimant failed to rebut the presumption that the goods belonged to the judgment debtor.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The judgment emphasized that a bald assertion of ownership is insufficient in interpleader proceedings.
cases_referred_to: Greenfield N.O. v Blignaught and Others 1953 (3) SA 597; The Sheriff of the High Court of Zimbabwe v Munyaradzi Majoni and Others HH 689/15; The Sheriff of the High Court of Zimbabwe v Virginia Sibanda and Another HH 275/18; Deputy Sheriff v Lameck and Others HH 269/18; Sabarauta v Local Authorities Pension Fund and the Sheriff SC 77/17; Sheriff of Zimbabwe v Mahachi and Leomarch Engineering HMA 34/18.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, r 63.
reviewer_comments: The filename uses the conventional form “v Chikango and Another”, while the extracted judgment heading is interpleader-style and lists parties as “The Sheriff of Zimbabwe and Kudzania Chikango and Owen Potani”. Exact document name preserved.

document_name: EX-CON~2.DOC
case_name: EX-CONSTABLE VENGAI CHIKURUNGENI versus THE COMMISSIONER GENERAL OF POLICE and THE POLICE SERVICE COMMISSION
subject_area: Administrative law; police service; review; eight-week rule; condonation; dismissal for lateness.
catchwords_flynote: Review – police trainee discharged as unfit for police duties due to stammer/communication problems – application filed outside eight weeks – no condonation sought – court had no discretion – application dismissed.
headnote: The applicant sought review of his discharge from the Police Service on the basis that he was found to be a stammer with serious communication problems. The respondents raised a point in limine that the review was filed outside the eight-week period required by rule 62(4) without condonation. The Court accepted documentary evidence that the applicant was advised of his discharge by 23 December 2021 and filed only on 29 March 2022. In the absence of condonation, the Court held it had no discretion and dismissed the application.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the review application was filed within the eight-week period required by rule 62(4).
    Whether the applicant proved that the discharge letter was backdated.
    Whether the court could entertain an out-of-time review without condonation.
legal_principles: Rule 62(4) requires review proceedings to be instituted within eight weeks of termination of the proceedings or decision complained of, unless the court extends time for good cause shown. Where review proceedings are out of time and no condonation is sought, the court has no discretion but to dismiss the application.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant failed to prove that the December termination letter was backdated or first supplied on 31 January 2022.
    Held, that documentary evidence showed he was advised of discharge by 23 December 2021.
    Held, that the review filed on 29 March 2022 was out of time.
    Held, that no application for condonation was before the court.
    Held, that the application was dismissed with ordinary costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that a review filed outside the prescribed eight-week period is not properly before the court unless accompanied by or preceded by condonation; absent such condonation, the court lacks discretion to consider the merits.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to:
Mutare City Council v Mudzime and Others 1999 (2) ZLR 140
    Forestry Commission v Moyo 1997 (1) ZLR 254 (S)
    Matsambire v Gweru City Council S-183-95

document_name: S v Nyamunda (687 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 687 (28 July 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: The State v Petros Nyamunda
subject_area: Criminal review; unlawful entry; possession of property reasonably suspected stolen; evidential requirements
catchwords_flynote: Criminal review - alternative charge of possessing property reasonably suspected stolen - accused acquitted of unlawful entry but convicted on alternative charge - satisfactory explanation for possession - requirement that suspicion arise from circumstances perceived by the person calling for an explanation - conviction unsafe - accused acquitted.
headnote: The accused was charged with unlawful entry into a hardware shop, alternatively possession of property reasonably suspected of being stolen. He was acquitted of the main charge after the trial magistrate accepted that there was insufficient evidence of unlawful entry. The accused had displayed the complainant’s goods in his shop with the complainant’s price tags still attached and explained that he had bought them from a known seller named Brighton. The trial court nevertheless convicted on the alternative charge because the price was negotiated down substantially. On review, the High Court held that the accused’s explanation was satisfactory and that the investigating officer had not explained any reasonable suspicion formulated from the circumstances of possession.
issues_for_determination: Whether the alternative conviction under s 125 was safe; whether the accused’s explanation for possession was satisfactory; whether the court could itself formulate suspicion; and whether the investigating officer had established the elements of the offence.
legal_principles: For possession of property reasonably suspected of being stolen, the State must prove possession of property capable of being stolen, circumstances giving rise to reasonable suspicion when the accused came into possession, and failure to give a satisfactory explanation. The suspicion must arise from the person calling on the accused to account, usually the police officer, and the court cannot formulate the suspicion on its own after the fact. A satisfactory explanation need not be perfect; it must be acceptable or good enough.
held_decision_points: The conviction and sentence on the alternative charge were set aside. The accused was found not guilty and acquitted.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that conviction under s 125 cannot stand where the accused gives a plausible explanation for possession and the State fails to prove the circumstances that gave rise to the required reasonable suspicion.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court cautioned that s 125 is difficult to prove and that judicial officers should be circumspect when returning guilty verdicts on that charge, especially because the suspicion must be established by evidence, not judicial assumption.
cases_referred_to: Barros and Another v Chimponda 1999 (1) ZLR 58(S); Nyahondo v Hokonya & Ors 1997 (2) ZLR 457; S v Gaviyaya 2008 (2) ZLR 159 (H).
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], ss 125, 131(1)(a); Magistrates Court Act [Chapter 7:10], s 57.

document_name: Mugari (Nee Tapfuma) v Mugari (689 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 689 (12 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Mugari (Nee Tapfuma) v Mugari
subject_area: Civil procedure; substituted service; civil imprisonment; personal service; liberty of person.
catchwords_flynote: Substituted service – summons for civil imprisonment – personal service required where liberty may be affected – service on workplace or attorneys in another matter insufficient – application dismissed.
headnote: The applicant sought substituted service of summons for civil imprisonment after a writ of execution yielded a nulla bona return. She wished to serve the respondent through a responsible person at his workplace and through attorneys acting for him in another matter. The court held that where liberty is at stake, r 15(12) of the High Court Rules requires personal service. The applicant had not shown that personal service was impossible or that substituted service was justified. The application was dismissed.
issues_for_determination: Whether substituted service could be authorised for summons for civil imprisonment; whether service on attorneys in another matter was permissible; whether personal service was mandatory.
legal_principles: Process affecting liberty or status must be served personally. Substituted service is not a substitute where reasonable efforts may still enable personal service.
held_decision_points: The application for substituted service was dismissed.
ratio_decidendi: Because the summons could affect the respondent’s liberty, r 15(12) required personal service. The applicant had not exhausted practical avenues for personal service and could not rely on service through unrelated attorneys.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court emphasized that civil imprisonment is harsh and drastic, requiring strict procedural compliance.
cases_referred_to: Chamunorwa Mutyambizi v Jose Masel, cited in the judgment.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, rr 15(12), 19 and 60(2).
constitutional_provisions_considered: Right to liberty implicated by the nature of civil imprisonment, though no specific constitutional section was quoted in the extracted text.

document_name: S v Tenha (690 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 690 (30 September 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: The State v Justin Tenha
subject_area: Criminal law; murder; identification evidence; recognition; voluntary intoxication; sentencing
catchwords_flynote: Murder - accused suspected deceased of stealing scrap wire - deceased assaulted at night after earlier confrontation - identification by witness familiar with accused - recognition evidence - alleged voluntary intoxication raised late - accused convicted - 20 years imprisonment.
headnote: The accused suspected the deceased of stealing scrap wire. Earlier in the evening he confronted the deceased at a shebeen. Later that night the deceased was attacked at his cabin and died from head injuries. The accused denied being at the cabin and alleged only an earlier fight at the shebeen. The State led evidence from witnesses who knew the accused, recognised his voice and saw or heard him assaulting the deceased. The court accepted the identification as reliable recognition. It rejected a belated intoxication argument and found the accused guilty of murder. The court held that the murder was not committed in statutory aggravating circumstances, but imposed a lengthy custodial sentence because of the brutal assault and lack of remorse.
issues_for_determination: Whether the accused was the person who attacked the deceased at the cabin; whether identification evidence was reliable; whether voluntary intoxication affected liability; whether the accused intended to kill; and what sentence was appropriate.
legal_principles: Evidence of identification must be treated with caution, but recognition by witnesses who know the accused, aided by voice, lighting, proximity and surrounding circumstances, may be reliable. A defence raised for the first time in closing submissions, without evidential foundation, may be rejected as an afterthought. Murder sentencing must consider the offender, the offence and societal interests, and statutory aggravating circumstances under s 47.
held_decision_points: The accused was found guilty of murder. The court found no statutory aggravating circumstances warranting death or the highest range but sentenced him to 20 years’ imprisonment.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that recognition evidence from familiar witnesses, supported by voice identification and surrounding conduct, can prove the identity of a nighttime assailant beyond reasonable doubt where the accused’s denial is palpably false.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court remarked that the late assertion of voluntary intoxication appeared to be an afterthought and an implicit concession that the accused was present at the scene.
cases_referred_to: S v Mtetwa 1972 (3) SA 766.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 47; Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], s 314.

document_name: ZI80FF~1.DOC
case_name: Zimbabwe Teachers Association (Mashonaland West) v Nyamudzoza and 2 Others
subject_area: Provisional sentence; acknowledgment of debt; liquid document; caveat subscriptor; SI 33 of 2019; Zambezi Gas; USD debt; renunciation of exceptions.
catchwords_flynote: Provisional sentence – acknowledgment of debt signed after underlying transaction – debt expressed in United States dollars or interbank equivalent – whether court should investigate underlying transaction and SI 33/2019 conversion – provisional sentence granted.
headnote: The plaintiff sought provisional sentence for US$155,138.75 based on an acknowledgment of debt signed by the defendants. The court considered whether the acknowledgment of debt was an attempt to circumvent SI 33 of 2019 or the Zambezi Gas decision, since the underlying loan arose before the effective date. It held that a claim may be founded on the acknowledgment of debt itself without investigating the underlying transaction, particularly where exceptions were renounced and no duress or invalidity was alleged. Provisional sentence was granted.
issues_for_determination: Whether the acknowledgment of debt was a valid liquid document; whether the court should investigate the underlying transaction and currency conversion under SI 33/2019 and Zambezi Gas; whether an acknowledgment of debt creates or supports a distinct cause of action; and whether provisional sentence should be granted.
legal_principles: A valid acknowledgment of debt is a liquid document capable of founding provisional sentence. A creditor may sue on the debtor’s admission of liability without founding the action on the original transaction. Under the caveat subscriptor rule, a party is bound by a signed contract or acknowledgment unless vitiating factors are shown. Renunciation of exceptions in an acknowledgment of debt limits the debtor’s ability to resist payment on those grounds.
held_decision_points: The court granted provisional sentence in the sum of US$155,138.75 or equivalent at the prevailing interbank rate, plus prescribed interest from 31 March 2020 to payment in full, and costs on the legal practitioner-client scale.
ratio_decidendi: The acknowledgment of debt was clear, certain, signed and not shown to be invalid. Because the action was competently founded on that acknowledgment, the court did not need to interrogate the underlying transaction or the SI 33/2019 conversion issue.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified beyond the court’s treatment of post-SI 33 acknowledgments of debt as distinct enforceable liquid documents where properly signed.
cases_referred_to: Caltex (Africa) Ltd v Trade Fair Motors and Another 1963 (1) SA 36 (SR); Barend van Wyk v Tarcon (Pvt) Ltd SC 49-14; Mahomed Adam (Edms) Beperk v Raubenheimer 1966 (3) SA 646 (TPD); Muchabaiwa v Grab Enterprises (Pvt) Ltd 1996 (2) ZLR 691 (SC); National and Grindlays Bank v Yelverton 1972 (1) RLR 365 (G); Zambezi Gas Zimbabwe (Pvt) Ltd v NR Barber (Pvt) Ltd and Another SC 3-20.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, r 14(1); Presidential Powers (Temporary Measures) (Amendment of Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe Act and Issue of RTGS Dollars) Regulations, 2019 (SI 33/2019).

document_name: Dengezi v Nyamaruru and 4 Others (693 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 693 (10 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name:
“Whether or not section 70 of the Police Act which sets the prescription period of eight (8) months for any person to institute civil proceedings against the Police is ultra vires section 56 (1) and 69 (3) of the Constitution and therefore unconstitutional.” 
    2.	Pending the Constitutional Court’s decision as in (1) above, the applicant’s action is hereby stayed.


    Tendai Biti Law, applicant’s legal practitioners
    Civil Division of the Attorney-General’s Office, fourth  and fifth respondents’ legal practitioners
citation: Dengezi v Nyamaruru and 4 Others (693 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 693 (10 October 2022)
subject_area: Constitutional litigation; referral to Constitutional Court; prescription; Police Act limitation period; third-party insurance statutory limits; road traffic accident damages.
catchwords_flynote: Constitutional referral – plaintiff injured and child killed in road traffic accident during police traffic enforcement – request to refer constitutionality of Road Traffic Act insurance limits and Police Act eight-month limitation – Road Traffic Act question frivolous/moot on facts – Police Act question referred.
headnote: The applicant sought referral of two constitutional questions arising from her damages action after she was injured and her one-year-old child was killed by a commuter omnibus fleeing police. The Court held that the question concerning statutory insurance limits under section 23 of the Road Traffic Act did not arise on her facts and was frivolous or moot because she retained a delictual remedy against the tortfeasors. However, the challenge to section 70 of the Police Act, which imposes an eight-month limitation period for civil proceedings against police, was not frivolous or vexatious. That question was referred to the Constitutional Court and the action was stayed pending that decision.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the request to refer the Road Traffic Act insurance-limit provisions was frivolous or vexatious.
    Whether the request to refer section 70 of the Police Act was frivolous or vexatious.
    Whether the constitutional questions arose from the facts and were live issues.
    Whether the High Court was obliged to refer under section 175(4) of the Constitution.
legal_principles: Under section 175(4) of the Constitution, a court subordinate to the Constitutional Court must refer a constitutional question if requested unless the request is frivolous or vexatious. The court must itself assess frivolity and vexatiousness and cannot abdicate that duty to the parties. Constitutional avoidance and subsidiarity require that constitutional issues not be referred where the litigant has an adequate non-constitutional remedy or where the question is moot, abstract or not raised by the facts.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the Road Traffic Act question did not arise from the applicant’s factual position and was frivolous/moot.
    Held, that section 23(3)(c) concerning property damage did not apply because the applicant claimed no property damage.
    Held, that the applicant had a delictual damages remedy, so referral of the insurance-limit question was not necessary.
    Held, that the challenge to section 70 of the Police Act was not frivolous or vexatious because the eight-month limitation directly affected her access to court.
    Held, that the Police Act question should be referred and the action stayed pending the Constitutional Court decision.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that a constitutional referral under section 175(4) depends on whether the question is live, factually connected and capable of affecting the litigant’s remedy; section 70 of the Police Act met that threshold, whereas the Road Traffic Act insurance-limit issue did not.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted that the right of access to courts is central because it is the platform through which other rights are vindicated.
cases_referred_to:
Tomana v Judicial Service Commission HH 281/16
    Nyika v Minister of Home Affairs & Ors CCZ 5/20
    Levy Nyagura v Ncube N.O. & Ors CCZ 7/19
    Sports and Recreation Commission v Sagittarius Wrestling Club & Anor 2001 (2) ZLR 501 (S)
    Nyika v Minister of Home Affairs HH 181/16
legislation_considered: Constitution of Zimbabwe, 2013, section 175(4); Road Traffic Act [Chapter 13:11], section 23(3)(b) and (c); Police Act [Chapter 11:10], section 70; State Liabilities Act [Chapter 8:15]; Prescription Act [Chapter 8:11].

document_name: Hatcliff Community Church v Northside Community Church and Another (695 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 695 (12 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: HATCLIFFE COMMUNITY CHURCH versus NORTHSIDE COMMUNITY CHURCH AND ANOTHER
subject_area: Civil procedure; taxation of costs; wasted costs; costs of the day; postponement; referral by taxing officer.
catchwords_flynote: Taxation – referral by taxing officer – meaning of “costs of the day” after postponement – whether preparatory trial costs recoverable – test whether costs rendered useless by postponement – some attendances allowed in limited form – no order as to costs.
headnote: After a trial was postponed sine die at the first defendant’s instance, the Court ordered the first defendant to pay the plaintiff’s costs of the day on a legal practitioner and client scale. The parties disagreed during taxation over whether “costs of the day” included all trial-preparation costs or only costs directly caused by the postponement. The Court held that only costs rendered useless or extra costs caused by the postponement were recoverable as costs of the day. It gave guidance on which items could be allowed and made no order as to costs.
issues_for_determination:
What is meant by “costs of the day” in a wasted-costs order following postponement.
    Whether trial-preparation costs before the hearing date became wasted costs.
    Which specific items in the plaintiff’s bill should be allowed or disallowed.
legal_principles: Wasted costs are costs rendered useless and unnecessary by the postponement. Costs of the day generally include extra costs caused by the postponement, including wasted attendance by legal practitioners and witnesses, but do not automatically include all preparatory costs that remain useful for the resumed trial.
held_decision_points:
Held, that preparatory costs not rendered useless by the postponement should not be allowed.
    Held, that items 45 and 47 should be allowed only to the extent that they related to preparatory work involving witnesses required to attend court.
    Held, that counsel’s preparatory work under item 48 was not rendered useless and must be disallowed.
    Held, that post-postponement costs are allowed only where directly caused by the postponement.
    Held, that there was no order as to costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that an order for “costs of the day” after postponement is compensatory for costs wasted or directly caused by the postponement, not a general recovery of all costs incurred in preparing for trial where that work remains useful.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified
cases_referred_to:
Carlis v Hay 1903 TS 317
    O’Reilly v Lakofski 1933 WLD 145
    Zimbabwe Banking Corporation Limited v Trust Finance Limited & Another HH 130/06
    Kika v Malaba & 3 Ors HH 297/21
    Anton Herbert Taute NO v Gert Petrus Johannes Heymans, Free State High Court case No. 6032/08

document_name: Masiya v Chikunguwo and 2 Others (696 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 696 (12 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Masiya v Chikunguwo and Others
subject_area: Contract; sale of immovable property; simulated transaction; illegality; Banking Act; material disputes of fact.
catchwords_flynote: Agreement of sale – immovable property – allegation that sale was security for loan – simulated transaction – loan shark – contravention of Banking Act – illegality – void ab initio.
headnote: The applicant sought an order compelling transfer and vacant possession of an immovable property allegedly sold to him by the first respondent for US$50,000. The first respondent denied any sale and said he had borrowed US$14,000 at interest, signing sale and transfer documents only as security. The court accepted that the purported sale was a simulated transaction intended to disguise an unlawful loan. It held that the transaction was void ab initio for illegality and dismissed the application with costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether the transaction was a genuine sale or a simulated loan agreement; whether there were material disputes requiring oral evidence; whether the court could enforce the agreement.
legal_principles: Courts will not enforce contracts entered in fraudem legis or contracts that are void for illegality. A simulated agreement may be exposed by examining surrounding circumstances and the parties’ true intention.
held_decision_points: The transaction was an illegal loan disguised as a sale. The agreement was void ab initio and no rights flowed from it. The application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The surrounding evidence, including repayment documents and the loan context, showed that the parties never intended a true sale; the sale documents were only designed to give credence to the simulation and avoid the law.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Had the court found genuine material disputes of fact, it would have referred the matter to trial, but the illegality was sufficiently established on the papers.
cases_referred_to: Authorities on simulated transactions and illegal money-lending as cited in the full text.
legislation_considered: Banking Act [Chapter 24:20]; High Court Rules, 2021.
reviewer_comments: Draft.

document_name: Nhau v Zuze (697 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 697 (12 October 2022) Copy(2).docx
case_name: Nhau v Zuze
subject_area: Civil appeal; interlocutory ruling; absolution from the instance; magistrates court appeal; finality of decisions.
catchwords_flynote: Appeal – magistrate’s refusal of absolution from the instance – ruling not final and definitive – premature appeal – interlocutory decisions appealable only if having final effect – appeal dismissed with higher costs.
headnote: The appellant appealed against a magistrates court ruling dismissing his application for absolution from the instance after the close of the respondent’s case in an eviction matter. The High Court held that the ruling was interlocutory and not final and definitive. Section 40(2)(b) of the Magistrates Court Act does not permit an appeal against such a ruling unless it has the effect of a final judgment. The appeal was premature and was dismissed with costs on a higher scale.
issues_for_determination: Whether an appeal lay from a magistrate’s refusal of absolution from the instance; whether the ruling was final and definitive; whether the appeal was premature.
legal_principles: An appeal from an interlocutory order of a magistrates court is limited to orders having the effect of a final and definitive judgment. Premature appeals against non-final rulings delay litigation and are generally incompetent.
held_decision_points: The appeal was dismissed with costs on a higher scale.
ratio_decidendi: The magistrate’s refusal of absolution did not determine the parties’ rights finally. The trial was to continue and the issues remained open for determination at the end of the proceedings.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court emphasised the need for finality and warned against using appeals against interlocutory rulings to delay proceedings.
cases_referred_to: Authorities on appealability of interlocutory decisions and finality as cited in the judgment.
legislation_considered: Magistrates Court Act [Chapter 7:10], s 40(2)(b).
reviewer_comments: Draft.

document_name: EXSERG~1.DOC
case_name: EX SERGEANT T MAJOR JAMBWA and 3 OTHERS versus THE COMMISSIONER GENERAL OF THE POLICE and THE TRIAL OFFICER SUPERINTENDENT CHINYAMA N.O.
subject_area: Administrative law; police disciplinary proceedings; review; bias; trial officer; exception; real and substantial justice.
catchwords_flynote: Police disciplinary proceedings – trial officer dismissed exception in brief ruling – trial officer opposed review and defended his own decision – bias and failure to give reasons – proceedings not in accordance with real and substantial justice – review granted.
headnote: The applicants, one retired and three serving police members, were charged under the Police Act and raised an exception that the charge did not disclose an offence. The trial officer dismissed the exception. On review, he filed an opposing affidavit defending his ruling. The High Court held that by entering the arena and defending his own decision, the trial officer exposed himself to a finding of bias. His brief ruling also failed to give reasons sufficient to enable review against the standard of real and substantial justice. The review was granted.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the trial officer’s conduct created a reasonable apprehension or proof of bias.
    Whether the ruling dismissing the exception was reasoned and in accordance with real and substantial justice.
    Whether the disciplinary proceedings should be reviewed.
legal_principles: Review of inferior courts and tribunals exists to ensure fair treatment by decision-makers. Under section 27 of the High Court Act, bias, malice, corruption and gross irregularity can justify review. A presiding officer whose decision is under review should ordinarily abide by the reviewing court’s decision or provide factual assistance, rather than oppose the application as a partisan defender of the decision.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the trial officer misdirected himself by filing a notice of opposition and defending his own work.
    Held, that the trial officer’s conduct supported the applicants’ allegation of bias.
    Held, that the ruling dismissing the exception was too brief and did not provide reasons adequate for review.
    Held, that the applicants proved their case on a balance of probabilities.
    Held, that the review application was granted.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that a disciplinary trial officer who opposes review proceedings and actively defends his own ruling descends into the arena and compromises the impartiality required of a quasi-judicial decision-maker; coupled with inadequate reasons, this justifies review.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court remarked that the trial officer’s decision to oppose the review created a trap for himself and gave the applicants their judgment “on a platter.”
cases_referred_to:
Magugu v Police Service Commission & Another 2010 (2) ZLR 185 (H)
    Chillufiya v Commissioner-General of Police & Others HH 89/10
legislation_considered: Police Act [Chapter 11:10], Schedule paragraphs 34 and 11; High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], section 27; High Court Rules, 2021, rule 62(1).

document_name: Chimanikire v Doves Funeral Assurance (Pvt) Ltd and 2 Others (699 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 699 (12 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: EULA CHIMANIKIRE versus DOVES FUNERAL ASSURANCE (PVT) LTD and 2 OTHERS
citation: Chimanikire v Doves Funeral Assurance (Pvt) Ltd and 2 Others (699 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 699 (12 October 2022)
subject_area: Civil procedure; rescission; condonation; dismissal for want of prosecution; judicial sale in execution; finality of litigation; abuse of process.
catchwords_flynote: Rescission – judicial sale in execution – repeated interlocutory litigation – delay and inadequate explanation – prospects of success in underlying matter – finality of litigation – application refused with costs.
headnote: The applicant sought to revive proceedings aimed at setting aside a confirmed judicial sale of her former matrimonial home. An earlier chamber application had been dismissed for want of prosecution; a first rescission attempt was withdrawn as defective; the present application was a second attempt. The Court held that the papers were deficient, the delay was inadequately explained, prospects of success were poor and finality of litigation required an end to repetitive procedural breaches. The application was refused with costs on the ordinary scale.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant gave a satisfactory explanation for the delay.
    Whether the applicant had prospects of success in the underlying attempt to challenge the judicial sale.
    Whether the application amounted to abuse of process.
    Whether finality of litigation and the administration of justice militated against rescission.
legal_principles: A party seeking rescission or condonation must explain the delay fully, show bona fides and demonstrate prospects of success. A party served with an application for dismissal for want of prosecution must treat it as a trigger to act. Courts discourage repetitive procedural default and litigation that undermines finality.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant's affidavits did not provide the requisite coherent and detailed explanation.
    Held, that the delay was not satisfactorily explained.
    Held, that prospects of success in the underlying matter were not established.
    Held, that repeated procedural breaches and the age of the dispute weighed against the applicant.
    Held, that the application was refused with costs on the ordinary scale.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that rescission cannot be granted where an applicant gives an inadequate explanation for extensive procedural delay, fails to show real prospects in the underlying challenge to a long-confirmed judicial sale, and seeks to prolong litigation contrary to finality and the administration of justice.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court reminded litigants and practitioners to present court papers with clear, complete and coherent factual narratives rather than attempting to fill gaps through heads of argument or submissions from the bar.

document_name: Nyamayaro v Albert Chitaunhike N.O and Another (700 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 700 (12 October 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: Nyamayaro v Chitaunhike N.O. and Another
subject_area: Civil procedure; rescission of judgment; joinder; direct and substantial interest; land lease; default judgment.
catchwords_flynote: Rescission under r 29 – applicant not cited in eviction proceedings – alleged verbal lease over farm – direct and substantial interest disputed – judgment not erroneously granted where court unaware of alleged interest – rescission refused.
headnote: The applicant sought rescission of a judgment granted in her absence in HC 1202/21 and consequential joinder. She claimed a direct and substantial interest as a lessee of a farm allocated to the late Ignatius Matungamire. The executor had obtained an order against the second respondent and all claiming through him. The court held that the applicant’s alleged direct interest was disputed and that the prior judgment had not been erroneously granted merely because the court was unaware of her alleged interest. The application was dismissed.
issues_for_determination: Whether the applicant had shown direct and substantial interest; whether non-joinder rendered the prior judgment erroneously granted; whether good and sufficient cause for rescission was established.
legal_principles: Rescission under r 29 requires a judgment erroneously sought or granted in the absence of an affected party. A judgment is not necessarily erroneously granted where the court was unaware of an alleged interested party. The interest relied on must be legal and direct, not merely financial or speculative.
held_decision_points: The application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The applicant’s alleged independent lease and interest were directly disputed and were not before the court that granted the original order. The prior judgment was therefore not shown to have been erroneously granted. The applicant did not establish sufficient cause for rescission.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted but did not decide the argument that any alleged agricultural lease might be invalid under s 28 of the Land Commission Act.

document_name: Ipomea Enterprises (Private) Limited v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority (701 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 701 (11 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: IPOMEA ENTERPRISES (PRIVATE) LIMITED versus ZIMBABWE REVENUE AUTHORITY
subject_area: Customs and excise; forfeiture; seizure of vehicles; smuggling; transporter liability; administrative decision-making; costs.
catchwords_flynote: Customs and Excise – seizure and forfeiture of trucks and tankers – alleged fuel smuggling under false declaration – transporter not importer – clearing agent submitted false notices – whether forfeiture justified – section 174(1)(d) Customs and Excise Act – points in limine on notice and prescription – forfeiture set aside – vehicles ordered released.
headnote: The applicant sought to set aside ZIMRA’s decision to forfeit its trucks and tankers used to transport fuel from Mozambique to Zimbabwe. ZIMRA alleged that fuel had been falsely declared as crude degummed soya bean oil. The applicant argued that it was merely the transporter and that the false declarations were made by the clearing agent without its knowledge or connivance. The Court dismissed ZIMRA’s preliminary objections and held that the forfeiture decision could not stand. The trucks and tankers were ordered to be released, with each party bearing its own costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the application was barred by failure to issue notice or by prescription.
    Whether the applicant, as transporter, contravened section 174(1)(d) of the Customs and Excise Act.
    Whether the clearing agent’s conduct justified forfeiture of the applicant’s trucks and tankers.
    Whether costs should be awarded against ZIMRA.
legal_principles: Civil proceedings against ZIMRA under the Customs and Excise Act must comply with statutory notice and time-limit provisions. A transporter’s liability for smuggling requires assessment of the conduct of the person in charge of the vehicle and whether reasonable precautions were taken. Forfeiture is serious and must be supported by facts connecting the owner or person in charge to the offending conduct. A party should not necessarily be penalised in costs for advancing a contrary legal position.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the respondent’s points in limine were dismissed.
    Held, that the decision forfeiting the listed vehicles to the State was set aside.
    Held, that ZIMRA must unconditionally release the vehicles to the applicant within 48 hours of service of the order.
    Held, that each party should bear its own costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that, where the applicant’s role was transport and the falsified declaration was made by the clearing agent without proof of connivance by the applicant or its drivers, the drastic step of forfeiture of the applicant’s trucks and tankers was not justified.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that opposing arguments on the law may enhance jurisprudence and do not automatically justify punitive or adverse costs.
cases_referred_to: Netone Cellular (Pvt) Ltd v Reward Kangai HH 441-19
legislation_considered: Customs and Excise Act [Chapter 23:02], sections 26, 174(1)(d), 193(12), 196(1) and 196(2); State Liabilities Act [Chapter 8:15].

document_name: Redan Gas (Private) Limited v Mashora (702 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 702 (12 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Redan Gas (Private) Limited v Mashora
subject_area: Civil procedure; condonation; rescission of default judgment; execution of judgment debt.
catchwords_flynote: Condonation for late rescission; default judgment founded on acknowledgment of debt; delay after execution; whether legal practitioner default excuses litigant; prospects of success.
headnote: The applicant sought condonation for late filing of an application for rescission of a default judgment and also sought rescission. The court considered the length and explanation of delay, prospects of success, wilful default and the administration of justice. The applicant had not contested the first attachment and sale in execution and only reacted when a second attachment followed. The court held that the explanation was unsatisfactory and that the proposed defence did not undermine the acknowledgment of debt or judgment debt. Both applications were dismissed.
issues_for_determination: Whether the delay in seeking rescission should be condoned; whether the applicant gave a reasonable explanation for the delay; whether there were prospects of success in rescission; whether default was wilful.
legal_principles: Condonation is discretionary and requires consideration of the degree of delay, explanation, prospects of success and the interests of justice. Rescission requires a sufficient explanation for default and a bona fide defence carrying prospects of success.
held_decision_points: The delay and explanation were unsatisfactory. The applicant had not challenged the acknowledgment of debt, the default judgment, or the initial execution process. No adequate prospects of success were shown. The application for condonation and the application for rescission were dismissed.
ratio_decidendi: A party that sits by during execution and seeks rescission only after further execution, without showing a reasonable explanation and a defence to the judgment itself, does not satisfy the cumulative requirements for condonation and rescission.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court observed that the applicant had not placed material evidence before it on how much was realised from the first sale or what remained due.
cases_referred_to: Bwititi v Stanley Farms (Pvt) Ltd and 20 Ors SC 112/21; Forestry Commission v Moyo 1997 (1) ZLR 254 (S); Simon Musanhu v Mari Nyoni & Anor SC 134/04; Elias Mabeka v Hilda Mabeka SC 19/03; Takunda Lawrence Madamombe v The State SC 117/21; Kereke v Maramwidze & Anor SC 86/21; Chikurumhe v Zimbabwe Financial Holdings SC 10/18.

document_name: UZ-UCSF Collaborative Research Programme v Husaihwevhu and 2 Others (703 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 703 (12 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: UZ-UCSF Collaborative Research Programme v Husaihwevhu and 2 Others
subject_area: Declaratur; currency conversion; judgment debt; labour arbitral award; writ of execution; RTGS conversion; SI 33 of 2019; Finance Act No. 2 of 2019; jurisdiction; points in limine.
catchwords_flynote: Declaratory order – judgment debt in United States dollars arising from arbitral award and writ issued before 22 February 2019 – whether converted to RTGS dollars at 1:1 by operation of SI 33/2019 and Finance Act No. 2 of 2019 – points in limine on legal personality, authority, jurisdiction and review dismissed.
headnote: The applicant sought a declaration that US$788,296.21 stated in a writ of execution issued in 2014 had converted to RTGS dollars at the rate of 1:1 by operation of SI 33 of 2019 and the Finance Act No. 2 of 2019. The respondents, former employees of the applicant, argued that the debt remained payable in United States dollars. They raised several preliminary points. The court dismissed the preliminary objections and held that the debt was a pre-effective-date judgment debt not falling within the statutory exceptions, and therefore converted at the 1:1 rate.
issues_for_determination: Whether the application was properly before the court; whether the applicant had legal personality and proper authority to sue; whether the High Court had jurisdiction despite the labour origins of the dispute; whether the application was truly for a declaratur and not a review; and whether the writ amount converted to RTGS dollars at the rate of 1:1.
legal_principles: The High Court may issue a declaratur under s 14 of the High Court Act. A judgment debt expressed in United States dollars before the effective date of SI 33/2019 and the Finance Act No. 2 of 2019 converts to RTGS dollars at 1:1 unless it falls within the statutory exceptions for Nostro FCA funds, foreign loans, or foreign obligations denominated in foreign currency. The fact that the liability is based on a court order does not by itself exempt it from conversion.
held_decision_points: All preliminary objections were dismissed. The court declared that the amount of US$788,296.21 in the writ of execution issued on 8 May 2014 was converted to RTGS dollars at the rate of 1:1 by virtue of SI 33 of 2019 and the Finance Act No. 2 of 2019. The respondents were ordered to pay costs on the attorney-client scale.
ratio_decidendi: The debt arose before 22 February 2019 and was expressed in United States dollars. It was not a Nostro FCA balance, foreign loan, or foreign obligation within the statutory exceptions. It therefore fell within the class of liabilities converted to RTGS dollars at parity.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified.
cases_referred_to: Zambezi Gas Zimbabwe (Private) Limited v NR Barber (Private) Limited and Another SC 3/20; Allan Charles Patrick Ingram Lock v Ellen Olivia Lock and Another HH 501/20.
legislation_considered: High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], s 14; Labour Act [Chapter 28:01]; Presidential Powers (Temporary Measures) (Amendment of Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe Act and Issue of Real Time Gross Settlement Electronic Dollars (RTGS Dollars) Regulations, 2019 (SI 33/2019); Finance Act No. 2 of 2019; Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe Act [Chapter 22:15], s 44C.
reviewer_comments: Full citation taken from filename and judgment heading. The court records the applicant name as UZ-UCSF Collaborative Research Programme.

document_name: Kasukuwere and Another v Mutyambizi and Another (704 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 704 (12 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: STANLEY KASUKUWERE AND ANOTHER versus OLIVER MUTYAMBIZI AND ANOTHER
subject_area: Civil procedure; leave to appeal; dismissal for want of prosecution; withdrawal after set down; prospects of success; grounds of appeal.
catchwords_flynote: Leave to appeal – applications dismissed for want of prosecution – purported withdrawal after dismissal application set down – consent/leave required once matter set down – grounds of appeal long and unclear – no prospects of success – application dismissed with costs.
headnote: The applicants sought leave to appeal against a judgment dismissing two matters for want of prosecution. They argued that they had withdrawn the matters before the dismissal application was heard. The Court held that the dismissal application and the two matters were inseparable once set down, and that withdrawal after set down required the consent of all parties or leave of court. The proposed appeal had no reasonable prospects of success and the application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether an application for dismissal for want of prosecution is severable from the matters sought to be dismissed.
    Whether a party may withdraw matters after a related dismissal application has been set down without consent or leave.
    Whether the proposed grounds of appeal were clear and concise.
    Whether the appeal had reasonable prospects of success.
legal_principles: Leave to appeal requires reasonable prospects of success. Grounds of appeal must be clear and concise. Once a matter has been set down, withdrawal without the consent of all parties or leave of court is incompetent. Appeals lie against orders, not reasons.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the proposed grounds of appeal were convoluted and not clear and concise.
    Held, that there were no reasonable prospects of success on appeal.
    Held, that the notices of withdrawal filed after set down were invalid.
    Held, that the application for leave to appeal was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the matters sought to be withdrawn were already before the court through the dismissal application once set down, and withdrawal at that stage required consent or leave; the proposed appeal therefore lacked prospects.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court described the withdrawal as a pre-emptive strike intended to avoid an inevitable dismissal for want of prosecution.
cases_referred_to: Kereke v Maramwidze & Anor SC 86/21; Chikurunhe v Zimbabwe Financial Holdings SC 10-18; S v Mutasa 1988 (2) ZLR 4 (S); Madamombe v The State SC 117/21; Smith v S 2012 (1) SACR 567 (SCA); Chikura N.O. and Another v AL Sham’s Global (Pvt) Ltd SC 171/17; S v Chimwaiwashe SC 18/13; JF Schalkwyk and Others v Minister of Justice and Constitutional Development; Meda v Maxwell Matsvimbo & Others CCZ 10/2016.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 1971, rule 236(3)(b); High Court Rules, 2021, rule 59(15)(b); Supreme Court Rules relating to grounds of appeal.

document_name: TSAMWI~1.DOC
case_name: Tsamwise v Minister of Lands, Agriculture, Water, Climate and Rural Settlement and Others
subject_area: Administrative law; land allocation; review of offer letter; Zimbabwe Land Commission; domestic remedies; Administrative Justice Act; agricultural land; jurisdiction.
catchwords_flynote: Review – offer letter issued to another person over land occupied and farmed by applicant – applicant sought setting aside and an order compelling Minister to issue offer letter to him – whether High Court could order issuance of offer letter – whether Zimbabwe Land Commission domestic remedies had to be exhausted first.
headnote: The applicant sought review of the first respondent’s decision to issue an offer letter to the second respondent over Plot 33 Triangle Ranch 3 (S13), which the applicant occupied and farmed. He sought an order setting aside the offer letter and compelling the first respondent to issue an offer letter to him instead. The court held that the Administrative Justice Act did not empower it to order the Minister to issue an offer letter in the manner sought. It further held that the dispute fell within the Zimbabwe Land Commission’s constitutional and statutory mandate and that the applicant had already lodged a complaint with that body.
issues_for_determination: Whether the court could direct the Minister to issue an offer letter to the applicant; whether the decision to issue the offer letter to the second respondent should be reviewed and set aside; whether the Zimbabwe Land Commission had primary jurisdiction; and whether the applicant had to exhaust domestic remedies first.
legal_principles: The Administrative Justice Act empowers the High Court to give directions to ensure lawful, reasonable and fair administrative action, but does not ordinarily empower it to substitute itself for the land-allocation authority by ordering issuance of an offer letter. The Zimbabwe Land Commission has constitutional and statutory authority to investigate and determine complaints and disputes regarding the administration and allocation of agricultural land. Where a matter is properly before that constitutional body, the High Court may decline to exercise jurisdiction until domestic remedies are exhausted.
held_decision_points: The court held that paragraph 2 of the draft order, requiring the first respondent to issue an offer letter to the applicant, was beyond the scope of s 3 and s 4 of the Administrative Justice Act. It further held that the applicant’s complaint was properly before the Zimbabwe Land Commission, which was best placed to investigate the facts and make recommendations. The High Court declined jurisdiction.
ratio_decidendi: Because the Constitution and Land Commission Act created a specific domestic mechanism for disputes over agricultural land allocation, and the applicant had invoked that mechanism, the court declined to intervene before the Zimbabwe Land Commission had dealt with the matter.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court observed that a High Court with broad jurisdiction should still promote legislated processes and should not readily take over functions expressly reposed in a constitutional body.
cases_referred_to: John Makarudza & Anor v Cosmas Bingu & 2 Others HH 08/15; Chawasarira Transport (Pvt) Ltd v Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe HH 86/2009; Moyo v Gwindingwi N.O. & Anor HB 168/2011.
legislation_considered: Administrative Justice Act [Chapter 10:28], ss 3 and 4; Land Commission Act [Chapter 20:29], ss 9 and 14.
reviewer_comments: Short filename TSAMWI~1.DOC preserved. Neutral citation field marked Not available because the filename does not supply a neutral citation, although the judgment heading records HH 705-22. Hearing date “16 November, 2011” is preserved as it appears in the judgment heading.

document_name: Sakadza v Charumbira (706 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 706 (12 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Phillip Sakadza v Jenifer Charumbira
subject_area: Civil procedure; rescission of judgment; divorce order enforcement; eviction; error under rule 29; costs
catchwords_flynote: Rescission - default judgment - eviction order enforcing prior divorce order - applicant sought rescission of enforcement order but did not attack extant main divorce judgment - r 29 error not established - bare denial of service insufficient - doomed application - dismissed with legal practitioner-client costs.
headnote: The respondent had obtained a default divorce order in which property was declared her sole and exclusive property. She later obtained an eviction order enforcing that judgment. The applicant sought rescission of the eviction order, alleging lack of service and that the default judgment had been snatched. The court held that the eviction order did not stand alone; it derived from the extant divorce judgment. Because the applicant did not set aside or stay the main order, enforcement could continue. He also failed to establish an error under r 29 and failed to meet the return of service with facts.
issues_for_determination: Whether rescission of an enforcement judgment could assist where the main judgment remained extant; whether the order had been erroneously granted under r 29; whether non-service had been adequately established; and what costs were appropriate.
legal_principles: A default judgment may be rescinded under the High Court Rules where the relevant rule is satisfied. Under r 29(1)(a), the error must be substantive and apparent from the record or facts placed before the court. A bare denial of service is insufficient where the return of service shows personal service. An enforcement order cannot be meaningfully rescinded while the underlying order remains extant and executable.
held_decision_points: The application was dismissed with costs on the legal practitioner and client scale.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that a party cannot succeed in rescinding a judgment that merely enforces an extant order without attacking or suspending the underlying order itself.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court observed that the application was predictably doomed and that legal-practitioner ineptitude had caused unnecessary costs; it would have considered de bonis propriis costs had they been sought.
cases_referred_to: Banda v Pitluk 1993 (2) ZLR 60 (H); Grantually (Pvt) Ltd and Another v UDC Ltd 2000 (1) ZLR 361 (S); TSANGA J judgment HH 625/22 in HC 319/22 referred to.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, rr 27, 28, 29; High Court Rules, 1971, r 449(1)(a) referred to by comparison.

document_name: Rutendo Housing Co-operative v Kumanja and 9 Others (835 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 835 (23 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Rutendo Housing Co-operative v Kumanja and 9 Others
subject_area: Civil procedure; condonation and rescission of default judgment; housing co-operative
catchwords_flynote: Condonation and rescission — combined application — failure by legal practitioners to enter appearance — draft plea and locus standi objections — default judgment against co-operative.
headnote: The applicant sought condonation for late rescission and rescission of a default judgment. The respondents raised several preliminary objections, including absence of a prayer for extension of time, no appearance-to-defend prayer, absence of draft plea, no affidavit from former lawyers, and lack of locus standi. The court considered whether a combined application was competent.
issues_for_determination: Whether condonation and rescission could be combined; whether absence of certain prayers or draft plea was fatal; whether the applicant had locus standi; whether delay and prospects justified rescission.
legal_principles: A court may consider combined applications for condonation and rescission where convenience and justice support the procedure. Rescission requires explanation for default, bona fide defence and absence of wilful default.
held_decision_points: The court accepted that combined condonation and rescission applications are not necessarily irregular and addressed the preliminary objections.
ratio_decidendi: The absence of a separate extension-of-time prayer is not fatal where a composite application seeks condonation and rescission together and the relief can be understood from the papers.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified

document_name: S v Musarurwa (707 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 707 (22 September 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Jesicah Tatenda Musarurwa v The State
subject_area: Criminal appeal; negligent driving; road traffic; assessment of evidence
catchwords_flynote: Negligent driving - Road Traffic Act s 52(2)(a) - lane change - collision with haulage truck - blind spot evidence - photographs and sketch plan - opinion evidence of attending detail - appeal against conviction dismissed.
headnote: The appellant appealed against conviction for negligent driving after her Toyota Allion collided with a haulage truck. The trial court found that she changed from the outer lane to the inner lane without checking, causing the truck to hit the rear right side of her car. On appeal she argued that no witness saw her change lanes and that the truck driver accelerated. The High Court held that the trial court properly relied on the positions of the vehicles, damage patterns, photographs, the sketch plan and the evidence of the truck driver. The appeal against sentence was abandoned.
issues_for_determination: Whether the trial court erred in finding that the appellant changed lanes negligently; whether the truck driver’s evidence about acceleration affected causation; and whether the attending detail’s observations corroborated the driver’s testimony.
legal_principles: An appellate court will assess whether the trial court’s factual finding is supported by the evidence. In negligent-driving cases, the court may rely on vehicle positions, damage patterns, sketch plans, photographs, and credible oral evidence. Witness opinions are not binding, but their observations may corroborate other evidence.
held_decision_points: The appeal against conviction was dismissed in its entirety; the appeal against sentence had been abandoned.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that direct eyewitness evidence of the lane change was not indispensable where the physical evidence and credible testimony supported the finding that the appellant changed lanes negligently and caused the collision.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court clarified that the attending detail’s admission that her opinion could be wrong did not undermine the certainty of her factual observations.
cases_referred_to: S v Lawrence & Anor 1989 (1) ZLR 29(S) not in this judgment; no specific case authority identified in the extracted judgment.
legislation_considered: Road Traffic Act [Chapter 13:11], s 52(2)(a).

document_name: S v Mundanda (708 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 708 (22 September 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Greatjoy Mundanda v The State
subject_area: Criminal law; electricity infrastructure; statutory interpretation; mandatory minimum sentence; criminal appeal
catchwords_flynote: Electricity Act - s 60A(3)(b) - cutting, damaging, destroying or interfering with electricity apparatus - no need to prove interruption of electricity supply under paragraph (b) - disused mill irrelevant - mandatory minimum sentence - special circumstances - appeal against conviction and sentence dismissed.
headnote: The appellant pleaded guilty to contravening s 60A(3)(b) of the Electricity Act after cutting electricity apparatus. He was sentenced to the mandatory minimum ten years’ imprisonment after the magistrates court found no special circumstances. On appeal the respondent initially declined to support conviction on the basis that there was no live electricity supply, but the High Court found that this argument relied on the wrong statutory paragraph. Section 60A(3)(b) protects infrastructure itself and does not require proof that electricity supply was interrupted.
issues_for_determination: Whether s 60A(3)(b) requires proof that electricity supply was interrupted; whether the disused state of the plant, the cable length or value was relevant; whether the guilty plea and conviction were proper; and whether special circumstances justified a sentence below the statutory minimum.
legal_principles: Section 60A(3)(b) criminalises cutting, damaging, destroying or interfering with electricity apparatus. Unlike s 60A(3)(a), it does not require proof that electricity supply was interrupted or cut off. The purpose is to protect infrastructure from vandalism or cannibalisation even where no electricity is passing through it. Once the offence is proved, the mandatory minimum applies unless special circumstances peculiar to the case are shown.
held_decision_points: The appeal against both conviction and sentence was dismissed. The court held that the conduct fell within s 60A(3)(b), that no special circumstances existed, and that the mandatory ten-year sentence stood.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that s 60A(3)(b) is concerned with damage to electricity apparatus itself and not with whether electricity supply was interrupted; disused infrastructure remains protected by the provision.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court rejected the respondent’s concession because it was based on a misreading of the statutory provision.
cases_referred_to: Not available.
legislation_considered: Electricity Act [Chapter 13:19], s 60A(3)(b), s 60A(4); High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], s 35; Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 113 referred to in argument.

document_name: MARKHA~1.DOC
case_name: Markham v Minister of Justice, Legal and Parliamentary Affairs and Another
subject_area: Constitutional law; public administration; constitutional obligations; mandamus; anti-corruption and asset disclosure legislation.
catchwords_flynote: Constitution – s 198 – principles of public administration and leadership – duty to enact measures requiring public officers to disclose assets – s 324 – constitutional obligations to be performed diligently and without delay – mandamus.
headnote: The applicant sought a declaratory order that the first respondent’s failure to initiate legislation envisaged by s 198(a) of the Constitution breached the obligation in s 324 that constitutional obligations be performed diligently and without delay. The respondents did not dispute that s 198 creates a constitutional obligation but argued that the legislative process involved other actors and that Parliament, Cabinet and the President had not been joined. The court held that the first respondent, who administered the Constitution, had failed to act within a reasonable time. A mandamus was justified, but the 45-day period sought was too short. The first respondent was ordered to gazette the Bill within three months.
issues_for_determination: Whether s 198(a) created a constitutional obligation; whether the respondents had failed to perform it diligently and without delay; whether non-joinder of other actors defeated the claim; whether a mandamus should issue.
legal_principles: Constitutional obligations must be performed diligently and without delay. Courts may grant a mandamus compelling public officials to perform constitutional duties. Non-joinder does not defeat a matter where the court can determine issues affecting parties before it.
held_decision_points: The first respondent’s failure to formulate the Bill within a reasonable time breached s 324. A mandamus was granted directing gazetting of the Bill within three months.
ratio_decidendi: The first respondent accepted a role in the legislative process and had not shown any lawful justification for the prolonged delay. The constitutional duty remained unfulfilled long after the Constitution came into force.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court warned against unrealistic timeframes in mandatory orders and emphasized that courts should make orders that can realistically be performed.
cases_referred_to: Chironga and Another v Minister of Justice and Others CCZ 14/20; other authorities cited in the full text.
legislation_considered: Constitution of Zimbabwe, 2013, ss 116, 117, 198 and 324; High Court Rules, 2021, r 32(11).
constitutional_provisions_considered: Sections 198(a) and 324 of the Constitution of Zimbabwe; legislative authority provisions including ss 116 and 117.
reviewer_comments: Draft. Short filename preserved.
