document_name: CAROLA~1(1).DOC
case_name: CAROL AND TATENDA MINING SYNDICATE versus THE MINISTER OF MINES AND MINING DEVELOPMENT N.O. and 3 OTHERS
subject_area: Mining law; urgent chamber application; interim interdict; farmer-miner dispute; service of court orders; contempt proceedings; urgency.
catchwords_flynote: Urgent application – mining dispute – interim relief pending contempt proceedings – service of court order – whether order relied upon had been properly served by Sheriff – prima facie right – urgency tied to alleged contempt – application struck off urgent roll.
headnote: The applicant sought suspension of mining determinations and leave to resume mining at Victory 100 A pending contempt proceedings. The dispute arose from a miner-farmer conflict and a determination that the mining location had been pegged on a farm under 100 hectares without the owner's written consent. The Court held that the urgency and prima facie right relied on were tied to alleged contempt of an order that had not been properly served in terms of the rules. Without proper service there could be no prima facie contempt basis. The matter was struck off the roll of urgent matters and the applicant was ordered to pay the third and fourth respondents' costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the application was urgent.
    Whether the applicant established a prima facie right pending contempt proceedings.
    Whether a court order relied upon for contempt had been properly served.
    Whether service outside the modes prescribed by the High Court Rules could found a contempt-based urgent application.
legal_principles: A party alleging contempt must show, at least prima facie, proper service or knowledge of the court order in issue. Court orders must be served in the manner prescribed by the rules, normally by the Sheriff unless the court authorises another mode. An urgent application whose cause of action depends on contempt cannot be urgent if the alleged contemnor was not properly served with the order relied upon.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant had not established proper service of the order allegedly breached.
    Held, that there could be no prima facie case of contempt where service of the order was denied and proper service was not shown.
    Held, that the urgency premised on alleged violation of the order fell away.
    Held, that the application was improperly before the court as urgent.
    Held, that the application was struck off the roll of urgent matters with costs against the applicant in favour of the third and fourth respondents.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that an urgent application for interim protection premised on contempt of court cannot be founded on an order that has not been served in accordance with the rules; absent proper service, no prima facie contempt right exists and urgency tied to the alleged contempt fails.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court remarked that substantial compliance is alien to service of process where the rules prescribe accepted modes of service.
cases_referred_to: None identified in the extracted text beyond references to prior proceedings between the parties.
legislation_considered: Mines and Minerals Act [Chapter 21:05], sections 31(1)(g), 50(1)(g)(i) and 354(5); High Court Rules, 2021, rules on service of process and urgent chamber applications.
subject_area_terms: Mining law; urgent chamber application; interim interdict; farmer-miner dispute; service of court orders; contempt proceedings; urgency.
cases_referred_to_terms: None identified in the extracted text beyond references to prior proceedings between the parties.
legislation_considered_terms: Mines and Minerals Act [Chapter 21:05], sections 31(1)(g), 50(1)(g)(i) and 354(5)
High Court Rules, 2021, rules on service of process and urgent chamber applications.

document_name: Zemqos Incorporated (Pvt) Ltd v City Parking (Pvt) Ltd (725 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 725 (19 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Zemqos Incorporated (Pvt) Ltd v City Parking (Pvt) Ltd
subject_area: Contract law; verbal contract; parking management system; specific performance; damages; prescription; conditions precedent; automated parking bays; software licence fees.
catchwords_flynote: Verbal contract – parking management system for Harare CBD parking bays – dispute whether contract covered 555 bays only or full 5000 bays – prescription special plea – conditional obligation dependent on funding – claim for specific performance, licence fees and damages – claim dismissed.
headnote: The plaintiff claimed that it had a verbal contract with the defendant to install a fully automated parking management system over 5,000 parking bays, implemented in phases, and that the defendant had breached the contract. The defendant admitted a verbal contract only for 555 bays, contended that those services had been paid for, and asserted that the software arrangement was terminated due to malfunctions. The court held that the claim had not prescribed, but that the plaintiff failed to prove the alleged enforceable long-term obligations or fulfillment of the condition precedent relating to funding for the remaining bays. The claim was dismissed.
issues_for_determination: Whether the claim had prescribed; what the terms of the verbal contract were; whether the contract covered 5,000 bays or only 555 bays; whether full automation of 4,445 bays was subject to a condition precedent; whether the defendant breached the contract; whether specific performance, licence fees, replacement costs or damages were available; and whether the alternative claim for cancellation was competent.
legal_principles: The party alleging an oral contract bears the onus of proving its existence and terms on a balance of probabilities. Courts assess witness credibility and surrounding documents to determine oral contractual terms. Courts do not create or rewrite contractual terms. A condition precedent suspends operation of the relevant obligation until fulfilled, and the party seeking enforcement must plead and prove fulfilment. Prescription runs from the date the cause of action arose, but continued engagements may affect the pleaded timeline.
held_decision_points: The court held that prescription was not established. It accepted that the parties had contracted for a parking management system involving 555 fully automated bays and a broader phased concept, but found that full automation of the 4,445 bays was conditional on funding and that this condition was not proved to have been fulfilled. No 15-year software licence arrangement was proved. The plaintiff’s main and alternative claims were dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The plaintiff’s claims depended on treating phase two as an enforceable perfected obligation, but the evidence showed that full automation of the remaining bays depended on availability of funds and further implementation steps. Since the condition precedent was not fulfilled, no enforceable right to specific performance or damages arose in respect of the 4,445 bays.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified beyond the court’s comment on the risks of sizeable verbal contracts for public parking systems.
cases_referred_to: Nicoz Diamond Insurance Ltd v Clovegate Elevator Co (Pvt) Ltd 2018 (1) ZLR 50 (H); Hees v Nel 1994 PH F11; Van Hoogstraten v Nelomwe SC 4-20; Kundai Magodora & Ors v Care International Zimbabwe SC 24/14; Delta Beverages (Pvt) Ltd v Pyvate Investments (Pvt) Ltd and Another HH 135-18; Brooker v Mudhanda and Another / Pearce v Mudhanda and Another 2011 (1) ZLR 33 (S); Branch v Vic Diamond and Son 1957 R&N 11; Farmers’ Co-op Society (Reg) v Berry 1912 AD 343; Haynes v Kingwilliamstown Municipality 1951 (2) SA 371 (A).
legislation_considered: Prescription Act [Chapter 8:11]; High Court Rules, 2021, r 42.
reviewer_comments: Full citation taken from filename and judgment heading. Judgment heading formats judgment number as HH725/22 and case number as HC10/21; preserved in fields with spacing for readability.

document_name: Jume v Yule and Another (726 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 763 (18 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: CLOUDIO JUME versus WAKIE YULE AND ANOTHER
subject_area: Declaratur; property sale; default judgment; fraudulent service; prescription; cancellation of agreement; eviction; preliminary points.
catchwords_flynote: Declaratory application – default judgment obtained without valid service – later set aside – whether judgment may revive – fraud/perjury – agreement of sale – prescription and cancellation – enforcement no longer legally possible – eviction ordered – punitive costs.
headnote: The applicant sought declarations relating to earlier proceedings over Stand 9462 Budiriro 5B. He alleged that a default judgment compelling transfer had been obtained without valid service and by fraudulent affidavit. The Court dismissed preliminary objections, declared that the default judgment was erroneously granted and could not revive, found the first respondent’s claims under the sale agreement prescribed and unenforceable, and ordered the first respondent to vacate the property.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the preliminary objections based on argumentative affidavit, declaratory/consequential relief, misjoinder, non-disclosure, res judicata, lis pendens and prescription had merit.
    Whether the default judgment in HC 3647/17 was obtained without valid service.
    Whether the first respondent’s contractual claims had prescribed or been forfeited by cancellation.
    Whether eviction should follow.
legal_principles: Section 14 of the High Court Act permits declaratory relief and consequential relief where appropriate. Fraudulent or invalid service can render a default judgment erroneously granted. Extinctive prescription bars enforcement of stale claims. An owner with title may vindicate property where an occupier’s contractual rights are no longer enforceable.
held_decision_points:
Held, that all preliminary points were dismissed.
    Held, that the first respondent did not validly serve the applicant in HC 3647/17 and the default judgment was granted in error.
    Held, that the judgment set aside in HC 3872/19 shall not and cannot revive.
    Held, that the first respondent’s claims arising from the sale agreement had prescribed and were legally incapable of enforcement.
    Held, that the first respondent must vacate the property within ten days, failing which the Sheriff may evict him.
    Held, that the first respondent shall pay costs on the legal practitioner and client scale.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that a judgment procured through invalid service and fraudulent conduct could not found revived enforceable rights, and the first respondent’s contractual rights under the sale agreement had in any event prescribed or been forfeited, leaving the applicant entitled to vindicatory relief.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court stressed that the applicant had proved ownership and a real and substantial interest in vindicating the property.
cases_referred_to: Authorities on declaratory relief, res judicata, lis pendens, prescription and rei vindicatio as cited in the judgment.
legislation_considered: High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], section 14; High Court Rules, 2021, rule 32(11); Prescription Act [Chapter 8:11].
subject_area_terms: Declaratur; property sale; default judgment; fraudulent service; prescription; cancellation of agreement; eviction; preliminary points.
cases_referred_to_terms: Authorities on declaratory relief, res judicata, lis pendens, prescription and rei vindicatio as cited in the judgment.

document_name: Gondo v Nhowe (727 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 727 (19 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: MONICA GONDO versus RAYMOND TAFADZWA NHOWE
judges: WAMAMBO J and MUCHAWA J; judgment by MUCHAWA J, WAMAMBO J concurring
subject_area: Civil appeal; eviction; rei vindicatio; magistrates court jurisdiction; defective relief; preliminary points; costs.
catchwords_flynote: Civil appeal – eviction claim dismissed by magistrates court for want of jurisdiction – appellant sought substitution order granting eviction and holding over damages – respondent objected that relief was incompetent – appellate court would usurp first-instance function – notice of appeal fatally defective – appeal struck off roll with costs.
headnote: The appellant appealed against a magistrates court ruling dismissing her eviction and holding-over damages claim for want of jurisdiction. She sought an order substituting the lower court’s decision with an eviction and damages order. The respondent raised points in limine, arguing that if the magistrate had not determined the merits, the proper relief was remittal rather than substitution. The High Court agreed that the relief sought was incompetent and irreconcilable with the grounds of appeal. The appeal was struck off the roll with ordinary costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the appeal relief seeking eviction and damages was competent.
    Whether, if the magistrates court found no jurisdiction, the proper appeal remedy should have been remittal.
    Whether the grounds of appeal and relief sought were contradictory.
    Whether the appeal should be struck off or decided on the merits.
    Whether higher-scale costs were justified.
legal_principles: An appeal court should not grant substantive relief as a court of first instance where the lower court did not determine the merits. Grounds of appeal and relief sought must be coherent and competent. A fatally defective notice of appeal may result in an appeal being struck off the roll. Higher-scale costs require exceptional circumstances such as dishonesty, mala fides or oppressive conduct, not merely an ill-conceived appeal.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the relief sought was incompetent.
    Held, that the appeal court could not substitute the lower court’s order with eviction and damages where the lower court did not decide the merits.
    Held, that the point in limine was upheld.
    Held, that the appeal was struck off the roll with costs.
    Held, that attorney-client costs were not justified.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that an appellant who challenges a lower court’s refusal of jurisdiction must seek a competent appellate remedy such as remittal for determination, not a substitution that requires the appellate court to make first-instance findings on the merits.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted that an ill-conceived appeal alone does not justify punitive costs absent evidence of objectionable conduct.
cases_referred_to:
Tamanikwa & Another v Zimbabwe Manpower Development Fund & Another SC 73/17
    Faust Products (Pvt) Ltd v Continental Fashions (Pvt) Ltd 1987 (1) ZLR 45 (HC)
    Davidson v Standard Finance Ltd 1985 (1) ZLR 173 (HC)
    Chioza v Sawyer 1997 (2) ZLR 178 (SC)
legislation_considered: Common-law rei vindicatio; principles of civil appellate procedure and magistrates court jurisdiction.

document_name: BAISHU~1.DOC
case_name: BAISHUN MINING COMPANY PRIVATE LIMITED versus CHINDA RESOURCES PRIVATE LIMITED and THE PROVINCIAL MINING DIRECTOR FOR MASHONALAND CENTRAL PROVINCE and THE MINISTER OF MINES AND MINING DEVELOPMENT
subject_area: Civil procedure; rescission of default judgment; service on company; registered office; wilful default; mining contractual dispute.
catchwords_flynote: Civil procedure – rescission of default judgment – rule 29(1)(a) High Court Rules, 2021 – service on company at address appearing in CR5 form – no domicilium in contract – return of service showing acceptance by self-identified caretaker – service valid – applicant shifting reasons for default – default judgment neither erroneously sought nor erroneously granted – wilful default established – rescission dismissed.
headnote: The applicant sought rescission of a default judgment granted in HC 2439/22. It argued that it was not served and that the application had been served at an invalid address on a person unknown to it. The Court held that the application had been served at the applicant company's principal place of business or registered office as appearing on its CR5 form and in compliance with the rules. The Court rejected the applicant's shifting explanations, found that the alleged recipient of service was likely known to the applicant, and concluded that the default judgment was neither erroneously sought nor erroneously granted. The applicant had been in wilful default and had not shown good and sufficient cause. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the default judgment had been erroneously sought or erroneously granted in the absence of the applicant.
    Whether service at 15 Drew Road, Chisipite, Harare was valid service on the company.
    Whether the applicant's default was wilful.
    Whether the applicant established good and sufficient cause for rescission.
legal_principles: Rule 29(1)(a) is an exception to the principle of functus officio and must be applied restrictively. To succeed the applicant must show that the judgment was erroneously sought or granted, was granted in its absence, and affected its rights. Service on a company at its registered office or principal place of business is valid. A return of service is prima facie proof of the facts recorded in it and a bare denial does not rebut it. An application for rescission requires a credible explanation for default and, where relevant, good and sufficient cause.
held_decision_points:
Held, that service was proper and valid because it was effected at the applicant's registered office or principal place of business.
    Held, that the applicant did not rebut the return of service and did not provide a satisfactory explanation about the person who accepted service.
    Held, that the applicant's explanations were contradictory and dishonest.
    Held, that the default judgment was not erroneously sought or granted.
    Held, that the applicant was in wilful default and had not shown good and sufficient cause.
    Held, that the application for rescission had to be dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that where process is served on a company at its registered office or principal place of business and the applicant fails to rebut the return of service with credible facts, a default judgment entered after non-appearance is not erroneously granted and rescission under rule 29(1)(a) is unavailable.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that accepting bare assertions that court papers were not brought to directors' attention would undermine finality and create an unintended lifeline for sluggard litigants.
cases_referred_to:
Munyimi v Tauro SC 41/13
    Phiri v Gweru Investments Ltd and Others HH 195/16
    Gundani v Kanyemba 1988 (1) ZLR 226 (S)
    Anabas Services (Pvt) Limited v Minister of Health and Others HB 88/03
legislation_considered:
High Court Rules, 2021, rule 29(1)(a), rule 15(13)(e)
    Company CR5 requirements relating to registered office/principal place of business

document_name: UNKIMI~1.DOC
case_name: Unki Mines (Private) Limited v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority and Another
subject_area: Tax law; mining royalties; garnishee notices; urgent chamber application; interim interdict; incompetent relief; ZIMRA collection measures; mining royalty computation; currency of payment.
catchwords_flynote: Urgent chamber application – ZIMRA issuing garnishee notices against taxpayer’s bank accounts for mining royalties – dispute over computation and currency of royalties – whether urgent – whether interim relief sought against ZIMRA was competent where garnishee notices had already been issued and no relief was sought against bank – application struck off.
headnote: The applicant sought an interim interdict restraining ZIMRA from instituting collection measures under garnishee notices issued to the applicant’s bank for large alleged mining royalty arrears. ZIMRA raised several points in limine. The court held that the matter was urgent because the need to act arose when the garnishee notices were issued. However, the court upheld the objection that the interim relief sought was incompetent: the garnishee notices had already been issued, and the applicant did not seek an order suspending their implementation against the bank. A decision on the merits would therefore serve no practical purpose.
issues_for_determination: Whether the matter was urgent; whether the relief sought against ZIMRA was competent; whether the applicant could interdict collection measures already taken; whether the applicant should have sought interim relief against the bank to suspend implementation of the garnishee notices; and whether the court should proceed to the merits.
legal_principles: A matter may be urgent where the need to act arises only upon issuance of garnishee notices causing immediate prejudice. An interdict is generally directed at future or continuing conduct and is not the proper remedy for an act already completed. Where a garnishee notice has already been served on a bank, practical interim relief must suspend implementation or payment by the bank; otherwise an order against the issuer alone may be ineffectual.
held_decision_points: The urgency objection was dismissed. The objection that the relief sought was incompetent was upheld. The applicant had not sought a sufficiently specific order suspending implementation of the garnishee notices against the bank. The application was struck off and the applicant was ordered to pay the respondent’s costs.
ratio_decidendi: Although the applicant acted promptly after the garnishee notices were issued, the interim relief was fatally defective because the notices had already been issued and the bank remained legally obliged to comply absent an order suspending implementation. The court could not grant useful interim relief on the terms sought.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified beyond the court’s emphasis on carefully framing urgent interim relief where garnishee notices have already been issued.
cases_referred_to: Bulawayo City Council v Button Armature HC 2070/14; Tianze Tobacco v Vusumuzi Muntuyedwa HH 626/15; Herbstein and Van Winsen, The Civil Practice of the High Courts and the Supreme Court of Appeal of South Africa, 5th ed.
legislation_considered: Mines and Minerals Act [Chapter 21:05]; Finance Act [Chapter 23:04]; Income Tax Act [Chapter 23:06], s 58.
reviewer_comments: Short filename UNKIMI~1.DOC preserved. Neutral citation field marked Not available because the uploaded filename does not supply a neutral citation, although the judgment heading records HH 729-22.

document_name: TAKAWI~1(1).DOC
case_name: Takawira v Minister of Lands, Agriculture, Fisheries, Water & Rural Development N.O. and Another
subject_area: Land law; compulsory acquisition; agricultural land; declaratur; constitutional acquisition provisions; errors in gazette notice
catchwords_flynote: Compulsory acquisition — agricultural land — notice identifying land under old owner and old deed number — subsequent constitutional validation — s 16B of Constitution Amendment No 17 — errors in notice do not invalidate vesting of title in the State.
headnote: The applicant sought a declaratur that the compulsory acquisition of land he had purchased from the previous owner was invalid because the Government Gazette notice identified the former owner and former deed rather than the applicant’s deed. The court held that the property itself had been identified in the acquisition notice and fell within s 16B of the former Constitution as inserted by Constitution Amendment No 17. Under s 16B(5), errors or inconsistencies in notices and title deeds do not invalidate acquisition or vesting in the State. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the compulsory acquisition was invalid because the Gazette notice named the former owner and old title deed.
    Whether identification of the land, rather than the owner, is the key requirement.
    Whether s 16B(5) validated errors or inconsistencies in acquisition notices.
    Whether the State could issue a 99-year lease over the property.
legal_principles:
Under s 16B of the former Constitution, agricultural land identified in the relevant Gazette notices vested in the State.
    The critical requirement is identification of the land.
    Errors, inconsistencies or non-endorsement in title deeds do not invalidate acquisition where the land falls within s 16B and Schedule 7.
    The constitutional acquisition regime was intended to validate acquisitions despite defects that might otherwise have nullified them.
held_decision_points:
The land was correctly identified in the acquisition notice despite the old owner and deed being cited.
    The property fell under s 16B(2)(a) and Schedule 7.
    Section 16B(5) cured the alleged inconsistencies and errors.
    The property vested in the State; the applicant was not entitled to the declaratory relief sought.
    The application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: Because the land had been identified and itemised under the constitutional land acquisition framework, any error in owner name or title deed reference could not invalidate the acquisition or prevent title vesting in the State.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified.

document_name: Chisango v Chisango and Another (732 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 732 (19 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: GIFT CHISANGO versus PRIVILEDGE CHISANGO and MINISTER OF LANDS, AGRICULTURE, WATER, CLIMATE AND RURAL RESETTLEMENT
citation: Chisango v Chisango and Another (732 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 732 (19 October 2022)
subject_area: Urgent chamber application; interdict; occupation and use of agricultural land; offer letter; actio rei vindicatio-like relief; punitive costs.
catchwords_flynote: Interdict – agricultural plot – applicant holding A1 land offer letter – respondent with no offer letter or basis of occupation – prior failed application by respondent concerning same property – urgency established – interim relief granted and punitive costs justified.
headnote: The applicant sought urgent interim protection against the first respondent’s entry, ploughing and planting on Plot No. 9 Carrisbrooke Farm. The applicant held an offer letter issued by the Ministry, while the first respondent failed to establish any right to occupy the plot and had earlier unsuccessfully litigated over the same property in HC 3750/21. The Court held that the matter was urgent, the applicant had established a prima facie right, irreparable harm and balance of convenience, and that attorney-client costs were justified by the first respondent’s conduct.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the matter was urgent.
    Whether the applicant had a prima facie right arising from the offer letter.
    Whether the first respondent had any legal basis to occupy or use the plot.
    Whether interim interdictory relief and eviction were justified.
    Whether punitive costs were warranted.
legal_principles: Urgency arises when the need to act arises and the matter cannot wait. An interim interdict requires a prima facie right, apprehension of irreparable harm, balance of convenience favouring the applicant, and no alternative remedy. A party with no offer letter or legal basis cannot defeat an offer-letter holder’s prima facie right to occupation and use.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the matter was urgent and was treated as such.
    Held, that the applicant’s offer letter established a prima facie right to occupy and use the plot.
    Held, that the first respondent had failed to establish any lawful basis for occupation.
    Held, that the first respondent’s conduct threatened irreparable harm and the balance of convenience favoured the applicant.
    Held, that attorney-client costs were justified because the first respondent persisted despite knowing she had no offer letter and had lost earlier litigation.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that an offer-letter holder who acts promptly may obtain urgent interdictory relief against a person with no legal right who invades and attempts to use the land, especially where prior litigation has already rejected that person’s claim.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court described the application as akin to an actio rei vindicatio and censured the first respondent’s persistence in defending a claim unsupported by any legal entitlement.
cases_referred_to: Kuvarega v Registrar-General and Anor 1998 (1) ZLR 188 (H)
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021; land offer-letter regime.
subject_area_terms: Urgent chamber application; interdict; occupation and use of agricultural land; offer letter; actio rei vindicatio-like relief; punitive costs.
cases_referred_to_terms: Kuvarega v Registrar-General and Anor 1998 (1) ZLR 188 (H)
legislation_considered_terms: High Court Rules, 2021; land offer-letter regime.

document_name: Watermount Estates Private Limited v Gumbo and 2 Others (733 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 733 (22 September 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Watermount Estates Private Limited v Gumbo and 2 Others
subject_area: Civil procedure; postponement; withdrawal of summons; exception; special plea; no cause of action; lis pendens; wasted costs.
catchwords_flynote: Postponement – plaintiff seeking postponement on day of hearing because preferred advocate unavailable – court refusing postponement – plaintiff thereafter withdrawing summons with tender of costs – matter dismissed on account of withdrawal – wasted costs.
headnote: The plaintiff had issued summons claiming USD 5 million or its equivalent. The defendants filed exceptions and special pleas, including no cause of action and lis pendens. At the hearing, the plaintiff sought a postponement because counsel of choice was unavailable. The court refused the postponement, finding that the request had been made at the eleventh hour and that postponement is not for the mere asking. After an adjournment, the plaintiff withdrew the summons with a tender of costs. The court dismissed the matter on account of the withdrawal and ordered wasted costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether postponement should be granted; whether unavailability of counsel of choice was a sufficient reason; whether the matter should proceed; and what order should follow the plaintiff’s withdrawal of summons.
legal_principles: A postponement is not granted for the mere asking. A party seeking postponement must give a sufficient and reasonable explanation and must be ready to proceed if the application is refused. Late briefing or unavailability of preferred counsel, without adequate explanation, may not justify postponement.
held_decision_points: The application for postponement was dismissed. The plaintiff then withdrew the summons with a tender of costs. The court ordered that the matter stood withdrawn and that HC 694/22 was dismissed on account of the withdrawal. The plaintiff was ordered to pay wasted costs.
ratio_decidendi: The plaintiff had not shown a reasonable basis for postponement. Once the postponement failed and the plaintiff withdrew the summons, the appropriate order was dismissal on account of withdrawal and wasted costs.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified.
cases_referred_to: None expressly relied on in the final reasoning beyond general principles on postponement.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, r 42(2) on exception/special plea implicated by the procedural background.
reviewer_comments: Full citation taken from filename and judgment heading. The judgment provides reasons after an ex tempore order.
export_history: Compiled into High_Court_Harare_Batch_27_2022_Law_Report_Drafts.txt.

document_name: Kewada N.O v United Harvest (Pvt) Ltd and Another (734 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 734 (13 September 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THAKOR RANCHOD KEWADA N.O. versus UNITED HARVEST (PVT) LTD AND ANOTHER
subject_area: Contract; sale of immovable property; deceased estate; cancellation; variation versus novation; Contractual Penalties Act; eviction; refund/counter-application.
catchwords_flynote: Sale of immovable property by deceased – purchaser defaulted – payment terms varied but original contract otherwise intact – no novation – valid cancellation after notice – eviction ordered – purchaser entitled to refund as claim against estate – counter-application costs.
headnote: The executor of a deceased estate sought cancellation of an agreement of sale and eviction of the purchaser from Lot 18 Gardiner East, Goromonzi. The purchaser admitted non-payment but argued that later payment proposals novated the agreement. The Court held that the payment arrangements varied, but did not novate, the original agreement; the breach clause remained operative and cancellation was valid. The purchaser was ordered to vacate. The estate was ordered to refund US$200 955.51 and the Master to accept the claim against the estate.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the subsequent payment arrangement novated or merely varied the original agreement.
    Whether the applicant validly cancelled the sale agreement.
    Whether the respondent was entitled to refund and/or improvements.
    Whether the property should be declared executable.
    What costs order should follow.
legal_principles: Novation requires a clear intention to extinguish an existing obligation and replace it with a new one. Mere modification of payment terms is variation, not novation. Under the Contractual Penalties Act, cancellation of an instalment sale must comply with notice requirements. A deceased estate acts through its executor, and claims must be dealt with in the estate process.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the agreement of sale was cancelled.
    Held, that the first respondent and those claiming through it must vacate within seven days, failing which the Sheriff may evict.
    Held, that the estate must refund US$200 955.51 and the Master must accept that sum as a claim against Estate Late John Vigo Naested DR 5089/21.
    Held, that the first respondent pays costs of the main and counter-application.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the later payment proposal was a variation of payment terms only, not a novation, so the original breach and cancellation provisions remained enforceable and cancellation was valid after adequate notice.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted that the counter-application was unnecessary because the applicant had already accepted and tendered repayment of the purchase price paid.
cases_referred_to: Klepman v Law Union and Rock Insurance 1957 (1) SA 506; Swadif (Pvt) Ltd v Dyke N.O. 1978 (1) SA 928 (A); Tauber v Von Abo 1984 (4) SA 482 (ECD); authorities on novation and variation as cited.
legislation_considered: Contractual Penalties Act [Chapter 8:04], sections 8 and 9(2); High Court Rules, 2021, rule 58(8); law of deceased estates.
subject_area_terms: Contract; sale of immovable property; deceased estate; cancellation; variation versus novation
cases_referred_to_terms: Klepman v Law Union and Rock Insurance 1957 (1) SA 506
Swadif (Pvt) Ltd v Dyke N.O. 1978 (1) SA 928 (A)
Tauber v Von Abo 1984 (4) SA 482 (ECD); authorities on novation and variation as cited.
legislation_considered_terms: High Court Rules, 2021, rule 58(8); law of deceased estates.

document_name: Petromocexor (Private) Limted v Energy Park (Pvt) Ltd and 4 Others (735 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 735 (21 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Petromocexor (Private) Limited v Energy Park (Private) Limited and Others
subject_area: Interdict; declaratur; lease; deceased estate; service station; possession and improvements.
catchwords_flynote: Twenty-five-year lease; fuel retail outlet; lessee’s improvements and equipment; executor’s conduct; occupation by third party; clear right; declaratory and interdictory relief.
headnote: The applicant held a long lease over a property used as a service station and had installed improvements and equipment. After the lessor died, the executor and others dealt with the property in a manner that allowed the first respondent and fourth respondent to operate there. The court found that the applicant had a clear right to possession and to its improvements and equipment. The first respondent’s denial of occupation was contradicted by its own documents and related proceedings. The application was granted.
issues_for_determination: Whether the applicant had a clear right to possession and to the equipment/improvements; whether the respondents were unlawfully occupying or using the property and equipment; whether declaratory and interdictory relief should be granted.
legal_principles: A final interdict requires a clear right, harm or apprehended harm, absence of adequate alternative remedy and a balance of convenience favouring relief. A declaratur requires a direct and substantial interest in an existing right. A party may not approbate and reprobate by denying occupation in one matter while asserting possession in another.
held_decision_points: The applicant proved its clear right and direct interest. The first and fourth respondents had no legal title to occupy or operate the property using the applicant’s assets. The application was granted as prayed.
ratio_decidendi: Where a subsisting lease confers possession and ownership of improvements on the lessee, an executor or third party cannot lawfully displace the lessee or use its improvements without cancelling or otherwise lawfully terminating those rights.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court emphasized the duty to conduct due diligence before contracting over property forming part of a deceased estate.
cases_referred_to: Setlogelo v Setlogelo 1914 AD 221; Mhungu v Mutindi 1986 (2) ZLR 171 (SC); Netone v Econet SC 47/18; Hlatshwayo v Mare & Deas 1912 AD 242.
legislation_considered: High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], s 14; Administration of Estates principles.
reviewer_comments: Filename contains misspelling “Limted”; exact document name preserved. Full judgment text extracted using python-docx.

document_name: S v Murira (736 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 736 (21 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: The State v Merjury Murira
subject_area: Criminal law; murder; domestic violence; self-defence; provocation; abuse
catchwords_flynote: Murder - accused killed abusive husband with axe - prolonged domestic violence, humiliation and threats - deceased had impregnated accused’s daughter and forced accused to watch sexual intercourse - self-defence - provocation - requirements of defence of person - complete defence established - accused acquitted.
headnote: The accused was charged with murdering her husband by striking him with an axe. The evidence showed sustained domestic violence and extreme humiliation on the day in question. The deceased had impregnated the accused’s daughter, beat the accused repeatedly, denied her food, threatened to kill her and forced her to watch sexual intercourse between him and the daughter. The accused struck the deceased with an axe when she believed she had an opportunity to immobilise him and escape. The court accepted that she acted in self-defence, and also considered the facts to amount to severe provocation.
issues_for_determination: Whether the accused had the requisite intention for murder; whether provocation reduced liability; whether the requirements of self-defence were satisfied; and whether a conviction for culpable homicide could arise where the court accepted self-defence.
legal_principles: Self-defence under s 253 of the Criminal Law Code requires an unlawful attack that has commenced or is imminent, action necessary to avert the attack, and reasonable means. Provocation may reduce murder to culpable homicide if the accused lacked intention or lost self-control. However, where self-defence is complete, neither murder nor culpable homicide is established.
held_decision_points: The court held that self-defence was available and complete. The State had not proved the requisite intention for murder, and culpable homicide could not be sustained because the accused was entitled to defend herself. The accused was found not guilty and acquitted.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that a woman subjected to prolonged, severe, imminent and continuing domestic violence may lawfully use force to avert further attack where escape has been prevented and the circumstances satisfy the statutory requirements of self-defence.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court observed that the facts also raised important questions about cumulative or prolonged provocation, particularly where anger and desperation build gradually under abuse. It recommended counselling and support for the accused and her daughter.
cases_referred_to: S v Banana 1994 (2) ZLR 271 (S); S v Kashiri HMT 13/18.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], ss 47, 239, 253; Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], ss 256(1), 314.
reviewer_comments: Full text extracted from the mounted DOCX.

document_name: Freda Rebecca Gold Mine v Jack (737 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 737 (21 October 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: FREDA REBECCA GOLD MINE versus CANAAN JACK
subject_area: Property law; rei vindicatio; employer-provided accommodation; labour law; jurisdiction; stated case.
catchwords_flynote: Rei vindicatio – employer seeking eviction from company house – employee reinstated by Labour Court – High Court jurisdiction not ousted – housing benefit discretionary and tied to employment – vindicatory right not dependent on final labour dispute – eviction granted – attorney-client costs.
headnote: The plaintiff sought eviction of the defendant from a company house. The defendant argued that the High Court lacked jurisdiction because the dispute arose from employment and that a Labour Court reinstatement judgment justified retention of the house. The Court held that rei vindicatio falls within the High Court’s jurisdiction and that the employer’s vindicatory right was not subject to the Labour Court dispute. The defendant had no right of retention, and eviction was granted with attorney-client costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the High Court had jurisdiction despite the employment background.
    Whether the plaintiff was entitled to evict the defendant notwithstanding Labour Court reinstatement.
    Whether the plaintiff’s rei vindicatio right was subject to determination of the employment dispute.
    Whether the Labour Court judgment provided a defence to eviction.
legal_principles: The High Court has inherent jurisdiction over common-law rei vindicatio unless expressly ousted. The Labour Court does not have exclusive jurisdiction over common-law vindicatory relief. The owner need prove ownership, possession by the defendant and existence/identity of the property. An employee’s employment-related claim does not by itself create a right to retain the employer’s property.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the High Court had jurisdiction to determine the rei vindicatio claim.
    Held, that the Labour Court judgment did not entitle the defendant to retain the property.
    Held, that the plaintiff was entitled to evict the defendant and all persons claiming through him.
    Held, that the defendant must vacate within seven days, failing which the Sheriff and police could eject him.
    Held, that the defendant should pay attorney-client costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that rei vindicatio is a property-law remedy within the High Court’s jurisdiction and an employee’s unresolved or successful labour proceedings do not defeat an owner’s right to recover property where the occupation was discretionary and incidental to employment.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court held that the defendant’s resistance to eviction lacked a legal basis and warranted censure by attorney-client costs.
cases_referred_to:
Derdale Investments Private Limited v Econet Wireless & Others HH 656-14
    Martin Sibindi and Another v Benson Chinemhute HH 131-04
    Nyahora v CFI Holdings SC 81-14
    Forestry Commission v Betty Muwonde HH 9-18
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, rule 52; Labour Act [Chapter 28:01], section 89; employment contract and housing policy terms.

document_name: Arifor Private Limited v Puroil (Private) Limited and 2 Others (739 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 739 (21 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: AFRIFOR PRIVATE LIMITED versus PUROIL (PRIVATE) LIMITED and TENGRUP ENERGY (PRIVATE) LIMITED and ECOBANK ZIMBABWE LIMITED
citation: Arifor Private Limited v Puroil (Private) Limited and 2 Others (739 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 739 (21 October 2022)
subject_area: Civil procedure; anti-dissipation interdict; interim interdict; default judgment; rescission; prima facie right; bank account freeze.
catchwords_flynote: Anti-dissipation interdict – applicant sought to freeze funds transferred pursuant to default judgment while rescission pending – court had already found applicant had no right to funds when dismissing counter-application for condonation and upliftment of bar – extant judgment cannot be indirectly reviewed through anti-dissipation order – no prima facie right, imminent infringement or irreparable harm established – application dismissed with costs.
headnote: The applicant sought an anti-dissipation order preventing the respondents from dissipating funds transferred under a default judgment in HCHC 196/22 and freezing a bank account pending rescission. The Court held that it had already determined, in the prior matter, that the applicant had no right to the funds when it dismissed the applicant's counter-application for condonation and upliftment of bar. That judgment was extant and could not be indirectly reviewed through an anti-dissipation application. The applicant had no prima facie right, no imminent infringement of a right, no irreparable harm and an alternative remedy if it later succeeded. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant established a prima facie right to the funds or to challenge the default judgment for purposes of anti-dissipation relief.
    Whether there was imminent infringement of that right.
    Whether irreparable harm would result absent interim relief.
    Whether an alternative remedy existed.
    Whether the balance of convenience favoured freezing the account.
legal_principles: The requirements for an anti-dissipation interdict are the same as for a prohibitory interdict: prima facie right, imminent infringement, irreparable harm, absence of another satisfactory remedy and balance of convenience. A pending rescission application does not by itself create a prima facie right to funds where an extant judgment has already determined that the applicant has no entitlement to them. A court cannot indirectly review its own extant decision through an interlocutory anti-dissipation order.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant failed to establish a prima facie right to the funds.
    Held, that recognising such a right would amount to the Court reviewing its own extant decision.
    Held, that no imminent infringement of a right could be shown because the Court had already decided against the applicant's perceived right.
    Held, that no irreparable harm was established because if the applicant later succeeded it could execute against the respondents.
    Held, that the balance of convenience did not favour interfering with execution of an extant judgment.
    Held, that the anti-dissipation application should be dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that anti-dissipation relief cannot be used to freeze funds obtained through execution of an extant judgment where the applicant has not shown an independent prima facie right and is effectively seeking to revisit the prior decision.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to:
Bozimo Trade & Development Co. P/L v Merchant Bank of Zimbabwe and Others 2000 (1) ZLR 1 (H)
    Mine Mills Trading Private Limited & Others v NJZ Resources (HK) Limited SC 40/2014
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021 provisions on urgent chamber applications and default judgment/rescission proceedings; common-law anti-dissipation/interdict principles

document_name: Coumbis v Theright Investments (Pvt) Ltd and 3 Others (740 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 740 (21 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: PHILIPPA ANN COUMBIS versus THERIGHT INVESTMENTS (PVT) LTD and 3 OTHERS
citation: Coumbis v Theright Investments (Pvt) Ltd and 3 Others (740 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 740 (21 October 2022)
subject_area: Company law; declaratur; shares and company property; transfer of immovable property; fraud; locus standi; divorce consequences.
catchwords_flynote: Shareholding – Supreme Court award of 80% shares in company – whether shareholder thereby owns company immovable property – application to cancel deed of transfer after sale by company – fraud must be pleaded and proved – failure to cite alleged fraudster – application dismissed with attorney-client costs.
headnote: The applicant sought cancellation of a deed of transfer in favour of the second respondent and revival of the deed in the first respondent’s name, relying on a Supreme Court order awarding her 80% shareholding in the first respondent after divorce litigation. The Court held that ownership of shares is not ownership of the company’s property and that the Supreme Court order did not award the applicant Stand 9064. The applicant also failed to plead and prove fraud or bad faith in the sale and transfer, and had made allegations against her former husband without citing him. The locus standi objection failed, but the application was dismissed with attorney-client costs in favour of the second respondent.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant had locus standi.
    Whether an order awarding 80% shareholding in a company meant the applicant had been awarded the company’s immovable property.
    Whether the applicant established fraud, bad faith or a sufficient cause of action to cancel transfer.
    Whether adverse findings could be made against the applicant’s former husband despite his non-citation.
    Whether attorney-client costs were justified.
legal_principles: A company is a separate legal person and shareholders do not own the company’s property merely by virtue of shareholding. An order awarding shares does not, without express language, transfer company assets to the shareholder. After immovable property has been transferred, a party seeking cancellation must plead and prove fraud, bad faith or knowledge of a defect. Serious allegations against a non-party cannot found relief without giving that person an opportunity to answer.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the point in limine on locus standi had no merit and was dismissed.
    Held, that the Supreme Court order awarding 80% shares did not award Stand 9064 to the applicant.
    Held, that the applicant failed to plead and prove fraud or bad faith by the seller or purchaser.
    Held, that alleged malice or fraud by the applicant’s former husband could not found relief when he was not cited.
    Held, that the application was dismissed with attorney-client costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that ownership of majority shares in a company does not equate to ownership of the company’s immovable property, and cancellation of a transferred title requires properly pleaded and proved fraud or bad faith by relevant parties before the court.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court criticised the invitation to make adverse findings of fraud and malice against a person who was not cited as a party.

Mauritius and Anor v Verspak Holdings (Pvt) Ltd and Anor SC 2-22
    Magauzi and Anor v Jekera SC 54-22
    Borland’s Trustees v Steel Bros & Co (Pvt) Ltd [1901] 1 Ch 279
    Wallersteiner v Moir (No. 2) [1975] All ER 849 (CA)
    Makuyana and Anor v Standard Bank of Zimbabwe and Ors HB 52-07
    Sookdeyi & Ors v Sahadeo & Ors 1952(4) SA 568(A)
    Twine Wire Agencies (Pvt) Ltd v CABS
    Mapedzamombe v CBZ

document_name: Matukutire v Madenga and Another (741 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 741 (26 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Matukutire v Madenga and Another
subject_area: Contract; sale of immovable property; cancellation; motion proceedings; material disputes of fact.
catchwords_flynote: Agreement of sale – application to confirm cancellation – purchaser alleging full payment through seller’s wife/record keeper – seller denying payment – material disputes of fact – motion proceedings inappropriate – application dismissed.
headnote: The applicant sought confirmation of cancellation of an agreement of sale over Stand 1258 Goodhope Township. The first respondent resisted, asserting that the purchase price had been paid and that the second respondent, the applicant’s wife, acted as record keeper and issued receipts. The second respondent’s affidavit confirmed receipt of certain payments. The court held that the papers disclosed material disputes of fact which could not be resolved robustly on affidavit. The applicant had proceeded by motion when the dispute required action proceedings. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether there were material disputes of fact; whether the court could decide the cancellation dispute on affidavits; whether the application should be dismissed or referred to evidence/action.
legal_principles: Where real, material disputes of fact are foreseeable and cannot be resolved on the papers without injustice, motion proceedings are inappropriate. Affidavits cannot simply be ranked by credibility where material facts are genuinely contested.
held_decision_points: The point in limine on material disputes of fact was upheld. The application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The applicant’s version of non-payment was directly contradicted by the first respondent and supported in part by the second respondent’s affidavit. Determining whether payment was made required oral evidence and credibility findings.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The counter-claim was struck out by consent before the court dealt with the merits.
cases_referred_to: Jirira v ZIMCOR Trustees Ltd & Anor 2010 (1) ZLR 375 (H); authorities on disputes of fact in motion proceedings.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, rr 58 and 59.
reviewer_comments: Draft.
export_history: Created in High_Court_Harare_Batch_14_2022_Law_Report_Drafts.txt from the supplied source document.

document_name: CHINAN~1(2).DOC
case_name: CONSILIA CHINANZVAVANA versus THE COMMISSIONER GENERAL OF THE ZIMBABWE REVENUE AUTHORITY and THE ZIMBABWE REVENUE AUTHORITY
subject_area: Civil procedure; mandament van spolie; tax and customs law; seizure of motor vehicle; notice of intention to sue; authority of deponent; joinder.
catchwords_flynote: Spoliation – seizure of motor vehicle by ZIMRA – whether mandament van spolie application competent without statutory notice – Customs and Excise Act – s 196 notice requirement – authority of corporate deponent – resolution not invariably necessary – improper joinder of Commissioner General – application struck off roll.
headnote: The applicant sought spoliatory relief after ZIMRA seized her Mazda BT50 at Nyabira following a red flag. The Court dismissed the applicant's objection to the authority of ZIMRA's deponent, holding that a person with personal knowledge may swear to the facts without necessarily attaching a resolution. The Court upheld the respondents' objection that the Commissioner General had been improperly joined and, more importantly, that the applicant had failed to give the statutory notice required by s 196 of the Customs and Excise Act before suing for recovery of the vehicle. The application was struck off the roll with no order as to costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the deponent to the respondents' opposing affidavit had authority to represent or depose for the respondents.
    Whether the Commissioner General of ZIMRA was properly joined.
    Whether the applicant was required to give notice under s 196 of the Customs and Excise Act before instituting spoliation proceedings.
    Whether the application could proceed despite non-compliance with that statutory notice requirement.
legal_principles: A company or statutory body's opposing affidavit may be deposed to by a person who can swear positively to the facts; a resolution is not required in every case. A statutory pre-litigation notice provision expressed in peremptory terms must be complied with before civil proceedings covered by the provision are instituted. A spoliation claim remains a civil proceeding and does not, by that label alone, escape the requirements of s 196 of the Customs and Excise Act.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the objection to the authority of the respondents' deponent had no merit.
    Held, that the Commissioner General was improperly joined.
    Held, that the applicant was obliged to comply with s 196 of the Customs and Excise Act before suing for recovery of the vehicle.
    Held, that the failure to give the statutory notice was fatal.
    Held, that the application was struck off the roll with no order as to costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that, where a statute peremptorily prohibits litigation against ZIMRA or its officials without prior notice, a litigant seeking spoliatory recovery of seized goods must first comply with that requirement; non-compliance deprives the application of procedural competence.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted that a resolution is not required in every case where an official deposes to an affidavit on behalf of a corporate or statutory body, provided the deponent shows a factual basis for personal knowledge and authority.
cases_referred_to:
Madzivire & Ors v Zvarivadza & Ors 2006 (1) ZLR 514
    BAUXIM Logistics (Pvt) Ltd v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority HH 138/22
    Newman Chiadzwa v Herbert Paulkner 1991 (2) ZLR 33
    BANCABC v PWC Motors (Private) Limited & Others HH 123/13
    Machacha v ZIMRA HB 127/14

document_name: Matingo v Humbe (744 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 744 (26 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Matingo v Humbe
subject_area: Summary judgment; rei vindicatio; immovable property; judicial sale; issue estoppel; res judicata.
catchwords_flynote: Summary judgment – eviction – rei vindicatio – registered owner – judicial sale – defendant alleging prior personal rights and fraud – issue estoppel – Supreme Court already determining rights – no bona fide defence.
headnote: The plaintiff, registered owner of Stand 67 Guildford Estate Township after purchasing it at a judicial sale, sought summary judgment evicting the defendant. The defendant alleged that he had earlier bought the property, that his rights preceded the plaintiff’s, and that the judicial sale and related proceedings were tainted by fraud. The court reviewed the extensive related litigation and held that the Supreme Court had already determined that the defendant had no real rights in the property, only personal rights. The allegations of fraud were bare and did not raise a triable defence. Summary judgment was granted and the defendant ordered to vacate within seven days.
issues_for_determination: Whether the defendant had a bona fide defence to summary judgment; whether pending proceedings or allegations of fraud defeated the plaintiff’s registered title; whether issue estoppel applied.
legal_principles: Rei vindicatio allows an owner to recover property from anyone holding it without consent. Registration of title conveys real rights. Issue estoppel prevents re-litigation of issues finally determined in prior proceedings. Bare allegations do not establish a triable issue for summary judgment.
held_decision_points: The defendant had no real rights capable of defeating the plaintiff’s title and no bona fide defence. Summary judgment was granted.
ratio_decidendi: The Supreme Court had already determined the essential issue of the defendant’s rights; those findings created issue estoppel. The plaintiff’s registered title prevailed, and the defendant’s alleged fraud did not impugn it.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court criticised unnecessary proliferation of proceedings relating to the same property and noted that lawyers were largely to blame for such waste.
cases_referred_to: Humbe v Muchina & Ors SC 81/21; Munemo v Muswera 1987 (1) ZLR 20 (SC); Willowvale Mazda Motor Industries v Sunshine Rent-a-Car 1996 (1) ZLR 415 (S); Chetty v Naidoo 1974 (3) SA 13; Alspite Investments (Pvt) Ltd v Westerhof 2009 (2) ZLR 226 (H); Takafuma v Takafuma 1994 (2) ZLR 103 (S); Hales v Doverick Investments (Private) Limited 1998 (2) ZLR 235 (H); Dube v Medical Services International Ltd 1998 (2) ZLR 280 (SC); Chindori-Chininga v National Council for Negro Women 2001 (2) ZLR 305 (H).
legislation_considered: Deeds Registries Act [Chapter 20:05], s 2; High Court Rules, 2021, r 71(36) and r 71(37).

document_name: KENCOR~1(1).DOC
case_name: KENCOR HOLDINGS (PRIVATE) LIMITED versus TARIRO NDLOVU N.O. MINING COMMISSIONER FOR MASHONALAND CENTRAL AND 3 OTHERS
subject_area: Civil procedure; mining appeal record; mandamus; counter-application; High Court Rules rule 95; correctness of record of proceedings.
catchwords_flynote: Mining appeal – record of proceedings – applicant sought order compelling return of record signed by Mining Commissioner – record incomplete/not proper – Registrar entitled to return record for further action – counter-application granted requiring proper record with specified documents – no order as to costs.
headnote: The applicant wanted the Mining Commissioner to return a previously signed record to the Registrar so that a mining appeal could be set down. The respondents argued that the record was incomplete and not a true record. The Court found no basis to compel return of the defective record and held that the Registrar was entitled to return it for further action. The main application was dismissed and the counter-application was granted, requiring the Mining Commissioner to prepare and lodge a record containing specified documents.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the Mining Commissioner should be ordered to return the record dated 26 October 2020.
    Whether the Registrar acted lawfully in returning the record for further action.
    Whether the record was complete and proper.
    Whether the counter-application for preparation of a proper record should be granted.
legal_principles: Rule 95 of the High Court Rules, 2021 governs preparation of records in appeals and reviews. Where there is no proper formal record or the record is incomplete, the Registrar may direct that additional documents be supplied or that a proper record be prepared. A court may not mero motu uplift a bar without an application for condonation or upliftment.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the main application was dismissed.
    Held, that the counter-application was granted.
    Held, that the Mining Commissioner must prepare and lodge a record within 30 days containing the specified mining documents.
    Held, that the appeal CIV (A) 43/20 must be set down within ten days after receipt of the record.
    Held, that there was no order as to costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that an appeal cannot properly proceed on an incomplete and contested record where the record-holder did not keep a proper record; justice required preparation of a proper record including key documents before set-down.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted that the applicant’s version shifted on whether its lawyers prepared the record or merely the index, leaving more questions than answers.
cases_referred_to: The President of Zimbabwe Robert Gabriel Mugabe N.O. and Others v Morgan Richard Tsvangirai SC 21/17; House of Assembly v Zigomo 1996 (2) ZLR 52 (S); authorities concerning records and mandamus as cited.
legislation_considered: High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], section 31; High Court Rules, 2021, rule 95(14) and (16); High Court (Miscellaneous Appeals and Reviews) Rules, 1975; Mines and Minerals Act [Chapter 21:05].
subject_area_terms: Civil procedure; mining appeal record; mandamus; counter-application
High Court Rules rule 95; correctness of record of proceedings.

document_name: ISOQUA~1.DOC
case_name: ISOQUANT INVESTMENTS (PVT) LTD T/A ZIMOCO versus PLANNING AND DESIGN STUDIO CONSULTANTS (PVT) LTD AND ANOTHER
court: HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE, COMMERCIAL LAW DIVISION, HARARE
subject_area: Contract; compromise; settlement of judgment debt; cession or transfer of immovable property; tacit contract; prescription.
catchwords_flynote: Opposed application – judgment debt – offer of residential stands in settlement – counter-offer and conduct of parties – compromise – tacit contract – debt not discharged by later unilateral payment – transfer/cession ordered – costs each party to bear own costs.
headnote: The applicant obtained a monetary judgment against the first respondent. The first respondent offered residential stands in settlement. The parties’ correspondence and conduct showed acceptance of a compromise involving seven stands, each measuring 300 square metres. The Court held that the compromise constituted a new contract and extinguished the original judgment debt. The respondents were ordered to execute documents for cession or transfer of the identified stands, failing which the Sheriff could sign. Each party was ordered to bear its own costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the claim had prescribed.
    Whether the parties concluded a compromise agreement.
    Whether later payment of the judgment debt discharged the first respondent’s obligations.
    Whether the respondents should be compelled to take steps to effect cession or transfer of the stands.
legal_principles: A compromise is a new contract that may extinguish the original obligation. A tacit contract can be inferred from correspondence and conduct. Where a judgment debt is compromised by agreement, it is not open to a debtor unilaterally to choose a different method of discharge inconsistent with the compromise.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the prescription point was abandoned.
    Held, that the offer of stands 5344 to 5350 constituted a compromise of the court order in HC 412/13.
    Held, that the respondents must sign all documents necessary to effect cession and/or transfer within 30 days, failing which the Sheriff may sign.
    Held, that each party shall bear its own costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the parties’ correspondence and conduct established a binding compromise, and once the judgment debt had been compromised, the first respondent could not discharge the obligation by later choosing to pay the original judgment debt instead.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that the applicant had sat on its laurels and did not ensure that the compromise came to fruition, which justified each party bearing its own costs.
cases_referred_to: Ilasha Mining (Pvt) Ltd v Yatakala Trading (Pvt) Ltd t/a Viking Hardware Distributors HB-03-18; authorities on tacit contracts and compromise as cited.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021; general law of contract and compromise.
subject_area_terms: Contract; compromise; settlement of judgment debt; cession or transfer of immovable property; tacit contract; prescription.
cases_referred_to_terms: Ilasha Mining (Pvt) Ltd v Yatakala Trading (Pvt) Ltd t/a Viking Hardware Distributors HB-03-18; authorities on tacit contracts and compromise as cited.
legislation_considered_terms: High Court Rules, 2021; general law of contract and compromise.

document_name: BOLTSP~1.DOC
case_name: BOLT SPEED CONTRACTORS (PRIVATE) LIMITED versus WANG HONG FANG and THE SHERIFF OF THE HIGH COURT N.O.
case_number:
HC 6945/22
    Ref Case No. HC 2474/22
    Ref Case No. HC 6390/22
subject_area: Civil procedure; urgent chamber application; stay of execution; eviction; rescission of default judgment; lease dispute; provisional order.
catchwords_flynote: Stay of execution – tenant defaulting pre-trial conference and facing eviction and execution – pending rescission application – preliminary points on urgency and draft order dismissed – court considering substantial justice pending return date – execution and eviction stayed as interim relief until return date.
headnote: The applicant leased premises from the respondent and was sued for cancellation of lease, eviction and arrear rentals. After default at pre-trial conference, default judgment was granted, followed by writs of execution and ejectment. The applicant promptly filed rescission and then sought urgent stay. The Court dismissed preliminary objections and held that grave injustice could occur if execution and eviction proceeded before the court considered the rescission-related dispute. Interim relief staying execution of movable goods and eviction from the premises was granted pending the return date.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the matter was urgent.
    Whether the draft order was fatally defective.
    Whether execution and eviction should proceed before determination of the rescission application.
    Whether the applicant established a prima facie case and balance of convenience for interim relief.
legal_principles: The court may grant interim stay of execution where substantial justice requires preservation of the status quo pending determination of rescission or other proceedings. The court considers urgency, prima facie case, risk of irreparable harm, balance of convenience, and whether prejudice would be reversible if execution proceeded and the judgment were later rescinded.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the preliminary objections were dismissed.
    Held, that allowing execution and eviction to proceed before the return date could cause grave and irreversible prejudice.
    Held, that the applicant established a prima facie case for interim relief.
    Held, that the balance of convenience favoured staying execution and eviction pending the return date.
    Held, that interim relief was granted with variations to clarify the stay.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that where a rescission application is pending and execution or eviction would cause prejudice that may not be reversible if rescission succeeds, the court may grant interim stay to preserve the status quo pending the return date.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court expressly refrained from determining the merits of the rescission application, reserving those issues for the return date.
cases_referred_to: Not available
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, rule 60(6); rules governing rescission and urgent chamber applications.
subject_area_terms: Civil procedure; urgent chamber application; stay of execution; eviction; rescission of default judgment; lease dispute; provisional order.
cases_referred_to_terms: Not available
legislation_considered_terms: High Court Rules, 2021, rule 60(6); rules governing rescission and urgent chamber applications.

document_name: Nehanda Housing Co-operative Society Limited v Harare City Council (748 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 748 (19 October 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: Nehanda Housing Co-operative Society Limited v Harare City Council
subject_area: Urban councils; rates clearance certificate; statutory interpretation; certificate of compliance; stated case.
catchwords_flynote: Urban Councils Act – s 282(2)(b) – rates clearance certificate – whether local authority may insist on development certificate of compliance as precondition – stated case – certificate must issue once statutory rates requirements met.
headnote: The plaintiff sought a declaratur and order compelling the City of Harare to issue rates clearance certificates for three properties. The City refused because the plaintiff had not produced certificates of compliance for the development. The parties proceeded by stated case on the proper interpretation of s 282(2)(b) of the Urban Councils Act. The court held that the provision does not make production of a certificate of compliance a precondition for a rates clearance certificate. The City was ordered to issue the certificates.
issues_for_determination: Whether s 282(2)(b) of the Urban Councils Act permits the City to withhold a rates clearance certificate for want of a certificate of compliance; whether payment of outstanding rates and charges is sufficient.
legal_principles: Statutory interpretation begins with the text. Courts must not read into legislation requirements that the legislature did not include. A public authority cannot impose extra-statutory preconditions for the exercise of a statutory right or process.
held_decision_points: The City had no right to withhold the plaintiff’s rates clearance certificates once the statutory rates requirement was met.
ratio_decidendi: The statute refers to rates and charges necessary for the rates clearance certificate. It does not include a certificate of compliance as a condition precedent. If the legislature had intended such a precondition, it would have included it expressly.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The City’s concerns about development compliance might be enforceable through other lawful mechanisms, but not by withholding the rates clearance certificate under s 282(2)(b).
cases_referred_to: Tapedza & Ors v Zimbabwe Energy Regulatory Authority and related statutory-interpretation authorities cited in the judgment.
legislation_considered: Urban Councils Act [Chapter 29:15], especially s 282(2)(b); High Court Rules, 2021.
reviewer_comments: Draft.
export_history: Created in High_Court_Harare_Batch_16_2022_Law_Report_Drafts.txt for consolidated 2022 Harare High Court law-report draft export.
subject_area_terms: Urban councils; rates clearance certificate; statutory interpretation; certificate of compliance; stated case.
cases_referred_to_terms: Tapedza & Ors v Zimbabwe Energy Regulatory Authority and related statutory-interpretation authorities cited in the judgment.

document_name: Siduna and Another v Tanyanyiwa and Another (749 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 749 (25 October 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: Siduna and Another v Tanyanyiwa and Another
subject_area: Family law; guardianship; custody; urgent chamber application; curator ad litem; best interests of the child
catchwords_flynote: Guardianship — stepfather seeking appointment as legal guardian and sole custodian — wrong statutory basis — interested party appointed curator ad litem — best interests of child — adoption route — High Court as upper guardian.
headnote: The first applicant, stepfather to a minor child, sought urgent appointment as legal guardian and sole custodian so that the child could obtain immigration status in Botswana under the first applicant’s work permit. The application relied on the Guardianship of Minors Act and the High Court Rules. The court held that the cited statutory provision caters for applications by parents in divorce or separation contexts, not a stepfather seeking guardianship. It also found the curator ad litem report defective because the curator was the child’s maternal grandmother and an interested family participant. Although the best interests of the child are paramount, the application lacked a proper legal foundation. It was dismissed with no order as to costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the urgent application was properly founded on s 4(b) of the Guardianship of Minors Act.
    Whether an interested family member could properly act as curator ad litem and prepare the report.
    Whether the best interests of the child justified granting the order despite procedural and substantive defects.
    Whether the appropriate route was adoption or a differently framed application invoking the High Court’s upper-guardian jurisdiction.
legal_principles:
A curator ad litem must conduct an independent and impartial investigation into the affairs and welfare of the minor child.
    The best interests of the child are paramount, but the court cannot ignore defects in legal foundation and process.
    Section 4 of the Guardianship of Minors Act applies to applications by parents in divorce or separation contexts.
    Guardianship cannot simply be transferred by agreement unless the law provides an appropriate route, such as adoption or another recognised basis.
    The High Court is the upper guardian of minor children, but parties must properly invoke that jurisdiction.
held_decision_points:
The first respondent was not a proper curator ad litem because she was an interested family member and part of the factual background requiring investigation.
    The curator’s report was impeachable for lack of independence.
    Section 4(b) of the Guardianship of Minors Act did not apply to a stepfather’s application of this nature.
    The application was dismissed with no order as to costs.
ratio_decidendi: The court could not grant guardianship and sole custody to a non-biological parent under a statutory provision that did not support that relief and on the basis of a curator report prepared by an interested party. The best-interests principle did not cure the absence of a proper legal foundation.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court observed that the applicants might have considered adoption proceedings before the Children’s Court or a properly framed approach to the High Court as upper guardian of the minor child.
cases_referred_to:
Kutsanzira v Master of the High Court 2012 (2) ZLR 91 (H)
    Dandarembizi v Hunda HH 447/18
    Mukundu v Chigumadzi & Ors 2015 (2) ZLR 332 (H); HH 818/15
    W v X HB 5/19
    McCall v McCall 1994 (3) SA 201
    In re Alice Maenzanise HH 39/20
legislation_considered:
High Court Rules, 2021, rr 60(1), 61(2)(b), 61(3), 61(4)
    Guardianship of Minors Act [Chapter 5:08], s 4(b)
    Children’s Act [Chapter 5:06]
    United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child, Article 3
    African Charter on the Rights and Welfare of the Child, Article 4
reviewer_comments: Mounted file was extractable. The judgment is important for the procedural role and independence of a curator ad litem in guardianship applications.
