document_name: Christ Citadel International Church v Mujaya N.O and Another (750 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 750 (26 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: CHRIST CITADEL INTERNATIONAL CHURCH (represented by LINDANI NDABA) versus HOSEA MUJAYA N.O. and THE STATE
citation: Christ Citadel International Church v Mujaya N.O and Another (750 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 750 (26 October 2022)
subject_area: Criminal procedure; review; application for discharge at close of State case; culpable homicide; negligence; duty of care; unterminated proceedings.
catchwords_flynote: Review – magistrate’s refusal of discharge at close of State case – culpable homicide after electrocution at church construction site – duty of care to members of public – superior court slow to interfere with unterminated criminal proceedings – review dismissed.
headnote: The applicant sought review of a magistrate’s refusal to discharge it at the close of the State case in culpable homicide proceedings following an electrocution at its premises. The Court held that the applicant owed a duty of care not only to church members but to members of the public who might attend or enter the premises. Evidence that the church structure was under a live 11kV power line and that there were no barricades or warning signs called for an answer. The applicant had not shown grounds for interference with the unterminated criminal proceedings. The review application was dismissed with each party bearing its own costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the magistrate’s refusal to discharge the applicant at the close of the State case was grossly irregular or irrational.
    Whether there was evidence on which a reasonable court might convict.
    Whether the applicant owed a duty of care only to congregants or to members of the public generally.
    Whether the High Court should intervene in unterminated criminal proceedings.
legal_principles: Superior courts are slow to interfere with unterminated criminal proceedings except in exceptional circumstances, such as gross irregularity or serious prejudice not remediable by ordinary process. A discharge at the close of the State case is granted only where there is no evidence on essential elements, no evidence on which a reasonable court could convict, or evidence so manifestly unreliable that it cannot safely be acted upon. A person or entity creating a hazardous situation owes a duty of care to foreseeable entrants and members of the public, not only invited members.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the magistrate did not act irrationally in placing the applicant on its defence.
    Held, that the applicant’s duty of care could not be confined to members of its congregation.
    Held, that evidence about construction under a live 11kV line and lack of barricades or warnings required an answer.
    Held, that the applicant failed to establish a basis for review intervention.
    Held, that the application was dismissed and each party was to bear its own costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the State had led evidence capable of establishing negligence and duty of care, and the applicant therefore had a case to answer; refusal of discharge in such circumstances did not justify High Court review intervention before completion of the criminal trial.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court emphasised that church premises are not exclusive to known congregants and that safety measures must contemplate ordinary members of the public who may enter the premises.
cases_referred_to:
Attorney-General v Makamba 2005 (2) ZLR 54 (S)
    Mamombe and Another v Mushure N.O and Another SC 10/22
    Banga v State 1998 (2) ZLR (S)
    AG v Bvuma & Anor 1987 (2) ZLR 96
    AG v Mzizi 1991 (2) ZLR 321
    AG v Tarwirei 1997 (1) ZLR 575 (S)
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], section 49(b); Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], principles governing discharge at close of State case.

document_name: WELLIW~1.DOC
case_name: Welli Will Industries (Pvt) Ltd v African Banking Corporation of Zimbabwe Ltd and Others
subject_area: Urgent chamber application; stay of execution; frozen bank account; sheriff’s attachment; deed of settlement; commercial court division; special circumstances; parallel proceedings.
catchwords_flynote: Stay of execution – funds attached from bank account under court order and deed of settlement – applicant alleging debt settled and interest unlawfully charged – parallel proceedings pending on parties’ rights – whether special circumstances justified interim stay – bank account to remain frozen pending resolution.
headnote: The applicant sought urgent relief staying execution against funds held in its BancABC account and challenging the freezing of that account. The dispute arose from a loan, deed of settlement and court order. The applicant argued that it had settled the debt and that the second respondent was unlawfully charging interest. The court dismissed three preliminary points and held that the disputes concerning settlement, exchange rate and interest had to be resolved in parallel proceedings. It granted interim relief staying execution, but required the account to remain frozen pending finalization.
issues_for_determination: Whether the matter was urgent; whether the Commercial Court Division had jurisdiction; whether the applicant had shown grounds for stay of execution; whether the bank lawfully froze the account; whether the Sheriff’s attachment should be stayed pending determination of HCHC 78/22; and what interim protection was appropriate.
legal_principles: Execution of a judgment is the normal consequence of a court order, and a party seeking a stay must show special circumstances and that real and substantial justice demands intervention. A bank may freeze an account pursuant to contractual terms and judicial attachment to avoid exposure to competing claims. Where unresolved parallel proceedings concern the validity and extent of the debt, interim preservation may be warranted.
held_decision_points: The preliminary points were dismissed. The court found special circumstances justifying interim stay of execution, given unresolved disputes in HCHC 78/22. It declined to order the account unfrozen; instead, the account was to remain frozen pending finalization of the matter. The Sheriff was ordered to stay execution against the funds pending HCHC 78/22.
ratio_decidendi: The existence of unresolved issues about whether the debt was fully paid, whether interest was lawful, and which exchange rate applied made it necessary to preserve the position. At the same time, the bank had acted cautiously and contractually, so the account should remain frozen rather than be released outright.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified.
cases_referred_to: None expressly cited in the extracted reasoning beyond general stay-of-execution principles.
legislation_considered: Prescribed Rates of Interest Act [Chapter 8:10]; High Court Rules, 2021; contractual banking terms, including clauses 24(j) and 24(l).

document_name: S v Gwafa (754 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 754 (26 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Gwafa
subject_area: Criminal law; criminal review; robbery; sentencing; first offender; suspension of sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal review – robbery – guilty plea – young first offender – failure to suspend portion on condition of good behaviour – sentence unduly harsh – review court interfering.
headnote: The accused pleaded guilty to two counts of robbery and received 30 months’ imprisonment with only one month suspended for restitution. The High Court held that the trial magistrate misdirected himself by failing to suspend a portion on condition of future good behaviour for a young first offender. A further six months were suspended.
issues_for_determination: Whether the sentence was unduly harsh due to failure to suspend part of the effective sentence on good-behaviour conditions.
legal_principles: Although robbery generally warrants imprisonment, a portion of a prison sentence for a young first offender is commonly suspended to encourage reform, unless circumstances justify otherwise.
held_decision_points: Sentence varied by suspending a further six months on condition of good behaviour.
ratio_decidendi: A sentencing misdirection occurs where a court fails to consider reformative suspension for a young first offender in circumstances where it is appropriate.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The matter was not remitted because the trial magistrate was on suspension.
cases_referred_to: Cases not specifically listed beyond general sentencing principles.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 126; Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07].

document_name: Magaya v Mazvel Investments (Pvt) Ltd and Another (755 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 755 (26 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Magaya v Mazvel Investments (Pvt) Ltd and Another
court:
2.1 a valid agreement of sale entered into in 2017 exists between the Applicant and Respondent over Stand 815 Sumben of Sumben Housing Project, Mount Pleasant, Harare, measuring 2,000 square metres (‘the stand’ or ‘property’); and
2.2 The applicant has a contractual right to specific performance of the agreement of sale.
3. Respondents, jointly and severally, are hereby ordered to, within 7 days of this order;
2.1 cause an agreement of sale to be signed by the parties embodying the terms of sale of the stand at a rate of RTGS$30 per square metre of land.
2.2 commence accepting instalment payments by the Applicant of the balance of the purchase price for the stand over a period of five (5) years at a currency rate of USD$1=1RTGS dollar with effect from the calendar month that follows the grant of this order;
2.3 grant the Applicant possession, and pass ownership title, of the stand to the Applicant upon his payment of the full balance of the purchase price stated in paragraph 2.2 above.
4. Respondents shall, jointly and severally, bear costs of suit on higher scale”
BACKGROUND FACTS
The following are facts are common cause. Sometime in the year, 2017 the
Respondents placed an advertisement in the local print media for the sale of land in Mount Pleasant, Harare under the name Sumben Mount Pleasant. The key aspects of that advertisement were that:
The residential stands were 2000m2. The price was US$30/ m2. A 15% deposit needed to be paid and the balance would be payable in monthly instalments over a period of 5 years. The Applicant responded to the advertisement and contacted the 2nd Respondent’s Offices and was attended to by Mr. Shadreck Mupunga. Mr. Mupunga sent the Applicant a standard email that outlined or spelled out the processes that were to take place and the requirements one had to meet before one could have the land as theirs.
The email in question reads as follows;
“Good day
Please find attached advert on the Sumben stands in Mt. Pleasant as well as Homesaver account opening form and signature card.
The first step is to open a Homesaver account and the requirements for account opening are as follows:
Copy ID
Proof of residence
2 passport photos
The minimum deposit required is $9,000 to enable application for the Stand. Once the deposit has been paid into the Homesaver account, you will then be given the stand application form for completion. Requirements for stand application are as follows:
Payslip/proof of income
3 months bank statement
Stand application will be processed within three weeks and an Agreement for sale will be done for signing. Monthly instalments will be due on the last day of the month that the Agreement of Sale is signed. The indicative monthly instalment will be $1,084 for the 5 years.
The balance to be paid over 5 years will be insured by ZB Life to cover death or incapacitation of applicant. There will be a monthly insurance premium over and above the stand loan instalment. The rate and amount will be advised on application.
Servicing of the stands will commence in the last quarter of 2017 and will be completed within 18 months.
Please get in touch with the undersigned for any clarifications.
Regards”
The applicant duly complied with the first step in the process indicated in the email and filled out the application form and submitted it to the Respondents for process. The process however stalled until sometime in 2020 when the Respondents reverted to the Applicant. An offer was made to the Applicant by the Respondents and its terms were different from the terms in the advertisement in 2017 and from the contents of the email referenced above. The price had changed to US$37/m2 and the repayment period had been slashed to 18 months from 5 years not to mention that by implication and logic the monthly instalments had been raised tenfold. The Applicant was to make up his mind within 4 days regarding whether or not he was interested in what was now christened Phase 1 of the project or whether he wanted to wait for Phase 2 which phase was still awaiting Environmental Management Agency approval. By February 2021 the Respondents sent to the Applicant a Memorandum of Agreement of Sale and called upon him to sign or be considered to have declined the offer by a particular date.
The applicant took all this to be a unilateral variation of the terms of a binding agreement he had with the Respondents way back in 2017 and therefore unlawful conduct and chose not to sign and instead instituted these proceedings. In a letter dated 23 February 2021, attached to the draft agreement of the sale the Respondents stated that they did what they did in coming up with those terms and conditions of the sale of the stands because there had been material changes in the market that they considered and had to ensure viability of the Project and had to deliver a finished product.
THE APPLICANT’S CASE
The Applicant has argued that in doing what the parties did in 2017, they concluded a binding agreement of sale of land in respect of stand N. 815 Sumben Township and it is that agreement that the Respondents are trying to unlawfully and unilaterally resile from. That a contract actuated on 11 August 2017 when he made a deposit into his Homesaver Account with the 2nd Respondent as required by the Respondents. To the Applicant by making a deposit of US$9050-00 into his Homesaver account, that was payment of a deposit for the stand since the account was a means or conduit by which instalments would be paid for a period of 5 years.
Relying on Kovi -v- Ashanti Goldfields Zimbabwe Ltd & Anor 2007(2) ZLR 354 in which at page 355B the following was sated,
“There are three essential requirements of a contract of sale. These are agreements (consensus ad idem) a thing sold (merx); and a price (pretium). Neither delivery nor payment is necessary to the creation of the contract, for they both fall within the category of its performance”
the Applicant argued that the processes and conduct of the parties evinced the existence of a sale. The absence of a written contract requisite in terms of S7 the Contractual Penalties Act [Chapter 8:04] did not and does not render the agreement void ab initio as he has discharged the onus on him imposed by the relevant statute to prove the existence of the contract and has done so by pleading the emails between the parties and proof of payment.
RESPONDENTS’ CASE
The Respondents have argued that there was never a contract of sale between the parties concluded in 2017 as alleged and believed by the Applicant. All that the Applicant did in compliance with the advertisement was to do two steps of a process that had to be done in stages.
Stage 1 – Applicant was to open a Homesaver account and deposit US$9000-00 into it to enable him to get an application form for the stand. He did that.
Stage 2 – He had to complete the application form. He did that too.
Stage 3 – The application form would be processed and if he qualified an Agreement of Sale would be drawn up and signed. That did not happen.
All they did was an invitation to treat. The stand was not identified and neither was it identifiable until November 2018 when they got the requisite Subdivision Permit. Since no agreement of sale was drawn and signed between the parties, there was no enforceable agreement of sale between the parties and this application must fail.
THE ISSUE
The controversy to be resolved between the Parties is whether or not in 2017 they entered into an agreement of sale of Stand No. 815 Sumben Township, Mount Pleasant, Harare.
RESOLUTION.
It is not argued by either Party that to answer that question one has to critically look at the standard email that the 2nd Respondent’s Shadreck Mupunga sent to the Applicant in 2017. It is clear from the literal reading of that email what a person in the position of the Applicant was expected to do and what steps would have to occasion before he got a Stand. Of critical importance is that after making a deposit into the Homesaver account one would then be given an application form for the Stand which he was to complete and attach to it certain required information or proof thereof to enable the application to be processed. After the processing of the application and being found to meet the requisite criteria an agreement of sale would then be prepared and signed.
The processing of the application meant that one could fail the vetting notwithstanding that he would have successfully complied with stage one. It would only be those who passed the screening who would be offered a Stand and a written agreement of sale would be drawn and signed to conclude a contract of sale. That the applicant made a bank deposit of US$9000-00 into the Bank is neither here nor there as the deposit was into his personal account and not either of the Respondent’s accounts. It was therefore not a deposit for any Stand. The fact that the Stand in question was not even identified in the emails of 2017, further drives the point home, that there was never a contract of sale of Stand number 815 Sumben, Mount Pleasant, Harare between the parties in this case in 2017.
Wherefore the application stands to fail and it fails.
IT IS HEREBY ORDERED THAT
1. The application be and is hereby dismissed with costs.
Ushewokunze Law Chambers, Applicant’s legal Practitioners.
Kantor & Immerman, 1st and 2nd Respondents’ Legal Practitioners.
subject_area: Contract; declaratur; specific performance; sale of immovable property
catchwords_flynote: Agreement of sale — residential stand — advertisement and standard email — invitation to treat — no identified stand — no signed agreement — specific performance refused.
headnote: The applicant sought a declaration that a binding agreement of sale had been concluded in 2017 for Stand 815 in the Sumben Housing Project and that he was entitled to specific performance at the originally advertised rate and payment period. The court held that the advertisement and email process were an invitation to apply and not an offer capable of acceptance. A stand had not yet been identified, the application still had to be processed, and no agreement of sale had been drawn or signed.
issues_for_determination: Whether the parties concluded a binding agreement of sale in 2017 over Stand 815; whether the applicant had a contractual right to specific performance on the original advertised terms.
legal_principles: An advertisement and application process may constitute an invitation to treat rather than an offer. A binding sale of immovable property requires consensus on material terms including the property sold. Specific performance depends on proof of an enforceable agreement.
held_decision_points: No enforceable agreement of sale was concluded in 2017. The deposit was made into the applicant’s own account, the stand was not identified or identifiable, and an agreement of sale was never drawn and signed. The application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: Because the standard email contemplated further application, vetting and later execution of an agreement of sale, it did not itself create contractual obligations to sell a particular stand.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified.
cases_referred_to: Not specifically identified in the extracted text.
legislation_considered: High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], s 14; general principles of contract law.
subject_area_terms: Contract; declaratur; specific performance; sale of immovable property
cases_referred_to_terms: Not specifically identified in the extracted text.
legislation_considered_terms: High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], s 14; general principles of contract law.

document_name: ZIMBAB~1(1).DOC
case_name: Zimbabwe Anti-Corruption Commission v Clerk of Court Harare Criminal Magistrates Court N.O. and 8 Others
subject_area: Urgent chamber application; certificate of urgency; locus standi; mandamus; constitutional bodies; prosecution notifications; bail conditions; passport release; contempt of court allegations.
catchwords_flynote: Urgency – certificate of urgency prepared before founding affidavit – self-created urgency – ZACC seeking magistrates court record and directions to NPA/prosecuting authorities to notify it of proceedings – whether ZACC had locus – whether mandamus requirements satisfied – application struck off with costs.
headnote: ZACC filed an urgent chamber application after learning from media reports that the ninth respondent, whom it had investigated and referred for prosecution, had obtained temporary release of his passport as part of bail-related proceedings. ZACC sought the record of proceedings and an order compelling prosecuting authorities to notify it of proceedings relating to its matters. The court held that the certificate of urgency was defective because it predated the founding affidavit, that urgency was self-created or absent, that ZACC lacked locus to supervise the prosecution or court officers in the manner sought, and that the requirements for mandamus were not met. The application was struck off with costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether the certificate of urgency was valid; whether the matter was urgent; whether ZACC had locus standi to compel the court/prosecuting respondents to provide records or notify it of proceedings; whether ZACC had a clear or prima facie right to mandamus; whether the release of the passport rendered the matter academic; and whether the applicant was improperly seeking an oversight role over other constitutional bodies.
legal_principles: A certificate of urgency must be prepared after the legal practitioner has read the founding papers; a certificate predating the founding affidavit is fatally defective. Urgency must be genuine, and self-created urgency is insufficient. Locus standi requires a direct and substantial interest. Mandamus requires a clear or prima facie right, irreparable harm, and no adequate alternative remedy. Constitutional bodies must exercise their functions independently and cannot assume supervisory control over one another absent constitutional or statutory authority.
held_decision_points: The court held that the application was defective and not urgent. ZACC had no locus to seek the supervisory directions it requested against court officials or prosecuting authorities. It also failed to establish the requirements for mandamus. The application was struck off the roll with costs.
ratio_decidendi: ZACC’s investigative mandate did not give it authority to supervise prosecutorial or judicial decision-making after referral for prosecution. Its remedies for accessing the record did not require urgent mandamus, and the urgent foundation was undermined by delay and the defective certificate of urgency.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court emphasized the need for constitutional bodies such as ZACC, the JSC, NPA and court officers to cooperate without unlawfully encroaching on one another’s independence.
cases_referred_to: Document Support Center (Pvt) Ltd v Mapuvire 2006 (1) ZLR 232 (H); Triple C Pigs & Anor v Commissioner-General, ZIMRA HH 7/07; Dilwin Investments (Pvt) Ltd t/a Formscaf v Jopa Engineering Company Ltd HH 116/98; Mlondurago Investments (Pvt) Ltd t/a Mbada Diamonds v Mutual Finance (Pvt) Ltd HH 630/15; ZIMTA & Ors v Minister of Education & Culture 1990 (2) ZLR 48 (HC); Deary N.O. v Acting President & Ors 1979 RLR 200 (G); Chironga & Anor v Minister of Justice & Ors CCZ 14/20; Universal Merchant Bank Zimbabwe Limited v The Zimbabwe Independent & Anor 2000 (1) ZLR 234 (HC).
legislation_considered: Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07]; Anti-Corruption Act; High Court Rules, 2021; Prosecutors’ Guidelines referenced.
constitutional_provisions_considered: Constitution of Zimbabwe, ss 254 and 255; constitutional provisions establishing ZACC, JSC, NPA and prosecutorial functions considered generally.
reviewer_comments: Short filename ZIMBAB~1(1).DOC preserved. Neutral citation field marked Not available because the uploaded filename does not supply a neutral citation, although the judgment heading records HH 756-22.
export_history: Compiled into High_Court_Harare_Batch_27_2022_Law_Report_Drafts.txt.

document_name: MANGEN~1.DOC
case_name: Mangenje v Minister of Lands, Agriculture, Fisheries, Water and Rural Development and 4 Others
subject_area: Land; administrative law; urgent interdict; offer letter and competing lease
catchwords_flynote: Urgent chamber application — interim interdict — offer letter previously confirmed by court — subsequent lease over same land — apprehension of eviction — status quo pending main proceedings.
headnote: The applicant held an offer letter to the Remaining Extent of Stuhm in Goromonzi, whose attempted withdrawal had previously been set aside. A lease over the same land was issued to another party. The applicant sought an urgent interdict preserving the status quo pending challenges to the lease. The court dismissed preliminary points and granted the provisional order.
issues_for_determination: Whether the matter was urgent; whether the applicant had a clear or prima facie right; whether the respondents should be interdicted from acting in respect of the land pending determination of related proceedings.
legal_principles: For an interdict the applicant must show a right, reasonable apprehension of harm, and absence of an adequate alternative remedy. Where rights to land are disputed and a prior court order recognises an offer letter, the court may preserve the status quo to avoid prejudice.
held_decision_points: The preliminary points were dismissed. The applicant established a clear right arising from the offer letter and occupation, a reasonable apprehension of harm, and no adequate alternative remedy. The provisional order was granted.
ratio_decidendi: A competing lease issued over land already subject to an extant, court-recognised offer letter justified interim protection pending final determination of the validity of the lease and related matters.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted that respondents did not adequately address the earlier judgment nullifying withdrawal of the applicant’s offer letter.
cases_referred_to: Setlogelo v Setlogelo 1914 AD 221; HH 377/13 referred to as confirming validity of applicant’s offer letter.
legislation_considered: Administrative Justice Act; land administration law; High Court Rules, 2021.
subject_area_terms: Land; administrative law; urgent interdict; offer letter and competing lease
cases_referred_to_terms: Setlogelo v Setlogelo 1914 AD 221
HH 377/13 referred to as confirming validity of applicant’s offer letter.
legislation_considered_terms: Administrative Justice Act; land administration law

document_name: TARUGA~1(1).DOC
case_name: Tarugarira and Another v Lafarge Cement Zimbabwe Limited and Another
subject_area: Civil procedure; urgent chamber application; stay of execution; rescission of default judgment; self-created urgency
catchwords_flynote: Urgency — stay of execution pending rescission — default judgment — attachment and removal — applicant delaying until after removal — self-created urgency — matter removed from urgent roll.
headnote: The applicants sought urgent stay of execution of a default judgment pending determination of an application for rescission. The first respondent argued that the matter was not urgent because the applicants knew of the default judgment and execution steps but delayed taking urgent action until after removal of property. The court accepted that any urgency was self-created and removed the matter from the urgent roll.
issues_for_determination:
When did the need to act arise?
    Whether the applicants treated the matter as urgent.
    Whether execution of a default judgment pending rescission justified urgent relief.
    Whether urgency was self-created.
legal_principles:
Urgency requires the applicant to show when the need to act arose, irreparable harm if the matter is not heard urgently, and that the applicant acted promptly rather than sitting on its laurels.
    Urgency arising from deliberate or careless abstention until the deadline or event occurs is self-created and not the kind of urgency contemplated by the rules.
held_decision_points:
The need to act arose when the default judgment was granted or, at the latest, when attachment and notice of removal were served.
    The applicants delayed until after removal had occurred.
    The urgency relied upon was self-created.
    The matter was not urgent and was removed from the urgent roll.
ratio_decidendi: The applicants had time to seek urgent relief before execution and removal but failed to do so. Having waited until after the removal had occurred, they could not rely on urgency of their own making.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified.
cases_referred_to: Kuvarega v Registrar General 1998 (1) ZLR 188
legislation_considered: None identified beyond urgent chamber application procedure.
reviewer_comments: Short filename preserved exactly. Neutral citation not supplied in the filename; judgment number taken from the judgment heading. Mounted file was extractable.
subject_area_terms: Civil procedure; urgent chamber application; stay of execution; rescission of default judgment; self-created urgency

document_name: Khaka v Rennie (760 of 760) [2022] ZWHHC 760 (27 October 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: ELIZABETH KHAKA versus PETER FREDERICK RENNIE
subject_area: Civil procedure; contempt of court; enforcement of civil orders; impossibility of compliance; deed of settlement.
catchwords_flynote: Contempt of court – civil order made after deed of settlement – compliance period pre-dated court order – order impossible to perform as framed – no wilful or mala fide non-compliance – application dismissed – each party to bear own costs.
headnote: The applicant sought to have the respondent declared in contempt of a court order arising from a deed of settlement. The order required transfer of Old Mutual shares and valuation of property within time periods calculated from July 2008, although the order itself was granted years later. The Court held that the respondent had notice of the order but that the order, as framed, was impossible to comply with. Because contempt requires wilful and mala fide non-compliance with a capable order, the application was dismissed with each party bearing its own costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the respondent had notice of the order.
    Whether the order was capable of compliance.
    Whether non-compliance was wilful and mala fide.
    Whether a committal order for contempt was warranted.
legal_principles: Civil contempt enforces court orders by coercive means. The applicant must establish a competent court order, service or knowledge of the order, and disobedience. Wilfulness and mala fides may be inferred from proved disobedience, but the respondent may rebut the inference. The law does not compel performance of impossibilities: lex non cogit ad impossibilia.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the order came to the respondent’s attention.
    Held, that the order was impossible to comply with because the compliance periods were calculated from 4 July 2008 although the order was issued years later.
    Held, that contempt and committal were not established.
    Held, that the application was dismissed with each party bearing its own costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio decidendi was that an order incapable of performance as worded cannot ground civil contempt because contempt requires wilful and mala fide disobedience of an order capable of compliance.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted that the order could have been corrected or amended through rule 449 of the old High Court Rules.
cases_referred_to: In Re Chinamasa 2001 (2) SA 902 (ZS); Cape Times Ltd v Union Trades Directories (Pty) Ltd and Others 1956 (1) SA 105 (N); Wiley NO v M 1979 RLR 144 (GD); Mafoshoro Farm (Pvt) Ltd v Nyanhongo & Another HH-32-09; Scheelite King Mining Co. (Pvt) Ltd v Mahachi 1998 (1) ZLR 173 (H); Haddow v Haddow 1974 (1) RLR 5.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 1971, rule 449; common law of contempt of court.
subject_area_terms: Civil procedure; contempt of court; enforcement of civil orders; impossibility of compliance; deed of settlement.

document_name: JK Motors (Private) Limited v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority (762 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 762 (28 October 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: JK MOTORS (PRIVATE) LIMITED versus ZIMBABWE REVENUE AUTHORITY
subject_area: Tax law; income tax assessments; declaratur; invalid assessments; objection and appeal procedures; exhaustion of domestic remedies.
catchwords_flynote: Tax assessments – taxpayer alleging invalidity of assessments – whether challenge must proceed only by statutory objection/appeal – declaratur – invalid assessment distinct from valid but disputed assessment – preliminary points dismissed in substance – application properly before court – costs in cause.
headnote: ZIMRA raised tax assessments and dismissed the applicant’s objections. The applicant approached the High Court seeking a declaration that the assessments were invalid because they did not contain taxable income and improperly assessed penalties. ZIMRA argued that the applicant had to pursue the statutory objection/appeal route and exhaust domestic remedies. The Court held that challenges to invalid assessments may be brought by declaratur and that the application was properly before the court.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the High Court could entertain a declaratory application challenging the validity of tax assessments.
    Whether sections 62 and 65 of the Income Tax Act were the exclusive route.
    Whether the applicant had failed to exhaust domestic remedies.
    Whether an assessment alleged to be invalid can ground objection or appeal proceedings.
legal_principles: Sections 62 and 65 of the Income Tax Act provide remedies for taxpayers aggrieved by assessments, but they do not exclude declaratory relief where the complaint is that no valid assessment exists. A null assessment has no legal consequences and may be challenged through a declaratur where the applicant has a direct and substantial interest.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the application was properly before the court.
    Held, that costs shall be in the cause.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the statutory objection and appeal route applies to valid assessments, but where the complaint is that assessments are invalid or unlawful, a taxpayer may seek declaratory relief in the High Court.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court distinguished cases concerning collection measures and noted the need for a declaration on validity where real legal issues exist between the parties.
cases_referred_to: Nestle Zimbabwe (Private) Limited v ZIMRA SC 148/21; Barclays Bank of Zimbabwe Ltd v ZIMRA; Delta Beverages v ZIMRA HH 811-16; relevant tax-assessment authorities as cited.
legislation_considered: Income Tax Act [Chapter 23:06], sections 62 and 65; Revenue Authority Act [Chapter 23:11], section 3; High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], section 14.
subject_area_terms: Tax law; income tax assessments; declaratur; invalid assessments; objection and appeal procedures; exhaustion of domestic remedies.
cases_referred_to_terms: Nestle Zimbabwe (Private) Limited v ZIMRA SC 148/21
Barclays Bank of Zimbabwe Ltd v ZIMRA
Delta Beverages v ZIMRA HH 811-16; relevant tax-assessment authorities as cited.

document_name: Gombera and 4 Others v Mazowe Rural District Council and Another (776 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 776 (1 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: NEBART GOMBERA and 4 OTHERS versus MAZOWE RURAL DISTRICT COUNCIL and MINISTER OF LOCAL GOVERNMENT AND NATIONAL HOUSING
subject_area: Civil procedure; class actions; access to information; mandamus; hybrid application; rural district council planning documents.
catchwords_flynote: Class action – application for leave to bring class action combined with mandamus for access to planning documents – Class Actions Act requires leave first – hybrid application fatally defective – application dismissed with costs.
headnote: The applicants sought leave to bring a class action and, in the same application, an order compelling the respondents to provide site and master plans relating to Rossa Clinic land. The respondents objected that the application improperly combined leave to bring class-action proceedings with substantive mandamus relief. The Court held that leave to institute a class action must be sought first and, only if granted and statutory requirements are met, may the main proceedings follow. The hybrid application was fatally defective and was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the application was bad at law because it combined leave for a class action with substantive mandamus relief.
    Whether the Department of Physical Planning was fatally misjoined or non-joined.
    Whether domestic remedies had to be exhausted.
    Whether the Court had jurisdiction.
    Whether the requested access-to-information relief could be granted in the same proceedings.
legal_principles: Under the Class Actions Act, a party must first apply for leave to institute a class action. If leave is granted, the Court appoints a suitable representative and the main action can then proceed in compliance with the Act. A combined application for leave and substantive mandamus relief may be fatally defective where it bypasses the statutory sequence.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the application was a hybrid application combining leave to bring a class action and mandamus relief.
    Held, that the applicants should have sought leave to institute class action proceedings first.
    Held, that the application was fatally defective.
    Held, that it was unnecessary to determine the other points in limine or merits.
    Held, that the application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the procedural structure of the Class Actions Act must be followed: leave and appointment of a representative precede the institution of class proceedings, and substantive relief cannot be bundled prematurely into the leave application.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted that once an application is found fatally defective, it is unnecessary to consider remaining preliminary points or the merits.
cases_referred_to: Mark Mupungu v Minister of Justice, Legal and Parliamentary Affairs & Others CCZ 07/21
legislation_considered: Class Actions Act [Chapter 8:17], sections 3 and 5; High Court Rules, 1971, rule 89.
subject_area_terms: Civil procedure; class actions; access to information; mandamus; hybrid application; rural district council planning documents.
cases_referred_to_terms: Mark Mupungu v Minister of Justice, Legal and Parliamentary Affairs & Others CCZ 07/21

document_name: Toyin Trailers (Pty) Ltd v Gelcole Logistics and Another (777 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 777 (2 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Toyin Trailers (Pty) Ltd v Gelcole Logistics (Pvt) Ltd and Another
case_number: HC 7104/22; Ref Case No. HC 7083/22; Ref Case No. HC 5340/22
subject_area: Urgent chamber application; stay of execution; attachment to found jurisdiction; peregrinus defendant; cause of action; property owned by third party; security for costs.
catchwords_flynote: Stay of execution – order attaching property to found jurisdiction – applicant admitted attached trucks belonged to another entity – whether applicant had cause of action – whether relief competent after execution had already occurred – whether peregrinus applicant furnished security for costs.
headnote: The applicant sought stay of execution of a judgment in HC 5340/22 pending a rescission application. The order in question attached property to found jurisdiction against a peregrinus company. The first respondent raised preliminary objections that the applicant had no cause of action, the relief was incompetent because execution had already taken place, and the applicant had not furnished security for costs. The court upheld the objections and dismissed the application.
issues_for_determination: Whether the applicant disclosed a cause of action; whether ex parte attachment to found jurisdiction was permissible; whether stay of execution was competent after execution had already occurred; whether the applicant was protecting its own right or the right of a separate entity; and whether security for costs was required.
legal_principles: Applications are generally made on notice, but may proceed ex parte where notice risks defeating the purpose of the application. An order to found jurisdiction against a peregrinus defendant is not a default judgment in the ordinary sense. A party seeking urgent relief must identify its own legal right to protect. A peregrinus applicant may be required to furnish security for costs.
held_decision_points: The applicant failed to show a cause of action because it admitted that the attached trucks belonged to a separate Mozambican entity. It therefore could not litigate to protect those assets in its own name. The relief sought was also incompetent because execution had already taken place. The applicant had not provided security for costs. The application was dismissed.
ratio_decidendi: A litigant cannot seek stay or vindication of property rights in assets it admits belong to a separate juristic person without showing authority or its own protectable legal interest.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The judgment clarified that ex parte applications may be justified where notice would risk defeating the purpose of attachment to found jurisdiction.
cases_referred_to: HMA 05/22 was referred to; no full case name identified in the extracted text.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, rr 29, 57(1)(a), 60(3)(c); principles on attachment to found jurisdiction and security for costs.
constitutional_provisions_considered: Constitutional right to be heard was raised by the applicant but did not determine the result.

document_name: Magora and 33 Others v Runde Rural District and 6 OthersCouncil and (799 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 799 (2 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Magora and 33 Others v Runde Rural District Council and 6 Others
subject_area: Civil procedure; res judicata; special plea; communal land/urban expansion dispute
catchwords_flynote: Res judicata — dismissal of earlier action — default judgment — same parties and cause of action — fresh summons instead of rescission — abuse of process — costs on attorney-client scale.
headnote: The plaintiffs, residents of Mudereri Village, challenged alleged annexation of their land and sale to FBC Building Society. The defendants raised special pleas of res judicata, relying on earlier proceedings under HC 1018/20 and HC 2744/20 in which the plaintiffs’ earlier claim had been dismissed. The court held that the plaintiffs could not bring fresh proceedings while the earlier default dismissal remained extant.
issues_for_determination: Whether the plaintiffs’ fresh claim was barred by res judicata; whether the previous dismissal required rescission before the same cause could be litigated again.
legal_principles: A default judgment remains binding until rescinded. Where a matter between the same parties on the same cause has been disposed of, the affected party must first deal with that judgment before instituting fresh proceedings. Res judicata promotes finality in litigation.
held_decision_points: The special pleas succeeded. The plaintiffs’ claims were dismissed, and costs were awarded against them on the attorney-client scale.
ratio_decidendi: The existence of an extant judgment dismissing the plaintiffs’ earlier claim meant that they could not re-litigate the same dispute by issuing fresh proceedings.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court criticised the plaintiffs’ conduct as calculated to deceive the court and deserving censure by punitive costs.
cases_referred_to: Makoto v Mahwe CCZ 03/20; Darare & Anor v Chiyangwa, discussed; other authorities on res judicata and default judgments considered in the full text.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules; principles governing special pleas and dismissal proceedings.

document_name: OTTODR~1(1).DOC
case_name: Otto Drives Construction (Private) Limited v Shanes Autoelectrics and Others
subject_area: Civil procedure; summary judgment; eviction from leased premises; commercial tenancy.
catchwords_flynote: Summary judgment; timing before pre-trial conference; quasi-mutual assent; statutory tenancy; tacit relocation; improvement lien; unliquidated holding-over damages.
headnote: The plaintiff sought summary judgment in an eviction claim and abandoned the holding-over damages component. The defendants raised several defences, including quasi-mutual assent, statutory tenancy, tacit relocation, improvement lien and reliance on the Contractual Penalties Act. The court held that the defendants had raised plausible defences and that the plaintiff could not selectively pursue only part of the relief in summary judgment in the manner adopted. Summary judgment was refused.
issues_for_determination: Whether the application for summary judgment was properly before the court; whether the defendants disclosed bona fide defences; whether partial summary judgment could be sought by omitting the unliquidated claim from the summons claim.
legal_principles: Summary judgment is a drastic procedure and the defendant’s threshold is low: it is enough to show a plausible or bona fide defence that may succeed at trial. The summary-judgment claim must correspond to the claim in the summons subject to the court’s power to grant part of the claim where appropriate.
held_decision_points: The defences of tacit relocation and statutory tenancy were plausible. The improvement-lien and Contractual Penalties Act issues were matters for trial. The application was ill-conceived and was dismissed.
ratio_decidendi: Summary judgment should not be granted where the defendant has disclosed plausible defences and the matter requires ventilation at trial.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court indicated that the meaning of a pre-trial conference being “held” for purposes of rule 30 required proper ventilation.
cases_referred_to: Musgrove & Watson (Rhod) (Pvt) Ltd v Rotta 1978 (2) SA 918 (R); Kazingizi and Anor v Equity Properties (Pvt) Ltd HH 797-15; Bastin v Madzima N.O. SC 37-20; Hughes v Donenek Investments (Pvt) Ltd 1998 (2) ZLR (H).
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, rule 30; Commercial Premises (Rent) Regulations, 1983; Contractual Penalties Act [Chapter 8:04].
constitutional_provisions_considered: Section 134 of the Constitution was discussed in relation to constitutional invalidity of delegated legislation.

document_name: Mpepereki v Mpepereki and 2 Others (781 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 781 (3 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Mpepereki v Mpepereki and 2 Others
subject_area: Deceased estates; urgent chamber application; interim interdict; executrix testamentary; disputed will; suspension of letters of administration; immovable property transfer.
catchwords_flynote: Urgent chamber application – deceased estate – disputed will – surviving spouse – sale and proposed transfer of immovable property – suspension of executrix’s powers – interim interdict pending declaratur proceedings.
headnote: The applicant, surviving spouse of the late Sheunesu Mpepereki, sought urgent interim relief to stop transfer of 22 Dulverton Road, Glen Lorne and to suspend the first respondent’s powers as executrix testamentary pending determination of proceedings challenging the authenticity of the will under HC 2782/22. The first respondent raised preliminary points including lack of urgency, incompetence of suspending an executrix instead of removing her, non-joinder and wrong procedure. The court dismissed the preliminary points. On the merits, the court held that the applicant had established a prima facie right open to some doubt, a well-grounded apprehension of irreparable harm and that the balance of convenience favoured interim protection of the estate pending resolution of the disputed will.
issues_for_determination: Whether the application was urgent; whether the court could suspend the executrix testamentary and restrain estate administration pending the declaratur action; whether non-joinder of the purchaser was fatal; whether the applicant established the requirements for interim relief.
legal_principles: The High Court has inherent jurisdiction except where limited by law. Interim interdict requirements are a prima facie right, though open to some doubt, a well-grounded apprehension of irreparable harm, absence of an adequate ordinary remedy, and balance of convenience.
held_decision_points: The preliminary points were dismissed. Interim relief was granted interdicting transfer of the Glen Lorne property, temporarily suspending the letters of administration and restraining the first respondent from performing functions as executrix pending determination of the matter and the related declaratur action.
ratio_decidendi: Because the authenticity of the will was under active challenge and the property was the remaining substantial estate asset, transfer before determination of the challenge could cause irreparable prejudice. Any prejudice to the executrix or purchaser could be managed, while loss of the property might leave the surviving spouse without effective relief.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted that the question whether the property was in fact the matrimonial home went to the merits and did not defeat the prima facie right required for interim relief.
cases_referred_to: Guwa & Anor v Willoughby’s Investments 2009 (1) ZLR 380 (S); Telecel Zimbabwe (Pvt) Ltd v Postal and Telecommunications Regulatory Authority of Zimbabwe & 3 Ors HH 446/15; Ericksen Motors (Welkom) Ltd v Protea Motors, Warrenton and Anor 1973 (3) SA 685 (A); Charuma Blasting and Earthmoving Services (Pvt) Ltd v Njainjai and Ors 2000 (1) ZLR 85 (S); Setlogelo v Setlogelo 1914 AD 221.
legislation_considered: Administration of Estates Act [Chapter 6:01]; High Court Rules, 2021.
reviewer_comments: Draft. The heading date line states “HARARE, 10, 12 & 3 November 2022”; treated as hearings on 10 and 12 October with judgment on 3 November 2022 based on the body of the judgment.
export_history: Created in High_Court_Harare_Batch_14_2022_Law_Report_Drafts.txt from the supplied source document.

document_name: S v Chieza (789 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 789 (4 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Chieza
hearing_date: Not applicable; criminal review determined on the record
subject_area: Criminal review – Railways Act – possession of railway material – evidential insufficiency – mandatory sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal review; Railways Act; possession; railway sleepers; wrong statutory section; unreliability of State case; conviction and sentence not in accordance with real and substantial justice.
headnote: The accused was convicted after trial for contravening s 38(4)(a) of the Railways Act and sentenced to the mandatory five-year term. The review court noted repeated confusion in the trial record between s 38(2)(a), s 38(4)(a) and penalty provisions, and material inconsistencies between the State outline and oral evidence.
issues_for_determination: Whether the accused was proved to have possessed railway property; whether the trial proceedings, conviction and mandatory sentence were in accordance with real and substantial justice.
legal_principles: A conviction requiring proof of possession must establish the requisite physical or mental control and knowledge. Review intervention is warranted where conviction and sentence are not in accordance with real and substantial justice.
held_decision_points: The court held that the State had not proved possession or knowledge beyond reasonable doubt. The conviction and sentence were quashed.
ratio_decidendi: Mere implication by third parties and subsequent sale allegations cannot substitute for proof of possession and knowledge of stolen railway property.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court criticised the lackadaisical preparation of State papers and repeated citation of incorrect statutory provisions in a serious offence attracting a stiff mandatory penalty.
cases_referred_to: S v Mupfumburi HH 64-14; State v Chikumbirike; State v Smith; State v Young; R v Pham; S v Chikanga; S v Mutsure SC, as referenced in the full text.
legislation_considered: Railways Act [Chapter 13:09], ss 38(2)(a), 38(3), 38(4)(a), 39; Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07].

document_name: DIVVYM~1(1).DOC
case_name: DIVVYMAN ENTERPRISES (PVT) LTD versus CESPERK MARKETING (PVT) LTD and EDMORE MAKUREYA
subject_area: Rescission of default judgment; pre-trial conference default; fraud by employee; companies; presumption of regularity; estoppel; COBA.
catchwords_flynote: Rescission – applicant defaulted at pre-trial conference after road map agreed before judge – negligence by legal practitioners – fraud by former employee central to merits – defence not hopeless because presumption of regularity subject to knowledge/reasonableness proviso – rescission granted with costs against applicant.
headnote: The applicant sought rescission of default judgment entered after it failed to comply with pre-trial directions and missed a pre-trial conference. The underlying dispute concerned goods allegedly procured fraudulently by its former employee from the first respondent. The Court accepted that there had been negligence and criticised the failure to follow clear court directions, but held that the applicant’s defence was not hopeless because both parties had been defrauded and the Companies and Other Business Entities Act presumption of regularity contains a proviso where the outsider knew or ought reasonably to have known the contrary. Rescission was granted, with the applicant paying costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant was in wilful default.
    Whether negligence by legal practitioners should bar rescission.
    Whether the applicant had a bona fide defence with prospects of success.
    Whether section 24 of the Companies and Other Business Entities Act bound the applicant despite the alleged fraud.
    What costs order was appropriate.
legal_principles: Rescission requires good and sufficient cause, including a reasonable explanation for default and a bona fide defence with prospects of success. Section 24 of the Companies and Other Business Entities Act creates a presumption of regularity and estoppel in business dealings, but the proviso excludes reliance where the third party had actual knowledge to the contrary or ought reasonably to have known the contrary. Fraud and estoppel issues are best resolved at trial where disputed facts exist.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant and its legal practitioners were negligent in failing to comply with the directions and attend the pre-trial conference.
    Held, that the default was not so gross as to make rescission impossible.
    Held, that the applicant’s defence was not baseless or hopeless because the fraud and COBA presumption required trial consideration.
    Held, that good and sufficient cause for rescission was shown.
    Held, that the default judgment should be set aside, but the applicant should pay costs on the ordinary scale.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that even where an applicant is blameworthy for procedural default, rescission may be granted if the underlying defence is bona fide and raises triable issues, especially where fraud and the statutory presumption of regularity require fuller factual determination.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that section 24 of COBA both protects outsiders dealing with companies and encourages diligence by parties in commercial transactions.
cases_referred_to: Authorities on rescission of judgment and pre-trial default; statutory reference to Companies and Other Business Entities Act principles.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, rule 49 and rule 52; Companies and Other Business Entities Act [Chapter 24:31], section 24(1) and (2).
subject_area_terms: Rescission of default judgment; pre-trial conference default; fraud by employee; companies; presumption of regularity; estoppel
cases_referred_to_terms: Authorities on rescission of judgment and pre-trial default; statutory reference to Companies and Other Business Entities Act principles.
legislation_considered_terms: High Court Rules, 2021, rule 49 and rule 52

document_name: Mudungwe and Another v Photo and 2 Others (791 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 791 (7 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Mudungwe and Another v Photo and Others
case_number: HC 4353/21 consolidated with HC 4383/21; Cross-ref HC 4936/20 and HC 2258/21
subject_area: Civil procedure; rescission of judgment; points in limine; material non-disclosure; preliminary objections; land allocation dispute.
catchwords_flynote: Rescission – default judgment over Carrick Creagh stand – allegations of judgment snatching – points in limine intertwined with merits – rolled-up approach – ruling on points deferred.
headnote: The applicants sought rescission of a default judgment confirming the first respondent’s rights over Stand 194 Carrick Creagh. The first respondent raised preliminary objections including incompetence of the relief and material non-disclosure. The court held that the alleged non-disclosure, competence of relief and related questions were inseparable from the merits, particularly given contested affidavits by legal practitioners. The court deferred determination of the preliminary objections to be argued together with the merits.
issues_for_determination: Whether the points in limine could be determined separately; whether alleged material non-disclosure and incompetent relief could dispose of the application without considering the merits.
legal_principles: Preliminary points should be taken only when meritorious and capable of disposing of the matter. Where preliminary objections are intertwined with the merits, the court may defer or roll them over for determination with the merits.
held_decision_points: Determination of the points in limine was deferred, with costs in the cause, for determination with the merits.
ratio_decidendi: The factual disputes and allegations underlying the preliminary objections were so closely connected to the merits that they could not fairly be separated at the preliminary stage.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court warned against routine, unmeritorious preliminary objections and noted that legal practitioners may be personally penalised for abusing points in limine.
cases_referred_to: Exavier Maoneke v Trustees of Mount Olive Trust HH 640-22; Mandaza v Mzilikazi Investments (Private) Limited HB 23-07; Mudekunye & Ors v Mudekunye & Ors HH 190-10; Chafanza v Edgars Stores Ltd & Anor 2005 (1) ZLR 301 (H); TFS Management Co (Pvt) Ltd v Graspeak (Pvt) Ltd & Another 2005 (1) ZLR 333; Zimbabwe Banking Corporation Limited v Trust Finance Limited & Another 2006 (2) ZLR 404; Telecel Zimbabwe (Pvt) Ltd v POTRAZ & Ors HH 446-15.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021.

document_name: Judicial Service Commission v Ndewere (792 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 792 (9 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: JUDICIAL SERVICE COMMISSION versus ERICA FUNGAI NDEWERE
subject_area: Civil procedure; preliminary objection; authority to institute proceedings; Judicial Service Commission; rei vindicatio; service vehicle.
catchwords_flynote: Rei vindicatio proceedings by JSC – respondent challenging authority of JSC Secretary – validity of JSC resolution – issue previously considered in related litigation – preliminary objection dismissed – costs in the cause.
headnote: The JSC instituted rei vindicatio proceedings to recover a service vehicle from a former judge. The respondent objected that the JSC Secretary lacked authority because the resolution relied upon was invalid or insufficient. The Court held that the challenge had no substance, given the validity of the resolution and its recognition in prior High Court, Supreme Court and Constitutional Court litigation. The preliminary objection was disallowed with costs in the cause.
issues_for_determination:
Whether there was a valid application before the court.
    Whether the JSC Secretary had authority to institute or depose in the proceedings.
    Whether the impugned JSC resolution was valid.
    Whether prior decisions had already resolved the same challenge.
legal_principles: A legal entity acts through authorised natural persons. A challenge to authority must be substantiated. Where a resolution authorising representation has been considered and accepted in related litigation by superior courts, a bare repeated challenge may be dismissed.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the challenge to the JSC resolution and to the Secretary’s authority had no substance.
    Held, that the preliminary objections raised by the respondent were disallowed.
    Held, that costs shall be in the cause.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the JSC Secretary’s authority under the impugned resolution had already been established in related litigation and the respondent provided no adequate basis to invalidate it in the present rei vindicatio proceedings.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that if the same resolution was adequate in matters concerning the removal of a judge, it was a fortiori sufficient for a lesser dispute concerning recovery of an asset.
cases_referred_to: Bere v JSC & 7 Ors HH 269-20; Bere v JSC & 7 Ors HH 510-20; Bere v JSC & 5 Ors SC 1-22; Bere v JSC & 6 Ors CCZ 10-22; Ndewere v Judicial Service Commission SC 113-22; Dube v Premier Service Medical Aid Society & Anor SC 73/19.
legislation_considered: Constitution of Zimbabwe, 2013, sections 187(8), 189 and 191; Judicial Service Act [Chapter 7:18], section 10(2); Companies and Other Business Entities Act [Chapter 24:31] as discussed.

document_name: Hunyenye v Kambani (793 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 793 (8 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: LASSIE SANDRA HUNYENYE versus TOBIAS TOBAIWA KAMBANI
subject_area: Civil procedure; pleadings; pre-trial conference; defective summons and amendments; self-actor litigation; case management.
catchwords_flynote: Pre-trial conference – pleadings muddled, unclear and argumentative – repeated amendments and unclear cause of action – court unable to conduct proper pre-trial conference – plaintiff ordered to regularise pleadings – filing restrictions imposed – Registrar directed not to accept irregular filings.
headnote: The matter was referred for pre-trial conference, but the record and pleadings were so confused that the Court could not identify the proper summons, declaration or issues for trial. The plaintiff, acting in person, had filed multiple amendments and unclear documents. The Court ordered the plaintiff to file proper amended summons and declaration meeting the Rules and a clear list of issues. She was barred from filing further documents outside the Rules or the court’s directions.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the matter was ready for pre-trial conference.
    What pleading constituted the operative summons and declaration.
    Whether the plaintiff’s filings complied with the High Court Rules.
    What case-management directions were necessary to regularise the matter.
legal_principles: Pleadings must clearly define the cause of action and issues to be tried. A pre-trial conference requires coherent pleadings and properly identified disputes. The court has case-management powers under the High Court Rules to control its process, direct amendments and prevent abuse or disorderly filing.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the pleadings were unclear, incoherent and confused.
    Held, that the plaintiff must file an amended summons and declaration complying with the Rules.
    Held, that the plaintiff must file a clear list of issues for determination.
    Held, that the plaintiff was barred from filing any other pleadings or documents except as directed or specifically provided by the Rules.
    Held, that the Registrar should not accept irregular filings contrary to the ruling.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that a court cannot meaningfully conduct pre-trial case management where pleadings do not disclose a clear cause of action or identify issues; the court may therefore direct regularisation and restrict further irregular filings to protect orderly process.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court commented that the plaintiff’s filings were inelegantly drafted, argumentative and vexatious, making the operative pleading impossible to identify.
cases_referred_to: None identified
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, rules 41 and 49(8), and general case-management powers.
subject_area_terms: Civil procedure; pleadings; pre-trial conference; defective summons and amendments; self-actor litigation; case management.
cases_referred_to_terms: None identified

document_name: SHAMIN~1.DOC
case_name: Shamina Holdings (Private) Limited v Tribac Tobacco (Private) Limited and 2 Others
subject_area: Contract; specific performance; title deeds; loan repayment; SI 33 of 2019; currency conversion; separate corporate personality
catchwords_flynote: Specific performance - loan advanced in United States dollars before 22 February 2019 - repayment after SI 33 of 2019 - one-to-one conversion - title deeds held as security - applicant discharged obligations - title deeds to be returned - counter-application dismissed.
headnote: The first respondent advanced US$50,000 to the applicant, secured by the applicant’s title deeds. The applicant made repayments partly in US dollars and partly in Zimbabwe dollars after the promulgation of SI 33 of 2019. The respondents argued that repayment after 22 February 2019 had to be calculated at the interbank rate and counterclaimed for a further amount. The court applied Zambezi Gas and held that the debt was a pre-effective-date domestic obligation converted at one-to-one. The applicant had discharged the debt and was entitled to return of its title deeds.
issues_for_determination: Whether the second and third respondents were properly joined; whether the applicant had discharged the loan; what exchange rate applied after 22 February 2019; whether specific performance should be granted; and whether the counter-application had merit.
legal_principles: A company is a separate legal person and its directors or employees are not ordinarily liable for acts of the company. Pre-22 February 2019 assets and liabilities valued in US dollars were converted to RTGS/Zimbabwe dollars at one-to-one under SI 33 of 2019 and s 22 of the Finance (No. 2) Act, except for excluded foreign obligations.
held_decision_points: The first respondent was ordered to surrender the title deeds held under Deed of Transfer 4373/2012. Any encumbrance placed on the property was set aside. The counter-application was dismissed. The first respondent was ordered to pay costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that the applicant’s pre-effective-date domestic loan obligation was discharged at one-to-one conversion, so retention of title deeds as security was no longer justified.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court distinguished foreign obligations from domestic obligations and held that Breastplate did not displace Zambezi Gas on the facts.
cases_referred_to: Zambezi Gas Zimbabwe (Pvt) Ltd v N.R. Barber (Pvt) Ltd & Anor SC 3/20; Breastplate Service (Private) Limited v Cambria Africa PLC SC 66/20; Star African Corporation Ltd v Zimbabwe Sugar Refineries Workers Union SC 65/21.
legislation_considered: Companies and Other Business Entities Act [Chapter 24:31], s 19; Statutory Instrument 33 of 2019, s 4; Finance (No. 2) Act, 2019, s 22; Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe Act provisions referred to in SI 33.
reviewer_comments: Exact short filename SHAMIN~1.DOC preserved. The mounted file is a legacy binary DOC and was extracted with antiword. Neutral citation field marked Not available because no neutral citation appears in the uploaded filename.

document_name: TWENTY~1.DOC
case_name: Twenty Third Century (Private) Limited v Minister of Higher and Tertiary Education, Innovation Science and Technology Development N.O. and Another
subject_area: Public procurement; urgent chamber application; suspension and cancellation of tender process; SAP software support services; alternative remedies; pending procurement appeal; restrictive tender conditions; costs.
catchwords_flynote: Public procurement – tender process – challenge to eligibility requirement that bidders hold SAP licence – prior review judgment setting aside award and review panel decision – appeal under procurement statute still pending – new tender process commenced before earlier statutory process was completed – whether new process should be suspended, cancelled and nullified – whether alternative remedy adequate and effective – whether non-joinder of ZIMDEF fatal.
headnote: The applicant sought urgent relief suspending a fresh procurement process for SAP software application support services under Domestic ZPCR 33-2022 and, on the return date, nullification and cancellation of that process. The application followed an earlier review judgment which had set aside the award of a similar tender and the decision of the Procurement Regulatory Authority review panel. The applicant argued that the first respondent could not lawfully start a new tender process containing the same contested SAP licence requirement while the statutory challenge and appeal process from the earlier tender had not been finalized. The first respondent raised preliminary objections, including non-joinder, improper approach to the court and absence of cause of action. The court dismissed the points in limine and held that the pending statutory process had to be completed before a new tender process could be instituted in a manner that effectively overrode it.
issues_for_determination: Whether ZIMDEF had to be joined separately; whether the applicant was bound to exhaust the statutory procurement challenge process before approaching the High Court; whether the application disclosed a cause of action; whether the first respondent could institute a fresh tender process while the earlier procurement appeal remained unresolved; and whether the tender process under ZPCR33-2022 should be suspended, cancelled and nullified.
legal_principles: A matter is not defeated by non-joinder where the court can determine the issues between the parties before it, and a party who considers another party necessary may apply for joinder. A statutory domestic remedy must be reasonable, adequate and effective; the existence of such a remedy does not always bar urgent High Court intervention. A cause of action consists of the facts necessary to support the relief sought. A procuring entity may not impose unduly restrictive eligibility criteria, and it may not bypass an unresolved statutory challenge or appeal by commencing a fresh procurement process which effectively circumvents the pending statutory process.
held_decision_points: The preliminary points were dismissed. The court held that ZIMDEF was not a necessary separate party in the circumstances and that, in any event, non-joinder was not fatal. The internal statutory remedy was not adequate or effective where the previous appeal remained unresolved and the new tender process would undercut it. The application disclosed a cause of action. On the merits, the first respondent could not lawfully commence the new tender process while the earlier appeal relating to the same disputed SAP licence condition remained undetermined. The tender process under Domestic ZPCR 33-2022 was cancelled and nullified.
ratio_decidendi: The decisive reason was that the earlier tender challenge process remained incomplete, and the first respondent’s new tender sought to proceed on the same disputed condition before the statutory appeal was determined. Commencing and defending the new process in those circumstances amounted to an unlawful circumvention of the unresolved procurement process.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The judgment emphasized that a statutory remedy must be effective in practice, and criticized the first respondent’s decision to oppose relief where the earlier statutory appeal had not been determined.

document_name: Apostolic Faith Mission in Zimbabwe v Nyahwema (796 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 796 (9 November 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: APOSTOLIC FAITH MISSION IN ZIMBABWE versus SIMON NYAHWEMA
citation: Apostolic Faith Mission in Zimbabwe v Nyahwema (796 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 796 (9 November 2022)
subject_area: Property law; rei vindicatio; eviction; church property; disputes of fact in motion proceedings; religious association split.
catchwords_flynote: Rei vindicatio – church seeking eviction from mission house and church building – respondent claiming community property and material disputes of fact – bare denial insufficient – common cause that property belonged to applicant before church split and respondent was deployed without applicant's authority – respondent conceded no legal relationship with applicant – applicant proved ownership and unlawful occupation – eviction granted with legal practitioner-client costs.
headnote: The applicant sought eviction of the respondent and those claiming through him from Chizinga Assembly at Chizinga Shopping Centre, Mhondoro. The respondent raised a point in limine alleging material disputes of fact and claiming the property was community property. The Court rejected the point, finding that the respondent had made bare denials and had not shown a bona fide dispute requiring oral evidence. On the merits, the applicant proved ownership and possession by respondent without consent. The respondent had no legal relationship with the applicant after the church split and had been deployed by a dissident pastor without authority. The application succeeded and the respondent was ordered to vacate within 48 hours, with punitive costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether there were material disputes of fact incapable of resolution on affidavit.
    Whether the applicant proved ownership or entitlement to vindicate the property.
    Whether the respondent had a right to retain occupation.
    Whether the applicant was entitled to eviction through rei vindicatio.
    Whether punitive costs were warranted.
legal_principles: A respondent alleging disputes of fact in motion proceedings must show a bona fide dispute with clear and cogent detail; bare denials do not suffice. Under rei vindicatio, an owner proves ownership and possession by another, after which the possessor must prove a right to retain possession. A member or pastor of a splinter group has no right to retain property belonging to the original voluntary association merely because of previous association with it.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the respondent's alleged dispute of fact was a bare denial and did not require trial evidence.
    Held, that the applicant proved its entitlement to the property.
    Held, that the respondent had occupied without applicant's authority and had no legal relationship with the applicant.
    Held, that the respondent was unlawfully occupying the premises.
    Held, that the application for eviction should succeed.
    Held, that the respondent should pay costs on a legal practitioner-client scale.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that an owner or entitled association may vindicate its property from a splinter-group occupant who has no legal relationship with it, and bare assertions of community ownership do not create material disputes of fact.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court emphasised a robust common-sense approach to disputes of fact on motion, to avoid defeat or delay of justice by bare denial.
