document_name: Warren Hills Golf Club v Sunshine Developments (Pvt) Ltd and Another (797 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 797 (7 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Warren Hills Golf Club v Sunshine Developments (Pvt) Ltd and Another
court:
a.   The land in question is municipal land owned by the City of Harare and       could only be disposed of in terms of the Urban Councils Act, which was not done in that;
    i. 	There is no resolution from the Finance Committee of the City of Harare allowing the sale;
    ii.	No resolution from the full council approving or ratifying the sale;
    iii.	No advertisement of the sale in any newspaper
    iv.	No procedures followed for the change of use of the land from recreational to residential.

    b.   There was no purchase price received by the City of Harare for the sale of
            the land
    c.   The purchase price mentioned in the title deed of ZW$30,000,000,000
          (Thirty billion) was in September 2008 equivalent to USD50,000 and no
          reasonable person could have approved the sale of prime public land at such  an extremely low price;
    d.  The people who signed the memorandum of agreement and shareholders
          agreement on behalf of the City of Harare with the main shareholder of the
          Plaintiff, Augur Investments, had no authority to do so from the City of
          Harare making the entire agreement invalid.
    e.   Even if such agreement was valid, Augur Investments, failed to perform in
          terms of such agreement, in particular they failed to “provide funding for
          the project in an amount between US$20,000,000(twenty million united
          states dollars) and US$30,000,000 (Thirty million United States Dollars)”
          meaning that the property was transferred without value.
    f.    Augur Investments, failed to comply with the provisions of the then
           Zimbabwe Investment Authority Act which makes it mandatory for
           investors to obtain an investment licence.  Where an investor has no licence,
           it cannot invest in Zimbabwe.
    g.   The development of municipal land is a service that ought to have proceeded
          through public procurements processes in terms of the then Procurement
          Act [Chapter 22:14] this was not done making the entire process illegal,
          null and void.
    		h.   The main shareholder Augur Investments misrepresented that it was a
           company registered in Ukraine yet it is in fact a company registered in
           Estonia.  The Company itself did not exist at the time the Shareholders
           agreement was signed.
    i.     The conveyancer, Theophilus Pharaoh Gambe, could not have lawfully
           transferred the property to the Plaintiff in terms of a general power of
           attorney executed in 1998 as this is prohibited by section 28 of the Deeds
           Registries Regulations, 1977 which required general powers of attorney to
           be specific.
    	AD PARA 4
    3.	It is correct that the 1st Defendant is in occupation of the property by virtue of a lease agreement granted to it by the City of Harare.  The lease agreement expired on 30 April 2019 but was further renewed up to April 2039.  The renewal is valid as the title and authority over the land remained with City of Harare in light of the fact that transfer to the Plaintiff was done fraudulently and corruptly.
    4.	Under case number HC549/2021 the Plaintiff’s title is being lawfully challenged in the matter of Zimbabwe Homeless People Federation & Others v City of Harare & Others.  Should that matter be decided in favour of the Applicant’s, the title in which Plaintiff base their rights will be restored to the City of Harare thereby extinguishing their cause of action.
    AD PARA 5 – 6
    5.	As stated above, 1st Defendant holds a valid lease which expired in 2039.  Such lease is valid as the property in question belongs to the City of Harare and not the Plaintiff. The 1st Defendant is therefore not in unlawful occupation.
    AD PARA 7
    6.	Holding over damages if any, can only be ascertained from current rentals which currently stand at Z$460.
    Wherefore, Defendant prays that the claim be dismissed with costs.”
    (my emphasis)
    THE LAW
    Rule 41 of the High Court Rules, 2021 allows a party to amend its pleading or document at any stage before judgment on notice to the other party.  The granting of an application for the amendment of a pleading is a matter for the discretion of the Court, to be exercised judiciously in light of the circumstances of the particular case.  An amendment will be allowed in order to afford proper ventilation of the controversy between the parties and by extension enable the Court to determine the real issues between them, so that the key obligation of the Court, which is to do justice between parties, is fully discharged.
    No amendment will be allowed in circumstances where;
     (a) It will cause prejudice to the other party as cannot be cured by an appropriate order for costs.
    (b) Is not accompanied by a satisfactory explanation.
    (c) Has no triable issue.
    (d) It is mala fide.
    (e) It is not sought timeously.
    This list is not exhaustive. The tendency in this jurisdiction is in favor of granting the application for an amendment, if it is found that it stands to assist in the ventilation of the issues between the parties and by extension assist in their ultimate determination
    See UDC Ltd -v- Shamva Flora (Pvt) Ltd 2000 (2) ZLR 210(HC), Mashonaland Turf Club -v- Susan Peters and Anor HH 07/21.
    RESOLUTION
    In this case, the amendment was not timeously sought.  The reasons given for that, are that the amendment sought was a result of the opinion of the 1st Defendant’s current counsel and that when the plea was filed the 1st Defendant was relying on the presumed expertise of some of its members in legal matters. A look at the amendment sought to be made reveals that it amends nothing but wipes the slate clean and substitutes the plea in its entirety. If the purported amendment is allowed the matter will be as good as starting afresh on the eve of the trial.
    In my view, what is sought to be introduced as an amendment of, or worse still, the substitution of the plea, was always known to the 1st Defendant. Whoever was assisting the 1st Defendant up to the Pre Trial stage could not have missed it, regardless of the degree of his or her knowledge of the law judging from how the pleadings were done and managed up to the Pre-Trial stage. In fact, the purported new plea is a detail or evidence of what the extant plea states and nothing else. Before Advocate Musarurwa came on board in this matter, Mr. Nyamayemombe was already representing the 1st Defendant and honestly, he could and should have picked the deficiencies in the Plea, if any, timeously. I am not convinced that the application is made with the requisite bona fides. I am not convinced by the explanation given either.
    In my view, all considered, from the Summons to the issues referred for trial, the extant plea allows the parties to ventilate their dispute, it will not hinder this Court from judiciously determining the real issue(s) between them. On the other hand, allowing the purported amendment to be made, will turn the matter on its head.  There is no value to be gained by any of the parties or the Court from allowing the amendment to take place.
    I, therefore, order as follows: -
    IT IS HEREBY ORDERED THAT: -
    1	 The application to amend the 1st Defendant’s plea in case number HC 6212/20 be and is hereby dismissed.
    2. 	There is no order as to costs.

    M. C Mukome, Applicant’s Legal Practitioners.
    Chinawa Law Chambers, 1st Respondent’s Legal Practitioners.
    Mbidzo, Muchadehama & Makoni, 2nd Respondent’s Legal Practitioners.
subject_area: Civil procedure; amendment of pleadings; application to amend plea; late amendment; bona fides; trial readiness; municipal land dispute; fraud and corruption allegations.
catchwords_flynote: Amendment of plea – application brought on eve of trial – proposed amendment deleting entire plea and substituting new factual allegations – whether amendment sought timeously and in good faith – whether extant plea already allowed ventilation of real dispute – application dismissed.
headnote: The applicant, defendant in the main matter, sought leave to amend its plea shortly before trial. The proposed amendment would delete the existing plea in its entirety and replace it with detailed allegations of fraud, corruption and irregularity in the transfer of municipal land. The court held that the amendment was not sought timeously, that the explanation was unsatisfactory, and that the existing plea already allowed the real issues to be ventilated. The application was dismissed with no order as to costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether leave to amend the plea should be granted under r 41; whether the amendment was timeously sought; whether the explanation for delay was satisfactory; whether the amendment introduced triable issues or merely evidence/detail; whether it was bona fide; and whether allowing it would prejudice the other parties and derail trial.
legal_principles: A court has a discretion to allow amendment of pleadings where it assists proper ventilation of the real issues. An amendment may be refused where it causes incurable prejudice, lacks a satisfactory explanation, discloses no triable issue, is mala fide, or is not sought timeously. The general tendency favours allowing amendments, but the court must exercise its discretion judicially in the circumstances.
held_decision_points: The court refused the amendment. The proposed new plea would effectively restart the matter on the eve of trial. The facts were always known to the applicant, and the explanation that current counsel advised differently was unsatisfactory. The extant plea was sufficient to ventilate the dispute.
ratio_decidendi: The proposed amendment was not a genuine late discovery of a necessary issue but a wholesale substitution of the defence with detail that could and should have been pleaded earlier. Allowing it would disrupt trial without adding value to determination of the real dispute.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified beyond the court’s caution against wholesale late amendments framed as plea refinement.

document_name: Nebula Agencies (Pvt) Ltd v French & Smith T_A Customs Services (798 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 798 (9 November 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: Nebula Agencies (Pvt) Ltd v French & Smith t/a Customs Services
subject_area: Contract; specific performance; damages for breach; carriage/clearing services; motion proceedings; disputes of fact.
catchwords_flynote: Specific performance – damaged imported PPGI coils during transport – respondent’s prior acknowledgement of liability – alleged ex gratia assistance rejected – specific performance and damages may both be granted where not mutually exclusive.
headnote: The applicant imported PPGI sheets and engaged the respondent to clear and transport them from Beira to Harare. The respondent’s vehicle was involved in an accident and the sheets were damaged beyond use. The respondent initially acknowledged liability and promised replacement, but later denied liability after lawyers became involved. The court held that the alleged disputes of fact were not genuine, that the respondent had not contracted out of liability, and that the applicant could claim both specific performance and damages for delay. The application succeeded.
issues_for_determination: Whether real disputes of fact required action proceedings; whether the respondent had contracted out of liability; whether its prior conduct amounted only to ex gratia assistance; whether specific performance and damages could be claimed together.
legal_principles: A dispute of fact must be real, genuine and not fanciful or an afterthought. Specific performance and damages for delay or consequential loss may be claimed together where the reliefs are not mutually exclusive and do not overcompensate the innocent party. Contractual damages aim to place the innocent party in the position it would have occupied had the contract been properly performed.
held_decision_points: The respondent was ordered to deliver seven rolls of Pre-Painted Galvanised Iron within seven days of service of the order, to pay USD 39 307.91 as damages, and to pay costs on an attorney-client scale.
ratio_decidendi: The respondent’s pre-litigation correspondence acknowledged liability and replacement obligations. The later denial was inconsistent with the communication trail and did not create a genuine dispute of fact. Performance would replace the damaged goods, while damages compensated the loss caused by delayed performance.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court treated the respondent’s reversal after obtaining legal representation as an afterthought.

document_name: HOMEOF~1.DOC
case_name: HOME OF ANGELS HOUSING CO-OPERATIVE SOCIETY LIMITED AND 5 OTHERS versus CITY OF HARARE
subject_area: Civil procedure; rescission of consent orders; wrong procedure; lis pendens; housing cooperatives; eviction; costs.
catchwords_flynote: Rescission – consent orders – housing cooperatives seeking rescission under wrong rule – old High Court Rules distinguished between rescission routes – failure to use procedure for consent judgments – lis pendens and procedural abuse – application struck off roll with costs.
headnote: Six housing co-operatives sought rescission of consent orders entered in eviction litigation concerning Lot 2 of Parkridge Estate, also known as Paddock 27, Crowborough Farm, Harare. The City of Harare opposed and raised preliminary objections, including wrong procedure and lis pendens. The Court held that the applicants had used an incorrect procedural route for rescinding consent orders and that defects in the application were fatal. The application was struck off the roll with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the acting town clerk had authority to depose to the respondent’s affidavit.
    Whether the deponents for the housing co-operatives had locus.
    Whether the applicants adopted the wrong procedure for rescission of consent orders.
    Whether lis pendens or procedural abuse defeated the application.
legal_principles: Different rules govern rescission of default judgments, orders erroneously granted, and consent judgments. A consent judgment under the repealed rules required a specific rescission route and good and sufficient cause. A litigant cannot avoid a more demanding procedure by invoking a rule that does not apply. A legal entity acts through natural persons with authority.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant’s challenge to the acting town clerk’s authority was dismissed.
    Held, that the respondent’s challenge to the applicants’ deponents’ locus was dismissed.
    Held, that the applicants adopted the wrong procedure for rescission of consent orders.
    Held, that the preliminary objections were fatal.
    Held, that the application was struck off the roll with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that consent orders cannot be rescinded through a rule designed for erroneously granted orders where the proper rule requires good and sufficient cause; using the wrong procedure rendered the application fatally defective.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court described the applicants’ procedural shift as an attempt to avoid the burden of showing good and sufficient cause.
cases_referred_to: Dube v PSMAS & Anor SC 73/19
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 1971, rules 56, 63 and 449(1)(c); High Court Rules, 2021, rules 27 and 29.
subject_area_terms: Civil procedure; rescission of consent orders; wrong procedure; lis pendens; housing cooperatives; eviction; costs.
cases_referred_to_terms: Dube v PSMAS & Anor SC 73/19

document_name: Mugumira v Ruwende and 3 Others (802 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 802 (8 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Mugumira v Ruwende and Others
subject_area: Land; eviction; gazetted land; offer letter; lawful authority; rei vindicatio distinguished.
catchwords_flynote: Eviction from gazetted land – holder of offer letter – lawful authority under Gazetted Lands legislation – locus standi of lawful occupier to evict unauthorised occupier – application granted.
headnote: The applicant sought eviction of the first respondent from the Remaining Extent of Kilmacdough Farm. Although he framed relief in rei vindicatio terms, he was not the owner but the holder of lawful occupation rights through an offer letter. The court held that under the Gazetted Lands framework, holders of offer letters, permits or land settlement leases are entitled to assistance from courts and public officials to assert their rights. The applicant proved lawful authority and the respondent failed to establish an overriding right. The application was granted as prayed in the amended draft order.
issues_for_determination: Whether the applicant had locus to seek eviction despite not owning the property; whether he held lawful authority; whether the first respondent had a lawful basis to remain.
legal_principles: In gazetted land matters, offer letters, permits and land settlement leases regulate lawful occupation. Holders may be assisted by courts and public authorities to assert occupation rights. Common law rei vindicatio is based on ownership, but statutory land occupation rights may confer standing to evict unauthorised occupiers.
held_decision_points: The applicant proved his case on a balance of probabilities. The application was granted as prayed in the amended draft order.
ratio_decidendi: The applicant’s offer letter constituted lawful authority to occupy and assert control over the land. The respondent had no comparable lawful authority.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court distinguished ownership-based rei vindicatio from eviction based on statutory land occupation rights.
cases_referred_to: Indium Investments (Pvt) Ltd v Kingshaven (Pvt) Ltd SC 40/15; Stanbic Finance Zimbabwe Ltd v Chivhunga 1999 (1) ZLR 262 (H); Commercial Farmers Union & 9 others v Minister of Lands & 6 others 2010 (2) ZLR 576 (S).

document_name: Chiwenga v Mubaiwa-Chiwenga and Another (804 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 804 (16 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: CONSTANTINO GUVHEYA DOMINIC NYIKADZINO CHIWENGA versus MARRY MUBAIWA-CHIWENGA and CHAWASARIRA TRUST
citation: Chiwenga v Mubaiwa-Chiwenga and Another (804 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 804 (16 November 2022)
subject_area: Civil trial; divorce; joinder; case management; pleadings; removal from roll.
catchwords_flynote: Divorce trial – joinder of trust – court previously ordered joinder of second defendant – second defendant had not filed appearance and plea – matter removed from roll and directions issued for pleadings.
headnote: The divorce matter had been set down repeatedly without progress. The Court discovered that Chawasarira Trust had already been joined as a second defendant by an earlier order under HC 7059/21, despite the issue being presented as if still awaiting determination. Because the second defendant had not filed an appearance to defend or plea, and the plaintiff had not filed heads of argument as agreed, the Court removed the matter from the roll and gave directions for pleadings and further pre-trial steps.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the Court still needed to determine joinder of Chawasarira Trust.
    Whether the matter was ready to proceed as a trial.
    What case-management directions were required after discovery that the trust had already been joined but had not filed pleadings.
legal_principles: Once a party has been joined by a court order, the matter must proceed on the basis of that joinder unless the order is set aside. A joined defendant must file appearance and pleadings in accordance with the Rules. A trial that cannot properly proceed because pleadings are incomplete may be removed from the roll and directions issued to regularise the matter.
held_decision_points:
Held, that there was no need to determine joinder because Chawasarira Trust had already been joined by order of MUSITHU J.
    Held, that the matter could not proceed while pleadings by the second defendant were outstanding.
    Held, that the matter should be removed from the roll.
    Held, that directions should be given for appearance to defend, plea, replication, possible supplementary summary of evidence and fresh set-down.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that a trial cannot proceed efficiently where a defendant has already been joined but has not filed the requisite pleadings; the appropriate course is to remove the matter from the roll and direct pleadings to be regularised.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court expressed concern that the matter had been set down since January 2022 with no progress and that a material prior joinder order had not been brought to its attention.
cases_referred_to: None identified.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, provisions governing appearance to defend, pleadings, replication and set-down.

cases_referred_to_terms: None identified.

document_name: VANLED~1.DOC
case_name: Vanledge Investment (Private) Limited and 3 Others v Chemical Procument Services Africa (Private) Limited
subject_area: Contract law; credit facility; cancelled contract; accrued obligations; specific performance; Magistrates Court jurisdiction by consent; collection commission; attorney-client costs; consolidated civil appeals.
catchwords_flynote: Contract – credit facility agreement cancelled after breach – whether obligations accrued before cancellation remained enforceable – Magistrates Court ordering payment and specific performance – whether specific performance without alternative damages was competent – costs and collection commission under contract – appeal dismissed, cross-appeal allowed.
headnote: The appellants challenged a Magistrates Court judgment ordering payment of outstanding amounts under a credit facility agreement and specific performance regarding stock ordered but not collected. They argued that because the agreement had been cancelled, the respondent could not sue on it. The respondent cross-appealed against refusal to award attorney-client costs, collection commission and interest in accordance with the agreement. The High Court held that cancellation did not extinguish accrued obligations and that the parties had consented to Magistrates Court jurisdiction. The appeal was dismissed and the cross-appeal allowed.
issues_for_determination: Whether a claim could be sustained on obligations accrued under a cancelled agreement; whether specific performance was competent; whether the Magistrates Court had jurisdiction despite the value of the claim; and whether the respondent was entitled to attorney-client costs, collection commission and interest under the agreement.
legal_principles: Cancellation of a contract differs from nullity. A cancelled contract may retain rights and obligations accrued before cancellation, whereas a void contract gives rise to no enforceable rights. Parties may consent to Magistrates Court jurisdiction where permitted by statute. A party may claim specific performance where contractual obligations have accrued. Where parties expressly agree to attorney-client costs, collection commission and interest, the court may enforce those terms.
held_decision_points: The appeal was dismissed. The court held that the respondent could sue for obligations arising before cancellation and that the Magistrates Court had jurisdiction by consent. The cross-appeal was allowed. The costs order was substituted to provide for attorney-client costs, collection commission and interest as stipulated in the agreement.
ratio_decidendi: The respondent’s rights accrued before cancellation and were therefore enforceable. The Magistrates Court was clothed with jurisdiction by the parties’ agreement. Having accepted that the agreement remained relevant for accrued obligations, the court a quo should also have enforced the agreed costs, interest and collection commission provisions.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified.
cases_referred_to: Honeycomb Hill (Pvt) Ltd v Herentals College (Pvt) Ltd HC 366/14; Mcfoy v United Africa Company Limited [1961] 3 All ER 1169; Firstel Cellular (Private) Limited v Netone Cellular (Private) Limited SC 1/2015; Malunga and Another v Wede 2016 (1) ZLR 388 (H); Zivanomoyo v Dingani HMA 2-19.
legislation_considered: Magistrates Court Act [Chapter 7:10], ss 11 and 14.

document_name: Chinouya v Arosume Property Development (Pvt) Ltd and Another (806 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 806 (10 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: OLIVIA TAFADZWA CHINOUYA versus AROSUME PROPERTY DEVELOPMENT (PVT) LTD and MINISTER OF LOCAL GOVERNMENT, PUBLIC WORKS & NATIONAL HOUSING N.O.
citation: Chinouya v Arosume Property Development (Pvt) Ltd and Another (806 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 806 (10 November 2022)
subject_area: Declaratory order; land development; housing stands; development costs; tripartite agreement; title deeds.
catchwords_flynote: Declaratur – housing stand – purchaser with title deed seeking declaration that she owed no development costs – developer’s right to recover costs under tripartite agreement – applicant not public official beneficiary of government commonage stands – declaratory relief refused.
headnote: The applicant sought declarations that she had fully paid for Stand 288 Carrick Creagh and owed no development costs to the developer. The Court held that the Tripartite Agreement entitled the developer to recover development costs from beneficiaries other than those exempted as government commonage/public official beneficiaries. The applicant was not a public official, not a co-operative member, and had previously paid development costs before stopping. She failed to establish an existing, future or contingent right entitling her to declaratory relief. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant was exempt from paying development costs.
    Whether the 1st respondent had a right to claim development costs under the tripartite agreement.
    Whether the applicant established a right justifying declaratory relief under s 14 of the High Court Act.
    Whether the court should determine issues which were also implicated in HC 8146/15.
legal_principles: A declaratory order is discretionary and is intended to clarify an existing, future or contingent right or obligation. It is not meant to declare bare matters of fact or prejudge disputes not before the court. A party may not approbate and reprobate by denying the binding effect of an agreement while seeking to benefit from selective exemptions under it.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the point in limine that the 1st respondent’s opposition was out of time was dismissed because the delay was attributable to electronic filing compliance difficulties and the respondent acted reasonably.
    Held, that the applicant was not exempt from development costs.
    Held, that the 1st respondent had a right to charge and recover development costs from beneficiaries such as the applicant.
    Held, that the applicant failed to establish any existing, future or contingent right entitling her to the declarations sought.
    Held, that the application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that, on the facts and the tripartite housing-development arrangement, the applicant was obliged to pay development costs and therefore lacked the legal right necessary for declaratory relief that she had no such obligation.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted the practical difficulties with the Integrated Electronic Case Management System and treated the 1st respondent’s filing issue reasonably.
cases_referred_to: Trinity Engineering v Karimazondo & Others HH 672/15
legislation_considered: High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], section 14.
subject_area_terms: Declaratory order; land development; housing stands; development costs; tripartite agreement; title deeds.

document_name: THEPIT~1(1).DOC
case_name: The Pit Bull Family Trust v First Trust Holdings Limited and Others
subject_area: Civil procedure; contract; sale of immovable property; specific performance; breach; mora
catchwords_flynote: Specific performance — immovable property — agreement of sale — purchaser seeking transfer — contractual breach procedure — notice to remedy breach — whether cause of action complete where notice not given — application premature.
headnote: The applicant trust sought an order compelling the first respondent to transfer several undivided shares in immovable property against payment of the purchase price. The first respondent argued that the applicant had failed to pay within the contractual period and that the agreement had been cancelled after notice. The court held that, because the sale agreement prescribed a process for dealing with breach and for claiming specific performance, the applicant had to allege and prove that it had placed the respondent in mora by giving the contractual notice before suing. It had not done so. The application was premature and was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the agreement of sale remained enforceable.
    Whether the applicant was entitled to specific performance without first giving notice to remedy breach under the agreement.
    Whether the applicant’s cause of action was complete.
    Whether the dispute required determination of the alleged cancellation notice.
legal_principles:
Where a contract prescribes steps that must be taken before cancellation or specific performance may be claimed, those steps form part of the cause of action.
    A party seeking specific performance must show compliance with contractual conditions precedent.
    A cause of action consists of the full set of facts which, if proved, entitle a party to relief.
    A court may dispose of a matter on a decisive issue and need not address every other issue raised by the parties.
held_decision_points:
The applicant had not pleaded or proved that it had given the respondent the contractual notice to remedy breach before seeking specific performance.
    The cause of action was incomplete because the contractual condition precedent had not been satisfied.
    It was unnecessary to determine all remaining issues, including the dispute over the respondent’s alleged notice of cancellation.
    The application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The agreement created a procedural precondition before a party could claim remedies arising from breach. Since the applicant sought transfer and specific performance but did not first place the seller in mora in the manner provided by the contract, the right to sue had not yet accrued. The application was therefore premature.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court observed that it was unnecessary to split hairs over whether the respondent’s notice of breach and cancellation was delivered, because the dispositive issue was the applicant’s own failure to satisfy the contractual condition precedent before instituting proceedings.
cases_referred_to:
Central Africa Building Construction v Construction Resources Africa (Private) Limited HH 122/12
    Muranda v Todzaniso & Ors 1998 (2) ZLR 235 (H)
    Peebles v Dairiboard (Pvt) Ltd 1999 (1) ZLR 41 (H)
    Gwaradzimba v CJ Rebron & Company (Proprietary) Limited SC 12/2016

document_name: S v Dzemwa (808 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 808 (11 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Dzemwa
judges: MUTEVEDZI J; Assessors: Mr Mabandla and Mr Chimonyo
subject_area: Criminal law; murder; automatism; mental disorder; criminal capacity
catchwords_flynote: Murder – accused killing minor daughter – alleged insane automatism – distinction between sane and insane automatism – mental disorder at time of offence – evidential burden – state failing to disprove sane automatism.
headnote: The accused killed his young daughter in an inexplicable and apparently trance-like episode. The defence framed the matter as insane automatism, but medical evidence was insufficient to prove mental disorder at the time of the act. The court nevertheless found that the evidence laid a foundation for sane automatism and that the State failed to disprove involuntary conduct. The accused was acquitted.
issues_for_determination: Whether the accused had mens rea for murder; whether the conduct was involuntary as sane or insane automatism; what evidence was required for mental disorder or defect.
legal_principles: Automatism under s 216 is a complete defence where conduct is not voluntary. If involuntary conduct results from mental disorder or defect, s 216(4) and s 29 of the Mental Health Act require a special verdict. Sane automatism, if reasonably raised, must be disproved by the State beyond reasonable doubt.
held_decision_points: The State failed to disprove sane automatism beyond reasonable doubt; the accused was found not guilty and acquitted.
ratio_decidendi: Criminal responsibility cannot be attributed where an accused’s conduct is involuntary and the State fails to prove voluntary conduct and mens rea beyond reasonable doubt.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The judgment contains an extended discussion on the practical difficulty of psychiatric assessments and the shortage of psychiatric expertise affecting criminal trials.
cases_referred_to: R v Kemp; R v Senekal; R v Mavonani; S v Khumalo HB 61/06; S v Sheena Chikunda HH 563/14; Obert Nyamini Mapfumo v The State AD 48/79; S v Chikandiwa HH 281/2017; S v Pamela Mashungu HH 375/13; R v Mkize; R v Falconer; R v Youssef.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], ss 47, 216, 227; Mental Health Act [Chapter 15:12], ss 28, 29; Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], s 314.
reviewer_comments: Drafted from the mounted source judgment and retained for legal editorial review.

document_name: Nyangoni v Mugawazi (809 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 809 (16 November 2022) Copy(2).docx
case_name: Nyangoni v Mugawazi
subject_area: Civil appeal; summary judgment; eviction; lease; alleged purchase from third party; bona fide defence.
catchwords_flynote: Summary judgment – eviction after expiry of lease – appellant alleging purchase from third party not shown to be owner/agent – alleged agreement pre-dated lease – no bona fide defence – appeal dismissed with attorney-client costs.
headnote: The respondent obtained summary judgment for eviction and holding-over damages after the appellant remained in occupation of leased premises after expiry of a three-month lease. The appellant claimed he had bought the property from a third party allegedly acting as the respondent’s agent. The High Court held that the documents relied on by the appellant did not establish a plausible defence, and in fact undermined his version. The lease had expired and the appellant had no right to remain in occupation. The appeal was dismissed with attorney-client costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether the appellant disclosed a bona fide defence to summary judgment; whether the alleged purchase from a third party defeated the respondent’s eviction claim; whether the matter required viva voce evidence.
legal_principles: To resist summary judgment, a defendant must set out a bona fide and plausible defence with sufficient clarity and completeness. Bare or contradictory allegations do not raise a triable issue. Summary judgment prevents abuse of civil procedure by defendants without a genuine defence.
held_decision_points: The appeal was dismissed with costs on the attorney-client scale.
ratio_decidendi: The appellant’s own documents did not prove a valid sale by the respondent or an authorised agent. The alleged sale pre-dated the lease, and the acknowledgement of debt contradicted the claim that the purchase price had been fully paid. The respondent’s eviction claim was unanswerable.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court held that the appellant’s conduct amounted to an abuse of court process warranting punitive costs.
cases_referred_to: Hales v Doverick Investments (Private) Limited 1998 (2) ZLR 235 (H); Kingstons Ltd v L D Ineson (Pvt) Ltd 2006 (1) ZLR 451 (S); Beresford Land Plan (Pvt) Ltd v Urquhart 1975 (1) RLR 260 and related summary judgment authorities cited in the judgment.
legislation_considered: Magistrates Court (Civil) Rules, 2019, provisions on summary judgment; High Court Rules, 2021.
reviewer_comments: Draft.

document_name: Guchu (nee Kazungaire) v Guchu (811 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 811 (30 May 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: PRECIUOS GUCHU (NEE KAZUNGAIRE) versus WILKINS CHAKAIPA GUCHU
subject_area: Family law; divorce; maintenance of minor children; matrimonial property distribution; spousal maintenance; costs.
catchwords_flynote: Divorce – irretrievable breakdown admitted – minor children residing abroad – maintenance obligations of parents – parties’ candour as to means – immovable matrimonial property – equality of division – counterclaim for spousal maintenance dismissed – attorney-client costs.
headnote: The plaintiff sought divorce and ancillary relief, including custody, child maintenance, tuition expenses and a 70% share in the matrimonial immovable property. The defendant admitted breakdown but resisted the maintenance claim, sought spousal maintenance and proposed that the plaintiff receive only 10% of the property. The Court held that both parents must support the minor children according to their means, found the defendant had not been candid about his income, and ordered him to pay maintenance and tuition contributions. The Court divided the immovable property equally and dismissed the counterclaim.
issues_for_determination:
How much maintenance the defendant should pay for each minor child.
    How the immovable property at No. 6 Flete Court, Hillview, Quinton Road, Greystone Park, Harare should be apportioned.
    Whether the defendant was entitled to spousal maintenance.
    What costs order was justified.
legal_principles: Under section 7(1)(b) of the Matrimonial Causes Act, the Court may order maintenance for a spouse or child. Maintenance of minor children is a joint obligation of both parents, each contributing according to means. Parties in matrimonial matters must be candid with the Court as to income and resources. Matrimonial property distribution is determined by fairness and the parties’ contributions in the circumstances.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the marriage had irretrievably broken down and a decree of divorce was granted.
    Held, that custody of the minor children was awarded to the plaintiff, with access to be agreed.
    Held, that the defendant must pay US$500 per month per child until majority or self-sufficiency.
    Held, that the defendant must contribute 50% of each child’s tuition expenses.
    Held, that the Greystone Park property was to be shared equally.
    Held, that the defendant’s counterclaim was dismissed.
    Held, that the defendant was to pay costs on an attorney-client scale.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that where a parent fails to give a candid account of income and lives in a manner inconsistent with alleged inability to pay, the Court may draw adverse inferences and make a maintenance order reflecting the children’s needs and the parent’s apparent means; on the evidence, equal apportionment of the immovable property was fair.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that the defendant’s shifting proposals for maintenance undermined his assertion of inability to contribute.
cases_referred_to: None identified
legislation_considered: Matrimonial Causes Act [Chapter 5:13], section 7(1)(b); Marriage Act [Chapter 5:11].

document_name: Steenbok and Another v Zviitwa (813 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 813 (16 November 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: Steenbok and Another v Zviitwa
subject_area: Civil appeal; spoliation; motor vehicle; jurisdiction; material disputes of fact; delay; consent to deprivation; condonation
catchwords_flynote: Spoliation — motor vehicle — magistrates’ jurisdiction — material disputes about how possession was lost — delay in bringing application — consent to deprivation — late heads of argument and condonation.
headnote: The respondent had obtained a spoliation order in the magistrates court for recovery of a Toyota VX. The appellants appealed. The High Court rejected the jurisdictional ground but upheld the appeal because material disputes existed about how the vehicle came into the appellants’ possession and because the respondent had delayed for months before seeking spoliation. The delay and correspondence supported an inference of consent or acquiescence to deprivation. The respondent’s late heads of argument were not condoned. The appeal was upheld with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the magistrates court had jurisdiction over spoliation involving a vehicle valued above its ordinary monetary jurisdiction.
    Whether material disputes of fact precluded spoliation on the papers.
    Whether lapse of time and the respondent’s conduct showed consent to deprivation.
    Whether respondent’s late heads of argument should be condoned.
legal_principles:
Spoliation protects peaceful and undisturbed possession and requires unlawful deprivation.
    Lawfulness of the applicant’s possession is not the issue in spoliation proceedings.
    A magistrates court spoliation application is about possession, not delivery or transfer of title.
    Material disputes of fact may defeat application proceedings.
    Delay and conduct after dispossession may imply consent to deprivation.
    Condonation for late heads of argument requires a proper, reasonable explanation and is not granted for the mere asking.
held_decision_points:
The magistrates court had jurisdiction because the claim was possessory, not one for transfer of property.
    Material disputes existed about whether the vehicle had been voluntarily delivered as a lien or unlawfully taken.
    The respondent did not specify the date, place and manner of dispossession adequately.
    The delay before seeking spoliation militated against the respondent and supported consent/acquiescence.
    The appeal was upheld with costs.
ratio_decidendi: Although spoliation is a robust possessory remedy, the applicant had to establish unlawful deprivation without consent. The conflicting versions, coupled with the respondent’s unexplained delay, made the magistrates court’s spoliation order unsustainable.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted that a party seeking condonation must apply promptly and give a proper explanation; a bare statement from the bar that counsel was ill is insufficient.

document_name: Baraide Investments (Private) Limited v Mashamhanda (815 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 815 (14 July 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: BARIADE INVESTMENTS (PRIVATE) LIMITED versus TENDAI MASHAMHANDA
citation: Baraide Investments (Private) Limited v Mashamhanda (815 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 815 (14 July 2022)
subject_area: Civil procedure; ejectment; lis alibi pendens; stay/removal from roll; pending Constitutional Court proceedings; judicial sale; property ownership dispute.
catchwords_flynote: Civil procedure – ejectment application by registered owner – respondent seeking removal from roll pending Constitutional Court matter – Supreme Court had resolved ownership dispute in applicant's favour – respondent pursued constitutional challenge – court discretion to stay or remove proceedings to avoid conflict with pending Constitutional Court determination – matter removed from roll pending finalisation of CCZ 12/22.
headnote: The applicant sought ejectment of the respondent from property it had purchased by judicial auction and transferred into its name. The Supreme Court had already resolved ownership in the applicant's favour. The respondent, however, had applied to the Constitutional Court for direct access to challenge the constitutionality of the Supreme Court decision. On the hearing date, the respondent sought removal of the ejectment application from the roll pending the Constitutional Court's decision. The Court held that justice, equity and balance of convenience favoured staying/removing the matter to avoid a judgment that might conflict with the pending Constitutional Court decision. The matter was removed from the roll.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the ejectment application should proceed despite the pending Constitutional Court matter.
    Whether a pending constitutional challenge created sufficient basis for removal from the roll or stay of proceedings.
    Whether justice, equity and balance of convenience favoured proceeding or waiting.
    Whether proceeding risked conflicting with the Constitutional Court's pending decision.
legal_principles: A court has discretion to stay or remove proceedings where there are pending proceedings concerning substantially related issues and where proceeding may create conflict, duplication or prejudice. The principles of lis alibi pendens and judicial comity require consideration of identity of issues, parties, convenience, equity and the interests of justice. A pending constitutional matter does not automatically suspend other proceedings, but the court may remove or stay a matter where justice requires.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the Constitutional Court matter was not shown to be frivolous or vexatious.
    Held, that determining the ejectment application immediately risked conflict with the Constitutional Court's reserved decision.
    Held, that the balance of convenience and justice favoured removal from the roll.
    Held, that any prejudice to the applicant could be addressed by a claim for holding-over damages if the respondent failed in the Constitutional Court.
    Held, that the present matter was to be removed from the roll pending finalisation of CCZ 12/22.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that where a pending Constitutional Court matter may directly affect the basis of an ejectment claim and judgment has been reserved, the High Court may remove the ejectment application from the roll to prevent conflicting judgments and unnecessary relitigation.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that a limited wait was preferable to risking conflicting judgments and further relitigation between the parties.
cases_referred_to:
Bariadie Investments (Private) Limited v Puwai Chiutsi and Others SC 24/22
    Chigami 2 Syndicate and 2 Others HMA 14/20
    Boka v Mavhunga 1996 (2) ZLR 261
    Authorities in Herbstein and Van Winsen on lis alibi pendens
legislation_considered: Constitutional Court Act [Chapter 7:21], section 6; High Court Rules, 2021.
constitutional_provisions_considered: Constitutional litigation under CCZ 12/22 was pending; specific constitutional provisions not fully identified in the judgment extract.

document_name: S v Shopa and 13 Others (816 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 816 (15 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Chauya Shopa and 13 Others v The State
subject_area: Criminal procedure; bail appeal; changed circumstances; public violence; effluxion of time; witness interference
catchwords_flynote: Bail appeal - public violence - changed circumstances - lapse of time since arrest and projected completion of investigations - police investigations cannot continue indefinitely to justify detention - original reasons for denying bail weakened - appeal allowed - bail granted with reporting and non-interference conditions.
headnote: The appellants were charged with public violence arising from disturbances in Nyatsime. Earlier bail applications and appeals had failed. They later applied for bail on changed circumstances, relying principally on passage of time and the fact that police investigations had overshot projected completion dates. The magistrate refused bail, treating the lapse of time as inconsequential. The High Court held that the magistrate misapplied the concept of changed circumstances. The original reasons for refusing bail included volatility in Nyatsime, risk to public order, possible interference with witnesses and the alleged strength of the State case. By the time of the later application, the tension had dissipated and the prosecution’s own position showed that investigations were still not finalised months later. The appeal was allowed and bail was granted.
issues_for_determination: Whether the magistrate properly understood and applied the concept of changed circumstances; whether effluxion of time was material; whether continued investigation and witness-interference concerns remained valid; and whether the appellants should be admitted to bail.
legal_principles: A bail appeal is decided on whether the lower court misdirected itself or exercised discretion improperly. In an application based on changed circumstances, the court must consider new facts together with the circumstances and reasons for the earlier refusal of bail. Passage of time can materially alter the risks of interference, volatility and abscondment. Police investigations cannot be allowed to continue indefinitely to the prejudice of accused persons.
held_decision_points: The appeal was allowed. All appellants were admitted to bail on conditions including deposit of $50,000 each, continued residence at their stated addresses, reporting to CID Law and Order three times a week, and non-interference with witnesses, investigations or evidence.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that where the key bases for bail refusal were the incompleteness of investigations, volatility and possible interference, a substantial and unexplained lapse of time beyond projected investigation dates can amount to changed circumstances justifying bail.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court made observations about judicial independence, professional conduct in bail litigation, and the impropriety of replacing legal argument with political or public-opinion rhetoric.
cases_referred_to: Chauya Shopa and 13 Others v The State HH 655/22; Mary Mubaiwa-Chiwenga v The State and Others SC 4/21; Belington Maronga and Others v The State HH 393/21; S v Maphosa and 2 Others HMT 1/21; S v Malunjwa 2003 (1) ZLR 275 (H); S v Chikumba 2015 (2) ZLR; Hopewell Chin’ono v The State HH 567/20; Felix Biri v The State HH 722/22; S v Ruturi HH 23/03.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 36(1)(a); High Court Rules, 2021, r 91; Supreme Court Rules, 2018, r 67 referred to by analogy.
constitutional_provisions_considered: Constitution of Zimbabwe, ss 56(1), 219; judicial independence discussed generally.
reviewer_comments: Full text extracted from the mounted DOCX. This is a bail appeal judgment arising from the same Nyatsime public-violence matter referred to in earlier related rulings.

document_name: Sanganayi Inn (Pvt) Ltd v The Crystal Club Lashers Bar & Night Club (817 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 817 (16 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Sanganayi Inn (Pvt) Ltd v The Crystal Club Lashers Bar & Night Club
subject_area: Civil appeal; landlord and tenant; eviction; arrear rentals; absolution from the instance; commercial court jurisdiction; non-payment of rent
catchwords_flynote: Lease - landlord’s claim for eviction, arrear rentals and ancillary relief - magistrates court granted absolution from the instance after trial - appeal - High Court jurisdiction despite Commercial Court rules - non-payment of rent proved - absolution set aside - eviction and monetary relief granted.
headnote: The landlord sued for eviction, arrear rentals, holding-over damages, City of Harare charges and surrender of liquor licence documents. The magistrates court granted absolution from the instance. On appeal the High Court dismissed preliminary objections, held that the Civil Division had jurisdiction, and found that the landlord had proved material breach through non-payment of rentals. The appeal succeeded and the magistrates court judgment was replaced with eviction and monetary relief.
issues_for_determination: Whether the High Court Civil Division had jurisdiction; whether a magistrate could grant absolution mero motu at the end of trial; whether the appellant had proved grounds for eviction, particularly non-payment of rentals; and whether the magistrate erred in granting absolution.
legal_principles: The High Court has jurisdiction over civil appeals from the Magistrates Court unless that jurisdiction is lawfully ousted. A magistrate may grant absolution at the end of a trial under s 18 of the Magistrates Court Act. In civil proceedings, the party making a positive allegation bears the onus on a balance of probabilities. A tenant has no right to occupy leased property except in return for rent.
held_decision_points: Appeal allowed with costs. The respondent was ordered evicted, to pay rental arrears and holding-over damages, outstanding City of Harare charges, interest, surrender the liquor licence and documents, and pay costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that once a landlord proves material breach by non-payment of rent under a lease, a magistrate errs in granting absolution from the instance; eviction and contractual monetary relief are competent.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court held that the Commercial Court was a division of the High Court and that the Commercial Court Rules did not oust the Civil Division’s jurisdiction.
cases_referred_to: Guwa & Anor v Willoughby's Investments 2009 (1) ZLR 380 (S); Nzara v Kashumba & Ors SC 18/18; Kingstons Ltd v D Ineson (Pvt) Ltd 2006 (1) ZLR 451 (S); FBC Building Society v Pavian Investments (Pvt) Ltd HB 34/17; Astra Industries Ltd v Peter Chamburuka SC 27/12; ZESA v Dera 1998 (1) ZLR 500 (SC); Altem Enterprises (Pvt) Ltd v John Sisk (Pvt) Ltd SC 4/2013.
legislation_considered: Magistrates Court Act [Chapter 7:10], ss 18 and 40; High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], s 30; Commercial Court Rules, r 43; Presidential Powers (Temporary Measures) (Deferral of Rent and Mortgage Payments During National Lockdown) Regulations, 2020, SI 96/2020.

document_name: Zimbabwe Revenue Authority v Trustus (Pvt) Ltd (820 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 820 (16 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Zimbabwe Revenue Authority v Trustus (Pvt) Ltd
subject_area: Customs and excise; seizure of motor vehicle; prescription; notice of intention to sue; release of seized goods; civil appeal.
catchwords_flynote: Customs seizure – truck seized by ZIMRA for suspected customs offence – owner seeking release – prescription and notice objections – whether proceedings were time-barred – whether owner proved lack of knowledge or authority – appeal dismissed.
headnote: ZIMRA appealed against a Magistrates Court order directing unconditional release of the respondent’s truck. The truck had been seized in Beitbridge after allegedly being used to carry uncleared foreign goods. ZIMRA argued that the release claim had prescribed, that the notice of intention to sue was invalid, and that the respondent had not proved lack of knowledge or authority. The High Court held that the lower court correctly treated the claim as not prescribed under the applicable statutory framework, that the notice substantially complied, and that the respondent proved ownership and lack of authorisation. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether s 193(12) or s 196 of the Customs and Excise Act governed prescription; whether the notice of intention to sue met the State Liabilities Act requirements; whether the lower court wrongly treated s 193(12) as regulating internal remedies; whether the respondent proved it was the owner; and whether it proved lack of knowledge or authorisation regarding the use of the truck.
legal_principles: In customs seizure matters, the Act provides a staged process: seizure notice, internal or administrative recourse culminating with the Commissioner-General, and civil proceedings. Section 193(12) regulates proceedings within that process, while s 196 governs civil proceedings and includes an eight-month period. The maxim generalia specialibus non derogant applies where general and specific provisions appear to conflict. A notice under the State Liabilities Act may be condoned where there is substantial compliance and no undue prejudice.
held_decision_points: The appeal failed on all grounds. The High Court held that the claim had not prescribed; the notice of intention to sue substantially complied; the respondent established ownership and that the vehicle had been used without its authority; and the lower court had not erred. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: Civil proceedings for release were brought within the proper statutory framework and the respondent had pursued internal remedies. ZIMRA did not displace the respondent’s ownership evidence or prove that the owner authorised the unlawful use. The lower court’s order for release was therefore not impeachable.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified.
cases_referred_to: Twotap Logistics (Private) Limited v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority HH 207/17; White Shadow Investments (Private) Limited v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority HH 683/21; Machacha v ZIMRA HB 186/11; Ebrahim v Controller of Customs and Excise 1985 (2) ZLR 1; Bon Espoir (Pvt) Ltd v Hearing Chabata & Ors SC 45/03; Air Duct Fabricators (Pvt) Ltd v A.M. Machado and Sons (Pvt) Ltd HH 54/16; R v Gwantshu 1931 EDL 29; Chamu Ndaza v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority HH 79/2004.
legislation_considered: Customs and Excise Act [Chapter 23:02], ss 187, 188, 193 and 196; State Liabilities Act [Chapter 8:15]; Revenue Authority Act [Chapter 23:11].

document_name: S v Chiwaya (821 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 821 (18 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Chiwaya
judges: MUTEVEDZI J; assessors Mr Mhandu and Mr Kunaka
subject_area: Criminal law – murder – mental disorder – insanity – Mental Health Act – treatment order
catchwords_flynote: Murder; paranoid schizophrenia; not guilty because of insanity; Mental Health Act s 29(2); accused returned for treatment at Chikurubi Psychiatric Unit.
headnote: The accused faced a murder charge arising from the death of Laque Jonasi, allegedly caused by striking him on the back of the neck with a machete. The accused pleaded not guilty and prayed for a verdict of not guilty because of insanity under s 29(2) of the Mental Health Act.
issues_for_determination: Whether the accused committed the act constituting murder; whether he was mentally disordered at the time so as not to be criminally responsible; what order should follow under the Mental Health Act.
legal_principles: Where the court is satisfied that the accused committed the act charged but was mentally disordered or intellectually handicapped at the time, s 29(2) of the Mental Health Act permits a finding of not guilty because of insanity and requires an appropriate order for treatment, custody or discharge.
held_decision_points: The court found that the accused was not guilty of murder because of insanity and declined the recommendation that he be released into his father’s custody because the charge was serious and medical assurances of safe discharge were insufficient.
ratio_decidendi: In serious offences, a finding of insanity does not automatically justify release to relatives; the court must protect society and require adequate medical assurance before ordering discharge.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court observed that, where the prosecution accepts that an accused was mentally disordered at the time of the offence, persistence with prosecution may not always serve the interests of justice, and that the Prosecutor General’s office should address the procedural gap.
cases_referred_to: None identified in the extracted text.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 47; Mental Health Act [Chapter 15:12], s 29(2); Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], s 314.

document_name: Kadungure v Pervaiz (822 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 822 (16 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: CHRIS KADUNGURE versus JUNAID PERVAIZ
subject_area: Delict; malicious prosecution; abuse of proceedings; absolution from the instance; damages; criminal justice process.
catchwords_flynote: Malicious prosecution claim – plaintiff previously arrested, convicted and later acquitted on appeal – defendant allegedly implicated him maliciously – absolution from the instance at close of plaintiff’s case – test whether court might find for plaintiff – application dismissed.
headnote: The plaintiff claimed damages for malicious prosecution and abuse of proceedings after being arrested and convicted for robbery, then acquitted on appeal. At the close of the plaintiff’s case, the defendant sought absolution from the instance. The Court held that the plaintiff had placed evidence before the Court on which a court might reasonably find in his favour, and dismissed the absolution application.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the plaintiff had led evidence on which a court might reasonably find for him.
    Whether the arrest and prosecution were instigated or procured by the defendant.
    Whether there was evidence of absence of reasonable and probable cause and malice.
    Whether absolution from the instance should be granted.
legal_principles: Absolution from the instance at the close of the plaintiff’s case is granted sparingly. The test is whether there is evidence upon which a court, applying its mind reasonably, might find for the plaintiff. In malicious prosecution, the plaintiff must prove that the defendant instigated or procured the prosecution, lacked reasonable and probable cause, acted with malice, and that the prosecution failed.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the plaintiff placed evidence before the court on which a court might reasonably find in his favour.
    Held, that the defendant’s application for absolution from the instance was devoid of merit.
    Held, that the application for absolution from the instance at the close of the plaintiff’s case was dismissed.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that, at the absolution stage, the evidence did not have to establish the plaintiff’s case finally; it was sufficient that the evidence could reasonably support a finding that the defendant maliciously procured or contributed to the prosecution.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court repeated the caution that trial courts should be extremely chary of granting absolution at the close of a plaintiff’s case.
cases_referred_to: Nherera v Shah; Supreme Service Station (1969) (Pvt) Ltd v Fox and Goodridge (Pvt) Ltd; United Air Charters (Pvt) Ltd v Jarman; further authorities on absolution as cited.
legislation_considered: General law of delict; High Court Rules, 2021.
subject_area_terms: Delict; malicious prosecution; abuse of proceedings; absolution from the instance; damages; criminal justice process.

document_name: EARTHR~1.DOC
case_name: EARTHROW INVESTMENTS (PVT) LTD versus MINISTER OF MINES AND MINING DEVELOPMENT N.O. and 4 OTHERS
case_number: HC 5473/20; related matter HC 1465/18 referred to
subject_area: Mining law; declaratur; prior pegging; registration certificates; lis pendens; disputes of fact; Mines and Minerals Act.
catchwords_flynote: Mining claims – applicant registered Koo Doo 62–65 in 2003 – later competing registration by fourth respondent cancelled – lis pendens defence based on review of cancellation dismissed – no material dispute requiring oral evidence – prior pegger protected – declaratur granted.
headnote: The applicant sought a declaration that it was the sole lawful holder of rights in Koo Doo 62–65 mining claims and that the fourth respondent’s operations were unlawful. The fourth respondent raised lis pendens and disputes of fact. The Court held that the pending review about cancellation of the fourth respondent’s certificate did not have the same cause of action as the declaratur and that the matter could be decided on the papers. Since the applicant was the earlier registered pegger and held valid certificates, the Court granted the declaratory relief.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the lis pendens defence applied because HC 1465/18 was pending.
    Whether there were material disputes of fact requiring viva voce evidence.
    Whether the applicant had valid rights as prior pegger and registered title holder.
    Whether the fourth respondent’s certificate was null and void.
legal_principles: Lis pendens requires pending litigation between the same parties or privies, on the same subject matter and same cause of action. Declaratory relief is available where justice demands a declaration of the existence and nature of a legal right. Under the Mines and Minerals Act, where competing mining titles overlap, priority and earlier registration may be decisive, particularly under section 177(3).
held_decision_points:
Held, that HC 1465/18 did not bar the present declaratur because the causes of action were different.
    Held, that there was no material dispute of fact preventing determination on the papers.
    Held, that the applicant was the sole legitimate holder of rights, title and interests in Koo Doo 62–65.
    Held, that the fourth respondent’s certificate of registration was null and void.
    Held, that the fourth respondent had no right to occupy or conduct mining operations in the claims.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the applicant’s earlier registration and current valid certificates, together with the absence of support from the mining authorities for the competing registration, entitled it to declaratory protection of its mining rights.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted that the custodians of mining claims did not oppose the application and had taken the position that the fourth respondent’s registration had been cancelled.
cases_referred_to:
Rolling River Enterprises v Minister of State for National Security, Land and Reform and Resettlement HC 2271/06
    Rupande v Grobellar & Others HH 654/18
    Mhunga v Mtindi 1986 (2) ZLR 171 (S)

document_name: Sakunda Holdings (Pvt) Ltd v Mutare Toyota and Another (826 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 826 (16 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Sakunda Holdings (Pvt) Ltd v Mutare Toyota and Another
subject_area: Contract; agency; company law; ostensible authority; specific performance; motor vehicle sale
catchwords_flynote: Contract - purchase of Toyota vehicles - company director/executive chairman negotiated transaction - plaintiff paid dealer’s bank account - only part delivery - authority and agency - ostensible authority and company-law indoor management principles - first defendant bound - second defendant also liable due subsequent conduct - specific performance or damages ordered.
headnote: The plaintiff paid for three Toyota Prado vehicles and five Toyota Land Cruiser LC200 vehicles after receiving quotations from the first defendant. Three Prados and one Land Cruiser were delivered; four Land Cruisers were not. The defendants disputed who had contracted with the plaintiff, with the first defendant saying the second defendant acted personally and the second defendant saying he acted for the first defendant. The court held that the second defendant, as shareholder, director and executive chairman, had authority or at least ostensible authority to bind the first defendant. The first defendant’s letterhead, bank account and employees were used. The second defendant’s continued involvement and refusal to facilitate a refund also justified personal liability.
issues_for_determination: Who contracted with the plaintiff; whether the second defendant had authority to bind the first defendant; whether the first defendant was liable on agency and company-law principles; whether the second defendant was personally liable; and whether specific performance or damages should be ordered.
legal_principles: A company acts through natural persons. A person dealing with a company may rely on ostensible authority and assume internal company processes have been complied with. A company may be bound by the acts of senior officers who appear authorised, especially where its letterhead, bank account and employees are used. Specific performance may be granted unless impossible or unduly harsh.
held_decision_points: Judgment was entered for the plaintiff against both defendants jointly and severally. The defendants were ordered to deliver four brand new Toyota Land Cruiser LC200 vehicles within 30 days, failing which they must pay US$633,257 or its ZWL equivalent at the prevailing auction rate plus interest. Defendants were ordered to pay costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that where a senior company officer with ostensible authority negotiates a transaction and the company’s own systems, letterhead, bank account and staff are used, the company is bound; personal liability may also attach where the officer’s subsequent conduct prolongs non-performance and frustrates resolution.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court rejected the argument that the contract was illegal for lack of procurement tender because the plaintiff was a private company not governed by public procurement laws.
cases_referred_to: Pillay v Krishna & Another 1946 AD 946; Lasagne Investments (Pvt) Ltd & Others v Hydon Investment (Pvt) Ltd & Others 2010 (1) ZLR 296 (H); Reed N.O. v Sager’s Motors (Pvt) Ltd 1969 (2) RLR 519 (A); Caronsel Investments (Pvt) Ltd v Spar Harare (Pvt) Ltd 2006 (1) ZLR 430 (H); Star Africa Corporation Ltd v Zimbabwe Sugar Refineries Workers Union SC 65/21; Grandwell Holdings (Pvt) Ltd v ZMDC & Others SC 05/20.
legislation_considered: Companies Act [Chapter 24:03], s 12(a); Companies and Other Business Entities Act [Chapter 24:31], s 24(1)(a), s 24(1)(b).
reviewer_comments: Full text extracted from the mounted DOCX. The heading records HH826/22 without a space; the filename citation [2022] ZWHHC 826 has been preserved.

document_name: Mujokoro v Chikwature (827 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 827 (16 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Mujokoro v Chikwature
subject_area: Property; co-ownership; tacit universal partnership; stated case; subdivision of jointly owned property.
catchwords_flynote: Unmarried partners – jointly registered immovable property – alleged tacit universal partnership – special/stated case – registration in joint names confers equal ownership – subdivision documents – plaintiff entitled to relief.
headnote: The parties had been in a romantic relationship and jointly acquired two Hatfield stands. The plaintiff relinquished one stand to the defendant but retained his joint ownership in Stand 734 and sought to compel subdivision. The defendant claimed a tacit universal partnership and greater contribution. Proceeding on a stated case, the court held that there was no tacit universal partnership because no profit-making enterprise was established. The title deed showed equal co-ownership. The plaintiff was entitled to compel the defendant to sign subdivision documents and to contribute half the valuation and subdivision costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether there was a tacit universal partnership; what rights flowed from joint registration; whether the defendant could be compelled to sign subdivision papers and pay half the costs.
legal_principles: A tacit universal partnership requires each party to contribute something, a business carried on for joint benefit, an object to make profit and a legitimate contract. Title deeds confer real rights enforceable against the world, and joint registration generally reflects equal rights unless displaced by proper proof.
held_decision_points: The plaintiff’s claim succeeded. The defendant was ordered to sign all relevant subdivision registration documents, failing which the Sheriff could sign on her behalf. The defendant was ordered to pay half of valuation and subdivision costs. Each party to pay own costs.
ratio_decidendi: The agreed facts did not establish a profit-making partnership. The property was jointly registered and, on the stated case, each party held an equal share. The defendant could not introduce disputed extraneous facts to defeat the agreed special case.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court stressed that in a stated case the parties are bound by agreed facts and cannot reopen them through heads of argument.
cases_referred_to: Leathout Investments (Private) Limited v Muvirimi & Anor SC 60/22; Chapendama v Chapendama 1998 (1) ZLR; Mbaraidzo v Jose HH 40-18; Isaacs v Isaacs 1949 (1) SA 952 (C); Butters v Mncora 2012 (4) SA 1 (SCA); Fryes (Pty) Ltd v Ries 1957 (3) SA 575; Ishemunyoro v Ishemunyoro and Ors SC 14/19.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, r 52; Regional Town and Country Planning Act [Chapter 29:12], s 40; Deeds Registries Act [Chapter 20:05], s 29.

document_name: Mahanda (nee Samukange) v Zimbodza N.O and 5 Others (828 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 828 (16 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Mahanda (nee Samukange) v Zimbodza N.O and 5 Others
subject_area: Deceased estates; fraud; sale and transfer of immovable property; cancellation of title
catchwords_flynote: Estate administration — fraudulent appointment of executor — sale of estate property — innocent purchasers — nemo dat — fraud taints subsequent transfer — cancellation of deed — eviction.
headnote: The applicant sought nullification of the sale and transfer of a Borrowdale West property from the estate of the late Webster Samukange. The appointment of the executor who sold the property had been procured through a fraudulent process and the administration had been set aside. The court held that the fraudulent foundation tainted the entire process, including the sale and transfer to third-party purchasers.
issues_for_determination: Whether the sale and transfer to the third and fourth respondents could stand where the appointment and authority of the executor dative were founded on fraud; whether the respondents could rely on bona fide purchaser or double-sale principles.
legal_principles: Fraud vitiates legal acts founded upon it. No valid rights can flow from an appointment or process that is void for fraud. The double-sale doctrine does not assist where the purported seller had no valid authority to sell.
held_decision_points: The sale was nullified, the deed of transfer in favour of the third and fourth respondents was cancelled, the previous deed was ordered to be revived, and the third and fourth respondents were ordered to vacate.
ratio_decidendi: Because the executor’s appointment was procured through fraud, he lacked authority to sell and transfer the property. The subsequent title could not stand even if the purchasers were not themselves part of the fraud.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court distinguished this case from true double-sale cases, where a valid owner sells to multiple purchasers.
cases_referred_to: Authorities on fraud, title and the effect of void transactions are considered in the full judgment.
legislation_considered: Administration of Estates Act; Deeds Registries law; High Court Rules, 2021.
subject_area_terms: Deceased estates; fraud; sale and transfer of immovable property; cancellation of title
cases_referred_to_terms: Authorities on fraud, title and the effect of void transactions are considered in the full judgment.

document_name: Zimbabwe National Road Administration v Kufa (829 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 829 (16 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Zimbabwe National Road Administration v Kufa
subject_area: Property law; actio rei vindicatio; employment benefits; conditions of service vehicle; right of retention; interpretation of employment contract; motor vehicle benefit after resignation.
catchwords_flynote: Actio rei vindicatio – employer seeking return of conditions-of-service motor vehicle from former employee – contract allowing employee to purchase vehicle at residual value after four years – vehicle issued later than contractually expected – tax benefit deductions continuing – whether former employee had right of retention defeating rei vindicatio.
headnote: ZINARA sought rei vindicatio for a Toyota Hilux allocated to the respondent as a conditions-of-service vehicle during employment. The respondent had resigned but retained the vehicle, contending that clause 6(b) of his employment contract entitled him to purchase the vehicle at a residual value after four years. He argued that the applicant should have issued him a replacement vehicle in 2015, but only did so in August 2016, while still deducting vehicle benefit tax. The court held that the respondent had established a contractual right of retention based on the vehicle benefit clause and dismissed the application.
issues_for_determination: Whether the applicant proved the requirements of rei vindicatio; whether the respondent’s possession was without lawful cause; whether clause 6(b) created a right to purchase and retain the vehicle; whether delayed issuance of the vehicle and vehicle benefit tax deductions affected the respondent’s entitlement; and whether costs should be punitive.
legal_principles: An owner invoking rei vindicatio must prove ownership and possession by the respondent. The possessor may defeat the claim by establishing a right of retention or contractual right to retain the property. Courts interpret written contracts according to their ordinary meaning and do not rewrite the parties’ bargain. In employment-property cases, an employee whose employment has ended ordinarily has no right to retain the employer’s property unless a contractual basis for retention is shown.
held_decision_points: The respondent established a right of retention based on clause 6(b). The court held that the vehicle benefit and the applicant’s continued deduction of vehicle benefit tax supported the respondent’s entitlement to purchase the vehicle. The application for rei vindicatio was dismissed. Costs were awarded on the ordinary scale.
ratio_decidendi: The applicant’s ownership of the vehicle was not enough because the respondent showed a contractual right to retain it. The respondent’s entitlement under clause 6(b), read with the applicant’s conduct in deducting vehicle benefit tax despite delayed delivery of the vehicle, defeated the rei vindicatio.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified beyond the court’s distinction of ordinary employer-property return cases from this contractual vehicle-benefit arrangement.
cases_referred_to: Nyahora v CFI Holdings SC 81-14; Chetty v Naidoo 1974 (3) SA 13 (A); Sheisam Consulting (Pvt) Ltd & Anor v Energy and Information Logistics (Pvt) Ltd HH 7-18; Old Mutual Property Investments v Metro International (Pvt) Ltd and Anor HH 53-06; William Bain and Co Holdings (Pvt) Ltd v Nyamukunda HH 309-13; Forestry Commission v Betty Muwonde HH 9-18; Netone Cellular (Pvt) Ltd v Reward Kangai HH 441-19.
legislation_considered: No statute centrally determined; employment contract and internal vehicle allocation policy considered.
reviewer_comments: Full citation and neutral citation taken from filename and judgment heading. The judgment concerns actio rei vindicatio but turns on the respondent’s contractual right of retention.
