document_name: Damson v Dzipange and Another (830 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 830 (16 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: ZUWA DAMSON versus LOVEMORE DZIPANGE and ELINAH DZIPANGE
citation: Damson v Dzipange and Another (830 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 830 (16 November 2022)
subject_area: Civil procedure; urgent chamber application; stay of execution; rescission of judgment; family land dispute; offer letter land.
catchwords_flynote: Stay of execution – application pending rescission of default judgment – real and substantial justice – family dispute over occupation of offer-letter farm – execution partly suspended – applicant limited to the land she occupied at date of judgment.
headnote: The applicant sought to stay enforcement of a judgment which had declared her parents to be the rightful offer-letter holders of Subdivision 2 of Lot 1 of Marivale and interdicted her from occupation and interference. She had already filed an application for rescission. The Court held that a stay of execution may be granted where real and substantial justice requires it. Although much of the interim relief sought went beyond the judgment to be stayed, enforcement was suspended pending the rescission application except for the interdict restraining interference with the respondents’ enjoyment of the property. The applicant was limited to the piece of land she occupied on the date of judgment.
issues_for_determination:
Whether execution of the earlier judgment should be stayed pending rescission.
    Whether real and substantial justice required protection of the applicant pending determination of the rescission application.
    Whether the applicant could obtain relief beyond the scope of the judgment whose execution she sought to stay.
    Whether non-joinder of the Sheriff was fatal.
legal_principles: Execution is a process of the court and the court has inherent power to control it. In a stay of execution application, the governing consideration is whether real and substantial justice requires the stay. A stay pending rescission may be granted where execution would render the rescission application academic or cause injustice. Non-joinder is not always fatal where the matter can be determined between the parties before the court.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the application for rescission was pending and was a lawful process.
    Held, that further execution could render the rescission application academic.
    Held, that most relief sought by the applicant went beyond the default judgment and could not be granted as framed.
    Held, that execution of the earlier judgment should be suspended pending rescission, except for the paragraph interdicting the applicant from interfering with the respondents’ use and occupation.
    Held, that the applicant could occupy only the portion she occupied on the date of the earlier judgment.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that, in the exercise of the court’s inherent power over execution, real and substantial justice justified preserving the applicant’s existing occupation pending rescission, while maintaining the restraint against interference with the respondents’ enjoyment of the rest of the farm.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that the family relationship had broken down severely and that a land dispute between parents and child was unfortunate, but the applicant had no justification for interfering with the respondents’ occupation of the rest of the farm.

document_name: S v Sigauke and Another (831 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 831 (7 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Peter Sigauke and Enock Mukwekwe v The State
subject_area: Criminal appeal; criminal abuse of duty as public officers; council land; corruption; factual findings; sentencing
catchwords_flynote: Criminal abuse of duty - public officers - council chief executive officer and planning officer - creation and sale of council stands - proceeds pocketed - false defence of lease under Ease of Doing Business concept - appeal against conviction and sentence - factual findings upheld - deterrent custodial sentence upheld.
headnote: The appellants, the chief executive officer and planning officer of Mutoko Rural District Council, were convicted of three counts of criminal abuse of duty as public officers. The court a quo found that they illegally created and sold commercial stands from council land and pocketed the proceeds. On appeal, they challenged credibility findings and argued the court had not adequately assessed all State witnesses. The High Court held that the evidence, including offer letters, purchaser testimony and back-dated lease agreements, overwhelmingly showed that the appellants had sold the stands and tried to cover their tracks. The appeal against conviction and sentence was dismissed.
issues_for_determination: Whether the trial court misdirected itself in assessing credibility and evidence; whether the land was sold illegally or merely leased under a council-authorised model; whether the appellants abused their duties; and whether the sentence was excessive.
legal_principles: An appellate court will not lightly interfere with factual findings unless they are outrageously irrational or unsupported by the evidence. Imperfections or inconsistencies in witness evidence must go to the root of the matter before they vitiate a conviction. Public officers who act contrary to their duties to show favour may commit criminal abuse of duty. In corruption-related offences, deterrence and public indignation are central sentencing considerations.
held_decision_points: The appeal against conviction and sentence was dismissed in its entirety.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that documentary evidence of offer letters, purchaser payments, absence of council authority and back-dated lease agreements can establish criminal abuse of duty where public officers unlawfully sell council land and retain proceeds.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted that a judgment need not mention every item of evidence to show that it was considered; form must not be elevated over substance.
cases_referred_to: State v Mashonganyika 2018 (1) ZLR 217; State v Swanhawk (Pvt) Ltd 1973 (2) RLR 70; R v Dhluwayo and Another 1948 (2) SA 677 (A); State v Lawrence and Another 1989 (1) ZLR 29 (S); State v Ngara 1987 (1) ZLR 91 (S); State v Mukwezva 1992 (2) ZLR 283 (S).
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 174(1)(a).
reviewer_comments: Full text extracted from the mounted DOCX.

document_name: Hilmax Engineering (Private) Limited v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority (832 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 832 (17 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: HILMAX ENGINEERING (PRIVATE) LIMITED versus ZIMBABWE REVENUE AUTHORITY
subject_area: Tax administration; search and seizure; spoliation; ZIMRA powers; laptops and documents; urgent chamber application.
catchwords_flynote: Urgent application – ZIMRA search and seizure during tax investigation – seizure of laptops and documents – whether officers authorised to seize computers – statutory distinction between documents and computer equipment – laptops unlawfully taken – documents lawfully taken – partial spoliatory relief granted.
headnote: The applicant sought return of laptops, documents and files seized by ZIMRA officers during an investigation. ZIMRA opposed and argued that its officers acted within statutory powers. The Court held that ZIMRA’s statutory powers permitted seizure of documents for investigation but did not authorise seizure of laptops or computer equipment. The application therefore succeeded only in respect of the laptops. ZIMRA was ordered to return the five laptops within 24 hours, and each party was to bear its own costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the opposing affidavit was properly authorised.
    Whether ZIMRA officers had statutory authority to seize laptops.
    Whether ZIMRA officers could seize documents and files in the course of investigation.
    Whether spoliatory relief should be granted.
legal_principles: A corporate litigant acts through authorised natural persons; authority may be challenged, and proof may be required depending on the circumstances. Tax statutes authorise ZIMRA to inspect and seize documents for investigation, but powers to take documents do not necessarily include powers to seize computers, laptops or information retrieval systems. Mandament van spolie restores possession where deprivation was unlawful.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the application succeeded in part.
    Held, that ZIMRA unlawfully seized the laptops.
    Held, that ZIMRA lawfully took documents and files under the relevant statutory provisions.
    Held, that ZIMRA must return the five laptops within 24 hours.
    Held, that each party bears its own costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that ZIMRA’s power to seize documents and computer printouts does not extend by implication to seizing laptops or computer equipment, so taking the laptops was unlawful and required restoration, while the seizure of documents during tax investigation was authorised.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified

    Madzivire & Ors v Zvarivadza HH 74/06
    African Banking Corporation of Zimbabwe Limited t/a Banc ABC v PWC Motors (Pvt) Ltd & 3 Ors HH 123/13
    Mayor Logistics v ZIMRA CCZ 7/13

document_name: S v Madzima (833 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 833 (22 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Madzima v The State
subject_area: Criminal procedure; condonation; late appeal; robbery; identification; sentence
catchwords_flynote: Condonation – late noting of appeal after seven years – self-actor – robbery convictions – identification and recovery evidence – no reasonable explanation or prospects – application dismissed.
headnote: The applicant sought condonation for the late noting of an appeal and leave to prosecute it in person, seven years after sentence for two counts of robbery. The High Court found the explanation for delay unsatisfactory and the appeal prospects poor. Evidence included dock identification, prior descriptions, interlinked counts and recovery of a stolen Highway Code from the applicant’s rural home. The application was dismissed.
issues_for_determination: Whether the delay should be condoned; whether there were prospects of success on conviction or sentence; whether leave to prosecute in person should be granted.
legal_principles: Condonation requires consideration of the length of delay, explanation for delay and prospects of success. A concession by the State does not bind the court. Identification-parade absence is not automatically fatal where the evidence as a whole proves guilt.
held_decision_points: Application for condonation and leave to prosecute appeal in person dismissed.
ratio_decidendi: A seven-year delay with a generalised explanation and weak appeal grounds does not justify condonation.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court rejected the State’s concession as unsatisfactory.
cases_referred_to: Kombayi v Berkhaut 1988 (1) ZLR 53 (S); Fuyana v Moyo SC 54/06.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 126; Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07].

document_name: HARRIS~1.DOC
case_name: HARRISON HOLDINGS (PVT) LTD v MUNAKIRI LEAF TOBACCO (PVT) LTD AND ANOTHER / MUNAKIRI LEAF TOBACCO (PVT) LTD AND ANOTHER v HARRISON HOLDINGS (PVT) LTD
subject_area: Arbitration; registration of arbitral award; setting aside arbitral award; public policy; currency law; lease; rentals; holding-over damages.
catchwords_flynote: Arbitration – consolidated applications – registration of arbitral award – counter-application to set aside award – award sounding solely in United States dollars – public policy and currency legislation – award refused registration and set aside – dismissal for want of prosecution abandoned.
headnote: Three matters were consolidated: an application to register an arbitral award, an application to set aside the same award, and an application for dismissal for want of prosecution. The award ordered payment of rental arrears and holding-over damages in United States dollars and eviction from leased premises. The respondents argued that the award violated Zimbabwean currency law and public policy. The Court held that the award was contrary to public policy insofar as it made an award solely in United States dollars. Registration was refused, the award was set aside, and the dismissal application was abandoned.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the arbitral award should be registered as an order of the High Court.
    Whether the award was contrary to the public policy of Zimbabwe.
    Whether currency legislation rendered the United States dollar award unlawful.
    Whether the award should be set aside.
legal_principles: Under the Arbitration Act, a court may refuse recognition or registration of an arbitral award if it is contrary to public policy. An award may also be set aside on public-policy grounds. Currency legislation and the legal status of payment obligations are relevant to the public-policy analysis.
held_decision_points:
Held, that HC 2995/22, the chamber application for dismissal for want of prosecution, was abandoned by consent.
    Held, that the application to register the arbitral award in HC 1229/22 was dismissed with costs.
    Held, that the application to set aside the arbitral award in HC 1566/22 was granted with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that an arbitral award that disregards applicable currency law and orders payment solely in United States dollars in circumstances where local currency legislation applies offends the public policy of Zimbabwe and should not be registered; the same public-policy defect justifies setting aside the award.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted that although it was bound by earlier authority, the currency-law position had been radically affected, making the award clearly wrong in its United States dollar formulation.
cases_referred_to:
ZESA v Maposa 1999 (2) ZLR 452 (S)
    Relevant authorities on public policy and currency law cited in the judgment.
legislation_considered: Arbitration Act [Chapter 7:15], Articles 34, 35 and 36; Exchange Control and currency statutory instruments including SI 212/19 and SI 85/2020 as discussed.

document_name: S v Mandiko (836 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 836 (18 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Mandiko
judges: MUTEVEDZI J; Assessors: Mr Chimonyo and Mr Barwa
subject_area: Criminal law; murder; mental disorder; insanity; Mental Health Act procedure; legal aid
catchwords_flynote: Murder – accused detained in psychiatric unit – killing fellow inmate with hoe – not guilty because of insanity – s 28 and s 29 Mental Health Act procedures explained – return to Chikurubi Prison Psychiatric Unit.
headnote: The accused, a suspected mental patient, was charged with murder after striking another inmate at Chikurubi Maximum Psychiatric Unit with a hoe. The State did not contest that he was mentally disordered at the time. The High Court explained the different procedures under ss 28 and 29 of the Mental Health Act and, on the medical evidence, returned a special verdict of not guilty because of insanity. The accused was ordered returned to Chikurubi Prison Psychiatric Unit for treatment.
issues_for_determination: Whether the accused was mentally disordered or intellectually handicapped at the time of the act; what procedure applies where mental disorder is raised at trial; what order should follow a special verdict.
legal_principles: Section 28 of the Mental Health Act concerns mental fitness during trial; s 29 concerns mental disorder at the time of committing the offence. Under s 29(2), the court must be satisfied from evidence, including medical evidence, that the accused did the act but had a complete defence due to mental disorder, and must then return a special verdict of not guilty because of insanity.
held_decision_points: Accused found not guilty because of insanity; ordered returned to Chikurubi Prison Psychiatric Unit for treatment.
ratio_decidendi: The medical and factual evidence established that the accused was mentally disordered at the material time and could not be held criminally responsible.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The judgment comments on pro deo allocation and the need to better match legal representation to criminal-law competence in serious cases involving mental illness.
cases_referred_to: S v Fungayi Chiwaya HH 821/22 cited in discussion of prosecution of mental-health cases.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 47; Mental Health Act [Chapter 15:12], ss 28, 29; Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], s 314.
constitutional_provisions_considered: Constitution of Zimbabwe, s 70(1)(e), on State-funded legal representation where substantial injustice would otherwise result.
reviewer_comments: Drafted from the mounted source judgment and retained for legal editorial review.

document_name: MIRACL~1.DOC
case_name: Miracles of Heaven Housing Cooperative and Another v Dangaranga and Others
subject_area: Housing cooperative; interdict; locus standi; bar for late heads of argument; material disputes of fact; State land.
catchwords_flynote: Housing cooperative – alleged unauthorised sale and allocation of State land – late heads of argument – upliftment of bar refused – material disputes of fact on authority and conduct – application struck off roll.
headnote: The applicants sought to interdict the respondents from selling or allocating State land in the name of Miracles of Heaven Housing Cooperative and to compel return of receipt books and cooperative documents. The respondents raised locus standi, lack of prima facie right and domestic-remedy points, and denied selling stands. Their heads of argument were filed late and the application to uplift the bar was refused. The court nevertheless considered the merits and held that there were material disputes of fact concerning whether the respondents were selling stands or acting without authority. The application was struck off the roll with no order as to costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether the respondents were barred for late heads of argument; whether the second applicant had authority/locus; whether domestic remedies under the Cooperative Societies Act had to be exhausted; whether disputes of fact prevented determination on the papers.
legal_principles: Late heads of argument may result in a bar unless uplifted on proper explanation. In motion proceedings, a court should not decide genuine, material disputes of fact on affidavit where injustice may result. An applicant who should foresee disputes of fact proceeds by application at risk.
held_decision_points: The respondents’ application for upliftment of bar was dismissed. The matter was considered on the merits. Because there were material disputes of fact that could not be resolved on the papers, the application was struck off the roll with no order as to costs.
ratio_decidendi: The central allegation of unauthorised/fraudulent sale and allocation of stands was disputed and could not be resolved without oral evidence. The interdict could not be granted on the affidavits alone.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court criticised counsel’s attempt to rely on the five-days-before-hearing rule despite the prior bar.
cases_referred_to: Lesley Faye Marsh (Pvt) Ltd t/a Premier Diamonds & Ors v African Banking Corporation of Zimbabwe (Pvt) Ltd SC 4/19; Masukusa v National Foods Ltd & Anor 1983 (1) ZLR 232 (H); Zimbabwe Bonded Fibreglass (Pvt) Ltd v Peech 1987 (2) ZLR 338 (S).
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, r 59; Cooperative Societies Act [Chapter 24:05], including ss 115 and 116.
reviewer_comments: Draft. Short filename preserved; neutral citation not supplied in filename.

document_name: S v Kabisira (838 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 838 (17 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Vhare v The State
subject_area: Criminal law; criminal appeal; robbery; firearm; sentence; aggravating circumstances
catchwords_flynote: Criminal appeal – robbery with firearm – complainant deceived by motor vehicle sale story – no need for recovery of weapon – robbery in aggravating circumstances – non-custodial sentence not competent – appeal dismissed.
headnote: The appellant appealed against conviction and sentence for robbery. The High Court rejected new allegations of bad blood from gold dealings, found no basis to interfere with credibility findings, and held that failure to recover the firearm did not negate the complainant’s evidence that a pistol had been used. The appeal against sentence also failed because robbery committed with a firearm is robbery in aggravating circumstances and attracts imprisonment.
issues_for_determination: Whether the conviction was safe despite alleged inconsistency, non-recovery of the firearm, and the appellant’s claimed prior business dealings with the complainant; whether the effective custodial sentence was excessive.
legal_principles: Recovery of the weapon is not essential if credible evidence proves the use of a weapon. Robbery committed with a firearm is committed in aggravating circumstances under s 126(3), and s 126(2)(a) prescribes imprisonment rather than a fine or community service.
held_decision_points: Appeal against conviction and sentence dismissed in its entirety.
ratio_decidendi: The State evidence proved the robbery beyond reasonable doubt; non-recovery of the firearm was not fatal and the sentence was lenient rather than excessive.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted that a five-year effective sentence for robbery with a firearm where nothing was recovered was very lenient.
cases_referred_to: None identified.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 126.

document_name: MUCHEN~1.DOC
case_name: Muchenje and Another v Minister of Local Government and Public Works N.O. and Others
subject_area: Spoliation; contempt of court; lease cancellation; Carrick Creagh property; counter-application; interim occupation.
catchwords_flynote: Mandament van spolie – occupation of stand – offloading bricks outside property and placing cabin on premises – no deprivation of possession – contempt of status quo order not established – counter-application granted by consent.
headnote: The applicants sought a mandament van spolie, alternatively contempt of court, after the first respondent delivered bricks, placed a wooden cabin at Stand 227 Carrick Creagh and wrote to the City of Harare regarding inspection of structures. The court held that the applicants remained in occupation. The alleged acts did not constitute deprivation of possession and did not breach the status quo order. The spoliation and contempt application failed. The counter-application, by consent, interdicted the applicants from constructing permanent structures pending eviction proceedings in the Magistrates Court.
issues_for_determination: Whether the applicants were deprived of possession; whether offloading bricks, bringing a cabin and writing to the City of Harare amounted to spoliation or contempt; whether counter-relief should be granted.
legal_principles: Spoliation requires peaceful and undisturbed possession and unlawful deprivation of that possession. Mere interference or nuisance is insufficient. Contempt requires wilful disobedience of a court order.
held_decision_points: The main application was dismissed. The counter-application was granted by consent, interdicting construction of brick and mortar or permanent structures pending eviction proceedings.
ratio_decidendi: The applicants were still in possession and had not been locked out or ejected. The acts complained of did not dispossess them and did not violate the status quo order.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: If the conduct made occupation uncomfortable, the appropriate remedy would have been an interdict, but the applicants would face difficulty establishing a right after withdrawal of the review challenging lease cancellation.
cases_referred_to: Botha & Anor v Barrett 1996 (2) ZLR 73 (S); Kama Construction (Pvt) Ltd v Cold Comfort Farm Cooperative & Others 1999 (2) ZLR 19 (S); Mutsotso & Ors v Commissioner of Police & Ors 1993 (2) ZLR 329 (H); Kwik-Pak (Pvt) Ltd v Mashiringwana & Anor 2009 (2) ZLR 292 (H); Lindsay v Lindsay (2) 1995 (1) ZLR 296 (S); Haddow v Haddow 1974 (1) RLR 5 (G).

document_name: Chingosho v Musariri (841 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 841 (18 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: CHRISETTA CHINGOSHO versus JULIET MUSARIRI
citation: Chingosho v Musariri (841 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 841 (18 November 2022)
subject_area: Civil trial; prescription; void agreement of sale; restitution; unjust enrichment; immovable property.
catchwords_flynote: Prescription – claim following void sale of immovable property – cause of action arose when High Court held sale nullity – criminal restitution later ordered – whether restitution interrupted prescription – civil claim dismissed as prescribed.
headnote: The plaintiff sued for US$15 000 after an earlier judgment held that an agreement of sale of immovable property was a nullity because the defendant had no authority to sell property belonging to a minor estate. The Court held that the plaintiff’s recourse against the defendant arose when that judgment was delivered in February 2011. The plaintiff should have instituted proceedings within three years. The later criminal prosecution and restitution order did not revive or interrupt a claim which had already prescribed. The claim was dismissed, each party bearing its own costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the plaintiff’s claim had prescribed.
    Whether the claim was competent after the sale agreement had been declared void ab initio.
    Whether either party was unjustly enriched.
    Whether criminal restitution affected prescription of the civil claim.
legal_principles: A debt prescribes three years after the cause of action arises unless prescription is interrupted in a legally cognisable way. A later criminal prosecution and restitution order do not revive a civil claim that has already prescribed. Section 18 of the Prescription Act deals with interruption by acknowledgment of liability, but there must be an effective acknowledgment before prescription has run its full course.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the cause of action arose at the latest on 17 February 2011 when the High Court held that the sale was a nullity and that the plaintiff’s recourse lay against the defendant.
    Held, that civil proceedings should have been instituted by 17 February 2014.
    Held, that the criminal proceedings instituted in 2015 and concluded in 2016 were of no moment because the civil claim had already prescribed.
    Held, that the remaining issues did not need determination.
    Held, that the claim was dismissed.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the plaintiff’s claim for recovery or damages after the void sale accrued when the court first declared the agreement a nullity and pointed to recourse against the defendant; failure to sue within three years rendered the later civil claim prescribed notwithstanding subsequent criminal restitution.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that criminal proceedings do not preclude the institution of civil proceedings for damages, but they do not revive a prescribed civil claim.
cases_referred_to:
The Forestry Commission v Varden Safaris (Pvt) Ltd SC 58/16
    HH 44/11, Chingosho v Musariri proceedings under HC 3370/07

document_name: Katsimberis and Another v Sharpe and 5 Others (842 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 842 (16 November 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: GEORGE KATSIMBERIS AND ANOTHER versus KENNETH RAYDON SHARPE AND 5 OTHERS
subject_area: Civil procedure; issue estoppel; res judicata; interdict; land development; joint venture dispute.
catchwords_flynote: Interdict – repeated attempts to restrain sale of disputed property/subdivisions – prior urgent applications struck off – issue estoppel – High Court cannot sit on appeal over previous judgment – application dismissed with costs.
headnote: The applicants sought an interdict barring the respondents from selling stands or subdivisions of a disputed land parcel pending disposal of other proceedings. The same broad dispute had previously featured in urgent applications that were struck off, including HH 771/20 where issue estoppel was discussed. The Court upheld the respondents’ point in limine that the matter was barred by issue estoppel and dismissed the application with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the application was barred by res judicata or issue estoppel.
    Whether the previous judgments prevented the applicants from bringing the same cause again.
    Whether the Court could revisit alleged errors in the previous judgment.
    Whether an interdict should be granted.
legal_principles: Issue estoppel is a species of res judicata and applies where an issue that was a necessary ingredient in a previous cause of action has already been decided or effectively disposed of. A matter struck off for procedural defects and not rectified within the required time may be treated as abandoned. The High Court cannot sit as an appeal or review court over its own judgment.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the respondents’ point in limine based on issue estoppel was upheld.
    Held, that the application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the applicants had previously presented the same essential issue—the right to stop sale, disposal or transfer of the disputed land—and after failing to regularise the earlier struck-off matters, they were estopped from bringing the same cause again.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court stated that the applicants argued the matter as if it were appeal proceedings against HH 771/20, which the High Court could not entertain.
cases_referred_to: Katsimberis and Another v Sharpe and Others HH 771/20; Bindura Municipality v Mugogo SC 32-15; authorities on res judicata and issue estoppel as cited.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 1971; High Court Rules, 2021; principles of res judicata and issue estoppel.
subject_area_terms: Civil procedure; issue estoppel; res judicata; interdict; land development; joint venture dispute.
cases_referred_to_terms: Katsimberis and Another v Sharpe and Others HH 771/20
Bindura Municipality v Mugogo SC 32-15; authorities on res judicata and issue estoppel as cited.
legislation_considered_terms: High Court Rules, 2021; principles of res judicata and issue estoppel.

document_name: Tobacco Industry and Marketing Board v Muvirimi (843 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 843 (21 September 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Tobacco Industry and Marketing Board v Muvirimi
subject_area: Urgent application; actio rei vindicatio; employer vehicle; retrenchment; possession after termination of employment; employment benefits; costs.
catchwords_flynote: Actio rei vindicatio – employer-owned vehicle allocated as employment benefit – employee retrenched and package approved – respondent refused to return vehicle – urgency based on risk of wear and tear – whether respondent had right of retention under motor vehicle policy.
headnote: The applicant sought vindication of a Toyota Hilux allocated to the respondent as an employment benefit. The respondent had been retrenched and had received his retrenchment package, but refused to return the vehicle. The court found that the applicant owned the vehicle and that the respondent possessed it without consent. The respondent’s reliance on a clause in the motor vehicle policy did not establish a right of retention.
issues_for_determination: Whether the matter was urgent; whether the applicant established the requirements of actio rei vindicatio; and whether the respondent had any valid defence or right to retain the vehicle after termination of employment.
legal_principles: For actio rei vindicatio, the applicant must prove ownership and that the respondent possesses the property without the owner’s consent. A former employee does not retain an employer-owned vehicle merely because a policy says vehicle benefits are to be handled under a retrenchment package, unless the approved package actually confers a retention right. A meritless defence does not automatically justify attorney-client costs absent mala fides.
held_decision_points: The court held the matter urgent. The requirements of actio rei vindicatio were established. The respondent had no right to retain the vehicle; the approved retrenchment package did not include it. The relief was granted, but attorney-client costs were deleted because mala fides were not shown.
ratio_decidendi: The respondent’s possession was originally lawful only as an employment benefit. Once the employment relationship ended and the retrenchment package did not give him the vehicle, his continued possession lacked legal basis.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified.
cases_referred_to: None specifically identified in the extracted judgment text.
legislation_considered: Common-law actio rei vindicatio; applicant’s motor vehicle policy; employment/retrenchment context.
reviewer_comments: The extracted judgment heading uses “and” rather than “versus” between the parties; the case name follows the filename convention.

document_name: BRIGHT~1.DOC
case_name: BRIGHTLAND FARMING (PRIVATE) LIMITED versus REDAN PETROLEUM (PVT) LTD and BEVERLEY CULVERWELL
hearing_date: 23, 24 and 25 January 2019; 12, 18 and 20 March 2019; 9 and 20 September 2019; 17 and 23 October 2019; 14 May 2021; 9 and 11 June 2021; 20 July 2021; 30 June 2022
subject_area: Delict; damages; unlawful removal of property; landlord and tenant; non-joinder; causation; proof of quantum; civil trial.
catchwords_flynote: Delictual damages – claim for USD1.3 million following removal and disposal of bakery equipment – plaintiff alleging defendants unlawfully removed equipment from leased premises – defendants saying landlord/owner caused removal after abandonment – causation and liability not proved against defendants – quantum not proved – plaintiff's claim dismissed with costs.
headnote: The plaintiff claimed damages for the removal and disposal of bakery equipment from premises it had leased from Crestlane Investments. It alleged that the defendants acted in common purpose to remove and dispose of the equipment. The defendants denied liability and asserted that the landlord removed the property after the plaintiff abandoned the premises. The Court held that although the plaintiff suffered loss, the loss was not caused by the defendants. The plaintiff also failed to prove the quantum of its damages because the evidence did not establish the property removed, its condition, or its value at the date of removal. The claim was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Who removed the plaintiff's property and in what circumstances.
    Whether the removal was unlawful or wrongful.
    Whether the plaintiff suffered loss as a result of the removal.
    Whether the loss was recoverable from the defendants.
    Whether the plaintiff proved the amount of damages.
    Whether the second defendant was properly joined.
legal_principles: A plaintiff in delict must prove wrongful conduct, causation, loss and quantum. The correct measure of damages for lost or damaged movable property is its value at the time of the delict. The plaintiff bears the onus of proving both the identity/condition of the items and their value. The court will not calculate damages for a litigant from incomplete or unexplained documentation.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the plaintiff suffered loss following removal and disposal of its property.
    Held, that the loss was not proved to have been caused by the defendants.
    Held, that the plaintiff failed to prove the quantum of loss because valuations and lists were insufficient and did not establish value at the date of removal.
    Held, that the plaintiff failed to prove liability and damages.
    Held, that the claim was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that a delictual damages claim for removed property fails where the plaintiff does not prove that the defendants caused the loss and does not prove the value of the property at the time of the alleged delict through reliable evidence.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted that the plaintiff could not simply rely on old valuations or ask the court to calculate damages from auction documents not properly proved by evidence.
cases_referred_to: Munhuwa v Mhukahuru Bus Service (Pvt) Ltd 1994 (2) ZLR 382 (H)
legislation_considered: Common-law delict and damages principles; lease/statutory tenancy principles considered in the background.

document_name: ZIMBAB~4.DOC
case_name: Zimbabwe Platinum Mines (Pvt) Ltd v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority and Another
subject_area: Tax law; income tax; declaratur; foreign currency and local currency income; allowable deductions; apportionment; Public Notices 36 and 57 of 2021; mining taxpayer.
catchwords_flynote: Declaratory order – taxpayer earning revenue and incurring expenditure in foreign and local currency – dispute over apportionment of allowable deductions – whether expenses should be deducted by currency of invoice or by 60:40 foreign-currency retention ratio – application dismissed.
headnote: The applicant sought a declaratur that its interpretation of ZIMRA Public Notices 36 and 57 of 2021 was correct. It argued that income tax liability should be calculated by deducting expenditure incurred in foreign currency from foreign currency revenue and expenditure incurred in local currency from local currency revenue. ZIMRA argued that deductions had to be apportioned consistently with the income split under the RBZ retention ratio. The court held that, reading s 4A of the Finance Act with s 15 of the Income Tax Act, ZIMRA’s approach was correct. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether declaratory relief was available instead of review; whether the applicant’s mixed-currency tax method was lawful; whether allowable deductions should follow the currency of the invoice; whether s 4A(10) of the Finance Act applied only to income or also required alignment of expenditure; whether ZIMRA’s public notices attempted to legislate; and whether the application should be dismissed.
legal_principles: Declaratory relief under s 14 of the High Court Act may be granted where an interested party seeks determination of an existing, future or contingent right, and the question is not abstract. In tax law, the court must adhere to the wording of the taxing statute. Expenditure is deductible only to the extent incurred for trade or in production of income. The matching principle requires income and expenditure to be matched for tax purposes. Mixed currency tax obligations must be interpreted by reading the Finance Act and Income Tax Act together.
held_decision_points: The preliminary objection to the use of declaratory proceedings was dismissed. On the merits, the court held that the applicant’s proposed apportionment by currency of invoice had no legal backing. ZIMRA’s approach, using the applicable retention ratio to align revenue and expenditure, was upheld. The application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The law required the matching of expenditure with the income it produced. Since the applicant could not identify which specific currency expenditure produced which income, and since the statutory regime treated the foreign-currency income through the retention ratio, the same logic had to apply to deductions. ZIMRA’s notices did not legislate but unpacked the statutory position.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified beyond the court’s public-policy comment that the declaratory issue was important to taxpayers with mixed foreign and local currency income and expenditure.
cases_referred_to: Adbro Investments Co Ltd v Minister of the Interior & Ors 1961 (3) SA 283 (T); Munn Publishing (Pvt) Ltd v Zimbabwe Broadcasting Corporation 1994 (1) ZLR 337 (S); Johnsen v Agricultural Finance Corp 1995 (1) ZLR 65 (S); Movement for Democratic Change v President of Zimbabwe & Ors 2007 (1) ZLR 257 (H); Bulawayo Bottlers (Pvt) Ltd v Minister of Labour, Manpower Planning and Social Welfare & Ors 1988 (2) ZLR 129 (HC); Family Benefit Friendly Society v Commissioner for Inland Revenue & Anor 1995 (4) SA 120 (T); Partington v Attorney-General (1896) LR 4 HL 100; Cape Brandy Syndicate v Commissioners of Inland Revenue 1921 (1) KB 64; Delta Beverages (Pvt) Ltd v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority SC 3-22.
legislation_considered: High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], s 14; Income Tax Act [Chapter 23:06], ss 6, 7, 8, 14, 15 and 37; Finance Act [Chapter 23:04], s 4A; Revenue Authority Act [Chapter 23:12].
reviewer_comments: Short filename ZIMBAB~4.DOC preserved. Neutral citation field marked Not available because the uploaded filename does not supply one, although the judgment heading records HH 845-22.

document_name: S v Chinyemba (846 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 846 (21 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Chinyemba
hearing_date: Not applicable; criminal review determined on the record
subject_area: Criminal review – domestic violence – sentence – assault with golf stick – leniency challenge
catchwords_flynote: Domestic violence; physical abuse; sentence review; assaults to the head; whether sentence too lenient; charge selection.
headnote: The accused was convicted after trial on two counts of physical abuse under the Domestic Violence Act for striking two complainants on the head with a golf stick. The trial court treated the counts as one and imposed 12 months’ imprisonment, with 5 months suspended and the remainder suspended on community service.
issues_for_determination: Whether the sentence was so lenient as to be contrary to real and substantial justice; whether the chosen charge and sentence required interference on review.
legal_principles: Review interference with sentence requires justification. Sentencing must consider the gravity of the assault, weapon used, part of the body targeted, injuries, mitigation and comparable cases.
held_decision_points: The court saw no reason to interfere with the trial court’s decision.
ratio_decidendi: Although striking complainants on the head with a golf stick is serious, the medical evidence and surrounding circumstances did not justify review interference with the sentence imposed.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court questioned why physical abuse under the Domestic Violence Act was charged instead of ordinary assault under the Criminal Law Code.
cases_referred_to: S v Madyambudzi; S v Shonhiwa; S v Modekayi Ncube.
legislation_considered: Domestic Violence Act [Chapter 5:16], ss 3(1)(a), 4(1); Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 89.

document_name: EASTRI~1.DOC
case_name: EAST RIVER INVESTMENTS (PVT) LTD and BISHOP JECHE versus HOSEAH MUJAYA N.O and THE STATE
hearing_date: 13 October, 3 November, 7 November, 8 November, 16 November, 17 November and 23 November 2022
subject_area: Criminal procedure; review of unterminated criminal proceedings; application for discharge at close of State case; perjury; fraud; money laundering; repeal and savings provisions.
catchwords_flynote: Review – magistrate’s refusal to discharge accused at close of State case – High Court interference with unterminated proceedings only in exceptional circumstances – perjury/fraud/money laundering charges – repealed offence saved for pre-repeal conduct – review dismissed and ruling upheld.
headnote: The applicants sought review of a magistrate’s decision dismissing their application for discharge at the close of the State case in criminal proceedings involving perjury, fraud and money laundering allegations linked to property transfer and affidavits. The High Court held that interference with unterminated criminal proceedings is exceptional and that the applicants had not established one of the recognised grounds. The argument that the money-laundering charge was invalid because the statute had been repealed failed because the alleged conduct predated repeal and was covered by savings provisions. The review was dismissed.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the High Court should interfere with an unterminated criminal trial.
    Whether the magistrate’s refusal to discharge the applicants was grossly irregular or irrational.
    Whether the alleged perjury/fraud evidence required the applicants to be placed on their defence.
    Whether a money-laundering charge under a repealed law remained competent for pre-repeal conduct.
legal_principles: Superior courts interfere with unterminated criminal proceedings only in very exceptional cases, such as proven gross irregularity, lack of jurisdiction, bias, or a decision so outrageous in defiance of logic that no reasonable court could have made it. At discharge stage, the test is whether there is evidence on which a reasonable court might convict; the standard is lower than proof beyond reasonable doubt. Repeal of an enactment does not necessarily affect prior liabilities, legal proceedings or offences where savings provisions apply.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicants had not established exceptional circumstances warranting review intervention.
    Held, that the first respondent’s decision did not satisfy the stringent test for interference with unterminated proceedings.
    Held, that the money-laundering charge was not invalid merely because the statute was repealed, since the alleged conduct predated repeal and was protected by a savings provision.
    Held, that the application for review was dismissed.
    Held, that the magistrate’s decision dismissing the discharge application was upheld.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the High Court will not use review proceedings to halt a pending criminal trial unless the ruling is vitiated by exceptional gross irregularity; none was proved, and the State had led enough evidence to justify putting the applicants on their defence.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court warned that routine interference with unterminated lower-court proceedings would paralyse trials and turn the High Court into an appeals court over interlocutory criminal rulings.
cases_referred_to:
Attorney-General v Makamba 2005 (2) ZLR 54 (S)
    Mamombe and Another v Mushure N.O and Another ZWCC 4/2022
    Mutasa case referred to in the judgment
    Authorities on review of unterminated proceedings and section 198(3) discharge
legislation_considered: Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], sections 146(1) and 198(3); Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], sections 183(1) and 136; Serious Offences (Confiscation of Profits) Act [Chapter 9:17], section 63(1)(b); Money Laundering and Proceeds of Crime Act [Chapter 9:24], section 104; Interpretation Act savings provisions.
constitutional_provisions_considered: Constitution of Zimbabwe, sections 56(1), 69(1), 70(1)(a) and 86(3)(e) referred to by the applicants.

document_name: S v Chiweza (848 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 848 (22 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Chiweza
judges: MUTEVEDZI J; Assessors: Mr Barwa and Mr Shenje
subject_area: Criminal law; murder; defence of person/self-defence; private defence; evidence
catchwords_flynote: Murder – fist fight between friends – deceased as aggressor – defence of person – requirements under ss 252-254 of the Criminal Law Code – reasonable force – state failing to prove murder beyond reasonable doubt.
headnote: The accused was charged with murder after a fatal fight with the deceased. The court accepted that the deceased had provoked and attacked the accused both at a bar and later near the accused’s home, and that the accused’s response with fists and booted feet occurred in circumstances of self-defence. The court found that the requirements for defence of person were satisfied and acquitted the accused.
issues_for_determination: Whether the accused caused the deceased’s death with the required mens rea for murder; whether the accused acted in defence of person.
legal_principles: A person may rely on defence of person where there is an unlawful attack, the attack has commenced or is imminent, the conduct is necessary to avert the attack, the means used are reasonable, and the harm is not grossly disproportionate.
held_decision_points: The defence of person succeeded; the accused was found not guilty and acquitted of murder.
ratio_decidendi: Where the state cannot exclude a reasonable possibility that the accused acted in lawful self-defence, criminal liability for murder is not proved beyond reasonable doubt.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court cautioned against using “nice intellectual callipers” to measure defensive conduct after the event; a practical and common-sense approach is required.
cases_referred_to: S v Sweswe HB 184/18; S v Nyawo HB 245/20; S v Justin Watanhauka HH 342/13.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], ss 47, 252, 253, 254; Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07].
reviewer_comments: Drafted from the mounted source judgment and retained for legal editorial review.

document_name: S v Scenara (849 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 849 (23 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: The State v John Scenara
subject_area: Criminal law; murder; domestic violence; confession; admissibility of confirmed warned and cautioned statement; circumstantial corroboration
catchwords_flynote: Murder - domestic violence - accused’s estranged wife killed with axe - confirmed warned and cautioned statement - challenge to confession - onus on accused under s 256 - independent proof of offence under s 273 - confession corroborated by surrounding evidence - accused found guilty.
headnote: The accused was charged with murdering his estranged wife by striking her with an axe. He denied killing her and alleged that the police assaulted him into making a statement. The State relied on admitted witness evidence, the post-mortem report, and a confirmed warned and cautioned statement. The court held that the confirmation procedure before a magistrate provided a clear opportunity to raise any coercion, and that the accused failed to prove the statement was not freely and voluntarily made. Independent evidence showed that the deceased died unnaturally, that the accused had threatened and attempted violence against her, and that his shirt and blanket were found wrapped around her body. The confession was therefore accepted as genuine.
issues_for_determination: Whether the accused’s confirmed warned and cautioned statement was admissible; whether the State proved independently that the offence was committed; whether the confession was genuine and sufficiently corroborated; and whether the accused was guilty of murder.
legal_principles: A confirmed warned and cautioned statement is received in evidence upon mere production, subject to the accused proving that it was not made by him or was not made freely and voluntarily. A court may convict on a confession if the offence is proved by competent evidence other than the confession, or if direct evidence confirms the confession. False-confession concerns require caution, but a properly confirmed confession supported by independent evidence may sustain conviction.
held_decision_points: The court held that the accused failed to prove undue influence, accepted the confirmed statement, found the confession genuine and corroborated, and convicted the accused of murder as charged.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that where a confirmed confession is freely and voluntarily made, and independent evidence proves the commission of the crime and connects the accused to it, the confession may lawfully sustain a murder conviction.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court discussed the danger of false confessions and the purpose of requiring independent proof under s 273.
cases_referred_to: S v Tevedzayi HH 206/2018; S v Frank Mbano and 2 Others HB 154/17; R v Taputsa and Others 1966 RLR 662.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 47; Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], ss 256, 273, 314.
reviewer_comments: Full text extracted from the mounted DOCX. The judgment text records conviction but contains no sentence section in the mounted document.

document_name: S v Jasina and Another (850 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 850 (23 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Jasina and Another
subject_area: Criminal law; criminal review; sentencing; restitution; theft
catchwords_flynote: Criminal review – theft – restitution – whether restitution is a sentence – compensation and restitution under Part XIX of the Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act – restitution as condition of suspended sentence – incompetent direct restitution without statutory application.
headnote: Two separate theft proceedings from Marondera were placed before the High Court on review because the trial magistrate treated restitution to complainants as part of the sentence. The court held that restitution is not a sentence. It may be imposed as a condition attached to suspension of sentence or, where Part XIX applies, as a civil compensation/restitution order after a proper application by the injured party or prosecutor. Because the records did not show compliance with either route, the sentences were not in accordance with real and substantial justice.
issues_for_determination: Whether the trial magistrate properly imposed restitution; whether the sentences were in accordance with real and substantial justice.
legal_principles: Restitution is not one of the main direct sentences under Part XVIII of the Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act. It is compensation/restitution and may operate either as a condition for suspended sentence under s 358 or as a civil award under Part XIX upon a proper application.
held_decision_points: The restitution components were incompetent. The sentences were set aside and both matters were remitted for resentencing with guidance.
ratio_decidendi: A sentencing court misdirects itself when it treats restitution as a direct criminal sentence without statutory basis; the procedural requirements for restitution must be observed.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The judgment emphasises the need for magistrates to understand the distinction between punishment and compensation/restitution.
cases_referred_to: S v Maxwell Mutetwa HH 374/15; S v Willard Chikumba CCZ 10/19.
legislation_considered: Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], Parts XVIII and XIX; s 358; s 368.

document_name: Merystake (Private) Limited v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority (851 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 851 (22 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Merystake (Private) Limited v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority
subject_area: Civil procedure; leave to execute pending appeal; customs and excise; forfeiture of trucks and tankers; tax authority.
catchwords_flynote: Execution pending appeal – ZIMRA forfeiture of truck horses and tanker trailers declared unlawful – appeal noted – whether successful party may execute – balance of hardship – leave granted.
headnote: The applicant sought leave to execute a judgment under HC 1343/21 despite ZIMRA’s appeal to the Supreme Court. The underlying judgment had declared forfeiture of two truck horses and two tankers unlawful and ordered their release within 48 hours. Applying the NetOne factors, the court held that continued retention exposed the applicant to irreparable commercial prejudice and further depreciation of the vehicles, while ZIMRA would suffer little prejudice if execution occurred. Leave to execute pending appeal was granted.
issues_for_determination: Whether leave to execute pending appeal should be granted; whether the appeal appeared bona fide and had prospects; where the balance of hardship lay.
legal_principles: Execution pending appeal is extraordinary but available where justice and equity require it. Relevant factors include irreparable harm to either party, prospects of success, whether the appeal is frivolous or for delay, and balance of convenience/hardship.
held_decision_points: Leave to execute pending appeal was granted. If ZIMRA failed to comply with the judgment in HC 1343/21, the Sheriff or deputy sheriff could take the vehicles and hand them to the applicant or its legal practitioners. ZIMRA was ordered to pay ordinary costs.
ratio_decidendi: The applicant’s loss of use and depreciation of commercial haulage assets outweighed any prejudice to ZIMRA. The circumstances justified execution despite the appeal.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court refused punitive costs and awarded ordinary costs.

document_name: Masomera v Beverly East Properties (Private) Limited and 4 Others (854 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 854 (22 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Masomera N.O. v Beverly East Properties (Private) Limited and Others
court: High Court of Zimbabwe, Commercial Division, Harare
subject_area: Corporate rescue; insolvency; affected persons; peremptory notice requirements; company law.
catchwords_flynote: Corporate rescue – Insolvency Act – application under s 124 – affected persons – standard notice – employees and trade unions – failure to notify – application a nullity – retrospective service not competent.
headnote: The applicant sought to place the first to third respondents under supervision and commence corporate rescue proceedings. The first and second respondents opposed and raised several preliminary objections. The court focused on the failure to notify affected persons under s 124(2)(b) of the Insolvency Act. Applying Metallon Gold v Shatirwa Investments, it held that notice to affected persons is peremptory and failure to comply renders the application a nullity. Retrospective service could not cure the defect, especially where employees and trade unions had not been notified. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether the applicant had complied with s 124(2) of the Insolvency Act; whether retrospective service could cure non-notification; whether the application was fatally defective.
legal_principles: Corporate rescue applications have far-reaching consequences and statutory notice to all affected persons is peremptory. Standard notice must comply with the Act. Non-compliance renders the application a nullity.
held_decision_points: The application was fatally defective for failure to notify affected persons. It was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: Affected persons such as shareholders, creditors, employees and trade unions have statutory rights in corporate rescue proceedings. Failure to notify them denies them participation and vitiates the proceedings.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted that employees’ rights may be severely affected by corporate rescue, reinforcing the need for notice.
cases_referred_to: Metallon Gold and Others v Shatirwa Investments (Pvt) Ltd and Others SC 107/21.
legislation_considered: Insolvency Act [Chapter 6:07], ss 2, 124 and 129.

document_name: Chaurura v City of Harare and Another (855 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 855 (22 November 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: ISHUMAEL CHAURURA versus CITY OF HARARE and MINISTER OF LOCAL GOVERNMENT, PUBLIC WORKS AND NATIONAL HOUSING N.O.
citation: Chaurura v City of Harare and Another (855 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 855 (22 November 2022)
subject_area: Urgent chamber application; certificate of urgency; mandament van spolie; municipal traffic enforcement; clamping of motor vehicle; procedural validity.
catchwords_flynote: Urgent application – certificate of urgency – certificate prepared before founding affidavit – legal practitioner certifying urgency must consider existing papers – certificate pre-dating founding papers invalid – spoliation application seeking final relief – application dismissed with costs.
headnote: The applicant sought spoliation relief after the City of Harare clamped his motor vehicle. The respondent objected to the certificate of urgency, contending that it was invalid because it was prepared by a practitioner allegedly linked to the applicant's law firm and because it pre-dated the founding papers. The first ground fell away after clarification, but the Court upheld the second. The certificate was executed two days before the founding affidavit, so the certifying practitioner could not have read and considered the papers. The certificate was invalid and the urgent spoliation application, which sought final relief, was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the certificate of urgency was valid.
    Whether a legal practitioner from the applicant's law firm may certify urgency.
    Whether a certificate of urgency that pre-dates the founding affidavit can support an urgent chamber application.
    Whether a defective urgent spoliation application seeking final relief may simply be struck off the urgent roll or must be dismissed.
legal_principles: A certificate of urgency must show that the legal practitioner independently considered the founding papers and formed an objective view on urgency. A certificate that pre-dates the founding affidavit cannot validly certify urgency because the certifying practitioner could not have considered non-existent papers. A final spoliation application filed as urgent cannot be converted into an ordinary application when the certificate is invalid; it falls to be dismissed.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the objection based on the certifying practitioner's alleged employment by the applicant's law firm no longer arose after clarification.
    Held, that the certificate of urgency was invalid because it pre-dated the founding affidavit.
    Held, that the certifying practitioner could not honestly have read and considered the papers before they existed.
    Held, that the application rested on an invalid certificate and was fatally defective.
    Held, that the application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that an urgent chamber application cannot be supported by a certificate of urgency executed before the founding papers existed; because urgency is assessed from the filed process, such a certificate is invalid and the application has no proper urgent foundation.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court cautioned against legal practitioners from the same law firm as the applicant certifying urgency, because the trust placed in the certificate depends on impartial and objective assessment.

General Transport & Engineering (Pvt) Ltd and Others v Zimbank Corp (Pvt) Ltd 1998 (2) ZLR 301
    Chafanza v Edgars & Anor 2005 (1) ZLR 301 (H)

    Condurago Investments (Private) Limited t/a Mbada Diamonds v Mutual Finance (Private) Limited HH 630/15
    Hwange Colliery Co. Ltd v Commissioner-General of Zimbabwe Republic Police NO HH 267/18

document_name: Baron v Baron and Another (856 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 856 (23 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THOMAS WILLIAM BARON versus THERESA BARON and GILBERT BARON
citation: Baron v Baron and Another (856 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 856 (23 November 2022)
subject_area: Civil procedure; decree of perpetual silence; vexatious litigation; family property dispute; access to courts; punitive costs.
catchwords_flynote: Civil procedure – application for decree of perpetual silence – applicant alleging respondents filed numerous proceedings – drastic remedy limiting access to courts – applicant failing to provide sufficient detail or prove vexatious serial litigation – applicant misleading court by including cases he had instituted himself – application dismissed with attorney-client costs.
headnote: The applicant sought a decree of perpetual silence against his wife and son, alleging that they had instituted numerous proceedings against him and harassed him through litigation. The Court held that the relief was drastic and only available where a litigant is shown to be a serial abuser of court process. The applicant had not provided adequate detail linking the listed cases to harassment or vexatious litigation. The respondents demonstrated that the applicant had included proceedings he himself had instituted. The application was dismissed with costs on the legal-practitioner-and-client scale.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the respondents had engaged in persistent vexatious litigation against the applicant.
    Whether the applicant established grounds for a decree of perpetual silence.
    Whether the listed cases showed harassment or abuse of court process.
    Whether the applicant had been candid with the court.
legal_principles: A decree of perpetual silence is a drastic remedy that restricts a litigant's access to courts and is granted sparingly. The applicant bears the onus of proving that the respondent is a serial litigator who abuses court process and harasses the adversary. Mere multiplicity of cases, without sufficient detail and proof of abuse, is insufficient.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant failed to provide material detail about the listed cases.
    Held, that the applicant failed to prove that the respondents were serial litigators or had harassed him or the courts.
    Held, that the respondents had in some proceedings sought to affirm their rights.
    Held, that the applicant misled the court by citing cases he had instituted as having been instituted by respondents.
    Held, that the application was dismissed with costs on a legal practitioner and client scale.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that a decree of perpetual silence cannot be granted merely because litigation between family members is numerous; the applicant must prove repeated vexatious or abusive litigation with sufficient detail, and lack of candour by the applicant is fatal.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court described the applicant's conduct of mischaracterising his own cases as respondents' litigation as unbecoming and deserving of punitive costs.
cases_referred_to: Mabwe Minerals (Pvt) Ltd v Tapiwa Gurupira & Ors HH 793/16
legislation_considered: Common-law principles governing vexatious litigation and decrees of perpetual silence.
constitutional_provisions_considered: Constitutional right of access to courts considered generally; specific provisions not fully identified.

document_name: Ready v Ready (857 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 857 (30 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Ready v Ready
subject_area: Civil trial; rei vindicatio; family property occupation; counterclaim for co-ownership.
catchwords_flynote: Registered owner suing son for eviction; alleged contribution to purchase price; title deed; rei vindicatio; counterclaim for 50% co-ownership.
headnote: The plaintiff, registered owner of a residential property in Braeside, sought eviction of his son. The defendant claimed he had paid arrears and contributed to acquisition of the property, seeking registration as 50% co-owner. The court held that the plaintiff proved ownership through the deed of transfer and that the defendant failed to establish a defence to rei vindicatio or prove entitlement to co-ownership. Eviction was granted and the counterclaim dismissed.
issues_for_determination: Whether the defendant paid the balance of the purchase price; whether he should be registered as co-owner; whether the plaintiff was entitled to evict him.
legal_principles: Under rei vindicatio, the owner must prove ownership, that the property exists and is possessed by the defendant, and that possession is without the owner’s consent. The registered owner’s right to recover property is strongly protected, and equitable pleas do not defeat ownership unless they amount to a recognised defence.
held_decision_points: The plaintiff proved ownership and lack of consent to the defendant’s continued occupation. The defendant failed to establish co-ownership or a defence to eviction.
ratio_decidendi: A person occupying family property cannot defeat the registered owner’s vindicatory claim merely by alleging historical payments or family arrangements without proving a legal right superior to or enforceable against the owner.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court gave the defendant seven days to vacate despite the plaintiff’s request for immediate vacation, considering the facts of the case.
cases_referred_to: Chetty v Naidoo 1974 (3) SA 13; Alspite Investments (Pvt) Ltd v Westerhoff 2009 (2) ZLR 236; Residents of Joe Slovo Community v Thabelisha Homes 2010 (3) SA 454; Gibson, Principles of South African Law.
legislation_considered: Property-law principles; Deeds registration principles.
reviewer_comments: Filename citation and heading judgment number align. Full judgment text extracted using python-docx.

document_name: Hiten v Barry N.O and 3 Others (858 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 858 (23 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: RENEE JUANITA HITEN versus STEVEN BARRY N.O. AND OTHERS
subject_area: Property law; actio communi dividendo; deceased estate; co-ownership; security for costs; affidavit authentication; defective founding affidavit.
catchwords_flynote: Actio communi dividendo – beneficiary seeking division of land bequeathed equally – founding affidavit signed in Zimbabwe but authenticated before South African notary on same date/time – affidavit defective – founding affidavit expunged – no valid application – application dismissed with attorney-client costs.
headnote: The applicant sought an actio communi dividendo order to avoid being registered as co-owner with the first respondent over land bequeathed to both in equal shares. The first respondent opposed and raised preliminary objections, including defects in the application. The Court held that the founding affidavit was defective because it purported to have been signed in Harare and authenticated before a notary in South Africa on the same date and time. Once the founding affidavit was expunged, there was no application before the court. The application was dismissed with attorney-client costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant should provide security for costs.
    Whether there was a proper application before the court.
    Whether actio communi dividendo was available before transfer into co-ownership.
    Whether the founding affidavit was validly executed and authenticated.
legal_principles: Actio communi dividendo allows a co-owner to claim division of joint property according to shares, generally after attempts to divide amicably. A founding affidavit is essential to motion proceedings. Authentication of an affidavit executed before a notary must assure the genuineness and due execution of the document; material inconsistencies in execution and notarisation may render the affidavit defective.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the founding affidavit was defective and had to be expunged from the record.
    Held, that once the founding affidavit was expunged, no application remained before the court.
    Held, that the application was dismissed.
    Held, that the applicant should pay costs on the legal practitioner and client scale.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that a motion application cannot survive without a valid founding affidavit; where the affidavit’s execution and authentication are internally inconsistent and unreliable, it is expunged and the application must fail without considering the merits.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court stated that the applicant could not have been in Zimbabwe signing the affidavit and in South Africa before the notary on the same day and time.
cases_referred_to:
Robson v Theron 1978 (1) SA 841 (A)
    C. H. Van Zyl, The Notarial Practice of South Africa
legislation_considered: Rules and principles on affidavits, notarial authentication and actio communi dividendo; Administration of Estates principles relating to deceased estates.

document_name: AIRCRA~1(1).DOC
case_name: AIRCRAFTSMAN BERNARD CHAMBE versus THE COMMANDER OF THE AIRFORCE OF ZIMBABWE and THE BOARD OF INQUIRY (SUITABILITY)
subject_area: Civil procedure; condonation; administrative review; defence forces discipline; delay; prospects of success; natural justice.
catchwords_flynote: Condonation – late noting of review after dismissal from Air Force – prior internal appeal nine months late and review fourteen months late – misjoinder/non-joinder point dismissed – explanation based on health condition inadequate – prospects of success poor because dismissal was by Commander and applicant had been afforded opportunity to cross-examine – condonation dismissed with costs.
headnote: The applicant sought condonation for filing a review application fourteen months late after dismissal from the Air Force following a Board of Suitability. The respondents argued, in limine, that the Defence Forces Service Commission should have been cited rather than the Board, but the Court held that misjoinder or non-joinder was not fatal at the condonation stage. On the merits, the Court found the delay excessive and inadequately explained. The applicant's prospects of success were poor because the decision challenged had been made by the Commander, not by the Board, and the record showed that the applicant had been given opportunities to cross-examine witnesses. Condonation was dismissed.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the non-citation of the Defence Forces Service Commission was fatal.
    Whether the applicant provided a reasonable explanation for a fourteen-month delay.
    Whether the intended review had prospects of success.
    Whether condonation should be granted despite the extent of delay.
legal_principles: In condonation applications the court exercises a discretion, considering the degree of non-compliance, the explanation for it, prospects of success, importance of the case, the respondent's interest in finality, convenience of the court and avoidance of unnecessary delay. Good prospects may justify condonation despite weak explanation, but absence of reasonable explanation and poor prospects will defeat the application. Non-joinder or misjoinder is not necessarily fatal if the court can determine the issue between parties before it.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the preliminary point on non-joinder/misjoinder was dismissed.
    Held, that the fourteen-month delay was substantial.
    Held, that the explanation based on the applicant's health condition did not adequately show why he could not file review proceedings personally, through counsel, or through someone else.
    Held, that the prospects of success were not bright because the dismissal decision was made by the Commander and the applicant had been afforded opportunities to cross-examine witnesses.
    Held, that condonation should be refused.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that condonation for a very late review will be refused where the delay is inadequately explained and the proposed review lacks reasonable prospects of success.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to:
FBC Bank Limited v Robert Chidziva SC 31/17
    State v Tanyanyiwa HH 389/19
    Mahiya v Independent African Church SC 58/07
    Paul Gary Friendship v Cargo Carriers Limited & Another SC 1/13
    Lovemore Sango v Chairman of the Public Service Commission and Another HH 28-96
    Moyo v President Board of Enquiry and Others 1996 (1) ZLR 319
legislation_considered:
Defence Act [Chapter 11:02], section 26, section 63, First Schedule paragraph 14(3)
    Defence (Regular Force) (Non-Commissioned Members) Regulations, section 15(1)
constitutional_provisions_considered: Natural justice principles referred to; no specific constitutional provision identified in the extracted text.

document_name: Danzvara and 9 Others v City of Zimbabwe and Other (860 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 860 (23 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: LUKE DANZVARA and 9 OTHERS versus CITY OF HARARE and ZIMBABWE ASSEMBLIES OF GOD
case_number: HC 5656/22; related matters HC 5140/21 and HC 9690/19
citation: Danzvara and 9 Others v City of Zimbabwe and Other (860 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 860 (23 November 2022)
subject_area: Administrative law; condonation; town planning; review; change of use; building permit; church construction in residential area.
catchwords_flynote: Condonation for late noting of review – earlier review struck off for procedural defects – applicants seeking to challenge permit for construction of church in residential neighbourhood – delay explained – prospects of success shown – condonation granted.
headnote: The applicants, residents of Bannockburn, Mount Pleasant, sought condonation to file a fresh review challenging the issuing of a permit to Zimbabwe Assemblies of God to build a church on a neighbouring stand. An earlier review had been struck off for procedural defects. The Court held that the delay after the earlier judgment was satisfactorily explained and that the proposed review raised important issues concerning statutory town-planning procedures, neighbour notification and change of use. Condonation was granted, with a ten-day extension to file the review application.
issues_for_determination:
Whether condonation should be granted after the earlier review application was struck off.
    Whether the delay had been reasonably explained.
    Whether the proposed review had reasonable prospects of success.
    Whether the interests of finality and the second respondent’s position outweighed the applicants’ right to challenge alleged statutory non-compliance.
legal_principles: Condonation requires the court to consider factors cumulatively, including length of delay, explanation, prospects of success, prejudice, finality, importance of the case, convenience of the court and avoidance of delay. The central enquiry is fairness and the best interests of justice. Rules exist for the court’s convenience, and non-compliance may be condoned where justice requires.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicants did not sit on their rights after HH 530-22 and explained the delay following that judgment.
    Held, that the proposed review had reasonable prospects because it raised questions about town-planning compliance and notification to affected neighbours.
    Held, that the need for finality did not outweigh the importance of reviewing alleged statutory non-compliance.
    Held, that condonation should be granted and the applicants given ten days to file the review.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that where an earlier review failed for procedural reasons but the applicants promptly seek fresh condonation and show reasonable prospects on a significant town-planning issue, the interests of justice favour condonation despite the respondent’s interest in finality.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that town-planning procedures are important to prevent landowners from changing land uses without lawful and transparent processes.
cases_referred_to:
Read v Gardener and Another SC 70/2019
    Forestry Commission v Moyo 1997 (1) ZLR 254 (S)
    Maheya v Independent African Church SC 58/07
    Paul Gary Friendship v Cargo Carriers Limited & Anor SC 1/13
    Chimeza and Another v Mangwana and Others HH 186/17
    Kodzwa v Secretary for Health and Another 1999 (1) ZLR 313 (S)
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021; town planning statutes and regulations applicable to change of use and permits.
