document_name: S v Silas (861 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 861 (23 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: The State v James Silas
subject_area: Criminal review; culpable homicide; road traffic death; sentencing; degree of negligence; licence cancellation
catchwords_flynote: Culpable homicide - road traffic accident - sentence wholly suspended - failure to conduct mandatory inquiry into degree of negligence - failure to consider Road Traffic Act provisions on prohibition from driving and cancellation of licence - distinction between culpable homicide and traffic offences - sentence set aside and remitted.
headnote: The accused pleaded guilty to culpable homicide after, while driving a haulage truck at excessive speed at night, he struck a pedestrian who died. The magistrate imposed a wholly suspended six-month sentence. On review, the scrutinising regional magistrate noted that the trial magistrate had not conducted the mandatory inquiry into degree of negligence and had not considered mandatory Road Traffic Act consequences for a heavy vehicle. The High Court criticised the tendency to treat road-traffic culpable homicide as routine traffic offending. It held that culpable homicide is a serious Criminal Law Code offence involving negligent killing, regardless of whether the instrument is a motor vehicle. The sentence was set aside and the matter remitted for sentence anew.
issues_for_determination: Whether the conviction and sentence were in accordance with real and substantial justice; whether the charge was defective; whether the sentence trivialised culpable homicide; and whether the trial court should have conducted an inquiry into negligence and considered driving prohibition and licence cancellation.
legal_principles: Culpable homicide under s 49 of the Criminal Law Code is negligent killing, not a traffic offence. The court must consider the degree of negligence when sentencing. Where a motor vehicle is involved, Road Traffic Act provisions on prohibition from driving and cancellation of driver’s licence may be mandatory. A defective charge may be cured by evidence or plea canvassing if the essential elements are properly admitted.
held_decision_points: The conviction was not set aside, but the sentence was set aside. The matter was remitted to the trial magistrate to recall the accused and sentence him anew, paying attention to the issues in the review judgment. A copy was to be availed to the Chief Magistrate.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that culpable homicide arising from negligent driving must be sentenced as a serious homicide offence, with careful assessment of negligence and applicable road-traffic statutory consequences, rather than as a routine traffic matter.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court recommended reconsidering the allocation of culpable-homicide matters in magistrates courts and cautioned against trivialising the loss of human life in road-traffic matters.
cases_referred_to: S v Mitchell SC 101/84; S v Chaita and Others 1998 (1) ZLR 213; S v Makanza HB 197/17; S v Mununuri Goto and Another HB 88/15; S v Sibanda and Another HB 97/15; Dzvairo and Others v The State 2006 (1) ZLR 49.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 49; Road Traffic Act [Chapter 13:11], s 64(3); Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], ss 202(3), 203; Magistrates Court Act [Chapter 7:10], s 57.

document_name: FINWOO~1(1).DOC
case_name: FINWOOD INVESTMENTS (PRIVATE) LIMITED versus TETRAD HOLDINGS LIMITED and TETRAD INVESTMENT BANK LIMITED
subject_area: Company law; declaratory relief; sale of shares; beneficial ownership; corporate personality; initial impossibility; Contractual Penalties Act.
catchwords_flynote: Declaratur – sale of shares in companies holding immovable property – applicant claiming beneficial ownership after alleged payment – seller not owner of shares – separate juristic personalities – Contractual Penalties Act not applicable to sale of shares – no basis to pierce corporate veil – application dismissed.
headnote: The applicant sought declarations that the cancellation of a 2014 agreement was void and that it was the sole beneficial owner of Holfren Investments (Pvt) Ltd and Ontarium Investments (Pvt) Ltd. The Court held that the contract was a sale of shares, not an instalment sale of land, so the Contractual Penalties Act did not apply. The evidence showed that the shares were owned by Tetrad Investment Bank Limited, not Tetrad Holdings Limited which purported to sell them. The applicant failed to establish a basis for treating the respondents as one entity or piercing the corporate veil. The application was dismissed with ordinary costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the transaction was an instalment sale of land or a sale of shares.
    Whether the Contractual Penalties Act applied.
    Whether Tetrad Holdings Limited had capacity to sell the shares.
    Whether the applicant established beneficial ownership of Holfren and Ontarium.
    Whether the corporate veil should be pierced between the respondents.
legal_principles: A company has separate legal personality from its shareholders and related companies. A party cannot transfer a right that it does not possess. The Contractual Penalties Act provisions governing instalment sales of land do not apply merely because shares in companies holding immovable property are sold. The corporate veil may be pierced only where fraud, dishonesty or improper conduct justifies departure from separate personality.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the agreement was a sale of shares, not a sale of land.
    Held, that the Contractual Penalties Act did not apply.
    Held, that Tetrad Holdings Limited could not transfer shares it did not own.
    Held, that the evidence did not justify treating the two Tetrad companies as a single unit or piercing the corporate veil.
    Held, that the applicant was not entitled to the declaratory relief sought.
    Held, that the application was dismissed with costs on the ordinary scale.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the applicant could not obtain declaratory relief premised on beneficial ownership where the contracting seller had no title to the shares and where no grounds were established for disregarding the separate legal personality of the respondent companies.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted that a mere reference in correspondence to both Tetrad entities did not prove that they were a single unit or justify piercing the corporate veil.

document_name: Matsvimbo and Another v Ndoro and 2 Others (865 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 865 (1 December 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Matsvimbo and Another v Ndoro and 2 Others
subject_area: Civil appeal; stay of execution; defective founding affidavit; uncommissioned affidavit; defective grounds of appeal.
catchwords_flynote: Civil appeal – application for stay of execution in magistrates court – founding affidavit not commissioned, signed or dated – no valid application before court – unclear and argumentative grounds of appeal – appeal dismissed.
headnote: The appellants appealed against a magistrate’s dismissal of their application for stay of execution. The magistrate had found that the founding affidavit was not commissioned, signed or dated and that there was therefore no valid application before the court. The High Court held that the appellant’s grounds of appeal were unclear, argumentative and repetitive, and that the court a quo had correctly dealt with the application on the record before it. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether the magistrate erred in treating the uncommissioned and unsigned affidavit as fatal; whether the appeal grounds disclosed a proper basis to interfere; whether the appeal should be postponed because of other pending proceedings.
legal_principles: An affidavit must be properly commissioned and court documents must comply with formal requirements. Grounds of appeal must be clear, concise and directed at specific errors. The clerk’s acceptance of papers does not cure substantive defects.
held_decision_points: The appeal was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The record contained no valid commissioned founding affidavit. The appellate court could not fault the magistrate for refusing to determine the merits when there was no proper application before the court.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court observed that the appellant had many pending matters and that the existence of other proceedings did not justify postponing the appeal.
cases_referred_to: No major authorities specifically identified in the extracted text; procedural rules on affidavits and appeals applied.
legislation_considered: Magistrates Court (Civil) Rules, 2019, including Order 34.
reviewer_comments: Draft. Appellant appeared in person; grounds of appeal were described by the court as unclear, terse, argumentative and repetitive.

document_name: S v Kagonye (866 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 866 (1 December 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Kagonye v The State
subject_area: Criminal procedure; leave to appeal; theft of trust property; public office; sentence
catchwords_flynote: Leave to appeal – theft of trust property – donated laptops intended for schools – duty to account – change of defence during trial – sentence for corruption-related conduct – leave refused.
headnote: The applicant sought leave to appeal after her appeal against conviction and sentence for theft of trust property was dismissed. The court held that there was no reasonable prospect that the Supreme Court would accept her arguments that she did not know she had to deliver or account for twenty donated laptops. The evidence against her was overwhelming and the sentence was not shockingly severe.
issues_for_determination: Whether the intended appeal against conviction and sentence had reasonable prospects of success.
legal_principles: Leave to appeal requires substance and reasonable prospects of success. A person entrusted with property for a specific purpose commits theft of trust property where she intentionally diverts or fails to account for it. Courts take a stern approach to corruption-related offences by high-ranking public officers.
held_decision_points: Application for leave to appeal to the Supreme Court dismissed in its entirety.
ratio_decidendi: The applicant’s proposed grounds were fanciful, contradicted by the record, and had no prospect of success; the sentence was lenient in context.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted that the applicant changed her defence during trial, first denying receipt of the laptops and later accepting receipt but denying the duty to account.
cases_referred_to: State v Mutasa 1988 (2) ZLR 4 (S); Whaley & Others v Cone Textiles (Pvt) Ltd 1989 (3) SA 574; State v McGown 1995 (2) ZLR 81 (S); Prosecutor-General of Zimbabwe v Intratrek Zimbabwe (Private) Limited & Ors SC 59/19.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 113(2)(a); High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], s 44(4); High Court Rules, 2021, r 94.

document_name: S v Sadiqi (867 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 867 (30 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Frank Buyanga Sadiqi v The State
subject_area: Criminal review; warrant of arrest; review mero motu; functus officio; dirty hands; fugitive from justice; judicial independence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal review - High Court review mero motu under s 29(4) of the High Court Act - magistrate cancellation of warrant of arrest - fugitive from justice - dirty hands doctrine - default enquiry incorrectly used - magistrate reviewing own order - functus officio - gross irregularity - proceedings set aside.
headnote: The matter concerned a warrant of arrest issued against Frank Buyanga Sadiqi in connection with criminal investigations. After extensive litigation involving child custody and orders of superior courts, a magistrate entertained a written chamber application and cancelled the warrant of arrest on the same day. The High Court invoked its review powers mero motu after the matter came to the notice of a judge. It rejected a recusal application and held that the magistrate had committed several gross irregularities, including purporting to overturn decisions of superior courts, creating a procedure not provided for in criminal proceedings, conducting proceedings without all interested parties, and effectively reviewing her own prior order after becoming functus officio.
issues_for_determination: Whether the magistrate’s cancellation of the warrant of arrest was properly before her; whether she could entertain the matter in the absence of the subject of the warrant and interested parties; whether she had jurisdiction to correct or cancel an order she had issued more than two years earlier; and whether her decision disregarded binding superior-court decisions.
legal_principles: Under s 29(4) of the High Court Act, a judge may review criminal proceedings of an inferior court mero motu where it comes to the judge’s notice that the proceedings may not accord with real and substantial justice. Magistrates derive criminal jurisdiction from statute and cannot invent procedures not provided by law. A magistrate who has made an order is functus officio and cannot review her own work. Inferior courts are bound by superior-court judgments. The dirty hands doctrine may deny court audience to a litigant in contempt or a fugitive from justice.
held_decision_points: The criminal proceedings before the provincial magistrate on 14 November 2022 culminating in cancellation of the warrant of arrest were set aside. The order cancelling the warrant was set aside and substituted with an order striking off the application for cancellation of the warrant.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that a magistrate cannot, under the guise of cancelling a warrant or conducting a default enquiry, review her own prior order or disregard binding decisions of superior courts, particularly through a procedure not authorised by statute.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court emphasised judicial independence and warned that administrative superiors of magistrates have no review or appellate power over judicial decisions of magistrates.
cases_referred_to: McMillan & Ors v Provincial Magistrate 2004 (1) ZLR 17; Foyer and Matimba 1963 ZLR 318; Frank Buyanga Sadiqi v Chantelle Muteswa & Ors CCZ 14/21; Frank Buyanga Sadiqi v Chantelle Tatenda Muteswa & Ors HH 281/20; Frank Buyanga Sadiqi v Chantelle Muteswa & Ors SC 132/21; Frank Buyanga Sadiqi v Chantelle Muteswa SC 275/19; S v Neill 1982 (1) ZLR 142 (HC); Sylow v The State HH 136-02; Nhapata v Maswi & Anor SC 38/16; Associated Newspapers of Zimbabwe (Pvt) Ltd v Minister of State for Information and Publicity & Ors SC 07/03; Denhere v Denhere CCZ 9/19.
legislation_considered: High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], ss 27, 29(2), 29(4); Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], ss 33(1)(c), 33(4); Interpol rules, arts 80 and 81 referred to in argument.
constitutional_provisions_considered: Constitution of Zimbabwe Amendment (No. 20) Act, 2013, ss 165(2), 165(3), 171(1)(b).

document_name: S v Chiramba (869 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 869 (24 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Chiramba
judges: MUNGWARI J; assessors Mr Barwa and Mr Chokuvinga
subject_area: Criminal law – murder – carjacking – confirmed warned and cautioned statement – aggravating circumstances – sentence
catchwords_flynote: Murder; robbery/carjacking; confirmed warned and cautioned statement; admissibility; circumstantial and direct evidence; aggravating circumstances; sentencing.
headnote: The accused was charged with murdering a taxi driver, Sydney Zuruvi, by stabbing him with a kitchen knife during an attempted carjacking. The State alleged that after other passengers disembarked, the accused demanded the car keys, stabbed the deceased and tried to drive away.
issues_for_determination: Whether the accused murdered the deceased; whether the confirmed statement and surrounding evidence proved guilt; whether aggravating circumstances affected sentence.
legal_principles: A confirmed warned and cautioned statement may be admitted under s 256(2) of the Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act unless properly impugned. Sentencing for murder committed in aggravating circumstances restricts the court’s discretion.
held_decision_points: The accused was convicted of murder. The court found aggravating circumstances and sentenced him to 25 years’ imprisonment after considering his period in custody.
ratio_decidendi: The confirmed statement, recovered items and witness evidence established beyond reasonable doubt that the accused intentionally caused the deceased’s death in the course of a carjacking.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted that without the five-year period already spent in custody, a 30-year sentence would have been appropriate.
cases_referred_to: R v Taputsa; S v Frank Mbano; Warren Hazvienzani Katiro v The State HH.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 47(1); Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], ss 256(2), 314.

document_name: S v Esau and Another (870 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 870 (28 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Esau and Another
judges: MUNGWARI J; Assessors: Mr Mhandu and Mr Mabandla
subject_area: Criminal law; murder; robbery; circumstantial evidence; confession; aggravating circumstances; sentencing
catchwords_flynote: Murder – taxi driver assaulted and robbed of motor vehicle – accused involved in accident in stolen vehicle – confirmed warned and cautioned statement – admissibility – circumstantial evidence – alibi – aggravating circumstances – sentence.
headnote: The accused were charged with murder after the deceased was assaulted, left for dead, and his Toyota Wish taken. The court admitted the first accused’s confirmed warned and cautioned statement, rejected the second accused’s alibi, and held that circumstantial evidence placed both accused in the stolen vehicle and at the crime. Both were convicted of murder committed in aggravating circumstances and sentenced to 25 years’ imprisonment each.
issues_for_determination: Whether the accused participated in the murder; whether the first accused’s confirmed warned and cautioned statement was admissible; whether the second accused’s alibi was reasonably possibly true; whether aggravating circumstances existed for sentence.
legal_principles: A confirmed warned and cautioned statement is admissible under s 256(2) unless the accused rebuts admissibility on a balance of probabilities. Circumstantial evidence must be consistent with proven facts and exclude reasonable inferences other than guilt. Murder committed in the course of robbery constitutes aggravating circumstances.
held_decision_points: Both accused were found guilty of murder and each sentenced to 25 years’ imprisonment.
ratio_decidendi: The proven facts and admissible confession established beyond reasonable doubt that both accused participated in the robbery-murder; the alibi was false and the offence was committed in aggravating circumstances.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court criticised a wrong approach to sentencing submissions which focused on extenuation rather than the statutory aggravating-circumstances framework.
cases_referred_to: S v Sibanda 1992 (2) ZLR 438 (S); S v John Scenara HH 849/22.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 47; Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], ss 256, 278, 314, 358.

document_name: S v Nyamande (871 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 871 (30 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: The State v Denford Nyamande
subject_area: Criminal law; murder; witchcraft belief; provocation; aggravating circumstances; sentence
catchwords_flynote: Murder - accused killed elderly father and aunt over belief in witchcraft - witchcraft belief not a defence under s 101 - provocation not established because killings were premeditated and not sudden - double murder in aggravating circumstances - witchcraft belief treated as mitigation - sentence of 35 years imprisonment.
headnote: The accused was charged with two murders after killing his 87-year-old father with an axe and his 89-year-old aunt with a hoe handle. He believed they had bewitched him and caused his illness and misfortunes. The court held that witchcraft belief could not operate as a defence to murder and that the facts did not support provocation because the accused had long held the belief, had threatened to kill the deceased persons, travelled to them, and killed them in separate locations. The court convicted him of both murders, but considered the genuine witchcraft belief as mitigation at sentence.
issues_for_determination: Whether belief in witchcraft can constitute a defence; whether the facts established provocation; whether the accused had the requisite mens rea for murder; whether aggravating circumstances were present; and the appropriate sentence.
legal_principles: Section 101 of the Criminal Law Code provides that belief in witchcraft is not a defence to murder, assault or other crimes, though it may mitigate sentence. Provocation requires a sudden response and loss of self-control; a longstanding grievance and planned retaliation do not suffice. Multiple murders in one episode, premeditation, and victims over 70 are aggravating circumstances under s 47.
held_decision_points: The accused was convicted on both counts of murder. Both counts were treated as one for sentence and he was sentenced to 35 years’ imprisonment.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is that witchcraft belief, however genuine, cannot excuse murder, and where killings are planned or revenge-driven rather than sudden, provocation does not reduce liability; the belief is relevant only to sentence under s 101.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court discussed African belief in witchcraft and clarified that “witchcraft-provocation” is not a standalone defence but, at most, an aspect of provocation or mitigation.
cases_referred_to: S v Hamunakwadi HH 323/15; S v Best Sibanda HB 139/18; S v Tshuma HB 171/22.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], ss 47, 101, 239.

document_name: PHIRIV~1.DOC
case_name: Phiri v Minister of Local Government, Public Works and National Housing
subject_area: Condonation; late noting of appeal; interlocutory order; eviction proceedings.
catchwords_flynote: Condonation for late appeal; appealability; interlocutory dismissal of points in limine; Zweni attributes of appealable judgment; prospects of success.
headnote: The applicant sought condonation for late noting of an appeal and extension of time after an earlier appeal was struck off. The intended appeal was against dismissal of points in limine in lower-court eviction proceedings. The court held that the order was interlocutory and did not have final and definitive effect. There were no prospects of success on appeal and the application was dismissed.
issues_for_determination: Whether the lower-court decision dismissing points in limine was appealable; whether condonation should be granted; whether there were prospects of success on appeal.
legal_principles: Condonation is an indulgence requiring a satisfactory explanation and prospects of success. An appealable judgment must be final in effect, definitive of rights, and dispose of the whole or a substantial portion of the relief. Simple interlocutory orders are generally not appealable.
held_decision_points: The intended appeal concerned an interlocutory order and had no prospects of success. Condonation was refused.
ratio_decidendi: A party cannot obtain condonation to appeal an interlocutory ruling that does not finally determine rights or dispose of a substantial portion of the proceedings.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted that an unrestricted right of appeal from every interlocutory ruling would encourage delay and obstruction.
cases_referred_to: Friendship v Cargo Carriers Ltd & Anor SC 1/13; Zweni v Minister of Law and Order 1993 (1) SA 523 (A); Pretoria Garrison Institutes v Danish Variety Products 1948 (1) SA 839 (A); Gillespies Monumental Works (Pvt) Ltd v Zimbabwe Granite Quarries (Pvt) Ltd 1997 (2) ZLR 436.
legislation_considered: High Court procedural rules on condonation and appeals.

document_name: Bouckaert v Duckworth and 5 Others (873 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 873 (16 November 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: ROBIN BOUCKAERT versus GARY DUCKWORTH and 5 OTHERS
citation: Bouckaert v Duckworth and 5 Others (873 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 873 (16 November 2022)
subject_area: Civil procedure; additional affidavits; declaratur; universitas; res litigiosa; governance of voluntary association/conservancy; employment/trust offices.
catchwords_flynote: Civil procedure – application for leave to file additional affidavit after pleadings closed – affidavit concerned subsequent AGM resolution ratifying removal of applicant from offices – whether additional affidavit would alter cause and prejudice applicant – res litigiosa argument raised from bar – additional affidavit refused with costs.
headnote: The applicant challenged his suspension/removal as chairperson and CEO of Save Valley Conservancy and as trustee of the SVC Trust. After pleadings closed, the SVC held an AGM which resolved to confirm his removal. Respondents sought leave to file an additional affidavit placing that resolution before the Court. The Court held that the additional affidavit would materially alter the cause and prejudice the applicant by introducing subsequent ratification of the impugned conduct. The matter should be decided as pleaded. Leave to file the additional affidavit was refused with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether respondents should be granted leave to file an additional affidavit after pleadings closed.
    Whether the later AGM resolution was relevant and admissible.
    Whether admitting the affidavit would materially alter the causa and prejudice the applicant.
    Whether the issue should instead be decided on the papers as pleaded.
legal_principles: Rule 59(12) of the High Court Rules, 2021 permits further affidavits with leave. The court considers relevance, explanation, prejudice, and whether admission would alter the cause of action. Points of law may be raised at any stage, but not where doing so unfairly prejudices the opposing party or requires a factual inquiry not properly pleaded.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the proposed additional affidavit would materially alter the causa.
    Held, that the subsequent AGM resolution raised issues not pleaded or properly placed before the Court on notice.
    Held, that admitting the affidavit would prejudice the applicant and aggravate the quarrel.
    Held, that the matter should be decided as pleaded.
    Held, that the application for leave to file the additional affidavit was refused with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that a further affidavit filed after pleadings have closed should not be admitted where it introduces subsequent events that materially alter the pleaded dispute and would prejudice the opposing party; the court should determine the matter as pleaded.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted that a fuller inquiry would be required before determining the propriety or effect of the AGM, and that such inquiry was not properly before the Court through a bar-raised point.

    El Elion Investments (Private) Limited v Auction City (Private) Limited SC 29-16

    Muskwe v Nyajina & Ors SC 17/12

document_name: The Right Investments (Private) Limited v Muponda and 5 Others (874 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 874 (7 December 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: The Right Investments (Private) Limited v Muponda and 5 Others
subject_area: Urgent chamber application; stay of execution; rescission of default judgment; company directorship and shareholding dispute; interim interdict; fraud allegations; preservation of status quo.
catchwords_flynote: Stay of execution – judgment affecting company title/directorship – pending rescission application – disputed authority to represent company – allegations of fraud – whether execution should be suspended to avoid irreparable prejudice – whether directors should be interdicted pending rescission.
headnote: The applicant sought suspension of the execution of a judgment granted in HC 5385B/22 pending the determination of a rescission application under HC 7557/22. It also sought an interdict restraining the first and second respondents from acting as its directors, shareholders or officers pending rescission. The respondents raised preliminary objections regarding authority, validity of the company resolution, urgency, non-joinder and the form of the draft order. The court dismissed the objections and found that execution of the judgment, which affected transfers and company control, should be suspended pending determination of the rescission application.
issues_for_determination: Whether the application was urgent; whether the individuals purporting to act for the applicant were authorized; whether the resolution authorizing the application was valid; whether non-joinder of the fourth respondent was fatal; whether the judgment should be stayed pending rescission; and whether the first and second respondents should be interdicted from acting for the company pending determination of the rescission application.
legal_principles: A court has inherent power to control execution of its process and may suspend execution where real and substantial justice so requires. Special circumstances are more readily found where execution concerns ejectment or transfer of property, because restitution may be difficult if the underlying judgment is later rescinded. In considering such relief, the court weighs prejudice and whether suspension merely preserves the status quo pending the determination of the rescission application.
held_decision_points: The preliminary objections were dismissed. The court held that the question of authority and directorship went to the substance of the dispute and could not defeat the urgent application at the threshold. The matter was urgent because the applicant acted within a short period after becoming aware of the order. The court found that execution should be suspended and that the first and second respondents should be interdicted from performing duties as directors, shareholders or officers pending the rescission application.
ratio_decidendi: Because the pending rescission application concerned the validity of proceedings and orders affecting the applicant’s property and corporate control, allowing execution to proceed could render rescission meaningless. Real and substantial justice required preservation of the status quo while fraud allegations and corporate authority issues were investigated by the court.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified beyond the court’s application of settled stay-of-execution principles.
cases_referred_to: Mupini v Makoni 1993 (1) ZLR 80 (S); Cohen v Cohen (1) 1979 ZLR 184; Santam Insurance Co. Ltd v Paget (2) 1981 ZLR 132; Chibanda v King 1983 (1) ZLR 116; Strime v Strime 1983 (4) SA 850.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021; rules relating to rescission and urgent chamber applications; Companies and Other Business Entities Act [Chapter 24:31] implicated by the directorship/shareholding dispute.
constitutional_provisions_considered: No constitutional provision was directly determined.

document_name: Banda and 9 Others v Takanhike and 47 Others (875 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 875 (7 December 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: JEFFERSON BANDA and 9 OTHERS versus WALTER TAKANHIKE and 47 OTHERS and COMMERCIAL WORKERS UNION OF ZIMBABWE
citation: Banda and 9 Others v Takanhike and 47 Others (875 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 875 (7 December 2022)
subject_area: Civil procedure; condonation; rescission of default judgment; trade-union governance; abuse of process; candour to the court.
catchwords_flynote: Civil procedure – composite application for condonation and rescission – defective appearance to defend – automatic bar – default judgment – applicant filing earlier rescission then fresh application under a different rule – need for frank and full explanation in condonation – abuse of process – application dismissed with costs.
headnote: The dispute concerned control of the Commercial Workers Union of Zimbabwe. The respondents had obtained default judgment after the applicants filed an appearance to defend out of time and did not seek upliftment of bar. The applicants initially filed an application for rescission under HC 6307/21, but later filed a fresh composite application for condonation and rescission. The Court found that the applicants had failed to meet the requirements for condonation, had not candidly explained their procedural failures, and had two rescission applications relating to the same default judgment. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the union was properly before the court.
    Whether the composite application for condonation and rescission was competent.
    Whether the applicants explained the delay and non-compliance with the rules.
    Whether the applicants had prospects of success.
    Whether the existence of the earlier rescission application rendered the current application abusive.
legal_principles: An applicant seeking condonation must disclose the degree of non-compliance, provide a full and honest explanation for the delay, address prospects of success, and take the court into confidence. The court may refer to its own records where necessary. Condonation is an indulgence, not a right. An application premised on misleading assertions and unexplained procedural duplications may be dismissed as an abuse of process.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the union was not properly before the court because of irregular affidavits filed on its behalf.
    Held, that the applicants failed to satisfy the requirements for condonation.
    Held, that the earlier rescission application remained pending and the applicants had not withdrawn it.
    Held, that the present application was untenable and abusive.
    Held, that there were no meaningful prospects of success while the bar remained operative.
    Held, that the application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that a litigant seeking condonation and rescission must make full, candid and coherent disclosure of the procedural history and must explain every material default; where the applicant conceals or misrepresents the existence of parallel proceedings and fails to address the bar, the application cannot succeed.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court emphasised that a litigant who infringes the rules must take the court into confidence; an application founded on what the Court described as a maze of lies cannot attract the court's indulgence.
cases_referred_to:
Mhungu v Mtindi 1986 (2) ZLR 171 (SC)
    NetOne v Econet SC 47/18
    Zimalate Quartzite (Pvt) Limited v Central Africa Building Society SC 34/17
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, rules 27 and 29(1)(a); rules governing appearance to defend and bar.
subject_area_terms: Civil procedure; condonation; rescission of default judgment; trade-union governance; abuse of process; candour to the court.

document_name: DELAFT~1.DOC
case_name: DELATFIN ENGINEERING (PVT) LTD versus POLICE COMMISSIONER GENERAL (G.T. MATANGA) and 3 OTHERS
subject_area: Mandament van spolie; land development; police occupation; possession; self-help; urgent chamber application.
catchwords_flynote: Spoliation – applicant land developer in possession of Haydon Farm – police support unit details forcibly removing guards and stopping works – police asserting infrastructure/security interests – spoliation established – restoration ordered but complete eviction of police refused.
headnote: The applicant was allocated Haydon Farm for residential development and had commenced road construction, pegging and stand development. Police details arrived and took over control, removed security guards and stopped construction activities. The Court held that the applicant was in possession and had been despoiled without consent. A final spoliation order was granted restoring possession and restraining interference, while permitting police to secure specific listed infrastructure. The request to evict police entirely was not competent in spoliation proceedings.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant was in peaceful and undisturbed possession of Haydon Farm.
    Whether the police conduct amounted to spoliation.
    Whether the applicant was entitled to restoration of possession.
    Whether the court could order complete eviction of police from the farm in spoliation proceedings.
    Whether costs should be awarded personally or on a higher scale.
legal_principles: The mandament van spolie protects peaceful possession and prohibits self-help. The remedy requires proof that the applicant was in possession and that the respondent unlawfully deprived the applicant of possession without consent. The merits of underlying rights, compensation, ownership or entitlement are not decisive in spoliation proceedings. A spoliation order is final in nature.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant had been in possession since at least August 2022 through workers, guards, road construction and stand development.
    Held, that police details took over the farm, removed guards and stopped construction without consent.
    Held, that spoliation was established.
    Held, that police could guard their infrastructure but could not interfere with the applicant’s project.
    Held, that the request for complete eviction of police was not legally competent in spoliation.
    Held, that costs were awarded against the first respondent in his official capacity on the ordinary scale.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that the police could not use alleged compensation or security concerns to dispossess a land developer who was in peaceful possession; spoliation required restoration of possession while leaving underlying land and compensation disputes for separate determination.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that the police may secure their infrastructure without controlling the entire farm or stopping the developer’s project.
cases_referred_to: Authorities on mandament van spolie and self-help, including general principles referred to in the judgment.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, rule 60; principles of mandament van spolie; land allocation framework relevant to the Ministry of Local Government.
subject_area_terms: Mandament van spolie; land development; police occupation; possession; self-help; urgent chamber application.
cases_referred_to_terms: Authorities on mandament van spolie and self-help, including general principles referred to in the judgment.
legislation_considered_terms: High Court Rules, 2021, rule 60; principles of mandament van spolie; land allocation framework relevant to the Ministry of Local Government.

document_name: VOEDSE~1(2).DOC
case_name: Voedsel Enterprises (Pvt) Ltd and Another v AFC Commercial Bank and Others
subject_area: Urgent chamber application; stay of execution; parate executie; Agricultural Finance Act; constitutional challenge; stare decisis; access to courts; improper citation.
catchwords_flynote: Stay of execution – bank intending auction sale of secured immovable property under Agricultural Finance Act – applicants seeking stay pending constitutional challenge to parate executie provisions – whether wrong citation fatal – whether constitutional issue already decided by Constitutional Court – effect of stare decisis.
headnote: The applicants sought urgent stay of execution of an intended auction sale of their immovable property by AFC Commercial Bank. The stay was premised on pending litigation challenging the constitutionality of the statutory power permitting the bank to sell security without further recourse to the debtor. The first respondent raised preliminary objections, including improper citation and that the constitutional issue had already been decided. The court upheld the improper citation point and, more importantly, held that the Constitutional Court had already determined the legality and constitutionality of parate executie. The urgent application was struck off with costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether the first respondent had been correctly cited; whether the High Court could entertain a constitutional challenge where the Constitutional Court had already pronounced on parate executie and access-to-courts arguments; whether the matter should be stayed pending the constitutional application; and whether the balance of convenience favoured interim relief.
legal_principles: The doctrine of stare decisis binds lower courts to legal principles pronounced by superior courts on materially similar issues. Parate executie, including a creditor’s right to sell pledged or secured property without prior court order where the debtor agreed or statute provides, has been held not to violate the constitutional right of access to courts. A debtor retains access to court to challenge the creditor’s conduct or manner of performance.
held_decision_points: The court upheld the improper citation point because the bank was AFC Bank (Private) Limited. The court also upheld the stare decisis point, finding that the constitutional legality of parate executie had already been resolved by the Constitutional Court. The urgent application was struck off the roll with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The applicants’ proposed constitutional challenge was not a sufficient basis for interim stay because the Constitutional Court had already determined that parate executie does not contravene access-to-court protections. The High Court could not reopen that settled question under the guise of urgent interim relief.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court emphasized that stare decisis prevents lower courts from travestying decided issues and avoids unnecessary litigation on settled constitutional questions.
cases_referred_to: Gariya Safaris (Pvt) Ltd v Van Wyk 1996 (2) ZLR 246 (H); Fadzai John v Delta Beverages Limited SC 40/2017; K and G Mining Syndicate v Ronald Mugangavari & Ors HB 159/2020; John Nyamukasa v Agricultural Finance Company SC 174/94; Chizikani v Agricultural Finance Company SC 123/95; Glens Removal & Storage Zimbabwe (Private) Limited v Patricia Mandala CCZ 06/17; Chief Lesapo v North West Agricultural Bank and Another 2000 (1) SA 409 (CC); SA Bank of Athens Ltd v May van Zyl [2006] 1 All SA 118 (SCA).
legislation_considered: Agricultural Finance Act [Chapter 18:02], s 38 and Second Schedule.
constitutional_provisions_considered: Constitution of Zimbabwe, s 69(2) and s 69(3).
reviewer_comments: Short filename VOEDSE~1(2).DOC preserved. Neutral citation field marked Not available because the uploaded filename does not supply a neutral citation, although the judgment heading records HH 877-22.

document_name: The Sheriff for Zimbabwe v Masocha and Another (878 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 878 (8 December 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: The Sheriff for Zimbabwe v Masocha and Another
subject_area: Interpleader; motor vehicle attachment; registration book as evidence of ownership; rebuttable presumption; loaned vehicle; execution against judgment debtor.
catchwords_flynote: Interpleader – Isuzu KB300 attached in execution – claimant produced registration book in his own name – judgment creditor argued vehicle was attached at judgment debtor’s premises and had been claimed by debtor in prior matters – whether claimant rebutted presumption and proved ownership.
headnote: The Sheriff attached a white Isuzu KB300 double cab motor vehicle during execution against a judgment debtor. The claimant, Robert Masocha, claimed ownership of the vehicle and produced its registration book in his name. The judgment creditor contended that the vehicle had been attached at the judgment debtor’s premises and that the debtor had previously claimed it. The court held that registration raised a presumption of ownership in the claimant’s favour and that the claimant had satisfactorily explained why the car was at the judgment debtor’s premises.
issues_for_determination: Whether the claimant proved ownership of the vehicle; whether registration in the claimant’s name was sufficient to raise a presumption of ownership; whether the judgment creditor rebutted that presumption; and whether the claimant’s explanation for the vehicle’s presence at the debtor’s premises was satisfactory.
legal_principles: In interpleader proceedings, the claimant must prove ownership of attached goods on a balance of probabilities. Registration of a motor vehicle in a claimant’s name raises a rebuttable presumption of ownership, but is not conclusive. Where the judgment creditor seeks to rebut that presumption, the court assesses the quality of the claimant’s explanation and the surrounding probabilities.
held_decision_points: The claimant proved ownership. The registration book bore his name and particulars. He satisfactorily explained that the vehicle had been loaned to another person and was therefore at the judgment debtor’s premises. The judgment creditor’s assertions did not rebut the presumption of ownership. The claim was granted.
ratio_decidendi: The vehicle registration book and claimant’s coherent explanation established ownership on a balance of probabilities. The judgment creditor’s reliance on location of attachment and the judgment debtor’s claims was insufficient to displace that proof.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The judgment stressed that quality of evidence, not quantity, determines interpleader claims.
cases_referred_to: The Sheriff for Zimbabwe v Masango & 2 Others HH 448/19; Bruce N.O. v Josiah Parkers & Sons Ltd 1972 (1) SA 68; Zanderberg v Van Zyl 1910 AD 258.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021 provisions on interpleader proceedings.
reviewer_comments: Citation and judgment number taken from filename and judgment heading. The judgment heading records claimant Robert Masocha and judgment creditor Lafarge Cement Zimbabwe Limited.
export_history: Compiled into High_Court_Harare_Batch_26_2022_Law_Report_Drafts.txt.

document_name: Payloads (Pvt) Ltd v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority (879 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 879 (8 December 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Payloads (Private) Limited v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority
subject_area: Customs and excise; seizure of vehicle; notice of intention to sue; prescription; special plea.
catchwords_flynote: Section 196 notice; wrong cause of action in notice; declaratur after notice of review; prescription under Customs and Excise Act; truck and trailer seized after undeclared goods found.
headnote: ZIMRA seized a truck and trailer after discovering marijuana and second-hand clothes in addition to declared fertilizer. The plaintiff gave notice of an intended review but then sued for a declaratur. ZIMRA raised special pleas that the notice was defective and that the claim had prescribed. The court held that the notice was fatally defective because it directed ZIMRA to a materially different cause of action from the one pleaded. The prescription plea failed because the action had been filed within the applicable eight-month period from the communicated refusal to release the vehicles. The matter was struck off the roll on the defective-notice plea.
issues_for_determination: Whether the s 196 notice gave sufficient detail of the cause of action; whether changing from review to declaratur invalidated the notice; whether the claim had prescribed under the Customs and Excise Act.
legal_principles: A statutory notice to ZIMRA must give sufficient detail of the intended cause of action so that the authority may investigate and decide whether to settle or defend. Failure to comply with a peremptory statutory notice requirement is fatal. Prescription begins when the relevant cause of action is complete and the claimant is aware of the facts and identity of the debtor.
held_decision_points: The notice was fatally defective. The prescription plea was dismissed. The claim was struck off the roll.
ratio_decidendi: A notice of intention to sue that identifies review proceedings does not validly found a later action for declaratory relief where the cause of action materially differs and prejudices the statutory defendant’s opportunity to investigate.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court commented on confusion created by the different three-month and eight-month periods in the Customs and Excise Act.
cases_referred_to: Schierhout v Minister of Justice 1926 AD 99; Machacha v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority HB 186/11; Care International Zimbabwe v Zimbabwe Revenue Authority & 2 Ors SC 76/17; Ebrahim v Controller of Customs and Excise 1985 (2) ZLR 1 (S).
legislation_considered: Customs and Excise Act [Chapter 23:02], ss 193(12) and 196; State Liabilities Act [Chapter 8:15]; Prescription Act [Chapter 8:11]; Administrative Justice Act [Chapter 10:28].
reviewer_comments: Filename citation and heading judgment number align. Full judgment text extracted using python-docx.
export_history: Generated as part of High_Court_Harare_Batch_17_2022_Law_Report_Drafts.txt for Harare High Court 2022 law-report extraction.

document_name: Mahati v Nyarenda (880 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 880 (19 December 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Mahati v Nyarenda
subject_area: Family law; divorce; matrimonial property; pre-trial conference
catchwords_flynote: Divorce — assets of spouses — property under mortgage — motor vehicle under staff loan — whether assets fall for distribution — need for evidence on equitable distribution.
headnote: In divorce proceedings, the parties agreed on most ancillary issues but disputed whether a CABS Budiriro house and Toyota Avensis motor vehicle formed part of the assets of the spouses. The court held that they did form part of the assets of the spouses under the Matrimonial Causes Act but required further evidence before determining equitable distribution.
issues_for_determination: Whether the house and motor vehicle were assets of the spouses for purposes of division under the Matrimonial Causes Act; if so, how they should be distributed.
legal_principles: The court’s discretion under s 7 of the Matrimonial Causes Act concerns the assets of the spouses, not only property fully paid for or already transferred by title. Equitable distribution requires evidence on values, income, contributions, needs and other statutory factors.
held_decision_points: The house and Toyota Avensis formed part of the assets of the spouses. The parties were directed to file affidavits of evidence and supporting documentation on their proposed distribution.
ratio_decidendi: A spouse’s interest in property acquired during marriage through a mortgage or loan may fall within the assets of the spouses, but equitable apportionment cannot be made without sufficient evidence on value and the parties’ circumstances.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted that submissions are not evidence and cannot substitute for affidavits or supporting documents on the statutory factors.
cases_referred_to: Gonye v Gonye 2009 (1) ZLR 232 (S); Manyoni v Manyoni HH 4/16; Field v Field nee Parham HH 68/21.
legislation_considered: Matrimonial Causes Act [Chapter 5:13], s 7.
subject_area_terms: Family law; divorce; matrimonial property; pre-trial conference

document_name: Savanhu v Ushewokunze and 14 Others (881 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 881 (14 December 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Georgina Dadirayi Savanhu v Herbert Sylvester Masiyiwa Ushewokunze VI N.O. and 14 Others
subject_area: Civil procedure; preliminary points; property; declaratory relief; rescission; joinder; prescription; lis pendens; non-disclosure; immovable property
catchwords_flynote: Opposed application - multiple preliminary points in property dispute - alleged fraud and historical transfer disputes - supporting affidavit withdrawal by barred party struck out - respondents’ points in limine dismissed - hybrid application not defective - matter to be set down on merits.
headnote: The applicant claimed ownership of three immovable properties historically registered in her name and challenged various transactions, judgments and subdivisions affecting them. Numerous preliminary points were raised by both sides. The court dealt only with the preliminary points. It admitted new letters of administration appointing a co-executor, granted condonation for a defective notice of opposition, struck out the fourth respondent’s withdrawal documents because she was barred, and dismissed all respondents’ preliminary points, including abuse of process, material disputes of fact, lis pendens, non-joinder, incompetent relief and attacks on the answering affidavit.
issues_for_determination: Whether the first respondent had locus to oppose as sole executor; whether the second and third respondents’ notice of opposition was defective; whether a barred fourth respondent could withdraw a supporting affidavit; and whether respondents’ preliminary objections defeated the application before the merits.
legal_principles: A barred party has no right of audience and may not file processes without condonation and upliftment of bar. Material disputes of fact must be real and incapable of resolution on the papers. Non-joinder does not necessarily defeat a cause. Points of law, including prescription, may be raised at any stage. Hybrid relief may be permissible where related issues are before the court and the requirements of each relief are addressed.
held_decision_points: Applicant’s first point fell away after appointment of a co-executor; the second point fell away after condonation; the third point succeeded and the fourth respondent’s withdrawal documents were struck out. All respondents’ preliminary points were dismissed. The Registrar was directed to set the matter down for determination on the merits.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio is procedural: the application could proceed to the merits because none of the preliminary objections established a basis to defeat or strike the matter before substantive determination.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court stressed that ownership documents, alleged prescription and alleged fraud affecting immovable property required interrogation rather than summary dismissal on preliminary objections.
cases_referred_to: Forestry Commission v Moyo 1997 (1) ZLR 254 (S); GMB v Muchero 2008 (1) ZLR 216; Supa Plant Investments (Pvt) Ltd v Edgar Chidavaenzi HH 92-09; Zimbabwe Bonded Fibreglass (Pvt) Ltd v Peech 1987 (2) ZLR 338 (S); El Elion Investments (Pvt) Ltd v Auction City (Pvt) Ltd SC 29-16; Muchakata v Netherburn Mine 1996 (1) ZLR 153 (S); Muskwe v Nyajina & Ors SC 17-12.
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 1971, rr 227(2)(c), 449(1)(a), 87; High Court Rules, 2021, r 32(11); High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], s 14.
reviewer_comments: Full text extracted from the mounted DOCX. Final order is interlocutory/procedural and does not determine the property merits.

document_name: S v Fichani (882 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 882 (22 May 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: S v Fichani
subject_area: Criminal procedure; condonation; late noting of appeal; murder conviction and sentence
catchwords_flynote: Condonation – late noting of criminal appeal – delay of seven years and ten months – incarcerated self-actor – need for full explanation – prospects of success – murder with actual intent – condonation refused.
headnote: The applicant sought condonation for late noting of an appeal against a 2014 conviction for murder and a 23-year sentence. Although the court acknowledged the challenges faced by incarcerated self-actors, it held that a delay approaching eight years required a detailed and plausible explanation. The proposed appeal also lacked prospects of success. Condonation was refused.
issues_for_determination: Whether the delay in noting appeal should be condoned; whether the explanation and prospects of success justified condonation.
legal_principles: In condonation applications, a court considers the length of the delay, explanation, and prospects of success, with the interests of justice as an overarching consideration. Incarceration may explain some delay but does not exempt an applicant from the rules.
held_decision_points: Application for condonation refused.
ratio_decidendi: A bare reliance on incarceration and self-representation is insufficient to explain an inordinate delay, particularly where the proposed appeal is free from no predictable failure.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court considered but rejected the respondent’s concession, finding it insufficiently reasoned.
cases_referred_to: Viking Woodwork (Pvt) Ltd v Blue Bells Enterprises (Pvt) Ltd 1998 (2) ZLR 249 (S); Mashave v ZUPCO 2000 (1) ZLR 478 (S); Potifa Sawaka v The State HH 262-20; Elvis Mucheri v The State HH 120-20; Petronella Kagonye v The State HH 657-22; Knowledge Mashopo v The State HH 614-22; S v Chikumba HH 724-15; S v Hlahla HMA 01-16; S v Jokwi HH 305-21; S v Muburirwa HMA 04-22; S v Ndlovu 2012 (1) ZLR 393.
legislation_considered: Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], s 47.
reviewer_comments: Drafted from the mounted source judgment and retained for legal editorial review.

document_name: Kauma and Another v Vambe and Another (883 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 883 (19 December 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: PILO KAUMA AND ANOTHER versus PINKY DAMBUDZO VAMBE AND ANOTHER
subject_area: Urgent chamber application; stay of execution; rescission; land sale; Contractual Penalties Act; material non-disclosure; punitive costs.
catchwords_flynote: Stay of execution pending rescission – applicant falsely claimed no knowledge of summons – material non-disclosure – alleged cancellation of instalment sale did not comply with Contractual Penalties Act – rescission prospects poor – urgent application dismissed with legal practitioner-client costs.
headnote: The applicants sought urgent stay of execution of a default order pending rescission. The Court found that the first applicant had materially misled the court about when he knew of the summons and that the rescission application had poor prospects. The alleged cancellation of the agreement of sale was unsupported by a compliant written notice under the Contractual Penalties Act. The application was dismissed with punitive costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the matter was urgent.
    Whether the applicants made material non-disclosures or misled the court.
    Whether the rescission application had prospects of success.
    Whether the agreement of sale had been properly cancelled under the Contractual Penalties Act.
    Whether stay of execution would serve real and substantial justice.
legal_principles: A stay of execution is discretionary and turns on real and substantial justice. The court may “peep into” the rescission application to assess prospects. Where the rescission lacks merit or the applicant has abused process, stay should be refused. Under section 8 of the Contractual Penalties Act, an instalment sale of land cannot be terminated for breach without compliant written notice giving at least the prescribed period to remedy the breach.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicants were not candid and had materially misled the court.
    Held, that the alleged cancellation of the sale failed to meet the requirements of the Contractual Penalties Act.
    Held, that the rescission application disclosed no prospects of success.
    Held, that the urgent application for stay of execution was dismissed with costs on a legal practitioner-client scale.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio was that an applicant seeking stay pending rescission must show real and substantial justice and prospects of rescission; where the underlying cancellation is legally defective and the applicant misleads the court, stay is not justified.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court described the first applicant’s explanations about subdivision and re-sale of the stand as illogical and designed to twist the court’s mind.
cases_referred_to: Cohen v Cohen 1979 (3) SA 420 (R); Mburuma v United Apostolic Church & Anor HH 142/15; Tonderai Katsa v Samuel Goredema & Anor HH 09/22; Zvidza & Anor v Mudoti HH 422/14; other stay-of-execution authorities as cited.
legislation_considered: Contractual Penalties Act [Chapter 8:04], section 8; High Court Rules, 2021, rule 60(9).

document_name: Nyamadzawo v Nyamadzawo nee Chikwiro (884 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 884 (19 December 2022) Copy(2).docx
case_name: Nyamadzawo v Nyamadzawo nee Chikwiro
subject_area: Family law; divorce; distribution of matrimonial assets; immovable property; maintenance; custody by consent.
catchwords_flynote: Divorce – ancillary relief – parties resolved most issues by consent – distribution of Budiriro and Chitungwiza properties – indirect contribution – fair and equitable division – defendant awarded Chitungwiza property and 40% of Budiriro property.
headnote: In divorce proceedings the parties agreed on dissolution of the marriage, custody, access, maintenance and movable property. The only outstanding issue was distribution of two immovable properties: Stand 4395 Budiriro 2, Harare and Stand 29925 Unit L, Seke, Chitungwiza. Applying s 7 of the Matrimonial Causes Act and authorities on direct and indirect contributions, needs and expectations, the court awarded the defendant the Chitungwiza property as her sole property and a 40% share in the Budiriro property, with the plaintiff retaining 60% and a right to buy out the defendant.
issues_for_determination: Whether the immovable properties formed part of the spouses’ assets; what distribution was fair and equitable; how to value and effect the buy-out.
legal_principles: Under s 7 of the Matrimonial Causes Act, the court exercises a broad discretion to distribute the assets of spouses. Both direct financial contribution and indirect contribution as spouse/parent are relevant, as are needs, means and future circumstances.
held_decision_points: A decree of divorce was granted. The defendant was awarded the Chitungwiza property as sole property. The Budiriro property was divided 60% to the plaintiff and 40% to the defendant, with plaintiff granted the right to buy out the defendant’s share.
ratio_decidendi: Although the plaintiff advanced claims based on registration and contribution, fairness required recognition of the defendant’s contributions and needs after a long marital relationship. A 40% share reflected a fair and equitable distribution of the Budiriro property in addition to the Chitungwiza award.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court reaffirmed that a wife is not to leave empty-handed merely because she made no direct financial contribution to the matrimonial home.
cases_referred_to: Gonye v Gonye 2009 (1) ZLR 232 (S); Sithole v Sithole & Anor HB 14/94; Muteke v Muteke (S) 88/94 and other matrimonial property authorities cited in the judgment.
legislation_considered: Matrimonial Causes Act [Chapter 5:13], especially s 7; Marriages Act [Chapter 5:11].
reviewer_comments: Draft.

document_name: Matonhodze v Minister of Justice N.O and 3 Others (885 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 885 (19 December 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Matonhodze v Minister of Justice N.O. and Others
subject_area: Deceased estates; succession; declaratur; community court jurisdiction; certificate of heir; general law marriage.
catchwords_flynote: Declaratur – deceased estate – certificate of heir issued by community court – deceased married under general law – customary-law succession inapplicable – certificate declared null and void – estate property.
headnote: The applicant sought a declaratory order nullifying a certificate of heir issued by the Chibi Community Court in favour of the late Frank Mapuvire in respect of the estate of Edward Gwainda Mapuvire, who was married to Nellie Mapuvire under general law. The respondents argued that the application was stale, sought consequential relief and offended the need for finality. The court held that where a deceased person had contracted a civil marriage under the Marriage Act, his estate was to be administered under general law and not customary law. The community court therefore lacked jurisdiction. The certificate of heir was declared null and void, and related transfer, if any, was declared null and void.
issues_for_determination: Whether the community court had jurisdiction to issue a certificate of heir over the estate of a deceased person married under general law; whether declaratory relief should be granted despite the age of the decision; what property declarations were appropriate.
legal_principles: The estate of a person who contracted a civil marriage under the Marriage Act is dealt with under general law, not customary succession law. The High Court may grant declaratory relief under s 14 of the High Court Act where there is a live controversy and an interested person.
held_decision_points: The application for a declaratory order was granted. The certificate of heir issued in favour of Frank Mapuvire was declared null and void. Any transfer of property from the estate of Frank Mapuvire to Florence Mapuvire was declared null and void. Chamahota Store and the rural homestead at Chivi were declared matrimonial property of Edward and Nellie Mapuvire. Each party was to bear its own costs.
ratio_decidendi: Because the deceased had contracted a general-law marriage, customary-law heirship before the community court could not validly determine his estate. The community court’s lack of jurisdiction rendered the certificate of heir a nullity.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court noted that paragraph 5 of the draft order, which sought to declare the applicant heir, was not sustainable and was struck out.
cases_referred_to: Mujawo v Chogugudza 1992 (2) ZLR 321 (SC); Magaya v Magaya 1999 (1) ZLR 100 (SC); Johnsen v Agricultural Finance Corporation 1994 (1) ZLR 95 (HC); Markham v Minister of Energy and Power Development & 3 Ors HH 275/21.
legislation_considered: High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], s 14; Marriage Act [Chapter 37] / [Chapter 5:11]; succession and estate-administration principles under general law.
reviewer_comments: Draft. The heading contains the typo “HICH COURT OF ZIMBABWE”; preserved in full-text field.

document_name: NMB Bank Limited v Tembo and Another (886 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 886 (21 December 2022) Copy(2).docx
case_name: NMB Bank Limited v Tembo and Another
subject_area: Civil procedure; dismissal of frivolous and vexatious action; mortgage settlement; sale and buy-back; eviction; lease cancellation.
catchwords_flynote: Rule 31 application – action alleged to be frivolous and vexatious – mortgage property sold to bank under compromise with option to buy back – option not exercised – first respondent later sued to undo sale – claim dismissed summarily – eviction and holding-over damages granted.
headnote: The applicant bank sought dismissal under r 31 of an action filed by the first respondent challenging the sale of a mortgaged property to the bank. The property had been transferred to the bank after a compromise/buy-back arrangement following a consent judgment declaring the property specially executable. The first respondent later occupied as a tenant and then sued alleging undue influence and deviation from the buy-back agreement. The court held that the claim was hopeless, mala fide and intended to delay enforcement. The action was dismissed as frivolous and vexatious, the lease cancelled, arrears and holding-over damages granted, and eviction ordered.
issues_for_determination: Whether the first respondent’s action was frivolous or vexatious; whether the lease should be cancelled; whether eviction and arrears/holding-over damages should be granted.
legal_principles: Rule 31 permits dismissal of an action that is frivolous or vexatious. Proceedings are vexatious where they are groundless, improper, instituted without sufficient basis, or pursued to harass or delay. The court will not allow abuse of its process.
held_decision_points: The first respondent’s claim was summarily dismissed as frivolous and vexatious. The lease was cancelled. Arrears, holding-over damages, eviction, and attorney-client costs were ordered.
ratio_decidendi: The documentary record showed a lawful sequence: mortgage default, consent judgment, compromise, transfer, tenancy and later agreement to vacate. The first respondent’s later claim lacked a bona fide foundation and sought only to delay the applicant’s rights as owner/lessor.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court found no basis to treat the alleged non-joinder point as live after it was not pursued in oral submissions.
cases_referred_to: Fisheries Development Corporation of SA Ltd v Jargensen & Anor; Fisheries Development Corporation of SA Ltd v AWJ Investments (Pty) Ltd & Others 1979 (3) SA 1331 (W); S v Cooper & Ors 1977 (3) SA 475; Lawrence v Norreys 39 Ch D 213; Room Hire Co (Pty) Ltd v Jeppe Street Mansions (Pty) Ltd 1949 (3) SA 1155 (T).
legislation_considered: High Court Rules, 2021, r 31.
reviewer_comments: Draft. The judgment text states HC 30/20 in the disposition, but the background identifies HC 30/22; both are preserved in substance here.

document_name: Shonhiwa v Matito and 3 Others (887 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 887 (21 December 2022) Copy(1).docx
case_name: Shonhiwa v Matito and 3 Others
subject_area: Civil procedure; immovable property; double sale; specific performance; material disputes of fact; non-joinder
catchwords_flynote: Specific performance — double sale of immovable property — applicant relying on unsigned or incomplete sale documents — competing purchaser in occupation with executed agreement and proof of payment — material disputes of fact — application proceedings inappropriate.
headnote: The applicant sought to validate an alleged agreement of sale for a Tynwald South stand, set aside the subsequent sale to the first and second respondents, compel transfer, and evict the respondents. The first respondent raised preliminary objections, including material disputes of fact and non-joinder. The court held that the applicant’s proof of purchase was incomplete and disputed, including absence of a signed agreement in the founding papers, lack of direct proof of payment, uncertainty over the role of estate agents, and absence of the late seller’s estate. The disputes could not be resolved on the papers. Because the applicant should have foreseen the disputes and nonetheless proceeded by motion, the application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the matter could be determined on the papers.
    Whether the applicant established a valid prior sale agreement.
    Whether the late seller’s estate and the Master should have been joined.
    Whether the applicant had locus standi to seek eviction and specific performance.
legal_principles:
Application proceedings are inappropriate where real, genuine and material disputes of fact are foreseeable and cannot be resolved on the papers.
    A material dispute of fact exists where the respondent’s version leaves the court with no ready answer without further evidence.
    Applicants who proceed by motion despite foreseeable disputes risk dismissal rather than referral to trial.
held_decision_points:
Material disputes of fact existed regarding whether the applicant had purchased the property, whether payment had been made, the role of Danai Properties, and whether there was a double sale by the late seller.
    Evidence from the executor of the late seller’s estate and from Danai Properties was necessary.
    The applicant proceeded by motion despite foreseeable disputes.
    The application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: Because the applicant’s alleged sale, payment, and transfer entitlement were seriously contested and not proved by primary documentation, the court could not grant specific performance or set aside the competing agreement on affidavit evidence alone. Motion proceedings were inappropriate and the application fell to be dismissed.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The court emphasised that litigants should not use application proceedings tactically where they know or should know that serious factual disputes will arise.
cases_referred_to:
Supa Plant Investments (Pvt) Ltd v Edgar Chidavaenzi HH 92/09
    Mashingaidze v Mashingaidze 1995 (1) ZLR 219 (H)
    Masukusa v National Foods Ltd & Anor 1983 (1) ZLR 232 (H)
    Tamarillo (Pty) Ltd v BN Aitken (Pty) Ltd 1982 (1) SA 398 (A)
legislation_considered: Civil Evidence Act [Chapter 8:01], s 11
reviewer_comments: Mounted file was extractable. The matter was disposed of on material disputes of fact; the remaining preliminary points were not considered.

document_name: Zungude v Rusike (888 of 2022) [2022] ZWHHC 888 (21 December 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: Zungunde v Rusike
subject_area: Family law; custody of minor children; best interests of the child; probation officer report; access arrangements; appeal from Magistrates Court.
catchwords_flynote: Custody – biological parents separated after customary union – father granted custody of two minor male children by lower court – mother appealing – whether best interests of children properly considered – whether reliance on probation officer report was justified – appeal dismissed but access order clarified.
headnote: The appellant appealed against a Magistrates Court order granting custody of two minor children to the respondent father. She argued that the lower court wrongly found that the parties had agreed that the father retain custody, failed to give sufficient reasons for refusing her custody, and over-relied on the probation officer’s report. The High Court held that the record supported the lower court’s approach, that the best interests of the children favoured continued custody with the respondent, and that the appeal lacked merit. The court amended the access order to specify access periods.
issues_for_determination: Whether the lower court had evidence of an agreement that the respondent retain custody; whether the appellant was unsuitable for custody; whether the best interests of the children were properly considered; whether the probation officer’s report was properly relied upon; and whether the access order required clarification.
legal_principles: In custody matters, the best interests of the child are paramount. A lower court may consider the age, sex, social and financial position of each parent, past conduct toward the children, the children’s environment and the probation officer’s report. An appellate court will not lightly disturb a custody decision where the lower court applied the best-interests standard and gave reasons supported by the record.
held_decision_points: All grounds of appeal failed. The court held that the lower court properly considered the circumstances and the probation officer’s report. The appeal was dismissed with costs. The lower court’s access order was amended to specify access every two weekends from Friday to Sunday, alternate public holidays, and two weeks of every school holiday.
ratio_decidendi: The respondent had retained actual custody after separation and provided a stable environment. The appellant had not shown that the lower court’s conclusion, based on the record and probation officer’s report, was wrong. The children’s best interests were better served by maintaining custody with the respondent, subject to clear access rights for the appellant.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified beyond clarification that vague “reasonable access” orders should be specified where necessary.
cases_referred_to: Hackim v Hackim 1998 (2) ZLR 61; Herbstein & Van Winsen, The Civil Practice of the Superior Courts in South Africa, 2nd ed.
legislation_considered: Guardianship of Minors Act [Chapter 5:08], s 5(1); High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], s 31.
reviewer_comments: Full citation taken from filename and judgment heading. Case name in judgment heading is Zungunde; filename records Zungude. Judgment heading spelling preserved in fields.
export_history: Compiled into High_Court_Harare_Batch_28_2022_Law_Report_Drafts.txt.
