document_name: Radhu v Radhu (nee Chiwara) (59 of 2022) [2022] ZWMSVHC 59 (27 July 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: JOSPHAT RADHU versus PATIENCE RADHU (nee CHIWARA)
citation: Radhu v Radhu (nee Chiwara) (59 of 2022) [2022] ZWMSVHC 59 (27 July 2022)
subject_area:
Family law
    Divorce
    Matrimonial property
    Maintenance
    Civil trial
catchwords_flynote: Family law – divorce – irretrievable breakdown common cause – division of matrimonial assets – joint registration of immovable property – rebuttable presumption of equal shares – direct and indirect contributions – commercial value of shop – maintenance for minor child – decree and distribution order granted
headnote:
The parties to divorce proceedings agreed that their marriage had irretrievably broken down and that a decree of divorce should be granted. They also agreed on the division of most movables and one immovable property, the Rujeko property, which was to be awarded to the defendant. The disputed issues concerned the distribution of the Chesvingo shop, the incomplete Zimre Park property and maintenance for the remaining minor child.
    
    The Court applied s 7 of the Matrimonial Causes Act [Chapter 5:13], taking account of the parties’ direct and indirect contributions, income-earning capacity, assets, obligations, standard of living, duration of the marriage and the need for a just and equitable distribution. It recognised that joint registration of the disputed properties gave rise to a rebuttable presumption of equal ownership, but found that the plaintiff’s cattle-broking and business activities had played a comparatively more significant role in building the matrimonial estate, while the defendant’s cross-border trading, domestic work and assistance in construction were also material. It also considered the commercial value of the Chesvingo shop, the Zimre property, the defendant’s award of a commercial minibus and the Rujeko property. The Court granted divorce, awarded custody of the minor child to the defendant, ordered maintenance of ZWL$35 000 per month, distributed movables, awarded the Rujeko property to the defendant, the Chesvingo shop to the plaintiff and the Zimre property in a 60/40 ratio in favour of the plaintiff. No order as to costs was made.
issues_for_determination:
Whether a decree of divorce should be granted.
    How the disputed matrimonial immovable property, namely the Chesvingo shop and Zimre property, should be distributed.
    What effect joint registration of the disputed properties had on the parties’ shares.
    What weight should be given to the parties’ direct and indirect contributions, the commercial value of the assets, and the assets already agreed or awarded to each party.
    What maintenance should be ordered for the remaining minor child.
    Whether costs should be awarded.
legal_principles:
Section 7 of the Matrimonial Causes Act [Chapter 5:13] governs division, apportionment or distribution of spouses’ assets on divorce and requires regard to all the circumstances, including income-earning capacity, financial needs, standard of living, age and condition, direct and indirect contributions, benefits lost on dissolution and the duration of the marriage.
    Asset division on divorce is not a simple arithmetical division of property values; the Court must consider intertwined contributions and all relevant circumstances.
    Where spouses are registered joint owners of immovable property in undivided shares, there is a rebuttable presumption of equal ownership, strengthened where the parties were married when ownership was acquired.
    A court should not depart from a registered owner’s share merely because it seems fair, reasonable or equitable, but must consider evidence capable of rebutting the presumption and the statutory factors.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the marriage relationship had irretrievably broken down and a decree of divorce was to be granted.
    
    Held, further, that the jointly registered disputed properties created a presumption of equal shares, but the distribution exercise under s 7 of the Matrimonial Causes Act required consideration of all circumstances, including the parties’ direct and indirect contributions and the nature and value of the assets.
    
    Held, further, that the plaintiff’s cattle-broking and related business activities played a relatively more significant role in building the matrimonial estate, though the defendant’s cross-border trading, domestic work and assistance in construction were also recognised.
    
    Held, further, that the plaintiff was to receive the Chesvingo shop and 60% of the Zimre property, while the defendant was to receive the Rujeko property and 40% of the Zimre property, together with the movables specified in the order.
    
    Held, further, that maintenance of ZWL$35 000 per month was justified for the minor child and there would be no order as to costs.
ratio_decidendi: The Court’s distribution of the disputed assets rested on s 7 of the Matrimonial Causes Act: although joint registration created a starting presumption of equal shares, the plaintiff’s greater business contribution, the commercial value of the shop, the incomplete Zimre property, and the assets already awarded to the defendant justified awarding the Chesvingo shop to the plaintiff and splitting the Zimre property 60/40 in the plaintiff’s favour.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

    Takafuma v Takafuma 1994 (2) ZLR 103

document_name: S v Pedzisai (60 of 2022) [2022] ZWMSVHC 60 (28 July 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: THE STATE versus TENDAI PEDZISAI
citation: S v Pedzisai (60 of 2022) [2022] ZWMSVHC 60 (28 July 2022)
judges:
MAWADZE J
    Assessors: Mr Chikukwa; Mrs Chademana
subject_area:
Criminal law
    Murder
    Constructive intent
    Evidence
    Self-defence
    Sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – murder – constructive intent – fatal head injury in bottle store – accused alleged deceased fell on damaged floor after being pushed – State witnesses and medical evidence showing accused struck deceased with metal bar stool – rejection of self-defence and accident – guilty of murder under s 47(1)(b) – sentence of 15 years’ imprisonment
headnote:
The accused was charged with murder after the deceased, a 72-year-old man, died from a severe head injury sustained inside a bottle store. The State alleged that the accused demanded a drink from the deceased after helping rescue his ox earlier that day, pinned him against a counter with a metal bar stool, and then struck him on the head with it. The defence was that the deceased was the aggressor, advanced towards the accused with a stool, and was fatally injured when the accused pushed him away and he struck his head on a damaged floor.
    
    The Court accepted the State evidence. It found the doctor’s evidence logical and consistent with the State case, because the head injury was consistent with a blow from the metal bar stool and inconsistent with falling onto a potholed floor. The evidence of the bar lady and other patrons materially corroborated the State version. The Court rejected the accused’s version and found that, even giving him the benefit of doubt that he did not intend to kill, he foresaw that using the weapon, the degree of force and the direction of the blow to the head could cause death. He was convicted of murder with constructive intent under s 47(1)(b) and sentenced to 15 years’ imprisonment.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the deceased sustained the fatal injury from being struck with the metal bar stool or from falling onto a damaged floor.
    Whether the State witnesses and medical evidence were credible and reliable.
    Whether the accused acted in self-defence, by mistake, or with constructive intent to kill.
    Whether the accused was guilty of murder and what sentence was appropriate.
legal_principles:
Murder with constructive intent under s 47(1)(b) is established where an accused foresees the real risk that his conduct may cause death and proceeds notwithstanding that risk.
    Medical evidence may be used to test competing factual versions about the mechanism of injury.
    Self-defence or accident must be assessed against the totality of the evidence and probabilities.
    A deliberate blow to the head with a heavy metal object, especially against an elderly and defenceless person, may support an inference of constructive intent.
    Sentence for murder ordinarily requires a lengthy custodial term; mitigating factors may affect the length of the sentence.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the deceased’s fatal head injury was caused by the accused striking him with a metal bar stool and not by the deceased falling onto a damaged or potholed floor.
    
    Held, further, that the State witnesses materially corroborated each other and the doctor’s evidence supported the State version.
    
    Held, further, that the accused’s self-defence or accident version was false.
    
    Held, further, that although actual intent to kill was not proved, the accused foresaw that the weapon used, the force applied and the direction of the blow to the head could cause death.
    
    Held, further, that the accused was guilty of murder with constructive intent under s 47(1)(b) and was sentenced to 15 years’ imprisonment.
ratio_decidendi: The conviction rested on the Court’s acceptance of the State and medical evidence that the accused delivered a severe blow to the deceased’s head with a heavy metal bar stool. That conduct, directed at a vulnerable part of an elderly person’s body, supported constructive intent because the accused foresaw the risk of death and proceeded.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: Mhere v Matenhese N.O and 2 Others (62 of 2022) [2022] ZWMSVHC 62 (7 September 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: TATENDA MHERE versus JEREMIAH MATENHESE N.O. (In his capacity as Executor Dative for Estate Late Obert Mhere) And THE MASTER OF THE HIGH COURT N.O. And THE ASSISTANT MASTER OF THE HIGH COURT FOR MASVINGO N.O.
citation: Mhere v Matenhese N.O and 2 Others (62 of 2022) [2022] ZWMSVHC 62 (7 September 2022)
subject_area:
Civil procedure
    Default judgment
    Chamber applications
    Administration of estates
    Removal of executor
catchwords_flynote: Civil procedure – application for default judgment – respondents absent despite service – chamber application for removal of executor dative – court’s power to seek further argument and avoid rubber-stamping – draft order affecting third parties and punitive costs – default judgment granted with variation
headnote:
The applicant sought default judgment removing the first respondent as executor dative of the estate of the late Obert Mhere and ancillary relief concerning the administration and sale of the estate’s residential property. He alleged that the first respondent had fraudulently obtained consent to sell the property, had entered into a double sale, and that there was a will affecting the devolution of the house. Although the first respondent had filed opposition denying impropriety, none of the respondents appeared when the matter was set down.
    
    The Court referred the matter to the unopposed roll and requested counsel to address concerns arising from the record, including whether the estate was registered as testate or intestate, whether the registration and appointment were being attacked as nullities, and why other beneficiaries or third-party purchasers were not cited. Counsel objected to the Court’s request for further argument. The Court held that a court considering default judgment is not a rubber stamp and may require further information under its inherent powers and rule 60(8)(b) of the High Court Rules, 2021. Although default judgment was granted, paragraph 5 of the draft order was deleted because it potentially affected third parties who had not been cited, and the request for punitive costs was refused. Ordinary costs were awarded against the first respondent.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the Court was obliged to grant default judgment exactly as sought once the respondents were in default.
    Whether a judge in a chamber application could require further argument or clarification before granting default relief.
    Whether the order sought was consistent with the applicant’s allegations about the registration of the estate and appointment of the executor.
    Whether paragraph 5 of the draft order should be granted where it potentially affected third parties not cited in the proceedings.
    Whether attorney-client costs were justified.
legal_principles:
A court or judge considering default judgment is not a rubber stamp and may decline or vary relief where the order sought is unlawful, contrary to public policy, outside jurisdiction or unsupported by the necessary requirements.
    A judge dealing with a chamber application may, in terms of rule 60(8)(b) of the High Court Rules, 2021, require a party’s legal practitioner to appear and present further argument.
    Removal of an executor under s 85 of the Administration of Estates Act [Chapter 6:01] presupposes a properly appointed executor pursuant to a properly registered estate.
    Default judgment proceedings should generally avoid deciding substantive contested issues, unless the relief sought is tainted by patent illegality, absurdity or is contrary to public policy.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the Court was not reduced to a rubber stamp merely because the respondents were in default. The Court could seek clarification and further argument to ensure that the order sought was lawful and justified.
    
    Held, further, that rule 60(8)(b) of the High Court Rules, 2021 empowered the judge to require counsel to provide further argument on issues of concern in a chamber application.
    
    Held, further, that paragraph 5 of the draft order potentially affected third parties who had not been cited and was to be deleted.
    
    Held, further, that there was no justification for costs on the attorney-client scale. Default judgment was granted subject to deletion of paragraph 5 and with ordinary costs against the first respondent.
ratio_decidendi: The Court granted default judgment only in varied form because, notwithstanding the respondents’ default, a judge must ensure that default relief is lawful, supported by the record and does not affect uncited third parties; the Court therefore deleted the problematic paragraph of the draft order and refused punitive costs.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to: None identified
legislation_considered:
Administration of Estates Act [Chapter 6:01], s 85
    High Court Rules, 2021, r 60(8)(b)
cases_referred_to_terms: None identified

document_name: Jakamoko Investments (Pvt) Ltd v De Bruyn (67 of 2022) [2022] ZWMSVHC 67 (28 September 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: JAKAMOKO INVESTMENTS (PVT) LTD versus BRENNAN JAMES MICHAEL DE BRUYN
citation: Jakamoko Investments (Pvt) Ltd v De Bruyn (67 of 2022) [2022] ZWMSVHC 67 (28 September 2022)
subject_area:
Civil procedure
    Application proceedings
    Material disputes of fact
    Property law
    Eviction
    Company law
    Actio rei vindicatio
catchwords_flynote: Civil procedure – opposed application for eviction – actio rei vindicatio – respondent alleging purchase of entire shareholding and company’s sole asset – disputed directorship, shareholding and inclusion of property in sale – material disputes of fact foreseeable – whether matter should be referred to trial or dismissed – application dismissed
headnote:
The applicant company sought the eviction of the respondent from residential property in Kwekwe, alleging that it was the registered owner and that the respondent occupied the property without right, claim or title. The respondent opposed the application, contending that he had taken occupation after purchasing the applicant company’s entire shareholding from Bryony Scrooby, and that the sale was a package deal including the property. He relied on prior Bulawayo High Court litigation and a deed of settlement relating to the sale.
    
    The respondent raised preliminary points, including the validity of the resolution authorising the application, material disputes of fact and failure to disclose a cause of action. The Court held that the challenge to the resolution was intertwined with the merits and deferred it. It found, however, that there were material disputes of fact on the papers concerning the relationship between Bryony Scrooby and the applicant, whether she remained a director, whether she held 100% of the shares, and whether the sale of shares included the property. The Court further held that the applicant should have foreseen these disputes given the previous Bulawayo litigation and that the applicant had gambled by proceeding by application. The application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the resolution authorising the applicant’s founding affidavit was valid.
    Whether material disputes of fact existed on the papers and, if so, whether they were foreseeable.
    Whether the respondent’s alleged purchase of shares and the property provided a defence to eviction.
    Whether the matter should be dismissed, referred to trial, referred to oral evidence or decided on the papers.
legal_principles:
A material dispute of fact arises where material facts alleged by an applicant are disputed by a respondent in such a manner that the court has no ready answer without further evidence.
    In application proceedings final relief may be granted only where the facts stated by the respondent, together with admitted facts in the applicant’s affidavit, justify the order.
    The actio rei vindicatio allows an owner to recover property from a possessor without the owner’s consent, but a respondent may raise defences, including that the applicant is not owner or that the respondent’s possession is lawful.
    Where foreseeable material disputes of fact arise in motion proceedings, the court may dismiss the application, refer the matter to trial, hear oral evidence, or adopt a robust approach; dismissal is appropriate where the applicant should have realised that serious disputes of fact would arise.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the challenge to the applicant’s resolution could not properly be determined as a preliminary point because it went to the merits and depended on the disputed relationship between the Scroobys and the applicant company.
    
    Held, further, that the papers disclosed material disputes of fact concerning the applicant’s shareholding, Bryony Scrooby’s alleged continuing directorship, and whether the agreement of sale of shares included the immovable property.
    
    Held, further, that the disputes were glaring and foreseeable, particularly in light of prior Bulawayo High Court litigation involving substantially the same subject matter, and the applicant therefore should not have proceeded by application.
    
    Held, further, that the application was to be dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The application was dismissed because the applicant sought final eviction relief by motion proceedings despite foreseeable and serious disputes of fact on the central issues of company control, shareholding and whether the property formed part of the transaction under which the respondent claimed lawful occupation.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: Mahakute v Equistruct (Private) Limited t_a ICP Farming and Another (7 of 2022) [2022] ZWMSVHC 7 (14 February 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: LISMATI MAHAKUTE versus EQUISTRUCT (PRIVATE) LIMITED t/a ICP FARMING and THE MINISTER (N.O) MINISTRY OF LANDS, AGRICULTURE, WATER, CLIMATE AND RURAL RESETTLEMENT
citation: Mahakute v Equistruct (Private) Limited t_a ICP Farming and Another (7 of 2022) [2022] ZWMSVHC 7 (14 February 2022)
subject_area:
Land law
    Agricultural land
    Lease of farm infrastructure
    Administrative law
    Opposed applications
catchwords_flynote: Land law – offer letter holder seeking cancellation of lease over pack shed – allegation that shed fell within applicant’s farm and lease was obtained by misrepresentation – applicant without lease to pack shed – location of shed not proved – Acquisition of Farm Equipment or Material Act not applicable to immovable pack shed – application dismissed
headnote:
The applicant, who held an offer letter for S/D 16 of Farm 54, Lot 3 of Buffalo Range, sought cancellation of a memorandum of lease granted to the first respondent in respect of a pack shed at Farm 54 Hippo Valley Estates. He alleged that the pack shed was within his farm, that he and neighbouring farmers used it for sorting fruit, and that the lease had been procured through false representations. The respondents opposed the application. The first respondent stated that she had applied to use the shed and took occupation only after authorisation by the second respondent. The second respondent maintained that the shed did not form part of the applicant’s land and that the applicant required a lease to utilise such property.
    
    The Court found that the applicant had not proved that the first respondent made misrepresentations to obtain the lease. The application form showed recommendations by the relevant officials that the structure was vacant and idle. The Court also found that it was common cause that the applicant held no lease to the pack shed and that he had not proved that the pack shed was situated on his gazetted land. The Acquisition of Farm Equipment or Material Act was not directly relevant because a pack shed did not fall within the definition of farm equipment. The applicant had no right to question the lease and the application was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the first respondent procured the lease by misrepresentation.
    Whether the applicant proved that the pack shed was situated on his gazetted land.
    Whether the applicant, without a lease to the pack shed, had a right to challenge the lease granted to the first respondent.
    Whether the Acquisition of Farm Equipment or Material Act applied to the pack shed.
legal_principles:
An applicant who seeks cancellation of a lease must establish the factual basis for the challenge, including any alleged misrepresentation.
    An offer letter for agricultural land does not by itself prove entitlement to a separate pack shed or government building where the applicant has no lease to that structure.
    The Acquisition of Farm Equipment or Material Act [Chapter 18:23] applies to farm equipment as defined, namely movables used for agricultural purposes; a pack shed described in the papers did not fall within that definition.
    Where an applicant holds no lease to the disputed structure and has not proved that the structure falls within his allocated land, he has no basis to question the lease granted to another party.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant had not proved that the first respondent made misrepresentations to officials of the second respondent in order to obtain the lease.
    
    Held, further, that the applicant had not proved that the pack shed was situated on his gazetted farm and it was common cause that he held no lease to the pack shed.
    
    Held, further, that the Acquisition of Farm Equipment or Material Act was not directly relevant because the pack shed was not farm equipment as defined.
    
    Held, further, that the applicant had no right to question the lease agreement granted to the first respondent and the application was unmeritorious.
ratio_decidendi: The application failed because the applicant did not prove either that the first respondent procured the lease by misrepresentation or that the pack shed was situated on the applicant’s gazetted land; in any event, the applicant held no lease to the pack shed and therefore had no established right to challenge the lease granted to the first respondent.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: Prosecutor General Zimbabwe v Chitungo (7 of 2022) [2022] ZWMTHC 7 (9 February 2022) Copy.docx
case_name: PROSECUTOR GENERAL ZIMBABWE versus AMOS CHITUNGO
court: ‘Amos Chitungo is found guilty as charged and the record is hereby remitted to the Magistrate’s Court for sentencing’.
case_number:
CA 50/21
REF CASE NO. CRB MTS 515/20
citation: Prosecutor General Zimbabwe v Chitungo (7 of 2022) [2022] ZWMTHC 7 (9 February 2022)
judges:
Panel: CHAREWA AND MUZENDA JJ
Judgment by: MUZENDA J
subject_area:
Criminal procedure
Criminal appeals
Road traffic offences
Negligent driving
Evidence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal procedure – criminal appeal by Prosecutor General against acquittal – negligent driving under s 52(2)(a) of the Road Traffic Act [Chapter 13:11] – contributory negligence not a defence in criminal matters – side-swipe collision – real evidence and point of impact in traffic cases – value of photographs, traffic accident records and sketch plan – absence of VID report not fatal where accused admitted vehicle defects – appeal allowed – acquittal set aside – verdict of guilty substituted – matter remitted for sentencing
headnote:
The appellant appealed against the respondent’s acquittal by the Magistrates Court at Mutasa on a charge of negligent driving in contravention of s 52(2)(a) of the Road Traffic Act [Chapter 13:11]. The respondent had been driving an Isuzu truck which side-swiped a Mazda B2500 driven by the complainant, causing the complainant to lose his arm. The trial court accepted that the complainant’s speeding or contributory negligence caused the accident, placed emphasis on the absence of a VID report, and concluded that the matter was evenly balanced.

The High Court held that the trial court misdirected itself. In traffic accident cases, credibility must be tested against real evidence, including photographs, the traffic accident book and the sketch plan. The sketch plan and gorge mark showed that the point of impact was in the complainant’s lane. The complainant’s alleged speed was irrelevant where he was travelling in his correct lane and did not anticipate obstruction. The absence of a VID report was secondary because the respondent had admitted that his vehicle had been pronounced unroadworthy, that the defects existed before the collision, and that the defect caused the truck to move diagonally. Those concessions supported the state case that the truck’s loading tray or rear section had encroached into the complainant’s lane. The appeal was allowed, the acquittal was set aside, a verdict of guilty was substituted, and the matter was remitted to the Magistrates Court for sentencing.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the Magistrates Court misdirected itself in treating the complainant’s alleged contributory negligence or speeding as a defence or cause of the collision.
Whether the court a quo failed to give proper weight to the real evidence, including the gorge mark, photographs and sketch plan showing the point of impact.
Whether the absence of a VID report was fatal to the State case.
Whether the State proved negligent driving beyond reasonable doubt.
Whether the acquittal should be set aside and substituted with a verdict of guilty.
legal_principles:
In a criminal trial the State bears the onus to prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt, and an accused person’s defence succeeds if it is reasonably possibly true.
Contributory negligence is not a defence to criminal liability for negligent driving.
In traffic accident cases, credibility cannot be measured by demeanour alone; testimony must be assessed against real or circumstantial evidence on the ground.
The point of impact is central in collision cases. Photographs of damaged vehicles, the traffic accident book and the sketch plan may supply decisive real evidence.
A VID report may be secondary where the accused has admitted the material vehicle defects and the evidence otherwise establishes the point of impact and negligent conduct.
An appellate court may interfere where the trial court misdirected itself on law or fact and the evidence proves guilt beyond reasonable doubt.
held_decision_points:
Held, that in traffic accident cases the court must compare oral testimony with the real evidence on the ground, including photographs, the traffic accident record and the sketch plan.

Held, further, that the court a quo misdirected itself by ignoring the real evidence, especially the sketch plan and point D1 gorge mark, which showed that the side-swipe occurred in the complainant’s lane.

Held, further, that the complainant’s alleged speeding was irrelevant where the evidence showed that he was travelling in his correct lane and did not anticipate obstruction by the respondent’s truck.

Held, further, that the absence of a VID report was secondary because the respondent had admitted that his vehicle was pronounced unroadworthy, that the defects existed before the accident, and that the defect caused the truck to move diagonally.

Held, further, that the respondent’s defence was not reasonably possible when assessed against the State evidence, the real evidence and his concessions. The State proved the particulars of negligent driving beyond reasonable doubt.

Held, further, that the appeal had to be allowed, the acquittal set aside, a verdict of guilty substituted, and the record remitted to the Magistrates Court for sentencing.
ratio_decidendi: The acquittal could not stand because the trial court ignored decisive real evidence showing that the point of impact was in the complainant’s lane and wrongly treated the absence of a VID report and the complainant’s alleged speed as creating reasonable doubt. The respondent’s own admissions about the unroadworthy, diagonally moving truck, together with the sketch plan and accident evaluator’s evidence, proved beyond reasonable doubt that the respondent negligently failed to keep proper control of the Isuzu truck and allowed it to encroach into the complainant’s lane.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

R v Ramotale 1992 (2) ZLR 397 at 399 B-D per MCNALLY JA
S v Ramotale, supra, at 401 F-G

S v Ramotale, supra, at 401 F-G

document_name: SC 61 - 22 BDO CHARTERED ACCOUNTANTS V ROBIN VELA & ANOR.docx
case_name:
(1) BDO ZIMBABWE CHARTERED ACCOUNTANTS
    v
    ROBIN VELA
    AND
    (2) THE AUDITOR GENERAL OF ZIMBABWE
    v
    ROBIN VELA AND BDO ZIMBABWE CHARTERED ACCOUNTANTS
court:
“The application be and is hereby dismissed with costs.”

    MATHONSI JA : I Agree

    CHIWESHE JA : I Agree

    Sawyer & Mkushi, the appellant’s legal practitioners.

    Rubaya and Chatambudza, the respondent’s legal practitioners
case_number: Ref. CASENO: SC 258/20; Ref. CASE NO: SC 285/20
judges:
Panel: BHUNU JA, MATHONSI JA & CHIWESHE JA
    Judgment by: BHUNU JA
    Concurrence: MATHONSI JA and CHIWESHE JA agreed.
subject_area: Administrative law; Judicial review; Auditor-General; Forensic audits; Partnerships and citation; Public power
catchwords_flynote: Administrative action – private accounting partnership engaged by Auditor-General to conduct forensic audit – whether private auditor is an administrative authority under Administrative Justice Act – source of public power – agency and delegation – s 309 Constitution and Audit Office Act – forensic audit report not a public administrative decision by private auditor – review proceedings set aside – partnership citation under Order 2A/r 11.
headnote:
Two consolidated appeals challenged a High Court judgment reviewing and setting aside a forensic audit report prepared by BDO Zimbabwe Chartered Accountants at the request of the Auditor-General. The High Court had held that the forensic audit constituted administrative action subject to review, and that the audit process was procedurally unfair to the first respondent, Robin Vela.

    The Supreme Court first rejected the preliminary objection that BDO, as a partnership, could not sue or be sued in its own name. The Court distinguished Gariya Safaris, which concerned a non-existent company, and held that partnerships and other associations were capacitated by procedural rules to sue and be sued under their names.

    On the merits, the Court held that BDO was not an administrative authority under s 2(1) of the Administrative Justice Act. It was a private partnership contracted to assist the Auditor-General by gathering information and preparing a report. Section 309 of the Constitution confers functions on the Auditor-General, not on private agents. Section 9 of the Audit Office Act authorises the Auditor-General to hire private auditors, but does not delegate public administrative authority to them. BDO made no public administrative decision and its conduct was not subject to review as administrative action. The High Court judgment was set aside and the review application dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether BDO, as a partnership, was properly before the Court and had locus standi to appeal.
    Whether BDO had a right of appeal against the High Court judgment.
    Whether a private auditor contracted by the Auditor-General was an administrative authority or exercised public administrative power when conducting a forensic audit.
    Whether BDO’s conduct and audit process were subject to review under the Administrative Justice Act.
    Whether the High Court’s review order could stand.
legal_principles:
Procedural rules permit partnerships, trusts, syndicates, clubs and other associations that are not bodies corporate to sue or be sued in their association names. This distinguishes such entities from non-existent fictitious companies.

    An “administrative authority” under s 2(1) of the Administrative Justice Act is confined to persons or bodies with lawful authority to carry out the administrative action concerned. The character of public power is assessed by factors including source of power, coercive capacity, public impact and whether the decision is exercised in the public interest.

    The Auditor-General’s constitutional and statutory functions are vested in the Auditor-General. The power to hire private auditors does not, without express delegation, confer administrative authority on the private auditor. A private contractor who gathers evidence or prepares a report for a public authority does not thereby make a public administrative decision.
held_decision_points:
BDO was properly cited and had standing because procedural rules allow partnerships to sue and be sued in their own names.

    BDO was not an administrative authority within the meaning of the Administrative Justice Act.

    The Auditor-General could hire BDO to assist with an audit, but could not thereby confer public administrative authority on BDO.

    BDO’s forensic audit report was not a public administrative decision. Its conduct and processes were therefore not reviewable under the Administrative Justice Act.

    All other review issues fell away. The appeal succeeded, the High Court judgment was set aside, and the review application was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio decidendi is that a private auditor engaged by the Auditor-General to conduct a forensic audit remains a private contractor or functionary unless statute confers public administrative authority on it. The mere fact of acting as an agent or aid of a public office does not convert the private auditor’s report or process into administrative action reviewable under the Administrative Justice Act.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

    Chirwa v Transnet (as cited in the judgment)

    Supreme Court Rules, 2018, r 11

document_name: SC 104 - 22 STEVEN MUSIMIKE vs THE STATE.docx
case_name:
STEVEN MUSIMIKE
    v
    THE STATE
judges:
Panel: GWAUNZA DCJ, UCHENA JA & KUDYA AJA
    Judgment by: KUDYA AJA
    Concurrence: GWAUNZA DCJ and UCHENA JA agreed.
subject_area: Criminal law; Criminal procedure; Public officers; Abuse of duty; Customs and revenue; Evidence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law - criminal abuse of duty as public officer - s 174 of the Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23] - customs officer detaining consignment without issuing detention documents - reverse onus under s 174(2) - mens rea - whether omission was for purpose of disfavouring importer - Customs and Excise Act [Chapter 23:02], s 201 - appeal allowed - Supreme Court review powers used for non-appealing co-accused.
headnote:
The appellant, a ZIMRA revenue supervisor stationed at Beitbridge container depot, was convicted with a co-accused of criminal abuse of duty as a public officer. A consignment of liquor imported by Turkey Trading (Pvt) Ltd had been referred to the container depot after the electronic clearance system red-flagged the importer for possible previous smuggling. The appellant detained the consignment but did not issue detention documents. The trial court and the High Court found that his failure to issue the documents prejudiced the importer and constituted criminal abuse of duty.

    On further appeal, the Supreme Court held that the actus reus of breach of public duty was common cause, but the decisive question was mens rea. Section 174(2) created a rebuttable presumption that once a prejudicial breach of duty was proved, the accused bore a legal burden to prove on a balance of probabilities that he did not act for the purpose of showing favour or disfavour. The Court held that the appellant discharged that burden. The evidence showed that the detention was connected to an investigation into possible previous smuggling and to the possible exercise of a statutory lien under s 201 of the Customs and Excise Act. The State failed to prove the requisite intention to disfavour the importer. The appeal was allowed. Using its review powers under s 25 of the Supreme Court Act, the Court also set aside the conviction and sentence of the non-appealing co-accused, Obert Tafadzwa Charamba.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the High Court erred in upholding the appellant's conviction for criminal abuse of duty as a public officer.
    Whether the State proved the essential elements of s 174 of the Criminal Law Code, particularly mens rea.
    Whether the appellant rebutted the statutory presumption in s 174(2) on a balance of probabilities.
    Whether the Supreme Court should exercise review powers in relation to the co-accused who had not appealed.
legal_principles:
Proof beyond reasonable doubt requires a high degree of probability but not proof beyond the shadow of a doubt.

    Section 174(1) of the Criminal Law Code requires that a public officer intentionally do or omit to do something contrary to duty for the purpose of showing favour or disfavour to a person. Section 174(2) creates a rebuttable presumption once a prejudicial breach of duty is proved. The State bears the onus of proving the actus reus beyond reasonable doubt; the accused bears a legal burden to disprove the presumed improper purpose on a balance of probabilities.

    The word 'intentionally' imports a deliberate or reckless state of mind, not mere inadvertence or an honest attempt to perform official functions.

    An appellate court may interfere with factual findings where the lower court misdirected itself, overlooked material facts, or made irrational findings.

    Section 201 of the Customs and Excise Act permits detention of goods under the control of customs as a lien for unpaid duty owed by the importer.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the appellant's failure to issue detention documents constituted a breach of duty, but the decisive question was whether he acted with the requisite intention to disfavour the importer.

    Held, further, that the totality of the evidence showed on a balance of probabilities that the detention was motivated by an investigation into possible smuggling and the higher ideal of recovering lost revenue, not by an ulterior motive to injure the importer.

    Held, further, that the appellant rebutted the presumption in s 174(2), and the State failed to establish mens rea.

    Held, further, that the co-accused's conviction also could not stand because his referral of the consignment was likewise connected to the investigation and his belief in the availability of s 201 of the Customs and Excise Act negated an ignoble motive.

    Held, further, that the appeal should be allowed and the co-accused's conviction and sentence set aside under s 25 of the Supreme Court Act.
ratio_decidendi: Although the appellant omitted to issue required detention documents, the evidence established on a balance of probabilities that his omission was connected to an ongoing customs investigation and possible statutory lien, not to an intention to favour or disfavour the importer. Since mens rea under s 174 was not proved after the presumption was rebutted, the conviction could not stand.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court considered it a proper case to exercise review powers in favour of the non-appealing co-accused because the same evidential foundation undermined his conviction.

document_name: SC 30 - 22 Riozim v Maranatha Ferro Chrome and Another.docx
case_name:
(1) RIOZIM LIMITED (2) RM ENTERPRISES (PRIVATE) LIMITED
    v
    (1) MARANATHA FERROCHROME (PRIVATE) LIMITED (2) JUSTICE NOVEMBER TAFUMA MTSHIYA (RTD)
court:
1.  “The application for the setting aside of the arbitrator’s ruling on jurisdiction and the existence and validity of the arbitral agreement is a nullity and is struck off the roll.

        2.  The application for the setting aside of the arbitrator’s interlocutory award on prescription shall proceed to a hearing on the merits” .

    3.  The case is remitted to the court a quo for it to hear and determine the application for the setting aside of the arbitrator’s interlocutory award on prescription on the merits.

    MAVANGIRA JA: I AGREE

      MATHONSI JA : I AGREE

    Coghlan, Welsh & Guest, appellants legal practitioners

    Kantor & Immerman, first respondent’s legal practitioners
judges:
Panel: MAVANGIRA JA, UCHENA JA & MATHONSI JA
    Judgment by: UCHENA JA
    Concurrence: MAVANGIRA JA and MATHONSI JA agreed.
subject_area: Arbitration; Practice and procedure; Statutory interpretation; Prescription
catchwords_flynote: Arbitration – UNCITRAL Model Law – arbitral tribunal’s preliminary ruling on jurisdiction and existence or validity of arbitration agreement – Article 16(3) – distinct from an award – High Court challenge required within thirty days – failure to use prescribed procedure not curable through Article 34 – jurisdiction challenge struck off. Arbitration – interim, interlocutory or partial award – Article 31(7) – “award” in Article 34 used generically and not confined to final award – arbitrator’s determination on prescription outside Article 16 and capable of challenge under Article 34 – High Court erred in dismissing entire application – partial success and remittal for merits hearing.
headnote:
The first appellant and first respondent entered into a shareholders’ agreement concerning the transfer of a 40 per cent interest in the second appellant. When the first respondent referred an alleged breach to arbitration, the appellants raised preliminary objections to the arbitrator’s jurisdiction, the existence and validity of an arbitration agreement binding the second appellant, and prescription. The arbitrator rejected the objections. He ruled that he had jurisdiction and that the second appellant was bound, and he decided that the claim had not prescribed.

    The appellants applied to the High Court under Article 34 of the Model Law to set aside the arbitrator’s decision. The High Court held that Article 34 applied only to final awards, that the appellants should have challenged the jurisdictional ruling within thirty days under Article 16(3), and that their application was an abuse of process. It dismissed the whole application.

    On appeal, the Supreme Court distinguished a “ruling” under Article 16(3) from an “award”. A preliminary ruling on jurisdiction and on the existence or validity of an arbitration agreement must be challenged in the High Court within thirty days under the special procedure in Article 16(3). It remains a ruling even if the parties or arbitrator call it an interim award, and Article 34 cannot revive a lost Article 16 remedy. The jurisdictional part of the appellants’ application was therefore a nullity.

    The prescription decision stood differently. Article 31(7), a Zimbabwean modification to the Model Law, expressly recognises interim, interlocutory and partial awards. In Article 34 the unqualified word “award” is generic; unlike Article 32(1), it is not restricted to a “final award”. The arbitrator’s determination on prescription was capable of classification as an interlocutory, interim or partial award and could be challenged under Article 34. The High Court therefore erred in dismissing that part without a merits hearing. The appeal succeeded only on prescription, the jurisdictional portion was struck off, and the case was remitted for the High Court to determine the Article 34 challenge to the prescription award. Each party bore its own costs.
issues_for_determination:
1. Whether an arbitral tribunal’s preliminary determination on jurisdiction and on the existence or validity of an arbitration agreement is a “ruling” that must be challenged under Article 16(3), or an “award” capable of being set aside under Article 34.
    2. Whether the arbitrator’s determination on prescription was an interim, interlocutory or partial award capable of challenge under Article 34.
    3. Whether the High Court erred in dismissing the appellants’ entire application rather than separating the jurisdictional ruling from the prescription award.
legal_principles:
Section 2(3) of the Arbitration Act requires regard to the international origin of the UNCITRAL Model Law and the desirability of uniform interpretation. Decisions from other Model Law jurisdictions may be considered, while due account must be taken of local modifications, identified in the First Schedule by italics.
    Article 16 distinguishes a preliminary “ruling” on jurisdiction and on the existence or validity of the arbitration agreement from an “award”. A party receiving such a preliminary ruling has thirty days to request the High Court to decide the matter. Failure to use that procedure within the prescribed period irrevocably loses the remedy; Article 34 cannot be used as a substitute.
    Article 31(7), a local modification, authorises interim, interlocutory and partial awards. Article 32(1) refers specifically to a “final award”, while Article 33 recognises corrected, interpreted and additional awards. The unqualified term “award” in Article 34 is therefore generic and includes forms of award other than a final award.
    Article 34 permits the setting aside of awards, not Article 16 rulings. A substantive determination outside jurisdiction or the existence or validity of the arbitration agreement, including a determination on prescription, may constitute an interim, interlocutory or partial award and may be challenged under Article 34.
    A statute must be interpreted so that effect is given to every word. Courts may not import words that the legislature did not use or treat deliberate terminology as superfluous.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the arbitrator’s preliminary determination on jurisdiction and the existence and validity of the arbitration agreement was a “ruling” under Article 16(3), not an award.
    Held, further, that the appellants were required to request the High Court to decide that ruling within thirty days. Having failed to do so, they could not use Article 34 to revive the lost remedy, and the jurisdictional part of the application was a nullity.
    Held, further, that “award” in Article 34 is used generically and is not confined to a final award terminating the arbitration. Interim, interlocutory and partial awards recognised by Article 31(7) may be challenged under Article 34.
    Held, further, that the arbitrator’s determination on prescription was outside the scope of Article 16 and was capable of classification as an interlocutory, interim or partial award. The appellants were entitled to a merits hearing of their Article 34 challenge to that award.
    Held, further, that the High Court erred in dismissing the entire application. The appeal succeeded in part, the jurisdictional portion was struck off, and the prescription portion was remitted for determination on the merits, with each party bearing its own costs.
ratio_decidendi: The Model Law deliberately uses “ruling” for a preliminary jurisdictional determination and supplies a specific thirty-day challenge under Article 16(3); such a ruling cannot be converted into an award or attacked under Article 34 after that period lapses. By contrast, Article 34 uses the broader term “award”, which, read with Articles 31(7), 32(1) and 33, includes interim, interlocutory and partial awards. Because prescription was not a jurisdictional matter governed by Article 16, the arbitrator’s prescription determination could be challenged as an interlocutory award under Article 34 and had to be heard on the merits.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

    R v Kirk 1914 CPD 564 at page 567

document_name: SC 53 - 22 Tendai Bonde v National Foods.docx
case_name:
TENDAI BONDE
    v
    NATIONAL FO-ODS LIMITED
judges:
Panel: GUVAVA JA, UCHENA JA & CHITAKUNYE JA
    Judgment by: UCHENA JA
    Concurrence: GUVAVA JA and CHITAKUNYE JA agreed.
subject_area: Civil procedure; Supreme Court practice; Rescission; Functus officio; Abuse of process; Labour appeals
catchwords_flynote: Supreme Court – finality of judgments – no appeal from Supreme Court judgment or order – limited rescission under r 73 of Supreme Court Rules read with r 449 of High Court Rules – review powers confined to inferior courts, tribunals and administrative authorities – functus officio – repeated rescission applications – abuse of process – punitive costs.
headnote:
The applicant repeatedly attempted to undo orders of the Supreme Court arising from a labour dispute concerning an employee incentive scheme. He had consented to dismissal of his appeal against the Labour Court, unsuccessfully sought rescission of that consent order in SC 57/20, and then filed a further application seeking rescission of SC 57/20 so that the earlier consent order and the Labour Court judgment could also be undone.

    The Supreme Court held that its judgments and orders are final except in the limited circumstances provided by the Rules. Its review powers under s 25 of the Supreme Court Act do not extend to reviewing its own decisions. Rule 73 of the Supreme Court Rules, read with r 449 of the former High Court Rules, permits correction, variation or rescission only where the judgment was erroneously sought or granted in the absence of an affected party, contains an ambiguity or patent error or omission, or was granted as a result of a mistake common to the parties.

    The applicant sought to raise a matter that had not been before the earlier bench and to use rescission as a disguised review or appeal against final Supreme Court decisions. The application was incompetent, the respondent was not barred from defending it, and the matter was struck off the roll with costs. The applicant’s preliminary point was dismissed on the legal-practitioner-and-client scale.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the respondent was barred from opposing the application because it sought to execute extant orders.
    Whether the application for rescission of SC 57/20 was competent.
    Whether the Supreme Court could review or rescind its own final judgment outside the limited grounds in the Rules.
    Whether s 5 of the Supreme Court Act disqualified a judge who had dealt with chamber applications from sitting in the subsequent rescission application.
legal_principles:
No appeal lies from a Supreme Court judgment or order except where constitutionally provided. The Supreme Court becomes functus officio once it has finally determined a matter.

    The Court’s review jurisdiction under s 25 of the Supreme Court Act is limited to proceedings and decisions of inferior courts, tribunals and administrative authorities. It does not permit the Supreme Court to review its own judgments.

    Rule 73 of the Supreme Court Rules imports High Court practice where the Supreme Court Rules are silent. Rule 449 of the former High Court Rules allows correction, variation or rescission only in the listed limited circumstances. A party may not use r 449 to relitigate, appeal, or review a final judgment.

    Section 5 of the Supreme Court Act concerns appeals from judgments in which the judge participated; it does not prevent Supreme Court judges from dealing with chamber applications or applications to correct, vary or rescind Supreme Court orders.
held_decision_points:
There was no order staying execution of the respondent’s extant judgment, so the respondent was entitled to be heard.

    The application was incompetent because the alleged common mistake had not been placed before the Court in SC 57/20, and a court cannot be faulted for failing to determine an issue not before it.

    The applicant’s continued attempt to undo final Supreme Court orders was an abuse of process.

    Section 5 of the Supreme Court Act did not disqualify the judge complained of because the earlier chamber applications did not constitute the appealed decision.

    The applicant’s preliminary point was dismissed with legal-practitioner-and-client costs; the respondent’s preliminary point was upheld and the application was struck off the roll with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio decidendi is that the Supreme Court cannot review its own final judgments, and its power to correct, vary or rescind them is confined to the narrow grounds in r 449 as imported by r 73. A litigant may not use successive rescission applications to evade finality or reopen matters already determined, particularly where the alleged basis for rescission was not before the earlier court.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to:
Unitrack (Private) Limited v Telone (Private) Limited SC 10/18
    Supreme Court judgment SC 57/20 involving the applicant’s earlier rescission application
legislation_considered:
Supreme Court Act [Chapter 7:13], ss 5, 25, 26
    Supreme Court Rules, 2018, r 73
    High Court Rules, 1971, r 449
cases_referred_to_terms: Unitrack (Private) Limited v Telone (Private) Limited SC 10/18

document_name: SC 83 - 22 BARON DUBE v THE STATE.docx
case_name:
BARON DUBE
    v
    THE STATE
judges:
Panel: GWAUNZA DCJ, CHITAKUNYE JA & MWAYERA JA
    Judgment by: CHITAKUNYE JA
    Concurrence: GWAUNZA DCJ and MWAYERA JA agreed.
subject_area: Criminal law; Criminal procedure; Evidence; Appeals; Sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law - murder with constructive intent - s 47(1)(b) of the Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23] - subjective realisation of real risk or possibility under s 15 - transferred consequence where person killed was not the apparent target - s 57 - appeal against conviction and sentence - appellate interference with credibility and factual findings - defence outline inconsistent with evidence in chief - sentence discretion - appeal dismissed.
headnote:
The appellant appealed against the whole judgment of the High Court sitting at Bulawayo, which convicted him of murder in contravention of s 47(1)(b) of the Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23] and sentenced him to 10 years imprisonment. The deceased was shot at Atlas Mine, Esigodini. It was common cause that the shot which killed the deceased was fired from the appellant's gun. The appellant contended that the revolver discharged accidentally when it fell and he picked it up. The State case, accepted by the trial court, was that the appellant deliberately fired in the direction of three men, including the deceased, during an exchange with one of them.

    The Supreme Court held that the appeal was essentially an attack on credibility and factual findings. A trial court's credibility findings will not be lightly disturbed unless they defy reason or are plainly wrong. The appellant's defence outline and his evidence in chief contained irreconcilable inconsistencies about how the firearm discharged. The evidence supported the trial court's rejection of the accidental-discharge version and its acceptance of the State witnesses' evidence. By firing a lethal firearm in the direction of the trio, the appellant realised the real risk or possibility of causing death and continued despite that risk. Section 57 also prevented him from escaping liability merely because the person killed was not the apparent intended target. The sentence ground was not persisted with and no basis for appellate interference with sentence was shown. The appeal against conviction and sentence was dismissed.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the High Court erred in rejecting the appellant's accidental-discharge defence and accepting the State witnesses' evidence.
    Whether the evidence proved beyond reasonable doubt that the appellant acted with constructive intent under s 47(1)(b) of the Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act.
    Whether the fact that the deceased may not have been the intended target affected liability.
    Whether there was any basis for appellate interference with the sentence of 10 years imprisonment.
legal_principles:
For murder under s 47(1)(b) of the Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act, the State must prove that the accused caused another person's death unlawfully and intentionally, with constructive intent arising where the accused realised a real risk or possibility that his or her conduct might cause death and continued despite that risk. Section 15 provides a subjective test involving awareness and recklessness.

    Section 57 of the Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act addresses cases where death is caused to someone other than the intended victim. Liability may still arise if the accused realised that the conduct involved a real risk or possibility of causing the death of someone other than the intended victim.

    Credibility findings are primarily for the trial court. An appellate court interferes only where such findings defy reason, are plainly wrong, or are inconsistent with proven facts and probabilities. A defence outline defines the nature of the defence; material inconsistencies between it and later evidence may properly undermine the defence.

    Sentence lies within the trial court's discretion. An appellate court will not interfere unless the discretion was not judiciously exercised, the sentence is vitiated by irregularity or misdirection, or it is so severe as to induce a sense of shock.
held_decision_points:
Held, that there was no basis for interfering with the High Court's credibility findings. The appellant's accidental-discharge account was materially inconsistent and improbable, and the State evidence supported the finding that he deliberately fired in the direction of the trio.

    Held, further, that the appellant acted with constructive intent because he realised, or must have realised, the real risk or possibility that firing a lethal firearm in the direction of people walking away could cause death, yet proceeded to do so.

    Held, further, that the fact that the deceased may not have been the person at whom the shot was aimed did not absolve the appellant, having regard to s 57 of the Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act.

    Held, further, that no argument was sustained against sentence and no basis existed for appellate interference. The appeal against conviction and sentence was dismissed.
ratio_decidendi: The conviction and sentence were upheld because the accepted evidence and probabilities showed that the appellant deliberately fired a lethal firearm towards people and thereby realised the real risk of causing death; the trial court's credibility findings were not plainly wrong, and the appellant's inconsistent accidental-discharge version did not raise reasonable doubt. No sentencing misdirection or shocking severity was shown.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

    Gumbura v The State SC 78-14
    Santam BPK v Biddulph (2004) 2 All SA 23 (SCA)
    S v Hitschmann SC 2-07
    Magodo v The State SC 25-03
    S v Mundowa 1998(2) ZLR 392

    Criminal Procedure and Evidence Act [Chapter 9:07], s 66(6)(b)

document_name: SC 86 - 22 CITY OF HARARE v EVERISTO MUNGATE.docx
case_name:
CITY OF HARARE
    v
    EVARISTO MUNGATE
judges:
Panel: GOWORA JA, MAKONI JA & BERE JA
    Judgment by: MAKONI JA
    Concurrence: GOWORA JA agreed. BERE JA was no longer in office.
subject_area: Delict; Negligence; Municipal liability; Contributory negligence; Pleadings; Damages
catchwords_flynote: Delict - negligence - municipal uncovered drainage catch pit - duty to maintain public infrastructure - damages for personal injury - contributory negligence - defence must be pleaded and apportionment claimed - pre-trial conference cannot create unpleaded defence - negligence in the air - costs - appeal dismissed.
headnote:
The respondent fell into an uncovered municipal drainage tunnel or catch pit at night near Simon Mazorodze Road while moving to board a commuter omnibus. He broke his leg and suffered a permanent limp. He sued the City of Harare for damages, alleging that the City had failed to cover the catch pit, erect warning signs, maintain lighting or otherwise make the area safe. The High Court found the City negligent and awarded damages of US$9 769 plus costs, but dismissed the claim for contumelia.

    The City appealed, contending that the respondent had been contributorily negligent and that damages should have been apportioned. The Supreme Court held that contributory negligence must be specifically pleaded and the alternative relief of apportionment claimed. The City had denied liability in toto and had not pleaded contributory negligence in meaningful detail. The issue could not properly be introduced through a pre-trial conference minute. In any event, the evidence did not establish negligence by the respondent causally connected to the harm. His conduct might have been careless, but it did not amount to negligence in law. The uncovered catch pit was the proximate cause of the fall. The High Court was correct, though for different reasons, to reject contributory negligence. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the High Court erred in finding no contributory negligence by the respondent.
    Whether the City had properly pleaded contributory negligence and apportionment under the Damages (Apportionment and Assessment) Act.
    Whether the respondent's conduct was causally connected to the harm suffered.
    Whether there was any basis to interfere with the High Court's costs order.
legal_principles:
The standard test for negligence is whether a diligens paterfamilias in the defendant's position would foresee reasonable possibility of harm, would take reasonable steps to guard against it, and failed to take those steps.

    Contributory negligence under s 4 of the Damages (Apportionment and Assessment) Act requires proof that the claimant's own fault partly caused the damage. The defence must be pleaded and the alternative relief of apportionment claimed.

    Pleadings define the issues between the parties. A party may not direct the other side to one issue in the pleadings and then seek to canvass another at trial. A pre-trial conference cannot properly introduce a defence that is not pleaded.

    Negligence is not established by carelessness in the abstract. It must involve breach of a duty and causal connection to the damage suffered.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the City did not properly plead contributory negligence or claim apportionment of damages. It denied liability in toto and did not set out the respects in which the respondent was alleged to have been negligent.

    Held, further, that the issue of contributory negligence could not properly arise for the first time through a pre-trial conference minute.

    Held, further, that the respondent's conduct did not amount to legally relevant contributory negligence. The uncovered catch pit was the proximate cause of the fall.

    Held, further, that the High Court's costs order was a discretionary order and no basis for appellate interference had been shown. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The appeal failed because contributory negligence was neither properly pleaded nor proved. The City's failure to cover or warn of an uncovered catch pit, which it knew posed a danger to the public, caused the respondent's injuries. The respondent's alleged carelessness was not causally connected negligence warranting apportionment.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that remarks by the High Court about the respondent's unrealistic quantum of damages were obiter and did not undermine the costs award, which followed the result after the respondent succeeded in the suit.
