document_name: SC 68 - 22 Muchaneta Theodora Chimbandi v Mabel Canvas (Private) Limited (2).docx
SC 1 - 22 FRANCIS BERE v JSC(1).docx
case_name:
FRANCIS BERE
    v
    (1) JUDICIAL SERVICE COMMISSION (2) SIMBI VEKE MUBAKO (3) REKAYI MAPHOSA (4) TAKAWIRA NZOMBE (5) VIRGINIA MABHIZA (6) THE PRESIDENT OF ZIMBABWE (7) MINISTER OF JUSTICE, LEGAL AND PARLIAMENTARY AFFAIRS
case_number:
“(i) to investigate into the matter of the removal from office of Honourable Justice Francis Bere JA;

      (ii) to investigate into the matter of Honourable Justice Bere’s conduct, whether it can be deemed to have been tantamount to gross misconduct;

      (iii) to investigate whether the Honourable Judge conducted himself or presided over matters where there was conflict of interest;

      (iv) to investigate any other matter which the Tribunal may deem appropriate and relevant to the enquiry;

      (v) to consider all information submitted by the Judicial Service Commission and any other relevant information in order to arrive at an appropriate recommendation to the President;

      (vi) to recommend to the President whether or not the Honourable Judge should be removed from office in terms of s 187 of the Constitution and;

      (vii) to report to the President, in writing, the result of the inquiry within a period of five (5) months from the date of swearing in of the members.”

    4.  On 13 May 2020, following the publication of the Proclamation referred to above, the appellant made an application in terms of s 4 of the Administrative Justice Act (the Act) before the court a quo. The application was made on the allegation that the first respondent failed to comply with s 3(1)(a) of the Act in that it failed to “act lawfully, reasonably and in a fair manner” in advising the President to set up a Tribunal to investigate the appellant. In making the application it was the appellant’s complaint that, by letter dated 3 March 2020, he was suspended from the office of a judge pending the determination of the question of his removal from office by a three member Tribunal appointed by the President.

    5.  The appellant further averred that the suspension was preceded by a letter addressed to him by the Acting Secretary of the first respondent dated 28 February 2020. In the letter the appellant was informed that at a meeting held on 13 December 2019, the first respondent had resolved to advise the President to consider the appointment of a tribunal to conduct and further investigate him for alleged misconduct.

    6.  The appellant raised five grounds, asserting that the first respondent failed to comply with s 3(1)(a) of the Act. Firstly, it was his averment that the first respondent had no jurisdictional grounds for referring the matter to the President. Secondly, the appellant averred that there was gross irregularity in the referral by the first respondent as it failed to accord him the right to be heard. Thirdly, he asserted that at the time the first respondent made its decision to refer the matter to the President it had become functus officio as it had previously made a determination on the matter exonerating him of any wrong doing. Fourthly, that the first respondent, in making its decision was not properly constituted as it lacked a proper quorum. Finally that the fifth respondent was biased as she was the person who referred the complaint which was made by Mr Moxon (Moxon) to the first respondent. It was thus argued that, as a secretary to the Tribunal, she was a judge in her own cause.

    7.  The second, third, fourth and seventh respondents submitted that they had no interest in the outcome of the application and would abide by the court’s decision. The President maintained that he was simply fulfilling his constitutional obligation when he appointed the Tribunal upon the recommendation of the first respondent.

    8.  The fifth respondent submitted that she was acting in her official capacity as a Permanent Secretary, Coordinator of the Special Anti-Corruption Unit in the office of the President and as Secretary of the Tribunal set up by the President. She stated that she received correspondence from Moxon wherein he complained about the manner in which the appellant had handled his case which was before him. She thereafter referred the complaint against the appellant to the first respondent. She further stated that, before receiving the complaint from Moxon, she had been made aware of allegations of improper conduct against the appellant by Mr Ndudzo, a legal practitioner from Mutamangira and Associates, and she duly included the two incidents in her referral letter to the first respondent and the Chief Justice of Zimbabwe. In denying that she was conflicted, the fifth respondent maintained that, she had no personal interest in the matter and was only carrying out her duties.

    9.  At the commencement of the proceedings the first respondent raised two preliminary points. It was submitted on behalf of the first respondent that the court a quo had no jurisdiction to review the constitutional powers vested in the President if he decides to appoint a Tribunal in terms of s 187 of the Constitution. Secondly, that the matter was lis alibi pendens as a similar application under HC 2162/20 had already been made by the appellant.

    10. On the merits of the matter, the first respondent averred that it acted lawfully, reasonably and in a fair manner in respect of the advice it gave to the sixth respondent in terms of s 187(3) of the Constitution. The first respondent maintained that the appellant was availed an opportunity to respond to the allegations made against him and he duly did so in writing. Further, that the decision to refer the appellant’s matter to the President was made by a properly constituted board of its Commissioners. The first respondent also averred that the onus to prove the purported allegation of an improperly constituted quorum lay with the appellant.

    11. The appellant in turn raised a preliminary point to the effect that the deponent of the first respondent’s opposing affidavit Mr Chikwana (the first respondent’s Acting Secretary) lacked authority to act on behalf of the first respondent. The appellant averred that on the authority of the case of Francis Bere v Judicial Service Commission and Ors HH 269/20 the court had already found that since it is the first respondent which has the constitutional duty to advise the President on the question of removal of a judge from office it could not delegate the function of defending that decision in a court of law to its Acting Secretary.

    DETERMINATION BY THE COURT A QUO

    12. The court a quo, in a well-reasoned judgment dismissed the appellant’s preliminary point. The court found that, in light of the first respondent’s board resolution authorizing Mr Chikwana to do so, he could properly act on its behalf. The court also dismissed the preliminary point taken by the first respondent that the matter before it was lis alibi pendens. The court reasoned that lis alibi pendens was not an absolute bar to a determination of the matter. The court thus dismissed the point as it did not dispose of the matter. The court also declined to deal with the part that the court had no powers to review the Constitutional powers vested in the President as it reasoned that the issue was on the merits and was not a preliminary part.

    13. On the merits of the matter, the court a quo held that on the point that there was no quorum at the first respondent’s meeting of 13 December, 2019, the appellant had made bare allegations without supporting affidavits of those present at the meeting and without attaching minutes of the meeting to show that there was no quorum. The court a quo further held that the first respondent had no obligation to assist the appellant in proving his allegation.

    14. With regards to the issue of whether the first respondent was functus officio and could not reverse its decision of 21 November 2019, wherein it had purportedly found the appellant not guilty of misconduct, the court a quo found that the appellant failed to substantiate his allegations. It found that he did not support his averment that he had received a phone call from the first respondent’s Deputy Acting Secretary informing him that a decision had been taken to exonerate him. The court noted that the appellant ought to have either produced minutes of the alleged meeting of 21 November 2019 or filed a supporting affidavit from the Deputy Acting Secretary.

    15. The court a quo also found, on the issue of the alleged violation of the appellant’s audi alteram partem Rule that the appellant was given an opportunity to respond to the allegations made against him. The court noted that the exchange of correspondences between the first respondent and appellant was sufficient. Thus the court found that the appellant’s right to be heard had not been violated.

    16. Concerning the absence of jurisdictional facts to ground the first respondent’s decision to advise the President, the court noted that it was not sitting as an appeal court to determine the correctness of the first respondent’s decision to refer the appellant’s matter to the President. Rather it was being called upon to decide whether any of the jurisdictional grounds for removal of a judge were disclosed in the first respondent’s papers. The court noted that s 187(1)(c) of the Constitution provides for gross misconduct as one of the jurisdictional grounds for the removal of a judge from office and that is what was averred in the papers to the President. The court, however, concluded that whether or not there was gross misconduct on the part of the appellant was not an issue to be determined by it as the terms of the Tribunal set up by the President covered that aspect. It was therefore left for that Tribunal to determine the guilt or otherwise of the appellant. In accordance with the above findings the court a quo dismissed the application with an order that the appellant pays the first respondent’s costs.
judges:
Panel: GUVAVA JA, UCHENA JA & KUDYA AJA
    Judgment by: GUVAVA JA
    Concurrence: UCHENA JA and KUDYA AJA agreed.
subject_area: Administrative law; Constitutional law; Judicial review; Practice and procedure; Public law
catchwords_flynote: Administrative law – judicial review – Judicial Service Commission advising President to appoint tribunal under s 187(3) of Constitution – subsequent removal of judge rendering appeal moot – discretion to determine moot matter in interests of justice – authority of Commission’s Acting Secretary to depose to affidavit – proof of quorum and functus officio allegations – audi alteram partem – waiver by walking out of tribunal hearing – appeal dismissed.
headnote:
The appellant appealed against the High Court’s dismissal of an application under s 4 of the Administrative Justice Act challenging the Judicial Service Commission’s advice to the President to appoint a tribunal under s 187(3) of the Constitution to inquire into his removal from judicial office. By the time the appeal was heard, the tribunal had completed its inquiry and the President had removed the appellant from office. The first respondent accordingly raised mootness.

    The Supreme Court held that the appeal had been overtaken by events and was moot because the relief sought could no longer have a practical effect. It nevertheless exercised its discretion to determine the merits because the legality of the process for removing a judge and the procedural issues raised were sufficiently important. The Acting Secretary of the Commission was authorised by a Commission resolution and by s 10 of the Judicial Service Act to depose to the opposing affidavit; this did not constitute delegation of the Commission’s constitutional function. The allegations that the meeting lacked a quorum and that the Commission was functus officio were unsupported by evidence. The audi alteram partem rule is flexible; the appellant had been afforded adequate opportunities to make representations and later waived the opportunity offered by the tribunal when he walked out of the hearing. The appeal was dismissed with no order as to costs.
issues_for_determination:
1. Whether the appeal had become moot and, if so, whether the Court should nevertheless determine it in the interests of justice.
    2. Whether the Commission’s Acting Secretary was authorised to depose to the opposing affidavit and act on behalf of the Commission.
    3. Whether the appellant proved that the Commission’s meeting of 13 December 2019 lacked a quorum.
    4. Whether the Commission was functus officio because it had allegedly exonerated the appellant on 21 November 2019.
    5. Whether the appellant’s right to be heard was violated.
legal_principles:
A matter is moot when subsequent events terminate the live controversy and any further judgment would have no practical effect. Mootness is not an absolute bar: a court retains a discretion to determine a moot matter where exceptional circumstances and the interests of justice justify doing so.
    A legal person acts through authorised natural persons. A deponent must be able to swear positively to the facts; a board or commission resolution may authorise an employee with knowledge of the facts to act in litigation without delegating the entity’s substantive statutory or constitutional function.
    A party who makes an allegation bears the duty of proving it; bare assertions unsupported by minutes, affidavits or other evidence do not establish lack of quorum or functus officio.
    The audi alteram partem rule has no fixed content and is governed by fundamental fairness in the circumstances. Written representations may suffice. A person who deliberately walks out of a hearing takes the risk that it will proceed and may thereby waive the right to be heard.
    A High Court judgment does not bind another High Court judge exercising parallel jurisdiction.
held_decision_points:
1.  “The court a quo erred in law in not upholding the appellant’s point in limine, namely that that (sic) the 1st Respondent was not properly before the court as the deponent to its opposing affidavit was prohibited by the Constitution of Zimbabwe from representing the 1st Respondent in an application brought by a judge pursuant to section 187 (3) of the aforesaid Constitution.

    2.  The court a quo erred in law in not finding that the 1st Respondent had no quorum at its meeting of 13th December 2019 and consequently erred in not finding that the 1st Respondent’s advice to the 6th Respondent pursuant to section 187 (3) of the Constitution was a nullity.

    3.  The court a quo erred in law in not finding that at the meeting of 13th December 2019, the 1st Respondent was already functus officio in respect of the complaints raised against the appellant and consequently erred in not finding that the 1st Respondent’s advice to the 6th Respondent pursuant to section 187 (3) of the Constitution was a nullity.

    4.  The court a quo erred in law in not finding that there had been a breach of the audi alteram partem rule in respect of the 1st Respondent’s handling of complaints against the appellant at its meeting on 13th December 2019 and consequently erred in not finding that the 1st Respondent’s advice to the 6th Respondent pursuant to section 187 (3) of the Constitution was a nullity.

    5.  The court a quo erred in law in finding that the court had no jurisdiction to determine the existence or otherwise of the jurisdictional facts under section 187 (1) of the Constitution and grounding the 1st Respondent’s advice to the 6th Respondent in respect of complaints against the appellant pursuant to section 187 (3) of the Constitution.

    SUBMISSIONS BEFORE THIS COURT

    18. At the hearing, the first respondent raised a point in limine to the effect that the matter before the court had become moot. The point was taken on the basis that the appellant had already been removed from the office of a judge following the findings of gross misconduct by the Tribunal appointed by the President. Counsel for the first respondent, Mr Chinake, submitted that the appeal had become moot arising from the fact that the appellant’s appeal was predicated on his suspension from office. He argued that this position had since been overtaken by events. He further submitted that the relief sought by the appellant intending to set aside the decision by the first respondent to refer the complaint against the appellant to the President under s 187(3) of the Constitution would result in a brutum fulmen. Counsel further argued that in light of the Proclamation removing the appellant from the office of a judge, the work of the Tribunal had been completed, the dismissal of the appellant was final and as such the appeal was devoid of merit.

    19. In opposition, counsel for the appellant Mr Madhuku argued that the matter was not moot as there was a live controversy which required the Court’s determination. Counsel submitted that the live issue related to whether or not the decision of the first respondent to advise the President in terms of s 187(3) of the Constitution was valid. It was counsel’s submission that this was a live issue which had to be determined by the court as it affected whether or not the appellant was lawfully removed from office. Counsel further argued that, in any event even if the matter is moot, this Court has a discretion to hear the matter. Counsel thus prayed for the dismissal of the preliminary point.

    20. On the merits of the matter, counsel for the appellant submitted that the Acting Secretary for the first respondent had no authority to depose to the opposing affidavit. Counsel argued that the court in HH 269/20 had already determined that the Acting Secretary could not so act. The court a quo could not make a contrary finding that Mr Chikwana had authority to act on behalf of the first respondent. Counsel also submitted that the quorum recommending that the complaint against the appellant be referred to the President was not properly constituted.

    21. Counsel further submitted that the appellant was not afforded the right to be heard in terms of the audi alteram partem rule as he was not given an opportunity to respond to the letter written by Mr Ndudzo. Counsel argued that the first respondent lacked the jurisdiction to refer the matter to the President without first conducting an internal hearing of its own. Lastly, it was counsel’s argument that each party should bear its own costs owing to the reason that the issues raised in the appeal were fundamentally important.

    22. Per contra, counsel for the first respondent argued that the court a quo did not err in finding that the first respondent’s Acting Secretary could depose to the opposing affidavit as he acted merely as an agent of the first respondent and he could swear positively to the facts as he sat in its meetings. Counsel further argued that the appellant had been afforded his right to be heard as he had been given an opportunity to reply in writing to the complaints levelled against him as well as an opportunity to be heard by the tribunal which right he opted not to exercise by walking out of the hearing. Counsel submitted that in terms of s 187(3) of the Constitution the first respondent was within its rights and had an obligation to refer the matter to the President. He further argued that the onus rested squarely on the appellant to prove his assertion that the first respondent’s quorum was not properly constituted. Counsel was not opposed to the prayer that each party bears its own costs.

    23. Counsel for the second to seventh respondents, Mr Chimombe submitted that none of the appellant’s grounds of appeal impugned the actions of the second to seventh respondents. Counsel submitted that the President was within his constitutional mandate in appointing a Tribunal to investigate the appellant’s alleged acts of misconduct. Counsel thus submitted that the Tribunal correctly investigated the matter and made recommendations to the President. It was his argument that the President correctly acted upon those recommendations in terms of his constitutional powers.

    DETERMINATION OF THE PRELIMINARY POINT

    24. The appellant approached the court a quo with an application for review of the first respondent’s decision to refer his matter to the President and other ancillary relief. Before the application was filed and heard by the court, the President in terms of his constitutional powers under s 187(3) of the Constitution appointed a Tribunal to investigate the alleged misconduct of the appellant. While the Tribunal was in the process of investigating the appellant and making recommendations to the President, the appellant launched this application and other applications before the court a quo. The application was dismissed. It is common cause and accepted by the parties that the appellant was subsequently found guilty of misconduct and removed from the office of a judge. It is on this basis that the first respondent raised the preliminary point to the effect that the appeal before us is now moot.
ratio_decidendi: Although subsequent removal from office rendered the challenge to the referral and suspension moot, the Court determined the merits in the interests of justice. The challenged administrative process was not shown to be unlawful: the Acting Secretary was duly authorised to litigate for the Commission, the appellant produced no evidence establishing lack of quorum or a prior final exoneration, and the opportunities for written representations and a tribunal hearing satisfied the flexible requirements of audi alteram partem, which the appellant ultimately waived by leaving the hearing.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

SC 101 - 22 Master of the High Court vs David Takaendesa and 5 Ors consolidated appeals, draft judgment.docx
case_name:
MASTER OF THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE N.O.
    v
    (1) DAVID TAKAENDESA (2) MACDONALD TAKAENDESA (3) DEN TAKAENDESA (4) HOUSE OF SARI (PVT) LTD (5) RUGARE MANDIMA N.O. (6) REGISTRAR OF DEEDS

    RUGARE MANDIMA N.O.
    v
    (1) DAVID TAKAENDESA (2) MACDONALD TAKAENDESA (3) DEN TAKAENDESA (4) MASTER OF THE HIGH COURT N.O. (5) HOUSE OF SARI (PVT) LTD (6) REGISTRAR OF DEEDS

    HOUSE OF SARI (PVT) LTD
    v
    (1) DAVID TAKAENDESA (2) MACDONALD TAKAENDESA (3) DEN TAKAENDESA (4) RUGARE MANDIMA N.O. (5) MASTER OF THE HIGH COURT N.O. (6) REGISTRAR OF DEEDS
case_number:
Civil Appeal No. SC 109/21
    Civil Appeal No. SC 15/21
    Civil Appeal No. SC 17/21
judges:
Panel: GUVAVA JA, KUDYA JA & MWAYERA JA
    Judgment by: GUVAVA JA
    Concurrence: KUDYA JA and MWAYERA JA agreed.
subject_area: Estates; Administration of estates; Administrative justice; Sale of estate property; Right to be heard; Costs
catchwords_flynote: Estate administration - Master of the High Court - consent to sell estate property by private treaty - s 120 of Administration of Estates Act [Chapter 6:01] - meaning of due inquiry - Master required to formulate independent opinion after inquiry - sale of three farms to settle small estate liability - beneficiaries not heard - Administrative Justice Act [Chapter 10:28] - lawful, reasonable and fair administrative action - costs against administrative authority - de bonis propriis costs - appeal allowed only in part.
headnote:
The deceased's estate included three farms and a butchery. Estate liabilities totalled US$16 287.13, and after partial payment by the beneficiaries the outstanding amount was US$14 047. The executor sought the Master's authority to sell all three farms by private treaty to settle the estate liabilities. The Master issued consents to sale under s 120 of the Administration of Estates Act on condition that the executor first communicate the decision to the beneficiaries. The farms were sold to a neighbouring purchaser for US$700 000. The beneficiaries had objected and had instructed that the butchery be sold instead. The Master stated that the consent could not be revoked because the decision had already been made.

    The High Court set aside the Master's consents, the agreements of sale and the transfers. It also awarded costs against the Master, the purchaser and the executor, including costs de bonis propriis against the executor. The Supreme Court held that s 120 requires the Master to conduct a due inquiry and independently form the opinion that private treaty sale would be advantageous to those interested in the estate. The inquiry is not a judicial inquiry, but it must be practical, financial, fair and directed at the interests of beneficiaries. On the facts, the sale of three farms to settle a relatively small liability, when another asset existed and the beneficiaries had objected, required the Master to hear the beneficiaries and satisfy himself that the sales were in their best interests. The Master failed to apply his mind and improperly relied on the executor. The High Court correctly set aside the consents and resulting sales. However, the costs orders were altered: administrative bodies should not be mulcted in costs absent mala fides, and costs de bonis propriis should not be ordered without the affected person being heard. The appeal was allowed only in part to substitute the costs order with each party bearing its own costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the Master complied with s 120 of the Administration of Estates Act before issuing consents to sell the three farms by private treaty.
    Whether the Master's duty to conduct due inquiry required him to hear or consider the views of the beneficiaries on the facts of this case.
    Whether the decision was lawful, reasonable and fair under the Administrative Justice Act.
    Whether the costs orders made by the High Court against the Master, executor and purchaser were justified.
legal_principles:
Section 120 of the Administration of Estates Act permits the Master, after due inquiry, to authorise an executor to sell estate property otherwise than by public auction if the Master is of the opinion that this would be to the advantage of persons interested in the estate and if the will contains no contrary provision.

    The Master must formulate his own opinion after due inquiry. The inquiry is not a judicial inquiry but a practical and financial inquiry into whether private treaty sale is advantageous compared with public auction and whether the sale serves the interests of beneficiaries.

    As an administrative authority, the Master must act lawfully, reasonably and fairly under s 3 of the Administrative Justice Act. Administrative action affecting rights or legitimate expectations may require adequate notice and a reasonable opportunity to make representations.

    The right to be heard is a basic tenet of law. The Master's obligation to hear beneficiaries depends on what fairness requires in the circumstances. Where the proposed sale is unusual or potentially prejudicial, such as selling three farms to settle a small liability when another asset may suffice, the Master must ascertain the beneficiaries' concerns before deciding.

    Costs should not ordinarily be awarded against an administrative body acting in its official capacity absent mala fides. Costs de bonis propriis should not be ordered unless sought and unless the affected person is given an opportunity to be heard.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the Master did not conduct the due inquiry required by s 120 of the Administration of Estates Act. He adopted too narrow an approach and improperly treated the decision to sell and the identification of assets for sale as matters exclusively for the executor.

    Held, further, that the circumstances required the Master to hear or at least seek the views of the beneficiaries. The sale of all three farms to settle a comparatively small estate liability, together with the existence of a butchery that could have been sold, raised issues requiring inquiry.

    Held, further, that the Master failed to act lawfully, reasonably and fairly under the Administrative Justice Act. The High Court correctly set aside the consents to sale, and the rights of the executor and purchaser fell with the impugned consents.

    Held, further, that the High Court's costs orders could not stand. There was no basis for costs against the Master absent mala fides, the executor was not heard before costs de bonis propriis were imposed, and the purchaser was a victim of circumstances. Each party was ordered to bear its own costs.
ratio_decidendi: The consents to sale were invalid because the Master failed to carry out the due inquiry required by s 120 and failed to act fairly and reasonably as an administrative authority. He did not independently assess whether selling three farms by private treaty to meet a small estate liability served the beneficiaries' interests, nor did he hear them despite circumstances requiring their input.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court emphasised that beneficiaries need not consent to a sale by private treaty, but the Master must be aware of their concerns before making a decision in circumstances where fairness demands that they be heard.

Madzingaidze N.O v Katanga Service Station HH 256/13
    Logan v Morris N.O. & Ors 1990 (2) ZLR 65 (S)
    Breen v Amalgamated Engineering Union (now Amalgamated Engineering and Foundry Workers Union) & Ors [1971] 2 QB 175; [1971] 1 All ER 1148 (CA)

Administration of Estates Act [Chapter 6:01], s 120
    Administrative Justice Act [Chapter 10:28], ss 2(1)(d), 3, 3(2), 4(1)

document_name: SC 102 - 22 SAMANTHA NHENGE vs ANDREW ZIGORA AND REGISTRAR OF DEEDS.docx
case_name:
SAMANTHA NHENDE
    v
    (1) ANDREW ZIGORA (2) REGISTRAR OF DEEDS N.O.
court:
'INTERIM RELIEF GRANTED
    Pending the return date of this provisional order, the second respondent is directed to place a caveat on the immovable property known as stand number 2 Glynde Avenue, Mabelreign, Harare held under deed of transfer number 2120/2017 registered in favour of the 1st respondent.'"
judges:
Panel: BHUNU JA, MATHONSI JA & CHATUKUTA JA
    Judgment by: MATHONSI JA
    Concurrence: BHUNU JA and CHATUKUTA JA agreed.
subject_area: Civil procedure; Urgent applications; Interdicts; Provisional orders; Family property disputes; Customary unions
catchwords_flynote: Urgent chamber application - interdict pendente lite - caveat over immovable property - unregistered customary union - tacit universal partnership alleged - prima facie right - choice of law - customary law and general law - High Court Rules, 2021 r 69(9) - provisional order procedure - court granting final order where interim relief sought - misdirection - appeal allowed in part.
headnote:
The appellant and first respondent had been in an unregistered customary union. After the union broke down, the first respondent instituted proceedings for division of property allegedly acquired during a tacit universal partnership, including a house registered solely in the appellant's name. Fearing that the appellant intended to sell the house before the main action was finalised, he filed an urgent chamber application seeking interim protection through a caveat and interdictory relief. The High Court held that the matter was urgent, found a reasonable apprehension that the house could be disposed of, and granted final relief pending finalisation of the main action.

    On appeal, the Supreme Court held that the first respondent had established a prima facie right open to some doubt. His declaration pleaded a tacit universal partnership and contributions to the property. The choice-of-law question did not defeat the urgent interim application; it could be dealt with in the main action, and an interdict pendente lite is not a matter resolved through customary law. The High Court was therefore correct to protect the status quo, but it erred by granting a final order in an urgent chamber application where interim relief had been sought. Under r 69(9) of the High Court Rules, 2021, where a prima facie case is established, the court grants a provisional order, either as drafted or varied. Final relief is not ordinarily triggered at the urgent interim stage, except in cases such as spoliation. The appeal succeeded in part. The High Court order was set aside and replaced with an amended provisional order directing the Registrar of Deeds to place a caveat over the property pending the return date.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the requirements for an interim interdict pendente lite were satisfied.
    Whether the High Court should have determined the choice-of-law issue at the urgent interim stage.
    Whether the High Court erred by granting a final order in an urgent chamber application where interim relief was sought.
legal_principles:
An interim interdict pendente lite requires a clear or prima facie right, though open to some doubt; a well-grounded apprehension of irreparable harm if interim relief is refused and the right is ultimately established; a balance of convenience favouring interim relief; and the absence of another satisfactory remedy.

    Under s 3(1) of the Customary Law and Local Courts Act, customary law applies where the parties agreed to its application or where it appears just and proper having regard to the nature and circumstances of the case. Where customary law is incapable of resolving the relevant procedural remedy or obligation, general law may apply.

    Rule 69(9) of the High Court Rules, 2021 provides that where the court is satisfied that an application for a provisional order establishes a prima facie case, it shall grant a provisional order either in terms of the draft filed or as varied. The purpose of provisional relief is to preserve the status quo pending the return date.

    A final order conclusively determines the dispute and leaves the issuing court functus officio. Except where final relief is legally appropriate, such as spoliatory relief, a court dealing with an urgent application for a provisional order should not grant final relief not sought at that stage.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the first respondent established a prima facie right open to some doubt by pleading a tacit universal partnership and contributions to the acquisition and improvement of the house. There was a well-grounded apprehension of irreparable harm if the property was disposed of before finalisation of the main action, and the balance of convenience favoured interim protection.

    Held, further, that the choice-of-law argument did not have to be resolved at the urgent interim stage. The causa before the court was an interdict pendente lite, and the appellant remained free to pursue the choice-of-law argument in the main action.

    Held, further, that the High Court misdirected itself by granting final relief in an urgent chamber application where interim relief was sought. The jurisdiction to grant final relief was not triggered.

    Held, further, that the appeal should succeed in part. The High Court judgment was set aside and replaced with a provisional order limited to interim placement of a caveat pending the return date.
ratio_decidendi: The first respondent had made out a case for interim protection of the disputed immovable property, but the High Court erred in converting an urgent application for provisional relief into final relief. The proper order was an interim caveat preserving the status quo pending the return date and determination of the main dispute.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that there appeared to be a failure of appreciation in the High Court that, when approached on an urgent basis for a provisional order, the court is ordinarily required to issue interim or provisional relief and not final relief, except in cases such as spoliation.

    Nzara & Ors v Kashumba N.O & Ors 2018 (1) ZLR 194 (S)

document_name: SC 105 - 22 Medson Mpofu vs NASSA.docx
SC 106 - 22 ROLEN TRADING (PVT) LTD v PARKSIDE HOLDIINGS (PVT) LTD CASE NO. SC 292.13.docx
case_name:
ROLEN TRADING (PRIVATE) LIMITED
    v
    PARKSIDE HOLDINGS (PRIVATE) LIMITED
judges:
Panel: MAKARAU JA, GOWORA JA & BERE JA
    Judgment by: GOWORA JA
    Concurrence: MAKARAU JA agreed.
    BERE JA was no longer in office.
subject_area: Contract; Lease; Civil procedure; Eviction; Damages; Appeal and cross-appeal
catchwords_flynote: Contract - lease agreement - breach by failure to pay rent on due date - mora ex re and mora ex persona - whether lessor required to give notice before cancellation - construction of disjunctive clause using 'or' - eviction following cancellation - alleged compromise not proved - arrear rentals paid by trial date - holding-over damages not proved - appeal allowed in part - cross-appeal dismissed.
headnote:
The appellant leased commercial premises from the respondent. It fell into arrears. The respondent's legal practitioners wrote a letter dated 26 September 2011 purporting to cancel the lease and demanding payment and vacant possession. The High Court held that the letter did not itself effectively cancel the lease because it gave three days instead of the fifteen days contemplated by the lease, but that summons issued after fifteen days validly enforced cancellation. It granted eviction, arrear rentals, holding-over damages, interest and costs. The appellant appealed against the whole judgment. The respondent cross-appealed against the finding that fifteen days' notice was required and against the finding that the letter did not cancel the lease.

    The Supreme Court held that the breach clause was disjunctive. The fifteen-day notice requirement applied to breaches other than non-payment of rent. Where rent was to be paid within the first seven days of each month, failure to pay by that date put the tenant in mora ex re and entitled the landlord to cancel without notice or demand. The lease was therefore validly cancelled and eviction was properly ordered. The alleged compromise was not proved because the draft deed of settlement was never signed and correspondence did not establish consensus. However, the monetary awards for arrear rentals and holding-over damages could not stand: arrear rentals had been cleared by trial, and no evidence established holding-over damages. The appeal succeeded in part; the eviction order stood; monetary claims were dismissed; the cross-appeal was dismissed; and the appellant was ordered to pay the respondent's costs of appeal.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the High Court should have ordered eviction of the appellant from the premises.
    Whether the High Court correctly made monetary awards for arrear rentals, holding-over damages, operating costs and interest.
    Whether the respondent was required to give fifteen days' notice before cancelling for non-payment of rent.
    Whether the parties reached a binding compromise.
    Whether the cross-appeal should succeed.
legal_principles:
Where parties have settled a contractual procedure for termination, they are bound by that procedure.

    The word 'or' is ordinarily disjunctive unless context provides a compelling reason to read it conjunctively.

    Where a time for performance is fixed in a contract, failure to perform by that time places the debtor in mora ex re. Where rent is payable by a specified date, failure to pay by that date may entitle the lessor to cancel without a further demand if the contract so permits.

    Where time for performance is not agreed, the debtor may be placed in mora ex persona by demand and notice making time of the essence.

    A compromise requires consensus on the terms of settlement. A draft deed of settlement that one party refuses to sign, without evidence of agreement on its terms, does not establish a binding compromise.

    A monetary claim for arrear rentals or holding-over damages must be supported by evidence; no award should stand where arrears were cleared and holding-over damages were not proved.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the breach clause in the lease distinguished failure to pay rent from other breaches. The fifteen-day notice requirement applied to general breaches and not to failure to pay rent by the agreed date.

    Held, further, that failure to pay rent within the stipulated period constituted mora ex re and the respondent was entitled to cancel the lease without giving notice to remedy the breach.

    Held, further, that no binding compromise agreement was reached because the appellant did not sign the deed of settlement and the correspondence did not establish consensus on the respondent's proposed terms.

    Held, further, that eviction was properly ordered because the lease had been validly cancelled.

    Held, further, that the awards for arrear rentals, operating costs, holding-over damages and interest were erroneous because arrears were no longer outstanding at trial and holding-over damages were not proved.

    Held, further, that the cross-appeal was dismissed and the appeal succeeded only in part.
ratio_decidendi: The lease fixed the date for payment of rent and treated non-payment separately from other breaches. The appellant's non-payment placed it in mora ex re, permitting cancellation without further notice. That justified eviction, but the monetary awards could not stand because the arrears had been cleared and holding-over damages had not been proved.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted and regretted the inordinate delay caused by the departure from the bench of the judge originally assigned to draft the judgment.

document_name: SC 108 - 22 ALPHONSE MUSHANAWANI  VS THE STATE.docx
case_name:
ALPHONSE MUSHANAWANI
    v
    THE STATE
judges:
Panel: MAVANGIRA JA, UCHENA JA & MAKONI JA
    Judgment by: MAKONI JA
    Concurrence: MAVANGIRA JA and UCHENA JA agreed.
subject_area: Criminal law; Criminal procedure; Evidence; Alibi; Dangerous drugs; Appellate interference
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law - unlawful dealing in dangerous drug - s 156(1)(c) of the Criminal Law Code - defence of alibi raised timeously - onus on State to disprove alibi beyond reasonable doubt - failure by police to investigate alibi - credibility findings and appellate interference - appeal against conviction and sentence allowed - co-accused's conviction quashed under s 25(2) of the Supreme Court Act.
headnote:
The appellant and a co-accused were convicted in the regional magistrates court of unlawful dealing in a dangerous drug after 710 kgs of dagga were recovered from a Toyota Hiace allegedly being driven by the appellant at Damafalls. The appellant raised an alibi, asserting that he had been arrested at his residence in Glen Norah while repairing another vehicle, and that the vehicle containing dagga had been hired out. The trial court rejected the alibi and convicted him. The High Court dismissed the appeal.

    The Supreme Court held that the alibi had been raised in good time, while investigations were not yet complete, and the State was obliged to investigate it. The police failed to investigate the appellant's claim, failed to locate the alleged hirers of the vehicle, failed to call relevant onlookers or the investigating officer, and relied heavily on a witness whose evidence was materially contradictory and unreliable. The trial court and High Court did not ask whether the alibi was false beyond reasonable doubt and instead treated the matter as a credibility contest. The Court held that the appellant had no onus to prove the alibi; if it might reasonably be true he had to be acquitted. The appeal succeeded. The appellant was acquitted, and the co-accused Brighton Dinda's conviction and sentence were quashed under the Court's review powers because his defence was the same.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the appellant was properly convicted in light of the alibi defence.
    Whether the State disproved the appellant's alibi beyond reasonable doubt.
    Whether the contradictions in the State evidence justified appellate interference with credibility findings.
    Whether sentence had to be considered if the conviction appeal succeeded.
    Whether the Supreme Court should exercise review powers in respect of the co-accused who did not appeal.
legal_principles:
An alibi is a defence that the accused was at a place different from the crime scene when the crime was committed.

    There is no onus on an accused to prove an alibi. If, on all the evidence, there is a reasonable possibility that the alibi evidence is true, the accused must be acquitted.

    An alibi must be considered in light of the totality of the evidence and the court's impressions of the witnesses. Where it is raised in time, the State should investigate it before trial and must disprove it beyond reasonable doubt.

    A court should not reduce an alibi case to a boxing-ring credibility contest between the complainant or State and the accused.

    An appellate court is slow to interfere with credibility findings, but may do so where the findings are plainly wrong or where inconsistencies go to the root of the matter.

    Under s 25(2) of the Supreme Court Act, the Court may use review powers to correct the conviction of a co-accused where the same defence and same defects affect that conviction.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the appellant's alibi was raised timeously, and the police were required to investigate it.

    Held, further, that the State failed to investigate the alibi, failed to call material witnesses, and relied on evidence that contained material contradictions about the place and circumstances of arrest.

    Held, further, that the trial court and High Court misdirected themselves by not asking whether the alibi was false beyond reasonable doubt and by effectively placing an onus on the appellant.

    Held, further, that the contradictions in Genius Ruzha's evidence and the police evidence went to the root of the conviction, justifying appellate interference.

    Held, further, that the appeal against conviction and sentence had merit and the sentence issue did not require separate determination.

    Held, further, that Brighton Dinda's conviction and sentence should also be quashed under s 25(2) of the Supreme Court Act because his defence was the same as the appellant's.
ratio_decidendi: The conviction could not stand because the State did not investigate or disprove a timeously raised alibi beyond reasonable doubt, and the evidence relied on to place the appellant at the scene was materially contradictory. The lower courts misdirected themselves by treating the alibi as a defence the appellant had to establish rather than a denial the State had to disprove.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court stated that review powers in favour of a co-accused can be invoked in circumstances where the appellant's defence and the co-accused's defence are the same.
cases_referred_to:
Matanga v The State S-17-15
    S v Morris S-29-98
    R v Hlongwane 1959 (3) SA 337 (AD)
    R v Biya 1952 (4) SA 514 (AD)

document_name: SC 109 - 22 Rita mbatha FINAL JUDGMENT.docx
case_name:
RITA MARQUE MBATHA
    v
    (1) VINCENT NCUBE (2) MESSENGER OF COURT, HARARE
judges:
Panel: GWAUNZA DCJ, MATHONSI JA & KUDYA JA
    Judgment by: GWAUNZA DCJ
    Concurrence: MATHONSI JA and KUDYA JA agreed.
subject_area: Civil procedure; Interdicts; Spoliation; Execution; Eviction; Court orders
catchwords_flynote: Civil procedure - appellant in default - r 53(3) of the Supreme Court Rules, 2018 - urgent chamber application for interdict restraining eviction - extant magistrates court eviction order - effect of spoliation order - spoliation not dispositive of substantive rights - interdict cannot restrain lawful process - appeal dismissed with costs.
headnote:
The appellant and first respondent had a long-running dispute over her occupation of a residential property leased from the first respondent. The first respondent obtained a default eviction order in the Magistrates' Court. The appellant's rescission application failed. She later obtained spoliatory relief after a prior attempt at eviction, then withdrew her review application against the magistrates court order on the view that the spoliation order had resolved the dispute. When the first respondent again instructed the Messenger of Court to evict her under the magistrates court order, she brought an urgent High Court application for an interdict restraining eviction. The High Court dismissed the application. The appellant appealed but defaulted at the appeal hearing; the Supreme Court proceeded under r 53(3) of the Supreme Court Rules, 2018.

    The Court held that the Magistrates' Court eviction order remained extant because it had never been set aside on appeal or review. The High Court spoliation order did not determine the parties' substantive rights; it merely restored possession pending lawful determination of rights. A spoliation order could not nullify or bar enforcement of an existing court order. An interdict is not available against lawful conduct, and the respondents were acting under a valid court order. The appellant had conflated spoliation and interdict requirements and failed to establish a prima facie right to the relief sought. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the Magistrates' Court eviction order remained extant and enforceable.
    Whether the spoliation order in HC 7310/18 set aside or barred enforcement of the eviction order.
    Whether an interdict could be granted to restrain respondents from executing a valid court order.
    Whether the appellant established the requirements for interdictory relief.
    Whether costs should be awarded against the appellant.
legal_principles:
An extant court order remains valid and enforceable until set aside on appeal or review.

    Spoliation proceedings restore peaceful and undisturbed possession and are not concerned with the legality of possession or the substantive rights of the parties. A spoliation order does not finally determine ownership, lease rights, or the validity of a separate eviction order.

    An interdict protects an existing prima facie right against unlawful conduct. It cannot be granted against past invasions of rights or lawful conduct, including execution of a valid court order.

    A threatened deprivation of possession may be addressed by a prohibitory interdict, but a mandament van spolie requires actual loss of possession.

    Costs ordinarily follow the cause, but the Court has discretion under r 15 of the Supreme Court Rules, 2018 to make a special order as to costs.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the order in MC 39520/16 remained extant because the appellant withdrew her review and did not set the order aside by appeal or review.

    Held, further, that the order in HC 7310/18 was a spoliation order and could not dispose of the parties' substantive rights or invalidate the magistrates court eviction order.

    Held, further, that the respondents' actions were anchored in a valid court order, and an interdict cannot be granted against lawful conduct.

    Held, further, that the appellant conflated spoliation and interdict requirements and failed to plead facts establishing a prima facie right to an interdict.

    Held, further, that the appeal had no merit and was dismissed with costs on the ordinary scale.
ratio_decidendi: Because the eviction order remained valid and enforceable, and because the spoliation order did not determine the parties' substantive rights or set aside that order, the respondents' execution process was lawful. An interdict could not issue against lawful execution, and the appellant failed to establish the prerequisites for interdictory relief.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted that as a self-actor, the appellant may have failed to appreciate the limited effect of spoliation proceedings.

document_name: SC 11 - 22 ZACC v MANGWIRO & ANOR.docx
SC 110 - 22 CONSTRUCTION RESOURCES AFRICA (PVT) LTD vs CENTRAL AFRICAN BUILDING CONSTRUCTION.docx
case_name:
CONSTRUCTION RESOURCES AFRICA (PRIVATE) LIMITED
    v
    CENTRAL AFRICAN BUILDING AND CONSTRUCTION COMPANY (PRIVATE) LIMITED t/a CENTRAL AFRICAN BUILDING CONSTRUCTION
judges:
Panel: BHUNU JA, MATHONSI JA AND KUDYA AJA
    Judgment by: KUDYA AJA
    Concurrence: BHUNU JA and MATHONSI JA agreed.
subject_area: Contract; Sale of land; Property; Rei vindicatio; Company law; Currency and monetary law; Civil procedure
catchwords_flynote: Contract - deed of sale of immovable properties - instalment sale of land - s 8 of the Contractual Penalties Act [Chapter 8:04] - adequacy of cancellation notice - whether exact amount owing required - foreign-currency denominated payment clause - Exchange Control Regulations - company authority to litigate - CR14 alleged to be invalid - unanimous assent and ratification - rei vindicatio - res litigiosa - appeal dismissed with attorney-and-client costs.
headnote:
The parties concluded interrelated agreements for the sale of movable assets and three immovable properties. The deed of sale for the immovable properties required payment of US$481 000 in instalments, with payment apportioned between United States dollars and Zimbabwe dollars. The appellant took possession but did not pay the purchase price in the agreed manner. The respondent gave thirty days' notice requiring rectification of the breach and later cancelled the deed of sale. It then sued for vindication and eviction. The appellant contended that the respondent was not properly before the court because the Vieiras were no longer directors, that a CR14 reflected new directors from the appellant's side, that the cancellation notice was defective because it did not state the exact amount owing, that the foreign-currency clause was unlawful, and that the respondent had not established rei vindicatio.

    The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal. It held that the alleged CR14 and minutes on which the appellant relied were invalid and could not confer directorship rights. The action was properly instituted at the instance of the Vieiras and was in any event ratified; unanimous assent principles applied. The Contractual Penalties Act required a notice to advise the purchaser of the breach and call for its rectification, but did not require the exact amount owing to be specified in every case. The notice sufficiently identified the breach, and the appellant's own conduct showed that it understood what was required. The deed of sale was validly cancelled. Agreements between incolas for payment in foreign currency in Zimbabwe were not unlawful; actual payment might require authority but the contractual promise was valid. The respondent was owner at litis contestatio, the appellant was in possession, and the appellant failed to prove a right of retention. The later alienation of the properties could not defeat vindicatory relief because they were res litigiosa. The appeal was dismissed with costs on the legal-practitioner-and-client scale.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the respondent was properly before the High Court and whether the proceedings were authorised or ratified.
    Whether the CR14 and alleged appointment of the Musukuma brothers affected the respondent's capacity or authority.
    Whether the notice to cancel under s 8 of the Contractual Penalties Act was sufficiently precise.
    Whether the deed of sale was validly cancelled.
    Whether the foreign-currency payment provisions were unlawful.
    Whether the respondent established the requirements of rei vindicatio despite later transfers of the properties.
    Whether costs on the higher scale were justified.
legal_principles:
A registered company litigates through an authorised natural person. Where authority is impugned, sufficient evidence must show that the litigation is being brought at the company's behest.

    A void CR14 cannot confer directorship rights. The unanimous assent principle may validate acts where all shareholders or members approve an intra vires act, and ratification may retrospectively validate previously unauthorised proceedings.

    Under s 8 of the Contractual Penalties Act, a seller under an instalment sale of land must give written notice advising the purchaser of the breach and calling upon the purchaser to remedy it within the stipulated period. The provision does not invariably require the exact monetary amount to be specified; adequacy depends on context and whether the breach is clearly identified.

    Cancellation takes effect when communicated. Where a valid notice has expired without rectification, a subsequent unequivocal cancellation is effective.

    A contract between incolas to pay in foreign currency in Zimbabwe is not per se unlawful; actual payment without authority may be unlawful, but the contractual obligation may be valid and enforceable subject to authority.

    For rei vindicatio, the plaintiff must prove ownership of a clearly identifiable asset and that the defendant was in possession at commencement of proceedings; the defendant then bears an evidential burden to prove a right of retention. Property that is subject to vindicatory proceedings becomes res litigiosa at litis contestatio, and later alienation cannot prejudice the plaintiff.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the respondent was properly before the High Court. The Vieiras' conduct established authority and unanimous assent, and later ratification also validated the proceedings.

    Held, further, that the purported minutes and CR14 relied on by the appellant were invalid and could not confer rights of directorship on the Musukuma brothers or defeat the respondent's claim.

    Held, further, that the notice of breach complied with s 8 of the Contractual Penalties Act because it adequately identified the breach and called for rectification; the exact amount owing did not have to be stated in the circumstances.

    Held, further, that the respondent cancelled the deed of sale ex nunc by letter of 12 December 2006 after the appellant failed to remedy the breach.

    Held, further, that the payment clause denominated in foreign currency was valid and did not fall foul of exchange-control law.

    Held, further, that the respondent established ownership and possession by the appellant at litis contestatio, and the appellant failed to prove a right to retain the properties.

    Held, further, that the later transfers could not defeat the action because the properties were res litigiosa.

    Held, further, that the appellant's conduct justified legal-practitioner-and-client costs.
ratio_decidendi: The appellant had not paid the immovable-property purchase price in the agreed currency apportionment and failed to remedy the breach after adequate statutory notice. The respondent validly cancelled the deed of sale, remained owner at litis contestatio, and was entitled to vindicate the properties from the appellant, whose asserted directorship and retention rights rested on invalid documents and unfulfilled contractual obligations.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: SC 111 - 22 RONALD MADHARA V FIDELITY PRINTERS & REFINERS.docx
SC 112 - 22 Benjamin  Rukanda and Ors v Registrar of SC and ors.docx
case_name:
(1) BENJAMIN RUKANDA (2) LUCIA MATIBENGA (3) PEOPLE’S DEMOCRATIC PARTY (Matibenga’s Breakaway Party)
    v
    (1) THE REGISTRAR OF THE SUPREME COURT (2) KUCACA IVUMILE PHULU (3) SETTLEMENT CHIKWINYA (4) WILLIAS MADZIMURE (5) REGAI TSUNGA (6) CHELESILE MAHLANGU (7) TENDAI LAXTON BITI (8) PEOPLE’S DEMOCRATIC PARTY (9) JACOB MUDENDA N.O (10) THE PARLIAMENT OF THE REPUBLIC OF ZIMBABWE (11) THE ZIMBABWE ELECTORAL COMMISSION
judges:
IN CHAMBERS
    Judgment by: UCHENA JA
subject_area: Practice and procedure; Supreme Court procedure; Registrar; Security for costs; Reinstatement of appeal
catchwords_flynote: Supreme Court procedure - r 13 of the Supreme Court Rules, 2018 - act, order or decision of Registrar - Registrar erroneously communicating that appeal deemed abandoned and dismissed - r 55(6) security for costs - security paid timeously - whether r 13 or r 70 procedure applicable - no condonation required - Registrar's communication set aside - appeal reinstated.
headnote:
The applicants had noted an appeal against a High Court decision. The parties appeared before the Registrar under r 55(2) of the Supreme Court Rules, 2018 for determination of security for respondents' costs. The Registrar fixed the security and the period for payment. Believing that the applicants had failed to pay timeously, the Registrar advised the parties that the appeal had been deemed abandoned and dismissed under r 55(6). The applicants then submitted proof that payment had in fact been made within the time set by the Registrar. The Registrar admitted the error but stated that she was functus officio and that the remedy was an application under r 13.

    The applicants applied under r 13 to set aside the Registrar's communication and reinstate the appeal. The second to eighth respondents argued that the application should have been brought under r 70 and that condonation was required. The Court dismissed the preliminary objection. It held that r 13 is a self-contained procedure for setting aside a Registrar's own act, order or decision not made at the direction of a judge. Rule 13 imposes no time limit and no condonation was required. Rule 55(6) is triggered only when an appellant actually fails to furnish security within the prescribed period. Since the applicants paid security on time, the deeming provision was not triggered. The Registrar's communication was erroneous and had to be set aside. The appeal was reinstated.
issues_for_determination:
Whether r 13 of the Supreme Court Rules, 2018 was the correct procedure for challenging the Registrar's erroneous communication.
    Whether the applicants were required to apply under r 70 or seek condonation.
    Whether r 55(6) had been triggered by failure to furnish security within time.
    Whether the Registrar's communication should be set aside and the appeal reinstated.
legal_principles:
Rule 55(6) deems an appeal abandoned and dismissed only if the appellant who is required to furnish security for costs fails to do so within the period specified under r 55(5). The operation of the rule is triggered by the occurrence of its specified facts, not by the Registrar's opinion.

    Rule 13 allows any person aggrieved by an act, order or decision of the Registrar, other than one made at the direction of a judge, to apply by chamber application to have it set aside.

    Rule 13 is a self-contained procedure for rectifying the Registrar's erroneous acts, decisions or orders. It does not prescribe a time limit and therefore does not require an application for condonation.

    Under r 13(4), a judge has wide discretion to make such order as he or she thinks fit, including reinstating an appeal where the Registrar erroneously regarded it as abandoned.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the application was correctly brought under r 13 because it challenged the Registrar's own erroneous communication and not a judicial direction.

    Held, further, that r 13 is self-contained and did not require the applicants to apply for condonation or proceed under r 70.

    Held, further, that r 55(6) had not been triggered because the applicants had in fact paid security within the period fixed by the Registrar.

    Held, further, that the Registrar's communication that the appeal was deemed abandoned and dismissed was erroneous and had to be set aside.

    Held, further, that the appeal was to be reinstated.
ratio_decidendi: Because the applicants paid security for costs within the period prescribed by the Registrar, the factual condition for abandonment under r 55(6) did not occur. The Registrar's contrary communication was her own erroneous act and was properly set aside under r 13, which empowered the judge to reinstate the appeal without requiring condonation.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that r 13 should be further simplified to achieve its objective of expeditiously and inexpensively rectifying Registrar errors, especially where the Registrar concedes the error.

document_name: SC 113 - 22 ERICA NDEWERE v JSC.docx
case_name:
ERICA NDEWERE
    v
    JUDICIAL SERVICE COMMISSION
judges:
Panel: GUVAVA JA, MATHONSI JA & CHATUKUTA JA
    Judgment by: GUVAVA JA
    Concurrence: MATHONSI JA and CHATUKUTA JA agreed.
subject_area: Appeal and review; Civil procedure; Interlocutory orders; Interim interdicts; Leave to appeal
catchwords_flynote: Civil procedure - appealability - interlocutory order of High Court - interim interdict placing motor vehicle under judicial attachment pending vindication proceedings - whether order final in effect - s 43(2)(d) of the High Court Act [Chapter 7:06] - leave to appeal required - appeal struck off the roll.
headnote:
The appellant, a former judge of the High Court, appealed against a High Court provisional order placing a Mercedes Benz motor vehicle under judicial attachment pending the return day of an urgent chamber application and pending related vindication proceedings. The respondent had sought preservation of the vehicle after demanding its return following the appellant's removal from judicial office.

    At the hearing, the Supreme Court requested submissions on whether the appellant was required to seek leave to appeal before noting the appeal. The appellant argued that the order, though interim in form, was final in effect because it deprived her of possession of the vehicle. The respondent argued that the order merely preserved the vehicle pending determination of the parties' rights in the vindication application.

    The Supreme Court held that the order was an interim interdict preserving the status quo. It did not award the motor vehicle to either party and did not finally determine the parties' rights. It therefore fell within s 43(2)(d) of the High Court Act, which requires leave to appeal from an interlocutory order. The Court distinguished Chiwenga v Mubaiwa SC 68/20 on the basis that the order in that matter was final and involved spoliatory relief. As the appellant had not sought leave to appeal, the matter was improperly before the Court and was struck off the roll, with each party bearing its own costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the High Court order placing the motor vehicle under judicial attachment was final in effect or interlocutory.
    Whether the appellant required leave to appeal in terms of s 43(2)(d) of the High Court Act.
    Whether the appeal was properly before the Supreme Court.
legal_principles:
An order that is final and definitive is appealable as of right even if cast in interim form.

    In determining whether an order is interlocutory, the court considers the terms and effect of the order. An order granted in aid of, and ancillary to, the main relief pending final determination of rights is interlocutory.

    The purpose of an interim interdict is to preserve or restore the status quo pending final determination of the parties' rights; it does not finally determine those rights.

    The grant of an interim interdict is interlocutory and requires leave to appeal under s 43(2)(d) of the High Court Act. Final interdicts, refusals of interim interdicts, and spoliatory orders may stand differently where they finally dispose of rights or preclude final relief.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the High Court's order did not finally determine ownership, possession, or entitlement to the motor vehicle. It preserved the vehicle pending the return day and determination of the vindication application.

    Held, further, that the order was an interim interdict falling within s 43(2)(d) of the High Court Act and the appellant was required to obtain leave to appeal.

    Held, further, that Chiwenga v Mubaiwa SC 68/20 was distinguishable because the order in that case was final in effect and was made in spoliation proceedings, whereas the present order was interim preservation.

    Held, further, that because leave to appeal had not been sought or granted, the appeal was improperly before the Supreme Court and had to be struck off the roll.
ratio_decidendi: The provisional order was an interim preservation order ancillary to pending vindication proceedings and did not finally decide the parties' rights in the motor vehicle. It was therefore interlocutory within s 43(2)(d) of the High Court Act, and the appellant's failure to obtain leave to appeal rendered the appeal incompetent.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court distinguished orders in spoliation proceedings, noting that a mandament van spolie is final and cannot be granted on an interim basis.

document_name: SC 114 - 22 OTILIA MASHOKO AND 5 ORS.docx
case_name:
OTILIA MASHOKO (in her capacity as the executrix dative in the estate of the late Albert Machengete Mashoko and Trustee of the Mashoko – Kusisa Family Trust) (2) THE MASHOKO-KUSISA FAMILY TRUST (3) TAFADZWA MASHOKO (4) MUNASHE MASHOKO (5) TATENDA MASHOKO (6) MEDELENE MASHOKO (a minor duly represented by her guardian Otilia Mashoko)
    v
    ALBERTINA HAZVINEYI MASHOKO (duly assisted by her guardian Barbara Maonde-Chikosi) (2) ALBERT TARIRO MASHOKO (3) SEEF PROPERTIES (in their capacity as trustee of the Mashoko-Kusisa Family Trust)
judges:
Panel: MAKONI JA, MATHONSI JA & CHATUKUTA JA
    Judgment by: MATHONSI JA
    Concurrence: MAKONI JA and CHATUKUTA JA agreed.
subject_area: Trusts; Civil procedure; Evidence; Property; Family property disputes
catchwords_flynote: Trusts - family trust - purported amendment or replacement of existing trust deed - effect of prior High Court order removing trustee and appointing estate agent - admissions in pleadings - approbation and reprobation - whether new trust validly constituted - accounting by trustee - eviction claim dependent on validity of trust - appeal partly allowed.
headnote:
The appeal arose from consolidated High Court proceedings concerning property held by the Mashoko-Chikosi Family Trust. The deceased settlor had established that trust for the benefit of two children of his second family. A High Court order later removed their mother as trustee and directed the appointment of an independent estate agent as trustee. Thereafter, the deceased and the estate agent executed a document purporting to amend the trust, changing its name to Mashoko-Kusisa Family Trust and adding four children from the deceased's first family as beneficiaries.

    The first and second respondents challenged the validity of the Mashoko-Kusisa Family Trust and sought accounts. The appellants claimed that the deceased had validly amended the original trust or created a new trust and sought eviction of the first respondent from the trust property. The High Court nullified the Mashoko-Kusisa Family Trust, ordered accounts from 8 May 2006, and dismissed the eviction claim.

    On appeal, the Supreme Court held that the appellants were bound by their pleadings, in which they had admitted that a new trust was established. They could not later re-characterise the transaction as a mere amendment. The Court held that the prior High Court order authorised only the removal of Barbara Maonde-Chikosi as trustee and the appointment of an independent estate agent; it did not authorise a change of name or addition of beneficiaries. Nor had the trust been terminated or amended in accordance with the original trust deed, which required unanimous trustee action and consultation with beneficiaries. The purported Mashoko-Kusisa Family Trust was therefore invalid. The Court however held that the first appellant could only be required to render accounts from the time when she assumed the position of trustee, not from 8 May 2006 when she was not yet in charge. The appeal succeeded only to that extent, with each party bearing its own costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the Mashoko-Kusisa Family Trust was properly constituted.
    Whether the first appellant was obliged to render accounts for periods before she became a trustee.
    Whether the first and second respondents should be ejected from the trust property.
legal_principles:
A litigant may not approbate and reprobate by taking inconsistent positions in the same proceedings.

    Admissions made in pleadings bind a party unless properly withdrawn with leave of the court. A party is not required to prove an admitted fact and the admitting party may not disprove it in the same proceedings.

    Where a court order directs a specific amendment to a trust deed, the parties must act within the authority conferred by that order. Powers to amend, rename, add beneficiaries to, or terminate a trust must be exercised in accordance with the trust deed.

    Where a trust deed requires unanimous trustee decisions and beneficiary consultation for termination or amendment, unilateral action by a settlor or trustee is invalid.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the appellants were bound by admissions in their pleadings that a new trust had been created and could not recast the transaction as a mere amendment of the original trust.

    Held, further, that the High Court order of 29 November 2005 authorised only the removal of Barbara Maonde-Chikosi as trustee and the appointment of an independent estate agent; it did not authorise the renaming of the trust or addition of beneficiaries.

    Held, further, that the deceased did not lawfully terminate the Mashoko-Chikosi Family Trust or validly amend it, because the requirements of the trust deed were not complied with.

    Held, further, that the first appellant could only render accounts for the period during which she was trustee and in charge of the trust affairs.

    Held, further, that because the purported Mashoko-Kusisa Family Trust was invalid, the appellants had no right on that basis to evict the first and second respondents from the property.
ratio_decidendi: The purported creation or amendment of the Mashoko-Kusisa Family Trust was invalid because it exceeded the prior High Court order and did not comply with the amendment or termination provisions of the existing trust deed. The only error in the High Court order was the requirement that the first appellant account from 8 May 2006, before she assumed the trusteeship.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

    S v Marutsi 1990 (2) ZLR 370
    Alliance Insurance v Imperial Plastics (Pvt) Ltd & Anor SC 30/17

document_name: SC 115 - 22 MOTA ENGENHARI CONSTRUCTION SA VS ZIMBABWE REVENUE AUTHORITY.docx
case_name:
MOTA ENGENHARI CONSTRUCTION SA
    v
    ZIMBABWE REVENUE AUTHORITY
judges:
Panel: GWAUNZA DCJ, MUSAKWA JA & MWAYERA JA
    Judgment by: MWAYERA JA
    Concurrence: GWAUNZA DCJ and MUSAKWA JA agreed.
subject_area: Tax and revenue; Statutory interpretation; Non-resident tax on fees; Corporate branches
catchwords_flynote: Tax and revenue - non-residents tax on fees - s 30 of the Income Tax Act [Chapter 23:06] and Seventeenth Schedule - foreign company operating in Zimbabwe through local branch - intra-company fees paid by local branch to non-resident head office - whether local branch obliged to withhold NRTF - penalty - appeal dismissed.
headnote:
The appellant was a Portuguese company operating in Zimbabwe through a registered local branch. The branch entered intra-company arrangements with the appellant's Portuguese head office under which the head office provided technical, managerial and administrative services for fees. The local branch claimed the fees as deductible expenses but did not withhold Non-Resident Tax on Fees.

    The respondent assessed the appellant for NRTF and imposed a penalty. The Special Court for Income Tax Appeals upheld the Commissioner's determination. On appeal, the appellant contended that its branch was not a separate legal person and could not be treated as a payer liable to withhold tax on fees payable to its head office. The respondent contended that the local branch was ordinarily resident in Zimbabwe for tax purposes and fell within the statutory definition of payer.

    The Supreme Court held that, on the ordinary and contextual interpretation of s 30 and the Seventeenth Schedule to the Income Tax Act, the local branch was the payer of fees from a Zimbabwean source to a non-resident person. It was therefore obliged to withhold NRTF. The Court further held that the Commissioner's penalty was an exercise of discretion with no demonstrated basis for appellate interference. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the appellant's local branch was liable to withhold Non-Resident Tax on Fees in terms of s 30 of the Income Tax Act as read with the Seventeenth Schedule.
    Whether the court a quo erred in upholding the 20 per cent penalty.
legal_principles:
Statutory interpretation begins with the ordinary grammatical meaning of the words used, read in context and consistently with the statute's purpose, unless that meaning would lead to absurdity, repugnancy, or inconsistency.

    Section 30 of the Income Tax Act imposes non-residents tax on fees in accordance with the Seventeenth Schedule. Under that Schedule, fees are deemed to be from a source within Zimbabwe if the payer is ordinarily resident in Zimbabwe; a payer of fees to a non-resident person must withhold the tax.

    A branch of a foreign company located in Zimbabwe is treated as a resident taxpayer for tax purposes and is liable to withholding tax on outward-bound fees paid to a non-resident, including its head office.

    An appellate court will not interfere with a discretionary penalty absent a showing of misdirection or unreasonableness.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the local branch was ordinarily resident in Zimbabwe and was the payer of fees to the appellant, a non-resident person.

    Held, further, that the fees paid by the branch to the head office were fees from a Zimbabwean source within the meaning of the Seventeenth Schedule to the Income Tax Act.

    Held, further, that the branch was obliged to withhold Non-Resident Tax on Fees and the failure to do so contravened s 30 as read with the Seventeenth Schedule.

    Held, further, that no basis had been shown to interfere with the Commissioner's penalty, which was upheld by the Special Court.

    Held, further, that the appeal lacked merit and had to be dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The local Zimbabwean branch was, for tax purposes, a resident payer of fees to a non-resident head office. The statutory language of s 30 and the Seventeenth Schedule required withholding of Non-Resident Tax on those fees; the contrary argument based on the branch not being a separate legal person failed in the tax context.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

Chegutu Municipality v Manyora 1996 (1) ZLR 262 (S)
    Grey vs Pearson (1857) 10 ER 1216
    Innscor Africa Limited & Anor vs Competition and Tarrif Commission SC 52/18
