document_name: SC 116 - 22 Movement for Democratic Change and 2 Others v Tendai Muchekahanzu and 7 Others.docx
SC 117 - 22 Bobby Dube and Others v TM Supermarkets(10).docx
case_name:
BOBBY DUBE (2) KHULEKANI MOYO (3) VINCENT SITHOLE (4) SIPHAKAMILE MPOFU (5) LUKE DUBE (6) THAPELO SEBETA (7) CUTHBERT MARADZA (8) EDITH DUBE (9) FAITH MAGARIRA
    v
    TM SUPERMARKETS (PRIVATE) LIMITED
judges:
In chambers: MATHONSI JA
    Judgment by: MATHONSI JA
subject_area: Practice and procedure; Supreme Court Rules; Reinstatement of appeals; Jurisdiction of single judge
catchwords_flynote: Practice and procedure - chamber application - reinstatement of appeal - appeal dismissed by full Court for want of prosecution - r 70 of the Supreme Court Rules, 2018 - Registrar notification prerequisite - whether single judge in chambers can reinstate appeal dismissed by full Court - application incompetent - struck off by consent.
headnote:
The applicants' appeal against a Labour Court judgment had been set down before the Supreme Court. When their counsel did not attend, the matter was rolled over to the following day. There was again no appearance for the applicants, and the full Court dismissed the appeal for want of prosecution with costs.

    The applicants then brought a chamber application purporting to seek reinstatement of the dismissed appeal under r 70(2) of the Supreme Court Rules, 2018. At the hearing, the judge asked counsel to address whether r 70(2) applied where no notification had been issued by the Registrar under r 70(1), and whether a single judge in chambers had power to set aside a full Court order.

    The Supreme Court held that r 70(2) is triggered by a Registrar's notification that an appeal has lapsed, been abandoned, or been deemed dismissed under the Rules. It does not apply to an appeal actually dismissed by the full Court for want of prosecution. The Court further held that, absent express statutory authority, a single judge sitting in chambers cannot reinstate a civil appeal dismissed by the full Court. Counsel properly conceded the incompetence of the application. The application was struck off the roll with no order as to costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether r 70(2) of the Supreme Court Rules could be used to reinstate an appeal dismissed by the full Court for want of prosecution.
    Whether a notification by the Registrar under r 70(1) was a prerequisite to an application under r 70(2).
    Whether a single judge sitting in chambers had jurisdiction to reinstate an appeal dismissed by the full Court.
legal_principles:
Rule 70(2) is only available after the Registrar has issued a notification under r 70(1) that an appeal is deemed to have lapsed, regarded as abandoned, or deemed dismissed under the Rules.

    An appeal actually dismissed by the full Court for want of prosecution is not a deemed dismissal under r 70.

    A single judge of the Supreme Court sitting in chambers has only the powers conferred by statute or the Rules and cannot set aside or reinstate an appeal dismissed by the full Court unless expressly authorised.
held_decision_points:
Held, that no Registrar's notification under r 70(1) had been issued and the applicants could not invoke r 70(2).

    Held, further, that the appeal had been dismissed by the full Court for want of prosecution and was not deemed dismissed under the Rules.

    Held, further, that a single judge sitting in chambers had no jurisdictional basis to reinstate a civil appeal dismissed by the full Court.

    Held, further, that the application was incompetent and had to be struck off the roll.
ratio_decidendi: Rule 70(2) did not apply because the applicants had no Registrar notification under r 70(1) and their appeal had been dismissed by the full Court, not deemed dismissed under the Rules. A single judge lacked authority to overturn the full Court's dismissal.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The judgment compared the 2018 Rules with the former Supreme Court Rules, 1964, noting that the old proviso to r 25(6) expressly allowed a judge to reinstate an appeal dismissed for want of prosecution, whereas the current procedural route invoked by the applicants did not.

document_name: SC 118 - 22 Cassandra and Anor v Adlecraft(9).doc
case_name:
(1) CASSANDRA MYBURGH (2) COLIN BAKER
    v
    ADLECRAFT INVESTMENTS (PRIVATE) LIMITED
judges:
Panel: MAVANGIRA JA, BHUNU JA & CHIWESHE JA
    Judgment by: MAVANGIRA JA
    Concurrence: BHUNU JA and CHIWESHE JA agreed.
subject_area: Property; Spoliation; Company law; Corporate governance
catchwords_flynote: Spoliation - company premises - employees preventing acting managing director from accessing premises and assets - whether company despoiled through interference with authorised agent - board resolution appointing acting managing director - s 196(1) of the Companies and Other Business Entities Act [Chapter 24:31] - appeal dismissed.
headnote:
The respondent company obtained spoliatory relief in the High Court after its employees, the appellants, resisted the authority of an acting managing director appointed by board resolution and prevented him from accessing the company's premises and assets. The appellants challenged the order, arguing that the resolution appointing the acting managing director was invalid and that there was no proof that the company had been dispossessed because work continued at the premises.

    The Supreme Court held that the resolution appointing the acting managing director was valid. It had been circulated and signed by the two available directors, constituting a majority in circumstances where the third director did not respond. The acting managing director was not litigating in his own name; he was the company's authorised agent and deponent.

    The Court held that the relevant possession was the company's possession. By preventing the duly authorised acting managing director from accessing the company's premises and exercising day-to-day control, the appellants unlawfully denied access to the company itself. The requirements for mandament van spolie were met. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the board resolution authorising Munyaradzi Gonyora to act as managing director was valid.
    Whether the High Court erred in granting a spoliation order in favour of the respondent company.
legal_principles:
A company acts through its directors collectively and through properly authorised agents.

    Section 196(1) of the Companies and Other Business Entities Act allows a board decision that could be voted on at a board meeting to be adopted by written consent signed by the directors entitled to vote; a valid written decision has the same effect as a board vote.

    For mandament van spolie, the applicant must establish peaceful and undisturbed possession and wrongful or forcible deprivation of that possession against its consent.

    An agent or representative who controls property for a principal acts for the principal; where the principal is dispossessed through interference with the agent's authorised control, the principal may seek spoliatory relief.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the resolution appointing Gonyora as acting managing director was validly passed by a majority of the available directors and complied with s 196(1) of the Companies and Other Business Entities Act.

    Held, further, that Gonyora was the respondent company's authorised agent and not the applicant in his personal capacity.

    Held, further, that the appellants' refusal to recognise Gonyora and their obstruction of his access to the company's premises and assets deprived the respondent company of possession.

    Held, further, that the High Court correctly granted spoliatory relief and the appeal lacked merit.
ratio_decidendi: Because Gonyora had been validly authorised to manage the respondent's day-to-day affairs, the appellants' obstruction of his access amounted to wrongful dispossession of the respondent company through interference with its authorised agent. The respondent therefore satisfied the requirements for spoliatory relief.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: SC 119 - 22 PHIBION GWATIDZO N.O vs WILLDALE LIMITED AND 2 OTHERS.docx
case_name:
PHIBION GWATIDZO N.O
    v
    WILLDALE LIMITED (2) TERRIERS – LAXTOP BANK (3) MASTER OF THE HIGH COURT
judges:
Panel: UCHENA JA, KUDYA JA & MWAYERA JA
    Judgment by: MWAYERA JA
    Concurrence: UCHENA JA and KUDYA JA agreed.
subject_area: Insolvency and liquidation; Company law; Civil procedure; Costs
catchwords_flynote: Insolvency and liquidation - removal of liquidator - unauthorised sale of company assets - duties of liquidator - ss 49(4) and 80 of the Insolvency Act [Chapter 6:07] - reliance on repealed Act not fatal where material facts pleaded - appellate interference with discretion - punitive costs - appeal dismissed.
headnote:
The appellant, a liquidator, appealed against a High Court order removing him as liquidator of Willdale Transport Services (Private) Limited and setting aside the purported sale of company assets. The company was in final liquidation, and the first respondent, a shareholder, alleged that the appellant had sold company assets without authorisation from the Master or creditors, had not disclosed the sale price or purchasers, and had attempted to procure later ratification.

    The Supreme Court held that the applicable law was the Insolvency Act [Chapter 6:07], notwithstanding references below to the repealed Insolvency Act. The first respondent's cause of action had been properly pleaded because the material facts, namely disposal of assets without authority and lack of transparency, were pleaded. The Court held that a liquidator may sell estate assets only in accordance with the Act and subject to the directions of the Master or creditors. The appellant's unauthorised and opaque sale was contrary to his duties and justified removal. No basis was shown for appellate interference with the High Court's discretion or punitive costs order. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the High Court erred in ordering the removal of the appellant from office as liquidator.
    Whether the High Court erred in granting costs on a higher scale against the appellant in his personal capacity.
legal_principles:
Section 80 of the Insolvency Act [Chapter 6:07] empowers the court, on application by the Master or an interested party, to remove a liquidator if it is in the interests of proper administration of an insolvent estate.

    Under s 49(4) of the Insolvency Act, a liquidator may sell or alienate property of the insolvent estate only if authorised by the Master or by resolution of creditors and subject to the prescribed directions.

    The removal of a liquidator is a drastic remedy, but it is justified where the liquidator fails to act transparently, fails to account properly, sells assets without lawful authority, or fails to act in the interests of the company and stakeholders.

    An appellate court will not readily interfere with a lower court's exercise of discretion unless the decision was not based on reasonable grounds or was based on a wrong principle.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the appellant sold assets of Willdale Transport without authorisation, contrary to s 49(4) of the Insolvency Act.

    Held, further, that the appellant's later attempt to obtain ratification did not sanitise the unauthorised sale.

    Held, further, that reliance by the parties or court a quo on a repealed Act did not vitiate the decision because the material facts giving rise to removal were pleaded and the applicable current law supported the outcome.

    Held, further, that the High Court properly exercised its discretion in removing the appellant as liquidator and in awarding punitive costs.

    Held, further, that the appeal was devoid of merit and had to be dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The appellant's unauthorised and non-transparent sale of company assets breached the statutory and fiduciary duties of a liquidator. Those facts justified removal under s 80 of the applicable Insolvency Act [Chapter 6:07], and no reviewable or appealable misdirection was shown.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: SC 12 - 22 SIMON CHINGANGA vs MUNASHE SHAVA & 2 ORS.docx
SC 120 - 22 Suwisai Marume v Chinhoyi University of Technology.docx
case_name:
SUWISAI MARUME
    v
    CHINHOYI UNIVERSITY OF TECHNOLOGY UNIVERSITY
judges:
Panel: BHUNU JA, CHIWESHE JA & CHATUKUTA JA
    Judgment by: CHATUKUTA JA
    Concurrence: BHUNU JA and CHIWESHE JA agreed.
subject_area: Labour and employment law; Disciplinary proceedings; Review; Statutory interpretation
catchwords_flynote: Labour law - dismissal - university employee - absence of registered employment code - application of National Employment Code of Conduct - distinction between disciplinary authority and disciplinary committee - Chinhoyi University of Technology Act s 26 - Labour Act s 12B and s 2A - Labour Act prevailing over inconsistent enactments - appeal dismissed.
headnote:
The appellant, a secretary employed by the respondent university, was dismissed after disciplinary proceedings conducted under the Labour (National Employment Code of Conduct) Regulations, SI 15/2006. She applied to the Labour Court for review, arguing that the disciplinary authority was unlawfully constituted because it did not contain the four members contemplated by s 26 of the Chinhoyi University of Technology Act. The Labour Court dismissed the review, holding that the panel was quorate and that dismissal was rational.

    On appeal, both parties accepted that the respondent had no registered employment code and that the CUT Act was not a registered employment code. The Supreme Court held that s 12B of the Labour Act required use of the model code in the absence of a registered code. Under the National Code, a disciplinary authority is distinct from a disciplinary committee and its composition is left to the employer's discretion. The disciplinary proceedings had been initiated before a disciplinary authority under the National Code, not a staff disciplinary committee under the CUT Act. The Labour Act prevailed over any inconsistent enactment in labour matters by virtue of s 2A.

    The Court held that the respondent properly terminated the appellant's employment under the National Code. Counsel abandoned the penalty ground, making it unnecessary to decide the rationality issue. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the disciplinary committee or authority was legally constituted.
    Whether rationality could be a ground for review in the circumstances.
legal_principles:
Where an employer has no registered employment code, s 12B(2)(b) of the Labour Act requires compliance with the model code made under s 101(9), namely the National Employment Code of Conduct.

    Section 2A of the Labour Act provides that the Labour Act prevails over any other enactment inconsistent with it in labour matters.

    The National Code distinguishes a disciplinary authority from a disciplinary committee. The composition of a disciplinary authority is not fixed by the Code and is left to the employer's discretion.

    A statute establishing an institution, such as the CUT Act, is not a registered employment code merely because it contains a provision about a staff disciplinary committee.
held_decision_points:
Held, that because there was no registered employment code and the CUT Act was not a registered employment code, the respondent was required to proceed under the National Employment Code of Conduct.

    Held, further, that the proceedings were before a disciplinary authority, not a disciplinary committee under s 26 of the CUT Act.

    Held, further, that the disciplinary authority was properly constituted under the National Code and the Labour Court erred in holding that the CUT Act prevailed over the Labour Act.

    Held, further, that the appellant abandoned the penalty issue, and the rationality issue was academic.

    Held, further, that the appeal lacked merit and had to be dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The respondent had no registered code, and the CUT Act was not such a code. The National Code therefore governed the disciplinary proceedings, and its concept of a disciplinary authority did not require the four-member composition in s 26 of the CUT Act. The disciplinary authority was consequently validly constituted.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: SC 121 - 22 Rita Marque Mbatha vs Vincent Ncube and Anor(1).docx
case_name:
RITA MARQUE MBATHA
    v
    VINCENT NCUBE
    MESSENGER OF COURT HARARE
judges:
Panel: GWAUNZA DCJ, MATHONSI JA & KUDYA JA
    Judgment by: MATHONSI JA
    Concurrence: GWAUNZA DCJ and KUDYA JA agreed.
subject_area: Practice and procedure; Supreme Court Rules; Rescission; Finality of judgments; Functus officio
catchwords_flynote: Practice and procedure - rescission of Supreme Court judgment - appeal determined on merits in appellant's absence under r 53(3) of the Supreme Court Rules, 2018 - distinction between dismissal in default and determination on merits - finality of Supreme Court decisions in non-constitutional matters - functus officio - application dismissed.
headnote:
The applicant sought rescission of a Supreme Court judgment dismissing her appeal in SC 443/21. In that appeal, she had challenged a High Court decision refusing to interdict her eviction from rented premises. She did not appear at the appeal hearing. The Court, exercising its discretion under r 53(3) of the Supreme Court Rules, proceeded to determine the appeal on the merits and dismissed it with costs. Reasons were later provided in SC 109/22.

    The applicant then applied for rescission under r 73 of the Supreme Court Rules as read with r 29(a) of the High Court Rules, 2021. The issue was whether rescission lay against a Supreme Court judgment that had been determined on the merits in a non-constitutional matter.

    The Supreme Court held that r 53(3) gives the Court a discretion either to determine or dismiss an appeal when the appellant does not appear. Where the Court determines the appeal on the merits, the resulting judgment has final juridical consequences. In non-constitutional matters, a Supreme Court decision rendered on the merits is final; the Court becomes functus officio and cannot rescind its own final judgment. The Court distinguished Zvinavashe v Ndlovu because the decision in issue had been determined under r 53(3) on the merits. The application was dismissed as groundless and bad in law.
issues_for_determination:
Whether it was competent for the applicant to seek rescission of a Supreme Court judgment rendered on the merits under r 53(3).
    Whether such a judgment was a default judgment susceptible to rescission.
    Whether the Supreme Court was functus officio after determining the appeal on the merits.
legal_principles:
Rule 53(3) of the Supreme Court Rules gives the Court discretion, where an appellant fails to appear or file heads, either to determine the appeal or to dismiss it and make an appropriate order as to costs.

    A determination of an appeal on the merits is distinct from a default dismissal. It produces final juridical consequences.

    Once a court has pronounced a final judgment or order, it becomes functus officio and ordinarily cannot correct, alter, supplement or rescind it.

    A decision of the Supreme Court on a non-constitutional matter is final and binding. It is not ordinarily susceptible to rescission by the Supreme Court itself after the Court has exercised its appellate jurisdiction on the merits.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the earlier appeal was determined on the merits under r 53(3), not merely dismissed in default.

    Held, further, that the Supreme Court became functus officio after rendering its judgment on the merits.

    Held, further, that a final Supreme Court judgment in a non-constitutional matter cannot be rescinded in the manner sought by the applicant.

    Held, further, that Zvinavashe v Ndlovu was distinguishable because the present judgment was rendered under r 53(3) after a merits determination.

    Held, further, that the application was without merit, wrong and bad in law and had to be dismissed.
ratio_decidendi: The Supreme Court had exercised its r 53(3) discretion to determine the earlier appeal on the merits. That determination was final in a non-constitutional matter and rendered the Court functus officio, leaving no competent rescission remedy.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court stated that it is not a reservoir for litigants unwilling to have matters finalised and that where abuse is apparent the Court will invoke r 53(3) to determine appeals on the merits to liberate parties from unending litigation.

document_name: SC 14 - 22 CRISPEN VUNDLA & ANOR v INNSCOR AFRICA BREAD &ANOR.docx
SC 15 - 22 UNKI MINE DRAFT JUDGMENT FINAL.docx
case_name:
UNKI MINE (PRIVATE) LIMITED
    v
    ZIMBABWE REVENUE AUTHORITY
judges:
Panel: GUVAVA JA, UCHENA JA & MAKONI JA
    Judgment by: GUVAVA JA
    Concurrence: UCHENA JA and MAKONI JA agreed.
subject_area: Tax and revenue; Company law; Statutory interpretation
catchwords_flynote: Tax and revenue – income tax – deduction of expenditure – payment by subsidiary to community share ownership trust under group indigenisation plan – legal obligation resting on foreign holding company – separate corporate and taxable personality – subsidiary unable to deduct expense incurred on behalf of holding company – capital or revenue character unnecessary to determine – appeal dismissed with costs.
headnote:
“a. Whether the expenditure (payment of US$10 million to the Tongogara Rural District Community Share Ownership Scheme made in 2011) claimed as a deduction is allowable as such.”

      On 8 January 2020, the court a quo in determining the appeal found that the donation of US$ 10 million was not designed to establish, improve, or add to the income earning capacity nor did it generate any income for the appellant hence it was not of a revenue nature but capital. The court a quo agreed with the respondent that the disbursed US$10 million was designed to preserve the appellant’s income-earning structure hence it was expenditure of a capital nature and not of a revenue nature.
issues_for_determination:
“1. The learned Judge erred at law in finding, in the main, that the payment of US$ 10 million to a Community Share Ownership Trust was a donation to such trust. He ought to have found that the payment was made to satisfy Appellant’s legal obligation to comply with Zimbabwe’s indigenous laws.

    2.  The learned Judge erred at law in making the following incidental/ancillary findings against Appellant:-

      (a) The Appellant was not obliged to comply with indigenisation despite it being a business as defined and obliged to be indigenous compliant in terms of the law.

      (b) Having earlier found that the Appellant’s parent company had submitted an indigenisation plan for itself and its subsidiaries (including Appellant) later contradicted himself in finding that:-

      “Appellant could not rely on an indigenisation implementation plan that did not relate to it to claim the deduction in question.”

    3.  The learned Judge erred at law in finding that the payment of US$ 10 million to the Trust was expenditure of a capital and not of a revenue nature.

      The appellant’s grounds of appeal in my view raise the following issues for determination:

    1.  Whether or not the appellant was obliged at law to donate the sum of US$10 million to the Community Share Ownership Trust?

    2.  Whether or not the court a quo erred in finding that the payment of US$10 million to the Trust was expenditure of a capital and not of a revenue nature?”

    SUBMISSIONS BEFORE THIS COURT

    16. Counsel for the appellant, Mr. Matinenga, submitted that the US$ 10 million donated by the appellant to the Trust was not a donation in the strict sense considering the context in which the payment was made. He insisted that the appellant was obligated to make the donation in order to be compliant with the requirement for indigenisation in accordance with the indigenisation laws of Zimbabwe.

      Counsel further submitted that the payment was a deductible expenditure of a revenue nature because it was for licensing purposes in order for the appellant to trade. He contended that, had the appellant not made the donation, its mining license would have been revoked. He thus urged the Court to allow the appeal.

    17. Per contra, counsel for the respondent, Mr Magwaliba, submitted that the donation was a donation proper which is not deductible in terms of part 4 (a) of the Twenty-Second Schedule of the Income Tax Act. He contended that the donation did not benefit the appellant but was designed to benefit Amhold, its parent company, a 3rd party. In any event it was his contention that there was no legal obligation on the appellant to make the donation or to be compliant with the indigenisation laws of Zimbabwe.

      Counsel explained that no consequence would have befallen the appellant had it not made the donation and even if there would have been any such consequence, it would have befallen the parent company, Amhold, which had the obligation to indigenise and not the appellant. He also submitted that the amendment to the Income Tax Act allowing deductions of payments made to Community Share Ownership Trusts was only made on 1 January 2013 hence the appellant could not claim a deduction in its tax assessment report compiled in 2011 in relation to the donation as the amendment did not apply retrospectively.

    ANALYSIS

    Whether or not the appellant was obliged at law to donate the sum of US$10 million to the Community Share Ownership Trust?

    18. The appellant’s contention is that the payment of US$10 million to the Trust was made in compliance with the indigenisation laws of Zimbabwe to enable it to continue operating its special lease mining operations. Further, that the payment was for purposes of trade or in production of income and not of a capital nature. On the other hand the respondent maintained that the payment of US$10 million by the appellant was strictly a donation as there was no legal obligation on it to make the donation. Further, that the legal obligation to be compliant with the indigenisation laws was on the parent company Amhold and not the appellant. It was also submitted, for the respondent, that the payment could only have been of a capital nature as it was not paid for the purpose of trade or production of income.

    19. The resolution of this appeal, in my view, revolves upon the determination of whether or not the appellant had a legal obligation to make the payment of US$ 10 million. In the event that it did, then the court must then determine whether the payment was of capital or revenue in nature.

    20. It should be borne in mind, from the onset, that the relationship between the GOZ, Amzim and its subsidiary companies has always been regulated by different laws and agreements.

      The Heads of Agreement were signed between the GOZ, Anglo American Platinum Limited, Amzim Holdings Limited, Southridge Limited and the appellant. Clause 1.4 of the agreement provides for Amzim Holdings Limited which is a member of the Anglo American Group (‘Amhold’). The appellant and Southridge Limited are in turn members of the Anglo American Group. Clause 2.15 of the agreement further defines “Amhold Group” means “Amhold and all its direct and indirect subsidiaries including Unki Mine ...” Clause 4.2 of the agreement provides for the nature of the indigenisation transaction as follows:

      “… 51% equity ownership of Amhold shall be issued to the indigenous entities, utilising a notional vendor funded mechanism to be facilitated by Amhold. The implementation of the Indigenisation Transaction will result in the Amhold Group being in compliance with the indigenisation Requirements for the duration of the Indigenisation Compliance Period.”

      Clause 9.2.1 of the agreement is also worth quoting as it provides as follows:

      “… following the implementation of the Indigenisation Implementation Plan through the Indigenisation Transaction, each member of the Amhold Group shall qualify as, and shall for the duration of the Indigenious Compliance Period continue to qualify as, an Indigenous Entity in compliance with the Indigenous Act…” (emphasis added)

      An Indigenous Entity is further defined in the agreement under clause 2.1.49 as follows:

      “means a company in which issued shares are owned directly or indirectly by Indigenous Zimbabweans in the percentage proportion no less than that prescribed from time to time under the Indigenisation Act as the ‘minimum indigenisation and empowerment quota’...”

      Lastly clause 3.4 of the agreement provides for the indigenisation implementation plan which was submitted by Amhold and approved by the Minister of Youth Development, Indigenisation and Empowerment in accordance with the Indigenisation and Economic Empowerment Act. The collective effect of the provisions of the Heads of Agreement show that Amhold is the parent company which has subsidiary companies being the appellant and Southridge Limited. Further it can be deduced that it is the parent company which submitted an indigenisation implementation plan which was approved by the Minister. It should also be noted that it was an agreed provision between the parties to the agreement that the parent company was obligated to issue 51 per cent of its shares to indigenous entities in compliance with the Indigenisation and Economic Empowerment Act. The Heads of Agreement further make it clear that during the implementation of the indigenisation plan the subsidiary companies of Amhold were to be treated as indigenous companies owned by indigenous Zimbabweans.

    21. It is thus apparent that the indigenisation laws and agreements placed an obligation on Amhold to issue 51 per cent of its shares in order to comply with the indigenisation laws of Zimbabwe. It was the parent company that was given that legal mandate as it is a foreign entity and not its subsidiary companies. It thus follows that the Heads of Agreement and indigenous implementation plan were made for and on behalf of Amhold so that it is able to fulfil its legal obligations, as a foreign company. Having arrived at this conclusion, the Court must determine whether or not the appellant, as a subsidiary of Amhold, had a legal obligation to make the payment of US$10 million.

      Clause 5.3 of the Heads of agreement provide as follows;

      “5.3 It is hereby recorded that as part of the indigenisation implementation plan, Amhold has donated US$ 10 000 000.00 to the Community Trust for purposes, inter alia funding community projects”.

      The Heads of Agreement show that the US$10 million was donated by Amhold, the holding company of the appellant to the Trust for purposes of funding community projects. It is quite apparent from the record that Amhold is a separate company from its subsidiary companies. Consequently, the appellant could not claim a deduction, based on the donation, because it was not incurred by it but by the holding company. It is trite that a holding company is a separate legal entity for income tax purposes. (See GC (Pvt) Ltd v Commissioner-General ZIMRA 2015(2) ZLR 116).

      In the Amhold revised indigenisation implementation plan dated 5 June 2012 it was stated that:

      “… in November 2011, Unki donated US$ 10 million as seed capital to the Tongogara Rural District Community Share Ownership Trust (“TSOT”)”

    22. The revised plan further showed that the Anglo American Platinum Limited (through its subsidiaries the appellant and Southridge Limited together being Amzim) donated US$10 million and further implemented a phase allocation of shares which would amount to 51 per cent. In the implementation of the phase allocation of the shares the appellant retained 100 per cent of its shares and Southridge limited retained 88.8 per cent. It is thus clear, from the revised plan, that the US$10 million payment made by the appellant was made on behalf of its parent company.

    23. In GC (Pvt) Ltd v Commissioner General, Zimra (supra), the principle was laid down that a taxpayer is not entitled to make deductions in respect of expenses incurred by other companies in the same group. Each individual company must be assessed according to its own taxes. Thus, the argument that the appellant paid the US$10 million to comply with the indigenisation laws of Zimbabwe is without merit as it had no legal obligation to do so. It was Amhold, the holding company, that was required by law to transfer 51 per cent ownership shares as a foreign-owned company to indigenous entities. The fact that the appellant paid the money on behalf of Amhold is of no moment.

    24. There was no legal obligation on the appellant in any way for it to donate US$10 million to the Trust. If there was, the appellant has not provided the basis mandating it to do so. The donation was an agreed amount that foreign mining companies pledged to donate to give effect to the indigenisation and empowerment schemes initiated by the GOZ.

    25. The main object of the Trust was to use the proceeds of the Trust Assets to undertake various development programmes for the benefit of the residents of the area. It is for this reason that it was Amhold which submitted the indigenisation implementation plan as required by the indigenisation laws. The entity which should have obtained an indigenisation clearance certificate is Amhold and not the appellant. Hence the agreement records that it was Amhold that donated the US$ 10 million to the Community Share Ownership Trust through the appellant.

    26. In this vein, it cannot be said that the court a quo erred in reaching the conclusion that the indigenisation implementation plan was that of the holding company and that the appellant made the payment for Amhold in fulfilment of obligations it had committed to meet under that plan.

    27. In our view, the decision arrived at by the court a quo cannot be faulted. The provisions of the Indigenisation and Economic Empowerment Act and its Regulations did not place a legal obligation on the appellant to donate to a Community Trust for it to be indigenous compliant. The parent company was obligated at law to comply with the indigenisation legislation by disposing of 51 per cent of its shareholding to indigenous partners which it did in its revised indigenous implementation plan which was approved by the Minister of Youth Development, Indigenisation and Empowerment.

    28. Having, concluded that the appellant had no legal obligation to comply with the indigenous laws and be indigenous compliant so as to enable it to continue operating its special mining license, it follows that the appellant did not have a legal obligation to pay the US$10 million. It is our view, that, in these circumstances, it is not necessary to determine whether or not the payment was of a capital or revenue nature in order to dispose of this appeal. In the same vein it was also not necessary for the court a quo to have determined this issue.

    29. Moreover, even if, the appellant had made the payment on its own behalf it was not entitled to claim such a deduction of the donation under the Income Tax Act in 2011 because, at that time it was made, the law did not allow for such a deduction. The provisions of s 15(2) (ii) only came into force on 1 January 2013 and hence it would not have been applicable to the appellant’s case as it did not have retrospective effect.

    DISPOSITION

    30. It is our view that the judgment of the court a quo cannot be impugned. The donation of USD $10 million was paid by the appellant on behalf of its holding company Amhold, which is a separate legal entity. The appellant had no legal obligation to comply with the Indigenisation and Economic Empowerment Act. The appeal is thus devoid of merit. The respondent has been successful in resisting the appeal and is therefore entitled to costs.

    In the result, it is accordingly ordered as follows:

    The appeal be and is hereby dismissed with costs.

      UCHENA JA I agree

      MAKONI JA I agree

    Gill Godlonton and Gerrans, appellant’s legal practitioners

    Zimbabwe Revenue Authority Legal and Corporate Services Division, for the respondent
legal_principles:
A holding company and its subsidiary are separate legal entities for income-tax purposes. Each company must be assessed according to its own tax position, and a taxpayer is not entitled to deduct expenditure incurred by another company in the same group.
    A claimed deduction must relate to expenditure incurred by the taxpayer itself; payment of an obligation belonging to a separate group company does not become the payer’s deductible expenditure merely because the payer effected the payment.
    Where a threshold conclusion disposes of an appeal, a court need not determine a further issue whose resolution cannot affect the outcome.
    A statutory deduction introduced after the relevant expenditure was incurred does not apply retrospectively where the legislation does not confer retrospective operation.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the indigenisation laws, the Heads of Agreement and the indigenisation implementation plan placed the relevant compliance obligation on Amhold, the foreign holding company, and not on the appellant.
    Held, further, that the Heads of Agreement recorded the US$10 million as Amhold’s donation and the revised plan showed that the appellant made the payment on behalf of its parent company.
    Held, further, that because Amhold and the appellant were separate legal and taxable entities, the appellant could not deduct expenditure incurred on behalf of Amhold.
    Held, further, that the appellant had no legal obligation to make the payment, and it was therefore unnecessary to determine whether the payment was capital or revenue in nature.
    Held, further, that the appeal was devoid of merit and was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The governing indigenisation instruments imposed the relevant obligation on Amhold and recorded the US$10 million as its donation. The appellant merely effected payment on behalf of that separate corporate and taxable entity. Because a taxpayer cannot deduct expenditure incurred by another company in the same group, the appellant was not entitled to deduct the payment. That finding disposed of the appeal without requiring determination of the capital-versus-revenue question.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: GUVAVA JA added that, even if the appellant had made the payment on its own behalf, s 15(2)(ii) of the Income Tax Act came into force only on 1 January 2013 and did not operate retrospectively to permit a deduction for the payment made in 2011.

SC 24 - 22 BARIADIE     INVESTMENTS VS  CHIUTSI circulation copy.docx
case_name:
BARIADIE INVESTMENTS (PRIVATE) LIMITED
    v
    (1) PUWAYI CHIUTSI (2) TENDAI MASHAMANDA (3) THE REGISTRAR OF DEEDS (4) THE SHERIFF OF THE HIGH COURT (5) ELIOT ROGERS
judges:
Panel: GWAUNZA DCJ, GUVAVA JA, BHUNU JA
    Judgment by: GWAUNZA DCJ
    Concurrence: GUVAVA JA and BHUNU JA agreed.
subject_area: Civil procedure; Property; Execution; Judicial review
catchwords_flynote: Civil procedure – execution – judicial attachment of immovable property – pignus judiciale – property passing out of judgment debtor’s control – subsequent private sale and transfer in defiance of extant confirmation order – fraudulent transfer – transferee aware of caveats and irregularities – defective title a nullity – cancellation of deed and transfer to purchaser at execution sale. Appeal and review – Supreme Court Act (Cap 7.13), s 25 – irregularity in separate High Court default proceedings coming to Supreme Court’s notice – main application determined when only application for leave to appeal was set down – relief not sought – proceedings and default judgment set aside.
headnote:
The fifth respondent held a judgment debt against the first respondent. In execution, the first respondent’s immovable property was attached, sold by public auction to the appellant and the sale was confirmed. The High Court dismissed the first respondent’s challenge to the confirmation and ordered that an appeal would not suspend the judgment. Applications to interdict transfer to the appellant also failed. Nevertheless, the first respondent privately sold the attached property to the second respondent and caused Deed of Transfer No. 708/19 to be registered in the latter’s name. The appellant’s application to cancel that deed was dismissed by the High Court, which treated the matter as res judicata and considered that a pignus judiciale had not been proved because the existence of a caveat had not been established.

    On appeal, the Supreme Court held that the earlier judgments remained extant and barred the first respondent from dealing with the property. Judicial attachment in execution created a pignus judiciale: the property passed into the custody of the Sheriff and out of the judgment debtor’s control. The first respondent therefore lacked capacity to sell it, and his subsequent sale and transfer were fraudulent and legally unsustainable. The second respondent was aware of material irregularities, including caveats and the unexplained disappearance and reappearance of the title deed, and was not an innocent purchaser. His title was a nullity and fell to be cancelled.

    During the appeal, the Court was shown a default judgment, HH 477/21, delivered the previous day and purporting to set aside the earlier judgment confirming the execution sale. Exercising its review powers under s 25 of the Supreme Court Act, the Court found that the High Court had determined the substantive main application when only an interlocutory application for leave to appeal was set down; had conflated the two applications; had allowed a represented litigant to seek default relief without a renunciation of agency; and had granted relief not sought, including setting aside an order favourable to the fifth respondent. The proceedings and default judgment were set aside. The appeal was allowed with costs, the deed in the second respondent’s name was cancelled, the prior deeds were revived and transfer to the appellant was directed.
issues_for_determination:
1. Whether the High Court erred in dismissing the appellant’s application to cancel Deed of Transfer No. 708/19, having regard to the extant judgments, the judicial attachment and confirmed execution sale, the first respondent’s subsequent conduct and the second respondent’s alleged bona fides.
    2. Whether the proceedings and default judgment in HH 477/21, which came to the Supreme Court’s attention during the appeal, were reviewable and liable to be set aside under s 25 of the Supreme Court Act.
legal_principles:
An order of a competent court must be obeyed unless and until it is discharged. Conduct in defiance of an extant order is legally invalid, and a party in contempt may be denied audience until the contempt is purged.
    A judicial attachment in execution creates a pignus judiciale over the attached property. The property is placed in the custody of the officer of court and passes out of the judgment debtor’s estate and control. The debtor cannot thereafter validly dispose of it. The existence of a registered caveat is not an essential element of the pignus judiciale.
    A person cannot transfer rights that he or she does not possess, and rights cannot lawfully be transferred through fraud. A party may not derive a benefit from his or her own illegality or wrongdoing. Title acquired contrary to law or through fraudulent means is a nullity.
    A caveat is a warning to the Registrar of Deeds and to a prospective transferee that transfer must not proceed until the impediment is removed. A prospective purchaser who proceeds despite known caveats and material irregularities cannot claim the protection accorded to an innocent purchaser.
    Under s 25 of the Supreme Court Act, the Supreme Court may review proceedings or a decision of an inferior court whenever an irregularity comes to its notice, even though those proceedings or that decision are not themselves the subject of the appeal or application before it. A court may not determine a matter that is not before it or grant relief that no party sought. These requirements apply also to orders granted in default.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the judgments confirming the execution sale and refusing to suspend or interdict its implementation were extant when the first respondent purported to sell and transfer the property, and the High Court erred in treating them as irrelevant.
    Held, further, that attachment in execution created a pignus judiciale and removed the property from the first respondent’s control. He lacked competence to dispose of it privately, irrespective of whether a caveat had been proved.
    Held, further, that the first respondent’s private sale and transfer were fraudulent and invalid, and that the second respondent’s knowledge of caveats and other irregularities discredited his claim to be an innocent purchaser. Deed of Transfer No. 708/19 was a nullity.
    Held, further, that the appeal had merit and the deed had to be cancelled, the previous deeds revived and transfer to the appellant effected.
    Held, further, that the High Court proceedings culminating in HH 477/21 were irregular because the court determined the main application when only an application for leave to appeal was set down, conflated distinct applications and granted relief not sought. The proceedings and default judgment were set aside under s 25 of the Supreme Court Act.
ratio_decidendi: The confirmed execution sale and judicial attachment placed the property under a pignus judiciale and beyond the first respondent’s power of disposition. His later private sale, undertaken in defiance of extant court orders, could confer no valid title; the second respondent’s awareness of caveats and irregularities defeated any claim of innocent acquisition. The deed was therefore void and had to be cancelled. Separately, HH 477/21 could not stand because the High Court granted substantive default relief in a matter that was not set down and granted relief that had not been sought, irregularities reviewable by the Supreme Court under s 25.

document_name: SC 25 - 22 Elliot Rodgers v Puwayi Chiutsi Circulation copy.docx
case_name:
ELLIOT RODGERS
    v
    (1) PUWAYI CHIUTSI (2) TENDAYI MASHAMHANDA (3) REGISTRAR OF DEEDS (4) SHERIFF OF ZIMBABWE (5) BARIADE INVESTMENTS (PRIVATE) LIMITED (6) LAW SOCIETY OF ZIMBABWE
judges:
Panel: GWAUNZA DCJ, GUVAVA JA & BHUNU JA
    Judgment by: GWAUNZA DCJ
    Concurrence: GUVAVA JA and BHUNU JA agreed.
subject_area: Civil procedure; Practice and procedure; Interdicts
catchwords_flynote: Practice and procedure – urgent chamber application – provisional order – interim relief identical in nature and effect to final relief – cancellation of deed of transfer sought as interim relief – provisional order temporary and protective, requiring prima facie right and return day – final order conclusive, requiring clear right – applicant declining alternative temporary interdict – court unable to grant relief not sought – application properly dismissed. Appeal – grounds concerning relief ruled non-urgent and then abandoned – issues not determined a quo not properly introduced on appeal – appeal dismissed with costs.
headnote:
The appellant had obtained judgment against the first respondent, a legal practitioner who had misappropriated part of the proceeds from the sale of the appellant’s property. The first respondent’s own property was sold in execution to the fifth respondent, but despite the confirmed sale and a caveat he caused it to be transferred to the second respondent. The appellant then brought an urgent chamber application. The final relief sought included striking the first respondent off the roll of legal practitioners, while the interim relief sought cancellation of Deed of Transfer No. 708/19, curatorship of the first respondent’s firm and suspension from practice.

    The High Court ruled that the curatorship and suspension relief was not urgent. It heard the remainder and drew attention to the fact that cancellation of the deed, although framed as interim relief, was final in nature and effect. The second respondent was prepared to accept a temporary interdict against further transfer, but the appellant declined to seek that alternative and elected to stand or fall by cancellation. The High Court dismissed the application.

    On appeal, the Supreme Court held that the grounds directed at curatorship and suspension concerned relief that had been ruled non-urgent, abandoned for purposes of the hearing and not separately appealed; those matters could not be introduced into the appeal. The sole live issue was whether the High Court should have granted cancellation as provisional or final relief. A provisional order is temporary, preserves the status quo, is granted on a prima facie right and anticipates a return day. A final order conclusively determines the dispute, requires proof of a clear right and leaves the court functus officio. Cancellation of the deed would have extinguished the rights flowing from it and left nothing for determination on a return day. The appellant could not obtain that final result on the evidential standard applicable to interim relief, and he neither sought nor proved an appropriate alternative interdict. The High Court’s reasoning was upheld and the appeal was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
1. Whether grounds relating to the curatorship and suspension relief, which the High Court had ruled non-urgent and which the appellant did not pursue or separately appeal, were properly before the Supreme Court.
    2. Whether the High Court erred in dismissing the urgent application because cancellation of Deed of Transfer No. 708/19 was final relief presented as interim relief, and whether the appellant had established entitlement to either provisional or final relief.
legal_principles:
An appellate court considers the correctness of decisions actually made by the lower court. A party cannot introduce on appeal issues that were not determined a quo, particularly where the relevant relief was abandoned and the ruling against urgency was not appealed in the prescribed manner.
    A provisional order is temporary and protective. It preserves the status quo against apprehended irreparable harm pending full determination on the return day and ordinarily rests on a prima facie right.
    A final order is conclusive and dispositive, has no return day and ordinarily requires proof of a clear right. Once made, it renders the issuing court functus officio on the determined issues.
    Interim relief that is identical to the substantive final relief, or that has the same dispositive effect, defeats the purpose of interim protection by giving a litigant final relief on proof of only a prima facie case. Relief must be framed so that an interim order remains ancillary to a meaningful final determination.
    A court does not grant an alternative form of relief that the litigant has not sought. Although final relief may exceptionally be granted in an urgent application, the applicant must establish the requirements for that relief and circumstances justifying such treatment.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the first two grounds of appeal related to curatorship and suspension relief that had been ruled non-urgent, abandoned for the proceedings a quo and not properly appealed. They were misplaced and without merit.
    Held, further, that cancellation of Deed of Transfer No. 708/19 was final in nature and effect because it would extinguish rights under the deed and leave nothing for confirmation or discharge on a return day.
    Held, further, that the appellant could not secure that final result through a draft provisional order and on the prima facie standard applicable to interim relief.
    Held, further, that an appropriate temporary interdict could have preserved the position, but the appellant did not seek it as alternative relief and failed to establish entitlement to either a provisional or final order.
    Held, further, that the High Court properly dismissed the application and the appeal was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The only substantive relief left for urgent determination was cancellation of the deed. That relief was conclusive and would have left no live question for a return day, yet it was framed as interim relief and pursued on the basis applicable to a provisional order. Because the appellant did not seek an available temporary interdict and did not prove the clear right required for final relief, the High Court correctly refused the order and dismissed the application.

SC 29 - 22 TECHNOIMPEX JSC vs RAJENDRAKUMAH JOGI & 4 ORS.docx
case_name:
TECHNOIMPEX JSC
    v
    (1) RAJENDRAKUMAR JOG (2) TECHNOIMPEX JC (PRIVATE) LIMITED (3) SARAH HWINGWIRI (4) REGISTRAR OF DEEDS (5) THE SHERIFF OF THE HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE
judges:
Panel: GUVAVA JA, UCHENA JA, & KUDYA AJA
    Judgment by: UCHENA JA
    Concurrence: GUVAVA JA and KUDYA AJA agreed.
subject_area: Appeal and review; Civil procedure; Company law
catchwords_flynote: Appeal and review – preliminary objections to validity of civil appeal – consent order – High Court Act [Chapter 7:06], s 43(2)(c)(i) – no appeal from order genuinely made with parties’ consent – consent confined to facts and outcome consciously contemplated – agreement tying provisional interdict to merits determination of rescission application – rescission application instead struck off for want of corporate authority – discharge of interdict not consented to – appeal against that paragraph valid. Civil procedure – interlocutory order – s 43(2)(d) – leave to appeal required unless statutory exception applies – order striking unauthorised company application off roll – no board resolution and no valid founding affidavit – no leave obtained – notice of appeal nullity – remaining appeal to proceed on merits.
headnote:
The appellant, a Bulgarian company claiming ownership of Bath Mansions Flats, obtained a provisional interdict in HC 6784/19 restraining the respondents from transferring, encumbering or otherwise dealing with the property. The parties subsequently agreed that the provisional order would remain in force and that its confirmation or discharge would depend on a determination on the merits of the appellant’s rescission application in HC 6771/19. The High Court did not decide that rescission application on the merits. It found that the deponent to the founding affidavit lacked authority from the appellant’s board of directors, held that there was no valid application and struck it off the roll. The court also ordered, “by consent of the parties”, that the provisional order be discharged.

    The appellant appealed against both paragraphs. The respondents raised preliminary objections that the discharge was a non-appealable consent order and that the striking-off order was interlocutory and had been appealed without leave. The Supreme Court held that consent to a court order must be consciously given with an appreciation of the relevant facts and law and is confined to the outcome contemplated by the parties. Their agreement linked the interdict to a grant or dismissal of rescission on the merits, not to a procedural striking-off for want of authority. The discharge of the interdict was therefore not an order to which the appellant had consented, and the appeal against that paragraph was valid.

    The striking-off order was interlocutory. None of the exceptions in s 43(2)(d) of the High Court Act applied, and the appellant had obtained no leave to appeal. In addition, the absence of a board resolution meant that there had been no valid founding affidavit or application before the High Court. The notice of appeal against that paragraph was fatally defective and a nullity. The matter was struck off the roll to that extent; the appeal against the discharge of the provisional order was directed to proceed on the merits before the same bench, with each party bearing its own costs.
issues_for_determination:
1. Whether the appellant had consented to paragraph 2 of the High Court’s order discharging the provisional interdict and was consequently barred by s 43(2)(c)(i) of the High Court Act from appealing against it.
    2. Whether the appellant could appeal without leave against paragraph 1, which struck the rescission application off the roll for want of authority.
legal_principles:
Under s 43(2)(c)(i) of the High Court Act, no appeal lies from an order made with the consent of the parties. Consent must be a conscious decision made with appreciation of the facts and law governing the dispute, and it extends only to the outcome actually contemplated. It cannot be inferred in relation to a materially different event that the parties did not contemplate.
    A consent order extinguishes the existing cause of action. By contrast, striking a matter off the roll for a procedural defect does not determine or extinguish the cause of action and does not prevent a properly constituted fresh application.
    Under s 43(2)(d) of the High Court Act, leave is required to appeal from an interlocutory order unless the matter concerns liberty or custody of minors, the grant or refusal of an interdict, or an order on a special case under arbitration legislation.
    A company must authorise the institution and conduct of proceedings through its proper corporate organs. Where the founding deponent lacks the required board authority, there is no valid founding affidavit and consequently no valid court application.
    A fatally defective process is incurably bad. Where there was nothing validly before the lower court, there is nothing to dismiss or to sustain an appeal; the appropriate procedural result is to strike the matter off the roll.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the parties agreed that the provisional order would depend on a merits determination of the rescission application. They did not agree that it would be discharged if the rescission application was struck off the roll for lack of corporate authority.
    Held, further, that paragraph 2 was not a consent order in the relevant sense, and the notice of appeal against it was valid. That appeal was to proceed to a hearing on the merits.
    Held, further, that paragraph 1 was an interlocutory order and none of the statutory exceptions to the requirement for leave applied. The appellant had not obtained leave to appeal.
    Held, further, that the lack of a board resolution rendered the founding affidavit and rescission application invalid, so the appeal against paragraph 1 was fatally defective and a nullity.
    Held, further, that each side had succeeded on one preliminary issue and each party should bear its own costs.
ratio_decidendi: The parties’ agreement contemplated confirmation or discharge of the interdict only after the rescission application was decided on its merits; a procedural striking-off for lack of authority was outside that agreement, so the discharge could be appealed. Conversely, the order striking the application off the roll was interlocutory, no statutory exception applied and no leave had been obtained. The appeal against that paragraph was therefore a nullity, while the appeal against the purported consent paragraph remained valid.

    Georgias & Anor v Standard Chartered Finance Zimbabwe Ltd 1998 (2) ZLR 488 (S) at p 496

SC 32 - 22 CUTHBERT DUBE vs PSMAS & ANOR.docx
case_name:
CUTHBERT ELKANA DUBE
    v
    (1) PREMIER MEDICAL INVESTMENTS (PRIVATE) LIMITED (2) PREMIER SERVICE MEDICAL AID SOCIETY
court:
“The application is hereby dismissed with each party to bear its own costs.”

    MAKONI JA : I agree

    KUDYA JA : I agree

    Lovemore Madhuku Attorneys, appellant’s legal practitioners

    Muzangaza Mandaza & Tomana, respondents’ legal practitioners
judges:
Panel: MAKONI JA, MATHONSI JA & KUDYA JA
    Judgment by: MATHONSI JA
    Concurrence: MAKONI JA and KUDYA JA agreed.
subject_area: Civil procedure; Practice and procedure; Appeal and review
catchwords_flynote: Civil procedure – application for dismissal for want of prosecution – High Court Rules, 1971, r 236(3)(b) – judicial discretion – relevant factors – length of delay and explanation, prospects of success, balance of convenience and possible prejudice – delay alone insufficient – lower court considering only delay and explanation – failure to consider remaining factors a material misdirection – appellate interference. Stare decisis – High Court bound by prior Supreme Court authority – later contrary High Court decision not to be followed. Civil procedure – service of dismissal application – no rule barring respondent from continuing to prosecute main application – later filing of answering affidavit and heads relevant to seriousness of prosecution.
headnote:
The appellant applied in the High Court for declaratory relief that he held ten million shares, representing a 20 per cent shareholding, in the first respondent. After the respondents opposed the application, the appellant failed to prosecute it. A first application for dismissal for want of prosecution was withdrawn on an undertaking that he would file the outstanding papers and prosecute the main matter. When he did not promptly do so, the respondents made a second application under r 236(3)(b) of the High Court Rules, 1971. The appellant thereafter filed an answering affidavit and heads of argument in the main matter, but the High Court dismissed that matter for want of prosecution, principally because of the delay and the inadequacy of his explanation.

    On appeal, the Supreme Court held that dismissal for want of prosecution is a judicial discretion requiring consideration of three factors: the length of and explanation for the delay, prospects of success on the merits, and the balance of convenience and possible prejudice. Under binding Supreme Court authority, delay and its explanation alone cannot justify dismissal, and a litigant is not barred from continuing to prosecute the main matter merely because an application for dismissal has been served. The contrary High Court approach in Melgund Trading (Pvt) Ltd vs Chinyama & Partners was inconsistent with Guardforce Investments (Pvt) Ltd v Ndlovu & Ors and offended the doctrine of stare decisis.

    The court a quo considered only the delay and the explanation for it. It overlooked the appellant’s express reliance on the other factors, the filing of his answering affidavit and heads, and material indicating that the underlying shareholding dispute should be determined on the merits. That failure to consider relevant matters was a misdirection permitting appellate interference. The appeal was allowed with costs, and the respondents’ application for dismissal for want of prosecution was dismissed, with each party bearing its own costs a quo.
issues_for_determination: Whether the court a quo properly exercised its judicial discretion in dismissing the appellant’s application for want of prosecution, particularly whether its failure to consider prospects of success, the balance of convenience and possible prejudice constituted a material misdirection permitting appellate interference.
legal_principles:
The discretion to dismiss proceedings for want of prosecution is a judicial discretion. The court must consider the length of the delay and the explanation for it, the prospects of success on the merits, and the balance of convenience and possible prejudice caused by the failure to prosecute. Delay and its explanation alone cannot found dismissal.
    There is no rule of law barring a litigant from continuing to prosecute the main application after service of an application for dismissal for want of prosecution. Steps taken to advance the main matter may demonstrate seriousness and are relevant to the exercise of the discretion.
    Under stare decisis, the High Court is bound by decisions of the Supreme Court. A later High Court judgment cannot displace a controlling Supreme Court decision.
    An appellate court may interfere with a lower court’s discretion where the lower court failed to take relevant considerations into account or otherwise materially misdirected itself.
held_decision_points:
Held, that an application for dismissal for want of prosecution cannot properly be determined by considering only the length of delay and the adequacy of the explanation. Prospects of success, the balance of convenience and possible prejudice must also be considered.
    Held, further, that the High Court was bound by Guardforce Investments (Pvt) Ltd v Ndlovu & Ors SC 24/16. The contrary proposition in Melgund Trading (Pvt) Ltd vs Chinyama & Partners 2016 (2) ZLR 547 (H), that a litigant could take no further step in the main matter after service of a dismissal application, could not prevail over the Supreme Court authority.
    Held, further, that the court a quo overlooked the appellant’s reliance on the additional factors, his filing of an answering affidavit and heads of argument, and the material bearing on the underlying claim. Its failure to consider those relevant matters was a misdirection which entitled the Supreme Court to interfere.
    Held, further, that the interests of justice required the shareholding dispute to be fully interrogated and determined on the merits rather than terminated solely because of the delay.
    Held, further, that the appeal succeeded with costs; the application for dismissal for want of prosecution was dismissed, with each party bearing its own costs in that application.
ratio_decidendi: The High Court exercised its discretion under r 236(3)(b) on the basis of only the delay and the appellant’s explanation, despite binding authority requiring consideration also of prospects of success, the balance of convenience and possible prejudice. Because it ignored those relevant considerations and the appellant’s subsequent steps to prosecute the main matter, it materially misdirected itself. That misdirection opened the discretion to appellate interference, and the available material justified determination of the underlying shareholding dispute on its merits rather than dismissal for want of prosecution.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: MATHONSI JA observed that several grounds of appeal were repetitive and inconcise and did not meet the threshold set by r 44, but declined to determine the appeal on that basis because the point had not been raised with the parties at the hearing.

    Mashangwa & Anor v Makandiwa & Ors SC 95/21
    Commercial Farmers Union v Mhuriro & Ors 2000 (2) ZLR 405 (S) at 407G – 408A
    Barros & Anor vs Chimphonda 1999 (1) ZLR 58 (S)

document_name: SC 33 - 22 ZIMRA VERSUS CONWAL CHEMICALS RULING.docx
SC 34 - 22 NICHOLAS MUKARATI VS PIONEER COACHES PVT LTD.docx
case_name:
NICHOLAS MUKARATI
    v
    PIONEER COACHES (PRIVATE) LIMITED
judges:
Panel: GWAUNZA DCJ, MATHONSI JA & CHITAKUNYE JA
    Judgment by: MATHONSI JA
    Concurrence: GWAUNZA DCJ and CHITAKUNYE JA agreed.
subject_area: Labour and employment law; Jurisdiction; Practice and procedure; Appeal and review
catchwords_flynote: Labour and employment law – disciplinary proceedings under employment code – determination made by internal hearing officer – attempted referral to Labour Officer for conciliation after completed determination – Labour Act [Chapter 28:01], ss 93 and 101 – Labour Officer lacking jurisdiction to entertain appeal disguised as referral – subsequent compulsory arbitration and Labour Court appeal invalid. Jurisdiction – invalidity – nothing lawful flowing from proceedings conducted without jurisdiction. Appeal and review – threshold nullity raised in Supreme Court – merits not reached – appeal not properly before Court and struck off – Supreme Court Act [Chapter 7:13], s 25(1) – review powers invoked to set aside Labour Officer, arbitral and Labour Court proceedings – no order as to costs.
headnote:
The appellant, the respondent’s Managing Director, was charged with misconduct under the National Employment Code of Conduct. An initial disciplinary process was set aside by consent and a de novo hearing was held before J. T. Mawire, the parties’ agreed hearing officer. Mawire found the appellant not guilty and ordered reinstatement without loss of salary and benefits. The respondent attempted to pursue an internal appeal, but after disagreement over the appeal authority an arbitrator directed that the merits be conciliated by a Labour Officer. Conciliation failed, the matter was referred to compulsory arbitration and the arbitrator, treating the matter as an appeal against Mawire’s determination, upheld that determination. The Labour Court allowed the respondent’s appeal, set aside the award and remitted the matter for determination of the appropriate penalty.

    At the hearing of the appellant’s appeal, counsel raised the threshold question whether the proceedings before the Labour Officer, arbitrator and Labour Court were nullities. The Supreme Court held that a Labour Officer has no jurisdiction under s 93 of the Labour Act to entertain a matter after a determination on the merits has been made through disciplinary proceedings under a code of conduct. The referral mechanism is not an avenue for challenging a completed determination. Mawire’s determination remained extant and the respondent’s remedy lay through the applicable appeal process, not conciliation and compulsory arbitration.

    The Labour Officer’s intervention was therefore without jurisdiction. The resulting arbitration and the Labour Court proceedings were likewise irregular, and the appeal could not properly be before the Supreme Court. The merits were not determined. The matter was struck off the roll with no order as to costs, and the Court exercised its review power under s 25(1) of the Supreme Court Act to set aside the proceedings before the Labour Officer, the arbitrator and the Labour Court.
issues_for_determination:
1. Whether a Labour Officer had jurisdiction under the Labour Act to conciliate a dispute after an internal hearing officer had made a determination on the merits under the applicable code of conduct.
    2. If the Labour Officer lacked jurisdiction, whether the ensuing compulsory arbitration, Labour Court proceedings and present appeal were valid, and what order should be made under s 25(1) of the Supreme Court Act.
legal_principles:
A Labour Officer does not have jurisdiction under s 93 of the Labour Act to entertain a matter once a determination on the merits has been made through disciplinary proceedings under a code of conduct. The statutory referral mechanism addresses an undetermined matter or delay; it is not an appeal against a completed determination.
    A referral under s 8(6) of the National Employment Code cannot confer appellate jurisdiction on a Labour Officer or arbitrator where the Labour Act withholds it. The Court relied on authority holding that s 8(6), to the extent of inconsistency with ss 93 and 101 of the Labour Act, cannot validate such intervention.
    Proceedings conducted without jurisdiction are invalid, and subsequent proceedings founded upon that invalidity cannot acquire legality.
    Where an irregularity in inferior proceedings comes to the Supreme Court’s attention, s 25(1) of the Supreme Court Act empowers the Court to exercise review jurisdiction and set those proceedings aside.
held_decision_points:
Held, that Mawire’s internal disciplinary determination had not been set aside and remained extant. The respondent’s challenge had to proceed through the applicable appeal process rather than by referral to a Labour Officer for conciliation.
    Held, further, that the Labour Officer lacked jurisdiction under s 93 of the Labour Act to entertain the matter after a determination on the merits had been made under the code of conduct.
    Held, further, that the compulsory arbitration generated by the invalid conciliation and the subsequent Labour Court appeal were also irregular; nothing lawful could result from the original invalidity.
    Held, further, that the appeal was not properly before the Supreme Court and the substantive merits therefore did not arise for determination.
    Held, further, that the matter was struck off the roll with no order as to costs and, under s 25(1) of the Supreme Court Act, the proceedings before the Labour Officer, the arbitrator and the Labour Court were set aside.
ratio_decidendi: Because an internal hearing officer had already made a determination on the merits under the employment code, the Labour Officer had no statutory jurisdiction under s 93 to conciliate what was in substance an appeal against that determination. The compulsory arbitration and Labour Court proceedings flowed from that jurisdictional invalidity and were themselves unlawful. The present appeal consequently had no valid foundation and had to be struck off, while the invalid inferior proceedings were set aside under the Supreme Court’s statutory review power.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: MATHONSI JA observed that most of the grounds of appeal attacked factual findings rather than raising questions of law as required by s 92 F(1) of the Labour Act, but did not decide the matter on that basis because the issue had not been canvassed with the parties.
