document_name: SC 35 - 22 JOHN BASERA vs THE REGISTRAR OF SUPREME COURT & 3 ORS.docx
case_name:
JOHN BASERA
    v
    (1) THE REGISTRAR OF THE SUPREME COURT OF ZIMBABWE (2) SAMUEL TENDAI MUVUTI (3) JOYLIN MUVUTI (4) MINISTER OF LANDS, AGRICULTURE, WATER, FISHERIES AND RURAL SETTLEMENT
judges:
Single judge in chambers: MATHONSI JA
    Judgment by: MATHONSI JA
subject_area: Practice and procedure; Judicial review; Statutory interpretation; Access to justice
catchwords_flynote: Practice and procedure – chamber application – review of Registrar’s decision under Supreme Court Rules, 2018, r 13 – application for condonation and extension of time – r 43(4) – application served on respondents within three days but proof of service filed later – deeming provision attaching only to failure to serve – no requirement to file certificate of service within three days – Registrar lacking power to deem application abandoned on that basis – decision erroneous and set aside – application reinstated. Statutory interpretation – literal, mischief and purposive approaches – comparison with repealed r 31(4) of Supreme Court Rules, 1964. Court rules – rr 39 and 43 – words “subject to” – specific provision dominant in event of conflict – procedural rules interpreted consistently with effective access to justice.
headnote:
On 5 October 2021 the applicant filed SC 362/21, an application for condonation of the late noting of an appeal and extension of time. He served the second and third respondents on that date and the fourth respondent on 7 October 2021, but filed the certificates of service only on 12 October. The Registrar thereafter notified him that the application was regarded as abandoned and dismissed under the Supreme Court Rules because it had not been served within the prescribed period. After the applicant protested, the Registrar acknowledged the error but stated that she could not review her own decision and invited an application under r 13. Her report in the review proceedings gave conflicting reasons, referring both to failure to serve and to delay in filing proof of service.

    The Supreme Court held that review by motion permits correction or setting aside of administrative decisions affected by disregard of statutory obligations, gross irregularity or irrationality, or illegality and error of law. On the text of r 43(4), an application must be filed and then served on the respondent within three days. Only failure to effect that service causes the application to be regarded as abandoned and deemed dismissed. The rule does not require proof of service to be filed within the same three days. Rule 11(2) requires proof to be filed but fixes no time limit. The same conclusion followed from the mischief and purposive approaches and from comparison with the former rules.

    The application had in fact been served in time. The Registrar’s stated factual premise was incorrect, and she had no power under the rules to dismiss the application merely because the certificates were filed later. Rule 39 applied generally but could not override the specific requirements of r 43 in the event of conflict. Reading additional restrictions into the rules would improperly impede access to justice. The Registrar’s decision was set aside, SC 362/21 was reinstated and each party was ordered to bear its own costs.
issues_for_determination:
1. Whether the applicant failed to comply with r 43(4) of the Supreme Court Rules, 2018 so that SC 362/21 was properly regarded as abandoned and deemed dismissed.
    2. Whether a delay in filing proof of timely service, as distinct from a failure to serve within three days, authorised the Registrar to dismiss the application.
    3. The relationship between the general provisions of r 39 and the specific requirements of r 43.
legal_principles:
Review by motion permits a court or judge to set aside or correct a public official’s decision where important statutory obligations were disregarded, there was gross irregularity or irrationality, or the decision involved illegality or error of law. Under r 13(4), the reviewing judge may amend, confirm or set aside the decision or make another appropriate order.
    Where statutory or rule wording is clear, its ordinary meaning must be applied. The mischief and purposive approaches may also consider the prior law, surrounding circumstances, objects and consequences of the provision.
    Rule 43(4) requires an application under that rule to be filed and thereafter served on the respondent within three days. Failure to serve within that period is fatal and triggers the deeming provision. The rule does not impose a three-day period for filing proof of service. Rule 11(2) requires proof of service but specifies no time frame.
    The phrase “subject to” establishes dominance and subordination between related provisions. Rule 39 applies generally to applications, but where it conflicts with r 43, r 43 is dominant.
    Procedural rules should be interpreted to promote efficient justice delivery and effective access to justice, not to introduce unstated conditions that stifle access.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant served SC 362/21 on all respondents within three days of filing and therefore complied with the service requirement in r 43(4).
    Held, further, that r 43(4) makes only failure to serve within three days fatal. It does not require a certificate or other proof of service to be filed within that period.
    Held, further, that the Registrar’s communicated reason for dismissal was factually incorrect and she could not revise it in her later report. In any event, neither r 43(4) nor r 11(2) authorised dismissal merely because proof of timely service was filed after three days.
    Held, further, that r 39 applies generally but is subordinate to r 43 to the extent of any conflict.
    Held, further, that the Registrar’s decision was made in error and had to be set aside; the condonation application in SC 362/21 was reinstated and each party was to bear its own costs.
ratio_decidendi: The deeming consequence in r 43(4) is expressly tied to failure to serve an application on the respondent within three days of filing. The applicant effected service within that period. Because the rules do not impose the same three-day deadline for filing proof of service, the later filing of certificates could not convert timely service into abandonment or empower the Registrar to dismiss the application. The decision rested on an incorrect fact and an error of law and was therefore reviewable and liable to be set aside under r 13.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: MATHONSI JA cautioned that the absence of an express time limit is not carte blanche to withhold certificates of service. They should be filed promptly and, because r 11 is silent, within a reasonable time; in an appropriate case an unreasonable delay may entitle the Registrar to regard the application as abandoned and dismiss it.

document_name: SC 36 - 22 CLEVER NJOVANA vs KUDAKWASHE KARAGA.docx
SC 39 - 22 MASIMBA CHARITY HUNI FUELS VS NATHAN AMOS KADURIRA AND ANOTHER - Copy(1).docx
case_name:
MASIMBA CHARITY HUNI FUELS (PRIVATE) LIMITED
    v
    (1) NATHAN AMOS KADURIRA (2) MAKONI RURAL DISTRICT COUNCIL
judges:
Panel: GUVAVA JA, BHUNU JA & MUSAKWA JA
    Judgment by: MUSAKWA JA
    Concurrence: GUVAVA JA and BHUNU JA agreed.
subject_area: Civil procedure; Interdict; Property
catchwords_flynote: Civil procedure – final interdict – requirements – clear right, irreparable harm actually committed or reasonably apprehended, and absence of alternative remedy – meaning of “clear right” – degree of proof on balance of probabilities rather than requirement of registered real right. Property – purchaser and allottee of unsurveyed commercial stands – local authority accepting purchase price and issuing allocation letter – later lease of same stands to third party – purchaser entitled to protect allocated contractual interest by interdict – occupation and development by lessee constituting continuing harm – no alternative remedy shown – appeal dismissed with costs.
headnote:
The first respondent had paid the second respondent for five commercial stands in Nyazura. Following earlier double allocations and replacement arrangements, the second respondent issued him a letter allocating stands 1497 to 1501. It later leased those stands to the appellant for a truck-hire business. When the first respondent discovered that the appellant had occupied and begun developing the stands, he obtained a provisional interdict from the Magistrates Court and ultimately an order prohibiting occupation or development, requiring removal of equipment and materials and restoring vacant possession. The High Court dismissed the appellant’s appeal.

    Before the Supreme Court, the appellant principally contended that the first respondent had no clear right because he had not proved ownership or a registered real right in the land. The Court held that the word “clear” in the requirements for a final interdict describes the degree of proof, not the juridical category of the right. The right must be established on a balance of probabilities and may arise from any branch of substantive law. Because the dispute concerned protection of the first respondent’s purchased and allocated interest against allegedly unlawful occupation, he did not have to establish registered ownership.

    The allocation letter and proof that the second respondent had accepted the purchase price were not rebutted and established a clear right. The appellant’s occupation and development had displaced the first respondent and the harm would continue for the term of the lease. The assertions of irreparable harm and absence of an alternative remedy had not been challenged. All requirements for the interdict were met. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether the court a quo erred in finding that the first respondent had established a clear right, and therefore the requirements for a final interdict, despite not proving registered ownership or another real right in the stands.
legal_principles:
A final interdict requires a clear right, irreparable harm actually committed or reasonably apprehended, and the absence of an alternative remedy.
    The word “clear” concerns the degree of proof required. The right must be established on a balance of probabilities; “clear” does not confine the remedy to a registered real right.
    The right protected by an interdict arises from substantive law and may derive from any relevant branch of substantive law. The purpose of an interdict includes prohibiting unlawful conduct, compelling a particular act and remedying the effects of unlawful conduct.
    Where evidence of allocation and payment is unrebutted, it may establish a sufficiently clear right to protect the allottee’s interest against unlawful occupation and development.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the first respondent was not required to prove registered ownership or another real right in order to establish a clear right for purposes of the interdict. The real dispute concerned protection of his purchased and allocated interest against the appellant’s occupation and development.
    Held, further, that the second respondent’s allocation letter and receipt of the purchase price, neither of which was rebutted, established the first respondent’s clear right on a balance of probabilities.
    Held, further, that the appellant’s occupation and development had displaced the first respondent and constituted harm that would continue until expiry of the lease in 2024.
    Held, further, that irreparable harm and absence of an alternative remedy had not been challenged and the requirements for a final interdict were satisfied.
    Held, further, that the High Court could not be faulted; the appeal was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The first respondent proved by unrebutted evidence that the local authority accepted his purchase price and allocated the identified stands to him. A clear right for a final interdict is a right established on a balance of probabilities and need not be a registered real right. That proved interest entitled him to restrain the appellant’s conflicting occupation and development, which caused continuing harm and for which no alternative remedy was shown. The lower courts therefore correctly granted and upheld the final interdict.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: SC 42 - 22 INGALULU INVESTMENTS & ANOR v NRZ& ANOR.docx
SC 43 - 22 LEGACY HOSPITALITY MANAGEMENT SERVICES LTD vs AFRICAN SUN LIMITED & ANOR.docx
case_name:
LEGACY HOSPITALITY MANAGEMENT SERVICES LIMITED
        v
        (1) AFRICAN SUN LIMITED (2) HON. JUSTICE MTSHIYA (RTD) N.O.
judges:
Panel: MAKONI JA, MATHONSI JA & MWAYERA JA
        Judgment by: MWAYERA JA
        Concurrence: MAKONI JA and MATHONSI JA agreed.
subject_area: Arbitration; Contract; Public policy; Exchange control
catchwords_flynote: Arbitration – setting aside arbitral award – Article 34 of Model Law – public policy defence – narrow interpretation – wrong decision insufficient. Contract – force majeure and supervening impossibility – management agreement requiring exchange-control approval – RBZ refusal to approve foreign-currency payment obligation – effect on performance. Pacta sunt servanda – sanctity of contract subject to legality and regulatory approval.
headnote:
The appellant, a foreign hospitality management company, and the first respondent entered into a hotel-management agreement requiring statutory and regulatory approvals, including exchange-control approval. The Reserve Bank of Zimbabwe approved the arrangement for one year but later declined to authorise its continuation unless the parties renegotiated. The first respondent terminated the agreement, asserting force majeure and supervening impossibility. The arbitrator upheld that position. The High Court refused to set aside the award under Article 34 of the Arbitration Act and registered it.
    
    The Supreme Court held that an arbitral award is not contrary to public policy merely because it may be wrong in fact or law. The public-policy ground is restrictively construed and applies only where the award is so illogical, inequitable, or morally offensive that it intolerably hurts the conception of justice. Because the agreement itself required regulatory approval and continuation of the foreign-currency obligation without RBZ approval would have been unlawful, the arbitrator’s conclusion that performance had become impossible was not outrageous or contrary to public policy. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether the High Court erred in refusing to set aside the arbitral award as contrary to public policy, and whether it erred in registering the award.
legal_principles:
Article 34 of the Model Law permits setting aside only on limited grounds, including conflict with public policy. The public-policy defence is interpreted restrictively to protect finality in arbitration.
        A wrong factual or legal conclusion by an arbitrator is not enough. The error must amount to a palpable inequity or defiance of logic or accepted moral standards.
        Force majeure or supervening impossibility may discharge contractual obligations where performance is prevented by superior force, including regulatory prohibition or absence of required approval.
held_decision_points:
Held, that RBZ refusal to authorise the continued foreign-currency obligation engaged the suspensive and force majeure structure of the agreement and made continued performance unlawful or impossible.
        Held, further, that the arbitrator’s reasoning was not so illogical or inequitable as to offend public policy.
        Held, further, that registration of the award did not undermine pacta sunt servanda; it gave effect to the agreement’s own regulatory-approval structure.
        Held, further, that the appeal lacked merit and was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: Because the agreement depended on exchange-control approval and that approval was not renewed, the arbitrator’s finding of force majeure and impossibility was legally tenable. Under Article 34, the Supreme Court could not interfere merely because the appellant disagreed with the award; no public-policy threshold was met.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court emphasised that fanciful public-policy objections and attempts to relitigate disputes already determined by arbitrators undermine the finality and utility of arbitration.

document_name: SC 44 - 22 ZIMRA    vs   F C PLATINUM.docx
case_name:
ZIMBABWE REVENUE AUTHORITY
        v
        FC PLATINUM
judges:
Panel: BHUNU JA, CHIWESHE JA & MWAYERA JA
        Judgment by: MWAYERA JA
        Concurrence: BHUNU JA and CHIWESHE JA agreed.
subject_area: Tax law; Statutory interpretation; Companies limited by guarantee
catchwords_flynote: Income tax – exemptions – Income Tax Act, s 14 and Third Schedule para 2(d) and (e) – company limited by guarantee – whether incorporated association remains an association for exemption purposes – ordinary meaning of statutory language – effect of registration under s 26 of repealed Companies Act.
headnote: FC Platinum, a company limited by guarantee formed to operate a football club for pleasure and recreation, claimed exemption from income tax. The Special Court for Income Tax Appeals held that it was entitled to exemption as an association. The Supreme Court held that, once the respondent registered as a company limited by guarantee under s 26 of the repealed Companies Act, it became a company enjoying company privileges and bearing company obligations. It ceased to be an ordinary association for purposes of para 2(d) of the Third Schedule to the Income Tax Act and did not fall under para 2(e). The appeal was allowed and the tax appeals were dismissed.
issues_for_determination: Whether the respondent, as a company limited by guarantee incorporated under s 26 of the repealed Companies Act, was entitled to exemption from income tax under s 14 of the Income Tax Act read with para 2(d) or 2(e) of the Third Schedule.
legal_principles:
Statutory language is generally given its ordinary, plain and literal meaning unless that meaning conflicts with legislative intention or creates absurdity.
        An association registered under s 26 of the repealed Companies Act as a company limited by guarantee assumes the status and obligations of a company.
        Exemption provisions must be applied according to the categories expressly identified in the statute.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the respondent’s incorporation as a company limited by guarantee divested it of ordinary association status for purposes of para 2(d).
        Held, further, that para 2(e) applied to ecclesiastical, charitable and educational institutions of a public character, not to the respondent.
        Held, further, that the Special Court erred in finding that the respondent was entitled to exemption.
        Held, further, that the appeal succeeded with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The respondent could not both enjoy the benefits of incorporation as a company limited by guarantee and claim the tax exemption reserved for unincorporated associations or institutions identified by the Third Schedule. The ordinary statutory language excluded it.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: None identified

document_name: SC 45 - 22 RUTSATE RUTSATE v NDAVENI WEDZERAI & ORS DRAFT JUDGMENT.docx
case_name:
RUTSATE RUTSATE
        v
        (1) NDAVENI WEDZERAI (2) MUDYANADZO CHIMOMBE (3) JOHN MUDYANADZO (4) ABIAS BOTE (5) SHAMU MUDYANADZO (6) TSAURAI MUDYANADZO (7) MINISTER OF LOCAL GOVERNMENT (8) DISTRICT ADMINISTRATOR, GUTU
judges:
Panel: GUVAVA JA, BHUNU JA & KUDYA JA
        Judgment by: GUVAVA JA
        Concurrence: BHUNU JA and KUDYA JA agreed.
subject_area: Constitutional law; Traditional leadership; Jurisdiction; Retrospectivity
catchwords_flynote: Traditional leaders – chieftainship dispute – s 283 of Constitution – whether High Court jurisdiction ousted – pending proceedings commenced before 2013 Constitution – Sixth Schedule para 18(9) – retrospective operation – High Court original jurisdiction.
headnote: The appellant, appointed as Chief Chimombe, challenged a High Court ruling that the High Court had jurisdiction over a chieftainship dispute filed in 2010. He argued that s 283 of the 2013 Constitution vested such disputes in the President and ousted the High Court’s jurisdiction. The Supreme Court held that s 283 is not itself a substantive code and does not retrospectively apply to pending cases or oust the High Court’s inherent jurisdiction in clear terms. Proceedings commenced before the Constitution’s effective date continue under the transitional provisions in para 18(9) of the Sixth Schedule. The appeal was dismissed and the matter remitted for continuation of trial.
issues_for_determination:
“283 An Act of Parliament must provide for the following, in accordance with the prevailing culture, customs, traditions and practices of the communities concerned—
    the appointment, suspension, succession and removal of traditional leaders;
    the creation and resuscitation of chieftainships; and
    the resolution of disputes concerning the appointment, suspension, succession and removal of traditional leaders; but—
    the appointment, removal and suspension of Chiefs must be done by the President on the recommendation of the provincial assembly of Chiefs through the National Council of Chiefs and the Minister responsible for traditional leaders and in accordance with the traditional practices and traditions of the communities concerned;
    disputes concerning the appointment, suspension and removal of traditional leaders must be resolved by the President on the recommendation of the provincial assembly of Chiefs through the Minister responsible for traditional leaders;
    the Act must provide measures to ensure that all these matters are
    dealt with fairly and without regard to political considerations;
    the Act must provide measures to safeguard the integrity of traditional institutions and their independence from political interference.”
    Section 283(c)(ii) of the Constitution thus provides for the settlement of disputes concerning the appointment, suspension and removal of chiefs. Such disputes must be resolved by the President on the recommendation of the Provincial Assembly of Chiefs through the Minister responsible for traditional leaders. The above provision was introduced in order to provide a domestic resolution mechanism for disputes on the appointment and removal of chiefs which had become rampant. It should be noted that s 283 further provides that an Act of Parliament must provide for the above mechanism.
    In dealing with a matter that called for the interpretation of s 283 of the Constitution, Patel JA (as he then was), had this to say in Marange v Marange & Others SC 01/21:
    “..section 283 of the Constitution does not constitute the actual code that governs the appointment and removal of chiefs or the resolution of disputes in that connection. What s283 does is to enunciate the template to be applied in the formation and implementation of that code……. As I have already stated, s 283 of the Constitution is not a substantive provision that impacts directly on the law governing the appointment and removal of traditional leaders. Rather, it declares what that law should provide in regulating, inter alia, the resolution of chieftainship disputes. Consequently, it cannot be construed, per se, as ousting the jurisdiction of the courts over such disputes.”
    It appears from the above case that s 283 cannot be construed as a substantive provision. It merely allows the Legislature to craft a law that will operationalize the Constitutional provision. The Traditional Leaders Act which deals with such issues has not been amended following the coming into effect of the Constitution. It is however of note that para 10 of the Sixth Schedule of the Constitution provides that existing laws must be construed in conformity with the Constitution.
    It seems to me that, even if it is accepted that s 283 may be construed as a substantive provision, there is nothing in its wording that seems to indicate that it has retrospective application or that it ousts the inherent jurisdiction of the High Court.  The Traditional Leaders Act which is the legislation in force for the appointment and removal of Chiefs does not appear to oust the jurisdiction of the High Court in any way.
    Section 283 was introduced in the Constitution and only came into operation in May 2013.This was after summons had been issued in this matter in 2010. The principles applicable in order to determine the question of whether or not a statute is intended to operate retrospectively and take away accrued rights have been stated in numerous cases.
    In Curtis v Johannesburg Municipality 1906 TS 308 at 311 INNES CJ said:
    “The general rule is that, in the absence of express provision to the contrary, statutes should be considered as affecting future matters only; and more especially that they should if possible be so interpreted as not to take away rights actually vested at the time of their promulgation.”
    In Bell v Voorsitter Van Die Rasklassifikasieraad En Andere 1968(2) SA 678(A) which is in Afrikaans the head note states that:
    “It is clear that our law accepts the rule that, where a statutory provision is amended, retrospectively or otherwise, while a matter is pending, the rights of the parties to the action, in the absence of a contrary intention, must be decided in accordance with the statutory provisions in force at the time of the institution of the action.”
    In Agere v Nyambuya 1985 (2) ZLR 336 (S) at 338 G – 339A GUBBAY JA (as he then was) stated the general rule as follows:
    “It is a fundamental rule of construction in our law, dating probably from Codex 1:14:7, that there is a strong presumption that retrospective operation is not to be given to an enactment so as to remove or in any way impair existing rights or obligations unless such a construction appears clearly from the language used or arises by necessary implication. For instance, where it is expressly retrospective, or deals with past events, or concerns a matter of procedure, practice or evidence.   The supposition is that the Legislature intends to deal only with future events and circumstances.”
    Lastly, in Nkomo and Anor v Attorney-General and Ors 1993 (2) ZLR 422 (S) GUBBAY CJ at 429 C said:
    “Care must always be taken to ensure that retrospectivity is confined to the exact extent which the section of the Act provides.”
    From the above authorities, it seems to me that, the guidance emanating from the jurisprudence of our courts is that, firstly, where the Legislature intends that a provision should have retrospective effect it states so in clear and unequivocal terms. (See also Craies on Statute Law (Seventh edition) p 388). Secondly, s 283 of the Constitution does not have retrospective application. There is nothing contained in s 283 of the Constitution or the Traditional Leaders Act that shows an intention to oust the jurisdiction of the High Court in determining disputes relating to the appointment and removal of traditional leaders that had already commenced before the effective date.
    As was stated in the Marange case (supra) Parliament is at large, subject to the Constitution, to curtail or oust the jurisdiction of any court. However, any such ouster must be effected in clear and unambiguous terms. In this case, even if s 283 of the Constitution were to be regarded as a substantive provision, I am unable to discern anything in its language that might be construed as meant to curtail or oust the jurisdiction of the High Court.
    There is also nothing contained in the Traditional Leaders Act, which might be taken as effecting any such ouster.
    It should be noted that s 283 must be read in conjunction with para 18(9) of the Sixth Schedule to the Constitution which provides as follows:
    “(9) All cases, other than pending constitutional cases, that were pending before any court before the effective date may be continued before that court or the equivalent court established by this Constitution, as the case may be, as if this Constitution had been in force when the cases were commenced, but:-
    the procedure to be followed in those cases must be the procedure that was applicable to them immediately before the effective date; and
    the procedure referred to in subparagraph (a) applies to those cases even if it is contrary to any provision of this Constitution.”
    Paragraph 18 (9) of the Sixth Schedule is clearly a transitional provision dealing with procedure that affects cases which would have commenced before the effective date. It is a common law principle that a statute, dealing with procedure, applies retrospectively to govern all pending and future proceedings, unless it is provided otherwise. (See Craies on Statute Law supra at p 401-402).  Paragraph 18(9)(a) is such provision which provides contrary to the common law.  It thus alters the common law position.  Paragraph 18 (9)(a) of the sixth schedule allows a matter which has commenced before the effective date of the Constitution to continue in terms of the old procedure. Paragraph 18 (9)(b) leaves the matter in no doubt and expressly provides that even if the procedure is contrary to the new Constitution the old procedure should be followed.
    It should also be noted that para 18(10)(b) of the Sixth Schedule, places it beyond doubt that s 283 of the Constitution does not divest the High Court of its jurisdiction. It states that for the purposes of para 9 a civil case is deemed to have commenced when summons were issued or the application was filed before a court as the case may be. In casu, summons were issued in 2010 long before the Constitution came into operation. The provisions of s 283 (c)(iii) can only be interpreted as becoming operational relating to matters that arose after the commencement of the Constitution in 2013. For the above reasons the court a quo was correct in finding that it had jurisdiction to entertain the matter.
    I was not persuaded by the appellant’s submission that s 283(c)(ii) of the Constitution has retrospective application and has the effect of ousting the jurisdiction of the High Court. I was also not persuaded by the appellant’s submission that the interpretation of s 283 of the Constitution which was given in the Marange case does not apply to the present facts. I take the view that the interpretation given to s 283 in the Marange case (supra) is of general application and is not just restricted to reviews.  Whilst I accept that in the Marange case the court was dealing with a review in terms of s 26 of the High Court Act in my view this does not affect in any way the finding in the Marange case that s 283 does not have retrospective application nor does it oust the jurisdiction of the High Court.  It is clear from the facts that in the Marange judgment, the court was dealing with a matter that had commenced after the promulgation of the Constitution.  The court however still found that the jurisdiction of the High Court had not been ousted.
    It is also important to note that the High Court has original jurisdiction to deal with all civil and criminal matters. Section 171(1) (a) of the Constitution provides:
    “171. Jurisdiction of High Court
    1. The High Court—
    a. has original jurisdiction over all civil and criminal matters throughout
    Zimbabwe”
    This section must be read together with s 13 of the High Court Act [Chapter 7:06] which reads as follows:
    “13.Original Civil Jurisdiction
    Subject to this Act and any other law, the High Court shall have full original civil jurisdiction over all persons and over all matters within Zimbabwe.”
    The original jurisdiction of the High Court is unlimited, that is to say, it can hear and determine any civil dispute, whatever the nature of the claim. Since s 283 of the Constitution and the Traditional Leaders Act both do not expressly oust the jurisdiction of the High Court from determining traditional leadership disputes, it follows that it still maintains its unlimited original jurisdiction over such matters.
    DISPOSITION
    The court a quo correctly found that it had the requisite jurisdiction to hear the matter. Section 283 (c) of the Constitution has vested the power to deal with disputes relating to chieftainship in the President as a domestic resolution mechanism. It is not a substantive provision as it requires operationalization through an Act of Parliament. The provision does not have retrospective application. It does not oust the inherent power of the High Court to determine such matters.  The matter commenced before the court a quo prior to the coming into force of the Constitution in 2013. It was therefore pending when the Constitution was promulgated.  That being the case, it must proceed in terms of para 18(9) of the Sixth Schedule of the Constitution.
    The respondents have been successful and, as is the norm in respect of such a party, they are entitled to their costs.
    In the result it is ordered as follows:-
    The appeal be and is hereby dismissed with costs.
    For the avoidance of doubt the matter is remitted to the court a quo for
    continuation of trial in terms of the Rules of that Court.
    BHUNU JA:			I agree
    KUDYA JA:			I agree
    Kantor & Immerman, appellant’s legal practitioners
    Mushonga, Mutsvairo & Associates, 1st -6th respondents’ legal practitioners
legal_principles:
Section 283 of the Constitution provides a template for legislation regulating traditional leadership matters; it does not itself constitute a complete substantive code.
        Statutes and constitutional provisions are presumed not to operate retrospectively unless clear language or necessary implication requires it.
        Pending civil cases commenced before the effective date of the Constitution continue under the procedure applicable immediately before that date in terms of para 18(9) and 18(10)(b) of the Sixth Schedule.
        The High Court has original jurisdiction over all civil matters unless expressly ousted.
held_decision_points:
Held, that s 283 did not retrospectively apply to the 2010 chieftainship proceedings.
        Held, further, that neither s 283 nor the Traditional Leaders Act expressly ousted the High Court’s jurisdiction.
        Held, further, that the matter remained pending before the High Court under the Sixth Schedule transitional provisions.
        Held, further, that the appeal was dismissed with costs and the trial was to continue.
ratio_decidendi: Because the chieftainship dispute was commenced in 2010 before the 2013 Constitution took effect, para 18(9) of the Sixth Schedule preserved the High Court proceedings. Section 283 did not clearly or retrospectively remove that jurisdiction, and therefore the High Court was competent to continue hearing the matter.

SC 47 - 22 ISMAIL MOOSA LUNAT v MOHAMMED PATEL.docx
case_name:
ISMAIL MOOSA LUNAT
        v
        MOHAMMED PATEL
court: HIGH COURT OF ZIMBABWE (as stated in heading)
judges:
CHATUKUTA JA
        Judgment by: CHATUKUTA JA
subject_area: Civil procedure; Condonation; Extension of time; Appeals
catchwords_flynote: Condonation – late noting of appeal – deliberate abandonment of earlier appeal – inordinate delay – unacceptable explanation – prospects of success not decisive – finality of litigation – punitive costs.
headnote: The applicant sought condonation and extension of time to appeal against summary judgment entered in 2019 on an acknowledgement of debt. He had previously filed an appeal timeously but deliberately abandoned it on legal advice by failing to pay record-preparation costs. He also pursued other proceedings, including a stay application and further condonation attempts. The Supreme Court held that the delay was inordinate and the explanation unreasonable. Even arguable prospects of success could not rescue a flagrant and deliberate disregard of the rules. The application was dismissed with legal practitioner and client costs.
issues_for_determination: Whether the applicant satisfied the requirements for condonation of non-compliance with the rules and extension of time within which to appeal.
legal_principles:
The factors for condonation include extent of delay, explanation, prospects of success, finality, convenience of the court, and avoidance of delay in the administration of justice.
        A litigant must give a full, honest and reasonable explanation for non-compliance.
        Prospects of success are important but not decisive; where the breach is flagrant and the explanation unacceptable, condonation may be refused irrespective of prospects.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the delay was inordinate and the applicant’s deliberate abandonment of the original appeal was indefensible.
        Held, further, that the application treated condonation as if it were available for the asking and undermined finality.
        Held, further, that alleged prospects of success could not cure a flagrant breach with no acceptable explanation.
        Held, further, that the application was dismissed with costs on the legal practitioner and client scale.
ratio_decidendi: The applicant deliberately failed to comply with the appeal rules and allowed his appeal to lapse, then returned years later without a reasonable explanation. In those circumstances, the cumulative effect of delay, disregard of procedure, and finality outweighed any alleged prospects of success.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted the public interest in peace, certainty and security in litigation and the need to close the book even though fresh arguments may later be found.

SC 50 - 22 Lonrho Logistics Pvt Ltd  vs  Ram Petroleum Pvt Ltd.docx
case_name:
LONRHO LOGISTICS (PRIVATE) LIMITED
        v
        RAM PETROLEUM (PRIVATE) LIMITED
judges:
Panel: UCHENA JA, MATHONSI JA & KUDYA JA
        Judgment by: MATHONSI JA
        Concurrence: UCHENA JA and KUDYA JA agreed.
subject_area: Contract; Sale; Statutory interpretation; Customs and excise; Pleadings
catchwords_flynote: Sale – contract perfecta – transfer of ownership by constitutum possessorium – later statutory price or duty increase – presumption against retrospectivity – Customs and Excise Act s 230(1) – prerequisites for seller to recover increased duty – no proof of payment. Contract – implied delivery timeline – court may not rewrite contract. Pleadings – plea as shield, not sword – positive relief cannot be granted without counterclaim.
headnote: The appellant paid for 150 000 litres of diesel before a January 2019 fuel-pricing and duty change. The respondent delivered 30 000 litres and refused to deliver the balance unless the appellant paid an additional sum said to arise from the new regime. The High Court found the sale had become perfecta and ownership had passed, but dismissed the appellant’s claim for delivery and granted the respondent cancellation and refund relief on its plea. The Supreme Court held that the new statutory regime had no retrospective effect, s 230(1) of the Customs and Excise Act was not engaged because no relevant duty payment had been proved, no 72-hour delivery term existed, and the High Court could not grant positive relief on a plea alone. The appeal was allowed and specific performance ordered.
issues_for_determination: Whether the appellant’s already-purchased diesel was subject to additional duty or price increases introduced after the sale; whether there was a contractual delivery timeline; and whether the High Court could grant positive relief to the respondent on a plea without a counterclaim.
legal_principles:
Statutes are presumed not to operate retrospectively unless clear language provides otherwise.
        Section 230(1) of the Customs and Excise Act permits recovery of increased duty only where the statutory requirements are met, including proof that the seller paid the relevant increased duty.
        Courts may not rewrite contracts or imply terms inconsistent with the parties’ agreement and conduct.
        A plea is a defence and cannot found positive relief in the absence of a counterclaim.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the January 2019 statutory regime did not apply retrospectively to diesel already sold and owned by the appellant.
        Held, further, that s 230(1) was not triggered because the respondent did not prove payment of additional duty and delivery had occurred before the increase.
        Held, further, that there was no evidence of a 72-hour delivery term.
        Held, further, that the High Court erred in granting cancellation and refund relief on a plea.
        Held, further, that the appellant was entitled to delivery of 120 000 litres of diesel.
ratio_decidendi: Because the sale was complete and ownership had passed before the later statutory instruments came into force, the appellant’s vested rights could not be impaired absent retrospective language. The respondent also failed to prove the statutory prerequisites for recovering increased duty and had no counterclaim on which positive relief could be granted.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that the respondent’s attempt to defend the High Court’s order was difficult because several of the lower court’s findings were contradictory.

document_name: SC 51 - 22 WONDER SIMUKA AND MONTANA CARSWELL MEATS.docx
case_name:
WONDER SIMUKA
        v
        MONTANA CARSWELL MEATS (PRIVATE) LIMITED
judges:
Panel: UCHENA JA, CHIWESHE JA & KUDYA JA
        Judgment by: CHIWESHE JA
        Concurrence: UCHENA JA and KUDYA JA agreed.
subject_area: Labour law; Disciplinary proceedings; Evidence; Dismissal
catchwords_flynote: Employment – disciplinary conviction for theft or fraud – employer’s onus – absence of evidence from cashier and manager – disputed acknowledgement of debt not proved – no evidence on which reasonable tribunal could convict – reinstatement. Labour appeal – procedural complaints better pursued by review where necessary.
headnote: The appellant was dismissed after a disciplinary committee found him guilty of theft or fraud in relation to missing cash while he temporarily stood in for a cashier. The Supreme Court held that the employer had not proved the amount allegedly entrusted to the appellant or contradicted his explanation that cash and receipts were checked and approved by a manager. The alleged acknowledgement of debt was disputed and no handwriting evidence was produced. There was therefore no evidence on which a reasonable tribunal could convict. The appeal succeeded, the disciplinary conviction was set aside, and reinstatement without loss of salary or benefits was ordered, failing which damages were payable.
issues_for_determination: Whether the appellant was properly convicted of theft or fraud and therefore properly dismissed from employment.
legal_principles:
An employer alleging theft or fraud must prove the essential facts on a balance of probabilities.
        Where the employee denies receipt or responsibility, evidence must establish what was entrusted to the employee and how the loss is attributable to him.
        A disputed document cannot be relied on as an admission where authenticity is not proved after the signature is challenged.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the respondent failed to call necessary evidence from the cashier or manager to prove what money was handed to the appellant or to rebut his account.
        Held, further, that reliance on the alleged acknowledgement of debt was misplaced because the appellant disputed the signature and no expert evidence was produced.
        Held, further, that there was no evidence on which a reasonable tribunal could convict the appellant of theft or fraud.
        Held, further, that the appeal succeeded with costs and reinstatement was ordered.
ratio_decidendi: The employer bore the onus of proving the disciplinary charge. Because it failed to prove the foundational facts linking the missing cash to the appellant and failed to authenticate the alleged acknowledgement of debt, the conviction and dismissal could not stand.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court stated that the appellant’s procedural complaints were unnecessary to determine once the evidentiary insufficiency disposed of the appeal, and such procedural issues should have been pursued by review before a competent court.

document_name: SC 52 - 22 AL SHAMS GLOBAL BVI LIMITED VS DEPOSIT PROTECTION CORPORATIO AND 3 ORS.doc
case_name:
AL SHAMS GLOBAL BVI LIMITED
        v
        (1) DEPOSIT PROTECTION CORPORATION (2) KIITUMETSI ZAWANDA (3) EQUITY PROPERTIES (PRIVATE) LIMITED (4) REGISTRAR OF DEEDS N.O.
judges:
Panel: MATHONSI JA, KUDYA JA & MUSAKWA JA
        Judgment by: MATHONSI JA
        Concurrence: KUDYA JA and MUSAKWA JA agreed.
subject_area: Civil procedure; Appeals; Insolvency and company liquidation; Interlocutory orders
catchwords_flynote: Civil procedure – appealability – striking off an urgent application for failure to obtain leave to sue liquidator – whether order final or interlocutory – simple interlocutory order appealable only with leave under s 43(2)(d) of High Court Act. Insolvency – litigation against company or bank in liquidation – leave to sue liquidator – concursus creditorum – need for court supervision. Costs – repeated disregard of leave requirements – legal practitioner and client scale.
headnote:
The appellant held the original title deed to immovable property owned by the third respondent as security linked to indebtedness involving Interfin Banking Corporation, which was in liquidation. After the third respondent discharged its debt to Interfin, the liquidator demanded release of the title deed so that the mortgage bond could be cancelled and the title deed returned. When the appellant did not release it, a replacement deed was issued by the Registrar of Deeds. The appellant then filed an urgent application for a mandamus and interdictory relief to compel surrender of the replacement deed and to prevent its use. The High Court struck the application off the roll because the appellant had not obtained leave to sue the liquidator.
    
    On appeal, the Supreme Court rejected the broad argument that the striking-off order was inherently non-appealable. However, it held that the order was interlocutory because it did not determine the merits of the underlying title-deed dispute and left the parties free to proceed once leave to sue had been obtained. Since the order did not fall within the exceptions in s 43(2)(d) of the High Court Act, leave to appeal was required. No leave had been sought or granted. The appeal was therefore improperly before the Court and was struck off the roll. Punitive costs were justified because the appellant had repeatedly ignored the procedural requirement for leave despite earlier guidance from the Supreme Court.
issues_for_determination: Whether the High Court’s striking-off order was appealable without leave, and whether the appellant’s failure to obtain leave to appeal rendered the appeal improperly before the Supreme Court.
legal_principles:
A creditor or litigant ordinarily requires leave before proceeding against a liquidator or a company in liquidation because liquidation creates a concursus creditorum and court supervision protects the orderly distribution of assets.
        A striking-off order that does not decide the merits and leaves the dispute capable of later determination may be a simple interlocutory order. Under s 43(2)(d) of the High Court Act, an appeal from an interlocutory order of the High Court requires leave unless the matter falls within a statutory exception.
        A court may impose legal practitioner and client costs to mark disapproval of repeated procedural disregard.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the High Court order did not determine the substantive dispute over the title deed or the replacement deed and therefore was interlocutory.
        Held, further, that although the order was appealable, it was appealable only with leave because it did not fall within the exceptions in s 43(2)(d) of the High Court Act.
        Held, further, that no leave having been obtained, the appeal was improperly before the Supreme Court and had to be struck off the roll.
        Held, further, that the appellant’s repeated disregard of the need for leave justified costs on the legal practitioner and client scale.
ratio_decidendi: The impugned order merely halted the urgent application because leave to sue the liquidator had not been obtained; it did not determine the parties’ substantive rights in the property dispute. It was therefore a simple interlocutory order. Section 43(2)(d) required leave to appeal, and because the appellant appealed without such leave, the Supreme Court lacked a properly constituted appeal.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court rejected the proposition that every procedural or preparatory order is non-appealable merely because it is interlocutory; statutory regulation determines the availability and conditions of appeal.

document_name: SC 54 - 22 Regis Mugauzi v Francis Jekera and Anor.docx
case_name:
REGIS MAGAUZI
    v
    (1) FRANCIS JEKERA (2) SHERIFF FOR ZIMBABWE N.O
judges:
Panel: MAVANGIRA JA, UCHENA JA & CHIWESHE JA
    Judgment by: UCHENA JA
    Concurrence: MAVANGIRA JA and CHIWESHE JA agreed.
subject_area: Currency conversion; Judgment debts; Civil procedure; Declaratory orders; Execution
catchwords_flynote: Judgment debt – United States dollar order granted on 22 February 2019 – effect of SI 33 of 2019 and Finance Act (No. 2) of 2019 – whether liability arose from prior acknowledgment of debt or extant court order – distinction between pre-effective-date liabilities and post-effective-date obligations – Zambezi Gas applied – declaratory order set aside.
headnote:
The first respondent had acknowledged owing the appellant US$26 100. A consent judgment for payment of that sum was granted on 22 February 2019, the effective date of SI 33 of 2019. The first respondent later paid Z$88 400 through the Sheriff and applied for a declaratory order that the payment fully satisfied the judgment debt at the one-to-one rate. The High Court held that the obligation arose before 22 February 2019 from the acknowledgment of debt and that the debt had been settled.

    The Supreme Court allowed the appeal. It held that once the consent order was granted, the operative obligation was the judgment debt, not the antecedent acknowledgment of debt. The order was granted on 22 February 2019 after SI 33 of 2019 had come into effect. It was therefore not a liability valued in United States dollars immediately before the effective date within s 4(1)(d) of SI 33 of 2019 and s 22(1)(d) of the Finance Act (No. 2) of 2019. The applicable provision was s 4(1)(e), requiring conversion using the interbank rate.

    The High Court had misapplied Zambezi Gas by focusing on the original acknowledgment rather than the extant court order. Its declaratory order was set aside and replaced with an order dismissing the application with legal-practitioner-and-client costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the relevant obligation for currency-conversion purposes arose from the acknowledgment of debt or from the consent judgment.
    Whether a judgment debt granted on 22 February 2019 fell under s 4(1)(d) or s 4(1)(e) of SI 33 of 2019 and the corresponding Finance Act provisions.
    Whether the first respondent’s payment in RTGS/Zimbabwe dollars fully discharged the judgment debt.
legal_principles:
A court order is binding and must be obeyed unless set aside or varied. Once judgment has been granted, execution proceeds on the basis of the court order rather than the underlying cause of action.

    Under Zambezi Gas, liabilities valued in United States dollars immediately before the effective date are converted at parity under s 4(1)(d) of SI 33 of 2019. Transactions or values arising after the effective date fall under s 4(1)(e), where exchange occurs by reference to the authorised-dealer rate.

    The effective date of SI 33 of 2019 was 22 February 2019. A judgment debt granted on that day after the instrument came into force was not a liability valued immediately before the effective date.
held_decision_points:
The High Court erred by relying on the acknowledgment of debt rather than the consent judgment.

    The first respondent’s legal obligation after 22 February 2019 arose from the court order. That judgment debt was granted after SI 33 of 2019 had taken effect.

    The judgment debt therefore fell under s 4(1)(e), not s 4(1)(d), and could not be settled at the one-to-one rate.

    The appeal succeeded. The declaratory order was set aside and the application was dismissed with legal-practitioner-and-client costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio decidendi is that where an underlying United States dollar debt has merged into an extant judgment debt granted on the effective date of SI 33 of 2019 after the instrument had come into operation, the operative obligation is the judgment debt. It is not treated as a liability valued immediately before the effective date merely because the original cause of action predated that date.

document_name: SC 55 - 22 LYTTON INVESTMENTS v STABDARD CHARTERED BANK.docx
SC 56 - 22 CENTRAL AFRICAN BUILDING SOCIETY.docx
case_name:
CENTRAL AFRICAN BUILDING SOCIETY
    v
    (1) FINORMACG CONSULTANCY (PRIVATE) LIMITED
    (2) RETIRED JUSTICE L.G. SMITH
judges:
Panel: GUVAVA JA, CHATUKUTA JA & MUSAKWA JA
    Judgment by: CHATUKUTA JA
    Concurrence: GUVAVA JA and MUSAKWA JA agreed.
subject_area: Arbitration; Bias; Public policy; Civil procedure; Affidavits
catchwords_flynote: Arbitration – application to set aside award – reasonable apprehension of bias – application stands or falls on founding affidavit – fresh allegations in answering affidavit – post-award opposing affidavit by arbitrator – whether post-award remarks can invalidate prior award – contradictory findings by High Court – award reinstated.
headnote:
The appellant and first respondent were parties to a consultancy contract. The contract was terminated and the dispute went to arbitration. The arbitrator found that the appellant lawfully terminated the contract on notice and dismissed most of the claims and counterclaims, save for a monthly payment due to the first respondent. The first respondent applied to the High Court to set aside the award, alleging bias and breach of public policy. The High Court found that the pre-award conversation between the arbitrator and the appellant’s managing director did not create a reasonable apprehension of bias, but nevertheless set aside the award after considering the arbitrator’s post-award opposing affidavit.

    The Supreme Court allowed the appeal. It held that an application stands or falls on the founding affidavit. The first respondent’s pleaded case of bias rested on the conversation during the arbitral proceedings; the post-award remarks in the opposing affidavit were raised later and could not be used to make a new case. The High Court also contradicted itself by first finding no reasonable apprehension of bias from the conversation and later using the same conversation, together with post-award material, to find bias.

    The post-award opposing affidavit could not show bias in the making of an award that had already been issued. The High Court judgment was set aside and substituted with dismissal of the application.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the High Court misdirected itself in finding a reasonable apprehension of bias warranting setting aside the arbitral award.
    Whether allegations or facts appearing only in the answering affidavit and post-award opposing papers could found the application.
    Whether conduct occurring after publication of the award could invalidate the making of the award.
    Whether it was necessary to decide the High Court’s power to remit the matter to a different arbitrator.
legal_principles:
An application stands or falls on its founding affidavit. The founding affidavit sets out the case that the respondent must meet, and an applicant may not make a new case in an answering affidavit.

    Bias sufficient to set aside an arbitral award must relate to the making of the award. Post-award conduct may not, without more, prove that the arbitrator was biased during the proceedings that had already ended.

    A court may not contradict its own finding in the same judgment by rejecting a pleaded allegation of bias and then relying on the same allegation, with later material not pleaded as the founding case, to reach the opposite conclusion.
held_decision_points:
The founding affidavit alleged bias based on the arbitrator’s conversation with the appellant’s managing director during the arbitration. The High Court correctly found that this did not create a reasonable apprehension of bias.

    The High Court erred by relying on fresh matter raised in the answering affidavit and on the arbitrator’s opposing affidavit filed after the award.

    The post-award affidavit could not invalidate the prior award.

    The appeal succeeded. It was unnecessary to decide the remittal issue.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio decidendi is that an arbitral award cannot be set aside for reasonable apprehension of bias on a basis not pleaded in the founding affidavit, particularly where the relied-upon conduct occurred after the award was issued and therefore could not have affected the making of the award.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable
cases_referred_to: Austerlands (Pvt) Ltd v Trade & Investment Bank Limited & Ors SC 92/05

    UNCITRAL Model Law, Article 34(2)(b)(ii), as scheduled to the Arbitration Act

document_name: SC 57 - 22 ERICA NDEWERE JUDGEMENT -.docx
case_name:
ERICA NDEWERE
    v
    (1) PRESIDENT OF ZIMBABWE N.O.
    (2) CHIEF JUSTICE N.O.
    (3) JUDGE PRESIDENT OF THE HIGH COURT N.O.
    (4) JUDICIAL SERVICE COMMISSION
    (5) MINISTER OF JUSTICE, LEGAL AND PARLIAMENTARY AFFAIRS N.O.
judges:
Panel: MAKONI JA, MATHONSI JA & KUDYA JA
    Judgment by: MAKONI JA
    Concurrence: MATHONSI JA and KUDYA JA agreed.
subject_area: Constitutional law; Judicial discipline; Mootness; Supreme Court jurisdiction; Declaratory relief
catchwords_flynote: Mootness – appeal against refusal of interim interdict to prevent appointment or continuation of tribunal under s 187(3) of Constitution – tribunal already appointed, proceedings concluded and judge removed from office – no live controversy – discretion to hear moot appeal – interests of justice – Supreme Court not court of first instance for declaratur – proposed amended relief introducing new declaratory claim – appeal dismissed.
headnote:
The appellant, formerly a High Court judge, sought an interim interdict in the High Court to prevent the President from establishing a tribunal under s 187(3) of the Constitution, or to stay the tribunal if already established, pending determination of the legality of the Judicial Service Commission’s referral. The High Court dismissed the application. By the time the appeal was heard, the tribunal had been established, had conducted its inquiry with the appellant’s participation, and the appellant had been removed from office.

    The Supreme Court held that the appeal was moot. The relief sought in the notice of appeal could no longer have any practical effect because the events sought to be interdicted had already occurred. Although a court may decide a moot matter where the interests of justice require it, this was not such a case. The appellant sought to amend her relief to obtain a declaratur that her referral to the President was unlawful and void. The Court rejected that attempt because the High Court proceedings concerned an interim interdict, not an original declaratory application. The Supreme Court is not a court of first instance for declaratory relief.

    The Court distinguished Bere, where the issues had been ventilated below and the Court exercised discretion to hear a moot appeal. Here, the proposed relief would substitute a new cause and turn the Supreme Court into a first-instance court. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the appeal had become moot because the tribunal had already been established, had completed its work, and the appellant had been removed from office.
    Whether the Supreme Court should nevertheless exercise its discretion to hear the moot appeal in the interests of justice.
    Whether the appellant could amend the relief on appeal to seek a declaratur not determined by the court a quo.
    Whether the Supreme Court could grant declaratory relief as a court of first instance.
legal_principles:
A matter is moot where changed circumstances remove the live controversy and a judgment would have no practical effect. The inquiry has two stages: first, whether the tangible dispute has disappeared; secondly, whether the court should nevertheless exercise discretion to hear the matter in the interests of justice.

    Relevant factors include practical effect, the importance and complexity of the issue, the fullness of argument, and whether resolution is necessary to settle a live legal controversy. Courts avoid advisory opinions.

    The Supreme Court has appellate jurisdiction and is not a court of first instance for declaratory relief. It cannot grant a declaratur in the first instance merely because a party seeks to amend relief on appeal.
held_decision_points:
The appeal was moot because the tribunal had already been established, had completed its inquiry, and the appellant had been removed from office.

    The original interdictory relief could no longer produce practical relief.

    The proposed amendment was not a true amendment but a substitution of new declaratory relief that had not been determined by the High Court.

    The case did not justify the Court exercising discretion to hear a moot matter in the interests of justice.

    The appeal was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio decidendi is that an appeal against refusal of interim interdictory relief becomes moot where the events sought to be interdicted have already occurred and no practical relief can be granted. A litigant cannot overcome mootness by seeking, for the first time on appeal, a declaratur that was not determined by the court below, because the Supreme Court lacks original jurisdiction to grant declaratory relief.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that mootness promotes efficient use of judicial resources and prevents courts from issuing advisory opinions. It also distinguished its earlier decision in Bere on the basis that Bere involved issues already ventilated below.

document_name: SC 58 - 22 CHINEKA MUPANDE judgment.docx
case_name:
(1) CHINEKA MUPANDE (2) GCOBANI MKWANANZI (3) RENIAS MAPFUMO
    v
    THE STATE
citation:
(a) It gave weight on the aggravating factors instead of balancing it with the mitigating factors and interests of the criminal justice machinery.”

    APPELLANTS’ SUBMISSIONS BEFORE THIS COURT

    In urging the Court to exercise its discretion and overturn the decision of the trial court, counsel for the appellants submitted that the court erred in convicting the appellants of culpable homicide based on the evidence of Madyara as he was an interested party and was therefore inclined to falsely implicate the appellants. He argued that the witness was part of the team of detectives who interviewed and interrogated the deceased. Given such, counsel contended that the court a quo erred in failing to treat Madyara’s evidence with caution as he was an accomplice yet the court took a restrictive interpretation of the word “accomplice”. Having misdirected itself and applied the strict interpretation of the definition of accomplice, the court did not warn itself of the inherent dangers of relying on the testimony of a suspect witness. Consequently, counsel for the appellants prayed that the conviction be set aside.

    RESPONDENT’S SUBMISSIONS BEFORE THIS COURT

    Per contra, counsel for the respondent submitted that the finding by the court a quo that Madyara was a witness and not an accomplice was unassailable. There was evidence placed before the court a quo which showed that the witness did not actively participate in the interrogation and assault of the deceased. Counsel submitted that the witness was assigned to assist the appellants by taking notes as they interviewed the suspects. He was also asked to attend to the recovery of the 1st appellant’s stolen television after the deceased had confessed to the theft. He further argued that the appellants were superiors to the witness, with the 1st appellant being an assistant inspector, 2nd appellant a sergeant whilst the witness was a constable and as such he could not stop his superiors from interrogating and assaulting the deceased.

    Counsel also submitted that an accomplice is a person who knowingly, voluntarily and with common intent unites with the principal offender in the commission of a crime, and may assist or encourage the principal offender with the intent to have the crime committed. He submitted that the witness could not have been found to be an accomplice as from the evidence on record, the witness did not actively participate in the interrogation and assault of the deceased. He stuck to his mandate of taking down notes and there was no evidence which showed that he had an intention to assist the appellants in assaulting the deceased. Consequently, counsel for the respondent prayed that the appeal be dismissed in its entirety.

    ISSUE FOR DETERMINATION

    It seems from the grounds of appeal and arguably made before the court that there is whether or not the court a quo erred in finding the appellants guilty of culpable homicide in terms of s 49 of the Act.

    APPLICATION OF THE LAW TO THE FACTS

    What can be gleaned from the judgment of the court a quo is that the appellants’ conviction was a result of an objective assessment of all the evidence upon which the court a quo made several findings of fact. These are that; Madyara was not a suspect witness, that his testimony was credible and could be relied on. The trial court also concluded that the evidence led did not show that Madyara had acted in common purpose with the accused persons and as such, he could not be regarded as an accomplice witness. The court a quo went on to highlight the inconsistencies evident in the appellants’ testimonies and it found that the inconsistencies in the appellants’ testimonies corroborated Madyara’s testimony as to what transpired during the interrogations.

    It is trite that an appellate court is loath to interfere with the findings of fact made by the trial court unless the findings are grossly unreasonable. This position was articulated by this Court in Hama v National Railways of Zimbabwe 1996 (1) ZLR 664 (S), wherein it held that:

    “The general rule of the law, as regards irrationality, is that an appellate court will not interfere with a decision of a trial court based purely on a finding of fact unless it is satisfied that, having regard to the evidence placed before the trial court, the finding complained of is so outrageous in its defiance of logic or of accepted moral standards that no sensible person who had applied his mind to the question to be decided could have arrived at such a conclusion.”

    It is not enough to merely aver that another court would have arrived at a different conclusion on the same set of facts. One must go beyond that to prove that the court in making its decision had taken leave of its senses and therefore the finding is irrational. The finding that Mudyara was not an accomplice witness cannot be faulted as in reaching that finding, the judgment of the court a quo shows that it was alive to the principles that ought to be applied when dealing with evidence of an accomplice. In Zimbabwe Banking Corporation Limited v Ndlovu SC 61/04, the Court held that:

    “It is trite that evidence is not rejected simply because it is given by an accomplice or a suspect witness. Rather, the court has to examine the evidence before deciding whether it is believable. If necessary, corroborative evidence then has to be sought.”

    In casu, the court found Madyara’s role to be that of taking notes following an instruction from the appellants who were his superiors in the police force. It also found his evidence to be believable and was corroborated by the inconsistencies in the appellants’ evidence. In my view, the findings of fact made by the trial court in concluding that the evidence of Madyara was credible cannot be faulted. The appellants have failed to demonstrate before this Court that the finding is so grossly unreasonable such as to warrant an interference by this Court.

    More importantly, the conviction of the appellants predicated on single evidence witness is permissible at law. Section 269 of Act permits a court to convict a person on the single evidence of a competent and credible witness. In R v Mokoena 1932 OPD 79 at 80, it was stated that the evidence of such a single witness must be found to be “clear and satisfactory in every material respect”. Hence, a court can make a decision to convict based on evidence of a single witness provided that it is reliable, credible and the court is convinced beyond a reasonable doubt that the sole witness has spoken the truth.

    The court a quo was convinced that Madyara told the truth based on his demeanour in the witness box and the detailed narration he gave of the assault that was perpetrated on the deceased by the appellants. The Court has noted that the ultimate finding by the court a quo that Madyara was a credible witness was not impugned in the appellants grounds of appeal placed before this Court. The Court therefore finds no basis of interfering with the finding that Madyara was a credible witness and the weight that was attached to his evidence by the court a quo. Further to that, Madyara’s evidence was corroborated by the post mortem report which concluded that the deceased died as a result of trauma from assault, among other causes of his death. The holding of the Court in S v Banana 2001 (1) ZLR 607 (S) is instructive where it remarked as follows:

    “Where the evidence of the single witness is corroborated in any way which tends to indicate that the whole story was not concocted, the caution enjoined may be overcome and acceptance facilitated. But corroboration is not essential. Any other feature which increases the confidence of the court in the reliability of the single witness may also overcome the caution.”

    The Court takes the view that the finding that Madyara was a credible witness, considered in the light of proven facts and probabilities, particularly the post mortem report, all lead to an inescapable conclusion that the appellants caused the death of the deceased.

    DISPOSITION

    It follows from the foregoing that this appeal is without merit and ought to fail. It is accordingly ordered as follows:

    “The appeal be and is hereby dismissed.”

    GOWORA JA : I agree

    BERE JA : I agree

    Nyoni Advocates’ Chambers, appellant's legal practitioners

    The National Prosecuting Authority, respondent’s legal practitioners
judges:
Panel: GOWORA JA, GUVAVA JA & BERE JA
    Judgment by: GUVAVA JA
    Concurrence: GOWORA JA and BERE JA agreed.
subject_area: Criminal law; Culpable homicide; Evidence; Appeals; Police misconduct
catchwords_flynote: Criminal appeal – culpable homicide – death following police assault during interrogation – reliance on evidence of single witness – alleged suspect or accomplice witness – appellate interference with factual findings – credibility findings by trial court – corroboration by post-mortem evidence – appeal against conviction and sentence dismissed.
headnote:
The appellants, police officers, were convicted of culpable homicide after a suspect died following assaults during interrogation. Their appeal attacked the trial court’s reliance on the evidence of Madyara, a police constable who had been present during the interrogation. They argued that he was a suspect or accomplice witness and that the court a quo failed to treat his evidence with caution.

    The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal. It held that an appellate court will not interfere with a trial court’s factual findings unless they are grossly unreasonable. The trial court had considered Madyara’s role, found that he merely took notes under instruction from superior officers, and accepted his evidence as credible. The inconsistencies in the appellants’ own evidence and the post-mortem report corroborated his account.

    A conviction may be based on the evidence of a single competent and credible witness. The appellants failed to show that the trial court’s factual and credibility findings were irrational or outrageous in defiance of logic.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the court a quo erred in treating Madyara as a credible witness rather than as a suspect or accomplice witness.
    Whether the trial court’s factual findings justified appellate interference.
    Whether the appellants were properly convicted of culpable homicide.
    Whether the sentence should stand.
legal_principles:
An appellate court is slow to interfere with factual findings by a trial court and will do so only where the finding is grossly unreasonable or irrational.

    Evidence is not rejected merely because it comes from an accomplice or suspect witness. The court must examine the evidence and, where necessary, seek corroboration.

    A conviction may rest on the evidence of a single competent and credible witness if the evidence is reliable, satisfactory in material respects, and satisfies the court beyond reasonable doubt. Corroboration facilitates acceptance but is not always essential.
held_decision_points:
The trial court did not err in finding that Madyara was not an accomplice. His role was found to be limited to taking notes under instruction from superior officers.

    Madyara’s evidence was credible and was corroborated by the appellants’ inconsistencies and by the post-mortem report indicating trauma from assault.

    The appellants failed to demonstrate that the trial court’s findings were grossly unreasonable.

    The appeal lacked merit and was dismissed.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio decidendi is that, where a trial court has made reasoned credibility findings supported by the record and corroborative evidence, an appellate court will not interfere merely because the appellants seek to characterise a key witness as suspect or accomplice. A conviction for culpable homicide may stand on credible single-witness evidence corroborated by objective medical evidence.
