document_name: SC 59 - 22 Delta Beverages vs Blakey Investments.docx
case_name:
DELTA BEVERAGES (PRIVATE) LIMITED
    v
    BLAKEY INVESTMENTS (PRIVATE) LIMITED
judges:
Panel: BHUNU JA, MAKONI JA & CHITAKUNYE AJA
    Judgment by: CHITAKUNYE AJA
    Concurrence: BHUNU JA and MAKONI JA agreed.
subject_area: Contract; Corporate authority; Private international law; Competition law; Exchange control; Public policy
catchwords_flynote: Contract – supply agreement with foreign supplier – ostensible authority of supply-chain director – Turquand rule – internal corporate approvals – choice of South African law – directly applicable Zimbabwean statutes – Competition Act and alleged exclusivity – Exchange Control Regulations – severability – party seeking to rely on its own alleged non-compliance to avoid contractual obligations – appeal dismissed.
headnote:
The appellant sought a declaratur that a written supply agreement with the respondent was void, invalid and of no legal effect. It alleged that its supply-chain director lacked authority, internal approval procedures were not followed, the agreement breached the Competition Act, Exchange Control Regulations and import-control laws, and the agreement improperly selected South African law. The High Court dismissed the application.

    The Supreme Court dismissed the appeal. It held that Cynthia Malaba, the appellant’s Supply Chain Director, had ostensible authority to conclude the agreement. The respondent had previously dealt with her and other senior procurement officials, and the appellant’s internal processes could not be invoked against an external contracting party where the circumstances created apparent authority. The Turquand rule protected outsiders dealing with company representatives in circumstances appearing regular.

    The Court further held that the alleged statutory breaches did not render the agreement void. The exclusivity clause was not shown to be unlawful and, in any event, could be severed. The payment clause did not require payment outside Zimbabwe, so no exchange-control breach was proved. The appellant had warranted compliance with necessary laws and could not rely on its own alleged failure to obtain approvals or permits after receiving and consuming goods. Public policy favoured sanctity of contract and did not permit a party to avoid payment by invoking its own alleged default.
issues_for_determination:
Whether Cynthia Malaba had actual or ostensible authority to bind the appellant.
    Whether failure to comply with internal approval processes invalidated the contract as against the respondent.
    Whether the agreement breached Zimbabwean laws, including the Competition Act, Exchange Control Regulations and import-control provisions.
    Whether the choice of South African law displaced directly applicable Zimbabwean statutes.
    Whether public policy permitted the appellant to avoid payment after receiving and consuming goods.
legal_principles:
Ostensible authority exists where the principal’s conduct leads a reasonable third party to believe that the agent is authorised. The principal is estopped from denying authority where it created that appearance.

    The Turquand rule presumes regularity of a company’s internal procedures in favour of outsiders dealing with company representatives.

    Parties to an international contract may choose governing law, but directly applicable statutes of the forum, such as mandatory exchange-control rules, apply according to their terms irrespective of the chosen law.

    An offending contractual clause may be severed where it is subsidiary and the parties would have contracted without it.

    Public policy supports freedom and sanctity of contract and generally prevents a party from relying on its own default or illegality to escape obligations after receiving contractual benefits.
held_decision_points:
The appellant’s supply-chain director had ostensible authority. Senior staff knew of the agreement, prior similar agreements had been signed and performed, and the respondent was entitled to rely on the apparent authority.

    The respondent was not affected by alleged irregularities in the appellant’s internal processes.

    The exclusivity clause did not, on the evidence, prevent the appellant from buying from others and was severable if necessary.

    No exchange-control breach was proved because the contract did not require payment outside Zimbabwe and no nominated South African account was shown.

    The appellant could not rely on its own alleged failure to obtain approvals or import permits after warranting compliance and receiving goods.

    The appeal was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio decidendi is that a company may be bound by a contract concluded by an employee with ostensible authority where the company’s conduct and previous dealings reasonably represented authority to the counterparty. Alleged internal irregularities or the company’s own statutory non-compliance cannot be used, after receipt of contractual benefits, to avoid payment where no illegality going to the core of the contract is proved and any offending clause is severable.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted that in international contracts mandatory forum statutes may override a governing-law clause, but held that no breach sufficient to invalidate the contract had been proved.

Joubert v Enslin 1910 AD 6
    Infrastructure Development Bank of Zimbabwe v Engen Petroleum Zimbabwe (Private) Limited SC 16/20
    Makate v Vodacom Ltd 2016 (4) SA 121 (CC)
    Hely-Hutchinson CA v Brayhead Ltd and Anor [1968] 1 QB 549 (CA)
    Govero v Ordeco (Private) Limited and another SC 25/14
    Andrew Mills v Tanganda Tea Company Limited HH 12/13

document_name: SC 6 - 22 JOEL SIMON SILONDA VS VUSUMUZI NKOMO(1).docx
case_name:
JOEL SIMON SILONDA (SUBSTITUTED BY EXECUTOR VUSUMUZI THOMAS SILONDA)
    V
    VUSUMUZI NKOMO
court:
“4. The prescribed rate of interest in respect of the amount in para 3 shall be with effect from 2 May 2019.

      5. The respondent shall vacate the immovable property within two weeks of the payment of the judgment debt together with interest thereon at the prescribed rate failing which the Sheriff or his Deputy shall evict him from the immovable property.”

    GUVAVA JA: I agree

    UCHENA JA: I agree

    Masiye-Moyo and Associates, appellant’s legal practitioners

    Calderwood, Bryce Hendrie & Partners, respondent’s legal practitioners
judges:
Panel: GUVAVA JA, UCHENA JA AND KUDYA AJA
    Judgment by: KUDYA AJA
    Concurrence: GUVAVA JA and UCHENA JA agreed.
subject_area: Contract; Property; Unjust enrichment; Civil procedure; Appeal and review
catchwords_flynote: Contract – illegal sale of unsubdivided land – ss 39(1)(i) and 40 of Regional, Town and Country Planning Act – unjust enrichment following void agreement – adequacy of pleadings and cure by evidence – relaxation of in pari delicto rule – equitable assessment of useful improvements by depreciated replacement value – improvement lien – conditional eviction – commencement of interest – partial appeal.
headnote:
The parties concluded an agreement for the sale of a 10-acre portion of the appellant’s land without a subdivision permit. The respondent paid most of the purchase price, took occupation and constructed substantial improvements. The parties accepted that the sale was illegal and void under the Regional, Town and Country Planning Act. The High Court upheld the respondent’s counterclaim for unjust enrichment, awarded $125 000 based on the depreciated replacement value of the land and improvements, ordered interest from 24 June 2015 and recognised an improvement lien, but omitted the contemplated conditional eviction from its operative order.

    The Supreme Court held that the respondent’s pleadings, read as a whole, disclosed an unjust-enrichment claim for the improvements. In any event, the issue had been identified at the pre-trial conference and fully canvassed in evidence, so any defect was cured without prejudice. The respondent proved enrichment on a balance of probabilities. The in pari delicto rule is not inflexible and may be relaxed to prevent injustice and unwind the consequences of an illegal transaction rather than enforce it. In Zimbabwe, a court has a broad equitable discretion to assess the value of enrichment, including by depreciated replacement value and with regard to prevailing economic and monetary conditions. The High Court’s assessment was not shown to be an improper exercise of discretion.

    Interest on an enrichment award assessed at judgment should run from the date of judgment, not from the date of the counterclaim. A bona fide improver’s lien confers a right of retention until compensation is paid, after which the owner is entitled to eviction. The appeal therefore succeeded only as to the commencement of interest and the omitted conditional-eviction order. Each party bore its own costs on appeal.
issues_for_determination:
1. Whether the respondent pleaded and proved unjust enrichment in respect of the improvements.
    2. Whether any pleading deficiency was capable of being cured by the pre-trial issues and evidence fully canvassed at trial.
    3. Whether the High Court properly relaxed the in pari delicto rule and whether the compensation based on depreciated replacement value improperly enforced the illegal agreement or constituted contractual damages.
    4. When interest on the enrichment award should commence.
    5. Whether a conditional eviction order should have been made and whether the costs order a quo was appropriate.
legal_principles:
A cause of action is the factual conspectus which entitles a litigant to a remedy. Pleadings must be read as a whole. An issue not precisely pleaded may be adjudicated where it was identified for trial, fully canvassed in evidence and the opposing party suffered no prejudice.
    The general requirements of unjust enrichment are enrichment of the defendant, impoverishment of the plaintiff, enrichment at the plaintiff’s expense and absence of legal justification, subject to the recognised limits on enrichment actions. Whether enrichment exists is a factual question.
    Courts will not enforce an illegal agreement under ex turpi causa. The allied in pari delicto rule ordinarily leaves loss where it falls, but may be relaxed in the interests of public policy, justice and equity to prevent unjust enrichment and release parties from the harmful consequences of the illegal transaction.
    Compensation for enrichment is restorative, not contractual damages. A court has a broad equitable discretion. A bona fide occupier may recover for necessary and useful improvements, with usefulness assessed objectively by added value. The usual lesser-of-enrichment-and-impoverishment ceiling may be applied with attention to use, occupation and prevailing economic and monetary conditions; Zimbabwean authority permits assessment by current or depreciated replacement value where that is necessary to achieve a fair result.
    An improvement lien is a real right of retention until the value due is paid. Conditional eviction should follow payment. Interest on an enrichment amount assessed at judgment ordinarily runs from the judgment date.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the respondent’s pleadings, read in context, disclosed an unjust-enrichment claim for the improvements and contained the material facts supporting that cause.
    Held, further, that any pleading defect would in any event have been cured because unjust enrichment was an issue at the pre-trial conference and was fully canvassed at trial without prejudice to the appellant.
    Held, further, that the respondent proved unjust enrichment on a balance of probabilities and the High Court properly relaxed the in pari delicto rule to undo the consequences of the illegal sale rather than enforce it.
    Held, further, that the High Court’s use of depreciated replacement value fell within its broad equitable discretion and was not shown to be improper.
    Held, further, that interest on the award should run from 2 May 2019, the date of the High Court judgment.
    Held, further, that the respondent’s improvement lien entitled him to retain occupation until payment, after which he was required to vacate and could be evicted.
    Held, further, that the appeal succeeded only in part; each party was to bear its own costs on appeal.
ratio_decidendi: The respondent was entitled to an enrichment remedy because the material facts establishing the appellant’s gain and the respondent’s corresponding impoverishment from fixed improvements were pleaded and fully tried. Relaxation of in pari delicto did not enforce the void sale; it prevented the appellant from retaining an unjust benefit. The High Court could equitably assess that benefit by depreciated replacement value. Because the amount was assessed at judgment and the respondent held an improvement lien, interest had to run from the judgment date and eviction had to be conditional upon payment.

document_name: SC 60 - 22 JOHN ALEXANDER CAMERON vs MARY MURDOCH HOWSON.docx
SC 62 - 22 TAFADZWA MUSHUNJE V ASSOCIATED NEWSPAPERS.docx
case_name:
TAFADZWA MUSHUNJE
    v
    ASSOCIATED NEWSPAPERS OF ZIMBABWE (PRIVATE) LIMITED
judges:
Panel: GUVAVA JA, MAKONI JA & CHITAKUNYE JA
    Judgment by: MAKONI JA
    Concurrence: GUVAVA JA and CHITAKUNYE JA agreed.
subject_area: Delict; Defamation; Civil procedure; Pleadings; Appeals and review
catchwords_flynote: Defamation – pleading requirements – plaintiff required to identify the particular words complained of or words to substantially the same effect – declaration referring generally to contents of article – failure to particularise defamatory passages and damages – defective pleadings not cured by evidence at trial – appellate disposal where point in limine upheld after full trial – review powers of Supreme Court – claim struck off rather than dismissed.
headnote:
The appellant sued the respondent newspaper for damages arising from an article reporting that she had appeared in court on allegations of deliberately infecting a child with HIV and assaulting him. The criminal charges had later been withdrawn after medical reports showed that both the appellant and the child were not HIV positive. The High Court found that the elements of defamation had been established but upheld the respondent’s defence of qualified privilege and dismissed the claim.

    On appeal, the respondent raised a point in limine that the appellant’s declaration was fatally defective because it did not plead the specific defamatory words complained of and did not particularise the damages claimed under separate heads. The Supreme Court upheld the objection. In defamation proceedings, a claimant must identify the particular words or passages relied upon, or words substantially to that effect, so that the defendant and the court know the case to be met. The appellant’s pleading referred only to the “contents” of the article and attributed meanings to the publication without identifying the passages complained of. Her evidence at trial also showed uncertainty about the basis and quantification of the claim.

    The Court held that the defective declaration was fatal and that the High Court should not have entered the merits. Because the point raised was procedural, the proper disposition was not to dismiss the claim on the merits. Invoking its review powers, the Supreme Court set aside the High Court proceedings and substituted an order striking the claim off the roll with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the appellant’s declaration disclosed a proper cause of action in defamation.
    Whether defects in the pleading were cured by evidence led at trial.
    Whether the High Court should have determined the merits despite the defective pleadings.
    What order the Supreme Court should make after upholding the procedural objection.
legal_principles:
Pleadings define the issues between litigants and bind both the parties and the court. A party may not rely on a case different from that pleaded unless the pleading is properly amended.

    In a defamation claim, the claimant must plead the words complained of, or at least words substantially to that effect, and must identify the defamatory publication or passages relied upon with sufficient precision. The defendant is entitled to know whether the whole publication or specific passages are impugned, the defamatory meanings alleged, and the heads of damages claimed.

    A defectively pleaded claim is not necessarily dismissed on exception if it may be cured by amendment. Where a court has wrongly determined a matter on fatally defective pleadings, the Supreme Court may invoke review powers under s 25(2) of the Supreme Court Act and substitute an appropriate procedural order.
held_decision_points:
The declaration was defective because it did not state whether the whole article or particular portions were defamatory, did not set out the actual words or words to that effect, and did not particularise the heads or quantum of damages.

    The deficiencies were not cured by evidence. The appellant’s evidence confirmed uncertainty about which words were complained of and how the damages were calculated.

    The High Court should not have determined the merits of the defamation claim. The respondent’s point in limine succeeded.

    Because the Court resolved the matter on a procedural issue, the claim was to be struck off the roll rather than dismissed on the merits. The High Court proceedings were set aside under the Supreme Court’s review powers.
ratio_decidendi: The ratio decidendi is that a defamation declaration which fails to identify the particular words or passages complained of, or words substantially to the same effect, and fails to particularise the damages claimed, does not properly disclose the case to be met. Evidence at trial cannot cure a fatally defective pleading where the party’s case was never properly pleaded. In those circumstances the court should not determine the merits; the proper order is to strike the claim off the roll, with review intervention where necessary.

document_name: SC 63 - 22 Reverend Clement Nyathi vs The Trustees of the Time Being of Apostolic Faith Mission.docx
SC 64 - 22 ELIZABERTH CHIDZAMBWA V MINISTRY OF LOCAL GOVERNMENT.docx
case_name:
ELIZABETH CHIDZAMBWA
    v
    MINISTER OF LOCAL GOVERNMENT PUBLIC WORKS AND NATIONAL HOUSING
judges:
Panel: BHUNU JA, MATHONSI JA & KUDYA JA
    Judgment by: BHUNU JA
    Concurrence: MATHONSI JA and KUDYA JA agreed.
subject_area: Property; Prescription; Civil procedure; State land; Eviction
catchwords_flynote: Property – eviction from government pool house – former civil servant occupying State house after failed purchase – alleged agreement of sale not proved – prescription – distinction between extinctive prescription and acquisition of ownership – Prescription Act [Chapter 8:11], ss 15 and 16 – no debt or ownership cause of action upon which appellant could rely – holding over damages – punitive costs for abuse of process.
headnote:
The appellant was a former government employee who had been allocated an institutional government pool house by virtue of her employment. After she ceased to be a civil servant, the respondent offered to sell the house to her as sitting tenant. The respondent alleged that she failed to accept the offer validly and failed to pay the required purchase price. The respondent sued in the Magistrates Court for eviction and holding over damages. The appellant pleaded lis pendens and prescription and asserted that she had bought the house and paid the full purchase price. The Magistrates Court granted the respondent’s claim and the High Court dismissed the appellant’s appeal with punitive costs.

    On further appeal, the Supreme Court held that the appellant’s own evidence defeated her case. She admitted under cross-examination that she had not signed an agreement of sale with the respondent. She also acknowledged the respondent as owner and accepted that she remained liable for rent pending revaluation. In those circumstances she had no lawful basis to claim ownership.

    The Court held that the appellant’s reliance on prescription was misplaced. The issue of prescription could not arise in respect of a non-existent sale debt or ownership right, and the appellant could not use extinctive prescription as a means to acquire ownership. Acquisitive prescription also did not arise because she occupied the property in recognition of the respondent’s ownership, not adversely as owner. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the respondent’s claim had prescribed.
    Whether the appellant had acquired ownership or lawful authority to occupy the house.
    Whether the appellant was liable for eviction and holding over damages.
    Whether the High Court erred in upholding punitive costs.
legal_principles:
Prescription under the Prescription Act begins to run when a debt is due. Where no sale agreement or debt giving rise to ownership exists, there is no basis on which prescription can begin to run in favour of a party asserting ownership.

    Extinctive prescription extinguishes a personal obligation or right; it is not a mode of acquiring ownership. Acquisitive prescription, by contrast, requires possession as if owner for the prescribed period. Occupation pursuant to a lease or other legal relationship that recognises another’s ownership is not adverse possession and cannot ground acquisition by prescription.

    A court of appeal will not interfere with a punitive costs order where the lower court properly exercised its discretion and the party’s conduct amounts to an abuse of process or warrants censure.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the appellant’s admission that she had not signed a contract of sale with the respondent divested her of any claim to ownership of the house.

    Held, further, that prescription could not begin to run over a non-existent debt or cause of action, and the question whether a vindication claim is a debt liable to prescription was irrelevant on the facts.

    Held, further, that the appellant’s occupation recognised the respondent’s ownership and was therefore not occupation as owner for purposes of acquisitive prescription.

    Held, further, that the appellant was liable for eviction and holding over damages because she had no sale agreement, had admitted rental liability, and occupied the house unlawfully.

    Held, further, that the punitive costs order was justified by the appellant’s dishonest and intransigent conduct, which wasted court time and prolonged her unlawful occupation.
ratio_decidendi: The appeal failed because the appellant could not establish any lawful basis for ownership or continued occupation. Her own evidence showed that no sale agreement existed and that she recognised the respondent’s ownership and her continuing rental obligation. In the absence of a valid sale or adverse possession as owner, prescription could not assist her, and the respondent was entitled to eviction, holding over damages and costs.

document_name: SC 65 - 22 DELTA BEVERAGES PVT LTD VS SPIWE MAPURANGA.docx
SC 66 - 22 Edmore Madhume vs The State (2).docx
case_name:
EDMORE MADHUME
    v
    THE STATE
judges:
Panel: BHUNU JA, MATHONSI JA & CHIWESHE JA
    Judgment by: CHIWESHE JA
    Concurrence: BHUNU JA and MATHONSI JA agreed.
subject_area: Criminal law; Criminal procedure; Fraud; Theft; Evidence; Sentence
catchwords_flynote: Criminal law – fraud – competent verdict of theft – Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23], ss 113, 136, 274 and 275 – employee receiving trust money and failing to account – direct evidence and credibility findings – partial success against conviction – fraud conviction corrected to theft – sentence upheld but restitution amount corrected.
headnote:
The appellant, an operations manager at Webbles Shipping Company, was convicted by the Magistrates Court of nine counts of fraud after funds from the company’s ZIMRA subsidiary account were used to pay customs duty for clients and the company was not reimbursed. The trial court sentenced him to four and a half years’ imprisonment, with part suspended on conditions including restitution of US$39 413.37. The High Court dismissed his appeal against conviction and sentence, but held that the facts proved theft rather than fraud.

    On appeal to the Supreme Court, the appellant challenged the use of a competent verdict, the quality of the evidence, and sentence. The Court held that, under the Code, theft is a competent verdict to fraud. The facts established that the appellant received or caused receipt of company funds and failed to account for them. The Court rejected the argument that the conviction rested only on circumstantial evidence, noting direct evidence from employees and clients and the trial court’s credibility findings.

    The appeal against conviction succeeded only to the extent that the appellant had to be formally found not guilty of fraud and guilty of theft as a competent verdict. The State conceded that one alleged deposit of US$4 621.63 was not proved and the prejudice was reduced to US$34 791.74. The appeal against sentence was dismissed, but the restitution amount reflected in the sentence was substituted with US$34 791.74.
issues_for_determination:
Whether theft is a competent verdict on a charge of fraud under the Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act.
    Whether the conviction rested on direct evidence or only on circumstantial evidence.
    Whether the trial court and High Court were entitled to accept the credibility of the State witnesses.
    Whether the amount of prejudice and restitution required correction.
    Whether the sentence imposed warranted appellate interference.
legal_principles:
Section 274 of the Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act permits conviction for another crime where the essential elements of the crime charged include the essential elements of the other crime, provided the facts proved establish that other crime and the crime charged is not proved. Section 275 and the Fourth Schedule provide that theft is a permissible verdict on a charge of fraud.

    A court may convict of a competent verdict where the evidence proves the lesser or alternative offence. Where a court convicts on a competent verdict, it should also enter an acquittal on the main charge that has not been proved.

    An appellate court does not lightly interfere with credibility findings of a trial court. Direct evidence of receipt and failure to account for money is distinguishable from a conviction resting solely on circumstantial inference.

    Appellate interference with sentence is justified only where there is a material misdirection or the sentence is disturbingly inappropriate. A proved reduction in prejudice may justify correction of the restitution component without disturbing the custodial sentence.
held_decision_points:
Held, that theft is a competent verdict to fraud under ss 274 and 275 of the Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act and the Fourth Schedule.

    Held, further, that the High Court correctly held that the facts proved did not establish fraud but did establish theft, because the appellant received trust money and failed to account for it.

    Held, further, that the conviction did not rest solely on circumstantial evidence. There was direct evidence that the complainant’s ZIMRA account was debited and that the appellant received the missing funds directly or through employees.

    Held, further, that the appellant should have been expressly found not guilty of fraud before being found guilty of theft as a competent verdict.

    Held, further, that the State’s concession about the unproved alleged deposit into Lindiwe’s account required the prejudice to be reduced from US$39 413.37 to US$34 791.74.

    Held, further, that there was no basis to interfere with the sentence, save for substituting the corrected restitution amount.
ratio_decidendi: The appellant’s conviction was sustainable not for fraud but for theft because the facts proved showed that he received or controlled company funds held for a specific purpose and failed to account for them. Theft is a statutory competent verdict on a charge of fraud. The proven prejudice excluded the unproved Lindiwe deposit, reducing the amount to US$34 791.74, but the evidence otherwise proved theft beyond reasonable doubt and disclosed no basis for interfering with the custodial sentence.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: SC 67 - 22 MINISTER OF JUSTICE v MUSKWE AND 4 ORS - Copy.docx
case_name:
MINISTER OF JUSTICE, LEGAL AND PARLIAMENTARY AFFAIRS
    v
    (1) JULIANA MUSKWE (2) NTOMBIZODWA MASUKU (3) EDWARD TOME (4) ANDREW LAWSON (5) MESSINA INVESTMENTS LIMITED
judges:
Panel: GWAUNZA DCJ, MATHONSI JA & KUDYA JA
    Judgment by: KUDYA JA
    Concurrence: GWAUNZA DCJ and MATHONSI JA agreed.
subject_area: Company law; Insolvency and corporate rescue; Administrative law; Statutory interpretation; Civil procedure
catchwords_flynote: Company law – State-indebted insolvent companies – reconstruction order – confirmation proceedings – Reconstruction of State-Indebted Insolvent Companies Act [Chapter 24:27], ss 2(a), 4 and 8 – Companies Act [Chapter 24:03], s 191 scheme of arrangement – Minister issuing reconstruction order outside 30-day statutory window after application for sanctioning scheme – peremptory statutory bar – invalid reconstruction order – effect of unregistered sanctioned scheme – court orders to be obeyed until set aside – application struck off rather than dismissed.
headnote:
The appellant issued a reconstruction order against Hwange Colliery Company Ltd under the Reconstruction of State-Indebted Insolvent Companies Act and applied to the High Court for confirmation. Before the reconstruction order, Hwange’s creditors and shareholders had approved a scheme of arrangement under s 191 of the Companies Act, and the High Court had sanctioned the scheme. The respondents, including directors, the scheme trustee and a shareholder, opposed confirmation. The High Court refused confirmation and dismissed the application with costs, holding among other things that the reconstruction order violated s 2(a) of the Act because it had been issued long after the 30-day period following presentation of the scheme application.

    On appeal, the central question was whether the High Court erred in refusing to confirm the reconstruction order. The Supreme Court held that s 2(a) of the Act was dispositive. Once an application for sanctioning a scheme of arrangement had been made to the High Court, the Minister could issue a reconstruction order only within 30 days of the presentation of that application. The scheme application had been filed on 3 May 2017 and sanctioned on 10 May 2017. The reconstruction order was issued on 26 October 2018, 541 days after the scheme application, and was therefore made outside the statutory window.

    The Court rejected the appellant’s alternative arguments that the scheme had been abandoned or that non-registration rendered it ineffectual. The founding affidavit had proceeded on the existence of a functioning scheme, and a party could not approbate and reprobate. Failure to register did not discharge the sanctioning order; court orders must be obeyed until set aside. The appeal was dismissed. However, because s 8(4) of the Act contemplated a fresh application on new evidence or correction of a mistake, the Supreme Court set aside the High Court’s dismissal order and substituted an order striking the application off the roll with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the High Court erred in refusing to confirm the reconstruction order.
    Whether s 2(a) of the Reconstruction of State-Indebted Insolvent Companies Act barred the Minister from issuing a reconstruction order more than 30 days after the filing of an application for sanctioning a scheme of arrangement.
    Whether non-registration of the sanctioned scheme under s 191(3) of the Companies Act removed the statutory bar or rendered the scheme ineffective.
    Whether the scheme had been abandoned.
    Whether the High Court should have dismissed the confirmation application or struck it off the roll.
    Whether costs should follow the result.
legal_principles:
Section 2(a) of the Reconstruction of State-Indebted Insolvent Companies Act excludes a State-indebted company from the Act where an application for the sanctioning of a compromise or arrangement under s 191 of the Companies Act has been made or presented, unless the Minister issues a reconstruction order within 30 days of that application. The provision is peremptory.

    A judicially sanctioned scheme of arrangement is a court-recognised consensual contract binding creditors and the company by operation of law. Failure to register the scheme under s 191(3) of the Companies Act does not discharge the sanctioning order; the order remains in abeyance until registered or set aside by court order. Court orders bind the State and all persons and institutions to which they apply and must be obeyed.

    Actions done in contravention of peremptory statutory provisions are invalid. A litigant may not approbate and reprobate by advancing inconsistent positions. An applicant’s case is made in the founding affidavit. Facts not denied on affidavit are taken as admitted.

    Where the statutory scheme allows a fresh application to correct defects or rely on new evidence, striking off may be the proper order instead of final dismissal.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the proper construction and application of s 2(a) of the Reconstruction Act was dispositive of the appeal.

    Held, further, that once Hwange had presented an application under s 191(2) of the Companies Act for sanctioning a scheme of arrangement, the Minister could issue a reconstruction order only within 30 days of presentation of that application.

    Held, further, that the reconstruction order issued on 26 October 2018 was made 541 days after the presentation of the scheme application on 3 May 2017 and was therefore outside the statutory window and invalid.

    Held, further, that failure to register the sanctioned scheme did not discharge or nullify the High Court’s sanctioning order; the order remained effective until registered or set aside.

    Held, further, that the appellant failed to prove abandonment of the scheme and could not approbate and reprobate by contending both that the scheme was abandoned and that it never came into effect.

    Held, further, that the appeal had to be dismissed, but the High Court order should be corrected from dismissal to striking off because s 8(4) of the Act allowed the Minister to bring a fresh application on new evidence or to correct a mistake.
ratio_decidendi: The reconstruction order was invalid because s 2(a) of the Reconstruction Act imposed a peremptory 30-day window for issuing a reconstruction order once an application to sanction a scheme of arrangement had been presented. Hwange’s scheme application was presented on 3 May 2017, but the reconstruction order was issued on 26 October 2018. That contravention of a peremptory statutory bar meant the confirmation application could not stand. The sanctioned scheme order was not displaced by alleged non-registration or abandonment, and the only correction warranted on appeal was to substitute a striking-off order for the High Court’s dismissal order.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court censured the post-hearing attempt by the appellant’s erstwhile legal practitioners to place correspondence before the Court and to suggest that the Court had directed such a filing. The Court adopted remarks from Zimcor Trustees Ltd & Ors v Rushesha & Ors SC 22/15 and substituted Ms V. Munyoro for Advocate Mpofu in those remarks.

document_name: SC 7 - 22  POLICE SERVICE COMMISSION AND ANOR VS DHERERA MANYONI(1).doc
SC 70 - 22 LLOYD MANOKORE v LAW SOCIETY DRAFT JUDGMENT.docx
case_name:
LLOYD MANOKORE
    v
    LAW SOCIETY OF ZIMBABWE
judges:
Panel: MAKONI JA, MATHONSI JA & CHITAKUNYE JA
    Judgment by: CHITAKUNYE JA
    Concurrence: MAKONI JA and MATHONSI JA agreed.
subject_area: Legal practitioners and professional discipline; Trust funds; Suretyship; Contract; Civil procedure
catchwords_flynote: Legal practitioners – professional misconduct – conflict of interest – legal practitioner acting for creditor and sureties under power of attorney – sale of sureties’ immovable property without consent or court order – benefit of excussion – Contractual Penalties Act [Chapter 8:04], s 4 – trust funds – remitting balance of sale proceeds to creditor instead of property owners – misappropriation of trust funds – disciplinary penalty – deletion from register upheld.
headnote:
The appellant appealed against a decision of the Legal Practitioners Disciplinary Tribunal which found him guilty of unprofessional, dishonourable and unworthy conduct and ordered the deletion of his name from the register of Legal Practitioners, Notaries Public and Conveyancers. The complaint arose from the sale of immovable property belonging to Perkins and Lillian Zhawari. The property had been given as security for a credit facility extended by National Foods Operations Limited to Celgrim Bakeries. The appellant’s firm acted for NFOL and also relied on a power of attorney signed by the Zhawaris to sell the property when Celgrim Bakeries failed to pay. The appellant sold the property without a court order and remitted the balance of the purchase price to NFOL instead of the Zhawaris.

    The Supreme Court held that the power of attorney created an agency or attorney-client relationship between the complainants and the appellant’s firm, while the firm also acted for NFOL. That created a conflict of interest. The Zhawaris were sureties, not principal debtors or co-principal debtors, and there was no renunciation of the benefit of excussion. The appellant ought to have proceeded against the principal debtor before selling the sureties’ property. The Court also held that the complainants had not consented to the sale; absent such consent, the appellant required a court order, particularly because the sale enforced a penalty stipulation within the Contractual Penalties Act.

    The Court further held that the proceeds remaining after payment of the debt were trust funds payable to the complainants. Remitting them to NFOL without authority amounted to misappropriation of trust funds. The Tribunal had properly found professional misconduct and properly exercised its sentencing discretion. The appeal against conviction and sentence was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the Tribunal erred in finding that the power of attorney established an attorney-client or agency relationship between the complainants and the appellant’s firm.
    Whether the appellant acted in a conflict of interest by acting for NFOL and also as attorney or agent for the complainants.
    Whether it was proper to execute against the sureties’ property before suing the principal debtor.
    Whether the complainants’ consent or a court order was required before the property could be sold.
    Whether remitting the balance of the purchase price to NFOL amounted to abuse or misappropriation of trust funds.
    Whether the Tribunal properly exercised its sentencing discretion in ordering deletion from the register.
legal_principles:
An attorney-client relationship may be founded on mandate. A power of attorney is strictly construed and may create an agency relationship in which the attorney is required to act in the principal’s interests with due care.

    A legal practitioner must not act where there is an actual or potential conflict of interest. The duty applies even where the conflict appears likely to prejudice the client’s interests.

    Sureties enjoy the benefit of excussion unless they expressly or impliedly renounce it, including by binding themselves as surety and co-principal debtor. Where there is no renunciation, the creditor must first proceed against the principal debtor before turning to the surety.

    Transfer of immovable property ordinarily requires appropriate authority from the seller, including a power of attorney to pass transfer and a seller’s declaration. Absent consent, a party may not resort to self-help and must obtain a court order. Enforcement of a penalty stipulation falls within the Contractual Penalties Act.

    Trust money held by a legal practitioner must be used only for the purpose of the trust and must be available to the client or intended beneficiary when payable. Use for an unauthorised purpose amounts to misappropriation even if the practitioner does not convert the money to personal use.

    An appellate court is slow to interfere with a disciplinary tribunal’s penalty unless discretion was improperly exercised, wrong principles were applied, relevant considerations ignored, or the sentence induces a sense of shock.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the power of attorney established a mandate and agency relationship between the complainants and the appellant’s firm, and the appellant purported to act under that mandate.

    Held, further, that the appellant acted in a conflict of interest because his firm acted for NFOL while also purporting to act as attorney and agent for the complainants, whose interests were adverse to NFOL’s interests.

    Held, further, that the complainants were sureties, not principal debtors or co-principal debtors, and had not renounced the benefit of excussion. The appellant therefore ought to have proceeded against Celgrim Bakeries before executing against the complainants’ property.

    Held, further, that the complainants had not consented to the sale of their property and, absent consent, the appellant required a court order. The sale also enforced a penalty stipulation and should have been pursued through a competent court.

    Held, further, that the balance of the purchase price was trust money payable to the complainants. Remitting it to NFOL without authority amounted to misappropriation of trust funds.

    Held, further, that the Tribunal properly exercised its discretion in ordering deletion from the register and the appeal had no merit.
ratio_decidendi: The appellant’s disciplinary conviction and deregistration were upheld because he acted under a mandate that placed him in a conflict of interest, sold sureties’ immovable property without their consent or a court order and before proceeding against the principal debtor, and misapplied trust funds by remitting the balance of the sale proceeds to NFOL rather than to the property owners. Those acts constituted unprofessional, dishonourable and unworthy conduct, and the Tribunal’s penalty of deletion from the register was a proper exercise of discretion.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: SC 71 - 22 Billy Rigava & 5 Ors vs Estere Chivasa & Anor.docx
Sc 72 - 22 Leah Nachipo and Anor v Admire Maticha and Ors Draft (2).docx
case_name:
(1) LEAH NACHIPO (2) GERALD NACHIPO
    v
    (1) ADMIRE MATICHA (2) TREVOR MWARAMBA (3) CLEOPAS MANYIKA (4) LEWIS CHASAKARA (5) WONDER MUSHURE (6) CITY OF HARARE N.O.
judges:
Panel: GUVAVA JA, BHUNU JA & MUSAKWA JA
    Judgment by: GUVAVA JA
    Concurrence: BHUNU JA and MUSAKWA JA agreed.
subject_area: Administrative law; Planning law; Civil procedure; Appeals and review
catchwords_flynote: Administrative law – Administrative Court – appeal against interlocutory order – application for condonation and extension of time to appeal against planning permit – whether leave to appeal required – Administrative Court Act [Chapter 7:01], s 20 – Regional, Country and Town Planning Act [Chapter 29:12], ss 38 and 61 – s 61 limiting appeals to points of law not displacing leave requirement – appeal fatally defective and struck off.
headnote:
The appellants owned a property in Shawasha Hills, Gletwyn Township, Harare, which they used as a wedding and events venue. The first to fifth respondents, who were neighbours, complained to the City of Harare about noise and environmental disturbance. Despite a petition from residents, the City granted the appellants a permit to use the property as a wedding and events venue. The respondents sought condonation and extension of time in the Administrative Court to appeal against the grant of the permit. The Administrative Court granted that application.

    The appellants appealed to the Supreme Court. The respondents raised a point in limine that the order appealed against was interlocutory and that, under s 20(2) of the Administrative Court Act, the appellants required leave before appealing. The appellants argued that the appeal was governed by s 61 of the Regional, Country and Town Planning Act, which permits appeals on points of law, and that leave was therefore unnecessary.

    The Supreme Court upheld the point in limine. Section 20(2) of the Administrative Court Act is a procedural provision prohibiting appeals against interlocutory orders of the Administrative Court without leave, except where another enactment provides otherwise. Section 61 of the Regional, Country and Town Planning Act restricts the type of appeal that may be brought to the Supreme Court to specified legal questions and evidential-admissibility issues. It does not regulate leave to appeal and does not authorise an interlocutory appeal without leave. The appeal was therefore fatally defective and was struck off the roll with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the Administrative Court’s order granting condonation and extension of time was an interlocutory order.
    Whether s 20(2) of the Administrative Court Act required the appellants to seek leave before appealing against that interlocutory order.
    Whether s 61 of the Regional, Country and Town Planning Act displaced the leave requirement under s 20(2).
    Whether the appeal was properly before the Supreme Court.
legal_principles:
An appeal against an interlocutory order ordinarily requires leave. Section 20(2) of the Administrative Court Act gives statutory effect to that principle by prohibiting appeals from interlocutory orders or judgments of the Administrative Court without leave, unless another enactment provides otherwise.

    Where statutory language is clear and unambiguous, courts give the words their ordinary grammatical meaning. Where two statutes concern the same subject, they must be construed harmoniously where possible.

    Section 61 of the Regional, Country and Town Planning Act restricts appeals from the Administrative Court to points of law, questions whether a matter is one of fact or law, and questions of admissibility of evidence. It does not regulate leave to appeal and does not remove the procedural leave requirement for interlocutory appeals imposed by s 20(2) of the Administrative Court Act.

    The requirement of leave for interlocutory appeals filters unterminated proceedings and avoids injustice caused by unrestricted appeals from every ruling of a lower court.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the Administrative Court order granting condonation and extension of time to note an appeal was interlocutory and procedural because it did not resolve the real dispute between the parties.

    Held, further, that s 20(2) of the Administrative Court Act directly governed appeals from interlocutory orders of the Administrative Court and required leave before such an appeal could be noted.

    Held, further, that s 61 of the Regional, Country and Town Planning Act did not displace the leave requirement. It restricted appeals to questions of law and admissibility, but did not create an open right to appeal interlocutory orders without leave.

    Held, further, that the appellants’ failure to obtain leave rendered the appeal fatally defective and the matter had to be struck off the roll.
ratio_decidendi: The decisive reason for the order was that the appeal lay against an interlocutory order of the Administrative Court. Section 20(2) of the Administrative Court Act expressly prohibits appeals against interlocutory orders without leave unless another enactment provides otherwise. Section 61 of the Regional, Country and Town Planning Act does not address leave to appeal and therefore did not exempt the appellants from that mandatory requirement. Because no leave had been obtained, the appeal was fatally defective.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

case_name:
ZESA HOLDINGS (PRIVATE) LIMITED
    V
    OBSON MATUNJA
court:
“The preliminary objection that the appellant has no right of appeal to the Labour Court is hereby dismissed.”
    3. The matter is remitted to the Labour Court for continuation.
    4. The registrar of this Court is directed to issue a corrigendum removing the judgment Pioneer Transport v Douglas Mafikeni SC 45/17 (2017 (2) ZLR 71) from the list of Supreme Court judgments and asserting that the authentic judgment of this Court is Pioneer Transport v Douglas Mafikeni SC 65/18.
judges:
Panel: MATHONSI JA, KUDYA JA & MWAYERA JA
    Judgment by: MATHONSI JA
    Concurrence: KUDYA JA and MWAYERA JA agreed.
subject_area: Labour and employment law; Statutory interpretation; Practice and procedure; Appeals; Judicial precedent
catchwords_flynote: Labour law - disciplinary proceedings - employer's right of appeal to Labour Court - s 92D of Labour Act [Chapter 28:01] - s 8(6) of Labour (National Employment Code of Conduct) Regulations, S.I. 15/2006 - meaning of 'person' and 'person or party aggrieved' - employer included - fake Supreme Court judgment - Pioneer Transport v Mafikeni SC 45/17 not authentic - authentic judgment SC 65/18 - matter remitted to Labour Court.
headnote:
The respondent, an employee of the appellant, was charged with misconduct relating to fuel and alleged gross inefficiency. An independent disciplinary authority dismissed the first two charges and issued a caution and discharge on the alternative charge. The appellant appealed to the Labour Court. The respondent objected that an employer has no right of appeal against the determination of a disciplinary authority appointed by it. The Labour Court upheld that objection and struck the appeal off the roll, relying on Pioneer Transport v Mafikeni 2017 (2) ZLR 71 (S), a reported judgment that appeared to conflict with Pioneer Transport v Mafikeni SC 65/18.

    The Supreme Court held that the 2017 Pioneer Transport 'judgment' was not a judgment of the Court. It had not been handed down or signed by the appeal panel and had no binding effect. The valid judgment was SC 65/18. The Court then interpreted s 92D of the Labour Act and s 8(6) of the National Employment Code. The ordinary meaning of 'a person who is aggrieved' and 'a person or party who is aggrieved' includes an employer. There was no statutory basis for restricting the right of appeal to employees. The appeal was allowed, with each party bearing its own costs because the conflicting reported judgments had created the need for clarification. The Labour Court's decision was set aside, the preliminary objection was dismissed, and the matter was remitted to the Labour Court for continuation. A corrigendum was directed in relation to the false Pioneer Transport judgment.
issues_for_determination:
Whether an employer is entitled under s 92D of the Labour Act and s 8(6) of the National Employment Code to appeal to the Labour Court against a disciplinary authority's decision.
    Which of the two conflicting Pioneer Transport judgments was authentic and binding.
    Whether the Labour Court erred in upholding the preliminary objection that the appellant had no right of appeal.
    What costs order was appropriate in light of the conflicting reported judgments.
legal_principles:
The ordinary grammatical meaning of clear statutory words must be applied unless it leads to absurdity, repugnance, or inconsistency with the statute as a whole.

    The words 'person aggrieved' are of wide import and include a person with a genuine grievance because an order or decision prejudicially affects that person's interests. A corporate employer is a person in law and may be aggrieved by a disciplinary determination.

    Section 92D of the Labour Act gives a person aggrieved by a determination made under an employment code a right of appeal to the Labour Court. Section 8(6) of the National Employment Code gives a person or party aggrieved by a decision a right of appeal. Neither provision confines the right of appeal to employees.

    A document not handed down or signed by the Supreme Court panel is not a judgment of the Court and has no binding effect.
held_decision_points:
Held, that Pioneer Transport v Mafikeni SC 45/17, reported as 2017 (2) ZLR 71 (S), was not an authentic judgment of the Supreme Court and had no binding effect. The authentic judgment was Pioneer Transport v Douglas Mafikeni SC 65/18.

    Held, further, that the words 'a person who is aggrieved' in s 92D of the Labour Act and 'a person or party who is aggrieved' in s 8(6) of the National Employment Code include an employer. An employer aggrieved by a disciplinary authority's determination has a right of appeal to the Labour Court.

    Held, further, that the Labour Court erred in upholding the preliminary objection that the employer had no right of appeal. The appeal was allowed and the matter remitted for continuation.

    Held, further, that each party should bear its own costs because the apparent conflict between two Supreme Court judgments created the need for clarification.
ratio_decidendi: The Labour Court's striking-off order was wrong because the clear language of s 92D of the Labour Act and s 8(6) of the National Employment Code grants a right of appeal to any aggrieved person or party, and that includes a corporate employer. The contrary reported Pioneer Transport judgment relied upon below was not an authentic Supreme Court judgment and could not restrict the statutory right.

document_name: SC 74 - 22 New Life Covenant v Trustees of the Harare Wetlands.docx
SC 75 - 22 ZVISHAVANE TOWN COUNCIL v ZETDC (Autosaved).docx
case_name:
ZVISHAVANE TOWN COUNCIL
    v
    ZIMBABWE ELECTRICITY TRANSMISSION AND DISTRIBUTION (PRIVATE) LIMITED
judges:
Panel: GUVAVA JA, MAVANGIRA JA & KUDYA AJA
    Judgment by: KUDYA AJA
    Concurrence: GUVAVA JA and MAVANGIRA JA agreed.
subject_area: Local government; Electricity law; Statutory interpretation; Administrative law; Prescription
catchwords_flynote: Local government - municipal by-laws - way leave charges imposed on electricity transmission and distribution company - whether by-laws ultra vires Urban Councils Act [Chapter 29:15] and Electricity Act [Chapter 13:05] - doctrine of objective invalidity - subsidiary legislation void ab initio - Prescription Act issue unnecessary after invalidity finding - appeal dismissed.
headnote:
The respondent sued the appellant for unpaid electricity bills. The appellant admitted indebtedness but counterclaimed for rates, services and way leave charges levied under local by-laws. The respondent accepted liability for rates and services other than way leave charges but disputed liability for way leaves, contending that the by-laws were ultra vires the governing legislation. It also pleaded prescription in respect of part of the claim. The High Court upheld the prescription plea, dismissed the way leave claim, and entered judgment for the respondent for the balance of the electricity debt.

    On appeal, the Supreme Court held that the dispositive question was whether the by-laws imposing way leave charges were valid. Although way leaves under s 44 of the Electricity Act were not free statutory servitudes because compensation was contemplated through the Land Acquisition Act, the appellant lacked authority under s 227(1) of the Urban Councils Act and the Third Schedule to impose way leave charges. Section 44 of the Electricity Act made the statutory servitude subject only to s 44 and compensation under the Land Acquisition Act, not to municipal way leave charges. The doctrine of objective invalidity was not confined to constitutional disputes and could be applied to legislative tension between statutes or between primary and subordinate legislation. The by-laws were ultra vires from inception, void ab initio, and incapable of conferring rights. Because that finding disposed of the appeal, the prescription issues did not arise. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the by-laws imposing way leave charges were ultra vires, void ab initio, and of no force or effect.
    Whether the High Court correctly invoked the doctrine of objective invalidity outside a constitutional setting.
    Whether the appellant had statutory authority under the Urban Councils Act or Electricity Act to impose way leave charges on the respondent.
    Whether the prescription issues required determination if the by-laws were invalid.
legal_principles:
The doctrine of objective invalidity is not confined to constitutional disputes. Its reasoning may apply where there is legislative tension between concurrent statutes or between a primary statute and a subsidiary enactment. A court may determine that legislation or subordinate legislation is invalid from inception if it was enacted in breach of superior statutory authority.

    A local authority may make by-laws only within the powers conferred by the primary statute. Section 227(1) of the Urban Councils Act permits by-laws in relation to matters specified in the Third Schedule or matters incidental to or connected with them.

    Section 44 of the Electricity Act permits an authorised undertaker to place transmission lines or pipelines into, out of or across land, subject to that section. Compensation is addressed through the Land Acquisition Act, but the section does not authorise local authorities to impose way leave charges under by-laws.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the High Court correctly invoked the doctrine of objective invalidity in determining the validity of the by-laws; the doctrine was not limited to constitutional disputes.

    Held, further, that s 227(1) of the Urban Councils Act and the Third Schedule did not authorise the appellant to levy way leave charges.

    Held, further, that s 44 of the Electricity Act was subject only to the other provisions of s 44 and to compensation under the Land Acquisition Act; it did not permit municipal way leave charges.

    Held, further, that the by-laws imposing way leave charges were ultra vires both the Urban Councils Act and the Electricity Act from inception and were void ab initio. A nullity conferred no rights.

    Held, further, that because invalidity disposed of the appeal, the prescription grounds did not arise. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The appellant's way leave counterclaim failed because the by-laws on which it depended were beyond the powers conferred by the Urban Councils Act and inconsistent with the statutory scheme of s 44 of the Electricity Act. Being ultra vires from inception, they were void ab initio and could not create a recoverable debt, making the prescription issues immaterial.

SC 78 - 22 BENJAMIN CHAMWAITA V MARY MUCHENGETI SC 94-18(1).docx
case_name:
[1] BENJAMIN CHAMWAITA [2] MARCELINE CHAMWAITA
    v
    MARY MUCHENGETI
judges:
Panel: GARWE JA, PATEL JA & BERE JA
    Judgment by: GARWE JA
    Concurrence: PATEL JA agreed.
    BERE JA: No longer in office.
subject_area: Property; Contract; Double sales; Specific performance; Civil procedure
catchwords_flynote: Property - double sale of immovable property before transfer - first in time stronger in law absent special circumstances - balance of equities - innocent second purchasers - occupation and development of property - appellate interference with discretion where relevant considerations ignored - eviction order set aside.
headnote:
The respondent bought Share Number 24 in a cluster development from Maffack Asset and Fund Managers. The first appellant originally bought Share Number 10 from another purchaser whose rights had been ceded to him with Maffack's acceptance. When Maffack later discovered that Share Number 10 had been sold to another person, it allocated Share Number 24 to the appellants by a variation agreement, although it had not terminated the respondent's agreement. The appellants moved onto Share Number 24, built a temporary structure, dug a well, installed electricity, and contributed to the Monavale Cluster Home Association after Maffack failed to complete development works. The High Court granted the respondent an eviction order, holding that there was no sale to the appellants and that, even if there was a double sale, the appellants were not innocent purchasers and the equities favoured the respondent.

    On appeal, the respondent conceded that there had been a double sale. The Supreme Court held that the concession was properly made. The Court found that the appellants were innocent purchasers: Maffack was in control of the project, the appellants had no access to Maffack's records, and there was no evidence that they knew of the respondent's pre-existing claim when Share Number 24 was allocated to them. The Court also held that the High Court failed to take into account weighty equitable considerations: the appellants had occupied the property for years, established a home, installed services, and made additional payments that helped rescue the development when the respondent refused to contribute. The High Court failed to exercise its discretion judiciously. The appeal succeeded with costs and the respondent's claim was dismissed.
issues_for_determination:
Whether there was a double sale of Share Number 24.
    Whether the High Court properly found that the appellants were not innocent purchasers.
    Whether special circumstances and the balance of equities justified preferring the appellants, as second purchasers, over the respondent, as first purchaser.
    Whether the High Court exercised its discretion judiciously in granting eviction.
legal_principles:
Where land is sold twice and transfer has not passed to either purchaser, the first purchaser ordinarily has the stronger claim and may obtain specific performance, while the second purchaser is left to damages. That rule yields where special circumstances affect the balance of equities.

    The second purchaser must establish a preponderance of equities or circumstances rendering it inequitable to apply the maxim qui prior est tempore potior est jure. Relevant considerations may include innocence, failure by the first purchaser to protect the claim, greater expenditure by the second purchaser, occupation, development, length of occupation, and payment of charges.

    The determination of the balance of equities involves the exercise of discretion by the trial court. An appellate court may interfere where the trial court acted on a wrong principle, considered irrelevant matters, mistook the facts, or failed to take relevant considerations into account.
held_decision_points:
Held, that there was a double sale of Share Number 24 because Maffack, while still bound to the respondent, allocated the same share to the appellants.

    Held, further, that the High Court's finding that the appellants were not innocent purchasers was not supported by the evidence. Maffack controlled the project and records, and there was no evidence that the appellants knew of the respondent's claim.

    Held, further, that the High Court failed to take into account substantial equitable considerations favouring the appellants, including long occupation, establishment of a home, installation of services, and additional contributions to rescue the development.

    Held, further, that the High Court did not exercise its discretion judiciously. The equities favoured the appellants and the eviction order had to be set aside.
ratio_decidendi: The appeal succeeded because the High Court misdirected itself both on the appellants' innocence and on the balance of equities. In a double sale before transfer, the first purchaser ordinarily prevails only absent special circumstances; here the appellants were innocent, had occupied and improved the stand for years, and had contributed to the development when the respondent had not, making it inequitable to prefer the respondent's claim to eviction.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court noted that judgment had been delayed because the judge originally assigned to prepare the draft left the bench before doing so, and that the resulting inordinate delay and prejudice to the parties were regretted.

document_name: SC 79 - 22 VERONICA NYONI VS BERNADETTE NDORO 1.docx
case_name:
VERONICA NYONI
    v
    BERNADETTE EVA NDORO N.O
judges:
Panel: UCHENA JA, MATHONSI JA & KUDYA JA
    Judgment by: MATHONSI JA
    Concurrence: UCHENA JA and KUDYA JA agreed.
subject_area: Contract; Civil procedure; Pleadings; Evidence; Unjust enrichment
catchwords_flynote: Contract - acknowledgment of debt - claim pleaded on acknowledgment of debt - need to prove written unequivocal admission of liability and undertaking to pay - pleadings binding parties and court - unpleaded unjust enrichment not available on appeal - onus on claimant - appeal dismissed.
headnote:
The appellant sued the late Dr Elias Zvenyika Ndoro for US$53 183, alleging that at a meeting held on 28 July 2010 he had acknowledged indebtedness to her arising from a property transaction. After Dr Ndoro's death, his widow and executrix dative was substituted as respondent. The High Court dismissed the claim after trial. It found that Dr Ndoro probably acknowledged indebtedness but had later revoked the acknowledgment.

    The Supreme Court held that the High Court's reasoning about revocation was problematic, because an acknowledgment of debt once validly made cannot simply be unilaterally revoked. However, the appellant failed on the pleaded cause of action. She had pleaded a claim based on an acknowledgment of debt, not a verbal agreement or unjust enrichment. A litigant is bound by the pleadings, and a court cannot grant judgment on an unpleaded cause of action. An acknowledgment of debt is a written document containing an unequivocal admission of liability coupled with an express or implied undertaking to pay. The appellant did not prove such a written acknowledgment, and even the alleged verbal acknowledgment was not proved. The legal practitioner's letter relied on by the appellant was written on her behalf, did not constitute an acknowledgment by Dr Ndoro, omitted essential requirements, and showed that the parties were still negotiating. The unjust enrichment argument was raised for the first time on appeal and had not been pleaded or supported by evidence. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the appellant proved the pleaded cause of action based on an acknowledgment of debt.
    Whether a verbal acknowledgment of debt, or the legal practitioner's letter recording what was allegedly said, could satisfy the pleaded claim.
    Whether the appellant could rely on unjust enrichment for the first time on appeal.
    Whether the High Court's dismissal of the claim should be upheld despite different reasoning.
legal_principles:
Pleadings define the issues between parties and give fair notice of the case to be met. A litigant is bound by the cause of action pleaded, and a court may not grant judgment on a cause of action not pleaded. A party seeking to change a cause of action must seek leave to amend.

    The onus rests on the party alleging a fact to prove it. Where a claim is based on an acknowledgment of debt, the plaintiff must prove an acknowledgment of debt meeting the requirements of law.

    An acknowledgment of debt is a document containing an unequivocal admission of liability by the debtor coupled with an express or implied undertaking to pay. It must identify the debtor, creditor, amount, payment terms and other essential features.

    Unjust enrichment must be pleaded and supported by evidence. It cannot be introduced for the first time on appeal where it did not arise on the pleadings or evidence.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the appellant was bound by her pleaded cause of action, which was based on an acknowledgment of debt. She did not plead a verbal agreement or unjust enrichment.

    Held, further, that a claim based on an acknowledgment of debt required proof of a written acknowledgment of liability. The appellant did not prove such a document.

    Held, further, that the letter of 17 August 2010 did not constitute an acknowledgment of debt by Dr Ndoro. It was written on behalf of the appellant, omitted essential requirements, and did not show an undertaking to pay.

    Held, further, that the appellant could not rely on unjust enrichment because it was neither pleaded nor argued a quo and did not arise in the evidence.

    Held, further, that although the High Court's reasoning about revocation was incorrect, the conclusion that the appellant failed to prove her pleaded claim was correct. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The appeal failed because the appellant pleaded and had to prove a valid acknowledgment of debt, but she produced no written acknowledgment containing an unequivocal admission of liability and undertaking to pay by Dr Ndoro. Since unjust enrichment and other possible causes of action were not pleaded or supported by evidence, the Court could not grant relief on them.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court observed that, if indebtedness had indeed been acknowledged, it would not ordinarily be open to the debtor to revoke the acknowledgment unilaterally. Resiling from an acknowledgment may occur only in limited circumstances such as duress or undue influence.
