document_name: SC 8 - 22 MOSES SHINGIRIRO CHINYENZE v THE LAW SOCIETY OF ZIMBABWE(1).doc
case_name:
MOSES SHINGIRIRO CHINYENZE
    v
    THE LAW SOCIETY OF ZIMBABWE
court:
1.  The amount and the purpose of a payment allegedly made by the late Christopher Chimbumu to the appellant;

        2.  The terms of the discussion between the appellant and the Registrar of Deeds;

        3.  The liability of Joseph Ngondonga to pay transfer fees for the three transfers, him having been liable for payment of occupational rent upon taking occupation of the property before transfer; and

        4.  Whether or not the appellant, who denied all the charges had abused the trust funds in issue, had not accounted for the funds, had not executed his client’s instructions or had failed to update his client on the progress of the matter.

    3.  The Tribunal further erred in finding that the appellant was obliged to communicate with the Registrar of Deeds through letters when the relevant regulations require that all communication with the Registrar must be conducted in person.

    4.  The Tribunal further erred in finding that the appellant must not have attempted a direct transfer of the property from the estate of the late Christopher Chimbumu to Joseph Ngondonga when the absolute discretion to determine whether or not exceptional circumstances exists is that of the Registrar of Deeds and not that of the Tribunal.

    5.  The Tribunal erred in not finding that the appellant was entitled to charge 80% of the fees for the three transfers to Joseph Ngondonga when the evidence in the record of the criminal proceedings which was placed before the Tribunal contained the draft deeds in respect of each of the transfers.

    6.  The Tribunal further erred in finding that the appellant failed to account in respect of trust funds which he received when it was apparent that such accounting had been done in advance upon presentation of the proforma statement of account as is the practice in conveyancing.

    7.  Alternatively, should it be found that the disputes of fact could be resolved on the papers, the Tribunal further erred in finding that the amount allegedly paid by Christopher Chimbumu was in respect of transfer fees for the same property and therefore that the appellant was paid twice for the same transfer when there was no evidence that this was so.

    8.  The Tribunal further grossly erred in making a finding that the appellant was not a fit and proper person to remain in practice as a legal practitioner, notary public and conveyancer before the appellant even made submission in mitigation.

    9.  The Tribunal further erred in imposing a penalty of deleting the appellant’s name in the register of legal practitioners, notary public and conveyancers which penalty induces a sense of shock and is grossly disproportionate to the offence committed.

    RELIEF SOUGHT

    WHEREFORE the appellant prays for an order allowing the present appeal with costs and setting aside the judgment of the Legal Practitioners Disciplinary Tribunal and substituting it with an order that:-

    “The application is dismissed with costs.””

    27. It is opportune to state at this juncture that in his heads of argument as well as in oral submissions before us, Mr Magwaliba, for the appellant, submitted that the third and fifth grounds of appeal are dispositive of this appeal. He also stated in heads of argument that the appellant would not be pursuing the first and second grounds of appeal. With regard to the fourth, sixth and seventh grounds of appeal, the submission was that they were also dispensed with as they were canvassed under the third and fifth grounds. In any event, no meaningful submissions were made in heads of argument to motivate the said grounds. The eighth and ninth grounds of appeal seem to be raising one issue; and counsel did not extensively dwell on them. However, it is noted that the issues arising from the said grounds will be addressed in the court’s determination of this appeal. This Court will, in this judgment, accordingly pay extensive regard to the third and fifth grounds of appeal.

    28. It is my view that the two grounds of appeal that the appellant mainly relies on essentially raise one issue, viz, whether or not the finding by the Tribunal that the respondent had managed to prove its case against the appellant beyond reasonable doubt was proper. This is against the appellant’s contention that he was improperly convicted owing to the Tribunal ignoring or disregarding evidence that was before it.

    ISSUE FOR DETERMINATION

    Whether the finding by the Legal Practitioners Disciplinary Tribunal, that the respondent had proved beyond reasonable doubt its case of misconduct against the appellant, was proper.

    29. The charges levelled against the appellant were that he had abused his client’s trust monies, failed to account to client, failed to execute the client’s instructions and failed to update the client on the progress of the matter. It was the respondent’s contention that the appellant was thereby in breach of By-law 70E of the Law Society By-laws, 1982.

    30. The appellant had been appointed by the High Court to facilitate the transfer of the immovable property from the late Mr Chimbumu and his wife to their five children. This was pursuant to a consent order in terms of which the transfer was to be effected on or before 31 October, 2007. As at the time of the hearing of this appeal, some thirteen years later, the transfer had not been effected. The late Mr Chimbumu had, before his demise, initiated the transfer process. He died in 2013 before the completion of the process. The Tribunal a quo did not find any valid explanation from the appellant for this state of affairs, particularly in view of the fact that the late Mr Chimbumu had paid capital gains tax, obtained the certificate thereof and had had the property valued.

    31. After the demise of Mr Chimbumu the appointed executrix, with the consent of the beneficiaries, sought to sell the immovable property. The appellant was again instructed to handle the conveyancing work and to facilitate transfer to Mr Joseph Ngondonga, the purchaser. Despite the payments that were made to the appellant for the said purpose, the appellant had not effected such transfer as at the date of the application a quo by the respondent and even as at the date of the hearing of this appeal. This is what triggered the complaint to the respondent by the executrix of the estate of the late Mr Chimbumu and the consequent application for his deregistration.
citation:
“IT IS ORDERED THAT:

    1.  In terms of Section 28(1)(a)(i) of the Legal Practitioners Act 27:07, the Respondent be deleted from the Register of Legal Practitioners, Notaries Public and Conveyancers in Zimbabwe;

    2.  The Respondent be and is hereby prohibited from operating any trust account or business account of his own accord in terms of the Legal Practitioners Act [Chapter 27:07]

    3.  Respondent’s law firm be and is hereby placed under curatorship to administer trust accounts or business accounts with such rights, duties and powers in relation thereto as the Legal Practitioners Disciplinary Tribunal may consider fit.

    4.  Respondent pay the expenses incurred by the applicant in connection with these proceedings”

    6. The basis of the application was that the respondent was not a fit and proper person to remain registered as a legal practitioner as he had acted in an unprofessional, dishonest and unlawful manner, the specifics of which will appear in the narrative below.

    7. On 30 October 2015, the respondent received a complaint on the conduct of the appellant from one Mrs Noreen Chikaka of Regional Executors and Trust (Private) Limited. She alleged that she had been appointed the executrix of the estate of the late Christopher Taruvinga Chimbumu on 24 May 2014. She indicated that before the demise of the said Christopher Chimbumu, he had been married to Florence Chimbumu but the two had since divorced.

    8. The two had entered into a consent paper in terms of which they donated to their five children an immovable property known as No. 2 Wessex Drive, Cotswold Hills, Mabelreign, Harare. Pursuant to the consent paper, the appellant had been appointed by the Registrar of the High Court (the Registrar), as conveyancer for the purposes of transferring the immovable property into the names of the five children.

    9. In terms of the order of the High Court, the transfer to the children was to be effected by 31 October 2007. However, this stipulation was not met. As at 22 August 2013, that being the date of Christopher Chimbumu’s death, the transfer to the beneficiaries had still not been effected.

    10. In the course of her duties as the executrix of the estate, Mrs Chikaka sold the immovable property to one Joseph Ngondonga after having sought and obtained the consent of both the Master of the High Court and the beneficiaries. Mrs Chikaka appointed the appellant’s law firm to execute the transfer of the property to Mr Ngondonga as the appellant had previously been appointed as conveyancer before the death of Mr Chimbumu. She handed over to the appellant letters of administration and consent to sell from the beneficiaries, fees for rates clearance and capital gains tax clearance certificate so that he could facilitate the transfer.

    11. The appellant then apparently instructed Mr Ngondonga to pay US$8 450,00 which amount was paid directly into the appellant’s trust account. The appellant was also said to have been paid for the transfer of the property during the lifetime of the late Mr Chimbumu. Despite these respective payments having been made, the transfer was not effected. Mrs Chikaka’s futile attempts to contact the appellant resulted in her writing a complaint about the matter to the respondent.

    12. There was also, in the application, an allegation that the appellant had overcharged on the Government stamp duty and that such overcharged amount was never refunded to the purchaser. It was further alleged that after having been paid the said amounts, as at 31 May, 2015, the appellant’s trust account only had a balance of US$205.25 when no disbursements had been made in respect of the anticipated transfer. In the absence of a valid explanation, the respondent concluded that the funds were misappropriated, in breach of By-law 70E of the Law Society By-laws, 1982.
judges:
Panel: GUVAVA JA, MAVANGIRA JA & MAKONI JA
    Judgment by: MAVANGIRA JA
    Concurrence: GUVAVA JA and MAKONI JA agreed.
subject_area: Legal practitioners and professional discipline; Evidence; Appeal and review
catchwords_flynote: Legal practitioners – professional discipline – misuse of trust monies and failure to account – conveyancing mandate left unperformed – proof beyond reasonable doubt before Legal Practitioners’ Disciplinary Tribunal – documentary evidence and bare denials – entitlement to 80 per cent conveyancing fee under SI 24 of 2013 – By-law 70E accounting duty – appellate interference with penalty – deletion from register upheld – reasons following ex tempore dismissal.
issues_for_determination:
1. Whether the Tribunal properly found, beyond reasonable doubt, that the respondent had proved professional misconduct against the appellant.
    2. Whether the appellant established that he had substantially completed work entitling him to 80 per cent of conveyancing fees for three transfers.
    3. Whether the Tribunal improperly treated the appellant as not fit and proper before mitigation.
    4. Whether deletion from the register was a shocking or disproportionate penalty warranting appellate interference.
legal_principles:
Criminal proceedings and professional disciplinary proceedings are distinct processes serving different purposes; an acquittal or discharge in a criminal matter does not preclude disciplinary proceedings, though dishonesty or theft allegations before the Tribunal must be proved beyond reasonable doubt.
    Courts and tribunals decide on evidence placed before them. A litigant’s bald assertion carries no weight where substantiating documents are available or within that litigant’s control but are not produced.
    By-law 70E requires a firm, within a reasonable time after performance or termination of its mandate, to deliver a clear written statement of all receipts, disbursements, fees and the balance payable. A practitioner who receives money from a purchaser is obliged to account to that person even if another person is the formal client.
    Under para 2 of the Schedule to the Law Society of Zimbabwe (Conveyancing Fees) By-laws, 2013, a conveyancer may charge 80 per cent only if all documents have been prepared and the work has been substantially carried out to the point of lodging. The entitlement is confined to conveyancing fees and does not extend to stamp duty.
    Sentence or disciplinary sanction lies primarily within the discretion of the court or tribunal of first instance. An appellate court interferes only where the discretion was not judicially exercised; the appellant must meaningfully motivate the alleged misdirection.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the Tribunal’s finding that misconduct was proved beyond reasonable doubt was supported by the evidence.
    Held, further, that the appellant’s assertions about draft transfer papers and communications could not assist him because the underlying documents were not placed before the Tribunal.
    Held, further, that the purchaser’s deposits were not retained in trust and no compliant written account was produced, justifying the finding of abuse of trust funds and failure to account under By-law 70E.
    Held, further, that the appellant did not prove substantial completion of work to the point of lodging and was not entitled to appropriate the funds, including stamp duty, as an 80 per cent fee.
    Held, further, that the Tribunal’s statement concerning fitness related to conviction; mitigation and aggravation were subsequently heard before sanction.
    Held, further, that no basis was established for interfering with deletion from the register. The appeal was dismissed in its entirety with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The Tribunal’s misconduct finding stood because objective bank records and the absence of any contemporaneous statement of account or completed conveyancing documents demonstrated that the purchaser’s money had not been preserved or accounted for, while the appellant’s contrary case remained unsupported assertion. Since the statutory tariff conditions for an 80 per cent fee were not proved and the sanction discretion was not shown to have miscarried, the conviction and deletion from the register were unassailable.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant:
“The third issue was that the respondent had been prosecuted in a criminal court and acquitted of contravening section 113 (2) (d) of the Criminal Law (Codification and Reform) Act [Chapter 9:23] the allegations having been that he had stolen money due to Everson Shepard Dandadzi (the complainant in respect of paragraphs 4.2-4.3). Mr Maanda submitted that the allegations before the Tribunal are the same as those presented before the Regional Magistrate Court. Further, the proof required before the Tribunal in a matter involving theft is the same as in a criminal matter that is proof beyond reasonable doubt. The respondent referred to Law Society of Zimbabwe v Mugabe & Anor 1994 (2) ZLR 356 where it was held that proof beyond reasonable doubt is required in a charge of theft of funds. He therefore raised the defence of autrefois acquit.”

    And at p 9:

      “In other words, even where the legal practitioner is acquitted in a criminal court on facts relied on in a disciplinary hearing, the Tribunal is not precluded from hearing the disciplinary matter as long as the applicant is required to prove its case beyond reasonable doubt. Where a respondent before it has been acquitted of a criminal offence, the Tribunal will require the applicant to prove its case beyond reasonable doubt. Section 28(3) therefore allows for a respondent who may have been convicted of an offence to be referred to the Tribunal. This is recognition of the fact that proceedings in a criminal court and before the Tribunal are different processes for different purposes with different requirements despite arising from the same facts. Criminal proceedings are generally initiated by the police for breach of criminal law regarded as a wrong against society as a whole. Disciplinary proceedings are on the other hand initiated by the applicant with the aim of regulating the relationship between a legal practitioner and the applicant and maintain discipline in the legal profession. Had the proceedings been the same, a legal practitioner convicted before a criminal court would escape proceedings before the Tribunal on the basis that he/she would suffer double jeopardy. That is not the position. The defence raised by the respondent is therefore not applicable in the present disciplinary proceedings.”

    20. The point in limine with regard to prescription was also dismissed on the basis that it was not applicable in casu. On the issue of the alleged premature set down of the matter, the Tribunal conceded that the matter had been pre-maturely set down but found that it would not render the proceedings to be improperly before it and was not prejudicial to the parties in any way.

    21. The raising of the issue of overcharging stamp duty in heads of argument was found to be prejudicial to the appellant and consequently all portions of the papers on which the averment was contained were struck out. With regard to the issue of the lack of clarity of the charges levelled against the appellant, the Tribunal found that the respondent had not specified the conduct of the appellant that could be considered unprofessional and unworthy under s 23 (2) (b) of the Legal Practitioners Act. On the last preliminary point that the matter could not be heard without the calling of viva voce evidence, the Tribunal held that there were no material disputes of fact in the matter.

    22. On the merits, the Tribunal found that apart from his mere say so, the appellant had failed to place any evidence before it to substantiate his defence. No transfer documents had been placed before the Tribunal to show that he had drafted any. No evidence was placed before the Tribunal to show that he had made a formal request to the Registrar of Deeds to make a direct transfer of the immovable property. The appellant also failed to produce invoices to show that he was accounting to the beneficiaries or the purchaser. In the absence of such evidence, which it considered to be fundamental, the Tribunal found itself unable to accept the appellant’s defence. On the issue of the appellant having allegedly taken 80 per cent of the money paid as his fees and his failure to produce before it the transfer documents that he claimed to have worked on, the Tribunal found the appellant had misappropriated the funds.

    23. On the question of who was supposed to meet the payment of the transfer fees, the Tribunal found that the beneficiaries of the estate of the late Mr Chimbumu were supposed to cater for the transfer fees for the transfer from their parents to them. The purchaser would then meet the transfer costs for the transfer from the beneficiaries to him. The Tribunal also found that no evidence had been placed before it to show that the purchaser was, nevertheless, willing to meet the transfer fees for the two transfers.

    24. Having received money from the purchaser and thereafter failing to account for it, the appellant was found to be in breach of By-law 70E of the Law Society By-laws.

    25. The Tribunal also found that the delay in the conveyancing of the property dated back to the time when Mr Chimbumu was still alive and had initiated the process of transfer of the property to his children. Mr Chimbumu had paid capital gains tax and had obtained the certificate thereof, which certificate he handed to the appellant. He had also had the property valued. The Tribunal found that there was no meaningful explanation as to why transfer had not yet been effected by the time that Mr Chimbumu died in 2013. On the basis of all these findings, the tribunal came to the conclusion that the respondent had established its case beyond reasonable doubt. Consequently, the application for the appellant’s deregistration was granted.

    THIS APPEAL
cases_referred_to:
Law Society of Zimbabwe v Douglas Mwonzora HH 306/18
    Law Society of Zimbabwe v Mugabe & Anor 1994 (2) ZLR 356
    Delta Beverages (Pvt) Ltd v Murandu SC 38/15

document_name: SC 80 - 22 MSELANGUBO FARM VS KERSHELMAR FARMS & OTHERS..docx
case_name:
(1) MSWELANGUBO FARM (PRIVATE) LIMITED (2) OBERT MPOFU (3) SIKHANYISIWE MPOFU
    v
    (1) KERSHELMAR FARMS (PRIVATE) LIMITED (2) ZEPHANIAH DHLAMINI (3) CHARLES MOYO (4) SIPHOSAMI PATRICK MALUNGA
judges:
Panel: GWAUNZA DCJ, CHITAKUNYE JA & MWAYERA JA
    Judgment by: MWAYERA JA
    Concurrence: GWAUNZA DCJ and CHITAKUNYE JA agreed.
subject_area: Property; Land law; Spoliation; Civil procedure
catchwords_flynote: Spoliation - mandament van spolie - acquired agricultural land - offer letter holder entering farm without court order - peaceful and undisturbed possession - self-help impermissible - ownership and legality of occupation irrelevant to possessory remedy - Gazetted Land (Consequential Provisions) Act [Chapter 20:28], s 3 - spoliation order upheld.
headnote:
The first respondent owned Esidakeni Farm. The Minister of Lands acquired the farm under the land reform programme by General Notice 3042 of 2020. The respondents challenged the acquisition in pending High Court proceedings. The appellants later entered the farm, claiming an offer letter, and began ploughing fields that the respondents said they had occupied and prepared for cropping. The respondents sought and obtained spoliatory relief in the High Court.

    On appeal, the appellants argued that their offer letter made them lawful occupiers and that the respondents, as former owners or occupiers of gazetted land, could not obtain spoliation relief. The Supreme Court rejected that argument. The mandament van spolie protects peaceful and undisturbed possession against unlawful dispossession. The merits of ownership or ultimate entitlement are not determined in spoliation proceedings. Even a holder of an offer letter may not resort to self-help; due process and a court order are required before dispossessing an existing occupier. The respondents were in de facto possession of the farm and had commenced land preparation. The appellants entered without a court order and disrupted that possession. The requirements for spoliation were satisfied. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the High Court erred in granting spoliatory relief to the respondents.
    Whether the appellants' offer letter entitled them to occupy the farm without obtaining a court order or following due process.
    Whether the respondents' alleged unlawful occupation of gazetted land barred spoliation relief.
    Whether the respondents had been in peaceful and undisturbed possession and were wrongfully or forcibly dispossessed.
legal_principles:
To obtain a spoliation order the applicant must establish peaceful and undisturbed possession and deprivation of that possession forcibly or wrongfully and against consent.

    The mandament van spolie restores the status quo ante before inquiry into the merits of ownership or entitlement. Its purpose is to prevent self-help and preserve public order by requiring parties to follow due process.

    An offer letter does not give the holder a self-executing right to take occupation before the current occupier is evicted through lawful process. Even where land has been acquired or gazetted, an offer-letter holder must seek eviction through a competent court or other lawful process, not by direct invasion.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the respondents were in de facto occupation of the farm and had commenced land preparation. It was not necessary for them or their workers to be physically present on every part of the farm.

    Held, further, that the appellants entered and occupied the land without a court order or lawful eviction process. Their offer letter did not entitle them to resort to self-help.

    Held, further, that ownership and the ultimate legality of occupation were not issues in spoliation proceedings. The decisive issue was whether the incoming possessors lawfully deprived the existing possessors of possession.

    Held, further, that the requirements for spoliatory relief were satisfied, and the High Court's decision was unassailable. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The spoliation order was upheld because the respondents were in peaceful de facto possession and the appellants, notwithstanding their offer letter, displaced that possession without lawful process. The mandament protects possession against self-help and does not permit an offer-letter holder to bypass eviction proceedings or a court order.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: SC 81 - 22  EDMORE MAPONDERA AND 55 OTHERS V FREDA REBECCA GOLD MINE HOLDINGS (Autosaved) -.docx
case_name:
EDMORE MAPONDERA AND 55 OTHERS
    v
    FREDA REBECCA GOLD MINE HOLDINGS LIMITED
court:
“(a) The preliminary points raised by the appellant relating to its miscitation be and is hereby dismissed.

    (b) The preliminary point raised by the appellant relating to the proper citation of the 2nd to the 60th respondents be and is hereby dismissed. The 2nd to 60th Respondents are hereby held to be properly before the court.”

    3. The court a quo’s determination on the merits of the appeal before it be and is hereby quashed and set aside

    4. The matter be and is hereby remitted to the court a quo for a hearing de novo of the appeal before a different judge.”

    MAVANGIRA JA I agree

    CHITAKUNYE JA I agree

    Dube, Manikai & Hwacha, appellant’s legal practitioners

    Gill, Godlonton & Gerrans, respondent’s legal practitioners
case_number:
Case No. SC 565/19
    Ref Case No. LC/H/82/11
judges:
Panel: MAVANGIRA JA, BHUNU JA & CHITAKUNYE JA
    Judgment by: BHUNU JA
    Concurrence: MAVANGIRA JA and CHITAKUNYE JA agreed.
subject_area: Labour and employment law; Arbitration; Practice and procedure; Citation of parties
catchwords_flynote: Labour law - arbitration - unfair dismissal dispute - miscitation of employer by trade name - whether proceedings nullity - correction of misdescription where existing party sufficiently identified - Labour Act [Chapter 28:01], s 90A - technicalities in labour proceedings - arbitrator bound by terms of reference - unnamed group of employees clarified during arbitration - matter remitted for hearing de novo before different Labour Court judge.
headnote:
The appellants, employees of Freda Rebecca Gold Mine Holdings Limited, challenged the termination of their employment after the respondent ceased operations and later sought to re-engage them on inferior terms. The dispute went to conciliation and then arbitration. The reference to arbitration used the name 'Freda Rebecca Mine' and referred to 'E Mapondera and 60 others'. The arbitrator dismissed objections concerning citation and identity of claimants, obtained clarification of the claimants' names, and awarded reinstatement. The respondent appealed to the Labour Court. The Labour Court held that the arbitration proceedings were a nullity because a non-existent employer had been cited and that the other employees were not properly before the arbitrator. It also addressed the merits and the absence of an alternative award of damages.

    The Supreme Court held that labour arbitration is not the same as ordinary trial proceedings commenced by summons. A reference to arbitration is drafted by a conciliator, not by the claimants, and s 90A of the Labour Act requires the Labour Court to do simple industrial justice without being shackled by technicalities. The employer was an existing entity misdescribed by a trade name; the same Labour Court judge had earlier granted an amendment regularising the citation and was bound by that order. The arbitrator was also bound by terms of reference requiring him to determine the dismissal of E Mapondera and 60 others. He properly obtained clarification of the identities of the employees and gave the respondent an opportunity to be heard. The Labour Court erred in treating the proceedings as a nullity and, after doing so, erred again by deciding the merits. The appeal was allowed, the Labour Court judgment was set aside, and the appeal was remitted for rehearing before a different judge.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the improper citation of the respondent as Freda Rebecca Mine rendered the arbitration proceedings a nullity.
    Whether the 2nd to the 60th appellants were properly before the arbitrator.
    Whether it was necessary to decide whether the arbitrator could order reinstatement without an alternative of damages in lieu of reinstatement.
    What remittal or substituted order was appropriate.
legal_principles:
Labour and arbitration proceedings are intended to achieve simple industrial justice and should not be shackled by unnecessary legal technicalities, provided that natural justice is observed. Section 90A of the Labour Act distinguishes Labour Court proceedings from ordinary courts by enabling a broader, less technical approach.

    Proceedings against a truly non-existent person are generally void, but where an existing entity is merely misdescribed by a trade name, colloquial name or incorrect description, amendment is permissible if made in good faith and without irreparable prejudice.

    Arbitral proceedings under the Labour Act are commenced by a reference drafted by the conciliator. A claimant should not be penalised for a conciliator's drafting where the real adversary was sufficiently identified and no prejudice was shown.

    An arbitrator is bound by the terms of reference and has no jurisdiction outside them. Where the reference requires determination of the dismissal of a named employee and others, the arbitrator may seek clarification of the identities of the other employees to discharge that mandate.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the reference to Freda Rebecca Mine was a misdescription of an existing employer rather than citation of a non-existent party. The same Labour Court judge had already granted an amendment regularising the citation and was bound by that order.

    Held, further, that the arbitrator properly dealt with the issue of the other employees by obtaining clarification of their identities and availing the list to the respondent. The respondent suffered no prejudice.

    Held, further, that the Labour Court erred in holding that the other employees were not properly before the arbitrator and further erred by deciding the merits after concluding that the proceedings were a nullity.

    Held, further, that it was unnecessary to decide whether the arbitrator could order reinstatement without an alternative of damages because the Labour Court's merits determination was itself a nullity.

    Held, further, that the Labour Court judgment had to be reversed and the matter remitted for a hearing de novo before a different judge.
ratio_decidendi: The Labour Court erred because the employer was an existing entity merely misdescribed in labour arbitration proceedings initiated by a conciliator's reference, and the arbitrator had properly clarified the identity of the employees covered by the terms of reference. Since no irreparable prejudice arose and the Labour Court had earlier regularised the citation, the proceedings were not a nullity; the Labour Court's merits determination following its contrary nullity finding could not stand.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: SC 85 - 22 BERNARD MUTANGA V TSITSI MUTANGA final judgment.docx
SC 87 - 22 FISANI MOYO v Blanket Mine circulation copy.docx
case_name:
(1) FISANI MOYO (2) VALENTINE MINE
    v
    (1) BLANKET MINE (1983) PRIVATE LIMITED (2) ZIMBABWE REPUBLIC POLICE GWANDA (3) PROVINCIAL MINING DIRECTOR N.O
judges:
Panel: GWAUNZA DCJ, CHITAKUNYE JA & MWAYERA JA
    Judgment by: GWAUNZA DCJ
    Concurrence: CHITAKUNYE JA and MWAYERA JA agreed.
subject_area: Property; Mining law; Spoliation; Urgent applications
catchwords_flynote: Spoliation - mining claim - urgent application - restoration of peaceful and undisturbed possession - removal of spoliator does not convert spoliation into eviction - urgency finding not substantive ground of appeal - temporary cessation of mining activities - possession retained through intention to resume operations - appeal dismissed.
headnote:
The first respondent applied to the High Court for a spoliation order, alleging that it had been in peaceful and undisturbed possession of the Valentine 56 mining claim before the appellants took occupation and began mining activities. The appellants argued that the matter was in substance an eviction application, that it should not have been heard urgently, that the first respondent had not shown physical control of the mine, and that the first appellant was the registered owner of the claim. The High Court granted spoliatory relief.

    The Supreme Court held that the application was properly characterised as one for spoliation. The fact that restoration of possession involved removing the appellants from the mining claim did not turn the remedy into an eviction. The finding of urgency was not, without more, a substantive ground of appeal and no gross misdirection had been shown. On the merits, the first respondent had previously physically occupied the mine and had only temporarily ceased drilling while awaiting valuation results. It retained the intention to resume operations and had not relinquished possession. The appellants took advantage of that temporary cessation and entered the mine. The requirements for spoliation were established. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the High Court mischaracterised an eviction application as one for spoliation and erred in hearing it urgently.
    Whether the finding of urgency was a competent substantive ground of appeal.
    Whether the first respondent had peaceful and undisturbed possession of the mine despite temporary cessation of drilling activities.
    Whether the appellants wrongfully deprived the first respondent of possession.
legal_principles:
To obtain spoliatory relief, an applicant must prove peaceful and undisturbed possession and wrongful or forcible deprivation of possession without consent. The merits of ownership or entitlement are ordinarily irrelevant at the spoliation stage.

    Restoration of possession may require removal of the spoliator from the property. That consequence does not convert spoliatory relief into eviction proceedings.

    Possession involves both physical control and the mental element of intention to possess. Temporary absence or temporary cessation of activity does not necessarily amount to relinquishment of possession where the intention to resume remains.

    A finding of urgency is a discretionary procedural finding and is not ordinarily a substantive ground on which to impeach the merits of a judgment absent gross misdirection or irregularity.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the first respondent's application was for spoliation and not eviction. Eviction of the appellants was only a natural consequence of restoring the status quo ante.

    Held, further, that the attack on urgency was without merit. The High Court's urgency decision had not been shown to involve gross misdirection.

    Held, further, that the first respondent had not relinquished possession of the mine. It had temporarily ceased drilling while awaiting valuation results and intended to resume operations.

    Held, further, that the appellants failed to disprove the evidence that the first respondent was in peaceful and undisturbed possession. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The appeal failed because the first respondent proved prior peaceful and undisturbed possession and wrongful dispossession. The temporary suspension of mining operations did not amount to abandonment of possession, and restoration of possession through removal of the appellants remained spoliatory relief rather than eviction.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

SC 9 - 22 MDC & ors vs Lilian Timveous & ors(1).docx
case_name:
(1) MOVEMENT FOR DEMOCRATIC CHANGE (TSVANGIRAI)
    (2) DOUGLAS MWONZORA (3) DR THOKOZANI KHUPE
    v
    (1) LILIAN TIMVEOS (2) THABITHA KHUMALO (3) SPEAKER OF THE NATIONAL ASSEMBLY (4) PRESIDENT OF THE SENATE (5) CHAIRPERSON OF THE ZIMBABWE ELECTORAL COMMISSION
judges:
Panel: GUVAVA JA, MAKONI JA & CHITAKUNYE JA
    Judgment by: CHITAKUNYE JA
    Concurrence: GUVAVA JA and MAKONI JA agreed.
subject_area: Practice and procedure; Civil procedure; Electoral law; Constitutional law; Review; Interdicts
catchwords_flynote: Practice and procedure – urgent chamber application – interim and final interdicts – prima facie right contrasted with clear right – High Court granting final order not sought and without return date – court confined to relief placed before it – r 240 of High Court Rules not licence to substitute final relief – Supreme Court review power under s 25 – matter struck off roll – ZEC as body corporate under s 319 of Constitution.
headnote:
After the first and second respondents were recalled from Parliament, they brought an urgent chamber application seeking a provisional interdict against replacement of their seats pending related High Court proceedings. They abandoned only the paragraph seeking to interdict ZEC. The High Court found a prima facie right but granted an order, pending disposal of the related cases, that had no return date and was final in effect. The order was materially different from the interim relief sought.

    On appeal, the Supreme Court raised the propriety of the order and both the appellants and the first and second respondents accepted that it was not the order sought. The Court restated that an interim interdict may be granted on a prima facie right and must be ancillary to final relief to be determined on a return date; a final interdict requires a clear right established on a balance of probabilities. A court must determine the dispute and relief placed before it. If it considers different relief necessary, it must put that possibility to the parties. Rule 240 did not authorise substitution of a final order for the provisional order sought. The High Court’s order was therefore an irregularity, leaving no proper appeal. The Supreme Court struck the matter off the roll and, exercising review powers under s 25 of the Supreme Court Act, set aside the High Court judgment, with each party bearing its own costs.

    For completeness, the Court stated that the older statutory provision treating the ZEC Chairperson as the cited representative was inconsistent with ZEC’s later corporate status under s 4A of the Electoral Act and s 319 of the Constitution. It did not determine whether non-joinder was fatal because relief against ZEC had been abandoned.
issues_for_determination:
1. Whether the order granted by the High Court was proper where it was final in effect, lacked a return date and had not been sought by the applicants.
    2. Whether the High Court erred in treating citation of the Chairperson of ZEC as proper and non-joinder of ZEC as unnecessary under s 14 of the Electoral Act.
    3. Whether the Supreme Court should invoke its review powers under s 25 of the Supreme Court Act.
    4. Costs.
legal_principles:
An interim interdict requires a prima facie right, a well-grounded apprehension of irreparable harm, a balance of convenience favouring relief and absence of another satisfactory remedy. A final interdict requires a clear right established on a balance of probabilities, actual or reasonably apprehended irreparable harm and absence of an alternative remedy.
    Interim relief must be ancillary to the main relief and ordinarily provide a return date at which the parties may contest final relief. Relief that is final in effect cannot be granted merely on a prima facie case.
    A court determines the dispute defined by pleadings, evidence, submissions, prayers and draft orders. It may not go on a frolic of its own and grant materially different relief without first putting the proposed relief to the parties. Rule 240 permits variation but does not authorise substitution of a final order for a provisional order.
    Section 25 of the Supreme Court Act empowers the Supreme Court to review proceedings and set aside a judgment affected by an irregularity.
    The Constitution is supreme and invalidates inconsistent legislation to the extent of inconsistency. An independent commission declared a body corporate by s 319 is capable of suing and being sued in its own name.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the High Court granted an order materially different from the provisional relief sought, without a return date, and final in effect despite only a prima facie right having been established.
    Held, further, that r 240 of the High Court Rules did not authorise substitution of that final order and the irregular order could not stand.
    Held, further, that the irregularity meant there was no proper appeal before the Court.
    Held, further, that the matter was to be struck of the roll and the High Court judgment set aside in the exercise of the Court’s review powers under s 25 of the Supreme Court Act.
    Held, further, that each party should bear its own costs because the decisive irregularity had not been expressly raised by any party.
ratio_decidendi: The High Court exceeded the case placed before it by converting a request for provisional relief into a final order that lacked a return date and was granted on proof of only a prima facie right. Because that material departure was made without inviting submissions and was not authorised by r 240, it constituted a reviewable irregularity. The Supreme Court therefore had to set the judgment aside under s 25 and strike the matter off the roll rather than determine the substantive appeal.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: CHITAKUNYE JA stated, for completeness after the first issue had disposed of the appeal, that s 14 of the Electoral Act was inconsistent with the later corporate status conferred on ZEC by s 4A of the Act and s 319 of the Constitution. By operation of s 2(1) of the Constitution, the former position that ZEC could be sued only through its Chairperson was invalid to the extent of the inconsistency. The Court did not decide whether non-citation of ZEC was fatal in this matter because the applicants had abandoned relief against ZEC.

document_name: SC 90 - 22 Morgan Havire  vs  Arosume Property Development Pvt Ltd.docx
case_name:
MORGAN HAVIRE
    v
    AROSUME PROPERTY DEVELOPMENT (PRIVATE) LIMITED
judges:
Panel: UCHENA JA, CHATUKUTA JA & MWAYERA JA
    Judgment by: MWAYERA JA
    Concurrence: UCHENA JA and CHATUKUTA JA agreed.
subject_area: Civil procedure; Interim interdicts; Property development; Land allocation
catchwords_flynote: Interim interdict - requirements - prima facie right - unlawful construction and unapproved survey plan - stand allocation agreement - conditions precedent to development - party cannot obtain interdict protecting unlawful conduct - balance of convenience - alternative remedies - appeal dismissed.
headnote:
The appellant had acquired an allocation of a residential stand at Carrick Creagh through the Sally Mugabe Housing Cooperative. The respondent was the developer under a tripartite arrangement involving the Cooperative and the Ministry responsible for local government. The appellant constructed a double-storey building and began erecting a boundary wall. The respondent stopped the construction and moved beacons, alleging that the appellant was encroaching onto another stand and that his development was not approved. The appellant applied urgently for an interim interdict barring the respondent from accessing or interfering with his alleged property. The High Court dismissed the application.

    The Supreme Court held that the appellant had not established the cumulative requirements for interim relief. His alleged right was premised on an allocation contract that contained conditions precedent to development, which he had not fulfilled. He relied on an unapproved survey, was developing beyond the 4 000 square metres allocated to him, and was undertaking unapproved construction. A party cannot establish a prima facie right by relying on unlawful conduct or seek an order protecting such conduct. The respondent had rights under the tripartite arrangement to supervise construction works and the correct placement of beacons. The balance of convenience and availability of lawful alternative remedies also weighed against relief. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the High Court erred in dismissing the urgent chamber application for an interim interdict.
    Whether the appellant had established a prima facie right, even one open to some doubt.
    Whether alleged illegality in the appellant's development was relevant to interim relief.
    Whether the balance of convenience and availability of other remedies favoured the appellant.
legal_principles:
An interim interdict is an extraordinary remedy granted in the court's discretion. The applicant must establish a prima facie right, infringement or apprehended infringement, a well-grounded apprehension of irreparable harm, absence of another satisfactory remedy, and a balance of convenience favouring interim relief. The requirements are considered cumulatively.

    A prima facie right must be a legal right capable of sustaining a cause of action. A party cannot establish a right contrary to law, and a court will not grant an interdict to protect unlawful conduct.

    The stronger or weaker the apparent right, the more or less weight may be required from the other interdict requirements, but illegality may vitiate the applicant's entitlement to interim relief.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the High Court properly considered the legality of the appellant's construction because his alleged prima facie right depended on that conduct.

    Held, further, that the appellant had not satisfied the conditions precedent in the allocation contract and relied on an unapproved survey plan. He was developing an area exceeding that allocated under the agreement and could not establish a prima facie right to protect unlawful conduct.

    Held, further, that the respondent had a lawful role in administering development at Carrick Creagh Farm and placing beacons correctly.

    Held, further, that the balance of convenience and availability of lawful alternative remedies did not favour interim relief. The appeal was dismissed and the appellant ordered to pay costs.
ratio_decidendi: The appeal failed because the appellant did not establish a prima facie legal right capable of protection by interim interdict. His claim depended on unapproved construction, an unapproved survey, and development beyond the allocated extent before fulfilment of contractual conditions. A court cannot grant interim relief to protect unlawful conduct.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: SC 91 - 22 TENDAI BONDE vs NATIONAL FOODS & 2 ORS.docx
case_name:
TENDAI BONDE
    v
    (1) NATIONAL FOODS LIMITED (2) LOVEJOY NYANDORO [as Chairman of Appeals Committee] (3) CHIPO NHETA [as Chairman of Works Council]
judges:
In chambers virtually: MAKONI JA
    Judgment by: MAKONI JA
    Concurrence: Not applicable
subject_area: Labour law; Leave to appeal; Condonation; Supreme Court procedure; Abuse of process; Costs
catchwords_flynote: Labour appeal - application for condonation and extension of time - s 92F of the Labour Act [Chapter 28:01] - leave to appeal refused by Labour Court - application brought under wrong rule - rr 60(2) and 61 of Supreme Court Rules, 2018 - repeated defective applications by self-actor - inherent power to prevent abuse of process - striking off instead of dismissal - punitive costs.
headnote:
The applicant, a former National Foods employee, sought condonation and extension of time within which to seek leave to appeal against a Labour Court decision. The application was his seventh attempt to seek substantially the same relief. The respondents argued that the application was fatally defective because the applicant relied on rr 60(2) and 61 of the Supreme Court Rules, which apply to a litigant who has acquired a right of appeal but failed to exercise it within time.

    The Supreme Court held that where leave to appeal from the Labour Court is refused, s 92F requires the litigant to seek leave from a Supreme Court judge within the prescribed period. If that period has expired, the proper application is condonation for the late filing of an application for leave to appeal, not condonation for late noting of an appeal under r 61. The applicant had relied on the wrong rule and the matter was not properly before the Court. Although repeated defective applications may justify dismissal to prevent abuse of process, the Court struck the matter off the roll because it had not been shown that the applicant had previously been warned of that consequence. Punitive costs on the legal practitioner and client scale were awarded.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the applicant's application for condonation and extension of time was properly before a judge of the Supreme Court.
    Whether rr 60(2) and 61 of the Supreme Court Rules applied to his situation.
    Whether repeated defective filings justified dismissal instead of striking off.
    Whether costs on a punitive scale were warranted against a self-actor.
legal_principles:
Section 92F of the Labour Act permits an appeal from the Labour Court to the Supreme Court on a question of law. A litigant must first seek leave to appeal from the Labour Court; if refused, leave must be sought from a judge of the Supreme Court within the prescribed time.

    Rules 60(2) and 61 regulate appeals after leave has been granted and condonation for late noting of appeals. They do not apply where the litigant has not yet obtained leave and is late in seeking leave from a Supreme Court judge.

    The Supreme Court has inherent power to prevent abuse of its process by frivolous, vexatious or plainly hopeless proceedings. Repeated defective applications may in an appropriate case be dismissed rather than struck off the roll, but the Court retains discretion.

    Self-represented litigants are not automatically protected from punitive costs where they abuse court process or persistently file defective applications despite guidance.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the applicant had relied on the wrong rule. Rule 61 applies to condonation for late noting of an appeal after leave has been obtained, whereas the applicant needed condonation for late filing of an application for leave to appeal.

    Held, further, that the application was defective and not properly before the Court.

    Held, further, that the applicant's repeated defective applications approached abuse of process and could justify dismissal in a proper case. However, because it had not been shown that he had been warned previously of dismissal as a consequence, the application would be struck off the roll.

    Held, further, that costs on the legal practitioner and client scale were warranted because the applicant repeatedly filed defective applications despite prior guidance and put the respondents unnecessarily out of pocket.
ratio_decidendi: The application was struck off because the applicant had not acquired leave to appeal and therefore could not invoke the rules governing late noting of an appeal. His proper remedy was an application for condonation for late filing of an application for leave to appeal under the appropriate procedure.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: The Court warned that, in future, defective applications of this kind may be dismissed rather than merely struck off the roll where repeated filings abuse the Court's process.

document_name: SC 92 - 22 ZISWA v CHADWICK.docx
case_name:
(1) VALENTINE ZISWA (2) MARGARET ZISWA
    v
    (1) GRAEME SHAUN CHADWICK (2) LANDOS FARM (PRIVATE) LIMITED
case_number:
“a.   The first defendant shall pay to the plaintiffs the sum of:
    US$ 53 925.91 in respect of claim (d).
    US$13 381 I respect of claim (f).
     The first defendant is absolved from the instance in respect of claims (j)   and (l).”

    4	The order of the court a quo in respect of paragraphs 6 and 7 are set aside and substituted with the following:
    “6.
    The first defendant shall pay to the plaintiff the sum of US$15 008 in respect of claim (h).
    The first defendant is absolved from the instance in respect of claim (k).

    7.   The first defendant shall pay the plaintiffs costs of suit.”


    UCHENA JA:		I agree

    MAKONI JA:		I agree

    Gasa, Nyamadzawo & Associates, cross appellants’ legal practitioners

    Scanlen & Holderness, cross respondents’ legal practitioners
judges:
Panel: UCHENA JA, MAKONI JA AND KUDYA AJA
    Judgment by: KUDYA AJA
    Concurrence: UCHENA JA and MAKONI JA agreed.
subject_area: Contract; Lease; Joint venture; Agricultural operations; Damages; Agency; Waiver; Evidence; Costs
catchwords_flynote: Contract - lease and joint venture agreements - farm and equipment - non-variation clause - waiver by conduct - rental percentage increased from 6 per cent to 8 per cent - agent and principal - company not privy to agreements - damages for arrear rentals, missing equipment and damage to farm infrastructure - proof of quantum - expert valuation - absolution from the instance - appellate interference with findings of fact and costs - cross appeal succeeds in part.
headnote:
The cross appellants leased a farm and inventoried equipment to the first cross respondent under a lease and joint venture arrangement. The first cross respondent farmed tobacco and other crops through the second cross respondent company. Disputes arose about arrear rentals, improvements, equipment, removal of property and damage to infrastructure. The High Court granted some claims against the first cross respondent, dismissed all claims against the company, granted absolution on some claims, and awarded part of the costs against the lessors. The main appeal was disposed of after a defective notice of appeal and failed condonation application, leaving the cross appeal for determination.

    The Supreme Court held that the company was not liable because it was not privy to the agreements and acted as agent for Chadwick. However, the High Court erred in several respects. The evidence established that the rental rate had been waived or varied by conduct from 6 per cent to 8 per cent, notwithstanding the non-variation clause, and additional rentals were due. Certain claims that had been dismissed should instead have resulted in absolution from the instance where liability was shown but quantum was not proved. The Court also interfered with findings relating to the LTC cable, damaged or missing property, barn repairs and costs. The cross appeal succeeded in part with costs and the High Court order was amended to award additional sums and substitute absolution on some claims.
issues_for_determination:
Whether Landos Farm (Private) Limited was liable as a party to the lease and joint venture arrangements or only acted as Chadwick's agent.
    Whether the non-variation clause prevented reliance on an increased rental rate or whether waiver by conduct was established.
    Whether the High Court erred in its factual findings and credibility assessment.
    Whether liability and quantum were proved for the various claims for rentals, developments, property, equipment and damage.
    Whether the High Court costs order warranted appellate interference.
legal_principles:
An agent acting for a disclosed principal is generally not personally liable on the principal's contract unless it assumes separate contractual obligations. A party not privy to a contract cannot ordinarily be sued on it.

    A non-variation clause is generally enforceable, but the judgment recognised that rights under a contract may be waived expressly or tacitly by conduct where the circumstances establish waiver. Waiver requires conduct inconsistent with an intention to enforce the right.

    An appellate court interferes with factual findings only where they are irrational, clearly wrong, or unsupported by the evidence. Caution is required in evaluating a single witness, but common sense must not be displaced.

    A plaintiff claiming damages must prove both liability and quantum. Where liability is shown but quantum is not established, absolution from the instance may be appropriate rather than dismissal. A court may estimate damages where exact calculation is difficult, but there must be an evidential basis.

    A party cannot recover amounts higher than pleaded without amending the pleadings. Costs are discretionary but may be interfered with where the discretion was wrongly exercised.
held_decision_points:
Held, that Landos was not privy to the lease and joint venture agreements and was not liable as a contracting party. The dismissal of claims against it was upheld, but with no order as to costs.

    Held, further, that the evidence established waiver by conduct of the 6 per cent rental rate in favour of 8 per cent for the relevant season. The lessors were entitled to an additional US$58 694.40 in respect of arrear rentals.

    Held, further, that the first defendant was liable for additional sums in respect of claim (e), and for claims relating to outstanding developments, damaged property and barn repairs to the extent established.

    Held, further, that for claims where liability was established but quantum was not proved, the proper order was absolution from the instance rather than dismissal.

    Held, further, that the High Court's costs order constituted a wrong exercise of discretion. The cross appeal substantially succeeded and costs followed the result.
ratio_decidendi: The cross appeal succeeded in part because the High Court failed to give proper effect to the evidence of waiver, rental calculations, and several claims where liability was established. It also erred in dismissing some claims instead of granting absolution where quantum was not proved. The company was not liable because it was only Chadwick's agent and not a party to the contracts.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: SC 93 - 22 LION MATCH ZIMBABWE LIMITED vs LION MATCH (PRORIATERY).docx
case_name:
(1) LION MATCH ZIMBABWE LIMITED (2) O G INVESTMENTS (PRIVATE) LIMITED
    v
    LION MATCH (PROPRIETARY) LIMITED
court:
“The appeal against the decision of the    Registrar be and is hereby dismissed with costs.”

    ISSUES
    		The sole issue for determination in this appeal is whether the appellant’s registered trade- marks had lapsed and were as such open to use by other parties.

    ANALYSIS
    	     In coming to the conclusion that the appellant’s trade marks had lapsed, the Tribunal dealt extensively with the law regarding lapse and loss of trademarks in general.  However the Tribunal failed to deal with one decisive factor which was raised before the Registrar by the appellants.  At para 4 of its founding affidavit the first appellant stated as follows:
    “4 Applicant has a valid Trade Mark on the register, Trade Mark 172/99 which is due for renewal in February 2019.  This mark comprises a sitting lion and the words “lion reds, superior quality safety matches” and registered subject to disclaimers.”

    Further at para 5 of the same affidavit it is stated as follows:

      “5.  The applicant’s Trade MARK “Lion Match” accompanied by a device of a sitting lion in numerous variations have been in use in Zimbabwe since 1906 as evidenced by the following marks:




    The appellant’s contention, inter alia, is that Trade Mark 172/99 above is still on the register and was due for renewal in February 2019.  The respondent has not disputed this fact, although it says it has lodged an application to deregister trade mark 172/99.  Until such an order for deregistration is granted, trade mark 172/99 remains alive and available for use by the appellants.

    		It is common cause that the respondent wishes to register the appellant’s trade marks on the basis that they have lapsed. However Trade Mark 172/99 has not lapsed. The lapsed trademarks which the respondent seeks to register bear a substantial resemblance to Trade Mark 172/99 which is still registered and therefore valid. Accordingly the Tribunal’s decision allowing the application by the respondent runs foul to the provisions of s 14 (1) (a) of the Trade Marks Act [Chapter 26:04] which provides:
    “Prohibition of registration or maintenance of registration of deceptive, etc matter
    14 (1) A mark-
    The use of which would be likely to deceive or cause confusion,	 or
    -------------------  or
    -------------------- or
    -------------------- or
    -------------------- or
             shall not be registered as a Trade Mark.”

    DISPOSITION
    	      The Tribunal erred in ordering registration of the respondent’s trade mark when there was already in existence a similar trade mark registered in the name of the appellant, namely trade mark 172/99.  The resemblance between the appellant’s trade mark 172/99 and respondent’s proposed trade marks would have caused confusion in the market. For this reason the appeal must succeed.

    Costs shall follow the result.
    	       It is accordingly ordered as follows:
    The appeal succeeds with costs.

    The Judgment of the Intellectual Property Tribunal of Zimbabwe be and is hereby set aside and in its place substituted  the following:
    “The appeal against the decision of the Registrar be and is hereby dismissed with costs.”




    UCHENA JA		:		I Agree




    CHITAKUNYE JA	:    	 	 I Agree





    Costa and Madzonga, appellant’s legal practitioners
    B Matanga IP Attorneys, respondent’s legal practitioners

    Label and design	34
    352/52	Lion Device	34
    15 – 18/85	Lion Device	16,28,34,35
    11-14/85	Lion PAC	16,28,34,35
    172/99	Lion Reds and Lion device	34

Panel: UCHENA JA, CHIWESHE JA & CHITAKUNYE JA
    Judgment by: CHIWESHE JA
    Concurrence: UCHENA JA and CHITAKUNYE JA agreed.
subject_area: Intellectual property; Trade marks; Trade mark registration; Goodwill; Appeals from specialist tribunal
catchwords_flynote: Trade marks - application for registration - lapsed marks - goodwill and common law rights - existence of similar registered mark - s 14(1)(a) of the Trade Marks Act [Chapter 26:04] - likelihood of deception or confusion - Intellectual Property Tribunal allowing registration despite existing mark - Tribunal decision set aside - Registrar's decision restored.
headnote:
The first appellant and the respondent disputed entitlement to Lion-related trade marks in Zimbabwe. The respondent had formerly owned the first appellant's predecessor and in 1954 sold its business, trade marks and goodwill to the first appellant. The first appellant later experienced business difficulties and some trade marks lapsed. The respondent then traded in Zimbabwe and applied to register marks substantially similar to those used by the first appellant. The Registrar of Trade Marks found in favour of the first appellant. The Intellectual Property Tribunal reversed that decision and allowed the respondent's application.

    On appeal, the Supreme Court held that the Tribunal had overlooked a decisive point: Trade Mark 172/99 remained on the register in the name of the appellant and was available for use until deregistered. The respondent's proposed marks bore a substantial resemblance to that existing mark. Registration of the respondent's marks would therefore run foul of s 14(1)(a) of the Trade Marks Act, which prohibits registration of a mark the use of which would be likely to deceive or cause confusion. The Tribunal erred in ordering registration of the respondent's trade marks. The appeal succeeded with costs and the Registrar's decision was restored.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the appellant's registered trade marks had lapsed and were open for use or registration by other parties.
    Whether the Tribunal erred in failing to consider that Trade Mark 172/99 remained registered in the appellant's name.
    Whether registration of the respondent's proposed marks would be likely to deceive or cause confusion under s 14(1)(a) of the Trade Marks Act [Chapter 26:04].
legal_principles:
The lapse or cancellation of a trade mark registration does not necessarily establish abandonment of all trade mark rights or goodwill. Whether non-use is justified may depend on the duration of use, intention to resume use and whether the circumstances causing non-use were beyond the registrant's control.

    Section 14(1)(a) of the Trade Marks Act [Chapter 26:04] prohibits registration of a mark whose use would be likely to deceive or cause confusion. Where a similar mark remains registered and valid, registration of another substantially resembling mark may be refused to avoid confusion in the market.

    A trade mark remaining on the register remains alive and available for use until an order for deregistration is granted.
held_decision_points:
Held, that Trade Mark 172/99 remained on the register in the appellant's name and was due for renewal in February 2019. The respondent had not obtained an order deregistering it.

    Held, further, that the respondent's proposed trade marks substantially resembled Trade Mark 172/99. Registration of the respondent's marks would be likely to cause confusion and was prohibited by s 14(1)(a) of the Trade Marks Act.

    Held, further, that the Intellectual Property Tribunal erred in ordering registration of the respondent's marks. The appeal was allowed with costs and the Registrar's decision was restored.
ratio_decidendi: The appeal succeeded because, regardless of arguments about lapse of some marks, one similar trade mark remained validly registered in the appellant's name. Until deregistered, that mark prevented registration of the respondent's substantially similar marks because their use would be likely to deceive or cause confusion under s 14(1)(a) of the Trade Marks Act.
obiter_dicta_where_relevant: Not applicable

document_name: SC 94 - 22 Robert Tindwa v The Sheriff of Zimbabwe and Anorf.docx
case_name:
ROBERT TINDWA
    v
    (1) THE SHERIFF FOR ZIMBABWE (2) INSTITUTE OF MINING RESEARCH
judges:
Panel: HLATSHWAYO JA, BHUNU JA & MAKONI JA
    Judgment by: HLATSHWAYO JA
    Concurrence: BHUNU JA and MAKONI JA agreed.
subject_area: Company law; Corporate personality; Execution; Interpleader proceedings; Civil procedure
catchwords_flynote: Company law - separate legal personality - piercing or lifting the corporate veil - alter ego - execution against company judgment debtor - attachment of director's personal immovable property - interpleader proceedings - whether separate proceedings required to lift the corporate veil - r 205A of the High Court Rules, 1971 - s 318 of the Companies Act [Chapter 24:03] - manifest injustice - appeal dismissed.
headnote:
The second respondent obtained judgment against Westcott Speciality Chemicals (Pvt) Ltd. When the Sheriff attempted execution at the judgment debtor's registered office, he found nothing to attach and issued a nulla bona return. The second respondent then discovered that the judgment debtor had moved its business to the appellant's residence. The appellant was a director and company secretary of the judgment debtor. The second respondent treated him as the company's alter ego and caused his immovable property to be attached. The appellant claimed the property in interpleader proceedings on the basis that it belonged to him personally and not to the judgment debtor.

    The High Court dismissed the claim, finding that the appellant was the alter ego of the judgment debtor and that the corporate veil should be lifted. On appeal, the Supreme Court held that a company is ordinarily a separate legal person and that courts will not lightly disregard that personality. However, the corporate veil may be pierced where fraud, dishonesty, improper conduct or manifest injustice would otherwise result. It was not necessary for the second respondent to institute separate proceedings to lift the veil where the interpleader proceedings contained the facts necessary for that determination. The appellant ran the company from his residential address, was the only director dealing with creditors, and the company and appellant were in substance inseparable. Failure to pierce the veil would have caused manifest injustice. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the High Court erred in lifting the judgment debtor's corporate veil during interpleader proceedings.
    Whether a judgment creditor must first institute separate proceedings specifically seeking to pierce the corporate veil before attachment can proceed.
    Whether the facts justified treating the appellant as the alter ego of the judgment debtor.
legal_principles:
A company, once registered, has a separate legal personality distinct from its members, directors and shareholders. Courts do not lightly disregard separate corporate personality.

    The corporate veil may nevertheless be pierced where fraud, dishonesty, improper conduct or misuse of corporate personality is present, or where maintaining the separate personality would produce manifest injustice. Each case depends on its own facts.

    The existence of another procedural route or remedy does not necessarily preclude piercing the corporate veil where the facts otherwise justify that relief. A court hearing interpleader proceedings may pierce the veil where the same facts show that a director and company are inseparable and that separate proceedings would serve only as an unnecessary technical obstacle.

    Section 318(1) of the Companies Act provides a statutory basis for personal responsibility of those knowingly party to reckless, grossly negligent or fraudulent conduct of company business.
held_decision_points:
Held, that separate corporate personality remains the cardinal principle of company law, but it may be disregarded in cases of fraud, dishonesty, improper conduct or manifest injustice.

    Held, further, that it was not necessary for the judgment creditor to institute fresh proceedings to pierce the corporate veil where the facts necessary for that determination were before the court in the interpleader proceedings.

    Held, further, that the appellant was the judgment debtor's alter ego: he was the director who dealt with creditors, the company secretary, the person present at meetings, the only contactable person, and the company operated from his residential address.

    Held, further, that the High Court properly exercised its discretion by lifting the corporate veil. Failure to do so would have caused manifest injustice. The appeal was dismissed with costs.
ratio_decidendi: The appeal failed because the facts before the interpleader court showed that the appellant and the judgment debtor were in substance inseparable and that the company's separate personality was being used to evade a judgment debt. In those circumstances, the court could pierce the corporate veil within the interpleader proceedings without requiring separate proceedings.

document_name: SC 95 - 22 DICKSON KANDAWASVIKA AND ANOR vs THE SHERIFF OF ZIMBABWE AND ORS.docx
SC 96 - 22 MINING COMPANY.docx
case_name:
REDWING MINING COMPANY (PRIVATE) LIMITED
    v
    (1) ASSOCIATED MINE WORKERS UNION OF ZIMBABWE (2) THE MASTER OF THE HIGH COURT (3) THE REGISTRAR OF COMPANIES
judges:
Panel: BHUNU JA, CHIWESHE JA & CHITAKUNYE JA
    Judgment by: CHITAKUNYE JA
    Concurrence: BHUNU JA and CHIWESHE JA agreed.
subject_area: Insolvency law; Corporate rescue; Company law; Labour-related insolvency proceedings; Civil procedure
catchwords_flynote: Corporate rescue - Insolvency Act [Chapter 6:07] - application by affected person - meaning of affected person - registered trade union representing employees of the company - trade union representing industry generally insufficient - standard notice - s 124(2)(b) - definition of standard notice in s 2 - whether newspaper advertisement constitutes standard notice - peremptory requirements - non-compliance rendering application a nullity - appeal allowed.
headnote:
The first respondent applied in the High Court for the appellant to be placed under supervision and corporate rescue proceedings in terms of s 124 of the Insolvency Act. It alleged that it was an affected person because it represented workers in the mining and related industries and because the appellant owed it union dues. The appellant raised preliminary objections, including that the first respondent had no locus standi as an affected person and that it had not notified each affected person by standard notice. The High Court dismissed the preliminary objections and placed the appellant under corporate rescue.

    The Supreme Court held that the first two grounds of appeal were dispositive. Under the Insolvency Act, an affected person includes a registered trade union representing employees of the company, not merely a trade union representing workers in the relevant industry generally. The first respondent had to establish that it represented employees of the appellant. The High Court erred by accepting industry representation as sufficient. The Court also held that the Act requires notification of each affected person by standard notice, which is defined as notice by registered mail, fax, e-mail or personal delivery. Newspaper advertisement is not standard notice. Failure to comply with the peremptory notice requirements rendered the corporate rescue application a nullity. The appeal was allowed with costs and the application for corporate rescue was dismissed.
issues_for_determination:
Whether the first respondent had locus standi to bring the corporate rescue application as an affected person under the Insolvency Act.
    Whether a trade union representing workers in an industry generally qualifies, without more, as a trade union representing employees of the company.
    Whether newspaper advertisements and e-mails to some known creditors complied with the statutory requirement to notify each affected person by standard notice.
    Whether failure to comply with the statutory notice requirements rendered the application a nullity.
legal_principles:
Corporate rescue proceedings under the Insolvency Act are designed to rehabilitate a financially distressed company through temporary supervision and the development and implementation of a rescue plan, while balancing the rights of all relevant stakeholders.

    Section 124(1) permits an affected person to apply for an order placing a company under supervision and commencing corporate rescue proceedings. Section 121(1)(a) defines affected persons to include shareholders, creditors, any registered trade union representing employees of the company, and employees not represented by a registered trade union or their representatives.

    Section 124(2)(b) requires an applicant to notify each affected person of the application by standard notice. Section 2 defines standard notice as notice by registered mail, fax, e-mail or personal delivery. Newspaper advertisement is not included in that definition.

    Where legislation prescribes a mandatory procedure in peremptory terms, a court cannot expand the procedure to include modes of service or notification not provided by the statute. Non-compliance with peremptory corporate rescue notification requirements renders the application a nullity.
held_decision_points:
Held, that the first respondent could not establish locus standi merely by showing that it represented workers in the mining and related industries. The Act requires a registered trade union representing employees of the company that is the subject of the proceedings.

    Held, further, that the first respondent failed to establish that it represented all employees of the appellant or that all affected persons not represented by it had been notified.

    Held, further, that standard notice under the Act means notice by registered mail, fax, e-mail or personal delivery. Newspaper advertisements are not standard notice and cannot constitute compliance with s 124(2)(b).

    Held, further, that the failure to comply with the peremptory provisions on locus standi and notification was fatal. The appeal was allowed and the High Court order was replaced with an order dismissing the corporate rescue application.
ratio_decidendi: The corporate rescue application was a nullity because it was brought by a party that did not establish affected-person status under the Insolvency Act and because each affected person was not notified by standard notice as the Act peremptorily requires. Newspaper advertisements could not substitute for registered mail, fax, e-mail or personal delivery.
